  - Inconsistent interpretations of coverage and cost-sharing across AI systems
  - Hallucinated or outdated benefit explanations
  - Divergence between plan documents, regulatory guidance, and AI-generated communications
  - Limited ability to audit, explain, or reproduce how a specific output was generated
- Current governance approaches are insufficient
  - Post-deployment monitoring, organizational governance, and improved data completeness still depend on a deterministic semantic layer
  - Data transport standards do not encode authoritative policy interpretation in a form AI systems can deterministically retrieve
- High-cost operational pressure points in the Medicare ecosystem
  - Low-dollar, non-clinical appeals triggered by semantic inconsistency between AI explanations and statutory benefit definitions
  - Interpretive drift in medical necessity criteria and overly aggressive denial logic in AI-assisted utilization management
  - AI-generated misinformation driving secondary and tertiary contacts to beneficiary support channels
- Risk of automated misrepresentation or opportunistic upcoding without authoritative anchoring
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a specific failure mode called "agentic drift" that is distinct from data quality or model accuracy issues—AI systems diverge from policy intent because authoritative semantic meaning is not deterministically retrievable, not because data is unavailable
- Proposes a "dual-surface publishing pattern" where the same web page serves human-readable content to users while embedding machine-readable semantic anchors for AI systems
- Frames the solution as "deflationary AI adoption"—reducing downstream reconciliation costs rather than adding new regulatory overhead
- Provides concrete cost estimates: reducing explanation-driven appeals by 20-25% could yield "hundreds of millions of dollars" in annual savings; improving semantic determinism could reduce call center escalation rates by 25-30%
- Offers specific technical implementation via YAML-in-HTML semantic fragments that are versioned, provenance-bound, and checksum-verifiable
- Positions the approach as compatible with existing RAG architectures—semantic fragments become authoritative constraints within retrieval-augmented systems
- Suggests provider directories as an ideal "low-stakes, high-signal" demonstration environment because inaccuracies are a persistent, well-understood problem
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The core challenge HHS faces is not whether AI can be deployed, but how to scale its use without expanding federal operational burden or relying on opaque, non-scalable model-level oversight."
- "Deterministic semantic fragments function as sovereign truth anchors, eliminating the 'interpretation delta' between policy intent and automated enforcement."
- "Effective AI governance in healthcare will not be achieved solely by constraining model behavior. It also requires strengthening the semantic signals those models consume."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Opening statement
  - This submission responds directly to each of the ten questions in the HHS Health Sector AI RFI
  - Structured to support cross-program review where each response stands alone while referencing related questions where system-level dependencies exist
  - Intent is not to recommend a single policy outcome but to surface implementation realities, governance gaps, and second-order effects when AI systems are deployed within real clinical workflows
  - A workflow-to-entity crosswalk is included to clarify how responsibilities intersect across HHS StaffDivs and OpDivs to avoid gaps between policy intent and operational execution
  - Consistent finding across AI deployments: adoption and safety are determined less by model accuracy in controlled settings than by how AI systems interact with real-world clinical workflows, governance structures, data stewardship practices, procurement decisions, and learning feedback loops
  - Clinical AI functions as a socio-technical system that shapes clinician behavior, redistributes responsibility, and generates compounding learning from data produced through routine operations

- HHS equities map (StaffDivs and OpDivs)
  - Clinical AI adoption is inherently cross-cutting—the same AI-enabled workflow can engage multiple authorities and equities across HHS
  - A practical way to avoid gaps is to map responsibilities to the workflow itself
  - Workflow-to-entity crosswalk (illustrative):
    - Clinical deployment and payment incentives
      - Primary equities: CMS (payment policy, quality measures, demonstrations)
      - Success looks like: Incentives reward measurable workflow value and outcomes, not superficial "AI presence"
    - Health IT, interoperability, data standards
      - Primary equities: ASTP/ONC (health IT standards, interoperability policy)
      - Success looks like: Standards enable safe integration and auditability; interoperability does not imply unconstrained secondary use
    - Privacy, patient rights, and enforcement
      - Primary equities: OCR (HIPAA privacy/security enforcement and related guidance)
      - Success looks like: Clear, enforceable boundaries on access, use, and disclosure; meaningful transparency and recourse
    - Cybersecurity and national-risk posture
      - Primary equities: CIO (enterprise security practices), ONS (national security risk and resilience processes)
      - Success looks like: Security-by-design requirements are enforceable; AI is treated as part of critical clinical infrastructure
    - International alignment, standards, and data-sharing regimes
      - Primary equities: OGA (international engagement, standards, agreements)
      - Success looks like: Policies reconcile domestic safeguards with cross-border standards and data-sharing realities
    - Contract enforcement and vendor/subcontractor controls
      - Primary equities: Office of Acquisition/contracting functions (across HHS)
      - Success looks like: RFPs and contracts require auditability, learning controls, and secure operations; flow-down to subcontractors
    - Safety of AI-enabled clinical products and decision support
      - Primary equities: FDA (device/SaMD, CDS policy)
      - Success looks like: Clear expectations for validation, change control, and post-market monitoring
    - Evidence generation and implementation science
      - Primary equities: AHRQ (evidence and outcomes research)
      - Success looks like: Evaluation frameworks reflect real-world workflow effects and heterogeneous settings
    - Public health integration (where AI crosses into surveillance)
      - Primary equities: CDC
      - Success looks like: Defined boundaries between clinical and public health uses; governance for secondary use
    - Research and biomedical data ecosystems
      - Primary equities: NIH
      - Success looks like: Strong stewardship of clinical-research interfaces and reuse pathways
    - Safety-net and underserved delivery settings
      - Primary equities: HRSA, IHS, SAMHSA
      - Success looks like: Adoption pathways account for resourcing constraints, equity, and operational realities
  - Cross-cutting note: acquisition is the control plane that converts policy intent into enforceable obligations
    - Interoperability, cloud use, vendor access, and model-update practices are ultimately determined by contract terms, monitoring rights, and operational controls

- Cross-cutting analytic lens: data, workflows, and learning
  - Practical governance test for clinical AI deployments:
    - Use case and end-user clarity: identify the decision-maker, operator, and accountable party; specify the action taken because of the model output
    - Data lifecycle boundaries: define what data enters, what leaves, retention periods, and what is used for care delivery versus model improvement
    - Learning accumulation and sovereignty controls: specify whether operational data may be used for model improvement and under what constraints; treat derived artifacts (embeddings, fine-tunes, prompts, logs, telemetry) as governed outputs
    - Security posture and access paths: enforce identity, least privilege, logging, incident response, and subcontractor controls; define cloud and remote access defaults with audit rights
    - Bias introduction points and drift: identify where bias can enter (training data, workflow selection, labeling, clinician reliance, feedback loops) and require continuous monitoring and rollback
  - Across multiple clinical domains (including precision oncology and advanced therapies), the most durable advantage—and most consequential vulnerability—often lies not in the AI model or the molecule, but in control over the learning system
    - The workflow-generated data, outcomes, failure modes, and process metadata that enable iterative optimization over time
  - Useful distinctions:
    - Privacy: who can see my data
    - Sovereignty: who determines what my data means and how much it is worth
    - Security: who has access to my data and what they can do with it
  - Many governance approaches address privacy and baseline security but do not explicitly govern learning accumulation (how data becomes compounding capability)
    - In clinical AI, learning accumulation increasingly determines long-run trust, competitiveness, and resilience

- Response to Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation and adoption/use in clinical care
  - Clinical AI frequently crosses mismatched risk frameworks (privacy, cybersecurity, clinical safety, enterprise risk, vendor product risk)
    - These frameworks do not align by default, and gaps emerge at their seams unless governance is explicitly integrated
  - Workflow misfit is the dominant barrier
    - AI systems that perform well in development can fail in practice when they add documentation burden, disrupt clinician cognitive flow, or create ambiguous responsibility at the point of care
  - Key barriers include:
    - Unclear accountability for AI-influenced decisions, especially when AI is adaptive or when vendor updates materially change behavior
    - Trust gaps driven by opacity, inconsistent performance across subpopulations, and unclear post-deployment monitoring
    - Data provenance and integration challenges (EHR heterogeneity, fragmented identifiers, incomplete documentation, variable labeling)
    - Operational and security constraints (network segmentation, device integration, patch management, identity and access management)
    - Procurement realities: contracts that do not specify auditability, model-update controls, data-use boundaries, or subcontractor constraints

- Response to Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes; CFR citations where applicable
  - Condition participation or incentives on auditability (minimum logs, update provenance, and model-change notification)
  - Make data-use boundaries enforceable, clarifying whether operational data may be used for model improvement and under what authorizations
  - Most leverage comes from making governance and monitoring enforceable at the workflow level while avoiding incentives for superficial AI adoption
  - Priority areas for HHS to consider:
    - CMS-aligned incentives that reward measurable workflow value and outcomes rather than mere AI integration
    - Programmatic designs that require lifecycle governance (monitoring, drift management, change control) as a condition of participation in demonstrations, quality programs, or certifications
    - Clear, enforceable expectations for data stewardship, auditability, and vendor/subcontractor controls, implemented through acquisition and contracting requirements where feasible
    - Interoperability policies that distinguish data exchange for care delivery from secondary use for model training and learning accumulation
  - If HHS seeks specific CFR citations, a targeted follow-on request scoped to particular programs (e.g., Medicare conditions of participation, quality reporting, certification programs) would enable more precise citations by commenters

- Response to Question 3: Non-medical devices: novel legal and implementation issues; HHS role
  - Governance and contracts should explicitly cover workflow metadata and derived artifacts (prompts, embeddings, fine-tunes, telemetry), which often represent the primary signals of value and intent rather than mere exhaust
  - For non-medical device AI used in clinical care (workflow, triage, documentation, scheduling, administrative decision support), key issues include liability allocation, indemnification, privacy/security obligations, and governance of adaptive behavior post-deployment
  - Novel legal and implementation issues commonly arise from:
    - Ambiguity in responsibility when AI influences clinician behavior or care pathways without being a regulated medical device
    - Contracting and indemnification gaps that fail to define change control, performance representations, or audit rights
    - Privacy/security frameworks that govern access and disclosure but do not explicitly govern learning accumulation or model improvement using operational clinical data
    - Third-party and subcontractor risk (cloud hosting, analytics, support), especially when access paths are opaque
  - HHS can help by publishing model contract clauses and minimum auditability/lifecycle governance expectations usable by providers and payers, aligned across OCR, ASTP/ONC, CIO/ONS, and CMS

- Response to Question 4: Non-medical devices: evaluation methods pre- and post-deployment; mechanisms
  - Evaluate impact on clinician behavior, including automation bias, alert fatigue, de-skilling, and reliance shifts over time
  - Most promising evaluation approaches combine pre-deployment validation with post-deployment monitoring sensitive to workflow effects, site-to-site variation, and distribution shift
  - Recommended evaluation components:
    - Human-centered workflow evaluation (cognitive load, alert fatigue, override rates, time-to-task, downstream workflow changes)
    - Robustness and stress testing across settings, subpopulations, and data quality conditions
    - Post-deployment drift detection and performance monitoring linked to outcomes where feasible
    - Change-control governance for model updates, including criteria for rollback
  - HHS can support these via contracts, grants, cooperative agreements, and prize competitions (often with AHRQ/NIH/CMS partners), prioritizing monitoring, auditability, and workflow-aware evaluation methods

- Response to Question 5: Support for accreditation, certification, testing, and credentialing
  - Establish a minimum governance checklist for certification programs, including update control, monitoring, disclosure of data use for training, and incident response
  - HHS can amplify private-sector accreditation, certification, industry-driven testing, and credentialing by helping establish baseline expectations for trustworthy clinical AI that are measurable and auditable
  - Support could include:
    - Reference expectations for auditability (logging, traceability of inputs/outputs, update history) and minimum cybersecurity posture for clinical AI deployments
    - Credentialing frameworks for AI governance roles within health care organizations
    - Model contract language and procurement checklists that incorporate security-by-design and lifecycle monitoring requirements
    - Public-private benchmarking and validation resources, including shared evaluation protocols and testbeds

- Response to Question 6: Where AI met/exceeded vs fell short; novel tools with greatest potential
  - Distinguish value created for patients and providers from value transferred to vendors through learning accumulation from operational data
  - AI tends to meet or exceed expectations when tightly coupled to well-defined workflows with clear success metrics (reducing administrative burden, improving triage consistency, accelerating access, reducing preventable harm)
  - AI tends to fall short when deployed as a generic overlay without governance, monitoring, or integration into decision rights
  - High-potential tool categories:
    - Workflow-aware clinical documentation support that measurably reduces administrative burden without introducing silent errors
    - Risk stratification and triage tools with clear accountability and monitoring, especially in resource-constrained settings
    - Tools that surface quality insights by analyzing workflow metadata and outcomes (not only clinical text)
    - Patient-facing navigation and care coordination supports that improve adherence and reduce friction across care settings
  - To accelerate learning on "what works," HHS can support standardized reporting and privacy-preserving benchmarking

- Response to Question 7: Organizational roles and administrative hurdles for adoption
  - Adoption decisions are typically shaped by clinical leadership, operational leadership, compliance/privacy, IT/security leadership, and (increasingly) AI governance committees or enterprise risk management functions
  - Primary administrative hurdles:
    - Unclear ownership for AI lifecycle management and monitoring across clinical, IT, and compliance functions
    - Procurement bottlenecks and contract limitations that impede auditability or change control
    - Data access and interoperability friction across EHRs and ancillary systems
    - Training, credentialing, and change management requirements for clinicians and staff
    - Security approvals and network integration constraints, especially where tools require cloud connectivity
  - This is why HHS-wide coordination across CMS, ASTP/ONC, OCR, CIO/ONS, OGA, and acquisition functions is essential

- Response to Question 8: Interoperability opportunities; data types, standards, and benchmarking tools
  - Interoperability expands the attack surface and secondary-use risk unless paired with access control, provenance, and enforceable training boundaries
  - Enhanced interoperability can widen market opportunities and accelerate AI where it improves data provenance, reduces integration friction, and enables benchmarking—while still respecting governance boundaries on secondary use and learning accumulation
  - Priority opportunities:
    - Standardized representations of clinical workflows and outcomes (including provenance/lineage metadata)
    - Consistent patient identity and record linkage across care settings
    - Data standards and benchmarking tools that support monitoring and post-deployment evaluation
    - Mechanisms for privacy-preserving benchmarking and federated evaluation where direct pooling is impractical
  - Interoperability policy should explicitly distinguish exchange for care delivery from secondary use for model training and learning accumulation

- Response to Question 9: Patient/caregiver desired challenges and concerns
  - Patients increasingly want clarity not only on who sees their data, but who benefits from derived learning and what recourse exists
  - Patients and caregivers often prioritize improvements that reduce friction, shorten wait times, improve communication, and increase transparency about decisions affecting care access and quality
  - Common concerns:
    - Privacy and security of sensitive health information, including secondary uses beyond direct care
    - Unclear accountability when AI influences clinical decisions or access to services
    - Bias and inequitable treatment, especially when monitoring and recourse mechanisms are weak
    - Reduced human agency or difficulty obtaining explanations, corrections, and escalation pathways
  - These concerns reinforce the need for lifecycle governance, auditability, and clear decision rights

- Response to Question 10: AI research priorities; literature on impact and costs/benefits/transfers
  - Prioritize methods to measure learning accumulation and value transfer across stakeholders, and governance models that integrate privacy, sovereignty, and security operationally
  - HHS research priorities that would accelerate responsible adoption include implementation science for clinical AI, workflow-aware evaluation methods, and scalable approaches to monitoring and governance
  - Priority research areas:
    - Methods for post-deployment monitoring, drift detection, and change control in heterogeneous clinical settings
    - Human-centered evaluation methods that quantify workflow impact and clinician reliance over time
    - Privacy-preserving benchmarking, federated evaluation, and secure data-sharing architectures
    - Governance models for adaptive systems that integrate privacy, sovereignty, and security considerations
  - HHS can help curate and translate literature into practical evaluation playbooks and clarify how costs/benefits/value transfer are measured across providers, payers, vendors, and patients

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
A consulting firm with national security and clinical AI expertise argues that effective AI governance hinges not on model accuracy but on workflow integration, contract enforcement, and controlling who benefits from the learning that clinical data generates.

---

- **Type:** Business

- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Advises on clinical AI implementation and governance; directly affected by how HHS structures requirements for AI adoption, contracting, and oversight

The biggest barriers to responsible adoption aren't algorithmic performance but workflow integration, lifecycle governance, and enforceable data stewardship. Acquisition and contracting are the primary control plane for making policy intent enforceable.

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- SANCIAN LLC submits response to HHS's Request for Information on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
  - Drawing on over 15 years of experience implementing AI governance across federal health agencies including HHS, CDC, FDA, and NIH
  - Offers operational framework for translating AI policy into sustainable clinical practice

- Core Position
  - HHS should treat AI not as technology to be adopted, but as care infrastructure to be governed
  - The gap between AI potential and AI reality is not a technology problem—it is a governance, implementation, and human factors problem

- Five Principles for Acceleration
  - Governance-first adoption
    - Establish accountability structures before technology procurement
    - Abstract frameworks fail at the point of care; organizations need actionable playbooks
  - Readiness before reimbursement
    - Require organizations to demonstrate governance maturity as a prerequisite for AI-related payment incentives
    - Premature adoption without readiness wastes resources and erodes trust
  - Payment aligned to burden reduction
    - Incentivize AI that saves clinician time, reduces documentation burden, and improves access
    - Not simply reward adoption of new technology
  - Implementation science over model development
    - Fund research on real-world deployment, workflow integration, and adoption drivers
    - Not just algorithmic performance in controlled settings
  - Human capacity as a success metric
    - Measure cognitive load reduction, time returned to patient care, and clinician trust
    - AI that adds burden—even if accurate—fails the people it aims to serve

- Why Now
  - Accelerating federal AI strategies, workforce strain, and rising care complexity create narrow 24–36 month window
  - Must embed governance and human-centered design before AI becomes irreversible infrastructure
  - Rural, safety-net, and underserved settings face greatest risk of being left behind or harmed if equity not embedded from start

- About SANCIAN LLC
  - Evidence-based AI governance and implementation advisory focused on public health, health systems, and regulated environments
  - Combines validated frameworks including NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 23894, PMBOK, Kotter, and ADKAR
  - Operational tooling for readiness assessment, equity evaluation, and sustainable adoption
  - Founder has over 15 years analyzing AI deployments across government and health systems
    - Doctoral-level expertise in public health and clinical management
    - Dual lens—clinical and population health—informs approach
  - Operating Model: Readiness → Selection → Implementation → Governance → Continuous Improvement
  - AI SoftLife™ Philosophy: Design AI as capacity infrastructure that reduces cognitive burden, protects clinicians' energy, and sustains humane care delivery
    - AI should support presence, not replace judgment

- Question 1: Barriers to Private Sector Innovation and Adoption
  - Observed Barriers
    - Fragmented governance and unclear accountability
      - Conflicting guidance across HHS divisions
      - No clarity on which agency's rules apply, particularly for non-device AI outside FDA's traditional scope
      - Uncertainty delays procurement and suppresses investment
    - Low organizational readiness
      - Gaps extend beyond technology to data governance, workflow optimization, change management, and leadership alignment
      - Organizations underestimate transformation required, leading to failed implementations that erode confidence
    - Trust gaps across stakeholders
      - Clinicians, patients, and administrators have legitimate concerns about bias, safety, explainability, and liability
      - Current deployments have not adequately addressed these concerns
    - Clinician burnout and workflow friction
      - AI that adds steps or cognitive load creates friction rather than relief—regardless of technical performance
      - Exhausted care teams cannot absorb poorly designed tools
    - Procurement complexity and reimbursement uncertainty
      - Traditional processes add 6–18 months to adoption timelines
      - Unclear ROI projections make business cases difficult to build
  - SANCIAN Response
    - AI ReadyCheck™ for organizational readiness baseline
    - Governance-first design aligned to NIST AI RMF
    - Workflow co-design with clinician input
    - Equity impact assessment (AI-EQUITYClear™) before deployment
  - HHS Opportunity
    - Standardize readiness and governance expectations across programs
    - Encourage "readiness before reimbursement"—ensuring organizations demonstrate governance maturity as prerequisite for AI-related payment incentives

- Question 2: Regulatory, Payment Policy, and Programmatic Changes
  - Regulatory Recommendations
    - Create tiered, risk-based AI governance pathway aligned with NIST AI RMF risk tiers
      - Lower-risk administrative AI could follow streamlined pathways
      - Higher-risk clinical decision support would require rigorous evaluation
      - Provides predictability while maintaining proportionate oversight
    - Issue guidance for non-device clinical AI accountability
      - Publish model accountability frameworks addressing roles, responsibilities, and oversight
      - Relevant touchpoints: ONC's Health IT Certification Program (45 CFR Part 170), Conditions of Participation (42 CFR Parts 482–485)
    - Enable pilots with real-world evidence requirements
      - Structured pilots integrating AI into care pathways with mandatory evidence collection
      - Would build evidence base for broader adoption
  - Reimbursement Recommendations
    - CMS should incentivize AI that delivers measurable value
    - Clinician time saved—reward AI that reduces documentation and administrative burden
    - Improved access and continuity—support AI that expands access to underserved and rural populations
    - Value-based AI add-ons—enable payment adjustments under MSSP, ACO REACH, and bundled payments when AI contributes to outcomes
    - Current fee schedule (42 CFR Part 414) requires new CPT codes or modifiers for AI-assisted services

- Question 3: Novel Legal and Implementation Issues
  - Key Issues
    - Shared liability
      - Liability allocation among AI developers, healthcare organizations, and clinicians remains unclear
      - Traditional malpractice frameworks assume human decision-makers—AI disrupts these assumptions
    - Auditability of adaptive models
      - Organizations need mechanisms to audit model behavior, track changes, and demonstrate deployed models match validated versions
    - Data provenance, consent, and security
      - Questions persist regarding training data sources, patient consent, and AI-specific cybersecurity vulnerabilities
      - HIPAA (45 CFR Parts 160, 164) requires interpretation for AI contexts
    - Indemnification gaps
      - Standard vendor contracts do not adequately address AI-specific risks
      - Includes performance guarantees, bias warranties, and monitoring obligations
  - SANCIAN Approach
    - Role-based accountability matrices
    - Continuous monitoring with model risk logs
    - Governance charters embedded in operations—not as standalone policy documents
  - HHS Role
    - Convene cross-sector standards development for liability and auditability
    - Publish reference governance architectures
    - Provide model contract provisions or safe harbor guidance
    - Could pilot reference architectures to demonstrate operational feasibility

- Question 4: AI Evaluation Methods
  - What Works
    - Pre-deployment: Bias testing across demographic subgroups, scenario-based validation, usability testing, and "shadow mode" deployment comparing AI recommendations to actual clinical decisions
    - Post-deployment: Outcome drift monitoring, safety signal surveillance, human override tracking, and periodic re-validation
    - Workflow impact: Traditional accuracy metrics are necessary but insufficient; clinical AI must be evaluated on operational integration—not just algorithmic performance
  - AI SoftLife™ Metrics
    - Cognitive load reduction—does the AI decrease mental effort?
    - Time returned to patient care—does the AI free clinicians for presence?
    - Trust and adoption—do clinicians actually use and trust the AI?
    - Structured equity evaluation using frameworks such as AI-EQUITYClear™ to assess algorithmic fairness across demographic subgroups
  - HHS Support
    - Fund applied evaluation frameworks
    - Support shared testbeds and benchmark datasets—particularly for rural and safety-net settings where evidence is thinnest
    - Public-private partnerships could validate methodologies across contexts

- Question 5: Supporting Private Sector Activities
  - The Need
    - Market needs third-party validation of governance maturity, bias and safety controls, and operational integration
    - Healthcare organizations lack reliable signals to distinguish quality AI products from marketing
  - Proposal: AI in Clinical Care Maturity Model
    - HHS should support development of standardized assessment frameworks for:
      - Organizations—readiness to adopt, implement, and govern AI responsibly
      - Vendors—product quality, transparency, safety, and governance features
      - Care pathways—effectiveness of AI integration into specific clinical workflows
  - Additional Mechanisms
    - Recognize private standards from NIST, IEEE, and healthcare bodies by reference
    - Provide regulatory safe harbors for certified AI
    - Fund workforce credentialing through HRSA for AI competencies

- Question 6: AI Performance and Opportunities
  - Where AI Works
    - Documentation automation (ambient intelligence, AI-assisted notes)
    - Imaging triage (radiology, pathology screening)
    - Risk stratification (sepsis prediction, early warning)
    - Population health analytics (care gap identification, resource allocation)
  - Where AI Falls Short
    - Workflow misalignment creates friction
    - Alert fatigue leads to override of valid recommendations
    - Black-box decisioning undermines trust
    - Equity gaps from non-representative training data worsen disparities in underserved populations
  - High-Potential Applications
    - Care navigation (guiding patients through complex journeys)
    - Social determinants integration (connecting patients to community resources)
    - Chronic disease coaching (personalized self-management)
    - Team-based decision support
    - Frailty/dementia care for aging populations
  - AI should support presence, not replace judgment; highest-value applications restore clinician capacity to be fully present with patients

- Question 7: Decision-Makers and Administrative Hurdles
  - The Reality
    - Multiple stakeholders hold effective veto power: CIO/CTO (infrastructure), CMIO/CMO (clinical safety), Compliance/Legal (regulatory exposure), CFO (budget)
    - Result: no single owner of AI outcomes
    - Decisions stall, accountability diffuses, promising initiatives die in committee
  - Primary Hurdles
    - Procurement and IT security cycles add 6–18 months
    - Absence of governance structures forces ad hoc decisions
    - Medical staff bylaws rarely address AI
    - Legal review delays from lack of AI expertise
    - Change management underinvestment undermines adoption
  - SANCIAN Solution
    - Named AI Executive Owner model—single accountable leader with cross-functional authority
    - Cross-functional AI governance councils—formalized structures with defined decision rights
    - Decision clarity before tooling—governance infrastructure before product evaluation
  - HHS should encourage accountable leadership models in guidance and grants
    - Including Conditions of Participation updates requiring AI oversight designation

- Question 8: Interoperability for AI Development
  - Priority Data Types
    - FHIR-based clinical data with bulk access for population AI
    - Social determinants of health (currently inconsistent)
    - Patient-reported outcomes captured outside encounters
    - Imaging and genomic data for precision medicine applications
  - Standards and Benchmarking
    - Model cards and audit metadata for standardized AI documentation
    - Benchmark datasets for fairness representing diverse populations
    - Provenance tracking for data lineage and model versioning
  - SANCIAN View
    - Interoperability must include governance data, not just clinical data
    - Standards for AI oversight artifacts—audit logs, performance results, bias assessments—would enable consistent governance and regulatory oversight
  - HHS should fund interoperability for AI oversight artifacts and require transparency standards in funded programs
  - USCDI expansion should prioritize AI-relevant data elements

- Question 9: Patient and Caregiver Perspectives
  - What Patients Want
    - Access (timely appointments, expanded availability)
    - Continuity (coordinated care across providers)
    - Clarity (understandable information, timely results)
    - Less friction (reduced paperwork, streamlined processes)
    - Personalized but fair care (tailored treatment without discrimination)
  - Patient Concerns
    - Bias and exclusion (fear of discrimination)
    - Loss of human connection (AI replacing empathy)
    - Data misuse (uncertainty about privacy)
    - Lack of recourse (unclear accountability for harm)
    - Accessibility (barriers for those with disabilities or limited digital access)
  - AI SoftLife™ Response
    - Human-in-the-loop by design
    - Transparency as trust infrastructure
    - Equity checks embedded early
  - HHS should make patient trust a core success metric—not a soft outcome, but the foundation of sustainable adoption

- Question 10: AI Research Priorities
  - High-Impact Research Areas
    - Implementation science in real clinics—how to deploy effectively, including change management and adoption drivers
    - Equity and bias mitigation in deployment—detecting and addressing bias as AI operates in practice
    - Human-AI teaming—optimal collaboration patterns and trust calibration
    - Safety and drift monitoring—detecting failures and ensuring robustness across contexts
    - Economic impact of burden reduction—effects on clinician time, costs, and workforce sustainability
  - Literature Gap
    - Plenty on models, not enough on adoption, outcomes, and workflow impact
    - Most studies evaluate controlled settings; evidence on real-world implementation is thin
    - Rural and safety-net settings are particularly underrepresented
  - HHS Opportunity
    - Fund longitudinal pilots tied to outcomes and workforce wellbeing
    - Support public-private learning collaboratives for sharing implementation lessons—including failures

- Closing Position
  - HHS should treat AI not just as technology, but as care infrastructure—governed, evaluated, reimbursed, and funded to:
    - Improve outcomes for patients across all populations, including rural and safety-net settings
    - Restore clinician capacity by reducing burden rather than adding it
    - Advance equity by embedding fairness from design through deployment
    - Build durable public trust through transparency, accountability, and human-centered design
  - Adoption fails when governance is abstract and care teams are exhausted
  - AI succeeds when it is designed for clarity, accountability, and human ease
  - Accelerate AI by making it operationally safe, economically viable, and emotionally sustainable for the people who deliver and receive care
  - The next 24–36 months represent a narrow window
  - SANCIAN LLC stands ready to support HHS in translating these recommendations into operational reality
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A veteran AI governance consultancy with 15 years of federal health agency experience urges HHS to treat clinical AI as "care infrastructure to be governed," requiring readiness assessments before reimbursement and measuring success by clinician burden reduction rather than technology adoption.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** SANCIAN LLC
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** AI governance and implementation advisory specializing in public health and regulated environments; founder has 15+ years analyzing AI deployments across HHS, CDC, FDA, and NIH with doctoral-level expertise in public health and clinical management
- **Geographic Scope:** National, based in Atlanta, GA
- **Stake in Issue:** Provides AI governance consulting services to federal health agencies and health systems; offers proprietary frameworks (AI ReadyCheck™, AI-EQUITYClear™, AI SoftLife™) that could be adopted or referenced in HHS guidance
structuredSections.corePosition: HHS should treat AI not as technology to be adopted, but as care infrastructure to be governed. The gap between AI potential and AI reality is not a technology problem—it is a governance, implementation, and human factors problem. Organizations need actionable playbooks, not abstract frameworks, and AI that adds burden—even if accurate—fails the people it aims to serve.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Require "readiness before reimbursement"—organizations must demonstrate governance maturity as a prerequisite for AI-related payment incentives
  - Prevents premature adoption that wastes resources and erodes trust
  - Use standardized readiness assessments as baseline requirements

- Create tiered, risk-based AI governance pathway aligned with NIST AI RMF
  - Streamlined pathways for lower-risk administrative AI
  - Rigorous evaluation for higher-risk clinical decision support
  - Provides predictability while maintaining proportionate oversight

- Issue guidance for non-device clinical AI accountability
  - Publish model accountability frameworks addressing roles, responsibilities, and oversight
  - Update Conditions of Participation to require AI oversight designation

- Align reimbursement to burden reduction, not technology adoption
  - Reward AI that reduces documentation and administrative burden (clinician time saved)
  - Support AI that expands access to underserved and rural populations
  - Enable value-based AI add-ons under MSSP, ACO REACH, and bundled payments
  - Create new CPT codes or modifiers for AI-assisted services

- Establish named AI Executive Owner model in healthcare organizations
  - Single accountable leader with cross-functional authority
  - Cross-functional AI governance councils with defined decision rights
  - Governance infrastructure before product evaluation

- Fund implementation science research over model development
  - Longitudinal pilots tied to outcomes and workforce wellbeing
  - Public-private learning collaboratives for sharing implementation lessons—including failures
  - Focus on rural and safety-net settings where evidence is thinnest

- Develop AI in Clinical Care Maturity Model
  - Standardized assessment frameworks for organizations, vendors, and care pathways
  - Recognize private standards from NIST, IEEE, and healthcare bodies
  - Provide regulatory safe harbors for certified AI
  - Fund workforce credentialing through HRSA for AI competencies

- Expand interoperability to include AI governance data
  - Standards for AI oversight artifacts—audit logs, performance results, bias assessments
  - USCDI expansion should prioritize AI-relevant data elements
  - Benchmark datasets for fairness representing diverse populations

- Convene cross-sector standards development for liability and auditability
  - Publish reference governance architectures
  - Provide model contract provisions or safe harbor guidance

- Make patient trust a core success metric
  - Human-in-the-loop by design
  - Transparency as trust infrastructure
  - Equity checks embedded early in development
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Fragmented governance creates uncertainty and delays adoption
  - Conflicting guidance across HHS divisions
  - No clarity on which agency's rules apply for non-device AI outside FDA's traditional scope
  - Uncertainty delays procurement and suppresses investment

- Organizations lack readiness for AI implementation
  - Gaps extend beyond technology to data governance, workflow optimization, change management, and leadership alignment
  - Organizations underestimate transformation required
  - Failed implementations erode confidence in future AI initiatives

- Multiple stakeholders hold veto power with no single owner of AI outcomes
  - CIO/CTO, CMIO/CMO, Compliance/Legal, and CFO all have blocking authority
  - Decisions stall, accountability diffuses, promising initiatives die in committee
  - Procurement and IT security cycles add 6–18 months

- Clinician burnout undermines AI adoption
  - AI that adds steps or cognitive load creates friction regardless of technical performance
  - Exhausted care teams cannot absorb poorly designed tools
  - Workflow misalignment and alert fatigue lead to override of valid recommendations

- Liability and auditability remain unresolved
  - Liability allocation among AI developers, healthcare organizations, and clinicians unclear
  - Traditional malpractice frameworks assume human decision-makers—AI disrupts these assumptions
  - Standard vendor contracts do not adequately address AI-specific risks

- Equity gaps threaten underserved populations
  - Non-representative training data worsens disparities
  - Rural, safety-net, and underserved settings face greatest risk of being left behind or harmed
  - Black-box decisioning undermines trust

- Research literature has critical gaps
  - Plenty on models, not enough on adoption, outcomes, and workflow impact
  - Most studies evaluate controlled settings; evidence on real-world implementation is thin
  - Rural and safety-net settings particularly underrepresented

- Patient concerns about AI remain unaddressed
  - Fear of bias and discrimination
  - Loss of human connection
  - Uncertainty about data privacy and accountability for harm
  - Accessibility barriers for those with disabilities or limited digital access
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a narrow 24–36 month window to embed governance before AI becomes irreversible infrastructure
  - After this window, retrofitting governance will be far more difficult and costly

- Proposes measuring AI success by "time returned to patient care" and "cognitive load reduction" rather than traditional accuracy metrics
  - Frames this as "AI SoftLife™ philosophy"—AI should support presence, not replace judgment

- Argues interoperability standards must include governance data, not just clinical data
  - Standards for audit logs, performance results, and bias assessments would enable consistent oversight
  - Currently overlooked in interoperability discussions

- Notes that medical staff bylaws rarely address AI
  - Creates governance vacuum at the clinical level
  - Legal review delays stem from lack of AI expertise in healthcare legal departments

- Observes that healthcare organizations lack reliable signals to distinguish quality AI products from marketing
  - Market failure in AI quality signaling creates procurement paralysis
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "HHS should treat AI not as technology to be adopted, but as care infrastructure to be governed."

- "AI that adds burden—even if accurate—fails the people it aims to serve."

- "Adoption fails when governance is abstract and care teams are exhausted. AI succeeds when it is designed for clarity, accountability, and human ease."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- This is a response to the RFI on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
- Because this is an RFI rather than a rulemaking action, its principal value is agenda-setting
  - It defines which governance dimensions will be treated as central as HHS explores policy, program, and operational levers
- Primary recommendation: AI governance that prioritizes transparency without enforceable contestability will fail in practice
  - Explanations, model cards, and "human-in-the-loop" language do not produce accountability unless affected parties can compel review, correction, and repair within defined time bounds
  - Affected parties means patients and clinicians
- Treat AI as a source of exposure, not a neutral tool
  - When AI influences clinical action, eligibility, documentation, or resource allocation, it becomes part of the causal chain of harm
  - Governance must reflect that by requiring durable decision provenance and enforceable reversal pathways
- Minimum governance requirements HHS should advance:
  - Decision provenance as a default output
    - AI-influenced actions should generate a durable record: inputs, model/version, confidence/uncertainty, downstream action taken, and accountable authority
  - Contestability with binding timelines
    - Create standard pathways for clinicians and patients to contest AI-driven outcomes
    - Must include deadlines and escalation procedures
  - Override and rollback as real control surfaces
    - "Human oversight" should be defined as a functional override and rollback capability
    - Not merely a nominal review step
  - Liability clarity and anti-evasion design
    - Avoid governance frameworks that allow responsibility to be laundered through vendor disclaimers or performative "human review"
    - System design often prevents meaningful intervention while claiming human oversight exists
- Accelerating adoption without enforceable remedy externalizes risk onto clinicians and patients and undermines trust
- The question is not whether an AI system can explain itself
  - It is whether harm can be forced to matter through correction, repair, and rollback
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: An independent systems researcher argues that AI governance without enforceable contestability and reversal mechanisms is meaningless theater, and urges HHS to require binding timelines, real override capabilities, and liability structures that prevent responsibility laundering.
