structuredSections.corePosition: AI governance that prioritizes transparency without enforceable contestability will fail in practice. When AI influences clinical decisions, it becomes part of the causal chain of harm—governance must require durable decision provenance and enforceable reversal pathways, not just explanations and nominal human review.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Require decision provenance as a default output
  - AI-influenced actions should generate durable records: inputs, model/version, confidence/uncertainty, downstream action taken, and accountable authority
- Establish contestability with binding timelines
  - Standard pathways for clinicians and patients to contest AI-driven outcomes
  - Include deadlines and escalation procedures
- Define "human oversight" as functional override and rollback capability
  - Not merely a nominal review step
- Design liability frameworks to prevent anti-evasion
  - Block responsibility laundering through vendor disclaimers or performative "human review"
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Transparency without contestability produces no real accountability
- "Human-in-the-loop" language often masks systems that prevent meaningful intervention
- Current frameworks allow responsibility to be laundered through vendor disclaimers
- Accelerating adoption without enforceable remedy externalizes risk onto clinicians and patients
- Trust will be undermined if harm cannot be corrected through formal mechanisms
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Frames AI not as a neutral tool but as "a source of exposure"—shifting the conceptual frame from capability to liability
- Distinguishes between systems that can "explain themselves" versus systems where "harm can be forced to matter"
- Identifies "responsibility laundering" as a specific design pattern to guard against
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI governance that prioritizes transparency without enforceable contestability will fail in practice."
- "The question is not whether an AI system can explain itself; it is whether harm can be forced to matter through correction, repair, and rollback."
- "Avoid governance frameworks that allow responsibility to be laundered through vendor disclaimers or performative 'human review' while the system design prevents meaningful intervention."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Executive Summary
  - FERZ AI welcomes the opportunity to respond to HHS's RFI on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
  - This response serves two purposes
    - Provide concrete recommendations addressing HHS's specific questions
    - Equip federal reviewers with a technical framework for evaluating AI governance claims from vendors
  - The AI governance market includes many solutions marketed as "guardrails," "responsible AI," "ethical AI," and "trustworthy AI"
    - These terms lack precise technical definitions
    - Makes it difficult for federal reviewers to evaluate competing claims
  - Acquisition officers benefit from distinguishing between
    - Marketing claims ("our AI is safe and responsible")
    - Statistical governance (probabilistic approaches providing confidence intervals)
    - Deterministic governance (fail-closed enforcement with reproducible, auditable decision artifacts)
  - Document provides
    - A taxonomy of AI governance approaches with strengths and limitations
    - Evaluation criteria for federal reviewers to assess vendor claims
    - Specific questions to ask vendors claiming governance capabilities
    - FERZ's recommendations for regulatory frameworks to accelerate trustworthy AI adoption
  - Key insight: The fundamental barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not AI capability—it is the inability to prove that an AI system's governance was functioning correctly at the time of any specific decision
    - Without such proof, liability cannot be allocated, regulatory compliance cannot be demonstrated, and patient trust cannot be justified
    - This is a governance problem, not an AI problem

- Part I: Understanding AI Governance—A Taxonomy for Federal Reviewers
  - 1.1 The Three Layers of AI Systems
    - Model Layer
      - The AI model itself (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini)
      - Includes training data, architecture, learned parameters
      - Model-level governance (RLHF, Constitutional AI) attempts to make the model "safer" through training
      - Inherently probabilistic—the model tends to behave well but cannot guarantee it
    - Application Layer
      - Software that wraps the model—user interface, integration points, business logic
      - Where prompts are constructed and outputs processed
      - Application-level governance (prompt engineering, output filtering) adds checks around the model
      - Still largely probabilistic—filters can miss edge cases
    - Deployment Layer
      - Operational environment where AI actions occur—clinical workflows, EHR integration, decision execution
      - The "last mile" before real-world impact
      - Deployment-level governance (FERZ's focus) enforces constraints at the execution boundary
      - Can be deterministic—nothing executes unless governance validates it
    - Critical distinction: Most AI "safety" approaches operate at Model or Application Layer where governance is inherently probabilistic
      - Deterministic governance operates at Deployment Layer where execution can be gated by mathematically verifiable constraints

  - 1.2 The Fundamental Problem: Probabilistic vs. Deterministic Governance
    - Probabilistic Governance: "Likely Compliant"
      - Includes RLHF, Constitutional AI, content filtering, most "guardrails"
      - Operates on statistical principles
      - Provides confidence that a system usually behaves correctly, expressed as accuracy percentages or confidence intervals
      - How it works: Train model to prefer "good" outputs; use classifiers to flag "bad" outputs; filter problematic content
      - What vendors claim: "95% accuracy," "reduced harmful outputs by 80%," "enterprise-grade safety"
      - Risk exposure: At clinical scale, even small residual failure rates produce frequent governance escapes
        - Question is whether escapes are prevented pre-execution and provable/auditable when they occur
        - Probabilistic approaches cannot guarantee either
      - Key limitation: Cannot provide proof that governance was functioning correctly for any specific decision
        - After adverse event, no way to demonstrate system was operating within governance boundaries
    - Deterministic Governance: "Provably Compliant"
      - Provides fail-closed enforcement with reproducible, independently verifiable audit artifacts at governance layer
      - Distinct from claiming underlying AI model is deterministic—it is not
      - Determinism achieved at execution boundary through
        - Pre-execution validation against explicit rules
        - Recorded snapshots of all inputs, policies, and outputs
        - Reproducible evaluation that can be independently verified
      - How it works: Every AI output passes through validation layer checking explicit rules
        - If validation fails, output is blocked ("deny-by-default" or "fail-closed")
        - Every decision generates cryptographic proof bundle capturing inputs, policy state, and verdict
      - What this enables: Bit-for-bit reproducible governance decisions, cryptographic audit trails, bounded residual errors quantifiable by rule coverage
      - In healthcare deployment: Every AI decision validated against explicit rules before execution
        - Failed validations blocked
        - Every decision can be independently verified and replayed
      - Key advantage: After adverse event, governance compliance can be independently verified by third parties
        - Liability can be allocated based on provable governance state at decision time

  - 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison: Critical Differences
    - Compliance assurance
      - Probabilistic: Statistical confidence (e.g., "95% accurate")
      - Deterministic: Verifiable conformance artifacts with bounded residual risk
    - Failure mode
      - Probabilistic: "Fail-open"—ungoverned outputs may proceed
      - Deterministic: "Fail-closed"—nothing executes without validation
    - After adverse event
      - Probabilistic: Cannot prove governance was working
      - Deterministic: Cryptographic proof of governance state
    - Decision reproducibility
      - Probabilistic: Cannot reproduce specific decisions
      - Deterministic: Bit-for-bit reproducible ("replay test")
    - Audit capability
      - Probabilistic: Logs of outputs and classifications
      - Deterministic: Cryptographic proof chain (Proof-Carrying Decisions)
    - Liability allocation
      - Probabilistic: Unclear—governance state unknowable
      - Deterministic: Clear—governance state provable
    - FDA 21 CFR Part 11
      - Probabilistic: Difficult to satisfy—records not verifiable
      - Deterministic: Designed for compliance—immutable audit trails
    - Regulatory inspection
      - Probabilistic: Requires trust in vendor's claims
      - Deterministic: Third-party verification possible

  - 1.4 Why This Distinction Matters for Healthcare
    - Healthcare is precision-critical domain where statistical assurances are insufficient
    - Scenario: Clinical AI assists with medication recommendations
      - Vendor claims high accuracy in avoiding dangerous drug interactions
      - Large hospital processes thousands of AI-assisted medication decisions monthly
      - With probabilistic governance
        - Some fraction of decisions may have ungoverned drug interaction risks
        - Fraction may be small but absolute number becomes significant at scale
        - After adverse event, no mechanism to prove whether governance was functioning correctly for that specific decision
      - With deterministic governance
        - Every medication recommendation validated against drug interaction rules before reaching clinician
        - Failed validations blocked and escalated
        - After any decision, cryptographic proof demonstrates exactly which rules were applied and whether they passed
    - Difference fundamentally changes liability allocation, regulatory compliance posture, and patient safety assurance

  - 1.5 Emerging Architectures: RAG, Agentic AI, and Governance Topology
    - Two architectural patterns accelerating AI deployment in healthcare
      - Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)—grounds AI outputs in retrieved documents
      - Agentic AI—enables AI systems to take actions autonomously
    - Why RAG and Agentic Patterns Increase Governance Requirements
      - These patterns do not reduce governance complexity—they increase it
      - RAG systems introduce dynamic, unpredictable inputs
        - AI outputs depend on retrieved context that changes with each query—patient records, clinical literature, formulary data
        - Model-level governance cannot anticipate what documents will be retrieved
        - Governance must validate outputs regardless of retrieval context
      - Agentic systems execute actions with real-world consequences
        - API calls, database writes, order placements, message transmissions
        - Post-hoc monitoring cannot reverse incorrectly administered medication, misconfigured treatment plan, or erroneous prior authorization denial
        - Governance must operate pre-execution, not post-detection
      - Reviewer Note: Be skeptical of claims that RAG or agentic architectures are inherently "safer" or "more accurate"
        - These architectures shift where errors occur, not whether they occur
        - Fundamental question remains: can you prove governance was functioning for any specific decision?
    - Governance Topology in Multi-Agent Systems
      - As agentic AI evolves toward multi-agent architectures, critical question: where does governance operate?
      - Per-Agent Governance distributes guardrails across individual agents
        - Creates structural vulnerabilities
        - Agent-to-agent communication may bypass governance entirely
        - Emergent behaviors from agent interactions remain ungoverned
        - No unified audit trail across agent ensemble
        - Inconsistent policy enforcement across agents with different guardrail implementations
        - "Governance arbitrage"—sophisticated actors may route sensitive operations through least-governed agent
      - Centralized Governance at Execution Boundary validates all actions before execution regardless of originating agent
        - Ensures consistent policy enforcement, unified auditability
        - Captures emergent behaviors at point of real-world impact
        - Governance layer is model-agnostic and agent-agnostic—governs actions, not agents
      - Example: Clinical agentic system with retrieval agent, reasoning agent, and action agent
        - With per-agent governance: Each agent has separate guardrails but no single point validates that chain of interactions produced compliant outcome
        - With centralized execution-boundary governance: Every action passes through deterministic validation regardless of initiating agent; audit trail captures full decision chain
    - Questions for Multi-Agent AI Proposals
      - Does governance operate per-agent or at execution boundary?
      - If per-agent, how are inter-agent communications governed?
      - How are emergent behaviors from agent interactions detected and controlled?
      - Is there unified audit trail across all agents, or separate logs per agent?
      - Can you replay a decision involving multiple agents and obtain identical results?

- Part II: Evaluating AI Governance Claims—A Guide for Federal Reviewers
  - 2.1 Red Flags: Marketing Language vs. Technical Substance
    - "AI Guardrails"
      - Usually means content filtering or output classifiers
      - Probabilistic, may miss edge cases
      - Ask: What happens when guardrails fail? Can you prove they were active for a specific decision?
    - "Responsible AI"
      - Usually means training-time interventions (RLHF, Constitutional AI)
      - Model tends to behave responsibly
      - Ask: How do you verify responsible behavior for a specific decision? What are your error bounds?
    - "Enterprise-Grade Safety"
      - Marketing term with no technical definition
      - May mean access controls or logging
      - Ask: Define "enterprise-grade." What specific guarantees do you provide? How are they verified?
    - "Explainable AI"
      - Post-hoc rationales
      - May explain why AI probably made a decision but cannot prove compliance
      - Ask: Can explanations be independently verified? Can decisions be replayed to confirm the explanation?
    - "AI Monitoring"
      - After-the-fact analysis
      - Detects problems after they occur, not before
      - Ask: How do you prevent violations rather than detect them? What is your pre-execution validation?
    - "99% Accurate"
      - 1% of decisions are ungoverned
      - At scale, this means thousands of potential failures
      - Ask: What happens to the 1%? Can you identify which specific decisions are in that 1%?

  - 2.2 The Four Tests: Evaluating Governance Capabilities
    - FERZ has published Four Tests Standard (4TS) as vendor-neutral framework for evaluating AI governance claims
    - Stop Test
      - Question: Can the system be halted before side-effects occur? Does governance validation happen before execution?
      - Verify: Ask "What happens if governance validation fails?" Answer must be "the action is blocked," not "we log it for review"
    - Ownership Test
      - Question: Is there clear authority signing governance policy? Can you identify who authorized AI's operational boundaries?
      - Verify: Ask "Show me the signature chain for governance policy." Must be cryptographic proof of who authorized what
    - Replay Test
      - Question: Can any historical decision be reproduced and verified? Can regulator independently confirm what happened?
      - Verify: Ask "Show me a decision from last week and reproduce it." Replayed decision must match original exactly
    - Escalation Test
      - Question: Are human oversight routes defined? When governance denies an action, what happens? Is there mandatory custody transfer?
      - Verify: Ask "What happens when you deny an action?" Must be defined escalation paths with human decision-makers
    - Key insight: Governance system that cannot pass all four tests provides statistical confidence at best
      - Cannot provide proof-level assurance required for regulated healthcare environments

  - 2.3 Technical Due Diligence Questions
    - Architecture Questions
      - At what layer does your governance operate? (Model, Application, or Deployment)
      - Is governance validation performed before or after execution?
      - What is default behavior when governance cannot validate an action? (Fail-open vs. fail-closed)
      - Can your system operate with any AI model, or is it tied to specific model provider?
    - Compliance Verification Questions
      - How do you prove governance was functioning correctly for a specific historical decision?
      - Can third party independently verify your compliance claims without trusting your attestations?
      - What cryptographic mechanisms ensure audit trail integrity?
      - How do you satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements for electronic records and signatures?
    - Error Bound Questions
      - What are your quantified error bounds? (Not accuracy percentages—actual mathematical error bounds)
      - How did you derive those bounds? (Empirical testing vs. mathematical proof)
      - What assumptions must hold for those bounds to be valid?
      - How do you detect and handle cases that fall outside your bounded domain?
    - Operational Questions
      - What is your governance validation latency? (Must not significantly impact clinical workflows)
      - How do you handle governance policy updates without disrupting operations?
      - What happens if your governance system experiences an outage?
      - Can you demonstrate air-gapped deployment for sensitive environments?

- Part III: Responses to HHS Specific Questions
  - Question 1: Barriers to Private Sector Innovation
    - Primary barriers are governance-related, not capability-related
    - The Governance Gap
      - Healthcare requires certainty; current AI governance provides only statistical confidence
      - Organizations cannot deploy AI when they cannot prove compliance
    - Liability Uncertainty
      - Without clear governance boundaries, liability cannot be allocated
      - Legal departments block deployments when governance state is unknowable
    - Audit Trail Inadequacy
      - FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires verifiable electronic records
      - Current AI systems produce outputs, not evidence
      - No cryptographic proof that governance was functioning at decision time
    - Regulatory Uncertainty
      - Non-medical device AI falls into gray zones
      - Organizations cannot invest when regulatory requirements are unclear
    - FERZ Recommendation: HHS should establish clear governance requirements distinguishing between statistical and deterministic assurance levels
      - Creates pathway for organizations to deploy AI with appropriate governance for their risk tolerance

  - Question 2: Regulatory and Policy Changes
    - Regulatory Recommendations with CFR Citations
      - Citations are illustrative, not exhaustive
      - Recommend HHS issue guidance clarifying application to AI governance rather than amending underlying regulations
      - 21 CFR Part 11 (Electronic Records and Signatures)
        - Issue guidance recognizing cryptographic proof bundles (Proof-Carrying Decisions) as satisfying §11.10 audit trail requirements and §11.50 signature manifestation requirements
        - Current language contemplates traditional electronic signatures
        - Guidance should clarify cryptographic attestation of policy state satisfies integrity and attribution requirements
      - 45 CFR Part 170 (Health IT Certification)
        - Consider adding AI governance attestation requirements to §170.315 certification criteria
        - Systems claiming clinical decision support functionality should demonstrate reproducible governance validation as certification condition
      - 42 CFR Part 493 (CLIA)
        - Issue guidance on how AI-assisted laboratory analytics should satisfy §493.1251 procedure manual requirements and §493.1283 retention requirements
        - Deterministic governance artifacts could satisfy "complete record" requirements
      - 45 CFR Part 164 (HIPAA Security)
        - Clarify how AI governance audit trails interact with §164.312(b) audit control requirements and §164.312(c) integrity requirements
        - Cryptographic proof chains may exceed current compliance expectations
        - Guidance should recognize this
    - Payment Policy Recommendations
      - Governance Attestation as Reimbursement Condition
        - For AI-assisted clinical workflows in high-risk categories, require governance attestation artifacts as condition of reimbursement
        - Creates market incentive for governance adoption
      - Incentives for Burden-Reduction Tools
        - Provide enhanced reimbursement for AI-assisted documentation, coding, and prior authorization when governance artifacts enable post-payment integrity verification
        - Aligns payer interests with governance adoption
      - Pilot Reimbursement Models
        - CMS should pilot reimbursement models for AI tooling where governance artifacts support audit
        - Generates evidence on governance value while managing risk

  - Question 3: Novel Legal Issues
    - The Black Box Problem
      - Current AI cannot prove governance state at decision time
      - Creates fundamental liability allocation challenges
      - Who is responsible when governance cannot be verified?
    - Temporal Governance
      - AI systems may be updated between deployment and adverse event
      - Without governance infrastructure capturing policy state at decision time, retrospective analysis is impossible
    - Multi-Party Accountability
      - Clinical AI involves model developers, governance providers, deploying institutions, and clinicians
      - Clear boundary definitions needed
    - Privacy-Preserving Audit
      - HIPAA requires protecting PHI while maintaining auditable records
      - Deterministic governance with cryptographic proofs can address this
      - Regulatory recognition needed
    - HHS's Role
      - Define minimum governance architecture standards for clinical AI
      - Create accountability frameworks for multi-party deployments
      - Publish reference architectures demonstrating acceptable approaches
    - Mapping to FDA SaMD and CDS Realities
      - Deterministic governance artifacts support auditability and change control regardless of whether tool is regulated as device
      - For CDS outside device regulation under 21st Century Cures Act criteria, governance artifacts provide documentation and traceability for institutional risk management
      - For tools qualifying as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD), same artifacts support FDA expectations for traceability, change control, and post-market surveillance
      - Governance layer provides compliance infrastructure scaling across regulatory spectrum without requiring different architectures for different categories

  - Question 4: AI Evaluation Methods
    - Most promising evaluation methods focus on governance verification rather than AI capability assessment
    - Pre-Deployment Evaluation
      - Governance Coverage Analysis: What percentage of AI outputs pass through deterministic validation? Target 100% for high-risk decisions
      - Rule Completeness Testing: Do governance rules cover all compliance requirements (HIPAA, FDA, state regulations)?
      - Adversarial Testing: Systematic testing of governance behavior under unusual or adversarial inputs
    - Post-Deployment Evaluation
      - Replay Verification: Can any historical decision be replayed and obtain identical results? Enables post-hoc audit without trusting logs
      - Escalation Analysis: How often do decisions escalate to human review? Are thresholds appropriately calibrated?
      - Governance Drift Detection: Monitor for changes in AI behavior indicating model drift affecting governance effectiveness
    - HHS Opportunity: Support development of governance evaluation tools and conformance testing infrastructure
      - 4TS conformance bundle provides a model

  - Questions 5-10: Summary Responses
    - Question 5 (Private Sector Activities)
      - HHS should recognize deterministic governance in certification criteria
      - Fund pilot programs
      - Create testing infrastructure
      - Establish public-private partnerships for reference architectures
    - Question 6 (AI Tool Performance)
      - AI succeeds in domains with clear boundaries and low compliance complexity (imaging, analytics)
      - Underperforms in governance-heavy domains
      - Common thread is governance inadequacy, not capability limitation
      - Deterministic governance unlocks high-value applications currently blocked by compliance concerns
    - Question 7 (Decision-Makers)
      - General Counsel and Risk Management are typically blocking stakeholders
        - Cannot approve deployments without governance guarantees
      - CMOs focus on clinical efficacy; CIOs on integration; Compliance Officers on regulations
      - Deterministic governance directly addresses Risk Management/Legal blockers
    - Question 8 (Interoperability)
      - Critical gaps exist in standards for exchanging governance attestations, audit trail formats, and AI governance metadata in FHIR
      - 4TS Proof-Carrying Decision (PCD) format provides foundation
      - Concrete recommendation: HHS could sponsor HL7 FHIR Implementation Guide for exchanging governance attestations alongside CDS Hooks responses or as extensions to relevant FHIR resources
        - Would enable EHRs and clinical systems to verify governance state without proprietary integrations
    - Question 9 (Patient Concerns)
      - Patient concerns about privacy, accuracy, dehumanization, and bias are fundamentally governance concerns
      - Deterministic governance addresses them
        - Cryptographic audit trails ensure verifiable privacy compliance
        - Reproducible decisions enable accuracy demonstration
        - Escalation protocols preserve human oversight
        - Bias detection provides equity assurance
    - Question 10 (Research Priorities)
      - HHS should prioritize research in
        - Deterministic governance methods
        - Cryptographic audit infrastructure
        - Causal inference for clinical AI
        - Human-AI collaboration frameworks

- Part IV: About FERZ—Deterministic Governance Infrastructure
  - FERZ AI develops deterministic AI governance infrastructure software
  - Unlike approaches attempting to make AI models "safer" through training, FERZ operates at deployment layer
    - Wraps any AI system in deterministic validation shell guaranteeing compliance with explicitly defined rules
  - Core Technology
    - LASO(f): Multi-tier linguistic and action governance framework
      - Eight validation tiers (syntax, semantics, pragmatics, stylistics, discourse, morphology, phonology/graphemics, lexicon)
      - Bidirectional propagation
      - Designed for low-latency, high-accuracy validation
    - DELIA: Flat constraint architecture with Z3 SMT solver for rapid deterministic validation
      - Ideal for action governance requiring ALLOW/DENY/ESCALATE decisions
    - CausaCore: Cross-domain causal modeling with four specialized engines
      - Drug interaction analysis, clinical trial design, precision medicine
    - 4TS Standard: Vendor-neutral open standard for AI governance verification (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0)
    - Performance benchmarks available upon request under appropriate evaluation conditions
  - Regulatory Alignment
    - Systems designed for regulatory compliance from ground up
    - FDA 21 CFR Part 11: Electronic signatures, immutable audit trails, record integrity verification
    - HIPAA: Access controls, encryption, audit logging for protected health information
    - EU AI Act: High-risk system governance, documentation requirements, explainability
    - SEC Rule 17a-4: WORM storage, tamper-evidence, 7-year retention
  - Offer of Collaboration
    - Pilot Programs: Deploy LASO(f) and DELIA in hospital pilots to generate real-world evidence
    - Standards Development: Collaborate on healthcare-specific adaptations of 4TS Standard
    - Technical Consultation: Provide briefings to HHS staff on deterministic governance approaches
    - Research Partnerships: Participate in HHS-sponsored research through cooperative agreements or CRADAs
  - Vendor-Neutral Guidance
    - Encourage HHS to adopt evaluation criteria and governance principles regardless of vendor
    - Four Tests Standard (4TS) published under open license to enable vendor-neutral assessment
    - Interest is in establishing clear standards that accelerate trustworthy AI adoption
    - FERZ benefits from well-defined market, not from regulatory ambiguity
  - Closing statement: The promise of AI in clinical care is immense; the barrier is trust; deterministic governance infrastructure can bridge that gap—transforming AI from liability concern into verified asset
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: AI governance infrastructure company argues that healthcare AI adoption is blocked not by capability limitations but by the inability to prove compliance at decision time, and urges HHS to establish standards distinguishing probabilistic "guardrails" from deterministic, cryptographically verifiable governance.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** FERZ AI (FERZ LLC), submitted by Neil Nair, Chief Business Officer
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Developer of deterministic AI governance infrastructure software; expertise in cryptographic audit trails, compliance verification, and deployment-layer AI governance
- **Geographic Scope:** National (U.S. federal regulatory focus)
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct commercial interest in HHS adopting governance standards that recognize deterministic approaches; would benefit from certification criteria, pilot programs, and interoperability standards aligned with their technology
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe the fundamental barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not AI capability—it is the inability to prove that an AI system's governance was functioning correctly at the time of any specific decision. Without such proof, liability cannot be allocated, regulatory compliance cannot be demonstrated, and patient trust cannot be justified. HHS should establish clear governance requirements that distinguish between statistical assurance ("likely compliant") and deterministic assurance ("provably compliant"), creating pathways for organizations to deploy AI with appropriate governance for their risk tolerance.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish clear governance requirements distinguishing statistical from deterministic assurance levels
  - Create pathway for organizations to deploy AI with governance appropriate to risk tolerance
- Issue guidance on existing regulations rather than amending them
  - 21 CFR Part 11: Recognize cryptographic proof bundles as satisfying audit trail and signature requirements
  - 45 CFR Part 170: Add AI governance attestation requirements to Health IT certification criteria
  - 42 CFR Part 493 (CLIA): Clarify how deterministic governance artifacts satisfy procedure manual and retention requirements
  - 45 CFR Part 164 (HIPAA): Clarify how AI governance audit trails interact with audit control and integrity requirements
- Implement payment policy changes to incentivize governance adoption
  - Require governance attestation artifacts as reimbursement condition for high-risk AI-assisted workflows
  - Provide enhanced reimbursement for AI-assisted documentation/coding when governance artifacts enable post-payment verification
  - CMS should pilot reimbursement models where governance artifacts support audit
- Define minimum governance architecture standards for clinical AI
  - Create accountability frameworks for multi-party deployments
  - Publish reference architectures demonstrating acceptable approaches
- Sponsor HL7 FHIR Implementation Guide for exchanging governance attestations
  - Enable EHRs and clinical systems to verify governance state without proprietary integrations
- Recognize deterministic governance in certification criteria
- Fund pilot programs and create testing infrastructure
- Establish public-private partnerships for reference architectures
- Prioritize research in deterministic governance methods, cryptographic audit infrastructure, causal inference for clinical AI, and human-AI collaboration frameworks
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Current AI governance provides only statistical confidence when healthcare requires certainty
  - Organizations cannot deploy AI when they cannot prove compliance
- Liability uncertainty blocks deployment
  - Without clear governance boundaries, liability cannot be allocated
  - Legal departments block deployments when governance state is unknowable
- Audit trail inadequacy under current approaches
  - FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires verifiable electronic records
  - Current AI systems produce outputs, not evidence
  - No cryptographic proof that governance was functioning at decision time
- Regulatory uncertainty for non-medical device AI
  - Falls into gray zones
  - Organizations cannot invest when requirements are unclear
- Marketing terminology obscures technical reality
  - Terms like "guardrails," "responsible AI," "enterprise-grade safety" lack precise technical definitions
  - Federal reviewers cannot evaluate competing claims
- Probabilistic governance fails at scale
  - Even small residual failure rates produce frequent governance escapes at clinical volume
  - Cannot prove governance was functioning for any specific decision after adverse event
- RAG and agentic AI architectures increase governance complexity
  - Do not reduce risk—shift where errors occur
  - Post-hoc monitoring cannot reverse real-world consequences
- Multi-agent systems create governance gaps
  - Per-agent governance allows inter-agent communications to bypass governance
  - Emergent behaviors remain ungoverned
  - No unified audit trail
  - "Governance arbitrage" possible through least-governed agent
- Temporal governance problem
  - AI systems may be updated between deployment and adverse event
  - Without capturing policy state at decision time, retrospective analysis is impossible
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Reframes the AI adoption problem: The barrier is not AI capability but governance provability—this is a governance problem, not an AI problem
- Identifies General Counsel and Risk Management as the actual blocking stakeholders for AI deployment, not clinical or technical leadership
- Proposes "governance arbitrage" concept: In multi-agent systems, sophisticated actors may route sensitive operations through the least-governed agent
- Distinguishes three layers where governance can operate (Model, Application, Deployment), noting most "safety" approaches operate at layers where governance is inherently probabilistic
- Offers vendor-neutral evaluation framework (Four Tests Standard) published under open license, explicitly encouraging HHS to adopt criteria regardless of vendor selection
- Notes that patient concerns about privacy, accuracy, dehumanization, and bias are fundamentally governance concerns addressable through deterministic approaches
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The fundamental barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not AI capability—it is the inability to prove that an AI system's governance was functioning correctly at the time of any specific decision. Without such proof, liability cannot be allocated, regulatory compliance cannot be demonstrated, and patient trust cannot be justified. This is a governance problem, not an AI problem."
- "Federal reviewers should be skeptical of claims that RAG or agentic architectures are inherently 'safer' or 'more accurate.' These architectures shift where errors occur, not whether they occur."
- "The promise of AI in clinical care is immense. The barrier is trust. Deterministic governance infrastructure can bridge that gap—transforming AI from a liability concern into a verified asset."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction
  - Submitting response based on more than two decades of experience in the U.S. healthcare ecosystem
  - Focus areas: healthcare operations, data integration, payment models, and technology implementation
  - Perspective grounded in realities of how clinical, administrative, and financial systems function together in practice, not theoretical AI capability
  - AI holds significant promise for improving clinical outcomes, reducing administrative burden, and lowering costs
  - Adoption often stalls not because of model performance, but because of structural, financial, and organizational friction
  - Comments focus on friction points related to private sector innovation and reimbursement alignment

- Response to Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care and its adoption in clinical care
  - Most significant barriers are not technical—they are organizational, administrative, and incentive-based
  - Ownership and accountability for adoption are often unclear
    - AI initiatives frequently treated as IT implementations
    - In reality, they require coordinated change across clinical, operational, compliance, and administrative teams
    - When responsibility for workflow redesign and ongoing maintenance is diffused, adoption stalls even when technology performs as intended
  - Recurring barrier: resistance driven by perceived increases in workload
    - Experience implementing large-scale provider data management systems prior to modern AI tools
    - Downstream teams resisted adoption because new workflows were viewed as "extra steps"
    - Long-term value of structured data capture (reliable, high-quality reporting) was not immediately visible to end users
    - Leadership desired improved analytics and insights but paid insufficient attention to explaining how outcomes depended on consistent, incremental workflow changes
  - Over time, these steps typically became easier and more efficient, but early resistance significantly slowed adoption
  - AI magnifies this dynamic
    - Without deliberate workflow design, education, and change management, AI tools risk being perceived as additive burden rather than enabling infrastructure
  - Most common point of failure is the handoff to end users
    - When cultural transformation and workflow ownership are not addressed explicitly, even well-designed systems fail to achieve sustained use
    - This is not a technology failure but an implementation and governance one

- Response to Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Misaligned incentives and uncertainty around value recognition remain significant obstacles
  - Payment models rarely account for trust, education, and governance work required for adoption
    - Many AI tools deliver value by improving efficiency, reducing administrative burden, or augmenting clinician decision-making rather than producing discrete billable events
    - Organizations struggle to justify investment when reimbursement pathways are unclear or nonexistent
  - Even relatively narrow AI tools face adoption hurdles when trust is not addressed
    - Example: implementing AI-enabled note-taking tools in healthcare settings
    - Frequently raises concerns about privacy, recording, and data usage
    - Despite conducting due diligence to ensure SOC 2, HIPAA, and GDPR compliance, adoption was initially limited
    - Adoption improved only after clear guardrails were established around how, when, and for what purpose tools would be used
    - Education and transparency were essential to building confidence and enabling responsible use
  - HHS could accelerate adoption by aligning reimbursement and program design with real-world implementation costs
    - Incentives should recognize not only clinical outcomes but also operational effort required to safely deploy AI
      - Including training, governance, and workflow integration
    - This would reduce adoption risk
  - Pilot programs, demonstration models, or value-based payment structures that explicitly accommodate AI-enabled efficiency gains would encourage experimentation while generating evidence to inform broader policy

- Conclusion
  - AI adoption in clinical care will not accelerate through technical innovation alone
  - Requires alignment across organizational governance, administrative capacity, and financial incentives
  - By focusing on incentive alignment, reimbursement clarity, and reduction of administrative friction, HHS can enable meaningful, responsible adoption
  - Goal: deliver real value to patients, clinicians, and the healthcare system as a whole
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A veteran healthcare operations consultant argues that AI adoption stalls not from technical shortcomings but from organizational friction, unclear accountability, and payment models that ignore the real costs of implementation.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Karina Lupercio, Founder & Principal Consultant, Lumenex Advisory LLC
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** 20+ years in healthcare operations, data integration, payment models, and technology implementation
- **Geographic Scope:** National (U.S. healthcare ecosystem)
- **Stake in Issue:** Advises healthcare organizations on technology implementation; directly observes barriers to AI adoption in clinical and administrative settings
structuredSections.corePosition: AI adoption in healthcare fails not because the technology doesn't work, but because organizations lack clear ownership, payment models don't recognize implementation costs, and end users see new tools as extra burden rather than help. HHS should focus on aligning incentives and reimbursement with the real-world effort required to deploy AI responsibly.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Align reimbursement and program design with real-world implementation costs
  - Recognize not just clinical outcomes but operational effort: training, governance, workflow integration
  - This would reduce adoption risk for organizations
- Create pilot programs, demonstration models, or value-based payment structures that explicitly accommodate AI-enabled efficiency gains
  - Encourage experimentation while generating evidence for broader policy
- Provide incentive structures that account for trust-building, education, and governance work
  - Current payment models don't cover these essential adoption activities
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Ownership and accountability for AI adoption are often unclear
  - AI initiatives treated as IT projects when they require cross-functional coordination
  - Diffused responsibility causes adoption to stall even when technology works
- Resistance from end users who perceive AI as additional workload
  - Long-term value not immediately visible to those doing daily work
  - Leadership fails to explain how outcomes depend on incremental workflow changes
- Payment models don't account for non-billable value AI delivers
  - Efficiency gains, reduced administrative burden, and decision support don't produce discrete billable events
  - Organizations can't justify investment without clear reimbursement pathways
- Trust barriers slow adoption even for narrow, compliant AI tools
  - Privacy and data usage concerns persist despite SOC 2, HIPAA, and GDPR compliance
  - Requires explicit guardrails and education to overcome
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Pre-AI implementation lesson: When implementing large-scale provider data management systems, downstream teams resisted because new workflows felt like "extra steps"—even though the long-term payoff was better reporting and analytics
  - Leadership wanted insights but didn't invest in explaining how daily workflow changes would produce them
  - Over time, steps became easier, but early resistance significantly delayed adoption
- AI-enabled note-taking tool deployment: Despite full compliance certifications, adoption remained limited until the organization established clear guardrails around how, when, and why the tools would be used
  - Education and transparency—not just compliance—were what built confidence
- Key insight: The most common point of failure is the handoff to end users; cultural transformation and workflow ownership must be addressed explicitly or even well-designed systems fail
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Adoption often stalls not because of model performance, but because of structural, financial, and organizational friction."
- "Without deliberate workflow design, education, and change management, AI tools risk being perceived as additive burden rather than enabling infrastructure."
- "This is not a technology failure, but an implementation and governance one."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- The Dimensions of Artificial Intelligence in the Healthcare Industry
  - HHS should be commended for seeking to leverage technology to lower healthcare costs and improve care
  - Since being diagnosed with Parkinson's disease 24 years ago, I have watched healthcare R&D proceed at a glacial pace across multiple disease fronts
  - Conversely, costs and related subsidies have skyrocketed
  - AI offers the potential to fundamentally change these trends
  - HHS should view AI's short-term effect as improving odds of success and shortening feedback cycles
  - To achieve significant breakthroughs, HHS via FDA will need to completely revamp its approach to drug discovery and development
  - Rather than being an overzealous gatekeeper at every step, FDA's role should shift to being an auditor with specifically delimited responsibilities

- Dimension #1: Incorporation of AI into Drug Discovery
  - The biggest benefit to healthcare industry performance from AI is achievable from drug discovery
  - Federal government expends $44 billion per year funding basic healthcare research
  - A small fraction of basic research initiatives pass through the "Valley of Death" and make it to clinical trial
    - Average length of this phase is approximately 15 years per endeavor
    - FDA involvement impacts the pace of progress
  - Even after overcoming this initial barrier, less than 10% of promising remedies receive final FDA approval
  - Accounting for costs of failures, average FDA drug approval costs society almost $3 billion and takes decades to reach market from lab inception
    - This system is a prescription for failure
    - Results appear every day in high costs and poor performance
  - AI identifies potential treatments much faster than traditional methods
    - Enables efficient exploration of biological variability
    - Can dramatically increase experiments by studying up to a trillion interactions between variables
    - Can process vast amounts of biological data, uncover hidden causal relationships, and generate new actionable insights
  - More than 50% of nation's healthcare costs are attributable to patients with multiple chronic illnesses
    - This figure exceeds 75% when complex diseases are included
  - AI is particularly promising for complex, multifactorial conditions
    - Neurodegenerative diseases
    - Autism spectrum disorders
    - Multiple chronic illnesses
    - Conventional reductionist approaches have failed for these conditions
  - Over 30 million people in the U.S. are affected by rare diseases
    - More than 7,000 different types exist
  - HHS should encourage AI initiatives to address these conditions
    - Potential to improve health of nation's most disadvantaged citizens
    - Potential to significantly cut costs of providing care
  - Let the scientists study science, not the FDA requirements
    - By being forced to have one eye on the finish line, scientists tend to craft their work to satisfy the FDA rather than the rudiments of a flawed biological process
  - In the short-run, HHS should direct grants toward AI-generated basic research with emphasis on hard-to-solve illnesses
  - FDA should put into place a new deregulatory system for AI-initiated programs to enable breakthrough treatments in compressed timetable

- Dimension #2: Incorporation of AI into the Drug Development Process
  - Simply relying on AI for drug discovery while subjecting advances to current approval process would undermine the technology
  - Current FDA clinical procedures entail an average of 12 years and $2 billion for each drug approval
    - Even scheduling a meeting can take six months for a "fast track" application
  - Enhancements to existing processes alone would not capture the true potential of AI
  - AI can already improve fulfilling exhaustive regulatory documentation requirements
    - Documentation adds up to as much as 30% of the cost of compliance
  - In the short-run, AI can improve drug development by:
    - Automating and validating regulatory documentation
    - Enhancing trial design and participant stratification
    - Monitoring safety and efficacy in near real-time
    - Reducing administrative and compliance costs
  - In the U.K., the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency reported clinical trial approval times were twice as fast with AI and associated reforms
  - To achieve much greater long-term gains:
    - HHS should not be involved in pre-clinical AI research
    - Should collapse all clinical work utilizing AI into one elongated trial rather than discrete Phase I, II and III trials
    - AI can be used to continually update and validate documentation
    - This change would not require statutory change or agency rulemaking because clinical trial design is not codified in FDA rules
  - As participants are added to a trial, safety results can be examined and reported in real time
  - Once trial surpasses certain number such as 1000 participants with proven efficacy and meeting specified safety protocols, it would be approved for roll-out
  - Government's role would be as auditor to validate trial output
    - Would include experimental validation, mechanistic understanding, and ethical oversight
  - Real-world data could more readily be collected after launch as another safety feature
  - FDA personnel would shift from episodic gatekeepers to continuous auditors
    - Requires fundamental change in organizational culture
  - Safety concerns would remain important but responsibility and accountability would be more equally shared with applicants and trial participants
  - Prolonged suffering of existing patients would be factored into public welfare analysis when reviewing preliminary safety results
    - 33 million people with one or more of 7000 rare diseases now have near 0% chance of relief under current regulatory procedures
    - Their plight must be considered while contours of biology are examined
  - Specific example: AI-generated treatment of Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP)
    - New treatment produced large, sustained reduction in polyps with no significant safety concerns
    - Company hopes to meet with FDA in 1H2026
    - Current FAP treatment is expensive, intrusive, and lifelong
      - Early detection strategies cost $10k+
      - Late detection costs $37k+
      - Cost to treat metastatic colorectal cancer (for which FAP predisposes) can reach $300,000
      - Constant exams and excisions are the norm
    - FDA is not inclined to ask these patients whether they would like to try the pill or whether continued excisions would be preferable
    - The answer from most patients would be obvious if given the choice

- Dimension #3: Enhance Data Collection to Empower AI
  - AI harbors the proverbial statistical stigma of "garbage in, garbage out"
  - Comprehensive and accurate data is essential to AI's success
  - This is another area where the healthcare industry has failed
  - Industry has evolved with each provider encouraging patients to sign up for customer portals
    - Providers typically treat portal information as their own for research purposes
    - However, providers do not own the data
    - Each patient owns his or her data
  - Few instances where comprehensive data has been collected
    - Example: In 2010, The Michael J. Fox Foundation launched the Parkinson's Progression Markers Initiative (PPMI)
      - Goal was to find biological markers of Parkinson's onset and progression
      - Led to impressive finding of a tool that can detect pathology in people diagnosed with Parkinson's and in individuals at high risk of developing it
      - However, study was geared only towards Parkinson's disease and has only a few thousand participants
  - To broaden scope and applicability of healthcare data, HHS should establish national standards for patient-facing data collection that:
    - Use interoperable formats
    - Capture both diagnostic outcomes and relevant explanatory variables
    - Preserve patient ownership and informed consent
    - Enable longitudinal tracking while protecting privacy and security
  - As information from participants is collected over time, researchers would use AI to identify combinations of variables pointing towards promising results
  - Once format is established, HHS should set goal of enrolling 100,000 participants within two years

- Dimension #4: Use of AI to Establish Standards of Care and Price Ceilings
  - No national standards of care for diseases or other health maladies exist in the United States
  - Patients oftentimes do not understand:
    - The nature of their affliction
    - The options to treat it
    - The costs of various options to remedy it
  - On a parallel track:
    - HHS might fund basic research targeted to a particular ailment
    - FDA might or might not approve it
    - Medicare might or might not cover it
    - Some insurance companies may cover the treatment and some may not
  - Costs of various treatments may vary greatly from facility to facility, unbeknownst to the patient
  - Healthcare practitioners have the desire and economic incentive to provide the best and likely most expensive possible service to their patients
  - As an economist would conclude, supply curves and demand curves in healthcare do not lead to a pareto optimal solution
    - There is a market failure, primarily relating to lack of actionable information
  - In the short-run, AI can help address these failures by:
    - Aggregating and analyzing how care is delivered across the country
    - Identifying patterns associated with better outcomes and lower costs
    - Informing evidence-based minimum standards of care
    - Improving transparency around pricing and performance
  - Over the longer term, outputs could be used to establish minimum standard of care for all or most ailments
    - These standards would be mandatorily covered by insurance
    - Outputs could be supplemented by regional price ceilings for various practices based on comprehensive industry analysis
  - As experience is gained, a future version could be programmed to automatically calculate prescribed minimum standards of care and price ceilings to mimic functioning of demand and supply curves
    - Algorithm could be constructed using specified level of federal subsidy as the equilibrium
    - As federal subsidy exceeds certain pre-set limits, AI would address disequilibrium by providing lawmakers options to lower price ceiling for certain conditions and/or lower minimum standard of care
  - In scenarios where stipulated federal subsidy was exceeded:
    - Some classes of patients would be denied payment for best available treatment unless they had supplemental insurance
    - Some healthcare providers would suffer diminution of profits
  - Such an approach would require Congressional approval
  - Such tradeoffs are occurring now without factual inputs or informed choices
  - AI could address the industry's massive information failure and tackle ever-increasing subsidies

- Dimension #5: Incorporation of AI into HHS's Internal Processes
  - AI can improve efficiency and effectiveness of HHS's internal operations
  - Potential percentage gains would be smaller than for drug discovery and development dimensions
  - Even modest improvements can yield meaningful savings given scale of federal healthcare spending
  - Early applications should focus on areas with clear return on investment and limited risk:
    - Fraud, waste, and abuse detection
    - Claims auditing and compliance monitoring
    - Internal workflow automation
  - HHS should deploy these tools incrementally, evaluate performance, and use lessons learned to inform broader adoption across divisions

- Dimension X: "Accelerating" the Adoption of "AI" as Part of Clinical Care
  - HHS also issued companion RFI on Dec. 23 seeking to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care
  - HHS should proceed very carefully on issues raised in this RFI
  - AI software defines clinical care as direct, hands-on medical treatment, diagnosis, and management of patients by trained healthcare professionals
  - HHS should not involve itself in anything beyond establishing standards of care for several reasons
  - First reason: In an expanded capacity, HHS is likely to slow down AI adoption
    - Example: A few years ago, HHS recognized need to reorganize Center for Evaluation and Drug Research
    - After multiple-year effort, described reorganization as: "The office is now comprised of six cross-functional support offices and eight clinical offices. The six cross-functional support offices oversee 12 divisions and several staff groups. The eight clinical offices oversee OND's 27 clinical divisions and six pharm/tox review divisions"
    - This is not the sort of thinking or organizational design to accelerate adoption of new technology
  - Second reason: We are in the first inning of AI system development
    - HHS should not be in the business of accelerating or selecting any one AI system or application
    - Should set a level playing field and let the market decide the best technologies
  - Third reason: HHS's legal authority does not extend to anything and everything related to healthcare
    - HHS derives authority from a series of laws
    - Public Health Service Act provides authority to lead responses to public health emergencies, declare emergencies, manage Strategic National Stockpile, and control diseases
    - HIPAA authorizes HHS to set standards for electronic health information exchange, privacy, and security
    - Other statutes govern specific areas such as SAMHSA for substance abuse, OHRP for human research, and OIG for oversight
  - Proactively selecting or pushing a particular technology for patient care would stretch HHS's legal authority
  - Could retard advancements as result of extended litigation
  - Multiple AI systems are "in the works"
  - HHS should not be "in the business" of picking winners and losers through a program of acceleration

- Conclusion
  - AI offers opportunity for significant improvements in healthcare outcomes and efficiencies
  - Only achievable if integrated into regulatory and governance framework designed for its capabilities
  - Shoehorning AI into existing structures will blunt its impact and increase risk of implementation
  - Each dimension described requires a separate dedicated, multidisciplinary team reporting to Office of the Deputy Secretary
  - After strategic direction for each dimension is established, teams should be tasked with:
    - Developing detailed implementation plans including budgetary requirements
    - Identifying any statutory or regulatory barriers
    - Establishing timelines, milestones, and evaluation criteria
    - Addressing ethical and equity considerations
  - Drug discovery and drug development represent highest-impact dimensions for AI implementation
  - HHS should make use of external expertise in fashioning details of appropriate regulatory framework for these dimensions
  - Detailed plans for implementing AI should be approved and finalized before end of 2026
  - HHS should take a proactive, forward-looking role in harnessing AI technology to deflate healthcare costs and make America healthy again
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A Parkinson's patient of 24 years and economist argues that FDA must transform from "overzealous gatekeeper" to auditor, collapsing clinical trials into single continuous processes to unlock AI's potential for drug discovery and slash the $3 billion average cost per drug approval.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Steven Zecola

- **Stake in Issue:** Direct personal stake as a patient with a chronic neurodegenerative disease who has watched healthcare R&D proceed at a "glacial pace" while costs have skyrocketed; advocates for patients with rare and complex diseases who have "near 0% chance of relief" under current regulatory procedures
structuredSections.corePosition: I believe AI offers the potential to fundamentally transform healthcare, but only if HHS completely revamps its regulatory approach. Rather than being an overzealous gatekeeper at every step, the FDA's role should shift to being an auditor with specifically delimited responsibilities. The current system—where drug approvals cost $3 billion and take decades—is a prescription for failure, and shoehorning AI into existing structures will blunt its impact.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Direct HHS grants toward AI-generated basic research, with particular emphasis on hard-to-solve illnesses including neurodegenerative diseases, autism spectrum disorders, and rare diseases
- Create a new deregulatory system for AI-initiated programs to enable breakthrough treatments on compressed timetables
- Remove HHS involvement from pre-clinical AI research entirely

  - AI can continually update and validate documentation
  - This change would not require statutory change or agency rulemaking
- Shift FDA personnel from episodic gatekeepers to continuous auditors
  - Once trials surpass approximately 1,000 participants with proven efficacy and meeting safety protocols, approve for roll-out
  - Government role would be to validate trial output through experimental validation, mechanistic understanding, and ethical oversight
- Establish national standards for patient-facing data collection that:
  - Use interoperable formats
  - Capture both diagnostic outcomes and relevant explanatory variables
  - Preserve patient ownership and informed consent
  - Enable longitudinal tracking while protecting privacy and security
  - Set goal of enrolling 100,000 participants within two years
- Use AI to establish evidence-based minimum standards of care and regional price ceilings
  - Standards would be mandatorily covered by insurance
  - Future AI systems could automatically calculate standards and price ceilings using federal subsidy levels as equilibrium
- Deploy AI incrementally for HHS internal operations focusing on:
  - Fraud, waste, and abuse detection
  - Claims auditing and compliance monitoring
  - Internal workflow automation
- Proceed very carefully on "accelerating" AI adoption in clinical care
  - HHS should not pick winners and losers among AI systems
  - Set a level playing field and let the market decide
  - HHS legal authority does not extend to proactively pushing particular technologies
- Create separate dedicated, multidisciplinary teams for each AI dimension reporting to Office of the Deputy Secretary
  - Develop detailed implementation plans including budgetary requirements
  - Identify statutory or regulatory barriers
  - Establish timelines, milestones, and evaluation criteria
  - Address ethical and equity considerations
- Finalize detailed AI implementation plans before end of 2026
- Use external expertise to fashion appropriate regulatory framework for drug discovery and development dimensions
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Current drug approval system is fundamentally broken
  - Average FDA drug approval costs society almost $3 billion
  - Takes decades to reach market from lab inception
  - Less than 10% of promising remedies receive final FDA approval
  - Average clinical trial phase takes 15 years
  - Even "fast track" meeting scheduling can take six months
  - Regulatory documentation adds up to 30% of compliance costs
- FDA's gatekeeper approach forces scientists to craft work to satisfy FDA rather than address flawed biological processes
- Patients with rare and complex diseases are systematically disadvantaged
  - 33 million people with 7,000+ rare diseases have near 0% chance of relief under current procedures
  - More than 50% of healthcare costs are attributable to patients with multiple chronic illnesses
  - Conventional reductionist approaches have failed for complex, multifactorial conditions
- Healthcare data collection is fragmented and inadequate
  - Providers treat patient data as their own when patients actually own it
  - Few comprehensive data collection initiatives exist
- No national standards of care exist
  - Patients don't understand their conditions, treatment options, or costs
  - Treatment costs vary greatly between facilities without patient knowledge
  - Healthcare practitioners have economic incentives to provide most expensive services
  - Market failure due to lack of actionable information
- HHS organizational culture is not suited to accelerating technology adoption
  - Recent reorganization of Center for Evaluation and Drug Research resulted in byzantine structure of divisions and offices
  - This is not the thinking or organizational design to accelerate new technology
- HHS may lack legal authority to proactively push particular technologies for patient care
  - Could result in extended litigation that retards advancements
- Prolonged suffering of existing patients is not factored into public welfare analysis when reviewing preliminary safety results
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Personal experience as a Parkinson's patient for 24 years watching healthcare R&D proceed at a "glacial pace" while costs skyrocketed
- Insight from The Michael J. Fox Foundation's Parkinson's Progression Markers Initiative (PPMI)
  - Launched in 2010 to find biological markers of Parkinson's onset and progression
  - Led to development of a tool that can detect pathology not only in diagnosed patients but also in high-risk individuals
  - However, limited to only a few thousand participants and focused solely on Parkinson's
- Case study of AI-generated treatment for Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP)
  - New treatment produced large, sustained reduction in polyps with no significant safety concerns
  - Company hopes to meet with FDA in first half of 2026
  - Current treatment is expensive, intrusive, and lifelong: early detection costs $10k+, late detection costs $37k+, metastatic colorectal cancer treatment can reach $300,000
  - FDA is not inclined to ask patients whether they would prefer to try the pill versus continued excisions
  - The answer from most patients would be obvious if given the choice
- UK comparison: Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency reported clinical trial approval times were twice as fast with AI and associated reforms
- Economist's perspective on healthcare market failure: supply and demand curves do not lead to pareto optimal solution due to lack of actionable information
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Let the scientists study science, not the FDA requirements. By being forced to have one eye on the finish line, scientists tend to craft their work to satisfy the FDA, rather than the rudiments of a flawed biological process."
- "The 33 million people with one or more of the 7000 rare diseases now have a near 0% chance of relief under current regulatory procedures, and their plight must be considered while the contours of biology are examined."
- "This system is a prescription for failure, and the results of such a scheme appear every day in the real world in the form of high costs and poor performance."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- I am a dermatologist whose research specializes in artificial intelligence applications in clinical care
- One of the challenges for introducing safe AI tools to improve healthcare is navigating the regulations surrounding it
  - Information for companies developing clinical AI products would be helpful to distinguish AI tools which do or do not require FDA approval
- In the future, autonomous medical decision making without involvement of a human clinician could be possible
  - It will be crucial to create regulatory standards for compliance of those tools
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A dermatologist specializing in AI research calls for clearer FDA guidance on clinical AI tools and proactive standards for future autonomous medical decision-making systems.

structuredSections.corePosition: As a dermatologist researching AI in clinical care, I see regulatory navigation as a key barrier to introducing safe AI tools in healthcare. Companies need clearer guidance on which AI products require FDA approval, and we need to proactively develop standards for autonomous AI systems before they become reality.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:

- Create regulatory standards for autonomous medical decision-making tools that operate without human clinician involvement

structuredSections.mainConcerns:

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Cover letter introduction
  - Submission focuses on auditability for AI-enabled and networked surgical systems
  - Need for reliable, cross-system reconstruction of procedural events
  - Supports safety, postmarket learning, and accountability as these technologies scale

- Auditability of AI-Enabled and Networked Surgical Systems
  - A foundational barrier has emerged in advanced, AI-enabled and networked surgical systems
    - These systems integrate devices, software, cloud platforms, networks, and clinician decision-making in real time
    - When adverse events, unexpected outcomes, or performance questions arise, it is often difficult to reconstruct what actually occurred
  - This is not primarily a data availability problem—it is an auditability problem
    - Relevant information is fragmented across manufacturers, hospitals, electronic health records, device logs, and cloud systems
    - No reliable way to assemble a coherent, verifiable account of events
    - This fragmentation constrains patient safety, postmarket learning, payer confidence, and public trust

- Proposed approach
  - Centralized control of all data is neither realistic nor desirable
  - HHS should elevate auditability as a system requirement for high-risk, AI-enabled care
  - Definition of auditability: authorized parties can reconstruct relevant aspects of a procedure across systems using preserved, reliable records under clear rules of access and use

- Specific recommendations for HHS
  - Coordinate across FDA, CMS, and ONC to define minimum attributes of an auditable procedural record
    - Traceability
    - Data integrity
    - Continuity across software updates
    - Authorized access for safety investigations and quality improvement
  - Align innovation funding and demonstration programs to prioritize systems that support auditability
  - Encourage interoperability standards that enable cross-system reconstruction of events
  - CMS could incorporate support for auditable records into innovation models or quality programs
    - Better align payment incentives with safer and more learnable AI deployment

- Expected benefits
  - Would reduce legal and implementation uncertainty
  - Support safer adoption of AI
  - Enable continuous learning across health systems without slowing innovation

- Closing argument
  - Fragmentation is inevitable; lack of auditability is not
  - Elevating auditability will help HHS achieve its goals of faster, safer, and more trustworthy AI adoption in clinical care
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A surgeon and former FDA official argues that HHS should make auditability a baseline requirement for AI-enabled surgical systems, enabling cross-system reconstruction of procedural events to support safety and learning without centralizing data control.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Binita S. Ashar, MD, MBA, FACS, MAMSE; Founder, Clarity Surgical Advisors LLC
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Surgeon (Fellow of American College of Surgeons), MBA, Member of American Medical & Biological Engineering (MAMSE), surgical systems advisor
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct professional interest in surgical AI systems—works at intersection of surgery, technology, and policy; advises on surgical system implementation
structuredSections.corePosition: HHS should elevate auditability as a foundational system requirement for high-risk, AI-enabled surgical care. The current fragmentation of data across manufacturers, hospitals, EHRs, and cloud systems makes it nearly impossible to reconstruct what happened when things go wrong—and this is a solvable problem that doesn't require centralizing data control.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Define minimum attributes of an auditable procedural record through FDA, CMS, and ONC coordination
  - Must include traceability, data integrity, continuity across software updates, and authorized access rules
- Align innovation funding and demonstration programs to prioritize systems supporting auditability
- Encourage interoperability standards enabling cross-system event reconstruction
- Have CMS incorporate auditable record support into innovation models or quality programs
  - Align payment incentives with safer, more learnable AI deployment
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Current AI-enabled surgical systems integrate devices, software, cloud platforms, networks, and clinician decisions in real time, but when problems arise, no one can reconstruct what happened
- Information fragmentation across manufacturers, hospitals, EHRs, device logs, and cloud systems
  - No reliable way to assemble coherent, verifiable account of events
- This fragmentation constrains patient safety, postmarket learning, payer confidence, and public trust
- Legal and implementation uncertainty without clear auditability standards
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Reframes the problem: this is not a data availability issue but an auditability issue—the data exists but cannot be assembled into a coherent account
- Offers a pragmatic middle path: centralized data control is "neither realistic nor desirable," but distributed systems can still be auditable with proper standards
- Identifies a specific gap: continuity across software updates as a key attribute that current systems lack
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "This is not primarily a data availability problem. Rather, it is an auditability problem."
- "Fragmentation is inevitable; lack of auditability is not."
- "By auditability, I mean that authorized parties can reconstruct relevant aspects of a procedure across systems using preserved, reliable records under clear rules of access and use."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Submitter Background
  - Board-certified neurosurgeon with approximately 15 years of frontline clinical experience
    - Emergency departments, operating rooms, and intensive care units
    - Sustained work in Level I trauma and referral centers
  - Holds Executive MBA with focus on healthcare operations and infrastructure resilience
  - Dual perspective integrates tactical clinical realities with strategic resource management for national health security
  - Experience includes firsthand observation of workforce strain, capacity degradation, and coordination failures under stress
  - Participated in collaborative and advisory efforts related to disaster preparedness and national critical infrastructure discussions
  - Comments submitted in personal capacity, views do not represent any employer, institution, or advisory body

- Executive Summary: The Case for "Deflationary Infrastructure"
  - U.S. Healthcare Sector operates beyond safe margins
    - AAMC projects deficit of up to 86,000 physicians by 2036
  - AI should be prioritized as "Deflationary Infrastructure"
    - A protective capability designed to preserve the system's most expensive and fragile asset: staffed clinical capacity

- Proposed Doctrinal Standard: Workforce Readiness Telemetry (WRT)
  - Proposes HHS adopt WRT as a critical interoperability standard
  - Definition: anonymized, aggregate indicators of operational strain, cognitive load, and staffing fragility
  - Intended for system-level planning and readiness assessment
  - Explicitly NOT designed for individual performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or employment decisions
  - Meant to complement existing interoperability frameworks (USCDI, TEFCA), not replace them
  - Focuses on upstream operational inputs that precede clinical events

- Question 1: Barriers to Private Sector Innovation and Adoption
  - Bottom line: Adoption is throttled by a "Readiness Bind"
    - Hospitals lack cognitive bandwidth to adopt tools that don't immediately reduce operational friction
  - The Gap
    - HHS lacks standardized metric for "Human Capacity"
    - Staffing measured in FTEs (Full-Time Equivalents), a lagging indicator
    - FTEs fail to capture burnout, moral injury, or disengagement
    - Facility may appear fully staffed on paper yet remain operationally degraded due to unmeasured cognitive load
  - The Solution
    - Prioritize AI that serves as Operational Radar
    - Tools predicting attrition 30-60 days in advance allow proactive administrator intervention
    - Reduces multi-million dollar annual turnover costs
    - WRT leverages existing metadata streams and minimal viable indicators
      - Low-burden for frontline systems
  - Strategic Alignment
    - Directly operationalizes NHSS 2023-2026 Objective 1.3 ("Strengthen the recruitment, retention, and preparedness of the HPH workforce")
    - Moves workforce retention from passive goal to active, measured capability
  - Data Support
    - Nursing turnover costs average hospital $5.7M annually
    - Loss of single high-acuity physician costs $500,000 to >$1M in replacement costs alone
      - Consistent with recent AMA and AMN Healthcare analyses
  - Revenue Impact
    - For high-revenue specialties (e.g., Neurosurgery), six-month vacancy results in >$3M in lost clinical capacity
    - AI preserving this capacity represents critical deflationary infrastructure

- Question 3: Governance and Implementation of Non-Medical Device AI
  - Bottom line: Workflow-embedded AI falls into a "Liability Gap" that chills adoption
    - Frontline clinicians currently bear disproportionate share of risk for AI-influenced workflows
  - The Gap
    - FDA regulation covers devices but not "Algorithm-Augmented Workflow"
    - Frontline clinicians shoulder ambiguity and accountability for AI-influenced workflows
