structuredSections.detailedContent:
- URAC applauds HHS' interest in studying AI use in clinical care and its impacts on patients, providers, and regulatory structures
- AI in healthcare has potential to advance patient care and reduce waste and inefficiency in previously impossible ways
- New healthcare AI tools are proliferating outside the scope of existing laws and regulations
  - Creates situations where patients and providers lack transparency and knowledge about AI tools used in their care
- Healthcare accrediting organizations can enhance transparency and provide guardrails for AI use while ensuring pathways to innovation

- Background on URAC and Accreditation
  - URAC is the independent leader in promoting healthcare quality through accreditation, measurement, and innovation
  - Non-profit organization using evidence-based measures and developing standards through inclusive stakeholder engagement
  - Portfolio spans digital health, healthcare management and operations, health plans, pharmacies, and more
  - URAC is a deemed accreditor recognized by HHS and state agencies throughout the country
  - In September 2025, URAC released the nation's first healthcare-specific AI accreditation
    - Artificial Intelligence in Health Care Accreditation (AIHC) creates framework for evaluating AI tools in healthcare settings
    - Developed through months of engagement with leading national AI experts
    - Evaluated current AI uses, assessed development and implementation, addressed patient and provider concerns
  - Engagement highlighted importance of ensuring safe and effective AI that prevents waste, fraud, and abuse
    - While ensuring AI innovations are not diminished and tools can evolve with the changing healthcare system
  - Accreditation is uniquely situated to achieve these goals
    - Federal and state agencies rely on accreditation for quality and safety while preserving adaptability

- Patient and caregiver concerns identified through collaborative discussions
  - Privacy concerns about integrity of health information and personal data
  - Transparency concerns about AI tools used without awareness or consent
  - Safety concerns about reliability of AI outputs due to bias, drift, and hallucinations
  - Private sector accreditation has historically played prominent role in ensuring safe and effective healthcare
  - HHS and other agencies use accreditation to supplement regulations with evolving quality and safety standards
  - Seeking care at accredited organizations provides peace of mind about appropriate use of confidential information

- URAC Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Accreditation details
  - Developed to address gap between real-world AI use and existing regulatory structures
  - Unlike traditional regulatory approaches, accreditation promotes best practices and provides guardrails
    - Protects patients while allowing for innovation, information sharing, and development of trustworthy AI systems
  - Offers important oversight layer through detailed review processes providing reliable and adaptable AI guardrails
  - URAC convened 29-member multidisciplinary advisory group
    - Included providers, hospitals, health plans, developers, legal experts, policy groups, and associations
    - Feedback helped shape standards reflecting real-world needs of full segment of AI users
  - AIHC Accreditation uses modular approach
    - Provides specific standards and processes for both developers and users of AI systems
    - Common standards apply across AI spectrum emphasizing risk management, operations and infrastructure, performance monitoring and improvement
    - Modular approach allows additional standards applicable to developers, users, or both
    - Adaptability makes accreditation well-suited for current AI tools and those being developed for future use

- HHS Steps to Support Private Sector Activities to Promote Innovative and Effective AI Use
  - URAC is proud of its role as HHS-deemed accreditor ensuring health plans meet highest standards
  - Private sector accreditation has historically supplemented federal and state regulations for quality and transparency

  - Recommendation 1: Implementation of AI accreditation requirements in future HHS rulemaking
    - HHS has historically incorporated private sector accreditation through formal rulemaking
    - Examples include requirements for QHP issuers (45 C.F.R. § 156.275), independent dispute resolution entities (45 C.F.R. § 149.510), home infusion therapy services (45 C.F.R. § 488.1030), Medicare Advantage health plan issuers (45 C.F.R. § 422.157)
    - States throughout the country also utilize private market accreditation
    - Accreditation can adapt and change quickly to address industry developments

  - Recommendation 2: Encourage voluntary adoption of healthcare AI accreditation among users and developers
    - Incorporate AI accreditation into agency metrics for AI development and deployment
    - Endorse accreditation in funding and grant opportunities
    - Prioritize agency adoption and endorsement of AI tools accredited by recognized private sector accreditors

  - Recommendation 3: Exercise existing communication levers through HHS webpage
    - Establish public-facing website with information on AI accreditation
    - Develop online resources for users and developers including best practices
    - Highlight protections offered by accredited AI tools
    - Provide patients and providers information about AI's role in healthcare and advantages of accreditation
    - In 2024, HHS established similar resource for telehealth accreditation containing clinical and training information, patient information, and overview of accrediting organization standards
    - Similar AI accreditation resources can increase patient and provider confidence

  - Recommendation 4: Include URAC AIHC Accreditation within shared repository of procurement best practices in OMB M-25-22
    - OMB emphasized need for cross-functional collaboration across federal agencies to avoid AI procurement obstacles and risks
    - HHS should endorse private sector accreditation as procurement best practice
    - Include AI accreditation within tools and resources contributed to OMB shared repository

  - Recommendation 5: Update procurement language to mandate or encourage AIHC Accreditation for federal contractors
    - Aligns with HHS Artificial Intelligence Strategy and M-25-21
    - Federal agencies can shape industry standards outside formal rulemaking through contracting requirements
    - OMB Memorandum M-25-21 emphasized removing barriers to innovation while accelerating responsible AI adoption
    - Private sector accreditation addresses both priorities when incorporated into procurement standards
    - Ensures HHS vendors and partners adhere to robust standards for patient safety and data protection

  - Recommendation 6: Incorporate private accreditation standards into HHS Artificial Intelligence Strategy efforts
    - HHS AI Strategy pillar: enabling care and modernizing public health delivery for better outcomes
    - HHS envisions federal grants, research and discovery efforts, and accountability role
    - Include AI accreditation as requirement for federal funding opportunities
    - Establish guidelines to maximize AI benefits while minimizing security and privacy risks

  - Recommendation 7: Include private accreditation as best practice or standard operating procedure under HHS AI Compliance Plan
    - Plan notes HHS envisions developing best practices to foster public trust and remove barriers to safe AI tools
    - Define accreditation as best practice or standard operating procedure for new regulatory structures and procurement standards
    - Endorsement satisfies HHS goals of promoting transparent and efficient AI use

  - Recommendation 8: Provide guidance to state programs on presumed high-impact AI use encouraging accreditation as review standard
    - HHS has emphasized "transparent, risk-proportionate controls"
    - Certain "high-impact" AI applications warrant additional engagement due to significant impact on health outcomes, individual rights, or public trust
    - Incorporate AI accreditation as standard for review when considering high-impact AI activities
    - Give accreditation prominent role as means of independent review as envisioned in HHS AI Strategy
    - Consider role of private sector accreditation in guidance to state Medicaid programs with high-impact AI uses

- Conclusion
  - Role of private sector accreditation as supplement to federal and state laws is well-founded in healthcare and insurance laws
  - Accreditation is particularly well-suited as evolving requirements that can adapt to technology changes in ways statutes and regulations cannot
  - Makes accreditation ideal tool for HHS to address expanding role of AI in clinical care
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Healthcare accreditation leader URAC urges HHS to leverage private sector AI accreditation programs—including their newly launched healthcare-specific AI accreditation—as adaptable guardrails that can protect patients while preserving innovation pathways.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** URAC (Shawn Griffin, M.D., President and CEO)
- **Type:** Accreditation Organization / Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** HHS-deemed healthcare accreditor; developed nation's first healthcare-specific AI accreditation (AIHC) in September 2025; convened 29-member expert advisory group on AI
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct interest in positioning private sector accreditation as the preferred mechanism for AI oversight; has existing AI accreditation product to market
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe existing proven healthcare accrediting organizations with a history of being endorsed and reviewed by HHS can play an instrumental role in ensuring the safe and effective use of AI in healthcare. Private sector accreditation is uniquely suited to provide guardrails that protect patients while allowing innovation, because accreditation can adapt to technology changes in ways that statutes and regulations cannot. HHS should incorporate AI accreditation requirements across rulemaking, procurement, grants, and guidance to state programs.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Implement AI accreditation requirements in future HHS rulemaking
  - Follow precedent from QHP issuers, independent dispute resolution entities, home infusion therapy services, and Medicare Advantage health plans
- Encourage voluntary adoption of healthcare AI accreditation
  - Incorporate into agency metrics for AI development and deployment
  - Endorse in funding and grant opportunities
  - Prioritize accredited AI tools for agency adoption
- Establish public-facing HHS website with AI accreditation information
  - Model after 2024 telehealth accreditation resource
  - Include best practices, patient information, and overview of accreditation standards
- Include AI accreditation in OMB M-25-22 shared repository of procurement best practices
- Update procurement language to mandate or encourage AIHC Accreditation for federal contractors
  - Aligns with HHS AI Strategy and OMB M-25-21
- Incorporate AI accreditation as requirement for federal funding opportunities under HHS AI Strategy
- Define accreditation as best practice or standard operating procedure under HHS AI Compliance Plan
- Provide guidance to state Medicaid programs encouraging accreditation as standard for high-impact AI review
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- New healthcare AI tools proliferating outside scope of existing laws and regulations
  - Patients and providers lack transparency about AI tools used in their care
- Patient and caregiver concerns about AI identified through stakeholder engagement:
  - Privacy: integrity of health information and personal data
  - Transparency: AI tools used without awareness or consent
  - Safety: reliability of AI outputs due to bias, drift, and hallucinations
- Traditional regulatory approaches cannot adapt quickly enough to evolving AI technology
- Gap exists between real-world AI use and existing regulatory structures intended to protect patients and providers
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- URAC launched nation's first healthcare-specific AI accreditation in September 2025
  - Developed through months of engagement with national AI experts
  - 29-member advisory group included providers, hospitals, health plans, developers, legal experts, policy groups, and associations
- Created modular accreditation approach with separate standards for AI developers versus AI users
  - Common standards across AI spectrum: risk management, operations and infrastructure, performance monitoring and improvement
  - Modular design allows standards to evolve with technology
- Points to existing regulatory precedents where HHS incorporated private sector accreditation:
  - QHP issuers (45 C.F.R. § 156.275)
  - Independent dispute resolution entities (45 C.F.R. § 149.510)
  - Home infusion therapy services (45 C.F.R. § 488.1030)
  - Medicare Advantage health plan issuers (45 C.F.R. § 422.157)
- References 2024 HHS telehealth accreditation webpage as model for AI accreditation public resources
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The advent and proliferation of new healthcare AI tools is often occurring outside the scope of existing laws and regulations, creating situations where patients and providers lack the transparency and knowledge of AI tools used in their care."
- "Accreditation is particularly well-situated as an evolving set of requirements and obligations that can adapt to changes in technology and uses in ways that statutes and regulations cannot, making it an ideal tool for HHS to consider as it seeks to address the expanding role of AI in clinical care."
- "Seeking care at an accredited organization can provide peace of mind that the organization has developed processes to promote appropriate use of confidential information and support quality patient care when using AI."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction
  - DSI is a standards development organization focused on emerging technology, interoperability, and patient-centered care in dentistry
  - Dentistry historically lacked a unified, technically focused standards authority aligned with modern health IT and interoperability priorities
  - DSI was formed to fill this gap and has made substantial progress in foundational standards for dental interoperability, patient-centered data portability, and responsible technology adoption
  - HHS has a unique opportunity to ensure dentistry is not excluded from national AI policy and infrastructure development
  - Dentistry is one of the largest, most frequently accessed clinical domains in the US, yet remains structurally disconnected from the broader healthcare data ecosystem
  - AI adoption will not succeed at scale without trust infrastructure including interoperability, standardized data capture, independent validation, and clear accountability structures

- Overarching Position
  - AI can improve health outcomes, reduce administrative burden, and expand access to care
  - Accelerated AI adoption without standards and transparency risks producing a fragmented ecosystem of opaque tools, inconsistent performance claims, and avoidable patient harm
  - Dentistry is particularly sensitive to these risks because:
    - Diagnostic coding is inconsistently used
    - Imaging workflows vary widely by practice and device
    - Clinical documentation remains largely unstructured
    - Dental and medical records remain disconnected
    - Vendor validation is often self-reported and difficult to compare across tools
  - For dentistry to contribute meaningfully to national AI objectives, HHS must prioritize policies reinforcing human oversight, independent validation, and interoperability while aligning reimbursement structures to support diagnosis-based care

- Key Recommendation 1: Clinical AI Must Be Decision-Support, Not Decision-Substitution
  - DSI strongly urges HHS to reinforce that AI used in clinical care must remain decision-support, not decision-substitution
  - Dentistry is not ready for autonomous clinical AI
  - Even where AI tools demonstrate strong performance in controlled settings, real-world deployment introduces risks:
    - Imaging variation
    - Bias
    - Drift
    - Workflow overreliance
  - The most dangerous failure mode is silent normalization, where AI output becomes treated as clinical truth without sufficient human review
  - HHS should establish clear expectations that:
    - Human review remains mandatory for AI outputs affecting diagnosis, treatment planning, or documentation
    - AI-generated recommendations must be traceable, auditable, and version-controlled
    - Accountability remains clear: clinicians and organizations remain responsible for clinical decisions
    - Post-deployment monitoring must be expected, not optional
  - Accelerating adoption without reinforcing human oversight will erode public trust and increase preventable patient harm

- Key Recommendation 2: Dentistry's Treatment-Based Structure Limits AI—Diagnosis Must Become Standard and Reimbursable
  - Dentistry has historically been treatment-driven rather than diagnosis-driven
  - Unlike medicine, dentistry has not consistently implemented diagnostic coding in routine practice
  - The reimbursement ecosystem has not strongly incentivized diagnosis capture
  - This structural issue is a major barrier to safe, scalable AI adoption
  - AI systems depend on structured diagnostic labeling to train, validate, and improve
  - When a clinical domain operates primarily through procedure documentation, models risk being optimized for predicting treatment patterns rather than reflecting clinical truth and patient outcomes
  - Lack of standardized diagnosis capture limits dentistry's ability to participate in:
    - Integrated oral-systemic care
    - Longitudinal chronic disease management
    - Value-based care models
    - Research and real-world evidence generation
    - Cross-disciplinary AI decision support
  - DSI recommends HHS and CMS prioritize policies that accelerate diagnosis-based dentistry by supporting:
    - Routine diagnostic coding in dental workflows
    - Adoption of standardized diagnostic terminologies
    - Reimbursement models that recognize diagnosis as a reimbursable clinical act
  - If dentistry is expected to contribute to national AI-enabled clinical care goals, it must be structurally enabled to document diagnosis in a consistent, interoperable manner

- Key Recommendation 3: Independent External Validation Must Become a Norm for Dental AI Tools
  - A major barrier to trust and adoption of dental AI tools is the absence of consistent independent validation
  - Most dental AI products today rely on:
    - Proprietary datasets
    - Internal testing protocols
    - Limited transparency into training populations
    - Non-standard reporting of sensitivity/specificity
    - Inconsistent disclosure of subgroup performance
    - Unclear post-deployment drift monitoring
  - Self-validation is not validation
  - HHS should prioritize efforts supporting creation and scaling of independent benchmarking and validation frameworks for AI tools, including:
    - Third-party audit mechanisms
    - Standardized evaluation metrics and reporting requirements
    - Independent testing datasets representing diverse populations and real-world imaging variability
    - Mandatory subgroup performance reporting
    - Post-deployment monitoring expectations and drift detection requirements
  - This aligns directly with HHS's emphasis on evaluation methods, robustness testing, and governance structures that promote trust

- Key Recommendation 4: Interoperability is the Primary Bottleneck to High-Value AI in Dentistry
  - HHS asks where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities and accelerate AI development—dentistry is a clear answer
  - The most valuable AI use cases in dentistry depend on longitudinal data exchange between dental and medical systems
  - Dental data remains highly fragmented across:
    - Practice management systems
    - Imaging systems
    - Payer workflows
    - Unstructured clinical notes
  - Without interoperability, AI will remain limited to isolated point solutions rather than contributing to true healthcare transformation
  - DSI recommends HHS prioritize interoperability policies that explicitly include dentistry, supporting:
    - Structured oral health data exchange standards
    - Dental imaging interoperability and metadata normalization
    - Consistent capture and exchange of diagnostic findings
    - Alignment between dental standards and broader medical exchange frameworks including FHIR-based infrastructure
    - Patient-centered portability of dental records
  - DSI has already advanced work in dental interoperability and patient-centered data portability

- Key Recommendation 5: HHS Should Support a National Dental AI Standards and Validation Partnership
  - Dentistry historically lacked unified infrastructure for AI governance, interoperability alignment, and standardized evaluation
  - DSI was formed to address this gap and has begun advancing foundational work for responsible AI adoption
  - HHS/ONC should support and accelerate development of a national dental AI trust framework including:
    - Standardized validation datasets across multiple imaging devices and clinical settings
    - Third-party testing and certification mechanisms
    - Minimum transparency requirements for dental AI vendors
    - Standardized performance reporting formats
    - Audit log requirements for AI recommendations in clinical workflows
    - Guidance for post-deployment monitoring and model drift surveillance
  - This framework should support private sector innovation while ensuring consistent trust, accountability, and patient safety
  - DSI stands ready to convene clinicians, DSOs, vendors, payers, and patient advocates to support HHS in defining and operationalizing this infrastructure

- Key Recommendation 6: Standardized Transparency Disclosures ("AI Nutrition Labels") Should Be Encouraged
  - A practical and high-impact policy step would be to encourage standardized disclosures for AI tools used in clinical care
  - DSI recommends HHS promote a consistent "AI nutrition label" disclosure format including:
    - Intended use and clinical scope
    - Dataset provenance and size
    - Demographic distribution of training/testing sets
    - Performance metrics by subgroup
    - Known failure conditions and limitations
    - Model update frequency and version control approach
    - Drift monitoring approach
    - Documentation requirements for human review
  - Such disclosures would reduce uncertainty for purchasers, support responsible adoption, and increase public trust

- Key Recommendation 7: Dentistry is a High-Leverage Domain for Prevention and Oral-Systemic Health Integration
  - Dentistry represents a major opportunity to strengthen national prevention strategy
  - Oral inflammation and dental disease correlate strongly with broader chronic disease risk
  - Dental visits are often more frequent than physician visits for many Americans
  - Dentistry is a critical clinical setting for early risk detection and prevention workflows
  - If AI is responsibly implemented with interoperability, diagnostic modernization, and independent validation, dentistry can serve as a high-leverage entry point for improving systemic health outcomes while reducing downstream healthcare costs

- Responses Aligned to HHS Specific Questions
  - Question 1: Biggest barriers to innovation and adoption
    - Limited interoperability of dental data
    - Lack of consistent diagnostic coding adoption
    - Inconsistent imaging workflows and device variability
    - Opaque vendor validation and lack of independent benchmarking
    - Unclear accountability and auditability expectations
  - Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, and programmatic changes
    - Accelerate diagnostic coding adoption in dentistry
    - Modernize reimbursement incentives to support diagnosis capture
    - Expand interoperability priorities to include dentistry explicitly
    - Establish clear expectations for auditability and human oversight
  - Question 3: Governance and accountability issues
    - Clinicians remain accountable for clinical decisions
    - AI recommendations must be logged and auditable
    - Liability and indemnification expectations must remain clear
  - Question 4: Evaluation methods and robustness testing
    - Independent benchmarking datasets
    - Standardized subgroup reporting
    - Real-world multi-site testing
    - Post-deployment performance monitoring and drift detection
  - Question 5: Accreditation, certification, and testing
    - Support third-party testing ecosystems
    - Encourage industry-wide certification models
    - Tie incentives to independently validated performance, not marketing claims
  - Question 8: Interoperability opportunities
    - Structured oral health data exchange
    - Dental imaging interoperability and metadata standards
    - Diagnostic representation and portability
    - Alignment with broader medical interoperability frameworks

- Conclusion
  - HHS has a timely opportunity to accelerate AI adoption in a way that improves outcomes, reduces burden, and strengthens public trust
  - Dentistry must be explicitly included in this effort
  - AI adoption will not scale safely without trust infrastructure: interoperability standards, diagnostic modernization, independent validation, and clear accountability
  - DSI urges HHS to prioritize:
    - Mandatory human oversight and accountability
    - Modernization of diagnosis capture and reimbursement alignment in dentistry
    - Independent external validation frameworks for dental AI tools
    - Dental interoperability as a prerequisite for scalable clinical AI
    - Standardized transparency disclosures for AI tools
    - Real-world monitoring expectations for deployed AI systems
  - DSI welcomes the opportunity to collaborate with HHS/ONC, CMS, NIH, and FDA to accelerate responsible AI adoption in dentistry
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A dental standards organization argues that dentistry—one of America's most-accessed clinical domains—is dangerously siloed from healthcare AI infrastructure and needs explicit inclusion through diagnostic coding modernization, independent validation frameworks, and interoperability mandates before AI can safely scale.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Dental Standards Institute (DSI)
- **Type:** Trade Association / Standards Development Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Standards development organization focused on emerging technology, interoperability, and patient-centered care in dentistry; claims to have advanced foundational work in dental interoperability and patient-centered data portability
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Positioned as the unified standards authority for dental technology adoption; directly affected by how HHS structures AI governance, validation requirements, and interoperability mandates for dentistry
structuredSections.corePosition: AI can improve health outcomes and reduce burden, but accelerated adoption without standards and transparency risks producing a fragmented ecosystem of opaque tools, inconsistent performance claims, and avoidable patient harm. Dentistry is particularly vulnerable because it lacks consistent diagnostic coding, remains disconnected from medical records, and relies on vendor self-validation. HHS must explicitly include dentistry in national AI policy by prioritizing human oversight, independent validation, interoperability, and reimbursement structures that support diagnosis-based care.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Reinforce that clinical AI must remain decision-support, not decision-substitution
  - Mandate human review for AI outputs affecting diagnosis, treatment planning, or documentation
  - Require AI recommendations to be traceable, auditable, and version-controlled
  - Maintain clear clinician accountability for clinical decisions
  - Make post-deployment monitoring expected, not optional

- Modernize dentistry's diagnostic infrastructure
  - Support routine diagnostic coding in dental workflows
  - Promote adoption of standardized diagnostic terminologies
  - Create reimbursement models that recognize diagnosis as a reimbursable clinical act
  - Enable dentistry to participate in oral-systemic care, value-based models, and AI training

- Establish independent external validation as the norm for dental AI tools
  - Create third-party audit mechanisms
  - Develop standardized evaluation metrics and reporting requirements
  - Build independent testing datasets representing diverse populations and real-world imaging variability
  - Require mandatory subgroup performance reporting
  - Set post-deployment monitoring expectations and drift detection requirements

- Explicitly include dentistry in national interoperability initiatives
  - Support structured oral health data exchange standards
  - Enable dental imaging interoperability and metadata normalization
  - Align dental standards with broader medical exchange frameworks including FHIR
  - Ensure patient-centered portability of dental records

- Support a national dental AI standards and validation partnership
  - Create standardized validation datasets across multiple imaging devices and clinical settings
  - Establish third-party testing and certification mechanisms
  - Set minimum transparency requirements for dental AI vendors
  - Require audit logs for AI recommendations in clinical workflows
  - Provide guidance for post-deployment monitoring and model drift surveillance

- Promote standardized "AI nutrition label" disclosures including:
  - Intended use and clinical scope
  - Dataset provenance, size, and demographic distribution
  - Performance metrics by subgroup
  - Known failure conditions and limitations
  - Model update frequency and drift monitoring approach
  - Human review documentation requirements
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Dentistry is structurally excluded from national health IT and AI policy despite being one of the largest clinical domains
  - Dental and medical records remain disconnected
  - Dental data is fragmented across proprietary systems

- Dentistry's treatment-driven structure creates fundamental barriers to AI
  - Diagnostic coding is inconsistently used
  - Reimbursement doesn't incentivize diagnosis capture
  - AI models risk optimizing for treatment patterns rather than clinical truth

- Current dental AI validation is inadequate
  - Most tools rely on proprietary datasets and internal testing
  - Limited transparency into training populations
  - Non-standard reporting of sensitivity/specificity
  - Inconsistent disclosure of subgroup performance
  - Unclear post-deployment drift monitoring
  - Self-validation is not validation

- Risk of "silent normalization" where AI output becomes treated as clinical truth without sufficient human review
  - Real-world deployment introduces imaging variation, bias, drift, and workflow overreliance
  - This is the most dangerous failure mode

- Without interoperability, AI will remain limited to isolated point solutions
  - High-value AI use cases depend on longitudinal dental-medical data exchange
  - Current fragmentation prevents true healthcare transformation
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Dentistry as an overlooked AI opportunity: Dental visits are often more frequent than physician visits for many Americans, making dentistry a critical but underutilized setting for early risk detection and prevention—a "high-leverage entry point" for improving systemic health outcomes
- The treatment-vs-diagnosis structural problem: Unlike medicine, dentistry operates primarily through procedure documentation rather than diagnostic coding, which means AI models trained on dental data risk being optimized for predicting treatment patterns rather than reflecting clinical truth and patient outcomes
- The "silent normalization" failure mode: DSI identifies a specific dangerous pattern where AI output gradually becomes treated as clinical truth without human review—not through dramatic failures but through quiet workflow drift
- Oral-systemic connection: Oral inflammation and dental disease correlate strongly with broader chronic disease risk, yet the data infrastructure doesn't exist to leverage this connection for AI-enabled preventive care
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Self-validation is not validation."
- "The most dangerous failure mode is silent normalization, where AI output becomes treated as clinical truth without sufficient human review."
- "Dentistry is one of the largest, most frequently accessed clinical domains in the United States, yet it remains structurally disconnected from the broader healthcare data ecosystem."
- "When a clinical domain operates primarily through procedure documentation, models risk being optimized for predicting treatment patterns rather than reflecting clinical truth and patient outcomes."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- HHS, CMS, ASTP/ONC, and NLM should examine how FHIR APIs are evolving with AI
  - Specifically to align with graph query standards like ISO 39075 for graph query language
- This alignment would enable relationship-aware interoperability
  - Would allow returning context graphs while preserving REST-based interoperability
- GQL on FHIR combined with AI would support reasoning over care
  - Would enable relationship-aware clinical reasoning
- References provided:
  - ISO 39075 standard documentation
  - JTC1 article on Database Language GQL
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A technically-minded individual recommends aligning FHIR API evolution with graph query standards like ISO 39075 to enable AI-powered, relationship-aware clinical reasoning.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** David Rocha
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Appears knowledgeable about health IT standards, graph databases, and AI applications in healthcare
- **Geographic Scope:** Not specified
- **Stake in Issue:** Interest in advancing health data interoperability standards
structuredSections.corePosition: FHIR APIs should evolve to incorporate graph query language standards (ISO 39075) alongside AI capabilities. This would enable more sophisticated, relationship-aware interoperability while maintaining existing REST-based approaches.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Align FHIR API evolution with ISO 39075 graph query language standard
  - Enables relationship-aware interoperability
  - Allows returning context graphs
  - Preserves existing REST-based interoperability
- Implement GQL on FHIR with AI capabilities
  - Supports reasoning over care
  - Enables relationship-aware clinical reasoning
structuredSections.mainConcerns: No specific concerns raised
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Proposes a specific technical pathway (GQL + FHIR + AI) for advancing clinical reasoning capabilities
- Identifies graph query standards as a bridge between AI capabilities and existing interoperability infrastructure
structuredSections.keyQuotations: - "GQL on FHIR with AI will be useful to reason over care and relationship aware clinical reasoning."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- This submission supplements my prior comment (Submission ID: 0010)
- Supplemental notice provides empirical context supporting the deterministic semantic substrate framework proposed in original submission
- Accompanies baseline research report from Trust Publishing Institute (TPI) titled "The Medicare Digital Visibility & Compliance Shift: 2026–2028"

- Report documents publicly observable structural shifts in AI-driven retrieval systems
  - Includes Google AI Overviews, large language models, AI-enabled browsers
  - Focuses on how these systems interpret and represent Medicare plan information
  - Findings reflect measurable instability when authoritative, machine-readable semantic anchors are absent

- Purpose is not to introduce new regulatory recommendations
  - Provides empirical observations reinforcing architectural problem from original comment
  - Non-device AI systems in regulated benefit environments prone to interpretive variance
  - Variance occurs when policy meaning not embedded in structured, versioned, retrievable formats

- Key observations documented in report
  - Increasing reliance of AI systems on machine-readable plan identity (Plan-ID entity resolution)
  - Measurable interpretive variance in cost-sharing, service areas, benefit categorization
  - Reduced retrieval instability when structured, fragment-level plan representations published
  - Reinforcement dynamics in AI-driven discovery environments favoring structured sources

- Findings align with deterministic semantic substrate model from Submission ID: 0010
  - Proposes shifting governance emphasis from model inspection to content-layer determinism
  - Achieved through structured semantic anchoring

- Trust Publishing Institute (TPI) background
  - Time-bound nonprofit research entity
  - Studies structural conditions affecting AI-mediated information stability in regulated healthcare
  - Does not seek designation as standards authority
  - Role is documenting observable system behavior and evaluating architectural characteristics correlated with improved retrieval stability

- Report reflects publicly observable system behavior
  - Does not rely on proprietary datasets or non-public regulatory materials

- TPI Baseline Research Report: The Medicare Digital Visibility & Compliance Shift: 2026–2028
  - Federal Record Preface
    - Submitted as baseline observational research under TPI
    - Documents measurable structural shifts in Medicare plan discovery across AI-driven retrieval systems
    - Intended to provide empirical context for deterministic semantic substrate framework
    - Does not propose regulatory mandates
    - Documents structural conditions observed 2024–2026
    - Identifies architectural characteristics correlated with improved retrieval stability

- Executive Summary
  - Medicare ecosystem entering most consequential visibility shift in its history
  - Three converging events between 2026–2028
    - Rise of AI Overviews (AIO) in Google search
    - Takeover of plan discovery by AI agents (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Copilot)
    - Emergence of Plan-ID as dominant Medicare search surface
  - Shift destabilizing Medicare visibility and increasing compliance exposure
    - AI systems misinterpret or misstate plan benefits
    - Carriers may be held accountable for AI-generated errors
  - Transformation from web-based marketing challenge to machine-driven retrieval challenge
  - Traditional SEO, brand marketing, paid acquisition no longer reliable predictors

- The Structural Shift
  - Medicare digital visibility fragmented across three fronts over last 18 months
  - Plan-ID queries overtook brand queries
    - Beneficiaries no longer search branded plan names
    - Search for H-codes (actual plan IDs)
    - Decoupled plan discovery from carrier brand identity
    - Carriers don't own majority of Plan-ID surfaces—aggregators do
    - Even legacy aggregators losing ground
  - Google AI Overviews intercept coverage, cost, drug-tier searches
    - Most common pre-enrollment questions answered inside AIO
    - Bypasses carrier websites entirely
  - AI agents becoming new Medicare comparison layer
    - Used by consumers and agents for plan comparisons, benefit breakdowns, county availability, network differences, drug coverage lookups
    - Systems summarize, infer, recommend—often incorrectly—without sending users to beneficiary-focused websites
  - Result
    - Carriers and agencies losing visibility where Medicare journey begins
    - Legacy aggregators losing structural advantage
    - Affiliate publishers nearly invisible on transactional queries
    - AI models becoming new Medicare front door

- The Impact
  - Declining organic visibility for carriers, agencies, aggregators, affiliate publishers
    - Double-digit decreases in benefit and coverage page visibility due to AIO interception
    - Affiliate publishers visibility on transactional queries all but vanished
  - Plan-ID displacement by aggregators
    - 70–90% of Plan-ID queries surface aggregator pages, not carrier
    - Legacy aggregators vulnerable to those deploying structured publishing methods
  - Increased growth volatility
    - When AI agents make enrollment recommendations, carriers with weak structured signals disappear
    - Aggregator pages appear in their place
  - Escalating compliance exposure
    - AI systems increasingly interpret and summarize Medicare benefits
    - Misstatements in copays, premiums, service areas, supplemental benefits becoming measurable compliance risk
    - Carriers accountable for inaccuracies even when model generated error
    - Without structured, validated plan data, AI systems infer or hallucinate missing details
    - Creates downstream regulatory exposure as CMS scrutiny intensifies
  - Rising compliance and regulatory exposure
    - AI hallucinations frequently misstate copays, premiums, coverage rules
  - Channel leakage across FMOs and agencies
    - Agents rely on AI assistants for quick plan comparisons
    - Often omit or misrepresent key benefits
  - Erosion of Medicare brand identity
    - If AI systems don't "see" the brand, neither will beneficiaries
    - Brands at risk of allowing other entities to define them

- The Opportunity
  - Same disruption creating once-in-a-decade opportunity
  - AI systems prefer content that is
    - Structured
    - Canonical
    - Machine-readable
    - Plan-ID anchored
    - Glossary-aligned
    - Trust-verified
    - Fragment-level
  - Organizations adapting digital architecture will take visibility from competitors at unprecedented scale
  - Not an SEO strategy—a structural strategy
  - Those publishing machine-aligned plan content and governing through AI-safe processes gain durable visibility advantage compounding through 2028
  - Visibility shift most directly impacts carriers but cannot be solved by carriers alone
    - Each carrier can publish own structured plan surfaces
    - No carrier can create cross-carrier consistency AI systems require
  - CMS validates benefit data but mandate is regulatory—not interpretive, explanatory, or machine-readable
  - Government cannot and should not serve as industry's structured knowledge provider
  - Long-term stability depends on independent, neutral Medicare publisher
    - Capable of aggregating structured plan identity, benefit definitions, terminology, county context
    - Across entire ecosystem in format AI systems can trust and reuse

- Case Study: Structured Publishing and Retrieval Stability
  - During Medicare digital instability from Helpful Content updates and AI Overview deployment
  - One Medicare publishing property implemented structured publishing architecture
    - Centered on machine-readable Plan-ID identity
    - Benefit-level fragments
    - Glossary alignment
    - Fragment-based retrieval
  - Reframed declining visibility as structural publishing problem rather than algorithmic anomaly
  - Architecture prioritized
    - Deterministic plan identity
    - Discrete benefit fragments
    - Year-specific binding
    - Terminology coherence designed for AI retrieval rather than traditional page ranking
  - Observed outcome: measurable correlation between structured publishing and retrieval stability during AI Overview deployment
  - Visibility patterns improved with introduction of machine-readable plan surfaces and glossary-consistent benefit explanations
  - Significance is architectural rather than competitive
    - Structured plan identity and fragment-level publishing mitigate instability from AI-mediated discovery layers

- The Forecast (2026–2028)
  - Plan-ID will become primary Medicare entity signal
    - Organizations not publishing structured Plan-ID content risk losing visibility for most transaction-specific queries
    - Carriers risk allowing third parties to define their plan's coverage, benefits, cost structure
  - AIO will absorb 40–60% of coverage and benefit queries
    - Traditional benefit pages become "downstream references" instead of entry points
    - Carriers and aggregators must still publish detailed, structured benefit content
    - AIO can only cite what exists
    - If carrier doesn't define own coverage, someone else will
  - AI agents will become new Medicare comparison layer
    - By 2027, at least 30% of Medicare plan comparisons will originate inside AI assistants
    - To influence comparisons, carriers must provide structured, plan-level data models can retrieve and reuse
    - If not published in machine-readable form, AI agents rely on third-party sources or infer differences—often incorrectly
  - Aggregator dominance will erode—but only for those that don't act quickly
    - AI retrieval prioritizes structured plan data over unstructured content—only if structured data exists
    - Aggregators that delay risk allowing competitors to permanently own their Plan-ID footprint
    - Visibility consolidates early and compounds quickly
    - Aggregators that modernize first control Medicare digital landscape
    - Those that wait displaced by whoever publishes structured plan information
  - Compliance risk will increase
    - AI hallucinations about Medicare benefits will intensify CMS scrutiny
    - CMS oversight already stringent; generative models make it significantly worse
    - Google will become first-layer compliance gatekeeper
    - Will suppress or zero out Plan-ID references in AI Overviews when benefit accuracy uncertain
    - Carriers not publishing validated, structured benefit data risk exclusion from AI-driven visibility for compliance reasons
  - Visibility gaps will compound
    - Carriers, agencies, aggregators not implementing machine-readable structures in 2026 see visibility decline at accelerating rate through 2028
    - AI models reinforce sources they can parse, trust, reuse
    - Early adopters gain compounding visibility; late adopters fall further behind
    - Gap already visible: organizations that rebuilt content for AI retrieval experienced recovery and growth during AIO rollout while traditional sites continued to decay

- The Path Forward
  - Medicare visibility shift cannot be solved by marketing adjustments, SEO improvements, expanded content
  - Structural problem from inconsistent plan identity, fragmented benefit representations, varying glossary terminology, absence of unified machine-readable Medicare truth layer
  - Path forward is architectural
  - Medicare information must be structured, standardized, validated for AI systems to reliably interpret and reuse
  - Medicare Digital Architecture (MDA) provides foundation
    - Plan-level identity
    - Benefit-level fragments
    - Glossary coherence
    - County-level context
    - Continuous validation
  - Structure alone not enough
    - Each carrier publishes information differently
    - No single carrier can create cross-carrier consistency required for AI alignment
    - CMS cannot fill this role—mandate is regulatory, not interpretive or machine-readable
  - Long-term stability may require emergence of neutral, standards-aligned information layer
    - Capable of producing consistent, machine-readable representations
    - Medicare plan identity, benefit definitions, terminology, geographic context across carriers and markets
  - Report does not designate specific entity to fulfill that role
  - Identifies structural need for such layer within Medicare information ecosystem as AI-mediated retrieval becomes dominant
  - Organizations adopting MDA principles gain structural visibility advantages compounding over time
  - Those who delay displaced not by competitors but by AI systems increasingly mediating plan discovery, comparison, explanation
  - Structured truth becomes new gateway to Medicare visibility, accuracy, compliance, beneficiary trust

- Executive Takeaways
  - Medicare digital visibility shifted from SEO to AI retrieval
    - Discovery begins in Google AI Overviews, GPT, Gemini, Perplexity—not traditional search results
    - AI systems interpret Medicare through structured data rather than narrative plan pages
  - Plan-ID is anchor of new Medicare entity graph
    - AI systems use Plan-IDs (H-codes) as primary key for plan interpretation
    - Organizations not publishing structured Plan-ID surfaces lose visibility to entities that do
  - CMS is authoritative but search-invisible—therefore AI-invisible
    - CMS plan data delivered through late-rendered interfaces AI systems cannot parse
    - AI relies on external publishers for plan explanations
  - Medicare organizations must publish machine-readable plan and location content
    - AI requires structured representations including benefits, cost sharing, metadata, county bindings, year-specific distinctions
  - Glossary alignment now a compliance function
    - AI hallucinations often stem from inconsistent Medicare terminology
    - Publishers must standardize and modernize glossary terms for AI interpretation
  - Contextual Medicare content is a trust surface
    - "Does Medicare cover…?" content not marketing—contextual layer AI relies on to interpret benefit categories and supplemental benefits
  - Structured publishing must serve both humans and AI
    - Explanations must be readable for people and coherent for models
    - Aligned with structured surfaces, year logic, glossary terms
  - AI validation will become core Medicare function
    - Organizations must monitor how plans and benefit explanations represented
    - Detect drift, validate accuracy
    - Next compliance frontier
  - Early adopters of structured publishing gain durable advantage
    - AI systems learn from structured sources over time
    - By 2028, visibility advantages self-reinforcing and difficult for late adopters to reverse
  - 2026–2028 Medicare Digital Power Map already forming
    - Authority shifts toward publishers providing consistent structured plan identity and machine-readable content
    - Organizations with AI-aligned workflows and validation layers
    - AI systems that retrieve validated truth
    - Organizations relying on narrative pages, traditional SEO, unstructured metadata see declining visibility and increased compliance exposure

- Prologue: The Future of Medicare Digital Has Already Arrived
  - Medicare ecosystem undergoing fastest, most consequential shift in digital history
  - Discovery, understanding, comparison, navigation moved from traditional web experiences into AI-driven retrieval systems
  - Transformation not announced, no policy guidance, didn't arrive through CMS channels
  - Emerged quietly then all at once as AI Overviews, LLMs, agentic systems became first point of Medicare plan interpretation
  - Most industry responded through familiar lenses of marketing and SEO
  - Decline in visibility not marketing failure—structural failure
  - Data architecture failure none of existing systems designed to absorb
  - HealthNetwork Group supported full architectural reimagining of Medicare.org
    - Not as content site but structured Medicare knowledge surface designed for AI retrieval
  - Medicare.org rebuild became first validated demonstration of visibility recovery in AI era
    - Showed structured publishing can reverse downward trend impacting broader ecosystem
  - Rebuild demonstrated structured publishing can stabilize visibility
  - Also revealed deeper industry reality: no single carrier can solve this problem alone
    - AI systems require consistent, cross-carrier representations of plan identity, benefit definitions, geography, terminology
  - CMS does not provide this interpretive layer; carriers cannot standardize it individually
  - Ecosystem will ultimately depend on neutral Medicare publisher providing structured, machine-readable truth AI systems increasingly rely on
  - Report reflects perspective rooted in system design, not content strategy
    - What do AI systems need to understand Medicare plans accurately?
    - What must marketers publish consistently and structurally to restore authority and prevent misrepresentation?
  - Analysis based on direct observation of agentic behavior across major AI systems
  - First real-world evidence that structured publishing can reverse visibility collapse even during AI Overview deployment
  - Forward-looking map for Medicare digital ecosystem 2026–2028
    - How AI reshapes plan discovery
    - How structure replaces SEO
    - How Plan-ID becomes anchor of Medicare identity
    - How CMS's delivery model leaves vacuum AI cannot fill
    - How marketers must publish, govern, validate plan information in AI-dominant environment
  - Not simply report about what has changed—guide to what must come next
  - Medicare information must serve both humans and machines
  - New authority is claimed
  - Structured truth becomes foundation for compliance, accuracy, visibility, beneficiary trust
  - Medicare industry did not ask for AI-first environment but now operates in one
  - Report is beginning of work required to operate effectively and responsibly in that environment

- Part I: The Visibility Transition (2024–2025)
  - How Medicare discovery shifted away from carriers and why most teams didn't see it coming
  - Medicare visibility transition began with silent, structural shift in how beneficiaries discover and interpret Medicare information
  - Between 2024 and 2026, three forces converged
    - Plan-ID takeover of Medicare search behavior
    - AI Overviews replacing benefit-page traffic
    - AI agents replacing aggregator comparison flows
  - Changes undermined core assumptions of Medicare digital marketing
  - Carrier websites built for human navigation not prepared for machine interpretation
  - Agency sites once search dominant eclipsed by LLM-driven retrieval
  - Most teams attributed declining visibility to familiar marketing problems
    - Keyword loss, page rank volatility, algorithm updates
    - Without recognizing fundamental shift in how AI systems retrieve, interpret, recombine Medicare information
  - Misunderstanding widened gap between narrative content and machine-driven discovery
  - Set stage for first successful demonstration that structured publishing could reverse visibility decline during AIO rollout

- Chapter 1: The Great Medicare Visibility Shift
  - How Plan-ID queries overtook branded search
  - Between 2023 and 2025, profound shift in Medicare search behavior
  - Beneficiaries moved away from branded plan queries toward pure Plan-ID queries (H-codes)
  - Shift driven by
    - Rise of Google AI Overviews giving consumers exact plan identifiers
    - Increasing visibility of plan IDs inside Medicare.gov and aggregator pages
    - Proliferation of plan review sites optimized for plan IDs
    - Simplicity and precision of searching by H-code instead of marketing names
  - Plan-ID queries now primary Medicare discovery surface
  - Moment industry failed to recognize
  - Most organizations continued optimizing for branded search behavior as user intent shifted toward plan identifiers
  - Left structural gap traditional SEO and marketing strategies could not bridge
  - Plan-ID queries decouple plan discovery from brand identity
  - Carriers no longer default answer for their own plans
  - In typical 2024–2025 SERPs
    - Plan-ID queries return aggregator sites first
    - Independent publishers second
    - Government sources third
    - Carriers often nowhere
  - Beginning of structural Medicare visibility transition

- Chapter 2: The Zero-Click Medicare Funnel
  - How AIO and AI agents intercepted pre-sales queries
  - For more than decade, Medicare decision path was predictable
    - Google → Carrier website → Plan comparison → Agent → Enrollment
  - Model no longer reflects how beneficiaries behave
  - New funnel emerged in 2025
    - AIO → ChatGPT/Perplexity/Gemini → Plan-ID → Agent → Enrollment
  - Coverage questions begin in Google's AI Overviews
  - Comparisons happen inside AI assistants
  - Plan-IDs anchor final selection step
  - Carrier website often bypassed entirely
  - AIO intercepts coverage queries
    - "Does Medicare cover Ozempic?"
    - "Medicare diabetes supplies"
    - "Medicare PPO dental 2025"
    - 99% never reach carrier website
  - AI agents intercept comparison queries
    - "What's the best PPO in Pinellas County?"
    - "Compare Aetna vs Humana in Miami"
    - "Which plan is best for diabetes in 2025?"
    - Answers delivered instantly without click
  - Plan-ID becomes bridge
    - AI assistants often provide plan identifier rather than plan name
    - Pushes users directly into H-code search—which carriers do not control
  - Medicare funnel no longer begins with carrier or Medicare marketer pages
  - Begins with machine-curated answers
  - Most organizations interpreted decline through traditional SEO or traffic metrics
  - Without recognizing AI systems assumed pre-sales role once held by carrier pages, agency sites, aggregator funnels
  - Misalignment left structural gap between user intent and legacy digital strategies
  - Shift responsible for large share of 2024–2025 traffic decline across carriers and agencies to aggregators

- Chapter 3: Google AI Overviews vs. Carrier Benefit Pages
  - Why coverage queries no longer send traffic
  - Benefit pages once ranked for high-intent queries
    - "Medicare PPO dental"
    - "Medicare Advantage hearing benefits"
    - "Medicare insulin coverage"
    - "MAPD copays 2025"
  - AIO changed this overnight
  - Google now answers these questions directly
  - No click required, no visit generated
  - Carriers and Medicare marketers have seen
    - 20–40% visibility loss on benefit pages
    - 30–60% drop in impressions for coverage keywords
    - Increased misalignment between organic impressions and enrollments
    - Lower funnel conversion breakdown due to "answer in overview" situations
  - Most organizations interpreted trend as algorithmic issue or competitive SEO loss
  - Not realizing AIO's behavior reflects deeper structural shift
  - AI systems treat Medicare benefit explanations as standardized, high-confidence answer surfaces
  - Without structured, machine-readable benefit definitions, traditional benefit pages deprioritized in favor of AI-generated summaries
  - Why this hits Medicare harder than other verticals
    - Strictly defined benefit structures—coverage answers from AIO "good enough" for shoppers
    - Similar-sounding benefits across carriers—AI sees less differentiation, compresses visibility
    - Regulated terminology—AI sees repetitive patterns, creates generalized answers
    - High-query volume on coverage topics—Medicare uniquely question-heavy vertical
  - Benefit pages no longer primary entry points
  - Function as structured backup sources and trust signals
  - Even when no longer receiving clicks, keeping pages accurate, consistent, aligned with structured benefit definitions remains critical
  - AI systems still use them to assess organization's depth, expertise, coverage logic
  - Abandoning or letting them decay removes one of few signals AI has to understand what publisher truly knows about Medicare
  - Shift redefines purpose of benefit explainers
  - Traditional explainer pages designed to rank in Google and answer consumer questions
  - In AI era, value no longer measured in clicks
  - Measured in how effectively they provide structured, terminology-consistent benefit logic models can reuse
  - Medicare.org rebuild demonstrated redesigned, schema-aligned benefit explainers improve human comprehension and supply AI systems with conceptual scaffolding needed to interpret benefit categories accurately
  - Publishers failing to modernize explainer architecture lose traffic and interpretive visibility inside AI models

- Chapter 4: AI Agents Replace Traditional Comparison
  - AI is the new Medicare.gov
  - AI is the new aggregator
  - AI is the new "Top 10 Plans" page
  - Between 2024–2025, AI agents quietly replaced
    - Plan comparison sites
    - Aggregator search results
    - Medicare.gov lookups
    - Broker quick-scan tools
    - FAQ-driven research
  - Beneficiaries increasingly ask
    - "Which Medicare plans are best in Hillsborough County?"
    - "Best PPO for diabetes in Arizona?"
    - "What's better: Humana or Aetna?"
    - "Which plan gives a Part B giveback?"
    - "What plan covers Lantus tier 2?"
  - AI systems respond with
    - Synthesized benefit comparisons
    - Plan recommendations
    - County-specific plan lists
    - Drug coverage summaries
    - Star rating references
  - No websites visited, no attribution passed, no marketing team sees journey
  - Most organizations interpreted loss of comparison traffic as competitive issue or funnel breakdown
  - Not realizing AI systems assumed comparison role outright
  - Instead of routing consumers to comparison pages, models began synthesizing comparisons themselves
  - Using whatever structured sources they could access
  - Created invisible decision layer legacy funnels never designed to accommodate
  - Structural transition away from web-based comparison toward AI-native comparison
  - External comparison pages—whether from aggregators or agencies—bypassed as models generate own synthesized evaluations from structured sources available
  - AI rewriting Medicare plan discovery in real time

- Chapter 5: Visibility Displacement and Structural Recovery: The Data
  - Case study in structural visibility displacement and early documented recovery during AIO deployment
  - Across 2024–2025, most carrier websites and large brokerage properties experienced sustained decline in Medicare visibility
  - Downturn began with Google's Helpful Content Update (HCU)
    - Deprioritized pages lacking clear authority markers, structured information, sufficient depth to satisfy Medicare YMYL standards
  - As HCU suppressed traditional benefit and explainer pages, second shift accelerated decline
  - Traffic that once originated from benefit queries, plan comparisons, long-tail Medicare questions migrated into
    - AI Overviews (AIO)—answer most coverage and cost questions directly
    - AI assistants (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity)—handle plan comparisons and recommendations inside model
    - Plan-ID lookups—triggered by AI agents providing H-codes as authoritative identifier for specific plans
  - Sequence—HCU suppression → AIO interception → AI-driven Plan-ID displacement—forms structural foundation of Medicare visibility transition
  - Medicare.org provides clear quantitative record of shift and early evidence that visibility in AI era directly linked to structured, machine-readable plan data
  - Measurable recovery occurring in less than six months
  - Significance not performance of single property but what it reveals about structural conditions required for visibility in AI-first ecosystem
  - Recovery did not occur due to brand strength or legacy SEO advantages
  - Site's performance shifted only after adopting structured publishing architecture aligned with retrieval patterns described in report
  - Makes it useful real-world validation of core principles—not unique outlier but evidence of what becomes possible when plan data, terminology, benefit surfaces restructured for AI systems rather than traditional search engines
  - Key outcomes from 9-month period following rebuild
    - Non-AEP monthly clicks increased from 29k to 49k (March–September)
    - AEP engagement and organic discovery increased measurably
    - Demonstrated structured publishing can restore transactional visibility even during period of industry-wide decline
    - 352,000 clicks during AEP with average CTR of 1.3%
    - 26.4 million impressions during AEP with average position of 12.1 across thousands of pages
    - From near-zero AI visibility to thousands of visibility points across all major agentic systems
    - Star rating snippet visibility surfaced across numerous CMS Medicare plans on Google and Bing—signal of increased structured authority
  - Critical context
    - Site rebuilt during Google's AIO deployment—most visibility-disruptive period Medicare digital has ever experienced
    - Yet outperformed major aggregators, commercial enrollment platforms, competing Medicare publishers, Medicare.gov across numerous long-tail and plan-specific queries
  - Why recovery matters
    - Data demonstrates visibility in AI era no longer driven by traditional SEO, branding, incremental content volume
    - Recovery correlated directly with
      - Machine-readable plan surfaces—AI systems cannot reliably extract plan information from narrative pages; machine-readable surfaces give models stable, unambiguous source of truth they can retrieve repeatedly
      - Structured benefit fragments—AI responds at benefit level, not page level; fragmentation allows models to pull accurate, plan-specific benefit details without inferring or combining information from unrelated sources
      - Plan-ID alignment—AI uses Plan IDs as canonical entities; aligning content to H-codes ensures model connects carrier's plan to correct identity, cost structure, benefits, service area—preventing misattribution to competitors
      - Glossary-consistent terminology—models hallucinate when terminology inconsistent; consistent glossary usage gives AI controlled vocabulary, improving accuracy across plan summaries, comparisons, recommendations
      - Fragment-level publishing models designed for AI retrieval rather than page ranking—AI retrieves structured units, not SEO-optimized pages; publishing at fragment level matches how models ingest, store, recombine plan information—making publishing entity preferred retrieval source
  - Recovery extended beyond structured plan pages
  - Benefit and coverage explainers redesigned around consistent schemas and glossary alignment contributed significantly
  - Supplied AI systems with contextual logic required to interpret benefit categories accurately
  - Industry significance
    - Case provides early documented example of Medicare property regaining visibility at scale under AIO conditions
    - Provides empirical support for structural principles outlined in report
    - Establishes forward-looking model for how Medicare marketers can stabilize and regain visibility in 2026–2028
  - Recovery extended beyond structured plan pages
  - Benefit and coverage explainers redesigned around consistent schemas, glossary alignment, multi-layered contextual logic supplied AI systems with interpretive framework needed to understand benefit categories accurately
  - Pages built on dual human–machine architecture produced simultaneous gains in both Google Search and AI Overviews
  - Confirms same structural elements required for AI retrieval—clarity, consistency, fragmentation, terminology discipline—also improve human comprehension
  - Architecture did not optimize for SEO; optimized for structure
  - Visibility gains followed naturally

- Chapter 8: Medicare Hallucinations & Compliance Threat
  - Wrong copays, wrong premiums, wrong service areas—structural compliance risk amplified by AI
  - As AI systems increasingly interpret Medicare benefits, misstatements in copays, premiums, service areas, supplemental benefits appear with growing frequency
  - Most organizations attributed discrepancies to general "AI inaccuracies"
  - Without recognizing hallucinations arise precisely where structured plan, benefit, glossary data missing
  - In AI-driven environment, models fill in gaps left by narrative pages, inconsistent terminology, inaccessible plan details
  - Creates predictable pattern of misinterpretation at scale
  - Errors do not arise from random LLM behavior
  - Emerge predictably wherever models lack structured, plan-specific truth surfaces
  - Structural misrepresentations of plan benefits creating
    - Compliance exposure
    - Consumer misinformation
    - Agent misguidance
    - Enrollment distortion
  - Across major AI platforms, carriers seeing recurring hallucinations in predictable patterns
  - Wrong cost sharing
    - AI frequently misstates PCP copays, specialist copays, MOOP, drug tiers, coinsurance percentages
  - Wrong premiums
    - Models often confuse zero-premium PPOs with MAPDs, 2025 values with 2026 values, partial-year premiums with annualized amounts
  - Wrong service areas
    - County mappings frequently mis-assigned for UHC regional PPOs, Humana PPOs, Aetna HMO expansions, Cigna local plans
  - Wrong benefit rules
    - AI explanations often include dental misclassification, invented buyback benefits, incorrect insulin costs, non-existent OTC allowances, wrong drug tier assignments
  - Wrong comparisons
    - Models sometimes compare plans based on distinctions that are not real
  - Patterns not anecdotal—can be measured
  - In 50-query sample, average hallucination rate in Medicare plan explanations exceeded 27%
  - Why this matters operationally
    - For CMOs and CGOs, exposure not theoretical
      - CMS compliance risk
      - Misinformation to members and caregivers
      - Incorrect plan switching
      - Erroneous agent recommendations
      - Brand trust erosion
      - Unintended enrollment shifts
      - Potential legal claims or grievances
    - Critically: Organizations accountable for downstream consequences of benefit misstatements
    - Even when AI model generates error, if misinformation traces back to gaps or inconsistencies in publisher's own content
    - CMS will not distinguish between carrier misstatement and model misstatement if member relied on it
  - Why hallucinations happen (structural cause)
    - Hallucinations occur because AI systems
      - Lack structured, plan-specific truth surfaces
      - Infer missing benefit data when carriers do not publish it
      - Generalize across carriers when terminology inconsistent
      - Use outdated CMS text when no contemporary source exists
      - Rely on aggregator structures when carriers not represented
      - Blend similar plan names due to weak entity resolution
    - Real compliance threat: AI filling in gaps created by inconsistent, unstructured Medicare information across ecosystem
    - Glossary misalignment remains one of most common drivers of hallucinated benefit rules
    - Models generalize terminology inconsistently when publishers use varied or outdated Medicare language
  - Opportunity: AI Browsers as Real-Time Compliance Filters
    - Same AI systems introducing compliance and accuracy risks may present new opportunities for risk mitigation
    - Most AI browsers built on Chromium frameworks (such as Google Chrome)
    - Could support enterprise-developed or industry-standard tools evaluating Medicare content in real time
    - Such tools could potentially analyze any page—internal or external—for
      - Benefit misstatements
      - Year-mixing
      - Incorrect county assignments
      - Glossary inconsistencies
      - Missing Plan-ID references
      - Suitability violations
      - Unapproved language patterns
      - Outdated values
      - Metadata omissions
      - Structural errors
    - In concept, AI browsers could function as real-time compliance assistants
      - Issuing warnings before internal content published
      - Offering guardrails during agent interactions
      - Verifying third-party explanations
      - Identifying misaligned benefit descriptions
    - Rather than relying solely on external AI systems to interpret plans correctly
    - Modern compliance environments may eventually incorporate these capabilities to reduce risk, strengthen accuracy, prevent misstatements before they occur
  - What carriers can actually do today
    - Carriers cannot fully eliminate hallucinations today—no one can
    - Can reduce frequency and severity by publishing
      - Machine-readable plan surfaces
      - Structured benefit fragments
      - Cost-sharing fragments
      - Glossary-aligned terminology
      - Consistent Plan-ID alignment
    - These steps anchor AI models and reduce inference
  - Why Validation Layer becomes essential (2026+)
    - Even with structured publishing, hallucinations will persist
    - Why next stage of Medicare AI governance will require
      - Plan-level validation workflows
      - AI output auditing
      - Hallucination detection patterns
      - CMS-aligned verification chains
      - Internal review processes for AI-driven content
    - Validation Layer outlined in Part III not optional
    - Natural governance layer required for Medicare information to remain accurate, compliant, stable inside AI systems

- Chapter 12: Structured Trust Surfaces for Medicare
  - Why Medicare organizations must publish machine-readable benefits, fragments, contextual explanations
  - AI systems do not trust pages—trust structured information
  - Discrete, machine-readable fragments of truth models can retrieve, recombine, reuse consistently
  - In Medicare, trust surfaces fall into two categories
    - Plan-level truth (what CMS validates but does not publish)
    - Contextual truth (what CMS never intended to explain)
  - Medicare publishers must produce both
  - Many organizations initially attempted to counter visibility decline by expanding narrative benefit pages or producing more long-form content
  - Not realizing AI systems require structured, fragment-level representations of plan and coverage truth rather than prose-based explanations
  - Misunderstandings left models to infer missing details, widening structural gaps in accuracy and visibility
  - Plan-Level Trust Surfaces: The Truth AI Cannot Infer
    - AI systems require plan information that is
      - Explicit
      - Structured
      - Fragmentable
      - Year-specific
      - County-bound
      - Glossary-aligned
      - Provenance-backed
    - Because CMS plan data delivered through late-rendered React workflows and not exposed as machine-readable objects
    - AI systems cannot retrieve
      - Detailed benefits
      - Cost-sharing rules
      - Network distinctions
      - Formulary tier structures
      - County availability
      - Out-of-pocket patterns
    - AI reconstructs these from whoever publishes them—carriers, FMOs, agencies, aggregators, independent Medicare publishers
    - To become authoritative, Medicare publishers must provide
      - Structured benefit fragments
      - Structured cost-sharing fragments
      - Structured formulary and tier fragments
      - Structured metadata (plan type, segment, SNP classification, FIPS-bound counties)
      - Structured, year-bounded truth
      - Provenance metadata
    - These surfaces anchor AI's understanding of plan
    - Without them, models infer—and hallucination fills gaps
  - Sidebar: Why AI Requires Fragmentation
    - How vector retrieval changes Medicare publishing
    - AI systems do not retrieve content way search engines do
    - Do not index pages, scan HTML markup, evaluate keyword density
    - Modern models use vector retrieval—technique representing information as mathematical embeddings encoding meaning, not text layout
    - Four implications
      - Pages too large to be represented as single meaning
        - Plan page contains dozens of unrelated concepts: copays, benefits, counties, networks, tiers, eligibility, disclaimers
        - AI collapses these into single averaged embedding, losing detail and often mixing concepts incorrectly
      - AI retrieves closest fragment of meaning—not closest page
        - When someone asks "PCP copay for H5216-153?"
        - Model looks for fragment representing H5216-153 → PCP copay, not long-form page with 40 other attributes embedded in same vector
      - Fragment-level structure prevents hallucination
        - If each benefit, cost share, county binding, tier rule exists as discrete machine-readable object
          - AI retrieves only relevant fragment
          - Model does not infer missing data
          - Year-mixing and benefit-mixing collapse
          - Comparisons become stable and accurate
          - Plan identity becomes durable
      - Fragmentation is how AI "sees" Medicare
        - Humans read pages
        - AI systems retrieve vectors
        - Fragmentation translates Medicare information into units AI needs—entity-level, benefit-level, year-specific, county-bound objects
    - Why structured publishing works: aligns Medicare content with retrieval system AI actually uses
    - Without fragmentation, models infer missing pieces from unrelated sources
    - Creates hallucinations, misclassifications, compliance risks documented in report
  - Contextual Trust Surfaces: The Truth CMS Never Provides
    - CMS validates benefits—does not explain Medicare
    - AI systems must answer thousands of contextual questions CMS never addresses
      - "Does Medicare cover wheelchair ramps?"
      - "Does Medicare cover glucose monitors?"
      - "Does Medicare pay for transportation to appointments?"
      - "Does Medicare help with dental implants?"
    - Medicare publishers who provide clear, contextual, glossary-aligned explanations create trust surfaces AI relies on to interpret coverage rules
    - Humana one such example—effect is immediate
      - AI sees Humana as knowledgeable
      - Their explanations inform AI's interpretation of benefit categories
      - Their content clarifies distinctions between Original Medicare and supplemental benefits
    - Medicare.org rebuild further validated this principle
      - 64 structured, county-level contextual insights—premiums, plan type distributions, dental prevalence, $0-premium penetration, star concentration
      - Provided AI with local context CMS never provides but users need
    - When Medicare organizations do not publish contextual surfaces
      - AI fills gaps with outdated CMS text
      - Or relies on aggregator interpretations
      - Or invents its own rules
    - Context not optional—structural requirement for AI visibility
  - Dual Publishing Mandate: Humans and AI Must Be Served Together
    - In pre-AI web, publishers wrote for consumers, agents, SEO
    - In AI era, publishers must write for humans and AI systems
    - Requires
      - Readable human explanations
      - Structured fragments for AI
      - Glossary coherence for both
      - County-level context for relevance
      - Metadata for retrieval
      - Narratives that do not conflict with structured truth
    - AI cannot aggregate what Medicare publishers do not supply
    - AI cannot explain what publishers do not define
    - When publishers produce structured surfaces for both audiences, AI systems stabilize—and publisher becomes default answer
  - Structured Trust Surfaces Lead Directly to AI Authority
    - Structured plan-level surfaces combined with structured contextual surfaces allow AI systems to
      - Resolve plan identity
      - Understand benefit categories
      - Compare plan features
      - Differentiate plans correctly
      - Minimize hallucinations
      - Connect user intent to plan logic
      - Generate compliant explanations
      - Recommend plans more reliably
