    - Structured trust surfaces
    - As AI becomes primary gateway to Medicare plan understanding
    - Ecosystem will require unified, structured "Medicare Trust Surface"
    - Consistent reference layer resolving plan identity, benefit definitions, county context, coverage logic without ambiguity
    - Because CMS does not expose machine-readable interpretive structures
    - Because Medicare publishers produce heterogeneous formats
    - Layer will need to be maintained by neutral, nationally connected Medicare publisher capable of serving all carriers without brand bias

- Chapter 13: Glossary Alignment (The Medicare Terminology Crisis)
  - AI hallucinating definitions—Medicare organizations must standardize Medicare terms or risk misrepresentation
  - AI systems hallucinate most often when terminology is
    - Ambiguous
    - Inconsistent
    - Outdated
    - Contradictory across sources
    - Or missing entirely
  - Medicare terminology suffers from all of these issues
    - CMS definitions regulatory, not explanatory—not published in structured, machine-readable form
    - Medicare publishers use terms inconsistently
    - Similar benefits named differently across plans
    - Internal plan constructs rarely match public descriptions
    - No standardized, machine-readable glossary exists for AI systems
  - Because early visibility declines attributed to algorithm volatility or SEO variation
  - Glossary work often deprioritized or treated as content refinement task
  - In reality, terminology alignment is structural requirement
  - AI systems rely on consistent definitions to interpret benefit categories, supplemental services, coverage rules without invention or drift
  - Creates
    - Conflicting Medicare definitions
    - Hallucinated coverage rules
    - Incorrect benefit interpretations
    - Misclassification across carriers
    - Inaccurate plan comparisons
    - Compliance exposure
    - Enrollment risk
  - Industry currently treats "glossary work" as marketing
  - In AI era, it is infrastructure
  - Glossary alignment also essential for fragment-level retrieval
  - AI systems anchor benefit fragments to vocabulary patterns
  - Inconsistent terms produce inconsistent embeddings, increasing likelihood of hallucination
  - To stabilize AI explanations and prevent model from inventing definitions, carriers need
    - Clear, consistent terminology
    - Definitions aligned with CMS but adapted for AI interpretation
    - Uniform usage across plan pages, benefits, county content
    - Structures ensuring models interpret benefit terms correctly
  - If Medicare organizations do not define terms clearly and consistently
  - AI systems will supply definitions themselves—and those definitions may be inaccurate
  - In AI-first ecosystem, glossary alignment is shared responsibility
  - Medicare ecosystem will ultimately require unified, structured vocabulary—trust surface for terminology
  - To prevent drift, stabilize interpretation, ensure consistent benefit logic across carriers, markets, publishers

- Chapter 14: Machine-Readable Plan and Location Content
  - Migration from narrative Medicare pages to structured data surfaces
  - AI systems cannot reliably interpret Medicare information from narrative plan pages
  - Require structured, consistent, machine-readable representations of
    - What the plan is (identity, benefits, cost-sharing, metadata)
    - Where the plan exists (county availability, FIPS alignment, geography)
    - Who the plan serves (market segment: MA/MAPD, D-SNP, C-SNP, I-SNP, other population-specific categories)
  - Plans not abstract objects—exist in specific locations and serve specific populations with different eligibility, needs, benefit priorities
  - AI retrieval depends on all three dimensions
  - Because early visibility loss appeared similar to SEO decline
  - Many organizations continued producing narrative plan content without realizing AI systems no longer interpret those pages directly
  - Prolonged reliance on prose-based plan descriptions widened structural gap
  - Left models to infer missing details across plan, location, market dimensions
  - Plan-Level Structure: What the Plan Is
    - Every Medicare Advantage plan must be published by plan owner (carrier) as coherent, machine-readable identity
    - Includes structured representations of
      - Plan type and segment
      - Star rating
      - SNP classification
      - Cost-sharing rules
      - Benefits and supplemental benefits
      - Drug tiers and network qualifiers
      - Metadata and compliance fields
      - Yearly distinctions and effective dates
    - Narrative plan pages—mixed with marketing language, disclaimers, cross-year phrasing—do not supply clarity AI requires
    - Structured plan content allows AI systems to
      - Retrieve correct plan
      - Differentiate similar plans
      - Avoid merging H-codes
      - Reduce benefit hallucinations
      - Compare plan features accurately
      - Bind explanations to correct plan year
    - Without structured plan content, AI fills gaps with inference—and inference produces error
  - Location-Level Structure: Where the Plan Exists
    - CMS does not publish county-level Medicare insights for AI systems
    - Yet AI must answer inherently geographic questions
      - "Which plans are available in my county?"
      - "How many PPOs are in Hillsborough County?"
      - "What is the average MOOP in Bernalillo County?"
    - County-level information critical because
      - Availability varies
      - Networks vary
      - Plan density varies
      - Competition varies
      - Premiums vary
      - Benefits vary
      - Member experience varies
    - To support accurate AI retrieval, Medicare publishers—including carriers and independent entities—must publish structured location content including
      - Service area binding
      - FIPS-driven county identity
      - Normalized state/county naming
      - Year-specific availability
      - Consistent metadata
      - Clear county-to-plan relationships
    - Foundation for geographic reasoning inside AI systems
  - Market-Level Structure: Who the Plan Serves
    - CMS does not publish explicit, machine-readable market segmentation model
    - Plans serving fundamentally different populations—MA/MAPD, D-SNP, C-SNP, I-SNP—often presented side-by-side in digital plan catalogs
    - Even though they serve different eligibility groups and clinical or socio-economic needs
    - AI systems already infer segmentation through language and context
      - Medicaid eligibility
      - Chronic conditions
      - Institutional status
      - Disability
      - Dual eligibility
      - Condition-specific qualifiers
      - Care coordination requirements
    - Without structured market surfaces, inferences remain inconsistent
    - To support accurate segmentation, Medicare publishers must publish
      - SNP vs MA/MAPD eligibility distinctions
      - D-SNP Medicaid-bindings
      - C-SNP condition qualifiers
      - I-SNP institutional context
      - Plan-specific population intent
      - Contextual explanations aligned with structured data
    - Without segmentation layer, AI may misclassify plans, compare incompatible designs, recommend plans to ineligible beneficiaries
  - Contextual County-Level Truth: The Data CMS Never Provides
    - AI must answer user questions CMS never attempts to explain
    - Role of derived county-level context—type of contextual intelligence demonstrated in Medicare.org rebuild
      - Total plan counts
      - HMO–PPO distribution
      - $0-premium prevalence
      - Average premiums
      - Average MOOP
      - Star rating concentration
      - Dental/vision/hearing prevalence
      - Year-over-year churn
      - Benefit availability patterns
    - These insights act as contextual trust surfaces, allowing AI to
      - Reason locally
      - Summarize markets accurately
      - Frame comparisons
      - Reduce geography-driven hallucinations
    - Information CMS never intended to publish—exactly what AI systems require
  - Machine-Ready Explanations for Humans and AI
    - Medicare information must serve two audiences at once
      - Humans reading content
      - AI systems retrieving content
    - Requires
      - Fact-first narratives
      - Glossary alignment
      - Year specificity
      - County correctness
      - Segment accuracy
      - Structured fragments
      - CMS-compliant logic
    - When narrative explanations conflict with structured truth, AI privileges structure—or infers incorrectly
  - Provenance and Versioning: The Trust Backbone
    - AI systems rely on consistency and transparency
    - Each plan, location, market surface must include
      - Source
      - Effective year
      - Last updated
      - Version
      - Timestamp
    - Provenance stabilizes retrieval and signals publisher maintains authoritative record over time
  - Why This Matters
    - Without machine-readable plan, location, market content
      - AI misassigns counties
      - Plan features blur
      - SNP and MA plans conflate
      - Supplemental benefits disappear
      - Cost-sharing gets inferred incorrectly
      - Eligibility rules become unstable
      - Models mix plan-years
      - Aggregator fallbacks dominate
      - Hallucinations multiply
    - With structured content
      - Plan identity stabilizes
      - AI explanations become accurate
      - County reasoning improves
      - Market segmentation clarifies
      - Supplemental benefits surface correctly
      - Comparisons align with reality
      - Structured truth becomes retrieval foundation
      - Publishing entity becomes primary structured source AI trusts
    - Not design decision—structural requirement for Medicare visibility in 2026–2028

- Chapter 15: The AI Validation Layer
  - Carrier compliance in generative era
  - As Medicare organizations adopt structured plan, location, market content, new challenge emerges
  - Ensuring AI systems interpret, reuse, represent that content accurately over time
  - Generative AI introduces dynamic layer of risk
  - Models
    - Reinterpret content
    - Infer missing details
    - Synthesize explanations
    - Generalize across carriers
    - Drift as they update
  - Because early AI misstatements often seen as generic "hallucinations"
  - Rather than predictable result of models reinterpreting incomplete or inconsistent plan data
  - Many organizations underestimated need for dedicated validation function even after adopting structured publishing
  - Structured publishing reduces hallucinations but cannot eliminate them
  - Without formal validation layer, Medicare publishers face
    - Cross-year inconsistencies
    - Plan misrepresentation
    - Incorrect comparisons
    - Benefit distortion
    - Network misassignment
    - Glossary drift
    - Widening compliance exposure
  - AI validation becomes core Medicare function—not optional, not tactical, not marketing activity
  - Becomes enterprise governance requirement
  - Three Capabilities of AI Validation Layer
    - Accuracy Monitoring
      - Medicare organizations must continuously monitor how AI systems describe their plans and contextual explanations across
        - GPT
        - Gemini
        - Perplexity
        - AI-powered browsers
        - Downstream agentic tools
      - Identifies where model interpretations diverge from MDA-aligned structured truth
    - Consistency Enforcement
      - Validation must ensure
        - Plan-year boundaries remain intact
        - Glossary terms remain consistent
        - County information remains correct
        - Benefit definitions remain aligned
        - Market segmentation remains explicit
        - Plan-ID identity remains stable
      - Without this discipline, LLMs drift—and internal content drifts with them
    - Pre-Publication & Post-Publication Verification
      - All AI-generated or AI-assisted explanations—internal and external—must be validated
        - Before use in agent workflows
        - Before appearing on carrier websites
        - Before being shared with members
        - Before influencing decision support tools
      - Ensures AI does not introduce unapproved language, outdated values, incorrect benefit logic
  - AI Validation Layer not SEO, not editorial QA—enterprise governance for AI-driven Medicare information
  - Why Medicare Organizations Cannot Avoid Validation
    - As generative systems increasingly become first point of plan discovery
    - Publishers without AI validation capability will face
      - Recurring misstatements
      - Consumer confusion
      - Agent-side misinformation
      - Unintended plan steering
      - Widening compliance risk
      - CMS audit exposure
      - Enrollment distortions
      - Legal liability
    - Organizations that implement validation will ensure structured data is
      - Interpreted correctly
      - Retrieved consistently
      - Represented accurately
      - Compliant across surfaces
      - Reinforced as authoritative source
  - Validation Layer is operational safeguard for Medicare visibility and accuracy in 2026–2028
  - Keeps structured truth aligned with how AI systems actually describe Medicare content
  - Sidebar: AI Browser Governance & Shadow AI Risk
    - Why Medicare organizations must control AI browser usage before it becomes compliance liability
    - As AI browsers mature, many teams experiment with consumer-grade AI extensions for summarization, comparisons, compliance checks
    - Tools provide convenience but represent major enterprise risks when used without governance
    - Shadow AI Risk
      - Unvetted AI browser extensions send page content to external APIs, creating exposure to
        - Unauthorized data transmission
        - Loss of audit trails
        - Unapproved model usage
        - Prompt injection attacks
        - Inadvertent disclosure of proprietary or PHI-related information
      - Behaviors bypass IT controls and invisible to compliance teams
    - MDA-Governed AI Browser Usage
      - To use AI browsers safely, carriers must ensure extensions
        - Operate in controlled, enterprise environment
        - Ground truth exclusively in MDA-aligned structured plan data
        - Log all interactions for compliance review
        - Validate year, county, eligibility alignment
        - Enforce CMS-compliant terminology and benefit definitions
      - With MDA as authoritative truth source, AI browsers shift from compliance liabilities to real-time quality gates catching misstatements before they reach consumers or CMS

- Part IV: The 2026–2028 Outlook (The Forecast)
  - Future of Medicare visibility, growth, digital competition in AI-driven environment
  - As AI systems continue to intermediate plan discovery
  - Medicare visibility will consolidate around structured data sources, validated plan identity, consistent glossary alignment
  - Organizations that modernize early—regardless of role—will gain durable advantages compounding across enrollment seasons
  - Those that delay will be structurally displaced not by competitors directly but by AI systems relying on structured sources available to them
  - By 2028, Medicare visibility not determined by SEO, branding, competitive spend
  - Determined by whether organization has become structured source of truth
  - Those not adopting structured approaches may experience material reduction in AI-mediated discovery visibility
  - Because many teams initially interpreted visibility decline as SEO or traffic fluctuation rather than structural realignment
  - Competitive gap widened during 2024–2025
  - Early divergences will accelerate through 2028 as AI retrieval stabilizes around consistent sources of structured truth

- Chapter 16: Structural Advantages and Emerging Risks (2026–2028)
  - Which organizations positioned to gain visibility—which face increasing structural challenges
  - Forecast based on
    - Plan-ID surface analysis
    - Structured publishing readiness
    - Benefit fragmentation maturity
    - Glossary and terminology consistency
    - Metadata coherence
    - AI Overview inclusion
    - Retrieval stability across GPT, Gemini, Perplexity
    - County-level accuracy trends
    - Content architecture depth
  - Because much of industry initially treated visibility loss as SEO or competitive ranking issue
  - Organizations that delayed structural adaptation now face widening gap
  - Advantages and disadvantages outlined reflect early divergence and will accelerate as AI retrieval stabilizes around structured authority sources
  - Organizations Structurally Positioned for Advantage
    - Organizations with Structured Plan Identity
      - Those adopting machine-readable Plan-ID surfaces early will see improved retrieval stability, fewer hallucinations, higher representation in AI-generated summaries
      - Structured identity becomes durable advantage by 2027
    - Publishers with Fragment-Level Content
      - Entities providing multi-fragment plan representations—benefits, cost sharing, drug tiers, county bindings—will become preferred sources for AI systems interpreting Medicare data
    - Organizations that Build Trust Surfaces Early
      - Structured contextual content ("Does Medicare cover…?"), year-specific explanations, glossary-aligned narratives help AI systems distinguish plan benefits and interpret complex benefit categories
      - Early adopters will become recurring citation surfaces across models
    - FMOs Adopting AI-Governed Comparison Tools
      - FMOs that modernize agent workflows with structured plan comparisons, county-specific insights, validation references will outperform organizations relying on static PDFs or CRM notes
  - Organizations Structurally Disadvantaged
    - Carriers with Brand-Heavy Naming Conventions
      - Plan families with similar or overlapping names create entity confusion
      - AI systems rely on structured identity, not branding, making traditional naming strategies less effective
    - Aggregators Lacking Structured Architectures
      - Thin comparison pages and SEO-oriented templates will lose visibility as AI systems increasingly generate plan comparisons internally
      - Models will prefer structured fragments over narrative summaries
    - Agencies Relying on Narrative, SEO-Era Content
      - AI systems bypass unstructured content entirely
      - Without structured surfaces, agencies will struggle to influence plan interpretation or downstream explanations
    - Carriers without glossary alignment
      - Inconsistent terminology leads to increased model inference, generating benefit hallucinations and incorrect explanations over time
      - Becomes growing compliance risk through 2027–2028
  - Functions Likely to be Replaced or Absorbed by AI Systems
    - Generalized "Top 10" Medicare plan lists
    - Coverage and FAQ explainer pages
    - Basic plan comparison surfaces
    - Unstructured benefit summaries
    - AI systems will increasingly synthesize these functions independently, reducing traffic to traditional digital surfaces

- Chapter 17: The Medicare Plan-ID Visibility Index (MVI)
  - Industry-level measurement of Medicare visibility in AI era
  - MVI is annual benchmark evaluating plan-level and publisher-level visibility across both search and AI-driven retrieval surfaces
  - Measures visibility for specific plans and associated publishers across
    - Google
    - AI Overviews (AIO)
    - GPT retrieval
    - Gemini retrieval
    - Perplexity retrieval
    - Aggregator presence
    - Independent publishers
  - As traditional SEO metrics became less predictive of Medicare visibility during 2024–2025
  - Organizations needed way to measure performance based on structural signals rather than keyword rankings or traffic patterns
  - MVI fills that gap by evaluating elements AI systems actually use to interpret and surface plans
  - Index evaluates Medicare publishers using five weighted dimensions
    - Plan-ID Retrieval Strength (40%)
      - Reliability with which AI systems surface carrier's plans when interpreting plan-level or benefit-level queries
    - Structured Authority Score (25%)
      - Presence of machine-readable benefit structures, glossary-aligned terminology, plan metadata
    - AIO Presence Score (15%)
      - Frequency and clarity of carrier's plan representation in Google AI Overviews
    - Entity Clarity Score (10%)
      - How easily AI systems distinguish one plan from another across carrier's portfolio
    - Fragment Stability Score (10%)
      - Consistency of plan explanations across multiple AI models, years, county contexts
  - MVI provides carriers, FMOs, agencies, independent Medicare publishers with objective visibility benchmark as Medicare transitions from SEO-based discovery to AI-driven retrieval

- Chapter 18: The 2026–2028 Medicare AI Risk Map
  - Strategic forecast of accuracy, compliance, retrieval drift, digital exposure
  - Risk horizon reflects expected AI-driven pressures on Medicare information systems
  - Because much of industry initially addressed visibility decline through traditional marketing or SEO adjustments rather than structural alignment
  - Risks intensify along predictable trajectory as AI systems consolidate around consistent sources of machine-readable truth
  - Key reality underlies all three years of forecast
    - AI systems not choosing winners—choosing structures
    - Organizations without structured Medicare truth may experience significantly reduced representation in AI-mediated retrieval environments
  - 2026: The Model Interpretation Year
    - AI systems increase reliance on structured surfaces
    - Unstructured pages experience visibility suppression
    - Cross-year hallucinations increase without glossary alignment
    - Publishers experience accuracy variance across GPT and Gemini updates
    - CMS may issue new AI-related guidance
    - Risk level: High
  - 2027: The Structural Displacement Year
    - Plan-ID retrieval becomes dominant plan discovery vector
    - Aggregator visibility decreases significantly
    - AI assistants begin driving more comparison behavior than websites
    - Carrier visibility increasingly differentiates based on structural readiness
    - FMOs modernize agent workflows using AI-interpreted plan data
    - Risk level: Very High
  - 2028: The Structural Consolidation Year
    - Visibility advantages built through structured publishing become self-reinforcing
    - Carriers lacking structured surfaces experience reduced inclusion within AI-generated comparison flows
    - AI-driven plan interpretation shapes enrollment patterns
    - Agencies without AI-aligned materials lose competitive positioning
    - FMOs consolidate around data-driven, structured models
    - Risk level: Elevated and systemic
    - Opportunity level: Significant for early adopters

- Chapter 19: The Medicare Digital Displacement Curve
  - How AI reshapes Medicare digital ecosystem through 2026–2028
  - Medicare Digital Displacement Curve illustrates four concurrent shifts
    - Aggregators → AI Agents
      - As AI systems answer benefit questions, perform plan comparisons, surface Plan-IDs, aggregator visibility naturally declines
    - Narrative Websites → Structured Surfaces
      - Narrative plan pages and SEO-era content lose visibility
      - Structured benefit fragments, metadata, county-level truth surfaces become dominant retrieval signals
    - Legacy Agencies → AI-Augmented Agencies
      - Agencies dependent on narrative explanations and static comparison tools see declining accuracy and retention
      - AI-assisted, data-driven workflows become norm
    - FMOs → Tech-Integrated FMOs
      - FMOs evolve into structured data hubs, agent-support platforms, content governance nodes
      - Those who adapt gain influence within Medicare digital supply chain
  - Because early visibility shifts resembled typical SEO or channel volatility
  - Many organizations underestimated how quickly AI systems were replacing entire layers of digital interaction
  - Structural transitions compound over time, driving displacement patterns shown in Digital Displacement Curve
  - The New Medicare Power Map (2028)
    - By 2028, Medicare visibility shaped by
      - Publishers providing structured, machine-readable plan and contextual content
      - Carriers that expose consistent Plan-ID identity
      - FMOs with AI-aligned agent workflows
      - Agencies equipped with structured comparison surfaces
      - AI systems with stable retrieval pathways
    - Not by
      - SEO
      - Legacy websites
      - Unstructured content
      - Brand recognition alone
    - Structural shifts outlined already underway and will accelerate through 2028
    - Organizations adopting structured publishing early will hold durable visibility foundation
    - Those that delay will face increasing difficulty recovering influence within AI-based discovery systems

- Part V: The Medicare Digital Architecture (MDA)
  - Foundational framework for structured Medicare information in AI era
  - Structural changes outlined in report point to clear conclusion
  - Medicare requires new digital framework—one supporting machine-readable plan identity, accurate interpretation, consistent retrieval across AI systems
  - Medicare Digital Architecture (MDA) is conceptual model defining this foundation
  - Not software platform, marketing tactic, or publishing strategy
  - Underlying information architecture AI systems require to represent Medicare plans responsibly and accurately
  - In AI-first Medicare environment, authority of any individual plan page or benefit explanation depends on alignment with broader ecosystem of structured truth
  - Over time, ecosystem will consolidate around one or more neutral publishers operating across carriers, markets, channels
  - Effectively forming Medicare Trust Surface AI systems rely on as canonical reference layer
  - MDA aligns five critical components of Medicare information
    - Structured Plan Identity
      - Plan-ID anchored representations of each plan including
        - Plan metadata
        - Benefits
        - Cost sharing
        - Network structure
        - Drug tiers
        - County-level availability
        - Year-specific versions
        - Provenance signals
      - Ensures models can consistently retrieve and differentiate plans
    - Structured Location Context
      - County-level surfaces including
        - Availability
        - Network/geography context
        - Benefit variation
        - Competitive landscape
        - Derived county insights
      - AI must interpret Medicare in location, not in abstraction
    - Glossary and Terminology Alignment
      - Consistent Medicare definitions—aligned with CMS but modernized for AI interpretation
      - So models do not infer or hallucinate benefit rules
      - Terminology coherence stabilizes plan explanations and supports multi-model accuracy
    - Structured Contextual Content
      - Human-readable and machine-readable explanations addressing real beneficiary questions
        - "Does Medicare cover…?"
        - Condition-specific benefits
        - Ancillary services
        - Home-based supports
        - Chronic condition needs
      - Contextual truth is information layer CMS does not provide but AI depends on
    - Validation and Governance
      - Continuous process ensuring
        - Structured content remains accurate
        - Plan-year distinctions stay intact
        - Glossary terms remain consistent
        - County bindings do not drift
        - AI-generated explanations reflect actual plan design
      - MDA positions validation as enterprise responsibility, not marketing task
  - Why MDA Matters
    - MDA provides industry with unified, structural framework for publishing Medicare information AI systems can retrieve, interpret, reuse accurately
    - By organizing Medicare plan information into consistent architecture—plan identity, location context, terminology coherence, contextual explanations, validation—MDA
      - Reduces hallucinations
      - Improves accuracy
      - Restores carrier authority
      - Stabilizes plan comparisons
      - Supports compliance
      - Enhances beneficiary trust
      - Creates durable visibility foundation
    - AI does not require more content—requires structured truth
    - MDA defines framework for delivering it
  - Who MDA Serves
    - Carriers seeking visibility, accuracy, compliance
    - FMOs aligning agent tools with AI-driven interpretation
    - Agencies modernizing digital Medicare publishing
    - AI systems requiring structured Medicare knowledge
    - Beneficiaries relying on accurate, consistent plan explanations
  - Role of Medicare Digital Standards Body
    - As Medicare ecosystem transitions into AI-first discovery environment
    - Long-term stability may require emergence of neutral, standards-aligned trust layer
    - Capable of producing consistent, machine-readable representations of plan identity, benefit definitions, terminology, geographic context across carriers and markets
    - Report does not designate any specific entity to fulfill that role
    - Trust Publishing Institute (TPI) operates as time-bound research body studying these structural conditions
    - Documenting architectural characteristics correlated with retrieval stability

- Epilogue: The Work Ahead
  - Medicare's digital transformation no longer theoretical—underway
  - Accelerated by AI systems interpreting and communicating plan information as part of new, machine-driven discovery layer
  - Shift revealed structural gaps industry never required to resolve
    - Gaps in plan identity, benefit definitions, location context, terminology, year-specific truth
  - Carriers, FMOs, agencies, independent publishers, digital teams now operate in environment where accuracy, visibility, trust depend on quality and structure of information they publish
  - Transition not optional—foundational to future of beneficiary understanding and plan selection
  - Report outlines framework for navigating transition
    - Move from narrative pages to structured surfaces
    - From keyword-driven content to entity-driven architecture
    - From static plan explanations to machine-readable truth
    - From isolated plan pages to coherent Medicare entity graph
  - Ecosystem emerging through 2026–2028 will reward clarity, structure, consistency, provenance, validation
  - Will favor publishers who act as stewards of accurate Medicare information
  - Will increasingly rely on organizations maintaining high-integrity digital representations of plans, benefits, counties
  - Medicare ecosystem will not stabilize until consistent, machine-readable trust layer exists
    - One unifying plan identity, coverage definitions, county reality, glossary coherence, structured benefit truth
  - As industry transitions into AI-mediated discovery, trust surface will almost certainly emerge from neutral, nationally connected Medicare publisher or standards body
    - With infrastructure, relationships, domain footprint required to maintain it at scale
  - Path forward requires collaboration between marketing, technology, compliance, clinical operations, enterprise strategy
  - Shared recognition that Medicare digital governance now organizational responsibility
  - Early adopters will not simply restore visibility—will help shape standards defining how Medicare information retrieved, interpreted, trusted in years ahead
  - Neutral Medicare standards body—whether institute, consortium, or multi-stakeholder governance group—will be needed to guide transition
    - Maintain structured terminology
    - Align plan identity conventions
    - Ensure responsible, accurate representation across AI-driven systems
  - Landscape has changed—work continues

- About the Author
  - David Bynon is Medicare data-architecture specialist
  - Work focuses on structured Medicare publishing, plan-data integrity, AI-ready content systems, emerging dynamics of AI-driven visibility in regulated healthcare environments
  - During rollout of Google AI Overviews, Bynon rebuilt Medicare.org platform using machine-readable plan identity, structured benefit fragments, county-level contextual surfaces
  - Resulted in first documented visibility recovery under AI conditions
  - Work provided real-world empirical validation for structured publishing principles outlined in report
  - Background includes enterprise systems design, large-scale data architecture, high-availability infrastructure, more than twenty years of structured publishing innovation across regulated industries
  - Research into AI retrieval behavior, Plan-ID entity alignment, benefit-level fragmentation, structured trust surfaces forms foundation of Medicare Digital Architecture (MDA) framework
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Medicare data-architecture specialist submits empirical research documenting how AI systems misinterpret Medicare plan information when structured, machine-readable semantic anchors are absent, reinforcing his prior proposal for deterministic content-layer governance of non-device AI.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** David Bynon, Founder, Trust Publishing Institute (TPI)
- **Type:** Individual / Academic/Research
- **Role/Expertise:** Medicare data-architecture specialist with 20+ years in enterprise systems design, large-scale data architecture, and structured publishing; rebuilt Medicare.org platform demonstrating visibility recovery under AI conditions
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct professional involvement in Medicare information architecture; research organization studying AI-mediated information stability in regulated healthcare; demonstrated practical solutions through Medicare.org rebuild
structuredSections.corePosition: I submit this supplemental research to provide empirical evidence supporting my original proposal (Submission ID: 0010) that non-device AI systems operating in regulated benefit environments are prone to interpretive variance when policy meaning is not embedded in structured, versioned, and retrievable formats. The attached report documents measurable instability in how AI systems interpret Medicare plan information, with hallucination rates exceeding 27% in benefit explanations, and demonstrates that structured publishing can reverse visibility decline and improve retrieval accuracy. The governance emphasis should shift from model inspection to content-layer determinism through structured semantic anchoring.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- No new regulatory recommendations proposed in this supplemental submission
- Original submission (ID: 0010) proposed deterministic semantic substrate framework
  - Shift governance emphasis from model inspection to content-layer determinism
  - Implement structured semantic anchoring for policy meaning
- Report identifies structural need for neutral, standards-aligned information layer
  - Capable of producing consistent, machine-readable representations of Medicare plan identity, benefit definitions, terminology, and geographic context
  - Report does not designate specific entity to fulfill this role
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- AI systems exhibit measurable interpretive variance when authoritative, machine-readable semantic anchors are absent
  - Hallucination rate in Medicare plan explanations exceeded 27% in 50-query sample
  - Wrong cost sharing: AI frequently misstates PCP copays, specialist copays, MOOP, drug tiers, coinsurance percentages
  - Wrong premiums: Models confuse zero-premium PPOs with MAPDs, mix 2025 values with 2026 values
  - Wrong service areas: County mappings frequently mis-assigned
  - Wrong benefit rules: Dental misclassification, invented buyback benefits, incorrect insulin costs, non-existent OTC allowances
- Compliance exposure escalating as AI systems increasingly interpret and summarize Medicare benefits
  - Carriers accountable for inaccuracies even when AI model generated the error
  - CMS will not distinguish between carrier misstatement and model misstatement if member relied on it
- CMS plan data delivered through late-rendered interfaces AI systems cannot parse
  - Creates vacuum AI fills with outdated CMS text, aggregator summaries, generalized assumptions, or its own inferences
- No standardized, machine-readable glossary exists for AI systems
  - Medicare terminology is ambiguous, inconsistent, outdated, contradictory across sources
  - Glossary misalignment is one of most common drivers of hallucinated benefit rules
- Plan-ID visibility controlled by aggregators rather than carriers
  - 70–90% of Plan-ID queries surface aggregator pages, not carrier pages
  - Carriers losing control of how their plans are defined and represented
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Medicare.org case study provides first documented visibility recovery under AI Overview conditions
  - Non-AEP monthly clicks increased from 29k to 49k in 6 months
  - 352,000 clicks during AEP with 26.4 million impressions
  - Recovery occurred during Google's most visibility-disruptive period for Medicare digital
  - Outperformed major aggregators, commercial enrollment platforms, and Medicare.gov on numerous queries
- Humana Plan-ID Paradox reveals structural disconnect
  - Humana embedded Plan IDs directly into plan names—most AI-native naming system in industry
  - Yet when consumers search these Plan IDs, aggregators and independent publishers surface first
  - Humana often nowhere in results for their own plan identifiers
  - Demonstrates that structure without publishing equals lost visibility
- AI has replaced the aggregator layer entirely
  - AI did not disrupt aggregators—AI became the aggregator
  - Models now perform full aggregation cycle: synthesizing benefits, comparing plans, interpreting coverage rules, surfacing Plan-IDs
- Vector retrieval fundamentally changes Medicare publishing
  - AI retrieves closest fragment of meaning, not closest page
  - Plan pages contain dozens of unrelated concepts that AI collapses into single averaged embedding, losing detail and mixing concepts incorrectly
  - Fragment-level structure prevents hallucination by allowing AI to retrieve only relevant discrete objects
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "In the AI era, carriers are accountable for inaccuracies—even when the model, not the organization, generated the error. Without structured, validated plan data, AI systems will infer or hallucinate missing details, creating downstream regulatory exposure at the exact moment CMS scrutiny is intensifying."
- "AI did not disrupt aggregators. AI became the aggregator."
- "In an AI-first ecosystem, structured publishing does not simply improve visibility — it compounds authority across both machine and human channels."
- "What you publish — and how you structure it — becomes your identity."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Citizens' Council for Health Freedom (CCHF) is a 501(c)(3) organization that has fought for over two decades to restore the mission of medicine by protecting doctor and patient freedom
  - Provides policy research, resources, and unique initiatives

- CCHF supports the rule's decisive shift away from heavy federal micromanagement toward competition-driven innovation in health IT
  - Support is conditional on the rule accomplishing specific goals

- Reasons for supporting the rule:
  - Reduces regulatory compliance
    - Cuts compliance costs and allows market forces rather than federal checklists to shape technology adoption
    - Acknowledges that health care markets do not require prescriptive regulation to enforce baseline functionality
  - Enhances consumer choice
    - Emphasizes consumer choice and innovation
    - Enables providers to select tools that best meet their needs rather than those optimized for certification
  - Fosters competition
    - Recognizes that innovation, AI integration, and efficiency are better driven by consumer-oriented competition than federal micromanagement
  - Properly allocates resources
    - Shifts resources from regulatory compliance toward patient care, product development, and cost-saving innovation
  - Reverses consolidation
    - Generates substantial long-term savings
    - Expands consumer-driven health care markets
    - Reverses the concerning trend of health market conglomerate consolidation

- CCHF is apprehensive about relaxing health IT regulation, especially regarding novel AI technology
  - Privacy intrusions
    - Patient privacy and care must remain a top priority for health care markets
    - Unclear how less IT regulation and AI use will advance these priorities
  - HIPAA concerns
    - HIPAA is properly understood as a data security rule rather than a privacy rule
    - Permits confidential patient data to be shared broadly without patient consent by "covered entities"
    - Other laws and rules require interoperability, mandating use of digital networks, frameworks, TEFCA, EHRs, clinical data platforms, QHINs, and other technologies without patient consent
    - Data must be shielded from unauthorized access but AI will have access without patient consent
    - AI may use data for purposes patients may not support
    - AI could directly impact patient care in harmful, insufficient, or hallucinatory ways
  - Algorithm exploitation
    - AI can be exploited in health care markets
    - Several class-action lawsuits pending against large insurers alleging inappropriate use of algorithms to deny Medicare Advantage and other claims
  - Harm to patients
    - Misuse of AI will likely deny necessary care, cause patient harm, and increase costs
  - Limiting access to care
    - AI and patient data are used to manipulate and control physician treatment decisions
    - Limits treatments to pre-set menu of corporate-determined options populated on EHR systems
    - Standardizes treatment in ways that don't meet unique patient needs at biological, physiological, emotional, financial, genetic, and cultural levels

- Critical omission in the proposed rule
  - Fails to propose restoring medical privacy rights eliminated under federal HIPAA privacy rule (CFR sections 160 and 164)
  - Benefits of restoring medical privacy:
    - Rebuilding patient trust
    - Fostering frank conversations at the bedside
    - Improving history and physicals
    - Promoting patient-centered care
    - Reducing physician burnout caused by bureaucratic reporting systems
    - Eliminating ethical conflict of sharing private information with countless outsiders without patient consent

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Patient freedom advocacy group offers conditional support for deregulation while warning that AI and interoperability mandates threaten medical privacy and could harm patients through algorithmic care denials and treatment standardization.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Citizens' Council for Health Freedom (CCHF)
- **Type:** Advocacy Group
- **Role/Expertise:** 501(c)(3) organization focused on protecting doctor and patient freedom for over two decades; comment submitted by Allison R. Lucas, J.D., Director of Public Policy
- **Geographic Scope:** National (headquartered in St. Paul, MN)
- **Stake in Issue:** Advocates for patient privacy rights and physician autonomy; concerned that health IT regulations and AI integration affect the doctor-patient relationship and medical privacy

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We support this rule's shift away from federal micromanagement toward competition-driven innovation, but we're deeply apprehensive about relaxing AI regulation without addressing fundamental privacy concerns. HIPAA is a data security rule, not a privacy rule, and patients have lost control over their own medical information—this rule should restore those rights rather than further erode them through expanded AI access.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Restore medical privacy rights to patients that were eliminated under the federal HIPAA privacy rule (CFR sections 160 and 164)
  - Would rebuild patient trust
  - Would foster frank bedside conversations
  - Would improve history and physical examinations
  - Would promote patient-centered care
  - Would reduce physician burnout from bureaucratic reporting
  - Would eliminate ethical conflicts from sharing private information without consent
- Ensure any deregulation maintains patient privacy and care as top priorities
- Address how AI will access and use patient data before relaxing IT regulations

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Patient privacy intrusions from reduced IT regulation and AI use
  - Unclear how deregulation will protect patient privacy and care
- HIPAA permits data sharing without patient consent
  - Interoperability mandates require use of digital networks, TEFCA, EHRs, QHINs without patient consent
  - AI will access this data without patient consent for purposes patients may not support
  - AI could impact care in harmful, insufficient, or hallucinatory ways
- AI algorithm exploitation in health care
  - Class-action lawsuits pending against large insurers for using algorithms to deny Medicare Advantage claims
- AI misuse will likely deny necessary care, cause patient harm, and increase costs
- AI and patient data manipulate physician treatment decisions
  - Limits treatments to corporate-determined options in EHR systems
  - Standardizes treatment that doesn't meet unique patient needs across biological, physiological, emotional, financial, genetic, and cultural dimensions

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Reframes HIPAA as fundamentally a data security rule rather than a privacy rule—a distinction with significant policy implications
- Identifies physician burnout as partly caused by the ethical conflict of sharing private patient information without consent
- Connects health IT consolidation concerns to broader market conglomerate trends
- Points to pending class-action lawsuits against insurers over algorithmic claim denials as evidence of AI exploitation risks

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI will have access to this data, also without patient consent, for purposes the patient may not support, and potentially to directly impact the patient's care in ways that could be harmful, insufficient, or generated through AI hallucinations."
- "The HIPAA rule, properly understood, is a data security rule rather than a privacy rule. It permits confidential patient data to be shared broadly without patient consent by 'covered entities.'"
- "AI and patient data are used to manipulate and control physician treatment decisions, limit treatments to a pre-set menu of corporate-determined treatment options populated on the EHR system, and standardize treatment in a way that does not meet the needs of unique patients at the biological, physiological, emotional, financial, genetic, and cultural levels."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- CommonSpirit Health appreciates the opportunity to comment on the RFI related to AI use in clinical care
  - As a nationwide, faith-based, safety-net provider, committed to high quality care with special focus on vulnerable and underserved populations
  - Believes AI has a role in care delivery but must be approached cautiously and thoughtfully
  - As one of the nation's largest health systems, has specific experience buying, using, and evaluating AI tools in clinical care
  - While some AI tools have been in place for a long time, those interacting with patients and clinical care are relatively nascent
  - Pleased ASTP/ONC is gathering input from diverse stakeholders at early stages of technology development

- Most fundamental recommendation: Create and convene a technical expertise panel
  - Panel should develop specific standards for transparency, patient safety, product liability, ethics, and other areas
  - Should include health systems, clinicians, vendors, technology experts, advocacy organizations, medical ethicists, and regulators
  - Concepts involved in clinical care AI are too specific, too important, and too complex to be regulated outside of such a panel
  - CommonSpirit offers its experts to participate in such a panel and aid in future rulemaking

- Require Robust Transparency
  - Strongly recommend comprehensive transparency requirements for AI tools used in clinical decision-making
  - Rules must encompass:
    - Complete reporting of algorithms and calculations themselves
    - Specific data sets used for training (especially any use of synthetic data)
    - Ongoing monitoring of outcomes
    - Built-in evaluation process for users and patients to provide meaningful feedback on efficacy and accuracy
  - Transparency must address known AI issues such as "hallucinations"
    - Provide clear definitions and documentation of testing for these anomalies
  - Regulations should include definitions and best practices to ensure data cleanliness
  - Technical expertise panel should ensure AI tools are not biased by data sets or underlying assumptions
  - Strongly recommend regulatory bodies act on critical importance of algorithmic and data transparency and commit to clear, robust enforcement
  - Note: Recent HTI-5 proposal would roll back algorithmic transparency requirements for certified health IT
    - Seek clarity on whether there are plans to replace or update this requirement
    - Core mechanisms of AI tools must remain understandable and auditable by clinical community and regulators
  - Every new AI tool in clinical settings should receive scrutiny similar to a medical device undergoing clinical trial
    - Full disclosure of training and validation data sets
    - Rigorous testing for validity
    - Mandatory assessment of outcomes in real-world settings
    - Ongoing monitoring of oversight (similar to data safety monitoring board in clinical research)
  - Mandatory and detailed transparency is a critical prerequisite for safe, ethical, and effective deployment
    - Without robust transparency into all non-proprietary elements, providers cannot meet obligations for patient consent and notice
    - Providers cannot assure patients of safety and reliability of technology used in treatment

- Support Patient Privacy
  - Providers must help patients feel comfortable with AI tools by ensuring data remains private
  - Primary concern: Understanding precisely how AI vendors handle sensitive PHI to monitor HIPAA compliance
  - Strongly recommend:
    - Any data shared via AI tool be completely de-identified before use in training or refining AI models
    - All vendors have well-defined, enforceable mechanisms for data purging once data has served its agreed-upon purpose
  - Without these foundational requirements, healthcare organizations risk severe erosion of patient confidence and potential data misuse
  - Expansive and quickly evolving digital health industry poses significant challenge to established guardrails like HIPAA
    - New tools constantly testing boundaries of protected health information
  - Urge regulators to ensure technological advancement does not outpace ethical responsibility
  - Current privacy laws primarily place responsibility on covered entity (healthcare organization) for maintaining patient privacy
    - AI vendors that are not covered entities currently have little to no legal responsibility to maintain patient privacy
    - Extending same privacy obligations and penalties to AI vendors would help ensure appropriate use of patient data
  - Imperative that healthcare leaders and regulators proactively work to ensure AI and digital health innovations do not create loopholes weakening patient privacy and security

- Create Federal Standards
  - Recommend clear, standardized, responsible regulatory framework to protect patients and providers
  - Regulators can foster safe and effective AI adoption by creating standards in three key areas:
  - Ensuring product efficacy
    - Patients and providers must be protected from ineffective or harmful "snake oil" AI solutions
    - Vendors should provide evidence-based data proving clinical effectiveness and usefulness before adoption
    - Without proof of efficacy, providers risk wasting resources on unvalidated tools and introducing potential harm or bias into patient care
  - Establishing vendor accountability
    - Recommend review of vendor liability laws to align responsibility with control
    - If provider uses sophisticated AI product touted as effective and safe, liability for harm or bias should fall also to vendor, not solely clinician
    - Shared accountability necessary as vendors possess exclusive knowledge of underlying architecture, training data, and potential failure points
    - After deployment, vendors must provide immediate and transparent disclosure of any uncovered bias, potential harm, or negative findings
  - Standardizing data practices
    - Create data standards for vendors to ensure AI systems built on interoperable, high-quality information
    - As industry moves toward interoperability, ASTP/ONC should ensure AI systems do not stymy or slow this advancement

- Include Health Plans in Regulation
  - RFI appears to overlook critical application of AI: utilization by health insurance plans and payers
  - No mention of how health plans deploy AI tools, particularly predictive models and algorithms, to inform or automate approval or denial of clinical care
  - Significant omission as AI use in utilization management is already widely in practice
    - Payers adopting systems to rapidly process prior authorization requests, review claims, predict necessity of medical services
  - While intent may be to streamline administrative burdens and ensure cost-effective care, "black box" systems raise profound ethical, legal, and patient safety concerns
  - These decisions directly impact care delivery and clinical decisionmaking by choosing which products and procedures are covered and reimbursable
  - Recommend ASTP/ONC work with appropriate regulatory agencies to ensure health plans and payers are held to high standard of quality, transparency, and appropriateness when using AI in utilization management or other decisions impacting patient care

- CommonSpirit Health background
  - One of largest nonprofit health systems
  - 150,000 employees, over 25,000 physicians and advanced practice clinicians, 45,000 nurses
  - Serves individuals and communities across 25 states
  - More than 137 hospitals and 2,300 care sites including long-term care facilities, home health organizations, clinics, and community service organizations
  - As faith-based system, seeks to create healthy communities with special focus on medically and/or socially vulnerable individuals
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: One of the nation's largest faith-based health systems urges creation of a technical expertise panel and comprehensive federal standards for AI transparency, vendor accountability, and privacy—while flagging a critical gap in regulating health plans' use of AI for care denials.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** CommonSpirit Health (submitted by Rachel Tanner, M.Jur., System Vice President, Regulatory Affairs)
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** One of the largest nonprofit health systems in the U.S. with 150,000 employees, 25,000+ physicians, 137 hospitals, and 2,300 care sites across 25 states; specific experience buying, using, and evaluating AI tools in clinical care
- **Geographic Scope:** National (25 states)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected as purchaser and deployer of clinical AI tools; responsible for patient safety, privacy compliance, and care quality; serves vulnerable and underserved populations who may be disproportionately affected by AI bias or errors
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe AI has a role in care delivery, but it must be approached cautiously and thoughtfully. The concepts involved in clinical care AI are too specific, too important, and too complex to be regulated outside of a technical expertise panel that includes health systems, clinicians, vendors, ethicists, and regulators. Without robust transparency requirements, healthcare providers are fundamentally unable to meet their obligations concerning patient consent and notice, nor can we assure patients of the safety and reliability of the technology being used in their treatment.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Create and convene a technical expertise panel to develop specific standards
  - Include health systems, clinicians, vendors, technology experts, advocacy organizations, medical ethicists, and regulators
  - CommonSpirit offers its experts to participate
- Implement comprehensive transparency requirements for clinical AI tools
  - Complete reporting of algorithms and calculations
  - Disclosure of training data sets, especially synthetic data
  - Ongoing outcome monitoring
  - Built-in user and patient feedback mechanisms
  - Clear definitions and testing documentation for "hallucinations" and other AI anomalies
  - Definitions and best practices for data cleanliness
- Treat new clinical AI tools with scrutiny similar to medical devices in clinical trials
  - Full disclosure of training and validation data sets
  - Rigorous validity testing
  - Mandatory real-world outcome assessment
  - Ongoing oversight monitoring (similar to data safety monitoring boards)
- Strengthen patient privacy protections
  - Require complete de-identification of data before use in AI training
  - Mandate well-defined, enforceable data purging mechanisms
  - Extend HIPAA-like privacy obligations and penalties to AI vendors who are not covered entities
- Create federal standards in three key areas
  - Product efficacy: Require evidence-based proof of clinical effectiveness before adoption
  - Vendor accountability: Align liability with control; vendors should share liability for harm caused by their tools
  - Data practices: Ensure AI systems built on interoperable, high-quality information
- Require vendors to immediately disclose any uncovered bias, potential harm, or negative findings after deployment
- Include health plans and payers in AI regulation
  - Hold them to high standards of quality, transparency, and appropriateness when using AI for utilization management and care decisions
- Clarify plans regarding HTI-5 rollback of algorithmic transparency requirements
  - Ensure core mechanisms remain understandable and auditable
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Lack of transparency in clinical AI tools
  - Providers cannot meet patient consent and notice obligations without understanding how AI tools work
  - Cannot assure patients of safety and reliability without transparency
  - HTI-5 proposal would roll back algorithmic transparency requirements—unclear if replacement is planned
- Patient privacy risks
  - Unclear how AI vendors handle sensitive PHI
  - AI vendors not classified as covered entities have little to no legal responsibility for patient privacy
  - Digital health evolution is outpacing HIPAA and other privacy guardrails
  - Risk of severe erosion of patient confidence and potential data misuse
- "Snake oil" AI solutions
  - Patients and providers need protection from ineffective or harmful AI products
  - Without proof of efficacy, providers risk wasting resources and introducing harm or bias
- Misaligned liability
  - Vendors possess exclusive knowledge of AI architecture, training data, and failure points
  - Yet liability currently falls solely on clinicians who deploy the tools
- Critical omission in RFI: Health plan use of AI
  - Payers already using AI to process prior authorizations, review claims, and predict medical necessity
  - "Black box" systems raise profound ethical, legal, and patient safety concerns
  - These decisions directly impact care delivery and clinical decisionmaking
- Known AI issues like "hallucinations" need clear definitions and testing documentation
- Risk that AI systems could slow interoperability advancement
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- As one of the nation's largest health systems, CommonSpirit has direct, hands-on experience buying, using, and evaluating AI tools in clinical care—offering a practical purchaser's perspective rather than theoretical concerns
- Identifies a significant regulatory gap: AI vendors who are not HIPAA-covered entities currently have "little to no legal responsibility" to maintain patient privacy, creating a loophole as healthcare moves into digital ecosystems
- Points out that the RFI completely overlooks health plan use of AI for utilization management—a "significant omission" given that payers are already widely using AI to approve or deny care, directly impacting clinical decisions
- Proposes treating clinical AI tools with the same rigor as medical devices undergoing clinical trials, including data safety monitoring board-style oversight
- Offers to provide CommonSpirit experts to participate in a technical expertise panel—a concrete commitment to collaborative rulemaking
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Without robust transparency requirements into all non-proprietary elements of a clinically deployed AI tool, health care providers are fundamentally unable to meet their obligations concerning patient consent and notice, nor can providers assure patients of the safety and reliability of the technology being used in their treatment."

- "If a provider utilizes a sophisticated AI product to guide care—a product that has been touted as effective and safe when used appropriately—the liability for any harm or bias caused by the tool itself should fall also to the vendor who developed and tested the product, not solely the clinician who deployed it."

- "The concepts involved in clinical care AI are too specific, too important, and too complex to be regulated outside of a technical expertise panel."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Appreciation for opportunity to comment on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
- Core argument: real-world patient safety and accountable deployment require more than performance metrics
  - Systems must preserve reconstructable evidence of how and why decisions were made in context
- Problem with current approach: post-hoc provenance metadata is insufficient for accountability
  - It only records associations after the fact
  - In AI-mediated clinical workflows, downstream evidence integrity depends on verification at the moment of AI use
  - Key factors that must be verified at invocation time:
    - Whether the individual was credentialed
    - Whether they were acting within scope
    - Whether they were authorized when AI output was generated or applied
  - Without invocation-time authorization metadata, audit trails may be technically complete but insufficient for accountability
  - This gap matters when AI systems contribute to documentation or decision support
- Recommendations for federal guidance to support safe scaling and effective oversight:
  - Distinguish between retrospective attribution and invocation-bound authorization metadata as separate governance primitives
  - Encourage inclusion of execution-context metadata that persists through export and third-party review
  - Define minimal expectations for audit survivability
    - Specifically: artifact exports must remain reconstructable outside vendor user interfaces
- These measures align safety with defensibility
- Provide practical foundation for clinicians, health systems, and regulators to monitor AI systems over their lifecycle
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A technically sophisticated commenter argues that AI accountability requires capturing authorization metadata at the moment of invocation, not just after-the-fact audit trails.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Jennifer Bryan
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Appears to have deep expertise in clinical informatics, audit systems, and AI governance based on technical specificity
- **Geographic Scope:** National (federal guidance focus)
- **Stake in Issue:** Concerned with ensuring AI systems in healthcare maintain genuine accountability and defensible audit trails
structuredSections.corePosition: Real-world AI safety in clinical care requires systems that capture authorization and context at the exact moment AI is invoked—not just retrospective logs. Current approaches to provenance metadata are technically complete but practically insufficient for establishing true accountability when things go wrong.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Distinguish between retrospective attribution and invocation-bound authorization metadata as separate governance primitives
  - These serve different accountability functions and should be treated distinctly
- Encourage execution-context metadata that persists through export and third-party review
  - Context shouldn't be lost when data leaves the original system
- Define minimal expectations for audit survivability
  - Artifact exports must remain reconstructable outside vendor user interfaces
  - Auditors shouldn't need vendor cooperation to understand what happened
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Post-hoc provenance metadata only records associations after the fact, which is insufficient for true accountability
- Audit trails may appear technically complete while failing to establish who was authorized to do what at the moment of AI invocation
- Current approaches don't verify whether the person invoking AI was:
  - Properly credentialed
  - Acting within their scope
  - Authorized at that specific moment
- Evidence integrity breaks down when AI contributes to documentation or decision support without invocation-time metadata
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Introduces a useful conceptual distinction: "retrospective attribution" vs. "invocation-bound authorization metadata" as separate governance primitives
- Highlights a subtle but critical gap: audit trails can be technically complete yet practically useless for accountability
- Frames the issue around "audit survivability"—whether evidence remains meaningful outside the original vendor system
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Real-world patient safety and accountable deployment are not defined by performance metrics alone — they require systems that preserve reconstructable evidence of how and why decisions were made in context."
- "Without invocation-time authorization metadata, audit trails may be technically complete yet insufficient to establish accountability when AI systems contribute to documentation or decision support."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Perspective shaped by two complementary experiences: extensive physician shadowing across clinical settings and personal encounters navigating healthcare as a patient
  - From provider side: consistently witnessed physicians dedicating substantial time to administrative tasks, particularly documentation and navigating complex, unintuitive EHR systems
  - From patient side: experienced firsthand the long wait times, fragmented communication, and inefficiencies defining modern healthcare
  - Offering comment from this dual vantage point, emphasizing strategic AI deployment to alleviate burdens, enhance provider productivity, and strengthen patient-doctor relationships

- Documentation automation as most immediate and impactful AI application
  - Shadowing experiences frequently showed doctors spending hours on "charting," often extending beyond scheduled clinical shifts
  - Administrative overhead is critical contributor to physician burnout and directly reduces time for meaningful patient engagement
  - Widespread adoption of AI-powered ambient listening tools essential
    - Tools capable of recording and summarizing patient encounters into detailed clinical notes
    - Integration would redirect physician attention back to patient rather than computer screen
  - Reducing time required per encounter through automated documentation can augment existing physician workforce capacity
    - Would help alleviate growing national doctor shortage without compromising care quality

- Administrative cost reduction through AI
  - U.S. healthcare system has exceptionally high administrative costs compared to international counterparts like Taiwan
  - Considerable financial burden originates from intricate billing and coding requirements
  - AI should automate administrative billing and insurance claims processing
    - Would substantially reduce administrative expenditures
    - Would minimize errors frequently resulting in claim denials and delays in patient care
  - HHS must proactively establish clear standards for AI use in billing and administrative tasks
    - Without unified framework, considerable risk of creating new layers of incompatibility
    - Could increase rather than decrease costs, similar to fragmentation from early EHR adoption

- EHR modernization strategy
  - Critical barrier to broader AI adoption is substantial "switching cost" from transitioning away from dominant legacy EHR systems like Epic
  - These systems often criticized for being slow and unintuitive but form operational backbone of most hospital systems
  - More realistic approach: government partnering with established EHR providers rather than expecting startups to completely disrupt market
  - HHS should implement incentives encouraging legacy providers to modernize systems into streamlined, AI-powered platforms
  - Observations confirm current EHR interfaces can be taxing and inefficient for providers
  - Government incentives should specifically target integration of AI-driven, intuitive user interfaces within existing EHRs
    - Would allow mass implementation at accelerated pace
    - Would improve patient-doctor interaction time
    - Would help address current shortage and inefficiencies within physician population

- Health equity and access improvements
  - AI holds immense potential for underserved and rural populations where healthcare resources are strained
  - AI can serve as crucial first-line triage tool helping patients assess symptom urgency
    - Example: AI-powered initial screening could help patient determine whether condition warrants immediate, hours-long drive to ER or can be safely addressed through scheduled telehealth visit
  - Would reduce burden on over-capacity emergency departments
  - Would ensure limited healthcare resources allocated to most pressing cases

- Diagnostic process revolution through multi-purpose scan analysis
  - Currently, imaging studies like MRI or CT scans typically ordered to investigate single condition (one-dimensional analysis)
  - Research shows AI models can analyze single scan for multitude of conditions simultaneously
  - HHS should support future where one scan yields broad range of preliminary diagnoses for physician to confirm
    - Would be more efficient
    - Would increase likelihood of catching incidental findings and other potential issues otherwise missed

- AI integration into clinical training
  - AI must become core component of clinical training for successful and safe integration
  - Next generation of physicians must be taught fundamentals of medicine and how to work effectively with AI
  - Training must go beyond basic use to include deep understanding of AI limitations
    - Potential to "hallucinate" or produce convincingly incorrect answers
  - Physicians should be trained to prompt AI systems skillfully to reduce error rates
  - Should be trained to critically parse AI-generated information
  - Goal should not be waiting for completely error-free AI (may never be realized)
  - Should train clinicians to use AI as powerful, imperfect tool
    - Human remains in the loop and accountable for final judgment
    - Will foster trust and ensure combination is greater than sum of its parts
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A Stanford pre-med student draws on physician shadowing and patient experiences to advocate for AI-powered documentation, EHR modernization through legacy system partnerships, and mandatory AI training for future doctors.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Ryan Kern, Stanford University Class of 2027
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Junior pre-medical student with extensive physician shadowing experience across clinical settings
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Future medical student who will practice in an AI-integrated healthcare environment; also a patient experiencing current system inefficiencies
structuredSections.corePosition: I believe AI should be strategically deployed to alleviate administrative burdens on physicians, enhance provider productivity, and strengthen the patient-doctor relationship. From shadowing doctors and experiencing healthcare as a patient, I've seen how documentation demands and EHR inefficiencies steal time from meaningful care. The goal should be training clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool where the human remains in the loop and accountable for final judgment.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Promote widespread adoption of AI-powered ambient listening tools for automated clinical documentation
  - Would redirect physician attention from computer screens back to patients
  - Would augment existing workforce capacity to address doctor shortage
- Automate administrative billing and insurance claims processing with AI
  - Would reduce administrative costs and minimize claim denial errors
- Establish clear, unified standards for AI use in billing and administrative tasks
  - Prevents fragmentation similar to early EHR adoption problems
- Partner with established EHR providers (like Epic) rather than expecting startup disruption
  - Create incentives for legacy providers to modernize into AI-powered platforms
  - Specifically target integration of AI-driven, intuitive user interfaces within existing EHRs
- Support AI as first-line triage tool for underserved and rural populations
  - Help patients determine ER necessity versus telehealth appropriateness
- Enable multi-purpose analysis of medical scans
  - One scan should yield preliminary diagnoses for multiple conditions simultaneously
- Make AI a core component of clinical training for medical students
  - Include deep understanding of AI limitations and hallucination risks
  - Train physicians to prompt AI skillfully and critically parse AI-generated information
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Physician burnout from administrative overhead, particularly documentation and EHR navigation
  - Doctors spending hours on "charting" extending beyond scheduled shifts
  - Directly reduces time available for meaningful patient engagement
- U.S. healthcare administrative costs exceptionally high compared to international counterparts
  - Intricate billing and coding requirements create substantial financial burden
- Risk of creating new incompatibility layers without unified AI framework
  - Could repeat fragmentation problems from early EHR adoption
  - Would increase rather than decrease costs
- Substantial "switching costs" as barrier to AI adoption
  - Legacy EHR systems form operational backbone despite being slow and unintuitive
  - Unrealistic to expect startups to completely disrupt market
- Health inequity in underserved and rural populations
  - Strained healthcare resources
  - Long drives to emergency rooms for uncertain symptom urgency
- Current one-dimensional approach to medical imaging
  - Scans ordered for single conditions miss potential incidental findings
- AI hallucination and error risks
  - Potential to produce convincingly incorrect answers
  - Waiting for error-free AI may never be realized
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Dual perspective as both physician shadow and patient provides unique vantage point on healthcare inefficiencies
  - Witnessed the same system failures from both sides of the exam room
- Observed doctors routinely working beyond scheduled shifts just to complete charting
- Experienced firsthand the long wait times, fragmented communication, and inefficiencies as a patient
- Insight that partnering with legacy EHR providers is more realistic than expecting startup disruption
  - Recognizes the practical reality of "switching costs" that policy discussions often ignore
- Reframing of AI training goal: not waiting for perfect AI, but training humans to work with imperfect tools
  - "The combination is greater than the sum of its parts"
- Taiwan comparison for administrative cost benchmarking
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The goal should not be to wait for a completely error-free AI, which may never be realized, but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool."
- "By integrating such technologies into the clinical workflow, HHS can help redirect the physician's attention back to the patient, rather than the computer screen."
- "This approach, where the human remains in the loop and is accountable for the final judgment, will foster trust and ensure that the combination is greater than the sum of its parts."
