structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Executive Summary
  - Cyberhill, in collaboration with ServiceNow, responds to HHS RFI on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
  - As an AI solutions firm with deep expertise in Department of Defense and Intelligence Community, we bring unique perspective on deploying secure, scalable, governance-compliant AI in highly regulated environments
  - Response addresses three core pillars: Regulation, Reimbursement, and Research & Development
  - Our approach emphasizes:
    - Security-First Architecture: HIPAA-aligned AI deployments with zero-trust principles
    - Knowledge Graph-Powered Intelligence: Ontology-driven clinical decision support
    - Agentic AI Workflows: Autonomous clinical task automation with human oversight
    - Interoperability by Design: FHIR-native, HL7-compliant data exchange
  - Proposed Clinical Care Intelligence Platform (CCIP) leverages ServiceNow Healthcare and Life Sciences Service Management platform, augmented with Cyberhill's custom ontology engineering and agentic AI

- Responder Qualifications
  - About Cyberhill Partners
    - Professional services firm headquartered in Austin, Texas
    - Specializes in complex software implementations for Fortune 500 companies and U.S. Government
    - Over 8 years of experience and 800+ successful enterprise software implementations
    - Core competencies span AI/ML, Cybersecurity, and Cloud domains
      - AI/ML: Custom AI Agent Development, Ontology Engineering, Knowledge Graphs, MLOps & Lifecycle, Model Security
      - Cybersecurity: Zero Trust Strategy, PAM & IAM, SIEM Implementation, Network Detection, DLP Solutions
      - Cloud: Hybrid Migration, Cloud Strategy, DevOps Automation, Data Engineering, Cloud Native Apps
  - ServiceNow Partnership
    - Strategic partnership with ServiceNow, the leading digital workflow platform
    - ServiceNow's Healthcare and Life Sciences Service Management (HLSM) provides enterprise foundation
      - FedRAMP High Authorization for government deployments
      - HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA) compliance
      - Native FHIR R4 integration capabilities
      - Now Assist AI capabilities with healthcare-specific training
    - Partner Contributions
      - ServiceNow provides: Enterprise workflow backbone with FedRAMP High/HIPAA compliance; healthcare-specific service management modules; native Now Assist conversational AI; enterprise integration hub with pre-built healthcare connectors; scalable cloud infrastructure with guaranteed uptime SLAs
      - Cyberhill provides: Custom clinical ontology engineering and knowledge graph development; graph-native AI architecture with explainable reasoning paths; agentic AI orchestration with human-in-the-loop controls; domain-specific model fine-tuning and prompt engineering; semantic layer design grounding AI outputs in authoritative clinical sources to reduce hallucinations; specialized integration with clinical terminologies (ICD-10, SNOMED CT, LOINC, RxNorm)
      - Cyberhill's unique value: transforming ServiceNow's enterprise platform into a clinically intelligent system that understands healthcare semantics, not just healthcare workflows
  - Relevant Experience
    - Defense Innovation Unit (DIU) – Next-Generation Biosurveillance Platform
      - Delivered alongside ServiceNow as strategic partner
      - Captures, catalogs, and visualizes health threat data from diverse sources including CDC, WHO, social media signals, and open internet monitoring
      - Provides near-real-time AI-derived summarization and reporting to the warfighter
      - Demonstrates ability to integrate highly heterogeneous data sources, deliver timely actionable intelligence, and operate within highly regulated and closed network environments
    - U.S. Air Force (AETC) – AI-Enabled Training Platform
      - First-of-its-kind platform leveraging existing approved training materials to dynamically create new learning pathways
      - Allows users to train and test knowledge while ensuring all AI-generated content is traceable to approved source documents
      - Ontology-first approach ensures every recommendation maps back to validated curricula, dramatically reducing hallucination risk and providing full auditability
    - Enterprise Healthcare (Cerebro Platform) – Healthcare Ecosystem Intelligence
      - Connects data across entire healthcare ecosystems, automatically generates domain ontologies, and provides executive-level decision support
      - Philosophy: "treat the patient, not the symptom"—addressing logistics, systemic patterns, and early warning indicators at population and operational level
      - Relies on metadata and aggregations rather than Protected Health Information (PHI)
      - Demonstrates that powerful healthcare AI does not require training models on confidential patient data
      - Addresses HIPAA concerns while delivering actionable intelligence for healthcare administrators and executives

- Technical Approach: Clinical Care Intelligence Platform
  - Solution Overview
    - CCIP designed as agentic AI system that augments clinical decision-making while maintaining strict governance and human oversight
    - Built on three foundational pillars:
      - Clinical Knowledge Ontology: Comprehensive semantic layer encoding healthcare regulations, diagnostic criteria, treatment protocols, and reimbursement rules
      - Agentic Workflow Engine: Autonomous AI agents that execute clinical tasks with human-in-the-loop checkpoints
      - Enterprise Integration Layer: FHIR-native connectivity with EHR systems, claims processors, and regulatory databases
  - Clinical Knowledge Ontology Architecture
    - Provides semantic backbone for all AI reasoning
    - Core Ontology Entities:
      - Diagnosis: ICD-10/SNOMED coded clinical diagnoses with severity levels (properties: icdCode, snomedCt, severity, chronicity, bodySystem)
      - Evidence: Clinical observations supporting diagnoses—labs, imaging, notes (properties: evidenceType, loincCode, value, unit, confidence)
      - TreatmentProtocol: Clinical practice guidelines and care pathways (properties: guidelineSource, steps, contraindications, cptMappings)
      - Regulation: Healthcare regulations—HIPAA, CMS rules, FDA guidance (properties: cfrPart, section, requirements, penalties, effectiveDate)
      - AIDecision: AI-generated recommendations with provenance tracking (properties: modelId, confidence, explanation, evidenceRefs, humanOverride)
  - Agentic AI Architecture enables autonomous clinical task execution with appropriate guardrails

- Responses to Specific Questions
  - Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for healthcare
    - HIPAA Compliance and PHI Access Control
      - One of the most significant barriers is ensuring HIPAA compliance when AI systems interact with clinical data
      - AI models that touch patient data present complex access control challenges—AI can produce PHI in outputs even when queried with seemingly innocuous prompts
      - Creates fundamental tension: AI systems require data access to function effectively, but existing access control frameworks were not designed for systems that generate, infer, or synthesize PHI
      - Organizations face uncertainty about minimum necessary standards for AI, audit trail requirements for AI-generated content, and liability when AI inadvertently discloses PHI
      - HHS should provide specific guidance on AI-PHI boundaries, including technical safeguards for preventing PHI leakage in model outputs and clear standards for AI access control
    - Regulatory Uncertainty
      - Absence of clear regulatory frameworks for non-medical device AI creates significant uncertainty
      - Organizations hesitant to invest when liability, accountability, and compliance requirements remain undefined
      - HHS could establish safe harbor provisions for AI systems meeting specified governance criteria
    - Data Interoperability Gaps
      - Despite progress with FHIR adoption, significant barriers remain in accessing comprehensive, standardized clinical data
      - Proprietary data silos, inconsistent implementations of standards, and limited access to training datasets impede AI development
      - HHS should accelerate enforcement of interoperability requirements under 21st Century Cures Act
    - Trust and Explainability Deficits
      - 61% of executives lack trust in AI outputs
      - Clinical AI requires explainable decision-making that clinicians can understand and verify
      - Investment in explainable AI (XAI) research and standards for model transparency would address this barrier
    - Reimbursement Misalignment
      - Current fee-for-service models do not incentivize AI adoption
      - When AI reduces unnecessary procedures or shortens hospital stays, it may decrease revenue under current payment structures
      - Value-based payment models that reward outcomes would better align incentives
  - Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
    - Regulatory Recommendations
      - Establish clear distinction between AI as medical devices (FDA jurisdiction) and clinical decision support tools (ONC oversight) per 21st Century Cures Act Section 3060(a)
      - Create certification pathways for clinical AI analogous to EHR certification under 45 CFR Part 170, with specific criteria for safety, effectiveness, and interoperability
      - Modernize HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR Part 164) to address AI-specific considerations including model training on de-identified data and synthetic data generation
    - Payment Policy Recommendations
      - Create new CPT codes for AI-assisted clinical services, enabling direct reimbursement for AI-enhanced care
      - Include AI deployment metrics in Merit-based Incentive Payment System (MIPS) quality measures under 42 CFR Part 414
      - Establish shared savings provisions for AI-driven efficiency gains under Medicare Shared Savings Program (42 CFR Part 425)
  - Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
    - Liability and Accountability
      - Most significant legal uncertainty concerns liability allocation when AI contributes to adverse outcomes
      - Current medical malpractice frameworks assume human decision-making
      - HHS should develop model liability frameworks that appropriately allocate responsibility among AI developers, healthcare organizations, and clinicians based on roles and degree of AI autonomy
    - Privacy and Security
      - AI systems present novel privacy risks including model inversion attacks, membership inference, and potential for re-identification of de-identified data
      - HHS should issue guidance on AI-specific security controls, potentially through updates to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards
    - Algorithmic Bias
      - AI systems trained on historical data may perpetuate or amplify existing healthcare disparities
      - HHS should establish requirements for bias testing and mitigation, building on Office of Civil Rights' authority under Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act
  - Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for clinical care
    - Pre-Deployment Evaluation
      - Prospective clinical validation studies with diverse patient populations
      - Robustness testing across demographic subgroups and edge cases
      - Red team exercises to identify failure modes and adversarial vulnerabilities
      - Human factors evaluation assessing clinician-AI interaction patterns
    - Post-Deployment Monitoring
      - Continuous performance monitoring with automated drift detection
      - Real-world outcome tracking linked to AI recommendations
      - Sentinel surveillance for adverse events potentially linked to AI
      - Feedback loops enabling continuous model improvement
    - Prize competitions would be particularly effective for developing standardized evaluation benchmarks and testing methodologies, following successful model of ONC Health IT Interoperability Standards Challenge
  - Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
    - Standards Development Support
      - Fund and participate in standards development organizations working on clinical AI standards
      - Support ANSI/AAMI work on AI in medical devices, HL7 efforts on AI data exchange standards, and IEEE standards for AI transparency
      - Provide ONC resources to develop clinical AI certification criteria
    - Testing Infrastructure
      - Establish or fund regional AI testing centers providing access to synthetic clinical data, standardized evaluation protocols, and expert consultation
      - Could operate similarly to ONC-Authorized Testing Laboratories for EHR certification
    - Workforce Development
      - Support training programs for clinical informaticists who can bridge gap between AI developers and clinical users
      - Partner with HRSA on health professions training grants that include AI literacy components
  - Question 6: Where AI tools have met or exceeded expectations and where they've fallen short
    - Successes
      - Medical imaging analysis (radiology, pathology, dermatology) achieving expert-level accuracy
      - Clinical documentation assistance reducing physician administrative burden
      - Sepsis and deterioration prediction enabling earlier intervention
    - Shortfalls
      - Alert fatigue from poorly calibrated clinical decision support
      - Workflow integration challenges creating additional burden rather than efficiency
      - Performance degradation when deployed in populations different from training data
    - High-Potential Novel Applications
      - Agentic AI systems that autonomously execute administrative tasks (prior authorization, care coordination, claims processing) with human oversight
      - Knowledge graph-powered diagnostic reasoning that explains its clinical logic
      - Multimodal AI integrating clinical notes, imaging, genomics, and social determinants
      - Continuous monitoring AI for chronic disease management in home settings
  - Question 7: Key decision-makers and administrative hurdles
    - Key Decision Makers
      - Chief Medical Officer: Highest influence—clinical validation and physician adoption require CMO sponsorship
      - Chief Information Officer: Critical for infrastructure, security, and EHR integration decisions
      - Chief Financial Officer: Controls budget allocation and requires clear ROI demonstration
      - Medical Informatics: Technical bridge between clinical and IT teams—drives implementation success
    - Administrative Hurdles
      - Procurement complexity—healthcare purchasing cycles average 12-18 months
      - Security and compliance review bottlenecks
      - Governance committee approval requirements
      - Change management and training resource constraints
      - EHR vendor ecosystem limitations and integration costs
  - Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
    - Priority Data Types
      - Unstructured clinical notes: Standardized clinical document architecture (C-CDA 2.1) with consistent section coding
      - Medical imaging: DICOM-SR structured reporting with AI-compatible annotation standards
      - Social determinants of health: SDOH FHIR Implementation Guide adoption
      - Genomic data: GA4GH standards for variant representation
    - Priority Standards
      - USCDI v4 expansion to include AI-relevant data elements
      - FHIR Bulk Data Access for efficient AI training data extraction
      - SMART on FHIR for AI application authorization and launch
      - CDS Hooks for standardized clinical decision support integration
    - Benchmarking Tools
      - HHS should fund development of standardized clinical AI benchmarking datasets enabling apples-to-apples comparison of AI performance
      - Datasets should include diverse patient populations, annotated ground truth, and defined evaluation metrics
  - Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns
    - Challenges AI Could Address
      - Care coordination burden—navigating complex healthcare systems
      - Access to specialist expertise in underserved areas
      - Understanding diagnosis and treatment options in plain language
      - Medication management and adherence support
      - Appointment scheduling and administrative tasks
    - Concerns
      - Privacy—fear of health data misuse or breach
      - Loss of human connection in healthcare relationships
      - AI errors leading to misdiagnosis or inappropriate treatment
      - Algorithmic bias resulting in discriminatory care
      - Lack of transparency about when AI is being used in their care
  - Question 10: AI research areas HHS should prioritize
    - Explainable Clinical AI: Methods to make AI reasoning transparent and understandable to clinicians
    - Bias Detection and Mitigation: Techniques to identify and correct algorithmic disparities
    - Privacy-Preserving AI: Federated learning and differential privacy for model training
    - Human-AI Teaming: Optimal interfaces and workflows for clinician-AI collaboration
    - AI Safety and Robustness: Methods to ensure reliable AI performance across diverse settings
    - Implementation Science: Research on effective strategies for AI deployment and adoption

- Conclusion
  - Key themes from response:
    - Regulatory clarity is essential—clear frameworks reduce uncertainty and enable investment
    - Payment alignment must incentivize AI adoption—value-based models reward AI-driven efficiency
    - Interoperability enables innovation—standardized data access accelerates AI development
    - Trust requires transparency—explainable AI and bias mitigation build confidence
    - Human oversight remains paramount—agentic AI augments rather than replaces clinical judgment

- Key Recommendations from Cover Letter
  - HHS should issue targeted guidance on AI-PHI boundaries under HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules
    - AI systems can generate or infer PHI even from de-identified inputs
    - Current regulatory framework does not adequately address this risk
    - Clear technical safeguard standards would reduce compliance uncertainty and unlock private sector investment
  - Payment policy reform essential to align incentives with AI-driven efficiency
    - New CPT codes for AI-assisted clinical services
    - Inclusion of AI deployment metrics in MIPS quality measures
    - Shared savings provisions under Medicare Shared Savings Program
  - Establish certification pathways for non-medical-device clinical AI analogous to existing EHR certification under 45 CFR Part 170
    - Would provide market with clear safety, effectiveness, and interoperability benchmarks
  - Investment in standardized clinical AI evaluation infrastructure
    - Benchmarking datasets with diverse patient populations
    - Regional AI testing centers
    - Prize competitions for evaluation methodologies
  - Powerful healthcare AI does not require training models on confidential patient data
    - Privacy-preserving approaches leveraging metadata, aggregations, and synthetic data can deliver actionable intelligence while fully respecting patient privacy

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- HHS, CMS, FEMA, FBI, ASTP/ONC and ASPR should consider a cross-sector and cross-agency AI interoperability accelerator
  - Focus areas: emergency response, public safety, and disaster operations
  - Goal: enable harmonized and standardized data exchange across all public safety, emergency response, and disaster domains
  - Implementation: standards-based APIs
  - Purpose: support AI clinical systems in emergency and disaster care
- Domains to cover:
  - EMS
  - Fire
  - Law Enforcement
  - Emergency Management
  - Shelter Coordination
  - Logistics
  - Search and Rescue
- Data systems and agencies to include:
  - NEMSIS (National EMS Information System)
  - NFIRS (National Fire Incident Reporting System) for fire suppression
  - Law enforcement systems
  - NIBRS (National Incident-Based Reporting System)
  - Disaster sheltering reporting systems
  - US Geological Survey
  - Emergency management agencies
  - Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response
  - Urban Search and Rescue Task Force under FEMA
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Individual commenter proposes a multi-agency AI interoperability accelerator to standardize data exchange across all emergency response, public safety, and disaster operations domains.

- **Name/Organization:** David Rocha

structuredSections.corePosition: Multiple federal agencies should collaborate to create an AI interoperability accelerator that standardizes data exchange across emergency response, public safety, and disaster operations. This would enable AI clinical systems to function effectively during emergencies and disasters by harmonizing data from EMS, fire, law enforcement, emergency management, and related domains.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish a cross-sector, cross-agency AI interoperability accelerator
  - Involve HHS, CMS, FEMA, FBI, ASTP/ONC, and ASPR
  - Focus on emergency response, public safety, and disaster operations
- Implement standards-based APIs for harmonized data exchange
- Include seven operational domains: EMS, Fire, Law Enforcement, Emergency Management, Shelter Coordination, Logistics, and Search and Rescue
- Integrate existing data systems including NEMSIS, NFIRS, NIBRS, disaster sheltering systems, USGS, and FEMA Urban Search and Rescue
structuredSections.mainConcerns: No specific concerns raised (comment is primarily a constructive proposal)

- Proposes connecting clinical AI systems with non-healthcare emergency data sources (geological survey, law enforcement, fire) to improve disaster care

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Background and context for response
  - Responding specifically to HHS's request for input on regulatory frameworks, reimbursement structures, and research priorities for safe, effective, and equitable AI adoption in clinical care
  - PeriGen is a perinatal technology company using supervised machine learning to provide clinicians with early-warning notifications of concerning trends in maternal and fetal clinical status
  - PeriGen products are FDA cleared and utilized in 22% of American hospitals and health systems that provide childbirth services
  - In 2025, PeriGen's AI obstetric software provided technology support for 800,000 births
  - Comments focus on maternal and neonatal health, a high-acuity area where AI can significantly improve outcomes

- Analysis of AI potential and barriers
  - AI offers significant potential to enhance patient safety, reduce clinician burden, and lower healthcare costs
  - In obstetrics, predictive analytics can identify early warning signs of maternal and fetal complications, enabling earlier intervention and reducing preventable adverse events
  - These adverse events impose substantial financial, emotional, and reputational costs on patients, families, communities, and healthcare providers
  - Barrier: Regulatory Uncertainty
    - Current frameworks for non-device software lack clarity
    - Creates delays and discourages innovation
  - Barrier: Payment Misalignment
    - Fee-for-service models rarely incentivize preventive technologies
    - AI tools that reduce complications and improve efficiency should be recognized within value-based care programs
  - Barrier: Data Interoperability
    - Fragmented systems and inconsistent standards limit AI's effectiveness
    - Enhanced interoperability and pathways for intentional workflow integration would widen market opportunities and accelerate innovation
  - These barriers risk slowing progress toward HHS's stated goals of improving outcomes and reducing costs
  - Without clear guidance, smaller providers and rural hospitals may struggle to implement AI solutions, exacerbating health disparities

- Recommendations
  - Modernize Regulatory Pathways
    - Establish clear, proportionate guidelines for AI-enabled clinical tools, including non-device software
    - Maintain patient safety and trust
  - Align Reimbursement with Outcomes
    - Create payment policies that incentivize technologies proven to reduce complications and improve efficiency
  - Invest in Interoperability and Research
    - Support initiatives that enhance data liquidity
    - Fund applied research to validate AI's impact on clinical outcomes, particularly in complex care scenarios such as obstetrics
  - Prioritize Equity
    - Ensure implementation strategies include support for resource-limited settings to prevent widening disparities

- Conclusion
  - AI can be a powerful tool to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes, reduce costs, and enhance provider efficiency
  - By modernizing regulations, aligning reimbursement, and investing in interoperability and research, HHS can accelerate safe and equitable adoption of AI in clinical care

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Perinatal AI company whose software supported 800,000 births in 2025 urges HHS to modernize regulations, align reimbursement with outcomes, and prioritize equity to accelerate safe AI adoption in maternal care.

---

- **Type:** Business

- **Geographic Scope:** National (serves 22% of U.S. hospitals providing childbirth services)

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We believe AI can significantly improve maternal and neonatal outcomes, reduce costs, and enhance provider efficiency, but regulatory uncertainty, payment misalignment, and data fragmentation are blocking adoption. HHS should modernize frameworks and align incentives to accelerate safe, equitable AI implementation—particularly in high-acuity areas like obstetrics where preventive technologies can reduce preventable adverse events.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Modernize regulatory pathways
  - Establish clear, proportionate guidelines for AI-enabled clinical tools, including non-device software
  - Maintain patient safety and trust while reducing delays that discourage innovation
- Align reimbursement with outcomes
  - Create payment policies that incentivize technologies proven to reduce complications and improve efficiency
  - Move beyond fee-for-service models that fail to reward preventive technologies
- Invest in interoperability and research
  - Support initiatives that enhance data liquidity across fragmented systems
  - Fund applied research to validate AI's impact on clinical outcomes in complex care scenarios like obstetrics
- Prioritize equity
  - Include support for resource-limited settings in implementation strategies
  - Prevent widening disparities for smaller providers and rural hospitals

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Regulatory uncertainty for non-device software
  - Current frameworks lack clarity, creating delays and discouraging innovation
- Payment misalignment
  - Fee-for-service models rarely incentivize preventive technologies
  - AI tools that reduce complications aren't recognized within value-based care programs
- Data interoperability gaps
  - Fragmented systems and inconsistent standards limit AI effectiveness
  - Lack of intentional workflow integration pathways constrains market opportunities
- Equity risks
  - Without clear guidance, smaller providers and rural hospitals may struggle to implement AI
  - Could exacerbate existing health disparities

---

- Scale of real-world deployment: PeriGen's AI obstetric software supported 800,000 births in 2025
- Market penetration data: FDA-cleared products are used in 22% of American hospitals providing childbirth services
- Focus on prevention: Predictive analytics can identify early warning signs of maternal and fetal complications before adverse events occur
- Hidden costs of inaction: Preventable adverse events impose financial, emotional, and reputational costs on patients, families, communities, and providers alike

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- It's time to let AIs prescribe medications
- ChatGPT is already a better doctor than 80% of human doctors I can go to
  - It's never out of network
  - I'm never going to get hit by a $400 bill on what should have been a $20 visit
    - This happened because my insurance company's website was down
    - I had no way to find out if a doctor took my insurance
    - His office wouldn't tell me
- Let the people choose
  - It's despicable that the AMA (a doctor cartel) controls what access to medicine we can only get through doctors
  - Of course they are going to gate every single thing from us to bleed us dry
- The only way we can get costs down meaningfully is to start building a low cost alternative system from scratch and let the people choose
- My insurance bill went up 20% this year and coverage decreased
- Let me opt out of this nonsense
  - I'll take ChatGPT plus generics from India any day over what we have
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A frustrated consumer fed up with rising insurance costs and billing surprises wants AI systems like ChatGPT authorized to prescribe medications as a low-cost alternative to the current physician-controlled system.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Sergey Alexashenko
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Healthcare consumer with direct experience navigating insurance and billing issues
- **Geographic Scope:** National (United States)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected by healthcare costs, insurance complexity, and access barriers to medical care
structuredSections.corePosition: I believe AI like ChatGPT should be allowed to prescribe medications because it would give consumers a low-cost alternative to the current system, which I see as controlled by a "doctor cartel" that gates access to medicine to maximize profits. The only way to meaningfully reduce healthcare costs is to build an alternative system and let people choose.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Authorize AI systems to prescribe medications
- Build a low-cost alternative healthcare system from scratch
- Allow consumers to opt out of traditional insurance-based healthcare
- Let people choose between AI-assisted care and traditional physician care
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The AMA functions as a cartel controlling access to medicine
  - Physicians gate every service to maximize revenue
- Current healthcare system is unaffordable
  - Insurance premiums increased 20% this year while coverage decreased
  - Unexpected billing surprises (e.g., $400 bill for what should be $20 visit)
- Insurance system is opaque and dysfunctional
  - Insurance company websites go down
  - Doctor's offices won't confirm insurance acceptance
  - Network status impossible to verify
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Got hit with a $400 bill on what should have been a $20 visit because he couldn't verify if the doctor was in-network—the insurance website was down and the doctor's office refused to tell him
- Frames the AMA as a "cartel" that deliberately restricts access to maximize physician income—a provocative framing of professional licensing
- Proposes a specific alternative: ChatGPT plus generic medications from India as a complete replacement for traditional healthcare
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "ChatGPT is already a better doctor than 80% of human doctors I can go to. It's never out of network."
- "It's despicable that the AMA (a doctor cartel) controls what access to medicine we can only get through doctors. Of course they are going to gate every single thing from us to bleed us dry."
- "Let me opt out of this nonsense, I'll take ChatGPT + generics from India any day over what we have."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- This is a bad idea
- It may be good to double check basic lab data
  - Acknowledges potential limited utility for straightforward data verification
- Doesn't see AI being able to suss out subtle nuances that may affect treatment of certain individuals
  - Concern about AI's ability to handle complexity and individual variation in patient care
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A skeptical individual sees limited value in AI for basic lab checks but doubts its ability to handle the subtle clinical nuances that affect individual patient treatment.

structuredSections.corePosition: I think this is a bad idea overall. While AI might help verify basic lab data, it can't pick up on the subtle nuances that matter when treating individual patients.

  - Concern about one-size-fits-all approach to complex clinical decisions
- Overall skepticism about the proposal's merit

- Highlights the gap between what AI can technically process and what clinical care actually requires
