structuredSections.detailedContent:
- AI in clinical care will support Observation Grouping to group clinical findings
  - Reference: https://build.fhir.org/observation.html#obsgrouping
- Grouping panels for clinical findings is becoming a bigger need for clinicians
  - Applies to laboratory, imaging, procedures, pathology, genomics
  - Clinicians need ability to group and filter these findings
- Benefits of observation grouping
  - Will help reduce clinician burden
  - Will help reduce administrative burden
- Potential stakeholders interested in this future effort
  - NLM (National Library of Medicine)
  - Terminology standards development organizations
    - SNOMED International
    - Regenstrief Institute
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Individual commenter advocates for AI-supported FHIR Observation Grouping capabilities to help clinicians organize clinical findings across multiple domains and reduce burden.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** David Rocha
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Not specified; demonstrates familiarity with FHIR standards and clinical terminology organizations
- **Geographic Scope:** Not specified
- **Stake in Issue:** Interest in clinical AI capabilities and reducing clinician/administrative burden
structuredSections.corePosition: AI in clinical care should support FHIR Observation Grouping functionality to help clinicians group and filter clinical findings across laboratory, imaging, procedures, pathology, and genomics. This capability would reduce both clinician and administrative burden.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Implement AI support for Observation Grouping as defined in FHIR standards
  - Enable grouping panels for clinical findings across multiple domains (laboratory, imaging, procedures, pathology, genomics)
  - Provide filtering capabilities for clinicians
- Engage terminology standards development organizations in this effort
  - NLM
  - SNOMED International
  - Regenstrief Institute
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Growing need for clinicians to group and filter clinical findings across multiple domains
- Clinician burden and administrative burden need reduction
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Points to specific FHIR specification (Observation Grouping) as technical foundation for AI-enabled clinical finding organization
- Identifies specific standards organizations that could contribute to this effort
structuredSections.keyQuotations: No standout quotations

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Building upon previous comment on grouping and filtering
- Proposing an AI-assisted physician review layer
  - Would use structured data grouping and terminology reasoning
  - Would give clinicians prioritized, specialty-specific review findings
  - Findings would need review, acknowledgement, and confirmation
- Core tension identified: all individual observations versus information that matters now
- Cross-specialist review capability
  - AI and other physicians in care team would know what other physicians already reviewed
  - Creates visibility across the care team
- Current problem: forcing physicians and clinicians to review everything is exhausting
- Proposed solution: only present information that actually requires review
  - AI could assist to verify what matters at that point
- AI-assisted physician review should prioritize clinical findings that are grouped/filtered for acknowledgement
  - Would replace manual review of entire records
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Individual commenter proposes an AI-assisted physician review layer that would prioritize and filter clinical findings for acknowledgement rather than forcing exhausting manual review of entire records.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** David Rocha
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Appears to have clinical workflow knowledge; references previous comment on grouping and filtering
- **Geographic Scope:** Not specified
- **Stake in Issue:** Concerned with reducing clinician burden and improving efficiency of health information review
structuredSections.corePosition: The current approach of forcing physicians to manually review everything is exhausting and unsustainable. An AI-assisted review layer could solve this by using structured data grouping and terminology reasoning to surface only the information that actually matters at any given point, with cross-specialist visibility.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Implement an AI-assisted physician review layer that:
  - Uses structured data grouping and terminology reasoning
  - Prioritizes specialty-specific findings requiring acknowledgement
  - Tracks which physicians have already reviewed specific information
  - Presents only information requiring actual review rather than entire records
- Enable cross-specialist review visibility so care team members know what others have already reviewed
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Physicians and clinicians are currently forced to review everything, which is exhausting
- Tension exists between reviewing all individual observations versus focusing on information that matters now
- Manual review of entire records is inefficient and unsustainable
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a specific workflow problem: the gap between "all individual observations" and "information that matters now"
- Proposes cross-specialist review tracking as a coordination mechanism—AI would help the care team know what's already been reviewed by whom
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "It's the tension of all the individual observations versus the information that matters now"
- "Instead of forcing physicians and clinicians to review everything (which is exhausting), only present the information that actually requires review"

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Declaration and author information
  - Views expressed are author's own, not representing affiliated institutions
  - Independent research synthesizing stakeholder submissions and academic literature
  - Portions drafted with AI writing assistance, all content reviewed and verified by author
  - Revised version will be submitted for peer-reviewed publication
  - Final responsibility for content verification rests with readers
  - Author credentials: Independent researcher with expertise in AI governance and healthcare policy
  - Educational background: International MBA from Bar-Ilan University, Israel; MSIT from Touro College, NY

- Stakeholder submissions analyzed
  - ADLM, AORN, BlueHalo, DSI, Ellipsis Health, Global Liver Institute, IAC, Interstella, National MS Society, SANCIAN LLC, SKAF, Van Pelt & Company
  - Total of 15 stakeholder submissions reviewed

- Key recommendations summary
  - Regulation: Tiered, risk-based governance frameworks for non-device AI aligned with NIST AI RMF; clarify accountability through named AI executive owners; issue guidance on liability allocation; create safe harbors for defined governance practices
  - Reimbursement: Expand CMS pathways for AI-supported assessment, monitoring, and outreach; align value-based payment models to recognize AI-enabled activities reducing total cost of care
  - R&D: Invest in implementation science; support public-private partnerships for shared infrastructure; fund workforce impact and equity research

- Introduction
  - AI integration into clinical care represents transformative opportunity to enhance healthcare delivery, reduce costs, improve patient outcomes
  - AI-enabled tools can enhance productivity, reduce burden, lower costs, improve outcomes for patients, caregivers, and communities
  - Realizing potential requires addressing systemic barriers impeding adoption
  - AI applications span continuum from administrative workflow optimization to clinical decision support to autonomous diagnostic systems
  - Generative AI and large language models introduce new capabilities and risks, particularly in mental health where chatbots may operate in regulatory gray areas
  - WHO has emphasized need for ethics and governance guidance for large multi-modal models
  - Recent regulatory developments in EU and U.S. reflect growing recognition of AI's unique oversight requirements

- Proposed architectural frameworks for clinical AI integration
  - Shared Capacity Data Utility Model (Van Pelt & Company)
    - Vendor-agnostic, public-private infrastructure leveraging existing systems and non-PHI operational data
    - Provides continuously available views of healthcare system capacity
    - Addresses gap where AI tools deployed as point solutions are blind to system-level constraints like staffing shortages, bed availability, regional surge conditions
    - Operates across multiple care settings:
      - Behavioral health: Real-time visibility into bed availability and staffing enables faster placement decisions, reduces ED boarding
      - Rural care: Supports decision-making about local management versus transfer to higher-acuity care, addresses equity concerns
      - Statewide coordination: Continuous situational awareness for preparedness, surge response, federal program alignment
      - Regional multi-state coordination: Interoperability across jurisdictional boundaries for disaster response and medical surge
    - Aligns with H.R. 2936 for real-time hospital capacity visibility and S. 1974 for healthcare system readiness coordination
    - Reflects EU AI Act principles regarding proportionate, risk-based oversight

  - Data Refinery Architecture for AI Readiness (Interstella's Lynqsys platform)
    - "Data Refinery as a Service" (DRaaS) model emphasizing real-time data integration
    - Addresses barriers: delayed data availability, inconsistent data quality, limited support for unstructured data
    - Key components:
      - Real-time data streams: Near-real-time streaming architectures replacing batch processes with days/weeks lag
      - Inline data governance: Privacy protections and quality checks at point of data creation rather than post-hoc audits
      - AI-ready data repositories: Shared datasets and testbeds for AI training and evaluation
      - Support for unstructured data: Integration of clinical notes, images, behavioral health data
    - Incorporates federated learning approaches for anonymizing data and training AI models through decentralized repositories

  - Continuous Monitoring and Post-Deployment Surveillance Framework (Dr. Akshaya Bhagavathula)
    - "Post-Deployment Algorithmic Surveillance" (PDAS) framework
    - Governs clinical AI as population-level interventions rather than discrete products
    - Recognizes AI systems reshape decisions, attention allocation, workflows, care delivery across patient populations
    - Five core elements:
      - Exposure characterization: Systematic measurement of who is exposed to AI-mediated decisions, how often, in what contexts
      - Continuous population-level performance monitoring: Longitudinal evaluation of calibration, discrimination, error patterns with mandatory stratification by key subgroups
      - Outcome-linked evaluation: Assessment of whether AI use improves downstream clinical outcomes, access, utilization patterns
      - Structured reporting of algorithmic harms: Mechanisms to capture delayed care, silent misclassification, workflow distortions, disparity amplification
      - Predefined regulatory response pathways: Clear protocols for recalibration, restriction, suspension, or withdrawal
    - Aligns with FDA guidance on predetermined change control plans for continuously learning algorithms
    - Treats performance drift as safety signal rather than technical footnote

  - Evidence-Based Medicine on FHIR (EBMonFHIR) Architecture (SKAF)
    - Interoperable framework for representing scientific knowledge to support AI-generated clinical recommendations
    - Builds on HL7 FHIR standards
    - Enables human-interpretable and machine-interpretable representation of evidence
    - Addresses FDA guidance requiring healthcare professionals to independently review basis for recommendations
    - Implementation Guide defines approximately 100 profiles, extensions, and value sets for representing:
      - Citations for identification, location, classification, attribution of knowledge artifacts
      - Research study design components including eligibility criteria and endpoint analysis plans
      - Research results including statistic findings, variable definitions, evidence certainty
      - Aggregation and synthesis of research results across multiple studies
      - Judgments regarding evidence syntheses and contextual factors
      - Recommendations with supporting evidence
    - SummaryOfNetEffect Profile combines data elements for clinical decisions:
      - Population specification
      - Intervention and comparator definitions
      - Outcome considerations with risk estimates and effect changes
      - Desirability and relative importance weighting
      - Net effect estimates combining contributions
    - Enables transparent, auditable AI recommendations clinicians can independently evaluate
    - Addresses concerns about generative AI producing contextually incorrect rationales to support predetermined answers
    - Supports individualized shared decision-making through tools like NetBenefitCalculator
    - Creates "GPS for healthcare" analogous to navigation software functioning through common data sharing expectations

  - Reference Architecture for Non-Medical Device AI (BlueHalo LLC)
    - Describes how evaluation, monitoring, interoperability, and governance components integrate across AI lifecycle
    - Addresses reality that many AI tools operate outside traditional regulatory pathways yet significantly influence clinical workflows
    - Components:
      - Evaluation layer: Pre-deployment testing in controlled environments combined with continuous post-deployment assessment across diverse populations and settings
      - Monitoring layer: Continuous drift detection, performance tracking, safety signal surveillance with automated flagging of deviations
      - Interoperability layer: Extended beyond data exchange to include metadata, data provenance, evaluation artifacts, governance policies
      - Governance layer: Clear role definitions, oversight mechanisms, escalation paths
    - Recognizes AI performance and safety depend on data quality, interoperability, human oversight, organizational context rather than model characteristics alone
    - Supports safe harbors for organizations implementing defined governance practices

  - Clinical Governance and Accountability Framework (Sancian LLC)
    - Operational framework emphasizing named accountability structures and readiness assessment before deployment
    - Key elements:
      - Named AI Executive Owner model: Single accountable leader with cross-functional authority to own AI outcomes
      - Cross-functional AI governance councils: Formalized structures with defined decision rights including clinical, technical, compliance, patient safety leadership
      - AI ReadyCheck™ assessment: Organizational readiness baseline evaluating data governance, workflow optimization, change management capacity, leadership alignment before technology procurement
      - AI-EQUITYClear™ assessment: Equity impact evaluation before deployment to identify potential disparities across demographic subgroups
      - Decision clarity before tooling: Governance infrastructure established before product evaluation
    - Incorporates "AI SoftLife™ philosophy": AI as capacity infrastructure that reduces cognitive burden, protects clinicians' energy, sustains humane care delivery
    - Measures success through cognitive load reduction, time returned to patient care, clinician trust rather than technical performance alone

  - Laboratory Data Harmonization Infrastructure (ADLM)
    - Infrastructure for harmonizing clinical laboratory test results to enable AI interoperability across institutions
    - Addresses critical barrier: different testing platforms produce varying numeric results for same analyte, degrading model performance when data pooled
    - Proposed infrastructure includes:
      - Standardized calibration protocols across assay systems
      - Metadata inclusion for test methods and validation parameters in AI models
      - Uniform guidelines for sample collection and reporting
      - Validation requirements for AI models across diverse clinical settings
      - Continuous quality monitoring including daily quality control, proficiency testing, trend analysis
    - Ensures AI systems using laboratory data can accurately interpret results regardless of testing site or method

  - Dental AI Standards and Validation Framework (Dental Standards Institute)
    - National dental AI trust framework addressing unique challenges in dentistry:
      - Diagnostic coding inconsistently used
      - Imaging workflows vary widely
      - Clinical documentation largely unstructured
      - Dental and medical records remain disconnected
    - Proposed framework includes:
      - Standardized validation datasets across multiple imaging devices and clinical settings
      - Third-party testing and certification mechanisms
      - Minimum transparency requirements for dental AI vendors
      - Standardized performance reporting formats
      - Audit log requirements for AI recommendations in clinical workflows
      - Guidance for post-deployment monitoring and model drift surveillance
    - Supports modernization of diagnosis capture in dentistry
    - Emphasizes "self-validation is not validation" and promotes independent benchmarking

  - Integration of proposed architectures
    - Frameworks are complementary and can form comprehensive AI governance and implementation ecosystem
    - Successful clinical AI adoption requires coordinated investment across multiple dimensions:
      - Data infrastructure: Shared capacity utilities, data refineries, laboratory harmonization, domain-specific frameworks
      - Standards: EBMonFHIR and similar interoperability standards for consistent representation
      - Monitoring: PDAS frameworks for continuous evaluation of performance and safety
      - Governance: Reference architectures and clinical governance frameworks for accountability structures
    - Treats AI as care infrastructure requiring coordinated governance rather than isolated technology deployment

- Technical architecture review of proposed AI frameworks
  - Taxonomy of AI models and agents in clinical architectures
    - Generative AI (LLMs): Conversational interfaces, clinical documentation; mental health chatbots, ambient listening, clinical note generation
    - Agentic AI systems: Autonomous decision support, workflow orchestration; care coordination, prior authorization, patient outreach
    - Predictive ML models: Risk stratification, early warning systems; sepsis prediction, readmission risk, deterioration detection
    - Computer vision models: Imaging analysis, diagnostic support; radiology, pathology, dental imaging
    - NLP models: Unstructured data processing; clinical notes, social determinants, patient-reported outcomes
    - Reinforcement learning: Adaptive treatment optimization; chronic disease management, personalized care pathways
    - Federated learning models: Privacy-preserving distributed training; multi-institutional model development
    - Knowledge graph models: Clinical decision support, evidence synthesis; EBMonFHIR, guideline-based recommendations

  - Agentic AI architectures for clinical workflow automation
    - Autonomous AI agents capable of perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions
    - Key components:
      - Data integration layer: Aggregates structured and unstructured data from EHRs, claims systems, IoT devices, patient-reported sources
      - Agent orchestrator: Coordinates task allocation among specialized agents, manages agent communication, maintains system state, implements safety protocols and escalation pathways
      - Specialized agent pool: Scheduling agents, triage agents, care coordination agents, predictive analytics agents, patient outreach agents
      - Human-in-the-loop interface: Ensures autonomous actions reviewed by clinicians when exceeding confidence thresholds or involving high-risk decisions

  - Generative AI integration patterns
    - Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pattern
      - Combines LLMs with external knowledge bases to ground responses in verified clinical evidence
      - Technical components: Vector databases storing embeddings of clinical guidelines and research; retrieval mechanisms with hybrid search; context integration injecting retrieved evidence into LLM prompts
    - Fine-tuning for domain adaptation
      - Adapts base LLMs to clinical domains using specialized datasets
      - Fine-tuned models achieved 98% accuracy in detecting and responding empathetically to patient statements versus 69% for rules-based systems
      - Technical considerations: Curated clinical conversations annotated with safety markers and empathy scores; parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA and adapter techniques); rigorous validation across demographic subgroups
    - Guardrail pattern for safe deployment
      - Critical for safe deployment of generative AI in clinical settings
      - Multiple guardrail layers:
        - Input guardrails: Detect harmful inputs, off-topic queries, crisis indicators (suicidal ideation, self-harm)
        - Model guardrails: Constrain output generation through controlled decoding, safety classifiers, refusal mechanisms
        - Output guardrails: Validate generated content against clinical guidelines, toxicity filters, fact-checking systems
        - Escalation guardrails: Trigger human clinician involvement when inputs or outputs exceed safety thresholds

  - Federated learning architectures for privacy-preserving AI
    - Enables collaborative model training across institutions without centralizing sensitive patient data
    - Key technical components:
      - Local training: Each institution trains models on local data, generating gradient updates or model weights
      - Secure aggregation: Cryptographic protocols (homomorphic encryption, secure multi-party computation) combine updates without revealing individual contributions
      - Differential privacy: Noise injection ensures aggregated models cannot be reverse-engineered to extract patient-level information
      - Federated averaging: Central server computes weighted averages of local updates to produce improved global models

  - Data pipeline architectures for real-time AI
    - Sophisticated architectures minimizing latency while maintaining data quality and governance
    - Components: Real-time ingestion (Kafka/FHIR), stream processing (Flink/Spark), inline governance (validation and anonymization), raw data lake, feature store, model registry, ML inference, real-time dashboards, API gateway

  - Knowledge graph and semantic reasoning architectures
    - Integrates clinical studies, guidelines, outcomes, interventions with FHIR resources
    - Enables semantic reasoning and evidence synthesis through SummaryOfNetEffect Profile

  - Continuous monitoring and drift detection architectures
    - Inputs: Model predictions, ground truth outcomes, feature distributions, population demographics
    - Monitoring: Drift detection statistical tests, bias monitoring subgroup analysis, safety signal detection
    - Outputs: Drift/bias/safety alerts, response orchestrator triggering model recalibration, usage restriction, or model withdrawal

  - Technical requirements for clinical AI architectures
    - Data ingestion: HL7 FHIR APIs, Kafka streams, real-time ETL; latency <100ms, throughput >10K events/sec
    - Model serving: GPU/TPU inference, model versioning, A/B testing; inference time <500ms, availability 99.9%
    - Agent orchestration: Workflow engines, state management, inter-agent communication; task completion <2s, scalability to 1000+ concurrent agents
    - Guardrails: Safety classifiers, output validation, escalation triggers; false positive rate <1%, recall >95% for safety events
    - Monitoring: Statistical drift detection, bias metrics, safety surveillance; detection latency <1 hour, alert precision >90%
    - Federated learning: Secure aggregation, differential privacy, encrypted communication; privacy budget ε<2, model accuracy within 5% of centralized
    - Knowledge graphs: Graph databases, semantic reasoning, FHIR integration; query latency <200ms, support for 10M+ triples

  - Technical architecture summary themes
    - Modularity: Layered architectures enable independent evolution of data infrastructure, models, agents, governance
    - Safety-by-design: Guardrails, monitoring, response mechanisms integrated from ground up
    - Interoperability: FHIR-based standards ensure compatibility across institutions and systems
    - Scalability: Cloud-native architectures support deployment from small clinics to large health systems
    - Privacy-preservation: Federated learning and differential privacy enable collaborative development without data sharing

- Future of work with AI adoption: Workforce transformation, job displacement, and educational challenges
  - Workforce impact spectrum: Automation vs. augmentation
    - Reality lies on spectrum with different clinical roles experiencing varying degrees of transformation
    - Spectrum ranges from high automation potential (radiology technicians, pathology assistants) through moderate (pharmacists, nurses) to hybrid (physicians, surgeons) to augmentation/enhancement (therapists, social workers, care coordinators)
    - Most effective healthcare AI does not seek to replace clinicians or focus only on automation but serves as "force multiplier for human care" that "extends the reach of care teams"
    - AI framed as capacity infrastructure reducing cognitive burden, enabling clinicians to focus on higher-value patient interactions

  - Job displacement: Realities and mitigation strategies
    - AORN position: "AI is suitable for augmentation of clinical decision making, but AI cannot and must never be a replacement for a human's clinical judgement. The human expertise, empathy and accountability required for clinical decisionmaking in practice cannot be replicated"
    - Risk factors for displacement:
      - High risk: Routine pattern recognition, rule-based decision making, data-intensive processing, highly scalable tasks
      - Low risk: Empathy requirement, clinical judgment, legal accountability, human touch
    - Quantitative impact estimates:
      - Administrative roles: Administrative work consumes one-sixth of U.S. physicians' working hours; AI automation of prior authorization, documentation, scheduling could significantly reduce burden without displacing clinical staff
      - Diagnostic specialties: Radiology and pathology face highest automation potential for routine image interpretation, but demand may shift toward complex case consultation and AI system oversight
      - Nursing and care management: Nurses, care managers, social workers already managing unsustainable caseloads; AI adoption more likely to reduce burnout and enable focus on direct patient care than eliminate positions

  - Emerging job categories and role transformation
    - AI Clinical Governance Officer: Oversee AI deployment, ensure safety and equity, manage algorithm lifecycle
    - Clinical AI Specialist: Bridge between clinical practice and AI development, validate model outputs
    - AI Quality Assurance Analyst: Monitor model performance, detect drift and bias, conduct audits
    - Human-AI Collaboration Specialist: Optimize workflow integration, train teams on AI tools, manage change
    - AI Ethics & Equity Officer: Ensure fairness across populations, address algorithmic bias
    - Data Steward: Manage data quality, governance, interoperability for AI readiness
    - AI-Enhanced Care Coordinator: Leverage AI insights for patient outreach and care management

  - Educational and training requirements
    - AI education essential and should be implemented in academic settings and organizations where AI-enabled technology is used
    - Competency should be assessed before using any AI-enabled technology
    - Core educational competencies:
      - Technical literacy: Understanding AI capabilities, limitations, appropriate use cases
      - Critical evaluation: Assessing AI outputs, recognizing errors, knowing when to override recommendations
      - Ethical awareness: Understanding bias, fairness, transparency, accountability in AI systems
      - Workflow integration: Incorporating AI tools into clinical workflows without disruption
      - Patient communication: Explaining AI involvement to patients and addressing concerns
      - Continuous learning: Staying current with evolving AI capabilities and best practices
    - Education framework across career stages:
      - Medical/nursing students: AI fundamentals
      - Residents/trainees: Critical evaluation, AI tool proficiency
      - Practicing clinicians: Ethical considerations, workflow integration, continuous learning
      - Clinical leadership: Clinical decision support, performance monitoring, governance frameworks, bias detection, change management, strategic planning

  - Labor and employment considerations
    - Scope of practice and professional boundaries
      - Clinicians should be full partners in preparing for future of AI intersecting their practice settings
      - As AI assumes tasks traditionally performed by specific professional groups, scope of practice definitions may need revision
    - Liability and accountability
      - Unresolved questions regarding responsibility, liability, oversight when automated systems fail or risks go unrecognized
      - Key questions: Who is liable when clinician relies on harmful AI recommendation? How is accountability shared among developers, organizations, clinicians? What protections exist for clinicians who override AI recommendations that later prove correct?
      - Clinicians should be transparent with patients about when and how AI tools assist their care
      - Clear communication and clinician accountability essential to maintaining trust
    - Collective bargaining and worker voice
      - Mechanisms for worker input into AI deployment decisions becoming critical
      - Cross-functional AI governance councils include representation from clinical, technical, compliance, patient safety leadership
      - Formal labor representation in these bodies remains inconsistent
      - Smaller institutions don't know how to move forward with clinical AI; larger institutions are overly bureaucratic in making decisions
      - Disparity suggests collective bargaining and worker voice mechanisms may be more accessible in larger, unionized settings

  - Human-AI collaboration models
    - Shadow mode: AI observes, no action; used for training and validation
    - Assistive mode: AI suggests, human decides; used for decision support and low-risk tasks
    - Collaborative mode: AI and human co-decide; used for complex diagnosis
    - Delegated mode: Human delegates, AI acts; used for routine operations
    - Autonomous mode: AI acts, human monitors; used for well-validated low-risk scenarios
    - Appropriate mode depends on task complexity, risk, and validation
    - Most clinical applications operate in assistive or collaborative modes
    - AI tools that patients trust and interact with consistently are more likely to surface early warning signs and less likely to miss critical information

  - Psychological and emotional impacts on workers
    - Positive adoption outcomes: Reduced cognitive load, focus on higher-value care, reduced burnout, increased job satisfaction
    - Negative adoption outcomes: Anxiety about job security, deskilling concerns, distrust of AI systems, resistance to adoption
    - AI SoftLife™ philosophy addresses these concerns by designing AI as capacity infrastructure that reduces cognitive burden, protects clinicians' energy, sustains humane care delivery
    - Worker wellbeing is prerequisite for successful AI adoption, not secondary consideration

  - Recommendations for workforce transition
    - For healthcare organizations:
      - Establish governance structures with worker representation to guide AI deployment decisions
      - Invest in continuous education and training programs preparing workers for AI-augmented roles
      - Design AI for augmentation, not replacement, prioritizing tools that reduce burden and enhance human capabilities
      - Create career pathways into emerging AI-related roles, offering retraining and upskilling opportunities
      - Monitor psychological impacts and provide support for workers experiencing anxiety or resistance
    - For policymakers:
      - Fund workforce development programs specifically targeting AI-related skills in healthcare
      - Update scope of practice regulations to accommodate AI-augmented care models while protecting professional standards
      - Establish liability frameworks that fairly allocate responsibility among developers, organizations, clinicians
      - Support research on workforce impacts, including longitudinal studies of job transformation and displacement
      - Incentivize inclusive governance incorporating worker voice in AI deployment decisions
    - For educational institutions:
      - Integrate AI competencies across healthcare curricula, from foundational education through continuing professional development
      - Develop interdisciplinary programs combining clinical expertise with data science and AI governance
      - Create simulation environments where students can safely learn to collaborate with AI systems
      - Emphasize ethical reasoning and critical evaluation skills alongside technical proficiency

- Current landscape of AI in clinical care
  - AI-enabled medical devices are routine part of clinical practice and many patients' lives
  - Applications include tumor detection on imaging scans, abnormal heart rhythm identification, digital therapeutic apps for chronic disease management
  - Generative AI has expanded applications to conversational agents for mental health support, clinical documentation assistance, predictive analytics for population health management
  - Adoption landscape remains highly uneven
  - Health and healthcare AI tools grow in liminal regulatory space, wedged uncomfortably between ambits of regulatory agencies
  - Regulatory ambiguity creates uncertainty for developers and healthcare organizations, potentially slowing innovation while exposing patients to unmitigated risks

- Regulatory frameworks and governance challenges
  - Federal oversight mechanisms
    - Regulatory landscape fragmented across multiple agencies and frameworks
    - FDA exercises jurisdiction over AI-enabled medical devices through premarket review pathways
    - Recent guidance addresses predetermined change control plans for continuously learning algorithms
    - Most generative AI-powered wellness apps will not be reviewed by health regulators, creating regulatory gray area
    - FDA Digital Health Advisory Committee has considered generative AI therapy chatbots for depression, signaling risk-based framework for mental health tools
    - Aligns with international approaches including EU AI Act addressing risks to health, safety, fundamental rights
  - State-level legislative developments
    - State-level legislation emerged as additional governance layer
    - Among 793 state bills reviewed, 143 identified as potentially impactful to mental health AI, with 20 enacted across 11 states
    - Patchwork of state approaches creates compliance complexity for developers while potentially leaving gaps in patient protections
    - Most laws treat mental health as incidental to broader AI or healthcare regulation; explicit mental health provisions remain rare
  - International regulatory approaches
    - Australia introduced national AI plan shaping investment, policy, business approaches
    - Canada developed compendium of best practices for human-centered AI development in world of work
    - UK's Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency responded to Regulatory Horizons Council with guidance on AI as medical device

- Barriers to AI adoption in clinical care
  - Data readiness and interoperability gaps
    - Data fragmentation and quality issues represent primary barriers
    - Lack of harmonization among clinical data sources degrades model performance when data pooled across institutions
    - Limits algorithm generalizability
    - AI regulatory framework formation requires convergence of ethical, regulatory, technical, societal, practical considerations with data standardization as foundational element
    - Data infrastructure critical for enabling autonomous AI systems capable of real-time clinical decision support
    - Fragmentation particularly acute in specialized domains like dentistry where data fragmented across practice management systems, imaging systems, payer workflows, unstructured clinical notes
  - Misaligned incentives and payment barriers
    - Current payment models often fail to capture value generated by AI tools preventing adverse events or improving care coordination
    - In fee-for-service environments, preventing hospitalizations may reduce revenue rather than creating it
    - Challenging for health systems to justify near-term investment in AI
    - Current payment models may not adequately capture long-term savings from preventive AI applications
    - Frameworks treating AI tools as static products fail to account for dynamic, service-oriented nature
    - Mismatch between static product logic and dynamic service reality complicates classification, evidence generation, post-market oversight
  - Workforce constraints and change management
    - Healthcare organizations operate under unprecedented workforce strain
    - Administrative work consumes one-sixth of U.S. physicians' working hours, underscoring potential value of AI-enabled workflow optimization
    - Initial AI adoption requires significant change management: workflow redesign, training and trust-building, governance and oversight processes
    - AI implementation must consider clinician cognitive load and workflow integration as critical success factors
    - AI that adds cognitive burden rather than reducing it creates friction regardless of technical performance
    - Exhausted care teams cannot absorb poorly designed tools
  - Governance uncertainty for non-device AI
    - Significant portion of AI used in care delivery does not meet definition of regulated medical device yet influences clinical prioritization and care coordination
    - Health systems struggle with unclear liability and accountability frameworks, uncertainty around appropriate clinical oversight, lack of accepted frameworks for AI system governance and assessment
    - Existing frameworks overlook AI's impact on human relationships and fail to establish clear duties of care toward users
    - Need for rigorous evaluation across credibility, user experience, crisis management, transparency domains
  - Patient trust and transparency concerns
    - Patients consistently express concern that increasing reliance on automated systems may erode human connection central to effective, compassionate health care
    - Privacy, informed consent, patient autonomy, health equity identified as central ethical challenges requiring regulatory attention
    - Patient trust depends on transparency, accountability, meaningful human oversight

- Evaluation methodologies for clinical AI
  - Pre-deployment evaluation frameworks
    - Several evaluation frameworks and reporting guidelines developed to facilitate healthcare organizations' evaluation of AI-enabled technology before widespread integration
    - Include model cards (standardized documents explaining key characteristics), expert consensus guidelines for reporting AI development and validation, evaluation frameworks for healthcare professionals
    - FAITA-Mental Health framework demonstrates application of systematic evaluation to generative AI platforms
    - Scores tools on credibility, user experience, crisis management, user agency, health equity, transparency
    - Effectively identifies key strengths and gaps in AI-driven mental health tools, highlighting need for stringent standards
  - Post-deployment monitoring and continuous evaluation
    - AI performance in healthcare is dynamic
    - Real-world safety depends not only on pre-deployment validation but on ongoing monitoring, bias detection, clinical review
    - Randomized controlled trial of generative AI digital mental health intervention provides initial blueprint for technical and conversational guardrails that can be replicated
    - Continuous monitoring architectures and automated compliance systems important for maintaining AI safety and effectiveness over time
    - FDA guidance on predetermined change control plans enables manufacturers to update AI-enabled devices without filing additional submissions, supporting continuous improvement while maintaining safety oversight
  - Human-centered and workflow-based evaluation
    - AI in clinical care operates within complex clinical workflows and human decision-making environments
    - Effective evaluation approaches include:
      - Workflow impact assessments to understand how AI changes care team behavior
      - Cognitive load and alert fatigue analysis
      - Qualitative feedback from frontline clinicians
      - Evaluation of how AI supports decision-making rather than overrides it
    - Core elements include formal AI governance committees, patient transparency, bias monitoring for safe AI implementation
    - Many AI failures in healthcare are not algorithmic failures but integration failures

- Policy recommendations
  - Modernizing payment models
    - Payment policy reform essential to incentivize AI adoption
    - Expand CMS reimbursement pathways for AI-supported assessment, monitoring, outreach in chronic disease management, behavioral health screening, transitional care
    - Value-based payment models should recognize AI-enabled activities that demonstrably reduce total cost of care
    - Value-based payment models essential for capturing AI's preventive benefits
  - Establishing clear governance frameworks
    - Clear governance frameworks for non-device AI needed to reduce uncertainty without over-regulating low-risk applications
    - Tiered, risk-based governance pathways aligned with frameworks such as NIST AI Risk Management Framework
    - Lower-risk administrative AI following streamlined pathways
    - Higher-risk clinical decision support requiring rigorous evaluation
    - Governance frameworks should distinguish between different risk levels and application contexts
    - Integrate technical standards, risk management protocols, policy recommendations
  - Supporting independent validation and certification
    - Independent validation and certification mechanisms can promote trust and accelerate adoption
    - Support third-party audit mechanisms
    - Standardized evaluation metrics
    - Independent testing datasets representing diverse populations
    - Mandatory subgroup performance reporting
  - Enhancing interoperability and data standards
    - Enhanced interoperability essential for AI development and deployment
    - Priority areas:
      - Integrating clinical, behavioral, and social data
      - Enabling longitudinal, near-real-time data access
      - Standardizing care management and engagement workflows
      - Supporting shared evaluation and benchmarking
  - Patient-centered AI design
    - Patient-centered AI design must prioritize transparency, engagement, trust
    - Plain-language disclosure standards for AI use in care delivery
    - Guidance emphasizing AI as extension of care team rather than replacement
    - Research into patient understanding and trust related to AI-supported care
    - Patients should retain ownership of personal health data with clear protections

- Research priorities
  - Implementation science for AI in real-world care delivery
    - Largest evidence gap is not whether AI can work but how it works when deployed at scale across diverse settings and populations
    - Priority research areas:
      - AI integration into clinical workflows
      - Change management and adoption dynamics among care teams
      - Longitudinal performance and safety monitoring
      - Organizational governance models for AI oversight
  - Equity and bias mitigation
    - Equitable AI requires evaluation beyond controlled datasets
    - Research priorities:
      - Performance monitoring across race, ethnicity, age, language, socioeconomic status
      - Stress-testing AI performance in populations with low digital literacy or limited access
      - Ongoing bias audits rather than one-time assessments
    - Importance of addressing algorithmic bias and ensuring equitable access
  - Workforce impact and sustainability
    - AI research has historically focused on patient outcomes while under-examining workforce effects
    - Priority areas:
      - Impact of AI on clinician workload and burnout
      - Redistribution of tasks across care teams
      - Training and competency development for AI-supported care
    - AI adoption will not scale if it increases burden or undermines workforce sustainability
  - Cost-benefit analysis and economic impact
    - Literature on AI cost impact remains fragmented and inconsistent
    - Research priorities:
      - Total cost of care impact over time
      - Attribution models for AI-enabled prevention
      - Financial effects across different payment models
      - Labor costs for equivalent tasks performed by AI
    - Clear evidence of cost reduction essential to align incentives and accelerate adoption

- Conclusion
  - Key findings
    - Primary barriers to AI adoption are not technical but structural: data interoperability gaps, fragmented governance, misaligned reimbursement incentives, workforce constraints
    - "AI systems do not fail because of algorithms; they fail because of data"
    - Stakeholder-proposed architectural frameworks offer coherent roadmap for addressing barriers
    - Successful AI governance requires named accountability structures
    - Future of work with AI presents both challenges and opportunities; predominant perspective emphasizes augmentation over replacement
    - Emerging roles require new educational competencies spanning technical literacy, critical evaluation, ethical awareness, workflow integration
  - Patient safety as foundation
    - Patient safety must remain cornerstone of AI deployment in healthcare
    - Most effective AI systems operate within clearly defined clinical guardrails, incorporate human oversight, are continuously monitored in real-world use
    - Engagement and empathy are safety features
    - AI tools that patients trust and interact with consistently are more likely to detect emerging concerns and less likely to miss critical signals
    - Empathy is not merely usability feature but patient safety imperative
  - Policy imperatives
    - HHS has unique and critical role in accelerating safe and effective AI adoption
    - Federal leadership should:
      - Align reimbursement with prevention and engagement
      - Clarify governance expectations for non-device AI
      - Support standardized evaluation and accreditation frameworks
      - Reduce administrative friction and duplication through mutual recognition of credible private-sector accreditation programs
      - Invest in real-world implementation science and public-private partnerships
      - Fund workforce development programs targeting AI-related skills across career stages
  - Call to action
    - AI represents rare opportunity to advance shared goals of patient safety, better outcomes, workforce sustainability, cost containment
    - Requires:
      - Patient-centered design grounded in empathy and trust
      - Strong clinical guardrails and continuous quality assurance
      - Incentives that reward prevention rather than volume
      - Federal policies that reduce uncertainty and accelerate adoption
  - Final reflection
    - "Clinicians should be full partners in preparing for the future of AI that intersects their respective practice settings"
    - Partnership approach—treating workers, patients, communities as collaborators rather than passive recipients of technological change—offers most promising path
    - Architectural frameworks, governance structures, policy recommendations provide roadmap for future where AI enhances rather than diminishes human dimensions of healthcare
    - Next 24-36 months represent narrow window to embed these principles before AI becomes irreversible infrastructure
    - With thoughtful leadership and collaboration, AI can help make healthcare safer, more humane, more affordable for all Americans

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
An independent researcher synthesizes 15 stakeholder submissions and academic literature into a comprehensive policy roadmap urging HHS to establish tiered governance frameworks, modernize payment models to reward prevention, and invest in shared data infrastructure to accelerate safe, equitable AI adoption in clinical care.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Satyadhar Joshi, Independent Researcher
- **Type:** Academic/Research
- **Role/Expertise:** Independent researcher with expertise in AI governance and healthcare policy; International MBA from Bar-Ilan University, Israel; MSIT from Touro College, NY; ORCID: 0009-0002-6011-5080
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Academic interest in informing federal AI policy through systematic analysis of stakeholder perspectives and peer-reviewed literature; no institutional affiliation

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
I believe AI has reached a point of maturity where it can meaningfully improve patient safety, strengthen care delivery, and reduce total cost of care—but only if deployed with empathy, accountability, and strong clinical governance. The primary barriers to AI adoption are not technical but structural: data interoperability gaps, fragmented governance across federal and state agencies, misaligned reimbursement incentives that reward volume over prevention, and workforce constraints. The greatest opportunity for AI in clinical care lies not in automation for its own sake, but in augmenting human care teams, engaging patients earlier and more consistently, and preventing avoidable deterioration before it becomes costly or harmful.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Regulation
  - Establish tiered, risk-based governance frameworks for non-device AI aligned with NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  - Lower-risk administrative AI should follow streamlined pathways; higher-risk clinical decision support requires rigorous evaluation
  - Clarify accountability through named AI executive owners with cross-functional authority
  - Issue guidance on liability allocation among developers, organizations, and clinicians
  - Create safe harbors for organizations implementing defined governance practices

- Reimbursement
  - Expand CMS pathways for AI-supported assessment, monitoring, and outreach in chronic disease management, behavioral health screening, and transitional care
  - Align value-based payment models to recognize AI-enabled activities that demonstrably reduce total cost of care
  - Shift from fee-for-service models that penalize prevention to models that reward it

- Research and Development
  - Invest in implementation science to understand how AI works when deployed at scale across diverse settings and populations
  - Support public-private partnerships for shared infrastructure including data utilities and evaluation testbeds
  - Fund workforce impact and equity research including longitudinal studies of job transformation and algorithmic bias

- Interoperability and Data Standards
  - Integrate clinical, behavioral, and social data across systems
  - Enable longitudinal, near-real-time data access
  - Standardize care management and engagement workflows
  - Support shared evaluation and benchmarking datasets

- Independent Validation
  - Support third-party audit mechanisms and standardized evaluation metrics
  - Require independent testing datasets representing diverse populations
  - Mandate subgroup performance reporting across race, ethnicity, age, language, and socioeconomic status
  - Reduce administrative friction through mutual recognition of credible private-sector accreditation programs

- Patient-Centered Design
  - Establish plain-language disclosure standards for AI use in care delivery
  - Emphasize AI as extension of care team rather than replacement
  - Ensure patients retain ownership of personal health data with clear protections

- Workforce Development
  - Fund workforce development programs targeting AI-related skills across career stages
  - Update scope of practice regulations to accommodate AI-augmented care models
  - Integrate AI competencies across healthcare curricula from foundational education through continuing professional development

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Data readiness and interoperability gaps
  - Data fragmentation and quality issues represent primary barriers to AI adoption
  - Lack of harmonization among clinical data sources degrades model performance when data pooled across institutions
  - "AI systems do not fail because of algorithms; they fail because of data"
  - Fragmentation particularly acute in specialized domains like dentistry

- Misaligned incentives and payment barriers
  - Current payment models fail to capture value generated by AI tools preventing adverse events
  - In fee-for-service environments, preventing hospitalizations may reduce revenue rather than creating it
  - Frameworks treating AI tools as static products fail to account for their dynamic, service-oriented nature

- Workforce constraints and change management
  - Healthcare organizations operate under unprecedented workforce strain
  - Administrative work consumes one-sixth of U.S. physicians' working hours
  - Initial AI adoption requires significant change management that exhausted care teams cannot absorb
  - AI that adds cognitive burden rather than reducing it creates friction regardless of technical performance

- Governance uncertainty for non-device AI
  - Significant portion of AI used in care delivery does not meet definition of regulated medical device yet influences clinical prioritization
  - Health systems struggle with unclear liability and accountability frameworks
  - Existing frameworks overlook AI's impact on human relationships and fail to establish clear duties of care

- Patient trust and transparency concerns
  - Patients consistently express concern that increasing reliance on automated systems may erode human connection central to compassionate health care
  - Privacy, informed consent, patient autonomy, and health equity identified as central ethical challenges

- Fragmented regulatory landscape
  - Regulatory landscape fragmented across multiple agencies and frameworks
  - Most generative AI-powered wellness apps will not be reviewed by health regulators, creating regulatory gray area
  - Patchwork of state approaches creates compliance complexity while potentially leaving gaps in patient protections

- Equity and bias risks
  - AI performance must be monitored across race, ethnicity, age, language, and socioeconomic status
  - Ongoing bias audits needed rather than one-time assessments
  - Populations with low digital literacy or limited access require stress-testing

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Synthesized eight distinct architectural frameworks from stakeholder submissions into an integrated ecosystem
  - Shared Capacity Data Utility Model for real-time operational visibility across behavioral health, rural care, and emergency response
  - Post-Deployment Algorithmic Surveillance (PDAS) framework treating deployed AI as population-level interventions requiring continuous monitoring
  - EBMonFHIR architecture enabling transparent, auditable AI recommendations through standardized evidence representation
  - Named AI Executive Owner model preventing diffusion of responsibility across multiple stakeholders

- Identified that most clinical AI applications should operate in "assistive" or "collaborative" modes rather than autonomous modes
  - Shadow mode (AI observes, no action) for training and validation
  - Assistive mode (AI suggests, human decides) for decision support
  - Collaborative mode (AI and human co-decide) for complex diagnosis
  - Delegated and autonomous modes reserved for well-validated, low-risk scenarios

- Mapped emerging job categories created by AI adoption
  - AI Clinical Governance Officer, Clinical AI Specialist, AI Quality Assurance Analyst, Human-AI Collaboration Specialist, AI Ethics & Equity Officer, Data Steward, AI-Enhanced Care Coordinator

- Technical requirements quantified for clinical AI architectures
  - Data ingestion latency <100ms with throughput >10K events/sec
  - Model inference time <500ms with 99.9% availability
  - Guardrails requiring <1% false positive rate and >95% recall for safety events
  - Federated learning privacy budget ε<2 with model accuracy within 5% of centralized training

- Identified narrow implementation window
  - Next 24-36 months represent critical period to embed governance principles before AI becomes irreversible infrastructure

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI systems do not fail because of algorithms; they fail because of data."

- "AI is suitable for augmentation of clinical decision making, but AI cannot and must never be a replacement for a human's clinical judgement. The human expertise, empathy and accountability required for clinical decisionmaking in practice cannot be replicated."

- "Clinicians should be full partners in preparing for the future of AI that intersects their respective practice settings."

- "Self-validation is not validation."

- "The most effective healthcare AI does not seek to replace clinicians or focus only on automation but rather serves as a force multiplier for human care that extends the reach of care teams."

- "Empathy is not merely a usability feature but a patient safety imperative."
