structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Regulation of Health Care AI
  - AMA appreciates HHS' interest in evaluating regulatory approaches to health care AI
  - Appropriate oversight structures will ensure AI with clinical applications is safe, efficacious, promotes innovation, and builds trust between developers, physicians, and patients
  - Survey of over 1,000 physicians found appropriate regulatory oversight was one of the top needs when considering whether to integrate AI into practice
  - AMA has long supported movement towards a harmonized national governance approach for health care AI
    - This approach has been lacking to date
    - Would provide developers, deployers, physicians, and patients with clarity and consistency
    - Would help enhance understanding and build trust in AI-enabled health care technologies
  - Terminology has not been harmonized across the federal government
    - FDA, Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy, ONC, OCR, and CMS all use very different terminology to describe the same technologies and functions
    - Stakeholders including industry, physicians, and patients have long advocated for consistent regulatory terminology across HHS
    - Recommend HHS consider this need for harmonization in any updated regulatory approach
  - AMA has consistently advocated that regulation of health care AI should follow a risk-based approach
    - AI-enabled technologies posing low risk to patient health likely do not need stringent regulatory scrutiny before coming to market
    - AI posing higher levels of risk needs appropriate regulatory scrutiny to ensure safety and performance with appropriate guardrails
    - Every AI-enabled technology impacting medical decision-making should have a rigorous post-market surveillance plan
    - Developers and deployers should be able to identify algorithmic drift and ensure algorithms are not introducing bias
    - FDA-regulated device software functions, even those under enforcement discretion, should have mandated labeling requirements for transparency
    - A clear, consistent, risk-based approach to regulation (not significant deregulation) is required to enhance trust in AI and move towards clinical integration

- Transparency
  - Critical element of successful risk-mitigation that can enhance trust in AI
  - AMA recommends certain AI tools be subject to transparency mandates outlining intended use, how to use the tool, risks and limitations, and other elements
  - For AI with clinical applications, AMA policy recommends specific elements be disclosed to physicians for informed decisions about purchasing and using AI-enabled tools
  - For regulated software device functions, this could come as a labeling requirement
  - Pleased to see FDA's January 2025 draft guidance "Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions: Lifecycle Management and Marketing Submission Recommendations" includes recommendations for updated labeling
  - Urge Administration to finalize guidance regarding labeling requirements
  - Urge FDA to recommend this apply to software device functions under enforcement discretion
  - For clinical decision support software where physicians need to review the basis for recommendations, a consistent approach to transparency is essential
  - Transparency helps physicians successfully navigate AI use in practice
    - Necessary to help physicians understand the product and select the right tool for the right patient at the right time
    - While some physicians will develop significant AI expertise, expecting all physicians to deeply understand AI-enabled technologies is unreasonable
    - Especially important for physicians in small practices or underserved areas who lack evaluation resources of large health systems
    - Developers and deployers should provide information understandable to physicians of all experience levels with AI
  - Suggest a standardized format for disclosures across industry participants
    - Consistent approach makes it easier for all physicians to understand and compare technologies
  - While FDA has a large role in ensuring transparency for software device functions, other technologies should provide some level of transparency as well
    - FDA authority does not extend to many administrative AI tools, direct-to-consumer chatbots, and other wellness applications
    - Important that certain information is disclosed to users of those tools
    - Lower-risk tools do not need the significant transparency required of a medical device
    - End users including consumers should understand the tool's intended use, limitations, and that they are not interacting with a human
  - FTC would be the most appropriate regulatory body to address transparency issues related to consumer protection
    - Encourage HHS to work with FTC on guidance regarding transparency requirements furthering consumer protections

- Regulation of Consumer-Facing Autonomous AI
  - New direct-to-consumer health care technologies are being introduced at a rapid pace
  - These technologies hold significant promise to increase patient understanding of health and wellness, improve health literacy, and expand access to care
  - Many will seek to operate autonomously, including AI chatbots and AI agents
  - These technologies are promising but carry risks that should be mitigated
  - Autonomous AI will need appropriate guardrails, oversight, and transparency
  - Consumers should have very clear understanding they are not interacting with a human through regulatory mandates requiring clear disclosure
  - Transparency mandates should include prohibitions on claims or insinuations that a chatbot or agent is a licensed clinician or can provide the same services
  - Regulators, industry, physicians, and patients should work together to develop clear and enforceable guardrails that mitigate risks and establish appropriate scope of activities for autonomous AI
  - AMA appreciates and supports FTC's investigation into AI chatbots raising mental health concerns
  - Encourages HHS to work with Congress and FTC to ensure appropriate guardrails are in place to mitigate harm to consumers

- AI "Deep Fakes" and Impersonation of Physicians
  - Advances in generative AI now allow realistic impersonation of physicians through synthetic images, audio, and video ("deepfakes")
  - When used maliciously or without consent, these tools can spread medical misinformation, damage physicians' reputations and careers, and undermine patient trust in evidence-based care
  - Existing privacy, employment, and intellectual property laws do not adequately address these risks in clinical contexts
  - This is about patient safety, not technology restrictions
    - Medical deepfakes can convince patients to follow harmful advice, delay care, or abandon evidence-based treatment
    - Consumers cannot reasonably distinguish real clinicians from AI-generated impersonations without clear protections and labeling
    - Patients rely on trust signals they cannot independently verify (physician's voice, image, credentials) which function as trusted cues
    - Deepfakes exploit those cues, creating a uniquely high-risk form of consumer deception in health care
  - Urge Administration to work with Congress in developing protections focusing on key elements that protect the patient/consumer:
    - Preventing impersonation reduces health misinformation at the source
    - Shared responsibility protects consumers more effectively
    - Deepfake misuse disproportionately harms vulnerable patients
    - Transparency supports innovation and trust
    - Patients benefit from default protections
    - Protecting physicians ultimately protects patients

- Physician Liability for Use of AI
  - AMA pleased to see HHS' acknowledgment that AI use in clinical care raises novel and complex legal questions for patients, physicians, hospitals, health systems, and malpractice insurers
  - Issue of liability crosses both device and non-device AI-enabled technologies
  - Particularly important to physicians as they begin to engage with AI in more meaningful ways
  - In AMA's AI Physician Sentiment Survey, respondents identified liability concerns as one of their top concerns when considering implementing these technologies
  - Question of who is responsible for medical errors involving AI use is currently without precedent in case law
  - No prevailing legal frameworks exist to address the issue
  - Difficult situation for physicians to navigate with significant implications for successful clinical integration of AI
  - Use of AI impacting patient-physician medical decision-making does not fall neatly within current legal constructs like product liability approaches
  - If physicians bear disproportionate liability risk for medical errors where poorly performing AI played a role, it could disincentivize use of tools in clinical care
  - Increased liability risks may impact malpractice insurance, potentially raising premiums, increasing deductibles, or resulting in policy riders specifically including or excluding certain AI uses
  - As we move toward more personalized AI, it may become a meaningful participant in medical decision-making
    - AI that can evaluate personalized health information (medical records, diagnostic results, images, wearable data) presents a new type of shared decision-making between human physicians and algorithms
    - These capabilities represent a significant step towards realizing AI's potential but also present novel risks
    - If AI is marketed as capable of performing accurately in ways a physician cannot, it can play a valuable role
    - If AI returns an incorrect output, that error can have serious consequences for patients if it contributes to misdiagnosis or improper treatment
    - Question: who should bear responsibility for resulting harm?
  - AMA policy states that in scenarios where AI contributes to diagnosis and care decisions, the developer should be accountable for its role in care decisions impacted by its performance
    - Especially true when underlying algorithms are "black box" meaning physicians and patients cannot fully understand the logic leading to outputs
    - If a reasonable physician did not know or have reason to know an AI error occurred when it should have been reasonable to rely on the outputs, the developer should be accountable for resulting harm
    - If developers have no accountability for tool performance, there will be very little incentive for physicians to meaningfully engage with them knowing they bear all the risk
    - Claims that physicians should never rely on AI outputs would seem to obviate the need for utilizing the tool or result in using them as confirmatory tools only
  - Physicians will still bear the burden of ensuring appropriate education in selection and use of AI technologies and validating outputs to the extent possible
  - If physician is negligent in tool selection and use (selecting wrong tool, using on wrong patient, using outside intended use), they should be accountable
  - It may not be possible for physicians to validate outputs of more complex, black box AI
  - At some point, physicians must be able to trust that AI works and performs as promised, given that with complex machine learning algorithms, not even developers can always easily ascertain where mistakes are made
  - Another complicated scenario: what risks do physicians face if they do not utilize AI, or if using their best judgment they disagree with AI's assessment and harm results?
    - Complex legal scenario with no solid precedent
    - Assigning liability to physicians on the basis they should not depend on AI outputs while also exposing them to penalties for doing so places them in an untenable, no-win position
  - AMA believes AI developers should always be accountable for their performance, as physicians are accountable for theirs
  - Regulatory structures will play important role in apportioning liability and providing assurances of safety and performance
  - Regulatory structures set legal standards developers must meet to be deemed appropriate for use in providing care
  - Lack of regulatory standards for AI impacting clinical care results in physicians potentially shouldering entirety of risk of harm
  - Lack of regulatory oversight, guardrails, or development standards can increase safety risks and create additional liability risks when physicians use unregulated tools
  - Example: recent final guidance on Clinical Decision Support Software outlines FDA plans to exercise "enforcement discretion" for wide range of CDS software
    - Clearly notes physicians are responsible for independently reviewing and validating results
    - States physicians should not rely on algorithmic outputs as basis of their decision
    - While AMA does not believe all CDS tools need stringent oversight and appreciates enforcement discretion if tool offers transparency, the guidance essentially expects physicians to utilize tools while creating situation where tools are not necessarily accountable for performance
  - AMA expects liability framework to evolve over time as clinical AI-enabled technologies improve and are incorporated into standard of care
  - As standard of care increasingly contemplates AI use, liability may become more clearly defined, provided there is broad agreement AI should be trustworthy and accountable
  - Even with greater clarity regarding standard of care, legal questions involving AI in clinical care will continue to be complex
  - Determining causation in medical malpractice cases is already difficult; including AI input adds additional layer of complexity

- Interoperability to Accelerate AI in Clinical Care
  - Enhanced interoperability can meaningfully widen market opportunities, fuel research, and accelerate safe AI deployment when it enables reliable, standards-based access to high quality clinical data across settings
  - Should not shift new workflow, liability, or privacy risk onto physicians and patients
  - Biggest upside comes from making it easier for physicians to use AI as native part of clinical care, embedded within EHR workflows and grounded in trusted, interoperable data
  - Rather than forcing physicians to step outside EHR into separate tools
  - Interoperability is most catalytic where AI depends on longitudinal, computable, and timely data, especially at points of care transition and high administrative friction
  - Priority data types include:
    - Problem lists/diagnoses
    - Medications
    - Allergies
    - Labs and vitals
    - Imaging reports and key findings
    - Procedures and care plans
    - Referrals and consult notes
    - Discharge summaries
    - Relevant outcomes data
  - Data available from both inside the clinic and from wearables, remote patient monitoring, and hospital-at-home environments
  - Equally important are administrative and operational data that drive burden and delays: prior authorization status and payer coverage rules
  - API-enabled access can reduce redundant documentation and enable AI to generate more context-aware recommendations fitting clinical workflows
  - FHIR-based APIs can be the practical bridge between EHRs and trustworthy AI if implemented consistently, support writeback where appropriate, and are paired with clear governance and accountability
  - HHS should prioritize an ecosystem in which:
    - Patients can access and share their data through standardized APIs with meaningful controls and transparency
    - Physicians and care teams can access AI-relevant data without manual chart abstraction and can integrate AI tools into clinical workflow with minimal clicks and redocumentation
    - AI developers can compete on performance and safety (not bespoke EHR integrations) by building against consistent FHIR profiles and implementation guidance, reducing integration costs and vendor lock-in
  - Interoperability must move beyond "data movement" to "data meaning"
  - HHS should emphasize consistent use of standardized, clinically validated code sets and terminologies so AI models train and operate on comparable inputs across settings
  - Core clinical concepts should be expressed in widely adopted standards (diagnoses/problems, labs, medications, procedures) and mapped in ways that are auditable and maintain clinical fidelity
  - Without strong semantic standardization, AI performance will vary unpredictably across sites, undermining physician trust and increasing patient risk
  - Expanded API access increases attack surface and magnifies risk of inappropriate secondary use of sensitive health data
    - Especially when third parties may not be regulated under HIPAA in the same way as covered entities
  - HHS should caution against interoperability policies that are too loose or overly deferential to developers
  - Instead pair data access with enforceable protections:
    - Strong security by default expectations for API implementations
    - Meaningful patient controls
    - Limits on data use beyond what patients authorize
    - Clear accountability for downstream entities that receive data
  - FTC and OCR enforcement should be leveraged to deter unfair/deceptive data practices and ensure privacy and security protections remain credible as data flows increase
  - HHS should consider adaptations to HITECH Act's Business Associate Agreement policies to include baseline transparency, safety, and security requirements for all third-party AI tools with access to protected health information

- Clinical Care Infrastructure
  - Independent practices operate with lean staffing and limited IT capacity yet are expected to navigate increasingly complex health care administration rules, quality reporting programs, documentation requirements, and multi-payer administrative workflows
  - When administrative overhead expands, consequences are predictable: less time with patients, delayed services, reduced capacity to take new patients, particularly in rural communities
  - Properly deployed, AI can help practices spend less time on administrative tasks that detract from delivering care
  - Administration can accelerate this transformation by aligning federal policy levers around three goals:
    - Make AI integrate into routine workflows (not separate portals or "shadow processes" that add clicks)
    - Enable competition on performance and burden reduction by reducing integration barriers and developer lock-in
    - Protect trust with baseline privacy, security, and transparency expectations proportionate to risk
  - AI has potential to reduce burden in several high-friction areas consuming disproportionate time in independent practices:
    - AI-enabled documentation support can reduce duplicative data entry and improve accuracy and compliance without encouraging "note bloat" that obscures clinical meaning
    - AI can streamline eligibility and benefits verification by reducing back and forth required to confirm coverage details, improving point-of-care clarity for patients and staff
    - AI can lessen manual chart abstraction and rework by converting routine clinical documentation into structured, reusable elements supporting quality reporting with fewer additional steps
  - AI-driven burden reduction is only possible with consistent, computable access to clinical and administrative data elements
  - Enhanced interoperability through reliable, standards-based API-driven exchange can lower integration costs, reduce reliance on bespoke interfaces, and enable more innovators to compete on performance and measurable workflow value
  - To realize benefits for independent practices, HHS should encourage EHR developers and ecosystem participants to:
    - Provide dependable, consistent API access supporting practice-facing workflows (not only patient-facing use cases)
    - Prioritize implementation consistency so practices are not forced into costly custom builds
  - AI cannot be safe, effective, or trusted if built on inconsistent or low-integrity data
  - For physicians, reliability of an AI recommendation is only as strong as the fidelity of clinical information feeding it
  - Trusted, standardized medical coding and terminology is foundational infrastructure, not a technical afterthought
  - HHS should elevate semantic standardization as a core requirement for responsible AI:
    - AI training, use, and outputs must be grounded in standardized clinical concepts and code sets and be auditable for clinical fidelity
    - Drive alignment across EHRs, payers, and clinical decision-support systems so "meaning" remains consistent as data moves through the health care ecosystem
  - Independent physician practices often lack bargaining leverage to negotiate robust contractual protections with developers and third parties
    - Those entities frequently control system design, data handling, and security practices that determine real-world risk
    - A purely market-based approach can inadvertently shift accountability onto physicians by default
    - Exposes practices to privacy, security, and compliance fallout tied to technology they do not fully control
  - When trust in data security and privacy is degraded, the flow of information halts
  - HHS can help correct this imbalance by using clear guidance and appropriate enforcement posture
  - Entities building and operating AI systems must carry privacy and security obligations and accountability

- Prior Authorization
  - Prior authorization is one of the most significant drivers of physician burden and delays in care
  - AI can be used constructively to streamline documentation, improve submission quality, and accelerate approvals
  - AMA remains concerned about payer use of automated systems, including AI-enabled tools, that increase inappropriate denials, obscure rationale for decisions, or create barriers to timely, medically necessary care
  - When automated or AI-enabled tools influence prior authorization approvals or denials, physicians and patients need clear, actionable explanations—not opaque outputs that make decisions effectively unreviewable
  - Prior authorization should not devolve into "black box" process where physicians cannot understand what drove an adverse determination or what information is needed to resolve it
  - Physicians should expect:
    - Meaningful transparency about the basis for adverse determinations, including key clinical or administrative factors contributing to outcome
    - Decision rationales delivered in a form that works in real clinical workflows
  - Any AI-enabled recommendation indicating limitations or denials of care, at both initial review and appeal levels, must be automatically referred to a physician for review
  - Critical policy question is not whether AI should be used in prior authorization, but under what conditions it can be deployed without undermining patient protections, due process, or physician trust
  - Administration should advance common-sense expectations distinguishing AI that speeds appropriate approvals from AI that scales inappropriate denials
  - To effectively mitigate harms associated with prior authorization, HHS policies should address several objectives:
    - AI can support prior authorization workflow, but adverse coverage decisions must not be fully automated
      - Denials should require timely review by a qualified, licensed physician
      - Escalation pathways to protect patient safety when care may be delayed
    - If AI influences coverage decisions, payers must demonstrate real-world accuracy and clinical appropriateness through ongoing monitoring for errors, inappropriate denial patterns, and bias
      - Independent auditing and corrective action when AI inappropriately limits patients' access to care
    - Standardized, API-driven interoperability should streamline prior authorization end-to-end within the EHR
      - APIs should reduce fragmented documentation, eliminate payer-specific variation, and rework
    - As API-enabled data access expands in prior authorization, privacy, security, and limits on downstream use must scale with it
      - FTC and OCR enforcement and clear chain-of-custody accountability must be established
      - Expanded access should not increase breach risk or undermine patient and physician trust

- Payment for AI-Enabled Technologies
  - AMA commends Administration on increasing focus on developing pathways to payment for emerging digital health technologies
  - Digital health technologies (digital therapeutics, AI-enabled diagnostics, augmentative to fully autonomous systems) are evolving rapidly with increasing physician interest in integration
  - For these tools to become available for patient care, they need a clear, consistent pathway to payment
  - AMA has long held that any digital health technology proposed for payment should meet goals of the quadruple aim:
    - Improving the patient experience
    - Lowering health care costs
    - Improving physician wellbeing
    - Improving health outcomes
  - Appreciated HHS' focus on ensuring safety and efficacy of AI is a priority when considering payment
  - Given regulatory updates are lagging behind rapid pace of innovation, ensuring payment systems prioritize safe and effective AI-enabled systems can enhance patient and provider trust while encouraging broader adoption
  - While AMA strongly supports efforts to develop pathways to payment for high-quality, high-value emerging technologies, costs raise concern over potential negative impact on Medicare physician fee schedule (MPFS)
  - AI-enabled technologies can be expensive; as increasing numbers come to market, costs to Medicare systems could be very significant
  - Due to budget neutrality rules, paying for these technologies on the MPFS would likely be unsustainable over the long term
  - AMA strongly recommends payment for digital health and AI-enabled technologies be paid outside of the MPFS
    - A new benefit category or alternative approach whereby digital health tools and AI-enabled technologies are paid separately from MPFS would preserve payment for physician services while providing clear, consistent pathway to payment
    - Unique nature of these technologies would warrant a new payment system tailored specifically to needs of developers, physicians, and patients
    - Analogous to CMS' approaches to physician-administered drugs paid under Part B and payment of durable medical equipment
  - While AMA recognizes limitations on CMS' authority to create new benefit categories, urge Agency to carefully consider how broad payment of increasing numbers of AI technologies may impact payment for physician services on MPFS
  - Physicians have seen unintended consequences of paying for high-cost disposable supplies in practice expense methodology as these services have grown in price and number
    - Currently 94 medical supply items embedded in codes with purchase price over $500
    - These high-cost medical supplies represent $1.53 billion in direct costs and 21% of all direct practice expense medical supply costs in non-facility setting
    - These supplies are paid for at expense of other specialties
    - Appreciate CMS is seeking input on how to price and pay for these services
    - AMA reiterates they should be priced annually and paid outside of MPFS
  - Concerns that same redistributive trend could emerge if digital health technologies are paid within MPFS

- Coding for AI-Enabled Technologies
  - AMA supports HHS' objective to accelerate responsible adoption of AI and other digital technologies in clinical care while fostering public trust, reducing uncertainty that impedes innovation, and aligning incentives
  - AMA believes these goals are best achieved when digital innovation is grounded in clinical reality, governed through transparent, clinical expert-led processes, and integrated into sustainable coding and payment pathways
  - Central mechanism for advancing these objectives is ongoing evolution of Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) code set
  - Through work of CPT Editorial Panel, CPT is being modernized to support digitally enabled value-based care models that are increasingly outcomes-focused, technology-enabled, and delivered by multidisciplinary teams
  - CPT already underpins many value-based care arrangements by supporting patient attribution, risk stratification, quality measurement, and longitudinal analysis
  - Panel has established a dedicated Value-Based Care Workgroup to evaluate how CPT can better reflect episode-based and digitally enabled care
    - Operating through open and transparent process actively seeking input from physicians, payers, innovators, federal agencies, and other stakeholders
  - CPT Editorial Panel is addressing rapid emergence of clinical AI by developing a distinct coding framework that accurately describes algorithmic services as part of patient care pathway
  - Recognizing existing payment systems often rely on ad hoc crosswalks that are not scalable or sustainable
  - Panel's Digital Medicine Coding Committee is advancing a new category of CPT codes: Clinically Meaningful Algorithmic Analyses
    - Intended to describe complete, codifiable clinical services in which algorithmic output meaningfully informs diagnosis or treatment
    - Clearly distinguishing machine-performed work from interpretive work of physicians and other qualified health care professionals
  - Panel recognizes increasing use of AI will have broader implications for physician work and how clinical services are described across CPT code set
  - Panel has prioritized:
    - Evaluating potential impacts on physician work
    - Identifying coding gaps
    - Assessing how existing codes may need to evolve as clinical practice changes
  - These considerations will inform future deliberations and guide next phase of CPT modernization to ensure continued clinical accuracy and relevance
  - Because CPT is embedded across EHRs, payer systems, quality programs, and federal health care infrastructure, updates to code set provide scalable mechanism for integrating digitally enabled value-based care and clinical AI across health system
  - While CPT Editorial Panel has taken leadership role in developing clinically grounded and transparent reporting framework for algorithmic services, AMA recognizes essential role of HHS in establishing sustainable pathways for coverage and payment
  - AMA looks forward to continued collaboration with HHS and other stakeholders to ensure advances in digital health and AI translate into patient benefit, physician adoption, and a more efficient, trusted, and sustainable U.S. health care system

- Foundational Context
  - AMA commends Administration for its consideration and commitment to exploring how AI technologies present unique opportunities in health care
    - Enhance care delivery
    - Strengthen patient-provider relationships
    - Reduce administrative burdens for clinicians
    - Empower patients to navigate the health care system better and get the care they need
  - Currently, unresolved questions about liability, regulatory authority, patient safety, and state oversight are converging, making AI governance one of the most consequential issues facing health law and medical practice
  - A growing liability vacuum has emerged as AI developers increasingly disclaim responsibility for errors or harm, even as clinicians are expected to rely on these tools in clinical and administrative decision-making
  - Regulatory ambiguity, unresolved coverage and reimbursement questions, data privacy risks, and limited public trust are inhibiting rapid adoption
  - Highlights need to modernize oversight frameworks that were not designed for the scale, speed, or complexity of AI-enabled care
  - AMA asserts foundational concepts, measures, and principles are crucial for successful integration of AI into health care system and practice of medicine
  - Physicians must play a central role in ethical development, deployment, and utilization of AI technologies in health care
    - Clinical expertise is indispensable in ensuring these tools are safe, effective, and trustworthy
  - Establishing strong digital health foundations is critical to enabling this transformation:
    - Telehealth infrastructure
    - Robust privacy and security protections
    - Seamless interoperability
  - When implemented responsibly, AI-powered technologies hold significant potential to enhance patient-centered care, improve clinical outcomes, and reduce costs

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
The nation's largest physician organization calls for harmonized, risk-based AI regulation with clear developer accountability, warning that the current "liability vacuum" where developers disclaim responsibility while physicians bear all the risk will stifle adoption and harm patients.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Medical Association (AMA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents physician and medical student members; extensive policy work on AI governance, liability, coding, and payment; conducted AI Physician Sentiment Survey of over 1,000 physicians
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Physicians are expected to integrate AI into clinical and administrative decision-making while facing unresolved liability questions, regulatory ambiguity, and potential malpractice insurance impacts; AMA also manages CPT code set which will need to accommodate AI-enabled services

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We believe physicians must play a central role in the ethical development, deployment, and utilization of AI technologies in health care, as their clinical expertise is indispensable in ensuring these tools are safe, effective, and trustworthy. A clear, consistent, risk-based approach to regulation—not significant deregulation—is required to enhance trust in AI and successfully move towards clinical integration. AI developers should always be accountable for their performance, as physicians are accountable for theirs; should developers have no accountability for tool performance, there will be very little incentive for physicians to meaningfully engage with them, knowing they bear all the risk.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Harmonize AI terminology across HHS agencies (FDA, ONC, OCR, CMS) to alleviate confusion and provide consistency
- Adopt a risk-based regulatory approach for health care AI
  - Low-risk AI does not need stringent pre-market scrutiny
  - Higher-risk AI needs appropriate regulatory scrutiny with guardrails
  - All AI impacting medical decision-making should have rigorous post-market surveillance plans
  - FDA-regulated device software functions, including those under enforcement discretion, should have mandated labeling requirements
- Establish transparency mandates for AI tools
  - Require disclosure of intended use, how to use the tool, risks and limitations
  - Finalize FDA guidance on labeling requirements for AI-enabled device software functions
  - Create standardized disclosure format across industry participants
  - Require disclosure that users are interacting with AI (not a human) for consumer-facing tools
  - Prohibit claims that chatbots or agents are licensed clinicians
- Address physician liability for AI use
  - Developers should be accountable for AI's role in care decisions impacted by its performance
  - Especially important for "black box" algorithms where physicians cannot understand the logic
  - If a reasonable physician did not know or have reason to know an AI error occurred, developer should be accountable for resulting harm
- Protect against AI deepfakes and physician impersonation
  - Work with Congress to develop protections focusing on patient safety
  - Require clear labeling and default protections
- Prioritize interoperability that supports AI integration
  - Implement FHIR-based APIs consistently with writeback support and clear governance
  - Emphasize semantic standardization so AI models train on comparable inputs across settings
  - Pair expanded data access with enforceable privacy and security protections
  - Consider adapting HITECH Act's Business Associate Agreement policies to include baseline requirements for third-party AI tools
- Support independent practices in AI adoption
  - Make AI integrate into routine workflows, not separate portals
  - Enable competition on performance by reducing integration barriers
  - Encourage EHR developers to provide consistent API access supporting practice-facing workflows
  - Elevate semantic standardization as core requirement for responsible AI
- Reform prior authorization AI use
  - Adverse coverage decisions must not be fully automated; denials require physician review
  - Payers must demonstrate real-world accuracy through ongoing monitoring
  - Require independent auditing when AI inappropriately limits access to care
  - Standardize API-driven interoperability for prior authorization within EHRs
- Create sustainable payment pathways for AI-enabled technologies
  - Pay for digital health and AI-enabled technologies outside of the Medicare physician fee schedule
  - Create a new benefit category or alternative approach to preserve payment for physician services
  - Price high-cost medical supplies annually and pay outside of MPFS
- Support CPT code set evolution for AI
  - Advance "Clinically Meaningful Algorithmic Analyses" codes to describe algorithmic services
  - Distinguish machine-performed work from physician interpretive work
  - Collaborate with AMA on sustainable coverage and payment pathways

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Liability vacuum where AI developers disclaim responsibility for errors while physicians bear all risk
  - No precedent in case law for who is responsible for medical errors involving AI
  - No prevailing legal frameworks exist to address the issue
  - Could disincentivize physician adoption of AI tools
  - May impact malpractice insurance through higher premiums, increased deductibles, or policy riders
- Regulatory ambiguity and lack of harmonization
  - Different agencies use different terminology for the same technologies
  - Lack of clarity inhibits trust and adoption
  - FDA enforcement discretion for CDS software creates situation where tools are not accountable for performance
- Physicians placed in "no-win" position regarding AI use
  - Expected to rely on AI but told not to depend on outputs
  - Potentially liable if they don't use AI or if they disagree with AI's assessment
  - Cannot validate outputs of complex "black box" algorithms
- Payer use of AI in prior authorization
  - Concern about automated systems increasing inappropriate denials
  - Opaque outputs making decisions effectively unreviewable
  - "Black box" processes where physicians cannot understand adverse determinations
- Privacy and security risks from expanded data access
  - Increased attack surface from expanded API access
  - Third parties may not be regulated under HIPAA like covered entities
  - Risk of inappropriate secondary use of sensitive health data
- Impact on independent practices
  - Lean staffing and limited IT capacity
  - Lack bargaining leverage to negotiate robust contractual protections
  - Market-based approach shifts accountability onto physicians by default
- Payment sustainability concerns
  - AI-enabled technologies can be expensive
  - Budget neutrality rules make MPFS payment unsustainable
  - Risk of redistributive trend similar to high-cost disposable supplies
    - Currently 94 medical supply items over $500 representing $1.53 billion in direct costs
    - These supplies paid at expense of other specialties
- Medical deepfakes and physician impersonation
  - Can spread medical misinformation
  - Damage physicians' reputations and careers
  - Undermine patient trust in evidence-based care
  - Existing laws do not adequately address these risks

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Survey data revealing physician priorities: In a survey of over 1,000 physicians across specialties and practice settings, appropriate regulatory oversight was identified as one of the top needs when considering whether to integrate AI into practice; liability concerns were also among top concerns
- The "no-win" liability trap: Physicians face an untenable position where they're told not to rely on AI outputs while also potentially being penalized for not using AI or disagreeing with its assessment—effectively damned if they do, damned if they don't
- The deepfake trust exploitation: Patients rely on trust signals they cannot independently verify (physician's voice, image, credentials), and deepfakes exploit those cues to create a uniquely high-risk form of consumer deception in health care
- Hidden cost redistribution already happening: 94 medical supply items embedded in codes with purchase prices over $500 represent $1.53 billion in direct costs and 21% of all direct practice expense medical supply costs—paid at the expense of other specialties, a pattern that could repeat with AI
- CPT modernization underway: The CPT Editorial Panel is developing "Clinically Meaningful Algorithmic Analyses" codes to distinguish machine-performed work from physician interpretive work—a practical framework for coding AI services
- The "data meaning" gap: Without strong semantic standardization, AI performance will vary unpredictably across sites because the same clinical concepts are expressed differently, undermining physician trust and increasing patient risk

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "A growing liability vacuum has emerged as AI developers increasingly disclaim responsibility for errors or harm, even as clinicians are expected to rely on these tools in clinical and administrative decision-making."
- "Assigning liability to physicians on the basis that they should not depend on AI outputs and must rely on their independent judgment, while also exposing them to penalties for doing so, effectively places them in an untenable, no‑win position."
- "Should developers of AI technologies have no accountability for the performance of their tool, there will be very little incentive for physicians to meaningfully engage with them, knowing they bear all the risk for its performance. Claims that physicians should never rely on the outputs of the technology would seem to obviate the need for utilizing the tool in the first place."

entities.category: Electronic Health Records and Health IT
entities.label: EHR

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- About Advocate Health
  - Leading nonprofit integrated health system in the United States
  - Operates under names Advocate Health Care (Illinois), Atrium Health (Carolinas, Georgia, Alabama), and Aurora Health Care (Wisconsin)
  - National leader in clinical innovation, health outcomes, consumer experience, and value-based care
  - Wake Forest University School of Medicine serves as academic core
  - Headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina
  - Serves approximately six million patients
  - Engaged in hundreds of clinical trials and research studies
  - Nationally recognized for cardiology, neuroscience, oncology, pediatrics, rehabilitation, organ transplants, burn treatments, and musculoskeletal programs
  - Employs approximately 167,000 team members
  - Operates 69 hospitals and more than 1,100 care locations
  - One of nation's largest graduate medical education programs with more than 2,000 residents and fellows across more than 200 programs
  - Five skilled nursing facilities with 572 licensed beds
  - Provides more than $6 billion in annual community benefits

- Background on AI Regulation and Current HHS Regulations
  - Primary federal frameworks affecting AI adoption are HIPAA and FDA oversight of medical devices
  - Federal policymakers should modernize HIPAA to address contemporary data sharing practices
  - Need to strengthen privacy and security protections
  - Need clearer guidance on appropriate use of patient data for AI development and deployment
  - Federal legislation should set clear, consistent national standards for liability, accountability, transparency, and responsible AI use
  - Standards should include uniform definition of "artificial intelligence"
  - Should distinguish regulatory expectations across different AI types including machine learning tools, computer vision systems, and large language models
  - Federal policymakers should outline appropriate boundaries for state-level AI laws
    - States should retain ability to regulate clinical practice
    - Should operate within federal framework preventing conflicting or overly restrictive rules
    - Essential for multistate health systems and nationwide AI development

- Background on Reimbursement Policy
  - Cost of implementing AI solutions presents significant barrier for small, rural, and critical access hospitals
  - Barriers also affect providers serving areas with significant socio-economic needs
  - Costs include infrastructure for monitoring, governance, model evaluation, workforce training, and cybersecurity
  - Without targeted reimbursement policies or federal financial support, these organizations cannot adopt emerging AI technologies
  - Disparities in access to advanced clinical tools may widen
  - This hampers administration's goal of improving population health outcomes
  - Federal policy should ensure payers have incentive and operational ability to support access to clinically validated, high-value AI tools
    - Establish clear reimbursement pathways for AI-enabled services
    - Offer supplemental payments or grant-based support for under-resourced providers
    - Create mechanisms to evaluate cost effectiveness and clinical benefit across diverse care settings

- Background on Research and Development Opportunities
  - HHS has critical role in supporting R&D for safe, equitable, evidence-based AI integration
  - Strategic investments should focus on:
    - Research into AI models addressing social determinants of health and structural inequities
    - Pilot programs testing AI applications in under-resourced settings including rural and safety net providers
    - Long-term R&D initiatives improving population health, care delivery efficiency, and equitable access to emerging technologies

- Question 1: Barriers to Private Sector Innovation
  - Barriers to clinical use of large language models
    - Systems may generate inaccurate or fabricated information ("hallucinations")
      - Creates risks if clinicians rely on outputs without sufficient validation
    - LLMs may omit critical clinical details or fail to surface relevant information
      - Particularly when inputs are incomplete or ambiguous
    - No clear or consistent framework governing liability when AI provides misleading, incomplete, or unsafe recommendations
      - Poses challenges for health systems, clinicians, and vendors
      - May slow adoption of promising tools
    - Financial burden of implementing LLM-based solutions is substantial
      - Includes vendor licensing fees, internal development costs, technical infrastructure, ongoing monitoring and governance
      - Especially prohibitive for small, rural, and resource-constrained organizations
      - Raises concerns about widening disparities in access to advanced clinical technologies
  - Advocate Health's mitigation strategies
    - Leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and small language models (SLMs)
    - Integrates core protocols to limit hallucinations
    - With guardrails in place, LLM use results in sensitivity better than human performance alone
    - Smaller organizations may not be able to easily implement these guardrails

- Question 2: Regulatory, Payment Policy, and Programmatic Changes
  - HIPAA, Privacy, and Security Considerations
    - HIPAA should be reviewed and modernized for privacy and security implications of transmitting PHI to technology companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google
    - These entities should adhere to same privacy, security, and compliance standards as traditional healthcare providers and certified health IT systems
    - Use of highly regulated data like MSSP information is governed by strict program requirements and contractual obligations
      - May limit ability of providers and ACOs to use such data in AI solutions
      - May prevent storage within AI-enabled platforms for internal use
    - Updated guidance needed to clarify permissible uses of regulated data in AI development and operational workflows
      - Particularly for organizations in federal value-based programs
  - FDA Oversight and Regulatory Alignment
    - FDA published revised final guidance on Clinical Decision Support Software and General Wellness Policy on January 6, 2026
    - Revised guidance addresses industry concerns about overregulation
    - Broadens categories of digital health products FDA chooses not to regulate
    - Commends FDA for publishing updated guidance
    - FDA's regulatory approach should be considered jointly with broader federal AI policies
      - Ensure coherence and minimize duplication
      - Create predictable expectations for developers, clinicians, and health systems
    - Clear definitions, risk-based frameworks, and coordinated interagency guidance would significantly improve regulatory environment
  - AI Use by Payers and Impacts on Care Access
    - Legislation and regulation must account for increasing payer use of AI for prior authorizations, coverage approvals, and claims denials
    - These tools exert direct and significant influence on clinical care patients receive
    - Payers using AI for coverage determinations should maintain transparency regarding:
      - Role of AI in claims decisions
      - Rates of denials, appeals, and overturns
      - Specific AI models or algorithms used
      - Demonstrated cost efficiencies achieved through AI
    - Financial savings from AI-enabled administrative efficiencies should translate into lower premiums or reduced cost sharing for beneficiaries
      - Should not solely accrue to payer institutions

- Question 3: Novel Legal and Implementation Issues for Non-Medical Devices
  - Liability Issues
    - AI tools increasingly influence real clinical and social service decisions even when not regulated as medical devices
    - Creates practical and legal challenges for safety-net providers and community-based organizations lacking extensive compliance or legal support
    - Significant uncertainty in community health settings regarding liability
      - Clinicians and community partners unsure about exposure if they rely on AI recommendation that results in harm
      - Also unsure about exposure if they decline to follow recommendation and harm occurs
    - Ambiguities place disproportionate burdens on federally qualified health centers, rural hospitals, and faith-based or nonprofit programs
      - Already operate with limited legal resources and heightened perceived risk
    - HHS regulations could provide clarity by:
      - Affirming reasonable reliance on validated clinical decision support tools does not create new or expanded liability
      - Encouraging malpractice and institutional liability frameworks to distinguish among clinician errors, organizational governance failures, and defects in AI tools
  - Privacy Issues
    - Many AI developers, data brokers, and digital health platforms operate outside HIPAA scope
    - Routinely process highly sensitive health and social information
    - When failures or misuse occur, lines of responsibility often unclear
    - Patients uncertain about where to seek recourse
    - HHS could address gaps by:
      - Requiring clear documentation of roles and responsibilities among AI developers, healthcare organizations, and vendors
      - Extending HIPAA-aligned privacy and security standards to key non-covered entities in healthcare-related AI activities
      - Establishing transparency requirements so patients and caregivers can identify who is accountable for AI-driven recommendations at each stage of care

- Question 4: AI Evaluation Methods for Non-Medical Devices
  - AI performance metrics should remain locally defined
    - Relevance depends on how each tool is integrated into clinical workflows
  - Federal regulation of specific metrics would be overly prescriptive and could hinder innovation
  - Federal guidance should encourage use of governance structures
    - Examples include Advocate Health's Framework for Appropriate Implementation and Review of AI (FAIR AI) principles
    - Internal AI oversight committees
  - Governance structures ensure organizations systematically evaluate AI tools for safety, equity, and alignment with patient-care priorities

- Question 5: Supporting Private Sector Activities
  - Efforts to promote innovative AI use should not rely on creating new accreditation, certification, or credentialing requirements
  - These processes often impose substantial administrative and financial burdens without meaningful improvements in quality or safety
  - Registry abstraction and reporting already represent significant expenses
  - Federal and state agencies and accrediting bodies should be constrained from adding requirements that increase administrative load without demonstrable value

- Question 6: AI Performance and Potential
  - Areas of Highest Potential Impact
    - Enhancing administrative efficiency
      - Billing, supply chain optimization, clinical data abstraction
    - Strengthening workforce capacity
      - Scheduling, documentation, radiology tools reduce burden and improve productivity
    - Accelerating research
    - Enabling more precise and timely personalized care
    - Assisting with clinician and patient satisfaction
      - Ambient listening and chart summarization reduce documentation burden
      - Enable clinicians to work at top of their license
      - Free clinicians to spend more meaningful time with patients
      - Help health systems manage ongoing workforce shortages more effectively
  - Shortcomings of Current AI Tools
    - Analysis of social drivers
      - Patients in community health portfolio often understand care instructions, adhere to medications, attend appointments, make lifestyle changes
      - Yet remain unable to carry out care plans due to significant social barriers
        - Lack reliable transportation
        - Cannot afford medications even with insurance
        - Lack stable housing or consistent access to nutritious food
        - Work multiple jobs with limited ability to take time off
      - Current clinical AI tools do not incorporate critical social factors into assessments
        - Cannot determine whether patient has means to attend follow-up or afford prescriptions once insurance benefits exhausted
    - Linguistic and cultural gap
      - Most AI systems trained predominantly on English language data and interactions from majority populations
      - Translation functionalities may be available but models lack cultural, linguistic, and contextual grounding
      - Recommendations often not meaningful or actionable in patients' day-to-day lives
  - Novel AI Tools with Greatest Potential
    - Integrated tool for social determinants of health (SDOH) screening and resource navigation
    - Could synthesize individual clinical data with community-level indicators
      - Food access, housing stability, transportation availability, other social determinants
    - Would assess patient needs accurately and predict likelihood of successful care plan completion
    - Assessment would extend beyond clinical risk factors to incorporate material, social, and logistical barriers
    - Could automatically match patients with real-time community resource availability
    - Generate referrals to social services, community-based organizations, financial assistance programs
    - Track resource utilization and identify unmet needs or systemic gaps
    - Inform individualized care planning and longer-term community investment strategies
    - Would augment rather than replace community health workers and care navigators
      - Equip them with data-driven insights and automated administrative support

- Question 7: Roles, Decision Makers, and Governance
  - Appropriate roles and governance structures vary based on organization size and complexity
  - Large systems like Advocate Health typically employ:
    - AI or Digital Health Officer
    - Data governance and AI governance committees
    - National and specialty service lines
    - Clinical informatics teams
    - IT departments responsible for implementing models and vendor solutions
  - AI deployment should be guided by organization's strategic priorities
  - Consider both immediate and long-term value
  - Returns on AI investment should be assessed through:
    - Short-term financial metrics
    - Broader impacts on patient and provider experience
    - Access to care
    - Overall system performance

- Question 8: Enhanced Interoperability
  - Ensuring Data Access and Transparency in AI Development
    - Near monopoly among few major EHR vendors
    - EHR systems should permit greater interoperability and connectivity with AI developers
      - Including health systems conducting internal AI development
    - EHR vendors control tightly how data can be exported and integrated into organizational data platforms
    - Even large systems like Advocate Health face constraints on accessing, processing, and using their own data
    - Limits innovation and slows progress toward data-driven care
  - Federal policy could establish transparent framework governing quality and risk profile of data used to train LLMs
    - Clearly indicate whether training data were scraped from internet, derived from medical records, or sourced from clinical research
    - Similar to how scientific evidence is graded
    - Greater transparency would allow organizations to assess reliability, safety, and appropriateness of LLM-based tools
  - Current federal interoperability requirements focused primarily on read-only access to individual patient-level data
    - Constrains scope and scale of AI applications
    - APIs facilitate patient-mediated data exchange and point-in-time clinical insights
    - Do not reliably support population-level data aggregation needed for model training

- Question 9: Patient and Caregiver Concerns
  - Patients express significant concerns about privacy of video, audio, and personal health data
  - Concerns about risk that agentic AI systems may provide incorrect or biased information
  - Updating and modernizing HIPAA would help address these concerns
    - Strengthen protections for emerging data types
    - Clarify expectations for safe, transparent use of AI in clinical and patient-facing settings

- Conclusion and Key Comments
  - Urgent need for clear, coordinated federal action given rapid pace of AI solutions, expanding vendor field, and growing patchwork of state legislation
  - Federal leadership essential to:
    - Modernize HIPAA
    - Increase transparency for AI used by payers
    - Prevent interoperability barriers created by EHR vendors
  - Adoption must focus on clinical value and long-term strategic benefit, not additional administrative burden
  - AI's highest near-term impact lies in administrative simplification, workforce support, personalized care, and research acceleration
  - Healthcare organizations need flexible governance structures, not prescriptive federal mandates
    - Evaluate AI tools in alignment with organizational strategy and patient outcomes
  - Adopting risk-based framework like Advocate Health's FAIR-AI enables responsible, scalable AI use
    - Avoids rigid rules that stifle innovation and slow adoption

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
A major nonprofit health system with 69 hospitals urges federal leadership to modernize HIPAA, establish clear liability frameworks, and prevent EHR vendor interoperability barriers while warning that current AI tools fail to account for social determinants that prevent patients from following care plans.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Advocate Health (James A. Crowder, Chief Technology and AI Officer)
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** Chief Technology and AI Officer at one of the nation's largest nonprofit integrated health systems; organization has developed its own FAIR-AI framework for responsible AI implementation
- **Geographic Scope:** National (Illinois, Wisconsin, Carolinas, Georgia, Alabama)
- **Stake in Issue:** Operates 69 hospitals and 1,100+ care locations serving 6 million patients; actively developing and deploying AI solutions including RAG and small language models; directly affected by HIPAA constraints, EHR vendor data access limitations, and lack of clear liability frameworks

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We need clear, coordinated federal action to modernize HIPAA, establish liability frameworks, and break down EHR vendor barriers that constrain AI innovation. Healthcare organizations need flexible governance structures rather than prescriptive federal mandates to evaluate AI tools. AI's highest near-term impact lies in administrative simplification, workforce support, and personalized care—but current tools fail to address the social barriers that actually prevent patients from following their care plans.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Modernize HIPAA to address contemporary data sharing practices
  - Require technology companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google to adhere to same privacy, security, and compliance standards as traditional healthcare providers
  - Clarify permissible uses of regulated data (like MSSP information) in AI development for organizations in value-based programs
  - Strengthen protections for emerging data types including video, audio, and patient-generated data

- Establish clear federal liability framework for AI in clinical care
  - Affirm that reasonable reliance on validated clinical decision support tools does not create new or expanded liability
  - Encourage malpractice frameworks to distinguish among clinician errors, organizational governance failures, and defects in AI tools themselves
  - Require clear documentation of roles and responsibilities among AI developers, healthcare organizations, and vendors

- Require transparency from payers using AI for coverage determinations
  - Disclose role of AI in claims decisions
  - Report rates of denials, appeals, and overturns
  - Identify specific AI models or algorithms used
  - Demonstrate cost efficiencies achieved
  - Require savings to translate into lower premiums or reduced cost sharing for beneficiaries

- Address EHR vendor interoperability barriers
  - Require EHR systems to permit greater interoperability and connectivity with AI developers
  - Enable health systems to access, process, and use their own data for AI development
  - Expand beyond current read-only, individual patient-level API requirements to support population-level data aggregation

- Establish transparent framework for LLM training data quality
  - Clearly indicate whether training data were scraped from internet, derived from medical records, or sourced from clinical research
  - Grade data sources similar to how scientific evidence is graded

- Create targeted reimbursement policies for under-resourced providers
  - Establish clear reimbursement pathways for AI-enabled services
  - Offer supplemental payments or grant-based support for small, rural, and critical access hospitals
  - Create mechanisms to evaluate cost effectiveness across diverse care settings

- Avoid creating new accreditation, certification, or credentialing requirements
  - These impose substantial administrative and financial burdens without meaningful quality improvements

- Allow AI performance metrics to remain locally defined
  - Federal regulation of specific metrics would be overly prescriptive and hinder innovation
  - Encourage use of governance structures like FAIR-AI principles rather than mandated metrics

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Liability uncertainty creates disproportionate burden on safety-net providers
  - Federally qualified health centers, rural hospitals, and faith-based programs lack extensive legal resources
  - Clinicians unsure about exposure whether they follow or decline AI recommendations
  - No clear framework governing liability when AI provides misleading or unsafe recommendations

- Current AI tools fail to incorporate social determinants of health
  - Patients who understand and want to follow care plans cannot due to transportation, cost, housing, and food access barriers
  - AI may identify patient as high-risk for readmission but cannot determine if they can afford prescriptions or attend follow-up
  - Most AI systems trained on English language data from majority populations lack cultural and contextual grounding

- EHR vendor monopoly constrains AI innovation
  - Vendors control tightly how data can be exported and integrated
  - Even large systems face constraints on accessing their own data for AI development
  - Current interoperability requirements don't support population-level data aggregation needed for model training

- Financial barriers will widen disparities in AI access
  - Implementation costs for infrastructure, monitoring, governance, training, and cybersecurity are prohibitive for small and rural providers
  - Without targeted support, under-resourced organizations cannot adopt AI even as it becomes standard for high-quality care

- Privacy gaps for non-HIPAA entities
  - AI developers, data brokers, and digital health platforms routinely process sensitive health information outside HIPAA scope
  - When failures occur, lines of responsibility are unclear
  - Patients uncertain where to seek recourse

- LLM hallucinations and omissions create clinical risk
  - Systems may generate inaccurate or fabricated information
  - May omit critical clinical details, particularly with incomplete inputs
  - Smaller organizations cannot easily implement guardrails like RAG that larger systems use

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Developed and published FAIR-AI framework (Framework for Appropriate Implementation and Review of AI) as peer-reviewed research for responsible AI governance
  - Offers this as model for flexible, risk-based approach rather than prescriptive federal mandates

- Uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and small language models with integrated core protocols to limit hallucinations
  - Reports that with these guardrails, LLM sensitivity is better than human performance alone
  - Notes smaller organizations cannot easily implement these same guardrails

- Describes specific patient scenarios from community health portfolio illustrating AI's blind spots
  - Patients who fully understand care instructions, adhere to medications, attend appointments, and make lifestyle changes
  - Still cannot carry out care plans due to transportation, medication costs, housing instability, food access, or multiple jobs
  - Current AI tools cannot assess whether a patient flagged as high-risk can actually afford prescriptions or attend follow-up

- Proposes novel AI tool concept: integrated SDOH screening and resource navigation system
  - Would synthesize clinical data with community-level indicators
  - Automatically match patients with real-time community resource availability
  - Track resource utilization and identify systemic gaps
  - Would augment rather than replace community health workers

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Many lack reliable transportation to medical visits. Others cannot afford their medications even with insurance coverage. Some do not have stable housing or consistent access to nutritious food. Many work multiple jobs with limited ability to take time off for medical needs."

- "Current clinical AI tools do not appear to incorporate these critical social factors—such as the availability of community resources, individual financial constraints, or the practical feasibility of care recommendations—into their assessments or outputs. These systems may identify a patient as high risk for hospital readmission, but they cannot determine whether that same patient has the means to attend a follow-up appointment or afford necessary prescriptions once insurance benefits are exhausted."

- "Any financial savings attributable to AI-enabled administrative efficiencies should be expected to translate into lower premiums or reduced cost sharing for beneficiaries rather than solely accruing to payer institutions."
