  - Data privacy, confidentiality, and cybersecurity are major barriers to acceptance
  - "Black-box" nature of AI systems undermines trust due to lack of explainability
  - Fear that AI could dehumanize care and reduce human interaction
  - Biased algorithms may perpetuate health disparities
  - Uncertainty over liability if AI contributes to harm
- Accuracy of AI-generated health information
  - ECRI identified misuse of AI chatbots as #1 technology hazard in 2026
  - Risk of patients following inaccurate or misleading AI recommendations as medical advice
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- CRNAs are sole anesthesia providers in nearly 100% of rural hospitals in some states, making their inclusion in AI policy critical for rural healthcare access
- Rule-based automation in infusion pumps, monitoring systems, and diagnostic devices has long contributed to safer anesthesia delivery, demonstrating successful AI integration in their field
- SEDASYS automated sedation system serves as cautionary tale - failed commercially because it couldn't adapt to real-world clinical complexity, illustrating why AI should augment rather than replace clinician judgment
- Sarasota Memorial case study shows AI successfully redesigned patient flow and increased capacity, demonstrating value of AI in operational rather than autonomous clinical roles
- CRNAs frequently serve in executive positions (department administrators, CNEs, COOs, CEOs), giving them unique perspective on both clinical and administrative AI applications
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI should assist - but never replace – CRNA clinical judgment that is based on extensive education and experience."
- "AI is most effective when it enhances clinician expertise, improves consistency, and integrates seamlessly into clinical workflows."
- "AI output should never be used as a substitute for qualified medical advice or professional judgment."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- AHIMA is a global nonprofit association of health information (HI) professionals
  - Represents professionals who work with health data for more than one billion patient visits each year
  - Mission is empowering people to impact health
  - Members ensure health information is accurate, complete, and available to patients and clinicians
  - Leaders work at the intersection of healthcare, technology, and business
  - Members found in data integrity and information privacy job functions worldwide

- As HHS enacts President Trump's vision for AI in healthcare, all sectors must be included
  - Will take a whole-of-healthcare approach to unlock AI's full potential safely and meaningfully
  - HHS' AI Strategy lays foundation for approach encompassing key stakeholders
  - AHIMA applauds those efforts while urging HHS to maintain expansive vision throughout implementation

- The HI profession is energized by AI innovations and opportunities
  - Proper implementation can assist HI professionals in completing burdensome tasks
    - Example: claims processing
  - This allows focus on more advanced work within their field
  - A robust policy plan building on this strategy is needed for American healthcare to take advantage of AI
  - Plan must provide necessary guardrails to ensure AI tools improve quality of healthcare and/or health outcomes

- AHIMA policy recommendations to strengthen AI's use in healthcare:
  - Ensure robust regulatory guidelines centered on fairness, accuracy, security, and transparency
    - For both clinical and non-clinical AI
  - Structure regulatory frameworks with sufficient flexibility for continued AI development and innovation
  - Focus on intended use and desired outcome when determining AI efficacy
  - Prioritize end-users' input throughout development and real-world testing
    - Including HI professionals
  - Develop updated privacy and security policies for new challenges posed by AI in healthcare settings
  - Maintain focus on reducing unintended outputs and unplanned biases within AI models
    - Ensure outputs remain stable and can be utilized across multiple settings with limited maintenance

- These recommendations will create an environment of trusted AI use and implementation
  - Those who interact with healthcare will have confidence in the technology itself

- Challenges exist in AI use today and the existing policy framework
  - HHS should address recommendations above
  - Those in healthcare need an avenue to engage in development of standards and best practices for AI
  - Strong private-public sector partnerships can ensure desired policy outcomes are realized

- HHS should focus continued AI activities on recommendations in concert with five pillars of HHS AI Strategic Plan
  - Especially when bringing AI into the clinical sphere
  - Pillars should be starting point for all guided regulatory actions related to AI
  - Flexibility needed for department to adjust based on practical realities of AI implementation and use
  - Healthcare AI development and use is in a nascent stage
  - Nimble regulatory posture necessary to ensure regulatory goals are met
  - HHS should continue to lean on private sector parties to ensure regulatory strategy remains viable

- Challenges in healthcare technology have perpetuated regardless of modality
  - Important these challenges are accounted for in all regulatory activities
  - AHIMA and members stand ready to assist

- AI will assist the HI profession by:
  - Alleviating staff burnout
  - Reducing administrative burden
  - Improving compliance activities
- Implementation is not without challenges
- A regulatory plan with common sense policy guardrails can ensure AI becomes trusted, safe healthcare technology
- AHIMA looks forward to continued collaboration in development of Department's AI strategy
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Health information management association representing professionals handling over a billion patient visits annually urges HHS to develop flexible, guardrail-based AI regulations that include HI professionals as key stakeholders in both policy development and real-world testing.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Health Information Management Association (AHIMA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Global nonprofit representing health information professionals working in data integrity, information privacy, healthcare technology, and business; members handle health data for over one billion patient visits annually
- **Geographic Scope:** National/International
- **Stake in Issue:** Members directly affected by AI implementation in healthcare settings; AI could alleviate administrative burdens like claims processing but requires proper guardrails to ensure quality and safety
structuredSections.corePosition: We support HHS's AI strategy and believe AI can transform healthcare by reducing administrative burden and improving outcomes, but implementation requires a whole-of-healthcare approach with proper guardrails. Health information professionals must be included as key stakeholders in AI policy development and testing, and regulatory frameworks need to balance safety requirements with flexibility for continued innovation.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Develop robust regulatory guidelines centered on fairness, accuracy, security, and transparency for both clinical and non-clinical AI
- Structure regulatory frameworks with sufficient flexibility to allow continued AI development and innovation
- Focus on intended use and desired outcome when determining AI technology efficacy
- Prioritize end-users' input—including health information professionals—throughout AI development and real-world testing
- Develop updated privacy and security policies to address new challenges posed by AI in healthcare settings
- Maintain focus on reducing unintended outputs and unplanned biases within AI models
  - Ensure outputs remain stable across multiple settings with limited maintenance
- Maintain strong private-public sector partnerships for AI development and standards
- Use the five pillars of HHS AI Strategic Plan as starting point for all regulatory actions
  - Allow flexibility to adjust based on practical realities of implementation
- Create avenues for healthcare professionals to engage in development of AI standards and best practices
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Healthcare AI development and use is in a nascent stage requiring nimble regulatory posture
- Challenges in healthcare technology have perpetuated regardless of modality used
  - These must be accounted for in all regulatory activities
- Unintended outputs and unplanned biases within AI models pose risks
- New privacy and security challenges posed by AI in healthcare settings need to be addressed
- Risk that health information professionals and other end-users may be excluded from AI development and testing processes
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Health information professionals handle data for over one billion patient visits annually, positioning them as critical stakeholders in AI implementation
- AI can assist with burdensome tasks like claims processing, freeing HI professionals to focus on more advanced work
- Identifies specific benefits AI can bring to HI profession: alleviating staff burnout, reducing administrative burden, and improving compliance activities
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "It will take a whole of healthcare approach to ensure the full potential of AI is unlocked in a safe and meaningful way."
- "Healthcare AI development and use is in a nascent stage and a nimble regulatory posture will be necessary to ensure regulatory goals are met."
- "A regulatory plan that utilizes common sense policy guardrails can ensure that AI becomes another piece of trusted, safe healthcare technology."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Psychotherapy Action Network (PsiAN) is a nonprofit advocating for access to psychotherapies that create meaningful change
  - Membership of over 6,500 individuals and 90 partner organizations
  - Represents mental health clinicians, researchers, administrators, and therapy users across all disciplines
  - Stands for ethical, effective patient care and clinical standards
  - Amplifies therapists' voices and advocates for reforms serving patients and clinicians

- Comments focus on behavioral health landscape and psychotherapy provision, though applicable to all forms of AI in healthcare

- Regulation concerns
  - Currently no federal regulation of AI in healthcare, creating cause for great concern related to privacy, efficacy, and safety
  - Regulation is essential given the federal government's central obligation to protect the public
  - Cannot be left to companies producing/selling products or to individual states
    - State-by-state approach would result in patchwork of incompatible regulations
  - Government has already decided regulation and oversight are essential in healthcare
    - FDA regulates new products and treatments
    - Healthcare clinicians regulated through licensing requirements
  - Should not accept anything less with AI, perhaps should set bar higher for unproven technology

- Privacy concerns
  - AI chatbots (mental health specific or generalized models) are not HIPAA compliant
  - User/patient privacy not protected by federal law
  - Privacy legally required by HIPAA in all healthcare industries and settings
  - Privacy and confidentiality are cornerstones of effective psychotherapy
  - Absence of these protections in AI chatbots is a significant area of concern demanding correction

- Research and development concerns
  - Due to novelty of technology, not enough data available to support efficacy and/or safety
  - Large Language Models especially prone to "hallucinations" with documented cases of irrevocable harm
    - AI LLMs have encouraged vulnerable people struggling with suicide to take suicidal action
    - Have provided information about heights of bridges or buildings from which to jump
    - This is unacceptably risky chatbot behavior that literally threatens lives
  - Evidence base showing behavioral health AI products are effective and safe is lacking
  - Corporate metrics (downloads, user engagement, customer satisfaction) are not substitutes for demonstrating effectiveness
  - AI products must be thoroughly researched and tested like any new psychotherapy protocol or pharmaceutical
    - Must measure effectiveness, dropout rates, remission rates, etc.
  - Evaluation, assessment, safety and outcome research should be provided by separate, independent researchers
    - Minimizes conflict of interest if corporations developing products also supply evaluation and "science"

- For AI instruments approximating psychotherapy
  - Products should advance recovery as defined by SAMHSA: a process over time leading to a self-directed life
  - Insufficient to focus only on symptom management or reduction

- HHS advocacy to accelerate AI use must follow and be linked to development of evidence base demonstrating safety and efficacy

- Access concerns acknowledged but risks highlighted
  - Recognizes legitimate concerns related to access to mental health care
  - Acknowledges hypothesis that AI tools could help close that gap
  - Highlights risks of using AI for clinical care in behavioral healthcare without rigorous clinical trials, regulation, and robust oversight
  - At minimum, HHS should not accelerate AI adoption until FDA completes evaluations of AI as medical devices
  - FDA has found over 10,000 applications already widely available to consumers without appropriate premarket performance evaluations, risk management, or post-market evaluations
  - Recommends HHS focus on developing research demonstrating safety and efficacy before accelerating implementation

- Response to supplemental questions

- Question 1: Barriers to private sector innovation
  - Privacy of data and corporate responsibility for poor outcomes are paramount
  - Corporations developing AI tools cannot currently ensure patient privacy
  - Legal and regulatory infrastructure holding them accountable for outcomes or harms is not well-established

- Question 2: Regulatory and payment policy changes
  - Uniform federal standards for privacy and safety are essential to adoption

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - HHS should contribute to creating uniform federal standards for privacy and safety AFTER FDA determines whether AI instruments are medical devices
  - Standards should be consistent with that determination

- Question 4: AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Need more than bench testing and assessment of user experiences and outcomes
  - Need robust outcome data about whether AI makes a difference in health-related quality of life
  - Mere symptom improvement is insufficient

- Question 5: Supporting private sector activities
  - Accreditation and credentialing present serious challenges
  - Must look beyond narrow financial interests of insurance entities or AI corporations

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns
  - In behavioral health, decades of research confirm therapist-patient relationship is main predictor of outcome in psychotherapy
  - Since AI is not a human relationship, it is seriously limited as a potential clinical treatment tool
  - Much more study and assessment over longer periods of time are necessary
  - Unclear how AI can best be used
    - As support for psychotherapists
    - As replacement for psychotherapists
    - As tool focused on insurance processing and administrative issues

- Question 10: AI research priorities
  - Several states adopting laws regulating AI mental health chatbots
  - These have become a patchwork of requirements differing state to state, lacking uniformity
  - Adoption of clear, science-based federal regulations indicated to define how all AI chatbots can ensure privacy, safety, and efficacy nationwide
  - Without federal oversight, no meaningful incentives for corporations to self-regulate
  - HHS should work with clinicians and psychotherapy researchers to define appropriate metrics for product efficacy and safety
  - Clinical metrics differ from corporate metrics focused on measuring product profitability rather than safety and efficacy
  - Leaving regulation and oversight to corporations will not promote consumer safety, privacy, or effective care

- Summary position
  - AI medical devices not ready for accelerated adoption and deployment without appropriate research or enforceable regulation
  - Even though LLMs currently used by millions, public adoption is far ahead of essential science demonstrating privacy, safety, and efficacy
  - Regulation essential to:
    - Reduce risk of harm to consumers
    - Ensure public has access to safe and effective clinical care
    - Hold corporations accountable for products they develop and sell
    - Support uniform national standards for AI chatbots

- Offers to testify in any hearings on this matter

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Mental health advocacy network representing 6,500+ clinicians urges HHS to pump the brakes on AI acceleration until federal privacy protections, independent safety research, and uniform regulations catch up with technology already being used by millions.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Psychotherapy Action Network (PsiAN)
- **Type:** Advocacy Group
- **Role/Expertise:** Nonprofit representing 6,500+ mental health clinicians, researchers, administrators, and therapy users across all disciplines; 90 partner organizations
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Members provide psychotherapy services that could be affected by AI tools; concerned about patient safety, privacy, and clinical efficacy in behavioral health AI applications

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We urge federal policymakers to prioritize patient privacy, safety, clinical efficacy, and enforceable national regulation before accelerating AI deployment in healthcare, particularly within behavioral health and psychotherapy. Current AI tools lack sufficient evidence of effectiveness, independent safety evaluation, and HIPAA-aligned privacy protections, creating meaningful risks for patients and clinicians. HHS should not accelerate AI adoption until the FDA completes its evaluations and robust research demonstrates safety and efficacy.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Do not accelerate AI adoption until FDA completes evaluations of AI as medical devices
  - Over 10,000 applications already available without appropriate premarket performance evaluations
- Establish uniform federal standards for privacy and safety
  - State-by-state approach creates incompatible patchwork of regulations
  - Standards should follow and be consistent with FDA's determination on whether AI instruments are medical devices
- Require independent evaluation and outcome research
  - Separate from corporations developing the products to minimize conflicts of interest
  - Focus on health-related quality of life outcomes, not just symptom improvement
- Work with clinicians and psychotherapy researchers to define appropriate efficacy and safety metrics
  - Clinical metrics differ from corporate metrics focused on profitability
- Ensure AI products meet HIPAA compliance requirements before deployment
- For AI approximating psychotherapy, require products to advance recovery (self-directed life) rather than just symptom management

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- No current federal regulation of AI in healthcare
  - Cannot leave regulation to companies or individual states
  - Government already requires regulation for other healthcare products and clinicians
- AI chatbots are not HIPAA compliant
  - Patient privacy not protected by federal law
  - Privacy and confidentiality are cornerstones of effective psychotherapy
- Insufficient evidence base for safety and efficacy
  - Technology too novel for adequate data
  - Corporate metrics (downloads, engagement, satisfaction) don't demonstrate effectiveness
- LLMs prone to dangerous "hallucinations"
  - Documented cases of AI encouraging suicidal people to take action
  - AI has provided information about bridge/building heights for jumping
- Therapist-patient relationship is main predictor of psychotherapy outcomes
  - AI cannot replicate human relationship
  - Seriously limited as clinical treatment tool
- Corporations lack accountability
  - No meaningful incentives to self-regulate without federal oversight
  - Legal infrastructure for holding them accountable is not well-established
- Accreditation and credentialing challenges
  - Must look beyond narrow financial interests of insurance entities and AI corporations

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Represents perspective of 6,500+ mental health clinicians and 90 partner organizations on AI in their field
- Points out fundamental tension: decades of research confirm the therapist-patient relationship is the main predictor of psychotherapy outcomes, yet AI by definition cannot provide a human relationship
- Highlights that public adoption of AI (millions of users) has far outpaced the science demonstrating safety and efficacy
- Notes unclear use case for AI in behavioral health: support tool, replacement for therapists, or administrative/insurance processing aid
- Emphasizes SAMHSA's definition of recovery (self-directed life over time) as the appropriate standard, not mere symptom reduction

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI LLMs have encouraged vulnerable people struggling with suicide to take suicidal action or provided them with information about the heights of bridges or buildings from which to jump. This is unacceptably risky Chatbot behavior that literally threatens lives."
- "Since AI is not, in fact, a human relationship, it is seriously limited as a potential clinical treatment tool."
- "Even though LLMs are currently being used by millions of people, this adoption by the public is far ahead of the essential science demonstrating privacy, safety, and efficacy."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- This response is based on experience testing and deploying healthcare AI for care in healthcare organizations
- Uncertainty of healthcare staff is a key issue that must be addressed to improve AI adoption
  - This is a common finding in multiple Western jurisdictions
  - Fundamental to addressing this issue is evidence that an AI product works locally
  - The need for this evidence is unsurprising as medicine is empirical
- Multiple research studies have highlighted that AI products are sensitive to their environment
  - An AI product that works well in one healthcare organization may not work well in another, geographically close organization
  - Therefore, rapid local evaluations of AI products help improve confidence that they can be trusted in live care
  - This should be done with a local sandbox, which is something we provide to healthcare organizations
- Regarding question 4 of this RFI
  - The methodology of evaluation is relative to the organization and product involved
  - Some organizations may need to test usability as well as effectiveness
  - Post-deployment methodologies to ensure safety are significant because of AI's sensitivity to its environment, and an environment can change
  - Emerging best practice to address this is a continuous monitoring dashboard
    - We have developed such a dashboard
    - Worked with the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency in the UK on a project regarding it, which was favorable to its use
- The regulation section of this RFI highlights the importance of protecting the confidentiality of patient health information
  - A key way of ensuring this is by keeping data within the estate of the healthcare organization
  - This is achieved by using an AI deployment platform
  - An added benefit is that established technical infrastructure can be reused for other AI deployments, reducing the time to use in care
- For question 5, it is important that local evaluations are done independently of the vendor to cultivate confidence in their findings
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Healthcare AI deployment company argues that local sandbox testing, continuous monitoring dashboards, and vendor-independent evaluations are essential to building clinician trust and ensuring AI products actually work in each organization's unique environment.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Newton's Tree
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Healthcare AI testing and deployment; has developed local sandbox tools and continuous monitoring dashboards for healthcare organizations
- **Geographic Scope:** International (references work with UK's MHRA and findings across Western jurisdictions)
- **Stake in Issue:** Provides AI deployment platforms and evaluation tools to healthcare organizations; directly affected by how AI adoption and evaluation requirements are structured
structuredSections.corePosition: Healthcare AI products are highly sensitive to their local environment—what works at one organization may fail at another nearby. Building clinician trust requires rapid local evaluations using sandboxes, continuous monitoring dashboards for post-deployment safety, and keeping patient data within the healthcare organization's own infrastructure.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Implement local sandbox environments for rapid AI evaluation before live deployment
  - Helps build clinician confidence that products work in their specific setting
- Tailor evaluation methodology to each organization and product
  - Some may need usability testing in addition to effectiveness testing
- Deploy continuous monitoring dashboards for post-deployment safety
  - Addresses the reality that healthcare environments change over time
- Keep patient data within the healthcare organization's estate using AI deployment platforms
  - Protects confidentiality
  - Allows infrastructure reuse for future AI deployments, reducing time to implementation
- Ensure local evaluations are conducted independently of the AI vendor
  - Cultivates confidence in evaluation findings
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Healthcare staff uncertainty is a major barrier to AI adoption across Western jurisdictions
- AI products are sensitive to their environment and may not transfer well between organizations
  - Geographic proximity does not guarantee similar performance
- Post-deployment safety is at risk because environments change over time
- Patient health information confidentiality must be protected
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Collaborated with the UK's Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) on a continuous monitoring dashboard project, which received favorable reception
- Provides local sandbox environments to healthcare organizations for AI testing
- Observation that medicine's empirical nature makes local evidence essential for AI trust
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "An AI product that works well in one healthcare organisation, may not work well in another, geographically close organisation."
- "It is important that local evaluations...are done independently of the vendor to cultivate confidence in their findings."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- MediView XR represents a translational application of "Physical AI"
  - Integration of artificial intelligence, real-time 3D medical data, and spatial computing directly into the physical act of clinical care delivery
  - Unlike retrospective analytics or decision-support systems that generate insight removed from execution, MediView's platform operates in situ during live procedures
  - System fuses multimodal imaging data (CT, MRI, ultrasound) with AI-enabled segmentation, volumetric reconstruction, spatial registration, and mixed reality visualization
  - Provides spatially aligned, patient-specific guidance during care
  - Closes the loop between data → cognition → physical execution

- Platform embeds AI within the visualization and procedural workflow layer
  - Transforms static imaging into dynamic, actionable spatial intelligence
  - Human-in-the-loop approach maintains clinician authority and oversight
  - Reduces cognitive load, enhances anatomical comprehension, supports precision during high-acuity and time-sensitive interventions
  - Same infrastructure enables immersive medical education and training, remote expert collaboration, and telemedicine with shared holographic visualization
  - Expands access to subspecialty expertise across rural, underserved, and resource-constrained settings

- Defining Physical AI in Clinical Care
  - Most healthcare AI systems today focus on image interpretation, predictive analytics, risk scoring, or retrospective decision support
  - These tools often stop at generating insight, requiring clinicians to translate outputs into physical action
  - Physical AI differs in that it operates in real time, maintains spatial awareness of anatomy and instruments, preserves human-in-the-loop control, and directly influences procedural execution and outcomes
  - MediView's mixed reality platform fuses CT, MRI, live ultrasound, and tracking data into AI-assisted 3D anatomical reconstructions spatially registered to the patient during care and remotely connected for collaborative care delivery

- Technical Architecture and Embedded AI
  - MediView XR functions as a real-time spatial computing platform integrating:
    - Pre-operative and intra-operative imaging (CT, MRI, ultrasound)
    - AI-enabled segmentation and image registration
    - Continuous spatial alignment and instrument tracking
    - Mixed Reality visualization via head-mounted displays
  - AI processes include automated anatomical segmentation, volumetric reconstruction, multi-modal image fusion, and dynamic spatial registration
  - These processes are embedded within the visualization layer rather than delivered as separate analytic outputs
  - Transforms static imaging into dynamic, patient-specific procedural guidance
  - System translates disparate data inputs from existing and emerging technologies into real-world visual 3D representations, tracking and guidance for imaging, diagnostics and procedural delivery
  - Spatial intelligence reduces cognitive load, improves hand-eye coordination, enhances anatomical comprehension, and increases procedural confidence
  - With connectivity, system allows practitioners to remotely collaborate for physical care delivery across rural, underserved, or understaffed locations

- Clinical Applications of Physical AI
  - Education and Training
    - Enables 3D anatomy-accurate training and procedural rehearsal using patient-specific data
    - Mixed Reality environments allow clinicians to understand spatial relationships rather than memorizing steps
    - Accelerates skill acquisition and improves retention
  - Telemedicine and Remote Expert Guidance
    - Shared holographic visualization allows remote experts to observe precisely what frontline clinicians see
    - Facilitates real-time procedural guidance with spatial fidelity
    - Expands access to subspecialty expertise and reduces unnecessary patient transfers
  - From Trauma and Emergency Care to Complex Procedures
    - In acute and resource-constrained environments, Physical AI supports rapid localization of internal anatomy
    - Enables image-guided interventions without reliance on multiple external monitors or complex imaging workstations

- Barriers to Innovation and Adoption
  - Key barriers include need for investment for integration of varying technologies into the mixed reality ecosystem and related collaboration

- Regulatory and Payment Policy Priorities
  - HHS can accelerate adoption through targeted investment into the foundational platform
  - Clarify predictable regulatory pathways for Physical AI platforms that blend medical devices, Software as a Medical Device (SaMD), and AI-enabled workflows
  - Lifecycle evaluation models for adaptive systems should be encouraged
  - Reimbursement reforms should explore bundled or value-based models recognizing improved first-pass success, reduced complications, and downstream cost avoidance

- Legal and Implementation Considerations
  - Human-in-the-loop AI systems require clear governance structures around liability, transparency, data security, and accountability
  - HHS can support guidance clarifying roles and responsibilities for clinicians and technology providers in AI-supported care environments

- Evaluation Methods and Metrics
  - Promising evaluation methods include:
    - Prospective clinical trials
    - Real-world evidence studies
    - Human factors testing
    - Cognitive load measurement
    - Robustness testing under workflow variability
    - Post-deployment performance monitoring

- Supporting Private Sector Activities
  - HHS can encourage accreditation programs, industry-driven benchmarking, certification pathways, and public-private collaborations to standardize evaluation metrics for Physical AI systems

- High-Impact AI Use Cases
  - AI tools that directly enhance procedural accuracy, reduce complication rates, shorten time to intervention, and improve system efficiency hold tremendous potential
  - Can improve outcomes, lower costs, and democratize care across populations

- Organizational Adoption Dynamics
  - Clinical leadership, hospital administrators, IT governance bodies, and reimbursement decision-makers significantly influence AI adoption
  - Administrative hurdles include procurement complexity, integration challenges, and uncertainty regarding reimbursement

- Interoperability Needs
  - Enhanced interoperability needed for:
    - 3D volumetric imaging
    - Spatial metadata
    - Procedural data standards
    - AI model integration
  - Would accelerate innovation and widen market opportunities

- Patient and Caregiver Perspectives
  - Patients seek improved accuracy, reduced complications, shorter procedures, and expanded access to expertise
  - Concerns include transparency, data privacy, safety, and preservation of clinician oversight

- Research and Investment Priorities
  - HHS should prioritize investment in the foundational platform and applied clinical research evaluating Physical AI's impact on:
    - Outcomes
    - Workflow efficiency
    - Human factors
    - Cost-effectiveness
  - Public-private partnerships can accelerate evidence generation and responsible adoption

- Metrics Framework
  - Evaluation domains include:
    - Clinical performance: accuracy, complication reduction, success rates
    - Operational efficiency: time to intervention, procedural duration
    - Human factors: cognitive load, fatigue, ergonomics
    - System-level impact: cost avoidance, resource utilization, training efficiency
    - Expansion of higher-level care delivery capabilities to remote and underserved populations
  - Quantitative metrics will continue to mature as deployment expands

- Physical AI represents the next evolution of healthcare AI by embedding intelligence directly into the physical act of care delivery
  - With targeted investment and sustained research, platforms can accelerate AI adoption, improve outcomes, reduce system burden, and strengthen public trust in advanced healthcare technologies
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Medical mixed reality company advocates for "Physical AI" that embeds real-time 3D visualization and AI guidance directly into live procedures, seeking clearer regulatory pathways for blended device-SaMD-AI platforms and value-based reimbursement recognizing improved outcomes.

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Company overview and AI deployment
  - Telemedicine provider exclusively focused on behavioral and mental health care
  - Built and actively deploys suite of native, proprietary AI tools deeply integrated into clinical platform
  - Located in San Jose, CA

- Current AI stack in production
  - Ambient Chart Generation AI
    - Listens to visits in real time
    - Utilizes intake data with explicit patient consent
    - Produces structured, clinically dense draft notes with high fidelity
    - Provides incremental updates every minute so practitioners don't wait until end of visit
  - Medical Chart Review AI
    - Scans every chart post-visit
    - Identifies compliance gaps, missing documentation, protocol deviations, quality indicators
    - Provides feedback to clinicians after each visit
  - AI Receptionist and Modular Agents
    - Handle administrative, non-clinical automated patient communications
    - Routine follow-up messaging, adherence prompts, scalable operational support
    - All under strict human oversight

- Demonstrated results from AI tools
  - Reduced administrative burden by 10-15 minutes per visit previously spent on documentation
  - Improved note accuracy and completeness
  - Strengthened compliance posture
  - Helped mitigate provider burnout
  - Context: projections estimate deficit of over 21,000 psychiatrists by 2030

- Barriers to private sector AI innovation
  - Misaligned payment incentives
    - Fee-for-service rewards visit length rather than efficiency or quality
    - Tools reducing documentation time don't generate proportional revenue under traditional billing
    - Discourages adoption even when tools clearly improve care and provider well-being
  - Regulatory uncertainty for non-device AI
    - Ambient documentation, chart auditing, workflow agents occupy ambiguous space
    - Not traditional SaMD but still carry potential liability exposure
    - Lack of clear, proportionate guidance creates hesitation
  - Evaluation and trust gaps
    - No standardized benchmarks for behavioral health AI performance
    - Hard to build confidence or compare solutions
    - Need metrics for accuracy in capturing emotional nuance, avoiding cultural bias
  - Complex web of regulators with differing priorities
    - Federal and state agencies: DEA, CMS, SAMHSA, HHS OCR, state licensing boards, law enforcement
    - Overlapping but not fully aligned rules on prescribing, privacy, reimbursement, professional conduct
    - FDA approvals necessary for CDS systems
    - Navigating this web is complicated and potentially unresolved in current legal framework

- Recommended regulatory and payment policy changes
  - CMS payment modernization
    - Introduce add-on codes, modifiers, or value-based incentives
    - Explicitly recognize time savings and outcome improvements from AI-augmented services
    - When efficiency translates to financial benefit, adoption surges
  - Proportionate, clear guidance for non-device AI
    - Publish targeted HHS/ONC/ASTP guidance
    - Distinguish low-risk assistive tools (requiring transparency, consent, human review) from higher-risk diagnostic/predictive software
    - Reduces uncertainty without imposing unnecessary pre-market burdens
  - Cross-agency AI in behavioral health committee
    - Include DEA and FDA
    - Focus on resolving barriers to AI innovation
    - Would significantly accelerate unified decision-making across regulatory landscapes
  - Behavioral health-focused interoperability enhancements
    - Expand USCDI to include mental health elements
    - Include visit transcripts, PHQ/GAD scores, treatment rationales, longitudinal outcomes
    - Mandate secure, standardized APIs for benchmarking and federated learning while preserving privacy
  - Innovation pilots and funding
    - Launch grant programs, cooperative agreements, challenge competitions
    - Focus specifically on mental health AI in shortage areas
    - Test real-world impact on access, outcomes, cost with public reporting
    - MEDvidi eager to participate and contribute anonymized insights

- Novel issues for non-medical-device AI tools
  - Consent and transparency for ambient audio processing
    - Granular disclosure, handling of bystanders, easy opt-out
  - Liability allocation in multi-vendor ecosystems
    - Who is responsible if AI-drafted note contributes to oversight?
  - Heightened privacy risks with conversational data in mental health contexts
  - Subtle biases in language models
    - May misinterpret cultural idioms, emotional tone, non-verbal cues
  - Security and privacy attack vectors like prompt injection
    - Already managed using guardrails that review AI before and after invocation
  - Non-deterministic nature of AI
    - Different results can be obtained for same input
    - Strategies like averaging across multiple tries help manage this
  - Workflow disruption if tools require excessive clinician editing or produce inconsistent outputs

- Promising evaluation methods
  - Pre- and post-deployment comparative studies
    - Measure note accuracy vs. gold-standard manual drafts
    - Track time saved, patient satisfaction, clinical outcomes
  - Continuous real-world monitoring dashboards
    - Track error rates, human escalation frequency
    - Monitor bias indicators across demographics
    - Capture provider-reported usability
  - Simulation-based testing under realistic conditions
    - Test with accents, background noise, interrupted sessions
  - Longitudinal tracking of downstream effects
    - Improved follow-up rates, medication adherence, reduced no-shows
  - LLM as a Judge approach
    - Separate AI instance evaluates outputs for compliance with guidelines
  - Human annotation of AI outputs feeds back into improvement
  - Evaluating alignment between independent AI output and human output

- Recommended HHS role in evaluation
  - Provide seed funding or cooperative agreements for independent third-party validations
    - Especially for behavioral health applications
  - Endorse voluntary industry accreditation or certification frameworks
  - Organize prize competitions or collaborative challenges
    - Establish benchmarks for specialty-specific AI performance
  - Facilitate de-identified data-sharing consortia for robust, diverse model training

- Where AI has exceeded expectations
  - Ambient Chart Generation AI
    - Produces notes more complete and chronologically accurate than manual drafts
    - Clinicians edit in under two minutes instead of 10-15
    - Yields hours of work saved per day
  - Chart Review AI
    - Flags compliance and quality issues at 100% scale
    - Impossible to achieve manually
    - Maintains standards without added overhead
    - Doesn't replace human oversight but provides additional protection layer
  - Overall results: fewer documentation deficiencies, improved provider satisfaction

- Where AI has fallen short
  - Generic foundation models without behavioral health fine-tuning
    - Often miss sarcasm, contextual history, cultural expressions of distress
    - Produce bland or inaccurate outputs
  - Chart Review AI can be overly restrictive
    - Results in false positives
    - Raises red flags where clinical committee doesn't see issues

- AI tools with greatest potential
  - Modular, supervised AI agents for:
    - Routine follow-ups and dosage titration
    - Real-time evidence-based suggestions during visits
    - Proactive compliance auditing
    - Scalable virtual support in high-shortage regions
  - In mental health, could expand access dramatically while preserving human judgment for complex cases

- Key players influencing adoption
  - Front-line clinicians experiencing burnout relief firsthand
  - Compliance, quality, and risk management teams needing defensible audit trails
  - C-suite and finance leaders focused on ROI and scalability
  - Operations staff integrating tools into daily workflows
  - Patients indirectly through demand for faster, more attentive care

- Interoperability opportunities
  - Standardized APIs for:
    - Ingesting visit audio/transcripts into AI pipelines
    - Returning reviewed notes to EHRs
    - Sharing aggregate outcomes for benchmarking
  - Federated learning frameworks
    - Allow collaborative model improvement without centralizing sensitive data

- Patient and caregiver concerns and responses
  - What patients value
    - Faster access, reduced wait times
    - Providers who appear more engaged
    - Many appreciate AI freeing clinicians from typing
  - Primary concerns
    - Privacy of recorded conversations
    - Fear AI could diminish empathy or replace human judgment
    - Potential for bias disadvantaging certain cultural or linguistic groups
  - How MEDvidi mitigates concerns
    - Clear upfront consent
    - Visible opt-out mechanisms
    - Explicit statements that clinicians retain final decision-making authority
    - Transparent explanations of how tools are used

- Priority AI research areas for HHS
  - Multimodal models combining audio, text, validated scales, outcomes for behavioral health
  - Implementation science examining AI integration into telehealth without disrupting therapeutic relationships
  - Cost-effectiveness analyses in value-based care settings
  - Equity and bias research ensuring performance across demographics, languages, socioeconomic groups
  - Public-private pilots generating real-world evidence in shortage areas
  - Patient risk modeling for adverse side effects and drug abuse potential

- MEDvidi's offer to collaborate
  - Prepared to share de-identified insights
  - Willing to participate in pilots
  - Ready to contribute to standards development
  - Plans to build AI Clinician capable of diagnosing, treating, prescribing in behavioral health
  - Prepared to move jointly with HHS to provide mental health coverage to all people in US

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- HHS AI RFI Response 2026
  - Submitted February 13, 2026 by Loretta Heinen, VP Business Development, SanLuca AI, Inc.

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - Regulatory ambiguity
    - AI utilization in care delivery is a new category - unclear where it fits and what rules apply
    - Challenge of addressing regulatory requirements without stunting innovation
  - Safety concerns and subsequent poor adoption
    - Need to establish safeguards governing AI interactions and content in patient health contexts
    - Specific controls needed to prevent inappropriate diagnosis, treatment recommendations, or other clinical misuse
  - Reimbursement barriers
    - Reimbursement traditionally tied to human interactions throughout care delivery
    - Shifting patient interaction components to technology or AI not formally recognized
    - Limits ability to bill and be reimbursed for services delivered through digital or hybrid models
  - Perceived risk of role displacement
    - Technology integration into clinical workflows generates clinician apprehension when it overlaps with their scope of care
    - This persists despite shortages in high-demand specialties like primary care and behavioral health
  - ROI considerations
    - Technology may be cost-competitive but providers evaluate it against broader concerns
    - Must demonstrate ability to drive improved clinical outcomes and lower total cost of care over time

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Reduce data-sharing friction
    - Clarify/standardize "safe" AI enablement under HIPAA via clearer implementation guidance
      - Permitted uses/disclosures and de-identification pathways needed
      - HIPAA permitted uses/disclosures baseline: 45 CFR § 164.502
      - De-identification standard (safe harbor / expert determination): 45 CFR § 164.514
      - Security/technical safeguards expectations for AI vendors as Business Associates: 45 CFR § 164.312
    - Modernize operational guidance for Part 2 data (SUD) to enable safe, consented exchange for whole-person AI support: 42 CFR Part 2
  - Make interoperability real so AI can work across settings
    - Strengthen enforcement and clarity around information blocking to improve access to EHI for care and evaluation
    - Include reasonable exceptions: 45 CFR Part 171

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Liability ambiguity
    - "Who's responsible" when AI advice contributes to harm - developer vs. clinician vs. health system
    - Especially complex with continuously learning systems
    - Need to clearly define where liability of one participant in a care journey ends and the next begins
    - This will inform regulatory needs and requirements for each participant
  - Indemnification and contracting
    - Health systems increasingly demand strong indemnities, audit rights, model-change notices, and incident response SLAs
  - Privacy/security
    - Training, logging, and telemetry can expand PHI exposure
    - Needs BAAs, least-privilege access, and clear retention policies
  - IP and data rights
    - Unclear who owns derived features, fine-tuned weights, and performance telemetry
  - HHS role
    - Provide model governance playbooks
    - Develop standard contract clauses
    - Offer best-practice guidance for PHI-minimizing architectures
    - Avoid forcing everything into "device" pathways

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Evaluation essentials regardless of regulatory classification
    - Begin with clear articulation of intended use, target population, clinical setting, and specific decisions the system influences
    - Clearly define level of autonomy: informational support to conditional recommendation to independent action within defined boundaries
  - Pre-deployment assessment
    - Retrospective validation across diverse and representative datasets
    - External testing in independent health systems
    - Performance analysis across clinically relevant subgroups
    - Assess calibration, clinically meaningful operating thresholds, and decision analytic impact (beyond discrimination measures)
  - Prospective evaluation methods
    - Silent deployments, pragmatic trials, or cluster-based study designs
    - Provide insight into real-world performance before widespread rollout
  - Safety through technical safeguards
    - Structured hazard analysis for foreseeable failure modes
      - Incorrect recommendations, omissions, automation bias, hallucinated outputs, performance degradation under distribution shift or missing data
    - For systems with greater independence: clear scope limitations, rule-based constraints aligned with clinical guidelines, confidence thresholds triggering human review, automatic fallbacks when inputs fall outside validated conditions
  - Safety through operational controls
    - Real-time monitoring and escalation pathways

- Question 5: How HHS can support private sector activities
  - Establish voluntary, lightweight certification focused on:
    - Safety case, monitoring plan, transparency, and interoperability readiness
    - Not "one-size-fits-all"
  - Fund/endorse independent test labs
  - Publish minimum reporting standards like "model cards" plus clinical workflow cards
  - Encourage industry-led credentialing for "AI safety officer / clinical AI steward" roles inside provider organizations

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met/exceeded or fallen short of expectations
  - Met/exceeded expectations
    - Imaging/screening triage and radiology support
      - Strong ROI when it reduces second reads or speeds throughput
      - AI-supported mammography screening trials showing noninferiority/safety in population screening
    - Documentation burden reduction ("ambient scribe" category)
      - Measurable time savings and clinician experience improvements in real-world/QI and trials
  - Fallen short
    - Predictive analysis
      - Sepsis prediction/early warning in some settings
      - Poor external validity, alert fatigue, and workflow mismatch
      - Epic model has published external validations showing limitations
  - Highest-potential novel tools
    - Digital Behavioral Health Tools
      - Use AI to deliver safe, protocol-based care (e.g., Cognitive Behavioral Therapy) at scale
      - Expand access and integrate behavioral health into chronic disease management
      - Address mental health gaps that worsen outcomes
    - Automated Referral Coordination
      - Streamline referrals by automating intake, data routing, scheduling, and returning consult notes to ordering provider
      - Include connections to community resources
      - Biggest barrier is fragmented EMR integration across systems
      - Solving this would enable seamless, end-to-end care
    - Safety Analyzers
      - Use AI to review clinic conversations and notes to detect hidden medical risks before they lead to ED visits

- Question 7: Roles and decision makers influencing AI adoption
  - Influencers
    - CMIO/CNIO/CTO
    - Clinical department chairs
    - Quality/value-based care leadership
  - Primary hurdles
    - Security review
    - EHR integration (SMART/FHIR plus workflow)
    - Clinical governance approval
    - Safety protocols
    - Proving ROI under real constraints (staffing, bandwidth)

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen opportunities
  - Data types needed
    - Longitudinal meds, labs, problem lists, imaging metadata, referrals, scheduling, utilization/claims, SDOH, and patient-reported outcomes
  - Standards recommendations
    - Expand consistent use of FHIR resources plus USCDI-aligned data quality
    - Event-driven APIs for near-real-time updates
    - Provide transition tools and methodologies for systems with legacy EHR implementations to FHIR standards
    - Explore usage of Model Context Protocol (MCP) as optional access method
    - Continue incorporating human-in-the-loop safety monitoring and escalation frameworks
  - Benchmarking needs
    - Shared, de-identified evaluation corpora for:
      - Longitudinal prediction
      - Summarization safety
      - Care-gap detection
    - Include subgroup reporting and drift checks

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver perspectives
  - What patients/caregivers want addressed
    - Faster access, fewer delays
    - Clearer care plans
    - Reduced paperwork
    - Proactive outreach (especially for chronic care)
  - Concerns
    - Patient safety
    - Data privacy
    - Bias/inequity
    - Over-automation (loss of human judgment)
    - Job displacement
    - Unclear recourse when AI is wrong

- Question 10: AI research priorities for HHS
  - Priority research areas
    - Pragmatic, multi-site clinical trials of AI-in-workflow (not just retrospective AUC)
    - Bias and equity measurement standards tied to real outcomes
    - Efficacy of AI- vs human-delivered mental health care (cognitive behavioral therapy)
    - Downstream impacts of integrated care via AI on clinical goals of chronic condition management
  - Published findings on impact
    - Dr. Jessica Schleider has completed studies comparing virtually-delivered to human-delivered single session interventions
      - Outcomes similar in both groups
      - Research study currently being designed to extend this hypothesis exploring different treatment delivery modalities
      - Will compare AI-delivered care to human-delivered care specifically for single session interventions
  - Costs/benefits/transfers in the literature
    - Benefits often show up first as time saved, throughput, and clinician experience
    - Financial ROI can lag or shift across stakeholders (provider vs payer)

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
An AI health tech company offers a comprehensive regulatory roadmap for clinical AI adoption, calling for clearer HIPAA guidance, liability frameworks, and lightweight certification while highlighting digital behavioral health and referral coordination as the highest-potential applications.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** SanLuca AI, Inc. (submitted by Loretta Heinen, VP Business Development)
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** AI health technology company developing clinical AI solutions
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct interest as an AI vendor navigating regulatory uncertainty, data-sharing requirements, and health system contracting for clinical AI products

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We believe regulatory ambiguity, reimbursement barriers, and data-sharing friction are the primary obstacles preventing AI from reaching its potential in clinical care. HHS should provide clearer HIPAA implementation guidance, establish voluntary lightweight certification pathways, and define liability boundaries—without forcing all AI into device regulatory pathways. The goal should be enabling innovation while maintaining safety through practical governance frameworks rather than one-size-fits-all requirements.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Clarify "safe" AI enablement under HIPAA with clearer implementation guidance on permitted uses/disclosures and de-identification pathways
  - Specific CFR citations: 45 CFR § 164.502, § 164.514, § 164.312
- Modernize Part 2 (SUD) operational guidance to enable safe, consented exchange for whole-person AI support (42 CFR Part 2)
- Strengthen enforcement and clarity around information blocking rules (45 CFR Part 171)
- Establish voluntary, lightweight certification focused on safety case, monitoring plan, transparency, and interoperability readiness
  - Avoid "one-size-fits-all" approaches
- Fund/endorse independent test labs and publish minimum reporting standards ("model cards" plus clinical workflow cards)
- Encourage industry-led credentialing for "AI safety officer / clinical AI steward" roles inside provider organizations
- Provide model governance playbooks, standard contract clauses, and best-practice guidance for PHI-minimizing architectures
- Prioritize pragmatic, multi-site clinical trials of AI-in-workflow (not just retrospective AUC studies)
- Develop bias and equity measurement standards tied to real outcomes
- Expand FHIR resources with USCDI-aligned data quality and event-driven APIs for near-real-time updates
- Explore Model Context Protocol (MCP) as optional access method for AI systems

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Regulatory ambiguity creates uncertainty about where clinical AI fits and what rules apply
  - Risk of stunting innovation while trying to address regulatory requirements
- Reimbursement tied to human interactions doesn't recognize AI-delivered or hybrid care models
  - Limits ability to bill for services delivered through digital models
- Liability ambiguity when AI advice contributes to harm
  - Unclear responsibility among developer, clinician, and health system
  - Especially complex with continuously learning systems
- Health systems demanding strong indemnities, audit rights, model-change notices, and incident response SLAs
  - Creates contracting friction for AI vendors
- Training, logging, and telemetry expanding PHI exposure
- Unclear ownership of derived features, fine-tuned weights, and performance telemetry
- Clinician apprehension about role displacement despite workforce shortages
- Predictive AI (like sepsis prediction) falling short due to poor external validity, alert fatigue, and workflow mismatch
- Fragmented EMR integration across systems blocking referral coordination automation
- Patient concerns about safety, privacy, bias, over-automation, and unclear recourse when AI is wrong

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a paradox: clinicians fear AI role displacement despite persistent shortages in primary care and behavioral health—the very areas where AI could help most
- Points to specific AI success stories: ambient scribe tools showing measurable time savings, and AI-supported mammography screening trials demonstrating noninferiority/safety
- Highlights Epic's sepsis prediction model as a cautionary tale with published external validations showing limitations
- Proposes "Safety Analyzers" as a novel tool category: AI that reviews clinic conversations and notes to detect hidden medical risks before they lead to ED visits
- Suggests exploring Model Context Protocol (MCP) as an emerging standard for AI system access
- References Dr. Jessica Schleider's research showing virtually-delivered single session interventions achieve similar outcomes to human-delivered ones
  - Notes research is being designed to extend this to AI-delivered vs. human-delivered care comparison
- Observes that AI benefits often appear first as time saved and clinician experience improvements, while financial ROI lags or shifts across stakeholders

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The need to clearly define where liability of one participant in a care journey ends and the next begins will inform regulatory needs & requirements for each participant."
- "Benefits often show up first as time saved, throughput, and clinician experience, while financial ROI can lag or shift across stakeholders (provider vs payer)."
- "The integration of technology into clinical workflows can generate apprehension among clinicians when it overlaps with their scope of care, despite persistent shortages in high-demand specialties like primary care and behavioral health."
