structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- State-by-state regulatory fragmentation creates major barriers to responsible AI innovation
  - Requirements for consent, data use, and clinical accountability vary significantly across states
  - States like Illinois impose stricter standards requiring design to most restrictive interpretation
  - Increases development cost, slows testing, impedes responsible scaling

- Structural accountability gap for AI in clinical workflows
  - Vendor contracts typically disclaim clinical liability
  - Providers bear full responsibility without clear rules governing acceptable AI use
  - Existing frameworks designed for human decision-making haven't adapted to AI-supported care

- AI tools can introduce risk when outputs lack appropriate context, limitations, or uncertainty signals
  - Particularly concerning in mental health where therapeutic relationships, patient history, and developmental context are central
  - Overgeneralization or overconfidence can unintentionally influence clinical judgment

- Clinician concerns about energy intensity and environmental impact of AI technologies
  - Can meaningfully limit willingness to adopt or expand AI use
  - No shared standards or best practices for energy-efficient deployment

- Patient and caregiver concerns about privacy, accuracy, transparency, and risk that AI could replace human judgment or weaken therapeutic relationships
  - Especially pronounced in pediatric mental health care

- Many AI applications reducing clinician burden lack clear reimbursement pathways
  - Limits investment in higher-value use cases

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Conducted formal case study with Wharton School's Health Care Management Department on AI scribe implementation
  - Documented measurable reductions in clinician documentation burden
  - Showed improvements in clinician experience through human-in-the-loop, clinically governed deployment model

- In certain well-defined use cases like matching patients to appropriate levels of care or identifying clinical fit, AI tools can perform at or above human benchmarks

- Challenges arise not from AI's analytical capability but from how decision-support outputs are framed, scoped, and integrated into workflows

- Environmental concerns among clinicians represent an emerging and underappreciated barrier to AI adoption
  - Clinicians are becoming more aware of computational and energy demands
  - Organizations left to navigate sustainability tradeoffs independently

- Organizations prioritizing patient safety must limit experimentation and narrowly scope AI use cases regardless of technical readiness due to regulatory uncertainty

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The result is a system in which responsibility is concentrated on providers, but the rules governing acceptable AI use remain underdeveloped."

- "In complex and nuanced care scenarios such as mental health, where therapeutic relationships, patient history, and developmental context are central, overgeneralization or overconfidence in AI outputs can unintentionally influence clinical judgment."

- "Responsible AI adoption must therefore prioritize clear communication about how AI is used, strong human oversight, and safeguards that reinforce, rather than diminish the human elements of care that patients and families value most."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Response to HHS Request for Information on Accelerating AI Adoption in Healthcare (Federal Register Doc. 2025-23641)
- State-led Health Command framework represents a structured, governance-based approach to integrating AI into clinical care
  - Preserves licensed provider authority
  - Strengthens rural healthcare institutions
  - Addresses workforce shortages, rising costs, and aging populations
- Health Command operates as a practical intermediary AI model
  - Augments clinicians rather than replacing them
  - Performs triage, care navigation, and workflow support
  - Escalates patients to licensed providers when appropriate
  - Reinforces local healthcare networks rather than displacing them
- Alabama and Kentucky have introduced legislation to implement Health Command
  - Alabama Senate Bill 97 (SB97)
  - Kentucky Senate Bill 175 (SB175)
  - Both establish state-guided AI healthcare frameworks
  - Propose federal partnership with HHS
- Avatar Tethering methodology is a distinguishing patented component
  - Enables contextual, relevance-based coordination among patients, providers, institutions, and approved partners
  - Improves care continuity
  - Enhances preventive engagement
  - Supports measurable reductions in unnecessary ER utilization
  - Incorporates transparent governance and oversight mechanisms
  - Ensures compliance with privacy, civil rights, and patient protection standards
- Health Command addresses payment system modernization concerns
  - Improves early intervention and chronic care management
  - Reduces duplicative utilization
  - Enables value-aligned partnerships
  - Creates modest, ethical revenue streams to stabilize rural hospitals and clinics
- Revenue generation capabilities through Avatar Tethering
  - Pharmaceutical sponsorships
  - Contextual advertising during AI interactions
  - Telemedicine services during escalation
  - Revenue shared with participating rural healthcare institutions
- Legislative materials and technical documentation submitted for review
  - Cover letter and overview
  - Avatar Tethering Framework document
  - Avatar Tethering Methodology technical appendix
  - Alabama SB97 bill text
  - Kentucky SB175 bill text
  - Presentation materials
  - Visual representations of state avatars and revenue models

- Health Command System Architecture
  - Operates as non-autonomous AI intermediary platform
  - Performs triage and care navigation
  - Supports chronic disease monitoring
  - Routes patients to licensed providers
  - Assists with workforce documentation and workflow
  - Integrates telehealth escalation pathways
  - Licensed clinicians retain authority for diagnosis, prescribing, and treatment decisions

- Regulatory Alignment
  - HIPAA/Privacy compliance through business associate frameworks, data minimization, role-based access, audit logging
  - FDA alignment as non-autonomous workflow support with human-in-the-loop oversight
  - CMS payment policy support for value-based care
  - Civil rights and bias mitigation through human oversight safeguards and algorithmic auditing

- Recommendations for HHS Regulatory Framework
  - Clarify AI intermediary classification for care navigation and workflow augmentation tools
  - Create risk-based regulatory framework distinguishing between:
    - Informational AI tools
    - Workflow augmentation tools
    - Clinical decision support tools
    - Autonomous treatment systems
  - Establish safe harbor pathways for state-guided AI systems operating under licensed clinician oversight

- Reimbursement Recommendations
  - Establish reimbursement codes for AI-augmented triage and navigation
  - Allow shared savings models for states piloting AI intermediary systems
  - Enable Health Command-style systems to participate in CMMI demonstration projects
  - Avatar Tethering creates contextual educational and sponsorship partnerships for revenue support

- Research and Development Opportunities
  - Cooperative Research and Development Agreements (CRADAs)
  - Applied AI implementation research
  - Rural care delivery demonstration pilots
  - Intermediary AI evaluation research on triage accuracy, workflow efficiency, cost reductions
  - Implementation science measuring adoption rates, provider acceptance, patient trust
  - Interoperability pilots integrating AI with EHR platforms using FHIR-based systems

- Barriers to AI Innovation Addressed
  - Regulatory ambiguity
  - Misaligned payment incentives
  - Liability uncertainty
  - Interoperability fragmentation
  - Provider adoption resistance
  - Health Command mitigates through state governance and provider integration

- Avatar Tethering Technical Details
  - Avatars represent digital identities for patients, providers, institutions, care navigators, sponsors, pharmaceutical information sources
  - Tethering refers to structured digital linking based on user needs, clinical context, and workflow stage
  - Contextual relevance engine operates on symptom-based triggers, preventive care indicators, chronic disease markers, workflow stage, geographic routing logic
  - Revenue sustainability model allows contextual educational sponsorship, ethical pharmaceutical information delivery, institutional partnership engagement, telehealth escalation routing

- Alabama SB97 Legislative Provisions
  - Establishes Alabama Health Command as independent agency
  - Director appointed by Governor with knowledge of AI-based technology platforms
  - Designates business entity headquartered in state to develop uniform, interoperable virtual AI-assisted health platform
  - Platform must use patented avatar-based virtual encounter system
  - Standards required for patent/licensing rights, health data sharing with privacy protection, guardrails against misinformation
  - Rural health care providers contracting with platform exempt from competitive bid laws
  - Effective date October 1, 2026

- Kentucky SB175 Legislative Provisions
  - Creates Kentucky Health Command within Commonwealth Office of Technology
  - Director appointed by Governor with AI technology experience
  - Must designate and contract with private sector entity by January 1, 2027
  - Establishes process for rural health care providers to contract for platform use
  - Revenue sharing process prioritizes reimbursing state for development costs
  - After cost recovery, distributes at least 80% of net advertising revenues to participating rural providers based on utilization rate
  - Permits display of commercial advertising, sponsored content, and paid promotions relevant to user health needs

- FastHealth Corporation Background
  - Headquartered in Tuscaloosa, Alabama
  - Nation's largest developer of rural hospital websites
  - Holds patent for Avatar Tethering system after U.S.P.T.O. appeal
  - Sole source provider for the technology
  - FastHealth.AI is a language model developer
  - Provides admin areas allowing rural hospitals to train AI with simple text prompts
  - Non-technical staff can administer AI

- Pilot AI Sites Currently Operating
  - Dale Medical Center (Alabama) - Rural Hospital AI
  - Mayers Memorial Hospital (California) - Rural Hospital AI
  - Choices for People (Missouri) - Rural Mental Health AI

- FastHealth Leadership
  - Kevin A. Foote, Chairman
  - James R. Andrews, M.D., Renowned Surgeon, Co-Founder
  - Robert C. Ross, Director
  - Ben F. Beard, Director

- Alabama state leaders requesting meeting with HHS regarding Health Command and SB97
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: FastHealth Corporation, an Alabama-based rural hospital technology company, proposes its patented "Health Command" AI platform with "Avatar Tethering" as a federally-partnered, state-governed model for rural healthcare transformation that generates revenue through pharmaceutical sponsorships while preserving clinician oversight.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** FastHealth Corporation / Kevin A. Foote, Chairman
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Nation's largest developer of rural hospital websites; holds patent for Avatar Tethering AI methodology; language model developer for healthcare applications
- **Geographic Scope:** National, headquartered in Tuscaloosa, Alabama with operations in Alabama, Kentucky, California, Missouri, and Washington D.C.
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct commercial interest as sole source provider of patented Avatar Tethering technology seeking federal partnership and adoption of their platform as national standard for rural healthcare AI
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe Health Command represents a state-led, governance-based model for AI integration that aligns directly with HHS's regulatory, reimbursement, and research objectives. The system preserves licensed provider authority while creating sustainable revenue streams for struggling rural hospitals through pharmaceutical sponsorships and contextual advertising during AI interactions. Alabama and Kentucky have introduced legislation to implement this framework, and we propose federal partnership with HHS to establish it as a national standard.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Clarify regulatory treatment of AI systems functioning as care navigation and workflow augmentation tools rather than autonomous diagnostic devices
- Create risk-based regulatory framework distinguishing between informational AI tools, workflow augmentation tools, clinical decision support tools, and autonomous treatment systems
- Establish safe harbor pathways for state-guided AI systems operating under licensed clinician oversight
- Create reimbursement codes for AI-augmented triage and navigation where measurable cost reductions occur
- Allow states piloting AI intermediary systems to participate in shared savings agreements tied to reduced ER utilization and improved quality metrics
- Enable Health Command-style systems to participate in CMMI demonstration projects to test alternative payment frameworks
- Invest in intermediary AI evaluation research studying triage accuracy, workflow efficiency gains, and cost reductions
- Support interoperability pilots integrating AI intermediary systems with existing EHR platforms using FHIR-based systems
- Consider Health Command as pilot or reference model for broader national implementation in partnership with HHS
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Rural healthcare systems face accelerating pressures from physician shortages, uncompensated ER utilization, aging populations, and payment model inefficiencies
- AI innovation is advancing rapidly without uniform governance structures or value-aligned reimbursement pathways
- Legacy fee-for-service payment structures discourage preventive care and rapid innovation adoption
- Regulatory ambiguity creates barriers to AI innovation
- Liability uncertainty for AI-assisted care models
- Interoperability fragmentation across healthcare systems
- Provider adoption resistance due to administrative burden and risk uncertainty
- Tightening budgets and declining traditional tax bases threaten rural hospital sustainability
- Fragmented private AI deployment without coordinated governance structures
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- FastHealth operates pilot AI sites at three rural healthcare facilities: Dale Medical Center (Alabama), Mayers Memorial Hospital (California), and Choices for People mental health facility (Missouri)
- The company has developed admin interfaces allowing non-technical rural hospital staff to train AI using simple text prompts
- Avatar Tethering methodology secured through U.S. Patent and Trademark Office appeal process
- Revenue model proposes pharmaceutical companies pay for contextual advertising during AI-patient interactions, with revenues shared with rural hospitals
  - Kentucky legislation specifies 80% of net advertising revenues go to participating rural providers after state recoups development costs
- System designed to allow AI avatars representing patients, providers, institutions, and pharmaceutical sponsors to be "tethered together" in unified visual environment during care interactions
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Rural hospitals having Ai implementation will now be able to generate new revenues from Avatar Tethering pharmaceutical sponsorships, ads, and telemedicine services during escalation of Ai processes and through referencing of interactions relevant to patient discourse and need."
- "Topic focused health discussions between ai and patients provide instant promotional value to pharmaceutical sponsorships that could generate billions for rural hospitals, rural clinics, and health departments needs."
- "Rather than fragmented adoption across independent systems, Health Command presents a coordinated, scalable framework that HHS could evaluate as a structured national model."

entities.category: Electronic Health Records and Health IT
entities.label: EHR

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and organizational background
  - AADA represents more than 17,500 dermatologists nationwide
  - Committed to excellence in medical and surgical treatment of skin disease
  - Advocates for high standards in clinical practice, education, and research
  - Drives continuous improvement in patient care while reducing disease burden

- Overall position on AI in healthcare
  - Supports thoughtful use of AI when it enables reliable, clinically accurate, high-quality patient care
  - AI should not overburden physicians
  - Urges HHS to implement ongoing evaluation to ensure tools maintain quality and effectiveness
  - Asks HHS to consider financial barriers to implementing AI systems
  - Concerned about AI impacts on diagnostic and utilization management tools including claims, coding, and prior authorization review

- Barriers to Private-Sector AI Innovation and Clinical Adoption
  - AI tools must never supplant or restrict patient access to in-person care when desired
  - Supports patient access to physician-led care and freedom to choose their own physicians, including specialists
  - Physicians must be able to access and implement AI if they choose
  - Financial cost to practices may be prohibitive, particularly for smaller, rural practices
  - Physicians should not be penalized for lacking resources to incorporate AI
  - HHS should develop guidance promoting AI affordability while accounting for necessary clinical expert oversight

- Regulatory, Payment Policy, and Programmatic Design Considerations
  - Requested guardrails for high-quality care delivery
    - Sensitive training data must comply with patient privacy laws and ethical standards
    - AI algorithms must use auditable data representative of the population it will serve
    - Patient care AI must undergo rigorous, transparent data validation
    - Routine quality and safety evaluation needed to assess model drifts
    - Ethical review required before deployment
    - Algorithms must be transparent, accessible, and comprehensible for intended end users
    - Design, implementation, and evaluation should incorporate input from physicians and patient advocates

- AI Payer Claims Review Programs
  - Major insurers have deployed AI to expedite claims review, potentially promoting timely reimbursement
  - Dermatologists report increasing payer downcoding of E/M claims using AI without requesting clinical records
    - Payers unilaterally adjust reported code levels and reduce payment automatically
    - Operating increasingly without notice to dermatologists
  - Improper downcoding consequences
    - Destabilizes accurate data collection and analyses regarding patient encounters
    - Undermines practices' financial stability
    - Wastes resources on appealing downcoded claims
    - Creates unnecessary administrative burden
  - Payer AI-adjusted risk scores in quality payment programs need appropriate attribution methodology based on valid, meaningful, transparent quality metrics
  - Human review necessary to ensure coding accuracy and prevent inappropriate downcoding and denials
    - Medical records demand careful judgment and thorough contextual understanding
    - AI systems may misinterpret nuanced or complex medical records
    - Skilled human coders should work in tandem with AI tools
    - Automation should support rather than replace human expertise
  - Flagged claims should be reviewed by healthcare professionals with relevant expertise examining patient records
  - Insurers should track and report accuracy of AI claim review programs to HHS
  - HHS should audit insurers whose AI claim review programs raise suspicions of misuse
  - Over-reliance on AI by payers results in real and sometimes irreversible harm to patients through delayed or inaccessible care
  - Payer AI use warrants special scrutiny to preserve clinical decision-making integrity and ensure timely access to medically necessary care
  - Recommendations for claims review
    - HHS should enhance regulatory oversight of AI-driven payer downcoding
    - Develop guidelines for best practices on using AI in claims review
    - Use AI as first step to flag claims suspected of inappropriate coding, followed by human examination
    - Ensure transparency and ongoing evaluation of AI in claims review

- Use of AI in Prior Authorizations
  - Poorly designed payer-driven prior authorization programs increase costs for physicians and patients
    - Result in delays in care
    - Add administrative burden to practices
    - Amplify patient stress
  - Novel AI tools have potential to alleviate prior authorization burdens
    - Ambient intelligence can populate clinical notes, capture patient data, and automatically complete prior authorization requests
    - Large language models can analyze clinical notes and images to strengthen prior authorization requests
    - AI-powered clinical decision support tools help ensure submissions meet payer-specific criteria
    - Automated systems can generate comprehensive prior authorization narratives based on patient records
  - Until AI systems are trained on clinical data from medical records rather than public data, payers' clinical staff must continue to review prior authorization denials
  - Recommendations for prior authorization
    - Implement public reporting requirements on payers' use of prior authorization to HHS
      - Include approval and denial rate by each service or medication
      - Would make payer prior authorization more transparent
      - Would enable AI systems to learn from and enhance the review process
    - Applauds Secretary Kennedy and Administrator Oz for securing 2025 insurer commitment to streamline prior authorization
    - Urges implementation of reporting requirements to ensure adherence to that commitment
    - Direct insurers to use AI solely for approving prior authorizations while requiring clinical staff to review denials
    - Offer grants to small, independent practices seeking to implement AI systems to relieve administrative burden

- AI in the Practice Setting: Physician Coding and Diagnostic Tools
  - Recognizes potential benefits for dermatology practices to integrate AI-driven coding and diagnostic tools
  - Continues to encourage inclusion of human examination in coding and diagnostics
  - Benefits of AI coding tools
    - Automate routine tasks such as code identification and compliance checks
    - Empowers dermatologists and coding staff to increase successful claims
    - Enables stronger focus on improving medical record documentation
    - Enhances AI accuracy in code selection
    - Results in fewer coding errors, more timely claim submissions, smoother revenue cycle
    - Contributes to greater operational efficiency and financial stability
  - Physicians may incorporate AI into diagnostic tools to improve efficiency, reduce tedium, and perform triage
  - Practices should consider AI as an assistant, not a replacement to human review
  - Caution urged on AI diagnostic tools
  - Contrast between payer and physician AI use
    - Payer AI too often results in barriers to care
    - Physicians adopt AI as supportive tool to enhance accuracy, efficiency, and patient outcomes
    - Physicians maintain critical human-in-the-loop oversight
  - Regulatory policies should aid physician access and avoid imposing additional compliance burdens

- Recommendations for practice-based AI
  - Most AI systems trained on public data, introducing accuracy and privacy issues
    - HIPAA adherence concerns with sensitive data
    - Presence of inaccurate or outdated information
    - Increased risk of errors due to claims complexity
  - HHS should convene work group of medical professionals and AI data experts to develop appropriate regulations on AI training and privacy
    - Dermatologists should be key part of this group given their work across wide range of services
  - Develop guidelines for best practices on AI diagnostic tools
    - Use AI as first step to review diagnostics and flag where additional human expert review is required before clinical decisions
  - Institute post-marketing surveillance of AI-driven coding tools to ensure safety
    - Routine evaluation will ensure technology evolves to protect patients and improve quality, cost, and effectiveness
  - Provide research grants focused on evaluating clinical effectiveness and patient safety of AI tools
    - Research will help protect patients and promote clinical effectiveness
    - Determine how deployment data can retrain and refine models for validation studies and post-deployment monitoring
    - Findings may enhance AI's potential to improve outcomes, give quality insights, and reduce costs
  - Collaborate with DOJ and OIG to identify regulations regarding physician liability when AI is used
    - AI imperfections may expose physicians to liability including medical malpractice concerns
    - AI for coding and billing without effective oversight may result in billing errors and overpayments with fraud and abuse implications
  - Establish safe harbor that clarifies and limits physician liability when AI is used as support tool
    - Applies in cases of documented clinical oversight, compliance protocols, and diagnostics
  - Financial cost of implementing AI systems deters independent physician practices
    - Costs include technology acquisition, integration, and staff training
    - Potential risks include system errors, underperformance, or failure to meet expectations
    - Practices deterred without clear evidence of return on investment
  - Offer federal and state grants to small, independent practices seeking to implement AI systems

- Conclusion
  - Applauds HHS focus on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
  - AI tools present opportunity to improve outcomes, give quality insights, and reduce costs
  - Tools also have potential to increase administrative burdens, delay care, and contribute to financial instability
  - Encourages HHS to thoroughly consider recommendations provided

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
National dermatology association representing 17,500 physicians supports AI adoption with strong guardrails, urging HHS to rein in payer AI that downcodes claims and delays care while helping small practices afford physician-controlled AI tools.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Academy of Dermatology Association (AADA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Professional medical association representing 17,500+ dermatologists; expertise in medical and surgical treatment of skin disease, dermatopathology, clinical practice standards
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Members directly affected by AI in claims processing, prior authorization, coding, and diagnostic tools; concerned about payer AI downcoding claims, financial barriers to AI adoption for smaller practices, and liability exposure

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We support thoughtful use of AI when it enables reliable, clinically accurate, high-quality patient care without overburdening physicians. However, payer-deployed AI is increasingly downcoding claims and denying prior authorizations without human review, causing real harm to patients and destabilizing practices. AI should support rather than replace human expertise, and physicians should have access to affordable AI tools without being penalized if they can't implement them.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Enhance regulatory oversight of AI-driven payer downcoding and develop best practice guidelines
  - Require AI to flag claims for human review rather than automatically adjusting payment
  - Require insurers to track and report accuracy of AI claim review programs to HHS
  - Audit insurers whose AI programs raise suspicions of misuse

- Reform prior authorization AI use
  - Implement public reporting requirements on payer prior authorization approval/denial rates by service or medication
  - Direct insurers to use AI solely for approving prior authorizations, requiring clinical staff to review all denials
  - Offer grants to small practices to implement AI systems that reduce prior authorization burden

- Establish AI development guardrails
  - Require sensitive training data to comply with privacy laws and ethical standards
  - Mandate auditable, representative training data
  - Require rigorous data validation, routine quality/safety evaluation for model drift, and ethical review before deployment
  - Ensure algorithms are transparent, accessible, and comprehensible for end users
  - Incorporate physician and patient advocate input in design, implementation, and evaluation

- Support physician AI adoption
  - Convene work group of medical professionals and AI data experts to develop regulations on AI training and privacy (include dermatologists)
  - Develop best practice guidelines for AI diagnostic tools emphasizing human review before clinical decisions
  - Institute post-marketing surveillance of AI-driven coding and diagnostic tools
  - Provide research grants evaluating clinical effectiveness and patient safety of AI tools

- Address liability concerns
  - Collaborate with DOJ and OIG to clarify physician liability regulations when AI is used
  - Establish safe harbor limiting physician liability when AI is used as support tool with documented clinical oversight and compliance protocols

- Reduce financial barriers
  - Offer federal and state grants to small, independent practices seeking to implement AI systems
  - Develop guidance promoting AI affordability while maintaining clinical expert oversight

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Payer AI claims review causing harm
  - Major insurers using AI to automatically downcode E/M claims without requesting clinical records
  - Operating increasingly without notice to dermatologists
  - Destabilizes accurate data collection and analyses
  - Undermines practices' financial stability
  - Wastes resources on appeals
  - Over-reliance results in delayed or inaccessible care—sometimes irreversible harm to patients

- AI limitations in understanding medical complexity
  - Medical records demand careful judgment and contextual understanding only human expertise can provide
  - AI systems may misinterpret nuanced or complex medical records
  - Most AI systems trained on public data, introducing accuracy and privacy issues
  - Until AI is trained on clinical data from medical records, payers' clinical staff must review denials

- Financial barriers to physician AI adoption
  - Costs of technology acquisition, integration, and staff training may be prohibitive
  - Particularly burdensome for smaller, rural practices
  - Potential risks (system errors, underperformance) deter adoption without clear ROI evidence
  - Physicians should not be penalized for lacking resources to incorporate AI

- Liability exposure
  - AI imperfections may expose physicians to medical malpractice liability
  - AI for coding and billing without effective oversight may result in billing errors with fraud and abuse implications

- Patient access concerns
  - AI tools must never supplant or restrict patient access to in-person care
  - Patients must retain freedom to choose their own physicians, including specialists

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Dermatologists report increasing instances of payers unilaterally adjusting E/M code levels and reducing payment automatically using AI tools, without first requesting and reviewing clinical records—and increasingly doing so without any notice to the physician
- The association draws a sharp contrast between how payers and physicians use AI: payers deploy it to create barriers to care, while physicians adopt it as a supportive tool to enhance accuracy and outcomes while maintaining human oversight
- Specific AI applications identified as beneficial for dermatology practices: ambient intelligence to populate clinical notes and auto-complete prior authorization requests, large language models to analyze clinical notes and images, AI-powered clinical decision support to ensure submissions meet payer-specific criteria
- Dermatologists should be included in any HHS work group on AI training and privacy given their work spans routine procedures to highly specialized treatments requiring precise data

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "While AI offers the potential to streamline claims processing and timely payment, over-reliance on the technology by payers results in real and—at times—irreversible harm to patients in the form of delayed or inaccessible care."

- "In contrast to payer use of AI, which too often results in barriers to care, physicians are adopting AI as a supportive tool to enhance accuracy, efficiency, and patient outcomes—and not to replace critical human-in-the-loop oversight."

- "The nuance and complexity of medical records demand careful judgement and a thorough contextual understanding that only human expertise can provide when determining the correct procedure modifiers and codes."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Executive Summary
  - ASRS supports HHS's goal of fostering regulatory, payment, and research environment for rapid, responsible AI adoption in clinical care
  - AI already demonstrates meaningful potential for retina specialists, particularly in image-based diagnoses and disease monitoring
  - Broader adoption is fundamentally constrained by systemic barriers HHS is uniquely positioned to address
  - Most significant barrier is lack of interoperable, standardized clinical and imaging data
    - Created by conflicting proprietary data formats
    - Incomplete implementation of existing standards
    - Misaligned vendor incentives
    - These challenges limit competition, raise costs, slow research, and reduce real-world AI effectiveness
  - ASRS urges HHS to prioritize:
    - Stronger enforcement and maturation of existing data standards (DICOM, FHIR, OMOP)
    - Payment policy modernization recognizing AI as clinical infrastructure capable of improving outcomes
    - Federal research and development oversight promoting open, sharable data and accelerating real-world implementation
    - NIH should withdraw or significantly modify its proposed Controlled-Data Access policy due to chilling effect on imaging data dissemination
    - Preventing private payer use of AI to deny care through prior authorization or recoup payments through auditing

- Background on Retina Specialists
  - Board-certified ophthalmologists with additional 1-2 year fellowship in vitreous body and retina care
  - Provide surgical care for retinal detachment and chronic care management for AMD, diabetic retinopathy, retinal vein occlusion, and inherited retinal diseases
  - Pioneered treatments including injectable medications, surgical techniques, and advanced imaging
  - Without these advances, millions of Americans would go blind every year
  - First FDA-cleared AI-enabled medical device is used for diabetic retinopathy screening
  - AI-assisted analyses of retinal and ocular data hold promise for diagnosing systemic diseases (cardiovascular, autoimmune, neurodegenerative)
  - ASRS created AI Task Force to identify integration opportunities and provide guidance

- Lack of Standardized Data
  - Greatest regulatory challenge facing AI adoption in retinal care
  - Gap between existence of standards (FHIR, DICOM, OMOP) and their implementation
  - Nearly unworkable for retina specialists and researchers to create large, aggregated, high-quality datasets essential for robust AI
  - Core problem in ophthalmology: widespread use of proprietary imaging formats and incomplete standards implementation by device manufacturers
    - Advanced imaging devices (OCT, fundus cameras, automated visual field analyzers) store data in proprietary formats
    - Even when using standardized formats, fail to standardize crucial metrics reporting (central subfield thickness, GA lesion size, subretinal fluid volume)
  - Non-structured or non-standardized EHR data lacks common nomenclature and coding for reliable aggregation
  - Clinical metrics like visual acuity lack standardized definitions across systems
  - ASRS is advocating to vendors that their proprietary, siloed approach hampers innovation
  - Private companies currently developing systems to convert proprietary outputs to standardized formats
  - HHS echoing need for standardization would amplify message and incentivize vendors
  - Recommends HHS enforce and strengthen existing data standards for EHRs and imaging/diagnostic equipment manufacturers
  - Recommends incentives rather than punitive measures to avoid passing costs to end users
  - Without addressing this data bottleneck, HHS cannot achieve AI advancement goals

- Barriers to Implementation in Physician Practices
  - Payment systems, particularly Medicare, must adapt as AI technologies integrate into practice
  - AI implementation may require significant initial capital outlay
  - Ongoing use creates additional physician work analyzing data and considering differential diagnoses
  - Current reimbursement structures not designed for emerging technologies
  - HHS should collaborate with medical community to prevent promising technology from failing due to financial non-viability
  - Inadequate reimbursement barrier already materializing
    - Medicare has struggled to develop consistent strategy for coding and valuing AI-enabled tools
    - AI-enabled diabetic retinopathy screening device has seen limited uptake
    - Device makes initial diagnosis, so Medicare assigns no physician work to CPT code 92229
    - Only reimburses practice expense for nurse/technician assisting patient
    - Primary care physicians have no way to cover initial device cost
    - CMS reluctant to reimburse equipment start-up costs
    - Without incentive, patients and Medicare don't benefit from lower treatment burden when disease caught early
  - Medicare payment system must be modified to encourage AI adoption without financially penalizing physicians
  - Strongly recommends HHS and CMS work with physician community to develop framework for valuing physician work and practice expense with AI
  - Potential starting point: billing modifier codes for AI-assisted image interpretation, similar to radiology's CADx tools
  - If CMS fails to develop workable framework, private payers will create fragmented systems with likely inadequate reimbursement
  - Without adequate reimbursement, HHS will not meet goal of improving patient outcomes

- Clinical Research
  - Data standardization critical for clinical research
  - AI aggregating and analyzing standardized clinical data may unlock answers for treating, curing, or preventing blinding retinal disease
  - Aligns with administration's goals for healthier America with less treatment burden, less caregiver reliance, and productive patients
  - ASRS pleased HHS is prioritizing AI for advanced clinical research

- Opposition to Proposed NIH Controlled-Data Access Policy
  - Concerned HHS has not fully integrated AI strategy across all agencies
  - Greatly concerned about NIH's December 2025 RFI on proposed Controlled-Access Data Policy
  - Potential chilling effect on imaging-based clinical research and AI development, particularly in retina
  - ASRS supports appropriate protections for human participant data
  - Draft policy's broad treatment of personal health data and imaging modalities risks unintentionally restricting responsible data sharing that has driven innovation
  - Retinal imaging (fundus photography, OCT/OCTA, ocular ultrasound) can become re-identifiable through metadata, rare diagnoses, or dataset linkage
  - Mandating controlled access for all such imaging will detrimentally slow research progress
  - Particular concern: NIH states user registration alone does not constitute controlled access
    - Many NIH-funded investigators share research-grade retinal image datasets through professional society portals like ASRS's Retina Image Bank
    - Despite robust data use requirements, these don't meet proposed full technical requirements of NIH-approved controlled-access
  - Requiring universal controlled-access could prevent development and implementation of new AI-enabled technologies
  - Recommends HHS provide oversight of new policy and work with professional societies to develop more workable, risk-based policy

- Oversight of Private Payers
  - Concerned about AI use by commercial payers for physician auditing and prior authorization
  - Often lacks transparency, clinical nuance, or meaningful human oversight
  - In retina care, treatment decisions are frequently time-sensitive
  - Payer-deployed AI tools risk denying medically necessary care, delaying treatment, and undermining physician-patient relationship
  - ASRS has long-standing opposition to payer utilization management tactics (prior authorization, step therapy)
  - Nearly every retina patient except those with original Medicare Part B must endure these tactics
  - Without clear HHS guardrails on AI in utilization management, private payers will likely significantly increase use
  - Will ultimately limit medically necessary care and add to physician administrative burden
  - Research supports patient concerns:
    - Study of 291 retina patients on 5-point Likert scale
    - Mean response of 4.2 when asked if patients should be given choice when AI tools are used
    - Both patients and retina specialists disagreed with trusting AI tools over physician clinical judgment
  - Urges HHS to restrict or prohibit AI use by payers for prior authorization and punitive physician auditing, particularly without transparency, appeal rights, or accountability

- Response to Questions 1&2: Barriers to Innovation and HHS Priorities
  - Single largest barrier: lack of interoperable, standardized clinical and imaging data
  - Main contributors:
    - Proprietary ophthalmic imaging formats limiting reuse and secondary analysis
    - Inconsistent or absent standards for key clinical variables (e.g., visual acuity)
    - High costs and contractual barriers to extracting data from EHRs and imaging platforms
  - HHS should prioritize incentives for vendors encouraging full adoption of existing interoperable standards
  - Specific example: HHS should not permit imaging vendors to claim DICOM adoption simply for file transfer capabilities
  - To be considered DICOM compliant, imaging equipment must have:
    - Equivalence in Imaging Storage: Images viewed clinically on proprietary systems should be stored identically in PixelArray in DICOM
    - Standardized Reporting: Mandatory uniform reporting of specific quantitative metrics (GA lesion size, fluid quantification) using established DICOM Structured Reporting templates
    - Open API for Extracted Metrics: Non-proprietary export interface for outside AI algorithms to securely access standardized findings without processing raw images
  - HHS should enforce FHIR and OMOP standards for clinical data
  - Should encourage standardized clinical terminology for eye exam findings across all EHRs
  - Should support full FHIR implementation for all relevant ophthalmic data elements, including consensus standard for visual acuity

- Response to Question 3: Novel Legal and Implementation Issues
  - ASRS shares AMA concerns regarding physician liability related to AI use in clinical care
  - AI introduction should not create expanded malpractice exposure beyond existing standards of care
  - Physicians should not be held accountable for errors attributable to algorithm design, training data deficiencies, or vendor-controlled system updates
  - While current AI tools may assume liability for errors, physicians and patients need to understand medicolegal implications
  - HHS should promote clear, risk-aligned liability framework assigning responsibility to party best positioned to manage and mitigate harm
  - Developers and vendors of clinical AI systems should bear appropriate responsibility for system performance, maintenance, updates, and known limitations
  - Payers using AI-driven utilization management should bear responsibility for harm caused
  - Physicians should retain clinical judgment and oversight but not be penalized for reasonable reliance on validated tools or choosing not to use AI where standards unsettled
  - HHS guidance would reduce adoption barriers, support responsible innovation, and preserve physician-patient relationship
  - Physicians must not be held liable for harm from private payer utilization management decisions (prior authorization, step therapy) increasingly "super-charged" by AI to prioritize cost over appropriate care
  - HHS should promote framework where developers, vendors, and payers bear responsibility for systems they control, ensuring physicians not penalized for blind reliance on or systemic failures of validated tools
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The nation's largest retina specialist organization urges HHS to break down proprietary data silos, fix Medicare's broken AI reimbursement, and stop insurers from weaponizing AI to deny care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Society of Retina Specialists (ASRS)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents over 3,500 board-certified ophthalmologists with fellowship training in medical and surgical treatment of retinal diseases; has established AI Task Force to guide AI integration in retinal care
- **Geographic Scope:** National (largest retina organization in the world)
- **Stake in Issue:** Members are early adopters of AI (first FDA-cleared AI device was for diabetic retinopathy screening); face direct barriers from proprietary data formats, inadequate Medicare reimbursement for AI tools, and payer AI-driven prior authorization
structuredSections.corePosition: We support HHS's goal of rapid, responsible AI adoption in clinical care, but the most significant barrier is the lack of interoperable, standardized clinical and imaging data created by proprietary formats and misaligned vendor incentives. Without addressing this data bottleneck and fixing Medicare's inability to adequately reimburse AI-enabled tools, HHS will not achieve its goals—and patients will lose access to technologies that could prevent blindness.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Enforce and strengthen existing data standards (DICOM, FHIR, OMOP) for EHRs and imaging equipment manufacturers
  - Use incentives rather than punitive measures to avoid passing costs to physicians
  - Require true DICOM compliance, not just file transfer capability
  - Mandate equivalence in imaging storage between proprietary and DICOM formats
  - Require standardized reporting of quantitative metrics using DICOM Structured Reporting templates
  - Require open API for extracted metrics so outside AI algorithms can access standardized findings
- Modernize Medicare payment policy to recognize AI as clinical infrastructure
  - Develop framework for valuing physician work and practice expense when using AI
  - Consider billing modifier codes for AI-assisted image interpretation, similar to radiology's CADx tools
  - Address equipment start-up cost reimbursement
- Withdraw or significantly modify NIH's proposed Controlled-Data Access policy
  - Develop more workable, risk-based policy for sharing potentially identifiable imaging data
  - Work with professional societies and institutions on implementation
- Restrict or prohibit private payer use of AI for prior authorization and punitive physician auditing
  - Require transparency, appeal rights, and accountability for clinical harm
- Establish clear, risk-aligned liability framework for AI in clinical care
  - Assign responsibility to developers, vendors, and payers for systems they control
  - Protect physicians from expanded malpractice exposure for reasonable reliance on validated tools
  - Ensure physicians not held liable for payer utilization management decisions
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Proprietary data formats and incomplete standards implementation create insurmountable barriers to AI development
  - Advanced imaging devices (OCT, fundus cameras, visual field analyzers) store data in proprietary formats
  - Even when using standards, vendors fail to standardize crucial metrics reporting
  - Clinical metrics like visual acuity lack standardized definitions across systems
  - Nearly unworkable to create large, aggregated datasets essential for robust AI
- Medicare reimbursement structure fails to support AI adoption
  - AI-enabled diabetic retinopathy screening device has seen limited uptake due to payment barriers
  - Medicare assigns no physician work to CPT code 92229 because device makes initial diagnosis
  - Primary care physicians cannot cover initial device cost
  - CMS reluctant to reimburse equipment start-up costs
  - Patients don't benefit from early disease detection when physicians can't afford the technology
- NIH's proposed Controlled-Data Access policy threatens imaging research
  - Broad treatment of imaging modalities risks restricting responsible data sharing
  - User registration alone not considered controlled access under proposal
  - Would affect ASRS's Retina Image Bank and similar professional society portals
  - Could prevent development of new AI-enabled technologies HHS seeks
- Private payers increasingly using AI for prior authorization and auditing without transparency
  - Lacks clinical nuance and meaningful human oversight
  - Time-sensitive retina treatment decisions at risk
  - Could significantly increase utilization management burden
  - Will limit medically necessary care and add to physician administrative burden
- Physician liability concerns with AI adoption
  - Risk of expanded malpractice exposure beyond existing standards of care
  - Physicians could be held accountable for algorithm design or training data deficiencies
  - Unclear medicolegal implications of new technologies
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- The first FDA-cleared AI-enabled medical device was for diabetic retinopathy screening—a retina specialty application—yet it has seen limited uptake because Medicare's payment structure doesn't work
  - Device makes the diagnosis, so Medicare assigns zero physician work value
  - Only reimburses for the nurse helping the patient at the terminal
  - Primary care doctors who would use it to catch diabetic eye disease early have no way to cover the cost
- ASRS operates the Retina Image Bank, a professional society portal where NIH-funded investigators share research-grade retinal images with robust data use requirements—but it wouldn't meet NIH's proposed controlled-access technical requirements
- Private companies are currently developing workaround systems to convert proprietary imaging outputs into standardized formats because vendors won't do it themselves
- Survey of 291 retina patients found mean score of 4.2 (out of 5) agreeing patients should have choice when AI tools are used; both patients and specialists disagreed with trusting AI over physician judgment
- AI-assisted retinal imaging holds promise for diagnosing systemic diseases beyond the eye—cardiovascular, autoimmune, and neurodegenerative conditions—but proprietary data silos prevent this potential from being realized
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Without these advances, millions of Americans would go blind every year."
- "Without any incentive to implement the technology, patients—and the Medicare system—do not have the opportunity to benefit from lower treatment burden and cost of care when their disease is caught early."
- "Without clear guardrails from HHS on the use of AI in utilization management, private payers will likely significantly increase their use of them. This will ultimately limit medically necessary care for patients and further add to already significant physician administrative burden."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and organizational context
  - AAPA represents over 190,000 PAs (physician associates/physician assistants) throughout the United States
  - Responding to HHS's AI Request for Information seeking feedback on harnessing AI's transformative potential while ensuring consumer protections
  - HHS identifies three approaches: regulation, reimbursement, and research/development to support rapid AI adoption

- The PA profession's role in healthcare
  - PAs work at the forefront of care provision alongside other health professionals
  - PAs improve access to care while providing quality and patient satisfaction comparable to physicians (citing MedPAC 2019 report)
  - PA profession is one of the fastest-growing occupations with projected 20% increase from 2024 to 2034 (Bureau of Labor Statistics)
  - PAs are and will increasingly be immersed in clinical and indirect processes where AI will be integrated

- AAPA's general position on AI
  - Appreciates HHS's commitment to improving beneficiary experience through AI
  - Shares HHS's optimism about this technology
  - Has long supported HHS efforts to incentivize technology use for productivity, outcomes, and cost reduction
  - Supports proactive regulation promoting safety and public trust while incentivizing AI development and adoption

- AAPA Recognizes the Various Potential Benefits of AI
  - First benefit: Aggregating large amounts of research data and drawing initial conclusions
    - Early disease detection
    - Assessing treatment and care regimen effectiveness
    - Illuminating trends in patient adherence
  - Second benefit: Expediting administrative processes to improve efficiency and reduce provider burnout
    - Prior authorization automation: identifying when needed, compiling required information from health information systems, drafting prompt payer responses
    - AAPA supported these concepts in previous comments (June 2025)
  - Third benefit: Reducing provider burnout through clinical documentation support
    - Taking and organizing notes during patient encounters
    - Developing suggested diagnoses and personalized treatment plans for health professionals to review
    - AI-proposed diagnoses and treatment plans act as supplemental data for consideration
  - While recognizing transformative power, AAPA also recognizes need for guidelines on proper development and use

- Considerations for the Implementation of AI in Healthcare
  - Given AI's significant potential, there is corresponding risk of negative outcomes if policy is not thoroughly developed and oversight is insufficient

- Avoid Unnecessary Restrictions on AI Usage
  - HHS must avoid restrictive language creating unintended barriers to care during initial policy development
  - Provider-inclusive language at the outset avoids need for later rectification
  - Language should not use physician-centric terms confining AI usage to physicians only
  - Language should be broad enough to include any appropriate health professional
  - Unnecessarily confining language can blunt AI use and minimize potential advantages
  - Scope of practice determinations for AI services should be made at the state level
  - Recommendation: Ensure language is broad enough not to restrict PAs from providing AI services and guarantee PA patients can benefit from AI

- Avoid Unnecessary Restrictions on AI Reimbursement
  - Reimbursement language must not be physician-centric
  - Language must include any health professional authorized to directly bill under Medicare
  - By 2033, of estimated 1.5 million practitioners, about 50% will be physicians and 50% will be PAs and other non-physician practitioners
  - Recommendation: Ensure reimbursement language doesn't restrict health professionals otherwise authorized to bill under Medicare, like PAs

- Align Reimbursement with the Value Proposition of AI
  - AI technologies generate value through improved efficiency, reduced administrative burden, enhanced clinical decision-making, and preventing unnecessary utilization
  - Benefits are often realized at system level rather than through discrete billable services
  - Under fee-for-service models, efficiency gains may be unrewarded or penalized, creating disincentive for adoption
  - Reimbursement policy should avoid framing AI as an add-on service
  - Should support payment structures enabling providers and payers to capture value of improved efficiency
  - Premature creation of AI-specific billing codes, modifiers, or payment add-ons risks incentivizing volume over value
  - Historically, such approaches contributed to spending growth without commensurate outcome improvements
  - Maintaining payment policy flexibility is essential to allow AI use cases to mature
  - Recommendation: Avoid unnecessary standalone billing codes or service-level payment add-ons; prioritize reimbursement rewarding outcomes, efficiency, and total cost of care through value-based, population-based, and shared-savings payment models

- Safeguard Sensitive Information
  - AI development must incorporate patient privacy protections against inadvertent dissemination of personal information
  - Patients using AI technologies must not be able to access other patients' individualized information
  - Providers should not access identifiable patient information outside their care
  - AI technology vendors must be treated as HIPAA-covered entities
  - All relevant patient protections must be incorporated into AI technologies
  - Recommendation: Ensure or develop regulatory requirements that AI technology users and vendors are considered and regulated as HIPAA-covered entities

- Ensure Appropriate Oversight of AI Diagnoses and Authorizations
  - AI has significant potential to aggregate data for accurate diagnoses and treatments and to expedite processes
  - Results are not guaranteed flawless from inception
  - AI requires regular informational feedback to learn and improve
  - Incorrect diagnosis, treatment, or coverage determination can harm patient health
  - Incorrect inputs, biases, or incomplete context could prove deadly
  - Healthcare AI cannot be granted assumption of veracity without licensed clinician oversight
  - Recommendation: Include clear requirement for safeguards, which could include human review of AI-generated diagnoses, treatment recommendations, or coverage determinations

- Support Adoption of AI Among Providers
  - AI use by health professionals presents significant opportunity to streamline processes and improve care
  - New technologies take time and money to implement
    - Infrastructure adoption needed
    - Processes must be developed
    - Training must be provided
  - HHS could expedite adoption through financial incentives, educational materials, training, and best practices
  - HHS should refrain from making AI adoption compulsory while technology is new
  - Health professionals are still understanding capabilities, limitations, and implementation approaches
  - HHS may set potential timelines for AI adoption in existing and future incentive programs
    - Financial benefits and penalties could increase over predetermined time
    - Multiple timelines may be required for different AI forms (diagnostic technology, EHR integration, prior authorization)
  - HHS should consider additional financial support for small provider groups to offset software and infrastructure costs
  - Recommendation: Develop timeline for incorporating financial bonuses and penalties for AI adoption into incentive programs; provide training and educational materials; provide supplementary financial support for small provider groups

- AI and the Importance of Accurate Data in Incentivizing and Publicly Reporting the Use of AI
  - AI integration is an opportunity to improve care provision
  - Health professionals appropriately using AI stand to benefit financially from improved quality and reduced costs
  - Those using AI usefully will likely see improvement in performance metrics and financial benefits under incentive programs
  - This may act as additional incentive for AI adoption
  - Financial incentives can only be recognized if services are accurately attributed to the health professional who provided them
  - CMS has acknowledged importance of appropriate service attribution in previous rules
  - Current fee-for-service Medicare policies obscure actual provider of care
  - "Incident to" billing provision allows services performed by one health professional to be submitted under another's name
    - PA services submitted under "incident to" and attributed to physicians skew assessments of care quality, resource utilization, and value-based care measures
    - Creates inaccurate data regarding both PAs and physicians
  - Split (or shared) visit billing and global surgical packages also obfuscate individual health professional contributions
  - Addressing these policies ensures financial and public ratings for proper AI use are attributed to deserving health professionals
  - Recommendation: Work with Congress to address inaccurate data collection from "incident to"; explore similar transparency concerns from split visit billing and global surgical packages so AI benefits are attributable to appropriate health professionals

- Professional Title
  - Requests all PA references in regulations be listed as "physician assistants/physician associates" per 20 CFR § 220.46 (a)(9)
  - This reflects PAs graduating with degrees as either title, state-licensed as either title
  - All PAs graduate from programs accredited by same organization (Accreditation Review Commission on Education for the Physician Assistant)
  - All certified by same organization (National Commission on Certification of Physician Assistants)
  - All have same scopes of practice
  - "Physician associate" now recognized as official title to more accurately reflect breadth of education, training, experience, and services
  - Reflected in AAPA title, other professional organizations, training programs, and state/territory laws
  - Title change from "physician assistant" will take time across all jurisdictions
  - Dual reference recommended to avoid confusion
  - Requests HHS implement federal protections regarding health professional title use by AI
    - Recommends reviewing California law (AB-489) effective January 1, 2026
    - Law enforces license misrepresentation laws against AI/GenAI using terms implying possession of healthcare license
    - Could serve as roadmap for similar federal protection
  - Transition to increased AI use will require broad review of existing protectionary laws to determine applicability to technology not yet included in regulatory language
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: National PA professional association representing 190,000 members urges HHS to ensure AI policies include all qualified health professionals in usage and reimbursement language, avoid premature billing codes that incentivize volume over value, require human oversight of AI clinical decisions, and fix attribution problems that obscure who actually provides care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Academy of Physician Associates (AAPA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** National professional organization representing over 190,000 physician associates/physician assistants; signed by Todd Pickard, DMSc, PA-C, DFAAPA, FASCO, President & Chair of the Board
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** PAs provide significant share of patient care and will be immersed in clinical and administrative processes where AI is integrated; by 2033, approximately 50% of 1.5 million practitioners will be non-physician practitioners including PAs; current billing policies obscure PA contributions and could affect AI-related reimbursement and performance attribution
structuredSections.corePosition: We support HHS's proactive efforts to ensure AI regulation promotes safety and public trust while incentivizing development and broad adoption for better patient care. However, AI policies must use provider-inclusive language that doesn't restrict PAs and other qualified health professionals from using AI or billing for AI-assisted services. Reimbursement should reward value and outcomes rather than creating premature billing codes that incentivize volume, and human oversight must be required for AI-generated clinical decisions.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Ensure AI usage language is broad enough not to restrict PAs and other qualified health professionals from providing AI-assisted services
  - Scope of practice determinations should remain at state level
  - Patients served by PAs must be able to benefit from AI

- Ensure AI reimbursement language includes all health professionals authorized to bill under Medicare
  - By 2033, about 50% of practitioners will be non-physician practitioners

- Avoid creating standalone AI billing codes or service-level payment add-ons
  - Prioritize value-based, population-based, and shared-savings payment models
  - Premature codes risk incentivizing volume over value
  - Fee-for-service models may penalize efficiency gains

- Require AI technology users and vendors to be regulated as HIPAA-covered entities
  - Protect against inadvertent dissemination of patient information
  - Prevent patients from accessing other patients' data through AI systems

- Include clear requirement for human review safeguards for AI-generated diagnoses, treatment recommendations, and coverage determinations
  - AI requires feedback to learn and improve
  - Incorrect inputs, biases, or incomplete context could prove deadly

- Develop timeline for incorporating AI adoption bonuses and penalties into incentive programs
  - Multiple timelines needed for different AI applications
  - Provide training and educational materials
  - Provide supplementary financial support for small provider groups

- Work with Congress to address "incident to" billing complications
  - Current policy creates inaccurate data about who provides care
  - Explore similar concerns with split visit billing and global surgical packages
  - Ensure AI benefits are attributed to appropriate health professionals

- Reference PAs by dual title "physician assistant/physician associate" in all regulations

- Implement federal protections against AI misrepresenting health professional credentials
  - Review California's AB-489 as potential roadmap
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Physician-centric language in AI policies could create unintended barriers to care
  - Could restrict PAs from providing AI-assisted services
  - Would minimize AI's potential advantages by excluding providers who deliver significant share of care

- Premature creation of AI-specific billing codes risks incentivizing volume over value
  - Historically, such approaches contributed to spending growth without outcome improvements
  - Fee-for-service models may penalize efficiency gains from AI

- AI results are not guaranteed flawless
  - Requires regular feedback to learn and improve
  - Incorrect inputs, biases, or incomplete context could prove deadly
  - Cannot be granted assumption of veracity without clinician oversight

- Patient privacy risks from AI technologies
  - Potential for inadvertent dissemination of personal information
  - Patients could access other patients' data
  - Providers could access patient information outside their care

- Current Medicare billing policies obscure actual provider of care
  - "Incident to" allows PA services to be attributed to physicians
  - Skews assessments of care quality and resource utilization
  - Creates inaccurate data for both PAs and physicians
  - Split visit billing and global surgical packages have similar problems
  - Would prevent accurate attribution of AI-related performance benefits

- AI adoption requires significant time and money
  - Infrastructure adoption, process development, and training needed
  - Small provider groups may need financial support

- AI could misrepresent health professional credentials
  - Need federal protections similar to California's AB-489
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- By 2033, the healthcare workforce will be approximately 50% physicians and 50% non-physician practitioners (PAs, NPs, etc.), making inclusive AI policy language essential rather than optional

- AAPA identifies a counterintuitive problem with AI reimbursement: under fee-for-service models, efficiency gains from AI may actually be "unrewarded or penalized," creating a disincentive for adoption even when AI improves quality

- The "incident to" billing provision creates a data accuracy problem that will compound with AI: services performed by PAs but billed under physicians' names will make it impossible to accurately measure which providers are effectively using AI

- California's AB-489 law (effective January 2026) provides a potential federal roadmap for preventing AI from misrepresenting health professional credentials
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Incorrect inputs, biases, or incomplete context considered by AI could prove deadly. As such, healthcare-related AI technology cannot be granted an assumption of veracity without oversight from a licensed clinician."

- "Under fee-for-service payment models, efficiency gains may be unrewarded or penalized, creating a disincentive for adoption even when AI may improve quality and patient outcomes."

- "PA services submitted under Medicare's 'incident to' billing provision and attributed to physicians skew assessments of provider care quality, resource utilization, and other measures relevant to value-based care, creating inaccurate data regarding both PAs and physicians."
