structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and organizational background
  - California Clinical Laboratory Association (CCLA) represents broad range of clinical laboratories serving patients across California
    - Large national diagnostic laboratories
    - Specialized and biotechnology-focused laboratories
    - Academic and health system pathology departments
    - Smaller independent clinical laboratories and service providers
  - California laboratories operate at forefront of biotechnology and digital health innovation
  - Regulatory clarity and reimbursement stability are critical to sustaining responsible AI advancement in clinical diagnostics

- Regulation section responses
  - Federal regulatory clarity is essential to enable laboratories to adopt AI safely and at scale
  - Current federal frameworks serve distinct and complementary roles
    - CLIA governs clinical laboratory services, including laboratory developed tests (LDTs) and algorithmic analyses performed as professional services
    - FDA oversight applies to regulated medical devices and software marketed by manufacturers
    - Clear jurisdictional boundaries necessary to prevent duplicative or misaligned oversight
  - AI-enabled medical devices require clear manufacturer transparency for local verification and validation
    - Model development datasets and intended-use populations
    - Performance metrics and validation methodology
    - Ongoing performance monitoring and change-control plans
    - Known limitations and deployment constraints
    - Configuration and customization parameters
    - Such disclosures support laboratory oversight, mitigate risks from dataset imbalance or model drift, enhance physician confidence
  - HHS should reevaluate 45 CFR 92.210 (Nondiscrimination in use of patient care decision support tools)
    - Current framework risks placing disproportionate responsibility on clinicians and laboratories for bias originating in vendor training data or model design
    - Liability for algorithmic bias should be appropriately allocated between AI developers and clinical end users
  - CLIA remains primary regulatory framework for laboratory services but was promulgated decades ago
    - Regulations predate proliferation of software, robust networking tools, and AI-enabled diagnostics
    - Targeted regulatory updates needed
  - Specific CLIA update recommendations
    - Definition of "laboratory" should be amended to expressly include facilities examining digital laboratory information (previously sequenced DNA, digital images of slides)
    - Should clarify that "algorithmic" examinations are included among laboratory functions
    - This will remove regulatory ambiguity and support continued innovation
    - CMS should codify enforcement discretion policy permitting pathologists and laboratory personnel to review digital data, results, and images remotely under primary site's CLIA certificate
    - HHS should reinstate or replace former Clinical Laboratory Improvement Advisory Committee (CLIAC) or establish comparable advisory body for expert guidance as AI transforms laboratory medicine
  - Defers to ACLA response for specific CLIA regulatory wording recommendations

- Reimbursement section responses
  - Sustainable AI adoption requires predictable, value-based reimbursement pathways
    - Must reflect clinical benefit and substantial investment to develop, validate, and maintain algorithmic analyses
    - Applies even when no new specimen is collected
  - AI-enabled algorithmic services improve diagnostic precision, better inform treatment decisions, reduce unnecessary downstream testing or ineffective therapies
  - Without viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries
    - Would exacerbate disparities in access
    - Would limit innovation within federally funded programs
  - Payment systems context
    - Services may be reimbursed under Medicare Clinical Laboratory Fee Schedule (CLFS) or Medicare Physician Fee Schedule (PFS) when physician professional time required
    - Clinical value and resources required should be appropriately reflected in both payment systems
  - Proprietary Laboratory Analysis (PLA) code pathway concerns
    - PLA has historically supported innovative laboratory services including algorithmic-only analyses
    - Recent changes to PLA guidance render algorithmic-only services ineligible for PLA coding
    - Creates uncertainty and risks impeding innovation
    - CMS should work with American Medical Association (AMA) to ensure appropriate coding pathways remain available
  - AI utilization management safeguards needed
    - AI and automated systems should not be used to deny or impede patient access automatically
    - AI-driven utilization management tools and automated edits must include meaningful human clinical review
    - Must have transparent denial rationales and clear appeal pathways
    - Ensures patients not denied medically necessary care
  - Medicare Advantage consistency requirements
    - MA plans should implement National Coverage Determinations (NCDs) consistently with Medicare fee-for-service standards
    - Should not apply AI-driven utilization tools or local coverage criteria that restrict access beyond established federal coverage policy

- Research and Development section responses
  - HHS should prioritize funding for public-private partnerships, CRADAs, and grant programs that:
    - Build diverse, high-quality validation datasets
    - Support interoperable data standards
    - Enable real-world evidence (RWE) generation
    - Develop synthetic data governance frameworks
    - Expand external proficiency testing models for AI-enabled laboratory services under CLIA
  - Investments in standardized benchmarking, external validation programs, and workforce training will reduce implementation risk and accelerate trustworthy AI deployment
  - Federal funding should recognize and offset operational costs laboratories incur when standardizing data and adopting interoperable formats
  - AI research and innovation advance most effectively when reimbursement is stable and regulatory expectations are clear, consistent, and technology-neutral
    - Predictable policy environments reduce innovation risk
    - Promotes responsible deployment of AI tools that enhance patient care
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: California laboratory trade association urges HHS to clarify CLIA's jurisdiction over AI-enabled diagnostics, fix reimbursement pathways for algorithmic-only services, and ensure liability for AI bias falls on developers rather than clinical end users.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** California Clinical Laboratory Association (CCLA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents clinical laboratories across California including national diagnostic labs, specialized/biotech labs, academic pathology departments, and independent laboratories
- **Geographic Scope:** State (California), with national policy implications
- **Stake in Issue:** Members develop, validate, and deploy AI-enabled diagnostic services; need regulatory clarity and sustainable reimbursement to continue innovation in clinical diagnostics
structuredSections.corePosition: We need federal regulatory clarity and stable reimbursement to adopt AI safely and at scale in clinical laboratories. Current frameworks like CLIA were written decades before AI-enabled diagnostics existed, and recent coding changes threaten to make algorithmic-only laboratory services unreimbursable—which could push innovative diagnostics away from Medicare beneficiaries entirely.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Amend CLIA definition of "laboratory" to expressly include facilities examining digital laboratory information and algorithmic examinations
  - Would clarify that CLIA is the applicable framework for algorithmic-only laboratory services
  - Removes regulatory ambiguity currently hindering innovation
- Codify CMS enforcement discretion policy allowing pathologists to review digital data and images remotely under primary site's CLIA certificate
- Reinstate or replace the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Advisory Committee (CLIAC) to provide expert guidance as AI transforms laboratory medicine
- Reevaluate 45 CFR 92.210 to appropriately allocate liability for algorithmic bias between AI developers and clinical end users
  - Current framework places disproportionate responsibility on clinicians and laboratories for bias originating in vendor training data
- Require manufacturer transparency for AI-enabled medical devices including:
  - Model development datasets and intended-use populations
  - Performance metrics and validation methodology
  - Ongoing performance monitoring and change-control plans
  - Known limitations and deployment constraints
- Work with AMA to restore PLA coding pathway eligibility for algorithmic-only laboratory services
  - Recent guidance changes create uncertainty and risk impeding innovation
- Require AI-driven utilization management tools to include meaningful human clinical review, transparent denial rationales, and clear appeal pathways
- Ensure Medicare Advantage plans implement NCDs consistently with fee-for-service standards without AI-driven restrictions beyond federal coverage policy
- Prioritize federal funding for:
  - Diverse, high-quality validation datasets
  - Interoperable data standards
  - Real-world evidence generation
  - Synthetic data governance frameworks
  - External proficiency testing models for AI-enabled laboratory services
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Regulatory ambiguity between CLIA and FDA jurisdictions
  - Unclear boundaries risk duplicative or misaligned oversight
  - CLIA regulations predate software, networking tools, and AI-enabled diagnostics
- Misallocated liability for algorithmic bias
  - Current 45 CFR 92.210 framework risks placing disproportionate responsibility on clinicians and laboratories
  - Bias may originate in vendor training data or model design, not clinical implementation
- Reimbursement pathway instability
  - Recent PLA guidance changes render algorithmic-only services ineligible for coding
  - Without viable payment pathways, innovative AI diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries
  - Would exacerbate access disparities and limit innovation in federally funded programs
- AI-driven utilization management risks
  - Automated systems could deny or impede patient access without meaningful human review
  - Medicare Advantage plans may apply AI-driven restrictions beyond established federal coverage policy
- Lack of manufacturer transparency
  - Laboratories cannot properly verify and validate AI tools without disclosure of training data, performance metrics, limitations, and change-control plans
  - Risks from dataset imbalance or model drift without adequate information
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a specific regulatory gap: CLIA regulations were written "decades ago, before the proliferation of software, robust networking tools, and AI-enabled diagnostics"—creating fundamental mismatch between current technology and governing framework
- Highlights that algorithmic analyses can be performed "even when no new specimen is collected"—a paradigm shift in laboratory services that current coding systems don't accommodate
- Points to concrete policy change causing immediate harm: recent PLA guidance changes that "render algorithmic-only services ineligible for PLA coding"
- Frames AI migration away from Medicare as an access equity issue, not just an innovation concern
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Absent viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries, exacerbating disparities in access and limiting innovation within federally funded programs."
- "The current framework risks placing disproportionate responsibility on clinicians and laboratories for potential bias originating in vendor training data or model design."
- "AI research and innovation in clinical laboratories advance most effectively when reimbursement is stable and regulatory expectations are clear, consistent, and technology-neutral."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- MSK's background and AI experience
  - Specialty cancer hospital and NCI-designated comprehensive cancer center
  - 2024 patient volume: 74,000+ patients, 26,000 inpatient admissions, 1+ million outpatient visits
  - Already deployed several FDA-approved AI systems in clinical practice
  - Clinicians, scientists, and research faculty actively developing new AI clinical tools
  - AI Governance Committee oversees registration, review, and approval of all clinical AI tools
    - Ensures thorough evaluation before deployment
    - Maintains continuous oversight throughout system life cycle

- Vendor intellectual property barriers to AI development
  - Restrictive foreground IP rights in vendor contracts are a significant administrative hurdle
  - Common in EMR and PACS company standard contracts
  - Although health systems legally own patient data, vendors argue AI models developed from this data constitute foreground IP they own
  - Creates chilling effect on innovation
    - Hospital systems discouraged from developing novel AI models since ownership cannot be guaranteed
    - Clinicians and patients lose out on advancing care
    - Removes those closest to everyday healthcare issues from development process
  - Creates vendor dependency
    - Vendors offer their own AI models requiring continued subscription or additional costs
    - Health systems effectively pay twice: once for initial subscription, again for AI derived partly from their own data
  - Even when barriers overcome, restrictions can prevent deployment with other vendors
    - Interoperability not guaranteed
    - Creates vendor-dependent ecosystems
    - Silos progress based on which health systems have contracts with which companies
  - HHS needs to dismantle these contractual barriers to foster provider ownership environment
    - Without this, development remains tethered to outside vendor interests
    - Results in noncompetitive and ultimately stagnant future

- Data curation challenges
  - Establishing pipelines for ongoing AI monitoring is highly burdensome
  - Case example: FDA-approved mammography screening AI tool
    - Generates suspicion score for each mammogram available to radiologists
    - Safe use requires ongoing monitoring of performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, etc.)
    - Calculating metrics across large exam numbers requires aggregating AI outputs with ground truth labels and demographic information from EHR
    - Vendor-generated AI outputs stored within each patient's exam jacket in PACS
    - Vendor provides no built-in support to aggregate scores into usable format
    - Simple task of generating AI output lists linked to patient IDs is resource-intensive

- Data analysis needs
  - Support development of third-party registries to receive curated data from clinical sites
  - Would provide option for centers wanting to offload analytical work of ongoing monitoring
  - Would enable dissemination of performance metrics across institutions
  - Offers valuable insights to broader clinical community

- Vendor feedback tool concerns
  - Some AI companies request user feedback using model monitoring as pretense
  - Actual purpose: provide company with data to validate newer models and collect labeled data for retraining proprietary weights without remuneration
  - Turns clinicians into unpaid annotators
  - Helps parent company's internal model development rather than providing oversight
  - Feedback tools should be redesigned to only provide feedback to health systems licensing the product
  - Would encourage providers to use quality assurance functions without fear of clinical judgments being monetized or repurposed for commercial training

- Short-term performance metrics for long-term prediction models
  - AI models making longer-term predictions (e.g., 5-year cancer risk) should have performance metrics on shorter time horizons (e.g., 1 year)
  - Allows potential problems or concerning trends to be addressed without undue delay

- Reimbursement barriers
  - Lack of clinical and technical funding is barrier to widespread adoption and responsible use
  - CMS should support payments for:
    - Clinician training and utilization
    - AI governance
    - Infrastructure
    - Continuous monitoring
  - Should not allocate from budget-neutral Medicare Physician Fee Schedule
    - Would require decreased reimbursement for other physician services

- Conclusion
  - Elimination of restrictive vendor IP policies is critical to accelerating AI development and adoption
  - Safe and effective AI governance is critical underpinning to adoption
  - Can be achieved through empowering healthcare institutions and clinicians with readily available, model-provided tools to monitor known risks of deployed AI systems

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Major cancer center with active AI governance program urges HHS to dismantle vendor IP restrictions that stifle hospital-led AI innovation and calls for standardized data formats, third-party registries, and dedicated reimbursement for AI oversight.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSK) AI Governance Committee
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** NCI-designated comprehensive cancer center; signatories include Chief Data Analytics Officer, Chief Medical Officer, and Assistant Attending Radiologist—all co-chairs or members of MSK's AI Governance Committee
- **Geographic Scope:** National (New York-based institution serving patients nationally)
- **Stake in Issue:** Actively deploying FDA-approved AI systems and developing new AI clinical tools; directly impacted by vendor IP restrictions and data curation burdens in monitoring AI performance

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We believe eliminating restrictive intellectual property policies by vendors is critical to accelerating AI development and adoption in clinical care. Safe and effective AI governance requires empowering healthcare institutions and clinicians with readily available, model-provided tools to monitor deployed AI systems. Without dismantling these contractual barriers, development will remain tethered to outside vendor interests, resulting in a noncompetitive and ultimately stagnant future.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Dismantle contractual barriers that allow vendors to claim foreground IP rights over AI models developed from health system data
  - Foster environment of provider ownership to enable exponential AI growth in healthcare
  - Prevent vendor-dependent ecosystems that silo progress

- Require AI output scores be stored in standardized formats
  - Analogous to DICOM standard for imaging data or HL7/FHIR standard for EHRs
  - Facilitate streamlined data extraction for performance monitoring

- Require vendors to provide software integrating with EHR data
  - Enable customers to monitor system performance and usage over time

- Support development of third-party registries for curated clinical data
  - Allow centers to offload analytical work of ongoing monitoring
  - Enable dissemination of performance metrics across institutions

- Redesign vendor feedback tools to only provide feedback to health systems licensing the product
  - Prevent clinicians from becoming unpaid annotators for commercial model training
  - Encourage use of quality assurance functions without fear of monetization

- Require vendors of long-term prediction AI models to establish short-term performance metrics
  - Example: 1-year metrics for 5-year cancer risk models
  - Address potential problems without undue delay

- CMS should support dedicated payments for AI-related activities
  - Cover clinician training and utilization, AI governance, infrastructure, and continuous monitoring
  - Fund outside budget-neutral Medicare Physician Fee Schedule to avoid decreasing other physician service reimbursement

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Vendor IP restrictions create chilling effect on hospital-led AI innovation
  - Health systems legally own patient data but vendors claim ownership of AI models derived from it
  - Discourages internal development since ownership cannot be guaranteed
  - Removes clinicians closest to everyday healthcare issues from development process

- Double-payment problem for health systems
  - Pay for initial vendor subscription
  - Pay again for AI models derived partly from their own data on vendor platforms

- Lack of interoperability creates vendor-dependent ecosystems
  - Restrictions prevent deployment of AI models with other vendors
  - Silos progress based on existing contract relationships

- Data curation for AI monitoring is highly burdensome
  - Vendors provide no built-in support to aggregate AI outputs into usable formats
  - Simple tasks like generating AI output lists linked to patient IDs are resource-intensive
  - Example: mammography AI tool outputs stored in individual patient exam jackets with no aggregation capability

- Some vendors exploit feedback tools for commercial gain
  - Use model monitoring as pretense to collect labeled data for retraining proprietary models
  - Clinicians become unpaid annotators without remuneration
  - Helps vendor's internal development rather than providing actual oversight

- Lack of funding for AI adoption and oversight
  - No dedicated reimbursement for clinician training, governance, infrastructure, or monitoring
  - Budget-neutral fee schedule would require cuts to other physician services

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Real-world data curation challenge with mammography AI
  - FDA-approved tool generates suspicion scores stored in individual patient exam jackets in PACS
  - No vendor support to aggregate scores into usable format
  - What should be a simple task of linking AI outputs to patient IDs became resource-intensive
  - Illustrates how vendors create monitoring burdens while providing no solutions

- Discovery that vendor "feedback tools" serve dual purposes
  - Presented as model monitoring but actually collect labeled data for retraining proprietary weights
  - Effectively turns clinicians into unpaid annotators for commercial development
  - Reveals misalignment between stated and actual purposes of vendor quality assurance features

- Insight on long-term prediction models
  - 5-year cancer risk predictions cannot be validated in real-time
  - Recommends requiring 1-year performance metrics as early warning system
  - Practical solution to monitoring challenge for predictive AI

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "This creates a chilling effect on innovation. Internal efforts within hospital systems are discouraged from developing novel AI models, since ownership cannot be guaranteed due to these restrictive contract conditions."

- "Health care systems then effectively pay twice – once for the initial subscription, and second for the AI model derived partly from their own data on that vendor's platform."

- "This turns clinicians into unpaid annotators, helping the parent company's internal model development rather than providing oversight."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - The most consequential barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not regulatory burden, reimbursement lag, or technical immaturity
  - It is the absence of governance infrastructure that would make adoption safe to accelerate
  - Private sector AI innovators face a structural paradox: clinical environments cannot answer three foundational questions about AI systems already operating within them
    - Who is specifically responsible for a clinical AI system's outputs — not who is aware of it, but whose name is attached to the outcome?
    - How do we know if it is working — is there a metric, attached to a threshold, that someone can point to?
    - What triggers when it isn't — when that metric crosses a threshold, what is structurally required to happen?
  - Evidence for consequences of this absence
    - 2025 AMA survey: 61% of physicians reported AI is increasing prior authorization denials
    - 82% reported patients abandoning treatment as a result of AI-driven prior authorization
    - 94% reported negative clinical outcomes associated with AI-mediated decision-making
    - These are outcomes already occurring in clinical environments where AI systems operate between physician and patient without accountability architecture
  - Barrier extends beyond clinical settings into institutional procurement
    - 75% of organizations have adopted AI usage policies, but only 36% have implemented formal governance frameworks
    - Only 48% actively monitor production AI systems for accuracy, drift, or misuse — drops to 9% among smaller organizations
    - Only 24% of organizations can control AI agent actions with guardrails and live monitoring
    - Corroborated by GAO federal AI audits, Cisco readiness index, and CSA/Google governance maturity data
  - Result is a specific kind of market failure
    - AI vendors capable of delivering genuine clinical value cannot cross the procurement threshold
    - Institutional buyers lack governance architecture to accept accountability for what the system does
    - Not fixed by reducing regulatory burden on vendors
    - Fixed by helping clinical institutions build accountability infrastructure for responsible AI adoption
  - Organizations with comprehensive AI governance were nearly twice as likely to report successful early adoption of agentic AI (46%) compared to organizations with governance still in development (12%)
  - Pattern holds across clinical AI categories — EHR-integrated clinical decision support, in-ICU prediction tools, prior authorization systems

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - HHS should prioritize one structural change above all others: reorienting regulatory and reimbursement instruments from behavioral requirements to architectural requirements
  - Current frameworks ask behavioral questions
    - Does the AI system meet a performance threshold?
    - Does it produce explainable outputs?
    - Does the vendor have a responsible AI policy?
    - These are post-hoc assessments of outputs
    - They do not address where clinical authority resides when an AI system operates between physician and patient
  - Evidence from federal government's own experience with AI governance
    - GAO December 2023 audit of AI implementation across 23 civilian agencies
    - All 23 had designated Responsible AI Officials and 20 of 23 had created AI inventories — full behavioral compliance
    - Yet 15 of 20 inventories contained incomplete or inaccurate data
    - Two included use cases later determined not to be AI at all
    - Government-wide guidance for AI acquisition — mandated years earlier — remained unissued
    - Agencies achieved compliance but governance architecture connecting compliance to accurate outcomes did not function
    - As of July 2025, nine of GAO's original recommendations remained open
  - Specific change: HHS should define a Clinical AI Accountability Architecture standard
    - For any AI system producing clinically consequential outputs, three structural conditions must be satisfied before deployment
      - First: a named responsible party — not a vendor, not a committee — whose accountability is operationally connected to the system's clinical outputs
      - Second: a measurement specification identifying what metric is being tracked, at what threshold, and at what frequency
      - Third: a consequence mechanism specifying what is structurally required when the threshold is crossed — not what may happen, but what must happen
    - For device AI, this standard would operate in parallel with FDA's premarket and postmarket requirements by specifying institutional authority structures
  - Standard would apply differently across device and non-device AI
    - For non-medical device AI — the category HHS identifies in Question 3 as raising novel issues — this standard provides governance specification existing frameworks do not supply
    - Not a new layer of compliance on top of existing compliance
    - It is the architectural foundation that makes existing compliance instruments meaningful
    - Represents a category correction in early AI policy
    - HHS's existing oversight instruments — CMS conditions of participation, ONC certification criteria, accreditation standards — are the substrate on which this architecture would be built
  - On reimbursement
    - CMS payment policy is the fastest market-structuring lever available to HHS
    - Conditioning reimbursement eligibility for AI-augmented clinical workflows on demonstrated accountability architecture would restructure vendor incentive architecture within a typical CMS policy cycle
    - Could begin as voluntary attestation with phased pilots before mandatory implementation

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical device AI
  - The novel issue is not liability, indemnification, privacy, or security individually
  - It is what happens when an AI system occupies the clinical decision space between physician and patient without any of those legal frameworks being able to reach where decisions are actually being made
  - Evidence is specific and documented
    - U.S. Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations found UnitedHealthcare deployed an AI model (nH Predict) to evaluate post-acute care claims for elderly Medicare Advantage patients
    - Error rate reported as approximately 90% in internal documents and contemporaneous reporting
    - Decisions overrode treating physicians' determinations
    - Denial rates for post-acute care rose from 10.9% to 22.7% between 2020 and 2022
    - Patients died during or after algorithmic termination of coverage
    - These cases predominantly sit on the payer side but occupy the clinical decision boundary patients experience as care
  - ProPublica investigation documented Cigna's PXDX algorithm
    - Denied over 300,000 payment requests in a two-month period
    - Reviewing physicians spent an average of 1.2 seconds per claim — consistent with no individualized clinical review
    - Class action (Kisting-Leung v. Cigna Corp.) proceeding in Eastern District of California
  - In both cases, AI system operated within existing regulatory frameworks
    - Legal frameworks address payer's contractual obligations, physician's professional standards, patient's appeal rights
    - None address where authority resided when the decision was made
    - Frameworks were not designed for a space where an AI system is the de facto decision-maker while human authority structures remain nominally intact
  - Same structural condition extends beyond payer utilization management
    - Clinical AI systems in diagnostic support, sepsis prediction, radiology triage, treatment recommendation occupy similar decision boundaries
    - System's output shapes clinical action but accountability architecture was designed for workflow where human clinician was sole decision agent
  - What HHS can do that no existing legal framework can do
    - Define where the authority boundary sits
    - Specify which clinical outputs require human decision authority as a structural prerequisite, not as an audit finding after the fact
  - Implementation role HHS should play
    - Develop a Clinical AI Accountability Registry
    - Federal-level enumeration of clinical AI use cases, accountability architecture each deploying institution has certified, and measurement evidence supporting that certification
    - Does not create new liability — creates information infrastructure that makes existing liability frameworks functional
    - Would be a schema and attestation container — not a centralized patient-data repository
    - Could be federated through existing reporting rails with voluntary attestation as default entry point

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods
  - Most consequential gap in current AI evaluation methodology is between pre-deployment testing and post-deployment governance
  - Most evaluation frameworks treat deployment as the endpoint
  - Governance requires treating deployment as the starting point
  - Pre-deployment evaluation answers: does this system perform acceptably on specified benchmarks?
  - Post-deployment governance answers: is the clinical authority structure surrounding this system still functioning as designed?
    - These are different questions
    - The second one is not currently being asked systematically anywhere in the clinical AI evaluation landscape
  - Structural concern with pre-deployment testing has deepened
    - 2026 International AI Safety Report found it has become more common for models to distinguish between test settings and real-world deployment, and to exploit loopholes in evaluations
    - Documented risk of evaluation gaming and test-deploy divergence now exists as a structural condition
    - Not a claim that clinical AI models are routinely gaming evaluations
    - The structural gap between test performance and deployment behavior is documented and must be governed
  - Specific failure mode in clinical care
    - A system that performs within evaluated parameters at the model level can simultaneously produce clinical harm at the institutional level
    - Human review structures nominally surrounding it may have been effectively bypassed by volume, time pressure, or operational design
    - The evaluation passed; the governance failed — these are not the same event
  - HHS should support development of governance integrity evaluation
    - Methodology for assessing not whether an AI system produces acceptable outputs but whether institutional accountability architecture remains structurally intact under operational conditions
    - Analogous to safety culture audits or quality-of-care audits, focused on governance layer rather than clinical outcome layer
    - Could be conducted through existing oversight channels — accrediting bodies, internal quality assurance structures, CMS survey and certification processes
    - Would assess at defined intervals:
      - Whether named responsible party is operationally connected to what the system produces
      - Whether measurement specification is generating data that reaches people with authority to act
      - Whether consequence mechanisms specified at deployment are being triggered at designed thresholds
    - Example: verifying quarterly that physician review process for AI-flagged sepsis alert system is still functioning as designed
  - This is evaluable and requires a different assessment framework than performance benchmarking
  - Measurement specifications are technically feasible with current institutional data infrastructure

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
  - Most valuable thing HHS can do is create institutional vocabulary that makes AI governance legible to decision-makers who control adoption
  - Currently, clinical AI governance conversations fail at institutional level
    - Not because technology is misunderstood
    - Because governance concepts required to evaluate it don't have stable, shared definitions
  - GAO documented six or more different definitions of "AI" existed simultaneously across federal law and guidance
    - Produces definitional instability that propagates through every procurement conversation, board-level governance discussion, and regulatory interaction
    - Every institution must rebuild foundational shared vocabulary from scratch
  - HHS should develop and publish a Clinical AI Governance Lexicon
    - Standardized set of governance terms with precise institutional definitions
    - Specifying what each term means in operational context and what evidence satisfies each requirement
    - Not a glossary — a governance measurement standard
    - An evidence-binding authority, not merely a definitional one
  - Benefits of the lexicon
    - Institution demonstrating its AI system meets defined standard for "clinical accountability architecture" can move through procurement, accreditation, and regulatory review with measurable confidence
    - Institution that cannot has a specific, addressable gap — not a general sense that something might be wrong
    - Each term maps to required evidence artifacts and escalation triggers — not aspirational principles
    - Would be iteratively updated through multi-stakeholder processes, consistent with how ONC and existing standards bodies operate
    - Enables standardized procurement templates, accreditation criteria, and CMS program integrity tools built on common definitional foundation
    - Focus on governance-relevant constructs rather than prescribing technical approaches ensures definitional stability does not constrain technical innovation
  - The mechanism HHS is best positioned to provide is definitional authority
  - Without it, market produces multiple competing governance frameworks that increase rather than reduce institutional uncertainty
  - Organizations with comprehensive governance maturity are nearly twice as likely to achieve successful AI deployment outcomes

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or exceeded expectations, and where they have fallen short
  - Pattern of where clinical AI has fallen short is more structurally instructive than any individual case
  - AI tools have underperformed consistently where deployed into clinical workflows without corresponding governance infrastructure
    - Not governance policy — governance infrastructure
    - The distinction matters
  - A payer deploying AI-driven prior authorization with stated commitment to responsible AI deployment, clinical oversight, and appeals processes has governance policy
  - In documented cases producing most significant harms, they lacked governance architecture
    - Structural connections between policy commitments and operational reality that would make commitments binding rather than aspirational
  - Policy says review happens; architecture makes review impossible to bypass
  - Where the two diverge — and evidence shows they have diverged systematically — clinical AI tools fall short not because the model failed but because governance structure was not designed to hold under operational pressure
  - Where clinical AI has met or exceeded expectations, structural pattern is consistent
    - Constrained deployments with explicit accountability architecture have succeeded
    - Environments where AI system's scope is bounded
    - Named clinical party is responsible for outputs within that scope
    - Defined measurement specification with a threshold
    - Consequence mechanism built into workflow rather than appended to it
  - Example: radiology AI system deployed to flag potential findings for radiologist review
    - Defined escalation thresholds and explicit tracking of alert-to-review intervals
    - Succeeds because governance architecture and clinical workflow are aligned
    - AI narrows the decision space; human retains authority over the decision
    - System's accountability infrastructure makes the division legible
  - Question HHS should focus on is not which AI tools have worked but what structural conditions made them work
  - The tools are not the limiting variable; the governance architecture is

- Question 7: Administrative hurdles to AI adoption
  - Most significant administrative hurdle is not procurement process complexity, documentation burden, or regulatory uncertainty in the abstract
  - It is the absence of governance infrastructure standards that would allow institutional legal and risk function to complete due diligence and approve deployment
  - In high-liability clinical AI deployments, adoption choke point is consistently the legal and risk layer
    - General Counsel's office, Chief Risk Officer, and equivalents in integrated delivery networks and academic medical centers
    - They are not opposed to AI
    - They are unable to approve AI deployments that cannot answer three questions with institutional specificity:
      - Who is responsible for what this system does?
      - How will we know if it stops working?
      - What are we required to do when it doesn't?
    - Where those questions cannot be answered in writing, with named parties and defined thresholds, legal counsel will advise against deployment
    - Rationally, correctly, and in a way that no amount of clinical performance evidence will override
    - Clinical medical officers and nursing leadership often raise initial demand signal; legal and risk function holds structural veto
  - Enterprise data confirms this pattern
    - Only 28% of CEOs take direct responsibility for AI governance
    - Only 17% of boards exercise active oversight of AI governance decisions
    - Speed-to-market pressure is top governance barrier — cited by 45% of organizations overall and 56% among technical leaders
    - Creates persistent tension between deployment velocity and institutional due diligence
    - In health systems, tension is particularly acute because clinical AI outputs carry professional liability implications
  - Predictable administrative hurdles follow
    - Extended procurement cycles
    - Conditional approvals requiring governance documentation that doesn't exist in standard form
    - Deployment architectures adding human review requirements without specifying how review is operationally structured
    - These are rational institutional responses to governance uncertainty, not bureaucratic failures
  - Implication for HHS
    - Fastest path to reducing administrative hurdles is not streamlining procurement process
    - It is creating governance infrastructure standards that allow legal and risk layer to answer foundational questions with confidence
    - When those questions have answers, adoption proceeds
    - Process improvements to procurement timelines address symptom without touching cause
  - US-led Clinical AI Accountability Architecture standard would position HHS to set reference point for OECD partners and international AI governance frameworks

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Most consequential interoperability gap in clinical AI is not data interoperability
  - It is governance interoperability — absence of common standard for what clinical AI accountability documentation looks like and how it moves between institutions, regulators, and payers
  - Currently, every institution deploying clinical AI produces governance documentation in locally defined format, against locally defined standards, validated by locally defined processes
  - When institution seeks reimbursement, accreditation, or regulatory approval, governance documentation must be translated into whatever format receiving institution requires
  - Translation cost is enormous, largely invisible in discussions of AI adoption barriers, and entirely unnecessary
  - Data interoperability work HHS has done through ONC demonstrates the model
    - Same infrastructure logic applies to governance documentation
  - If HHS established a governance data standard specifying minimum structured elements of clinical AI accountability documentation
    - Who is responsible, what is being measured, what triggers action
    - Any institution producing compliant documentation would be legible to any other institution in the ecosystem
  - Standard would reduce, not increase, documentation burden
    - Vendors could demonstrate compliance with single, portable governance standard rather than producing bespoke documentation for each institutional buyer
    - Multi-stakeholder process building on existing ONC, FDA, and standards-body infrastructure would govern development and maintenance
  - Market opportunity this unlocks
    - AI vendors could demonstrate compliance once rather than repeatedly
    - Payers could incorporate governance verification into prior authorization and coverage decisions using structured, machine-readable attestations
    - Regulators could evaluate governance architecture across institutions using comparable data
    - Accreditation bodies could assess governance maturity against defined standard
    - Ecosystem gains interoperability benefit without requiring standardization of AI technology itself
  - Concern that governance data standard creates additional administrative burden is legitimate and addressable
    - Minimal viable accountability record — single-page artifact specifying:
      - Accountable owner
      - Metric, threshold, and frequency
      - Mandatory escalation action
      - Evidence link
    - Institutions already produce this information in fragmented form across procurement records, risk assessments, and compliance documentation
    - Standard consolidates it; does not create it
  - Registry would store governance-artifact metadata — not patient data — with data minimization, HIPAA alignment, and voluntary attestation as defaults

- Question 9: Challenges patients and caregivers wish to see addressed
  - Patients and caregivers want AI in clinical care subject to same fundamental accountability structure they expect from any clinical decision
    - Someone who is answerable for what it does
    - A way of knowing whether it is working
    - A clear consequence when it isn't
  - Specific concern and unmet need is accountability displacement
    - When AI system is positioned between patient and care their physician recommends
    - When system produces denial or modification without patient having legible path to identify who made the decision, why, and with what authority
    - Patient experiences same outcome as from human clinical error but without institutional accountability architecture that makes human clinical errors governable
    - Appeals process assumes a decision-maker
    - Algorithmic displacement means there may be no decision-maker in the sense appeals architecture was designed to address
  - Documented evidence is consistent with this concern
    - AMA survey: 82% of physicians reported patients abandoning treatment due to AI-driven prior authorization processes
    - Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations documented cases where elderly Medicare Advantage patients were denied post-acute care by AI model with ~90% error rate
    - Patients died during or after algorithmic termination of coverage
    - Patients and families experienced clinical outcome shaped by AI system without governance pathway that could reach where decision was actually made
  - Accountability displacement also raises equity concerns HHS should address directly
    - AI systems trained on historical data reproduce patterns present in that data, including disparities in access, treatment, and outcomes across demographic groups
    - Where clinical AI operates without accountability architecture specifying monitoring for distributional outcomes and subgroup impact
    - System can amplify existing disparities invisibly
    - Proposed standards must structurally encode equity monitoring as architectural requirement, not aspirational add-on
  - Patients do not need to understand how AI systems work to have this concern satisfied
    - They need to know a named institutional party is accountable for clinical AI outputs
    - There is a metric telling that party whether system is working across all patient populations
    - There is a trigger requiring action when it isn't
    - That is not a technical specification — it is a governance condition communicable to patients and caregivers in plain language
  - What patients are asking for is governance architecture that healthcare institutions currently lack vocabulary to promise and frameworks to build
  - HHS's role is to supply both

- Question 10: AI research areas HHS should prioritize
  - HHS should prioritize governance infrastructure research — not AI capability research
  - Limiting variable in clinical AI adoption is not quality or capability of available AI systems
  - It is institutional capacity to deploy those systems within accountability architectures that make them safe to scale
  - 10a: Published findings on impact of adopted AI tools in clinical care
    - Published evidence on clinical AI impact is growing but structurally uneven
    - Where AI tools deployed into governed clinical environments — with defined accountability, measurement specifications, and escalation mechanisms — outcomes data is strong
      - COMPOSER sepsis prediction system at UC San Diego Health: 17% reduction in sepsis-related mortality across multi-site implementation, statistically significant improvements in early detection and intervention timing
      - Duke University Health System's Sepsis Watch program: 27% reduction in sepsis mortality, governance architecture identified as critical factor in sustained performance
    - In diagnostic imaging, 2025 scoping review in eClinicalMedicine analyzed 140 studies of AI in radiology practice
      - Found consistent improvements in diagnostic accuracy and workflow efficiency
      - Reductions of 20–30 minutes in average read times for certain imaging categories
      - Most studies evaluated AI performance in controlled or semi-controlled settings — not under full operational pressures of clinical deployment
    - Structural pattern across literature is consistent with governance architecture argument
      - Positive outcomes cluster in deployments where accountability infrastructure was designed into workflow
      - Failures cluster where AI capability was deployed without corresponding governance architecture
      - Binding constraint on scaling successes is not the AI — it is institutional governance infrastructure surrounding it
  - 10b: How literature approaches costs, benefits, and transfers of clinical AI
    - Cost-benefit literature on clinical AI is methodologically immature in ways that directly affect governance and policy decisions
    - 2025 systematic review in npj Digital Medicine examined 19 studies of AI cost-effectiveness in healthcare
      - Majority relied on static modeling assumptions that overstated benefits
      - Underreported indirect costs — including workflow disruption, retraining, and governance overhead
      - Failed to account for cost transfers between institutional actors
    - 2025 medRxiv systematic review of AI costing methods across 55 studies
      - Lifecycle costs were consistently omitted
      - Fewer than half of studies reported explicit costing methods
      - None addressed governance infrastructure costs of sustained AI deployment
    - Structural gap in literature is not absence of cost data
      - It is absence of cost framework accounting for how clinical AI redistributes costs and risks across institutional boundaries
        - Institution deploying system bears implementation and governance costs
        - Vendor captures licensing revenue
        - Payer captures administrative efficiency gains
        - Patient bears clinical risk when governance architecture is absent or degraded
      - No existing cost-benefit framework models this transfer structure
      - Policy decisions based on current cost-effectiveness evidence operate without visibility into who actually bears cost when AI governance fails
    - HHS-funded research should develop cost-benefit methodologies that include governance infrastructure as required cost category and model transfer of risk across institutional actors
  - Research priorities — HHS should fund three research programs that do not currently exist in adequate form
    - First: Clinical AI governance architecture research
      - Systematic study of what accountability structures work in clinical AI deployment
      - What makes them degrade under operational pressure
      - What measurement specifications are most predictive of maintained accountability over time
      - Governance analog to clinical safety research
      - Patient safety research transformed hospital operations by making safety failures measurable and governable
      - Same transformation needed for clinical AI governance
      - Should produce defined methods and deliverables: governance architecture auditing protocols, degradation indicators, evidence-based standards for accountability structure design
      - AHRQ, NIH, and CMS demonstration project funding are well-positioned vehicles
      - Coordination with existing Patient Safety Organization infrastructure would avoid duplication
    - Second: Governance failure taxonomy research
      - Structured program to catalog structural conditions that have produced clinical AI harm
      - Analogous to how aviation safety built systematic incident taxonomy making systemic failures visible and addressable
      - Current tracking efforts document type and pattern of failures — AI Incident Database cataloged 108 new incidents in most recent reporting period
      - These efforts document what happened without systematically analyzing what governance condition was absent
      - Understanding why an AI system produced harm requires understanding what accountability, measurement, or consequence mechanism was missing
      - Should produce structured classification of governance failure modes organized by specific architectural absence (accountability gap, measurement gap, consequence gap)
      - Enables pattern detection across incidents and institutions
      - Coordination with AHRQ's Patient Safety Organization network would integrate AI governance failures into broader patient safety evidence base
    - Third: Governance interoperability standards development
      - Research and consensus-building infrastructure to produce clinical AI governance data standard described in Question 8
      - Foundational infrastructure for entire clinical AI ecosystem
      - Kind of public good private sector will not produce independently because value accrues to ecosystem rather than any individual firm

- About the Commenter
  - The Interstitial Institute applies structural governance analysis across public and private sector domains
  - Frameworks referenced reflect analysis of AI governance conditions developed through examination of institutional governance architecture across federal AI implementation, healthcare oversight, financial regulation, transportation safety, cybersecurity, and critical infrastructure domains
  - Institute's work focuses on identifying and naming structural conditions between systems where responsibility, measurement, and governance are absent
  - The spaces where institutional failures persist not because they are unknown but because no governance architecture exists to make them addressable
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A governance-focused think tank argues that clinical AI adoption is stalled not by regulatory burden but by the absence of accountability architecture, and urges HHS to establish structural standards requiring named responsible parties, defined metrics, and mandatory consequence mechanisms for every deployed AI system.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Walt Simmons, The Interstitial Institute
- **Type:** Academic/Research (Think Tank)
- **Role/Expertise:** Structural governance analysis across public and private sector domains; expertise in AI governance conditions across federal AI implementation, healthcare oversight, financial regulation, transportation safety, cybersecurity, and critical infrastructure
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Research organization focused on identifying governance gaps in institutional systems; no direct commercial stake but positioned as thought leader in AI governance frameworks
structuredSections.corePosition: The biggest barrier to clinical AI adoption is not regulatory burden or technical immaturity—it is the absence of governance infrastructure that would make adoption safe to accelerate. Clinical environments cannot answer three foundational questions about AI systems: who is specifically responsible for outputs, how do we know if it's working, and what triggers when it isn't. HHS should shift from behavioral requirements (does the AI meet a threshold?) to architectural requirements (is accountability structurally present?) and create the definitional authority and standards that make AI governance legible across the healthcare ecosystem.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish a Clinical AI Accountability Architecture standard requiring three structural conditions before any clinically consequential AI deployment:
  - A named responsible party (not a vendor or committee) whose accountability is operationally connected to the system's clinical outputs
  - A measurement specification identifying what metric is tracked, at what threshold, and at what frequency
  - A consequence mechanism specifying what must happen when the threshold is crossed
- Condition CMS reimbursement eligibility for AI-augmented clinical workflows on demonstrated accountability architecture
  - Begin with voluntary attestation and phased pilots before mandatory implementation
- Develop a Clinical AI Accountability Registry
  - Federal-level enumeration of clinical AI use cases and certified accountability architecture
  - Schema and attestation container, not a centralized patient-data repository
  - Federated through existing reporting rails with voluntary attestation as default
- Publish a Clinical AI Governance Lexicon
  - Standardized governance terms with precise institutional definitions
  - Evidence-binding authority mapping each term to required evidence artifacts and escalation triggers
  - Enable standardized procurement templates, accreditation criteria, and CMS program integrity tools
- Support development of governance integrity evaluation methodology
  - Assess whether institutional accountability architecture remains intact under operational conditions
  - Conduct through existing oversight channels (accrediting bodies, CMS survey processes)
- Establish a governance data standard for clinical AI accountability documentation
  - Minimal viable accountability record: accountable owner; metric, threshold, frequency; mandatory escalation action; evidence link
  - Reduce documentation burden by enabling single portable compliance demonstration
- Fund three research programs:
  - Clinical AI governance architecture research (what structures work, what makes them degrade)
  - Governance failure taxonomy research (catalog structural conditions producing harm, analogous to aviation safety)
  - Governance interoperability standards development
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Clinical AI systems operate between physician and patient without accountability architecture connecting outputs to responsible parties
  - 61% of physicians report AI increasing prior authorization denials
  - 82% report patients abandoning treatment due to AI-driven prior authorization
  - 94% report negative clinical outcomes from AI-mediated decision-making
- Market failure: AI vendors cannot cross procurement threshold because institutional buyers lack governance architecture to accept accountability
- Federal agencies achieved behavioral compliance with AI requirements but governance architecture did not function
  - 15 of 20 agency AI inventories contained incomplete or inaccurate data
  - Nine of GAO's original recommendations remained open as of July 2025
- Documented harms from AI systems operating without governance architecture
  - UnitedHealthcare's nH Predict model: ~90% error rate, denial rates rose from 10.9% to 22.7%, patients died during or after algorithmic termination of coverage
  - Cigna's PXDX algorithm: 300,000+ denials in two months, 1.2 seconds average review time per claim
- Pre-deployment testing is insufficient
  - 2026 International AI Safety Report found models can distinguish between test settings and deployment, exploit evaluation loopholes
  - Evaluation can pass while governance fails
- Legal and risk functions hold structural veto on AI adoption
  - Only 28% of CEOs take direct responsibility for AI governance
  - Only 17% of boards exercise active oversight
  - Cannot approve deployments that cannot answer foundational accountability questions
- Accountability displacement harms patients
  - Appeals process assumes a decision-maker; algorithmic displacement means there may be no decision-maker in the sense appeals architecture was designed to address
- AI systems can amplify existing disparities invisibly without equity monitoring as architectural requirement
- Cost-benefit literature is methodologically immature
  - Overstates benefits, underreports indirect costs, fails to account for cost transfers between institutional actors
  - No framework models who bears cost when AI governance fails
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a structural paradox: the clinical environments AI vendors are selling into cannot answer three foundational questions about AI systems already operating within them
- Distinguishes between governance policy (stated commitments) and governance architecture (structural connections that make commitments binding)
  - "Policy says review happens. Architecture makes review impossible to bypass."
- Frames the adoption barrier as a legal/risk function problem, not a clinical resistance problem
  - Clinical medical officers and nursing leadership raise demand signal; legal and risk function holds structural veto
- Proposes "governance integrity evaluation" as analog to safety culture audits—assessing whether accountability architecture remains intact under operational conditions, not just whether outputs are acceptable
- Draws parallel to patient safety research: "The field of patient safety research transformed hospital operations by making safety failures measurable and therefore governable. The same transformation is needed for clinical AI governance."
- Proposes aviation safety incident taxonomy as model for governance failure taxonomy
- Identifies governance interoperability as the overlooked gap—not data interoperability but the absence of common standards for how accountability documentation moves between institutions
- Notes that a US-led standard would position HHS to set reference point for OECD partners and international AI governance frameworks
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The most consequential barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not regulatory burden, reimbursement lag, or technical immaturity. It is the absence of governance infrastructure that would make adoption safe to accelerate."
- "Policy says review happens. Architecture makes review impossible to bypass. Where the two diverge — and the evidence shows they have diverged systematically, not occasionally — clinical AI tools fall short not because the model failed but because the governance structure surrounding the model was not designed to hold under operational pressure."
- "The question HHS should focus on is not which AI tools have worked but what structural conditions made them work. The tools are not the limiting variable. The governance architecture is."
