structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Background and context for this comment
  - I am a senior leader responsible for digital health and inclusion-focused innovation at a safety-net academic medical center in Brooklyn
  - I am developing an institutional framework for responsible AI governance and adoption
  - My perspective is informed by direct implementation experience deploying patient-facing AI tools in a safety-net setting
  - I submit these comments in my individual professional capacity
  - I am responding to RFI Question 2 (policy priorities), Question 9 (patient and caregiver needs), and Question 10 (research priorities)

- Focus of this comment: patient- and caregiver-facing clinical AI
  - Portal chatbots, voice agents, automated intake workflows, and AI-enabled outreach used directly by patients and caregivers
  - These tools can improve access and efficiency
  - Benefits will not reach everyone unless tools are built for real-world conditions
    - Limited English proficiency
    - Low health literacy
    - Disability access needs
    - Uneven digital access
  - In safety-net settings, the most helpful federal actions are practical requirements that protect patients without imposing unrealistic burden

- Existing civil rights and accessibility obligations apply to AI-enabled care
  - HHS should make clear that AI-enabled patient communication does not reduce or replace existing legal responsibilities
  - Title VI and Section 1557, the ADA and Section 508, and the Plain Writing Act apply to AI-enabled communication pathways just as they apply to in-person, written, and telephonic communication
  - HHS can accelerate adoption by translating these obligations into baseline requirements that are clear for vendors and feasible for under-resourced providers

- Four baseline requirements for patient-facing AI (RFI Q2 and Q9)
  - Requirement 1: Transparency
    - Patients should be told at the start of an interaction that AI is involved, what it can and cannot do, and how to reach a person at any time
    - Recommended baseline: plain-language disclosure at the start of the interaction, plus a persistent "get help from a person" option
  - Requirement 2: Language access
    - In safety-net settings, language access is a basic safety requirement
    - If disclosures and instructions are only available in English, patients with limited English proficiency are shut out of safe use
    - Recommended baseline: support English and the top 2–3 languages by patient volume at the site, with an easy language switch and a clear pathway to human help in all supported languages
  - Requirement 3: Plain language
    - Transparency only works if patients understand what they are being told
    - Patient-facing AI should be written at or below a 6th-grade reading level, consistent with the Plain Writing Act
    - Should prioritize short, single-task instructions
    - Recommended baseline: plain-language scripts, one action per step, and brief comprehension supports for critical actions (for example, "Would you like me to repeat that?")
  - Requirement 4: Human escalation
    - AI tools should not become a gatekeeper to care
    - If a patient requests help or an issue remains unresolved after one clarification attempt, the tool should immediately route the patient to appropriate staff—without additional automated menus
    - Recommended baseline: immediate escalation on request, with appropriate routing (nurse, pharmacist, scheduling), and clear emergency guidance when clinically appropriate

- Illustrative example of preventable harm
  - A portal chatbot provides accurate information but only in English, with high reading complexity and no confirmation of understanding
  - A caregiver misunderstands next steps, leading to delayed care
  - This is preventable through multilingual support, plain language, comprehension prompts, and reliable escalation

- Pre-deployment readiness and ongoing monitoring (RFI Q2)
  - Before go-live, organizations should:
    - Define readiness criteria in advance
    - Stress-test predictable failure modes through structured scenario-based simulation covering language, literacy, and device barriers
    - Verify performance against explicit Go/No-Go thresholds
    - Informal usability checks are not sufficient
  - Once in use, providers and vendors should:
    - Monitor completion, fallback, and escalation rates—disaggregated by language group—as early warning indicators of uneven performance
  - HHS should encourage organizations to integrate AI-related risks into existing patient safety monitoring systems
    - Classify AI workflow incidents as high-impact when they could affect clinical decisions, documentation accuracy, or care access
  - Recommended baseline: if fallback or escalation rates exceed 20% for any language group for two consecutive months, require corrective action plans and pause high-risk pathways until issues are resolved

- Research and development priorities (RFI Q10)
  - Federal grants and contracts should prioritize tools developed with and validated in diverse populations
    - Medicaid beneficiaries
    - People with disabilities
    - Limited English proficiency populations
    - Safety-net settings
  - Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers
  - HHS should support scenario-based simulated caregiver persona testing as an accepted preliminary validation step in proposals for patient-facing AI tools
    - This pre-deployment method identifies language, literacy, accessibility, and device-specific failures before patients are exposed
    - Reduces compliance risk, lowers remediation costs, and prevents avoidable care delays
  - HHS should fund implementation research on adoption in resource-constrained settings
  - HHS should support shared infrastructure that under-resourced providers can use without prohibitive licensing costs

- Summary of recommendations
  - Establish four baseline requirements—transparency, language access, plain language, and reliable escalation—via guidance, regulation, or contract language
  - Pair these baselines with practical pre-deployment readiness criteria, simple ongoing monitoring, and targeted research investment
  - This approach will protect patients, reduce avoidable burden, and improve access and outcomes across diverse communities

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
A digital health leader at a Brooklyn safety-net hospital urges HHS to establish four practical baseline requirements—transparency, language access, plain language, and human escalation—for patient-facing AI tools to ensure benefits reach underserved populations.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Vikki S. Small, MPH, LSSGB (submitted in individual professional capacity)
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Senior leader for digital health and inclusion-focused innovation at a safety-net academic medical center; developing institutional AI governance framework; direct experience deploying patient-facing AI tools
- **Geographic Scope:** Local (Brooklyn, NY)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly responsible for implementing AI tools in a setting serving patients with limited English proficiency, low health literacy, and uneven digital access; sees firsthand how poorly designed tools create barriers

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
Patient-facing AI tools like portal chatbots and voice agents can improve access and efficiency, but those benefits will not reach everyone unless tools are built for real-world conditions—limited English proficiency, low health literacy, disability access needs, and uneven digital access. HHS should establish clear, practical baseline requirements that protect patients without imposing unrealistic burden on under-resourced providers, and should make explicit that existing civil rights and accessibility laws apply fully to AI-enabled care.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish four baseline requirements for patient-facing AI via guidance, regulation, or contract language:
  - **Transparency:** Plain-language disclosure at interaction start plus persistent "get help from a person" option
  - **Language access:** Support English and top 2–3 languages by patient volume, with easy language switch and human help pathway in all supported languages
  - **Plain language:** 6th-grade reading level or below, one action per step, comprehension supports for critical actions
  - **Human escalation:** Immediate routing to appropriate staff on request or after one failed clarification, with clear emergency guidance

- Require pre-deployment readiness standards:
  - Define readiness criteria in advance
  - Conduct structured scenario-based simulation testing for language, literacy, and device barriers
  - Establish explicit Go/No-Go thresholds
  - Informal usability checks are insufficient

- Implement ongoing monitoring requirements:
  - Track completion, fallback, and escalation rates disaggregated by language group
  - Integrate AI-related risks into existing patient safety monitoring systems
  - Classify AI workflow incidents as high-impact when they could affect clinical decisions, documentation accuracy, or care access
  - If fallback or escalation rates exceed 20% for any language group for two consecutive months, require corrective action plans and pause high-risk pathways

- Prioritize research and development for underserved populations:
  - Federal grants should prioritize tools developed with and validated in diverse populations (Medicaid beneficiaries, people with disabilities, LEP populations, safety-net settings)
  - Accept scenario-based simulated caregiver persona testing as preliminary validation in grant proposals
  - Fund implementation research on adoption in resource-constrained settings
  - Support shared infrastructure accessible to under-resourced providers without prohibitive licensing costs

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- AI tools built and validated in well-resourced systems are deployed in safety-net settings with weaker performance and new access barriers
- English-only AI tools shut out patients with limited English proficiency from safe use
- High reading complexity in AI communications leads to misunderstanding and delayed care
- AI tools risk becoming gatekeepers to care if human escalation pathways are inadequate
- Informal usability checks are insufficient to catch predictable failure modes before deployment
- Under-resourced providers face unrealistic burden if requirements aren't practical and feasible
- Existing civil rights and accessibility obligations (Title VI, Section 1557, ADA, Section 508, Plain Writing Act) may not be clearly understood to apply to AI-enabled communication

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Offers a concrete failure scenario from safety-net implementation: a portal chatbot provides accurate information but only in English with high reading complexity and no comprehension confirmation—a caregiver misunderstands next steps, leading to delayed care
- Proposes a specific, actionable threshold: 20% fallback or escalation rate for any language group over two consecutive months should trigger corrective action and pathway pause
- Advocates for "simulated caregiver persona testing" as a pre-deployment validation method—a practical approach to catch language, literacy, accessibility, and device-specific failures before patients are exposed
- Frames language access not as a nice-to-have but as "a basic safety requirement" in safety-net settings
- Emphasizes that existing civil rights laws already apply to AI—HHS just needs to translate them into clear, vendor-friendly baseline requirements

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI tools should not become a gatekeeper to care."
- "Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers."
- "If disclosures and instructions are only available in English, patients with limited English proficiency are shut out of safe use."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Writing as a mental health provider and parent to reiterate PsiAN's statement on the use of AI in mental healthcare
- Emphasizing the need for regulation and the removal of AI companies as the primary drivers and decision makers in this area
- Therapeutic work is based in human relationships
  - AI prioritizes profit and validation of a user's thoughts without clinical discernment
- There is a lack of data privacy and transparency that requires oversight
- As a parent with children growing up in the era of AI
  - Looking for effective, neutral, human-centered oversight of AI before there is sweeping adoption of this tool
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Mental health provider and parent calls for human-centered AI oversight, warning that therapeutic work requires human relationships, not profit-driven technology that validates without clinical discernment.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Hannah Lee (referencing PsiAN)
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider / Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Mental health provider; parent
- **Geographic Scope:** Not specified
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected as a clinician whose therapeutic practice depends on human relationships; personally invested as a parent raising children in the AI era
structuredSections.corePosition: I believe therapeutic work is fundamentally based in human relationships, not AI systems that prioritize profit and validate users' thoughts without clinical discernment. We need effective, neutral, human-centered oversight of AI before widespread adoption in mental healthcare.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Remove AI companies as primary drivers and decision makers in mental healthcare AI policy
- Establish effective, neutral, human-centered oversight of AI
- Implement regulation before sweeping adoption of AI tools in mental health
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- AI in mental healthcare prioritizes profit over therapeutic outcomes
- AI validates user thoughts without clinical discernment
- Lack of data privacy in AI mental health tools
- Lack of transparency requiring oversight
- Sweeping adoption of AI tools before adequate regulation exists
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Dual perspective as both a mental health clinician understanding therapeutic relationships and a parent concerned about children's exposure to AI
- Distinction between human therapeutic relationships and AI that "validates without clinical discernment"
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Therapeutic work is based in human relationships, rather than AI that prioritizes profit and validation of a user's thoughts without clinical discernment."
- "As a parent with children growing up in the era of AI I am looking for effective, neutral, human-centered oversight of AI before there is sweeping adoption of this tool."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Background and credentials
  - Retired Marriage and Family Therapist from California
  - Practiced psychoanalytic psychotherapy for 30 years
  - Still very involved in the mental health field
  - Currently a therapy patient myself

- Concerns about AI in healthcare and mental health
  - Very worried about what feels like unbridled enthusiasm for incorporating AI into healthcare
  - Especially concerned about AI in the mental health field
  - AI has a place in the modern world, but we must be careful
  - Should not attempt to replace actual human beings when dealing with other humans in vulnerable situations

- Fundamental limitations of AI in therapeutic contexts
  - AI can't replicate human emotions, nor should it
  - AI is great for analyzing data but can't feel empathy
  - It can only deliver lines that sound like empathy
  - No actual human connection exists in dialogue between a human and a machine

- Evidence of AI causing harm
  - AI has already caused damage to actual people
  - Examples include encouraging people to harm themselves
  - This happens because AI simply reflects back their pain
  - Some safeguards have been put in place recently
  - AI still has a long way to go before it can be considered truly safe and effective

- Recommendation
  - Please consider slowing this process down
  - More R&D needed before incorporating AI into settings with vulnerable human beings
  - People in these settings are expecting to be helped

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
A retired psychotherapist with 30 years of experience urges caution on AI in mental health, warning that machines delivering "lines that sound like empathy" cannot replace genuine human connection for vulnerable patients.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Carol Gould
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Retired Marriage and Family Therapist with 30 years practicing psychoanalytic psychotherapy; currently a therapy patient
- **Geographic Scope:** State (San Francisco, California)
- **Stake in Issue:** Professional expertise in mental health treatment and personal experience as a current therapy patient

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
I believe AI has a place in the modern world, but we must be very careful not to replace actual human beings when dealing with vulnerable people in mental health settings. AI can analyze data but cannot feel genuine empathy or create real human connection—it can only deliver lines that sound like empathy.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Slow down the process of incorporating AI into healthcare
- Conduct more research and development before deploying AI in settings with vulnerable populations
- Ensure AI is truly safe and effective before use in mental health contexts

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Unbridled enthusiasm for AI in healthcare without sufficient caution
- AI cannot replicate human emotions or genuine empathy
  - Only delivers scripted responses that mimic empathy
  - No actual human connection in human-machine dialogue
- AI has already caused documented harm
  - Encouraged people to harm themselves by reflecting back their pain
- Current safeguards are insufficient
  - AI has a long way to go before being truly safe
- Vulnerable people seeking help may be harmed instead

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Offers dual perspective as both a 30-year practitioner and current therapy patient
- Draws distinction between analyzing data (AI strength) and feeling empathy (human necessity)
- Identifies specific failure mode: AI reflecting pain back to vulnerable people can encourage self-harm

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI can't feel empathy. It can only deliver lines that sound like empathy."
- "There is no actual human connection in a dialogue between a human being and a machine."
- "AI has already caused damage to actual people, such as encouraging them to harm themselves, simply because it is reflecting back their pain."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Response to Questions 3 and 8 regarding governance, accountability, and interoperability
- Recommends consideration of a principle to support responsible and trusted AI adoption in clinical care
- AI systems that significantly influence clinical documentation or clinical decision-making should:
  - Incorporate appropriate identity and authorization controls at the point of use
  - Enable clear user attribution and purpose-of-use awareness
  - Support interoperable records consistent with existing federal standards
- Applying existing identity, authorization, and interoperability principles to AI-enabled clinical workflows can:
  - Reduce uncertainty
  - Strengthen accountability
  - Promote patient trust
  - Maintain alignment with established federal health IT frameworks
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Health IT company recommends extending existing identity, authorization, and interoperability standards to AI systems that influence clinical documentation and decision-making.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Quiet Signal Technologies LLX
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Health IT technology company (implied focus on identity/authorization solutions)
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Develops technology that would be affected by AI governance standards in healthcare
structuredSections.corePosition: AI systems influencing clinical documentation or decision-making should be held to the same identity, authorization, and interoperability standards already established for health IT. This approach leverages existing federal frameworks rather than creating new ones, reducing uncertainty while strengthening accountability and patient trust.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- AI systems that significantly influence clinical documentation or decision-making should:
  - Incorporate appropriate identity and authorization controls at the point of use
  - Enable clear user attribution and purpose-of-use awareness
  - Support interoperable records consistent with existing federal standards
- Apply existing federal health IT principles (identity, authorization, interoperability) to AI-enabled clinical workflows
structuredSections.mainConcerns: No specific concerns raised
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Offers a pragmatic approach: rather than creating new AI-specific governance frameworks, extend existing federal health IT standards to cover AI systems
- Frames the solution around three established concepts: identity controls, user attribution, and interoperability
structuredSections.keyQuotations: - "AI systems that significantly influence clinical documentation or clinical decision-making should incorporate appropriate identity and authorization controls at the point of use, enable clear user attribution and purpose-of-use awareness, and support interoperable records consistent with existing federal standards."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- WellSpan Health's vision and background
  - Integrated delivery system focused on value-based care
  - More than 2,700 employed providers
  - More than 250 locations
  - Nine award-winning hospitals
  - Home care and behavioral health organization
  - Serves central Pennsylvania and northern Maryland

- Introduction and framing
  - U.S. healthcare stands at a critical juncture
  - After decades of fragmentation, rising costs, and uneven outcomes, we now have technological capabilities to transform care delivery through market-driven solutions powered by AI
  - Federal government must prioritize strong incentives rather than punitive regulatory or enforcement postures
  - A cohesive, multi-perspective, nationally consistent framework is essential for all tiers of AI
  - National framework of legal and regulatory incentives will support robust contracting practices
  - Need to align regulation, reimbursement, and R&D investments to reduce uncertainty impeding innovation
  - Requires federal leadership and unified national vision that promotes innovation, protects patients, and empowers providers

- WellSpan Health's successes in clinical deployment of AI
  - AI is a clinical support tool helping teams deliver faster diagnoses, expand access, and spend more time with patients
  - Safety, privacy, and human judgment remain at the center
  - Early detection and intervention improves outcomes and reduces costs
  - Uses FDA-cleared clinical AI to help radiologists identify and prioritize urgent findings
    - Stroke, pulmonary embolism, brain hemorrhage, aneurysm, spinal fractures
    - AI analyzes imaging and flags high-risk cases within minutes
    - Radiologist expert still reads every image
    - AI supports triage and prioritization, does not replace clinical judgment
  - Only health system in central Pennsylvania to deploy AI for breast cancer detection during screening mammograms
    - Helps identify cancers difficult to detect, particularly in women with dense breast tissue or implants
    - Earlier detection allows quicker connection to follow-up care, sometimes before patient leaves appointment

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation and adoption
  - Financial constraints
    - Substantial up-front and ongoing costs for implementing, monitoring, and maintaining AI systems
    - Responsible use requires human oversight, safety reporting, continuous performance auditing
  - Liability concerns
    - Fear of legal exposure for missed findings or automation errors
    - Unclear risk allocation between vendors and providers
  - Malpractice impact
  - Change-management challenges
    - AI introduces new workflows, training needs, integration dependencies
    - Can slow adoption and create additional failure points
  - Infrastructure complexity
  - Data accessibility and interoperability among legacy platform providers
  - Patient and clinician trust issues
    - Skepticism that AI could diminish clinician's role or produce inaccurate outputs
    - Need for thoughtful design, education, and transparent quality reporting
  - Need for national operating standard
    - Would support development of contracting norms
    - Help providers evaluate trustworthiness, reliability, and measurable benefit of AI tools
    - Clear expectations for risk allocation, transparency, avoiding "black box" data gaps, reporting requirements, insurability
  - Fee-for-service reimbursement creates structural disincentive
    - AI tools that prevent costly interventions or improve efficiency are not rewarded
    - Productivity gains would be punitive under fee-for-service, creating self-defeating cycle
    - Value-based models would allow system to absorb AI's deflationary benefits and reinvest savings

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Payment policies recognizing AI-enabled care with predictable, proportionate oversight
    - Reimbursement pathways permitting separate payment for AI-based services when demonstrably contributing to clinical value
    - Example: post-discharge telephonic follow-ups conducted by AI agent
    - Adoption should not be penalized relative to fully human workflows
  - Outcomes-oriented pathway for AI-based healthcare services
    - Radiology triage, early detection models
    - Acknowledge throughput gains and earlier, lower-cost interventions
  - Maximum flexibility for providers using validated tools
    - Avoid prescriptive constraints suppressing capacity gains
    - Particularly important in regions with acute workforce shortages
  - Provisional clearances with structured, stepwise pathway to full accreditation
    - Support timely innovation while allowing evidence to accumulate
  - Expand AI-supported care that is separately paid
    - Dedicated AI-related billing codes
    - Incentive programs tied to demonstrated improvements in value-based care
  - Federal ban on payer-mandated "AI-prohibition" clauses for revenue-cycle automation
    - Creates significant inequity: payers use AI for claims processing and denial management while restricting providers
    - Providers should deploy AI on equal footing
    - Would promote fairness, reduce administrative burden, support balanced claims ecosystem
  - Regulatory priorities for nationally consistent policies
    - Cybersecurity policy should be incentive-based
      - Reward providers for maintaining reasonable, diligent adherence to evolving best practices
      - Not penalize for sophisticated AI-driven attacks overwhelming well-prepared organizations
    - Federal leadership to replace patchwork of 50+ different state AI laws
      - Many conflict with existing federal privacy and disclosure requirements
      - Preemptive federal standard would reduce uncertainty and streamline compliance
    - Standardized Business Associate Agreement requirements
      - Close transparency gap between AI vendors and end-users
      - Vendors use opaque contracting practices and "black-box" data silos
      - Current practices shift disproportionate liability to providers
    - Clear transparency and audit requirements
      - Federal baselines for algorithmic explainability
      - Ban black-box opacity
      - Require vendors to provide audit information to end-users
    - Modernization of Stark Law and Anti-Kickback Statute
      - Include safe harbors and exceptions reducing legal uncertainty
      - Enable responsible collaboration around data sharing and cybersecurity

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Unresolved governance challenges impeding adoption
    - Liability allocation between providers and vendors
    - Standards for indemnification
    - Privacy and security obligations for training data and post-market monitoring
    - Accountability for ongoing model updates
  - HHS role in addressing gaps
    - Clarify safe-harbor conditions for providers following approved governance practices
    - Encourage shared-risk contracting models aligning vendor accountability with claimed performance
    - Promote standardized incident-reporting pathways integrating AI-related errors into existing patient-safety systems
    - Avoid punitive disincentives to disclose
  - WellSpan already routes AI-related errors through patient-safety workflows
    - Example: automated agent confirmed appointment that was never scheduled
    - Illustrates need for normalized "learn-from-events" processes
  - Patient consent frameworks require modernization
    - HHS should clarify HIPAA obligations under 45 CFR §164.508 for AI-related disclosure and consent
    - Include clear safe harbors
    - Address when and how patient consent is required for AI-supported analysis
    - Clarify scope of opt-out rights
    - Set expectations for continuously learning systems
    - Would support predictable patient communication, reduce legal ambiguity, strengthen trust

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods
  - Effective evaluation requires coupling technical robustness measures with human-centered metrics
    - Technical: drift detection, hallucination and error monitoring, bias assessments
    - Human-centered: clinician trust, handoff safety, escalation efficacy
  - WellSpan's approach
    - Supervisory AI systems monitoring for drift and triggering alerts
    - Frontline error-intake processes embedded in existing patient-safety infrastructure
    - Blended model ensures high-fidelity detection and accountability
  - Pre-deployment: real-world simulations and pilot proofs-of-concept anchored to clinical KPIs
  - Post-deployment: continuous monitoring and registries tracking performance, safety events, outcome signals
  - HHS support mechanisms
    - Contracts, grants, cooperative agreements to fund multi-site evaluations
    - Develop common registries and shared reference datasets
    - Prize competitions to accelerate transparent, rigorous robustness-testing frameworks

- Question 5: Supporting private sector activities
  - HHS should endorse and align with accreditation pathways
    - Recognize existing FDA labeling where applicable
    - Establish new models mirroring FDA principles
    - Ensure baseline safety and effectiveness with post-market surveillance
  - Preserve room for innovation sandboxes and provisional scaling
    - Allow developers to iterate and build evidence without halting progress
  - Support ACR/ABR/DNV/CMS-aligned frameworks
  - Expand registry infrastructure for outcomes tracking
  - Offer education and implementation supports for providers
  - Deemed Status model opportunity
    - HHS designates qualified accreditation organizations (similar to 42 CFR Part 488)
    - Certify AI systems meeting safety, effectiveness, and equity standards
    - Healthcare organizations using certified tools receive regulatory advantages
    - Reduces need for extensive internal evaluations
    - Especially benefits smaller community hospitals and rural health systems lacking capacity
    - Democratizes access to high-quality AI beyond well-resourced academic medical centers
  - Support clinical AI credentialing programs
    - Train healthcare professionals to evaluate, implement, and oversee AI systems
    - Essential for safe deployment, informed adoption decisions, long-term governance

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or fallen short of expectations
  - Strong performance from assistive imaging technologies
    - Triage tools and incidental finding detection
    - Meaningful throughput efficiencies
    - Earlier identification enabling lower-cost interventions and improved outcomes
    - Even with 100% human over-reads, realized measurable FTE efficiency while improving detection rates
    - Demonstrates productivity and quality gains can occur simultaneously
  - Underperformance in some conversational and agentic tools
    - When workflow integration is immature
    - Results in errors or patient friction (e.g., failed appointment confirmations)
  - High-impact opportunities remain
    - Acute-care decision support
    - Population-scale screening models (e.g., mobile low-dose CT with AI interpretation)
    - AI-augmented follow-up pathways expanding capacity without proportional staffing increases
  - Generative AI agents represent major potential
    - Automate routine administrative tasks: communications, prior authorizations, documentation, scheduling
    - Could reclaim 15-20 hours per week of clinician time
    - Directly addresses burnout, allows focus on patient care
  - Medium-term HHS role
    - Enable widespread deployment of generative AI agents
    - At scale, could automate 1-2 billion hours annually of administrative work
    - Alleviate workforce crisis pressures
    - Contribute to reductions in administrative spending (currently $250-400 billion per year)

- Question 7: Roles and decision makers influencing AI adoption
  - Adoption hinges on clinical and implementation champions with demonstrated organizational POCs
  - Supported by strong governance, finance, and IT partners
  - Influence may be top-down or bottom-up
  - Success requires ground team engagement, prioritization across competing initiatives, resources for ongoing monitoring
  - Administrative hurdles
    - Insufficient local IT capacity
    - Fragmented governance
    - Unclear funding mechanisms for new responsibilities (monitoring, auditing, validation)
    - Fear that AI could devalue clinician expertise
  - Critical to align roles, incentives, and education while budgeting for durable oversight

- Question 8: Enhanced interoperability opportunities
  - Standardized, low-cost FHIR-based exchanges would reduce vendor lock-in
  - Improve secure data sharing
  - Enable AI models to operate on consistent data elements across settings
  - Improves care quality (less test duplication, clearer cross-system understanding of patient needs)
  - Lowers integration cost for innovators
  - Benchmarks should emphasize common data types and metrics
  - Support fair head-to-head evaluations and post-market monitoring
  - Couple with registries capturing system analytics and patient outcomes over time

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver concerns
  - Privacy and data security
    - Who has access to health information
    - Whether AI systems might inadvertently expose sensitive data
  - Algorithmic bias
    - Possibility of inferior or inequitable care to certain populations
  - Transparency and explainability
    - Want to understand why AI recommended particular treatment
  - Accountability
    - If something goes wrong, who is responsible
  - HHS should address concerns by
    - Clarifying liability frameworks so patients understand rights when AI involved
    - Creating clear, modernized medical malpractice structures
    - Appropriately assigning responsibility across AI developers, healthcare organizations, and clinicians
    - Ensuring AI adoption genuinely serves patient interests and improves experience and outcomes
    - Not primarily benefiting healthcare organizations or technology vendors

- Question 10: AI research priorities
  - Priority research should target multicenter, real-world trials linking AI use to patient-level outcomes
    - Not only process metrics
    - Today's literature is niche and organization-dependent
    - Limited ability to report outcomes absent robust analytics
  - HHS can catalyze shared registries and common analytics
    - Capture clinical efficacy, workflow safety events, economic endpoints across diverse sites
  - Cost/benefit studies should reflect
    - Capacity gains (growth, earlier detection)
    - Ongoing costs of safe operation (human oversight, monitoring, vendor fees)
    - Model transfers across stakeholders (providers, payers, patients)
  - Embed validated predictive models into clinical and drug trials to hasten evidence generation
  - Education research should tackle AI literacy for clinicians and public to address perception gaps
  - Programmatic payment pathways to consider
    - AI "Meaningful Use" style framework
      - Define usage requirements
      - Tie incentives to Medicare/Medicaid
      - Integrate with national standards for safety, transparency, interoperability
      - Start where value-based models already track quality (mortality, readmissions, HAIs)
    - AI Add-on Payments in Medicare
      - Time-limited supplemental payments (3-5 years with step-down percentages)
      - For clinically valuable AI
      - Guardrails against overuse (per-episode caps, documentation standards, periodic audits)
      - Structured transition to permanent codes or bundled models after evidence review
      - Reduces financial barriers, rewards bias mitigation and interoperability, curbs fraud risk

- Conclusion
  - Historic opportunity to transform U.S. healthcare through AI-driven innovation
  - Unprecedented chance to reshape healthcare using market-driven solutions built on applied AI
  - Near-term AI value lies in expanding capacity and improving productivity
  - Addresses workforce shortages while enhancing access, quality, and patient outcomes
  - With sensible reimbursement, clear oversight, robust evaluation, and targeted R&D support, HHS can unlock value at scale
  - Especially in high-cost, acute-care, and imaging-intensive settings
  - Without compromising safety, equity, or public trust
  - Requires national consistency and alignment between federal legislators and agencies
  - Need incentivizing legal architecture rather than punitive one
  - Federal guidance should preempt conflicting state activity wherever feasible
  - Given complex patchwork of state requirements and power imbalances between AI vendors and clinical end-users
  - Recommendations reflect broad, practical concerns of providers and patients
  - Would deliver substantial benefit across early and mid-stage AI-in-healthcare ecosystem development
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A major Pennsylvania integrated health system with hands-on AI deployment experience urges HHS to create incentive-based national standards, fix fee-for-service disincentives, and establish federal preemption over conflicting state AI laws to unlock AI's potential for expanding healthcare capacity.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** WellSpan Health
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** Integrated delivery system with 2,700+ employed providers, 9 hospitals, 250+ locations; active deployer of FDA-cleared clinical AI for radiology triage and breast cancer detection
- **Geographic Scope:** Regional (central Pennsylvania and northern Maryland)
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct operational experience implementing clinical AI; affected by reimbursement structures, liability frameworks, state regulatory patchwork, and vendor contracting practices
structuredSections.corePosition: We support a responsible, innovation-friendly, and transparent regulatory framework for AI in healthcare. The federal government must prioritize strong incentives—not punitive regulatory postures—that encourage innovation rather than create fear-driven delays. A nationally consistent framework with aligned reimbursement, clear liability rules, and federal preemption of conflicting state laws is essential to unlock AI's potential to expand capacity, reduce costs, and improve patient outcomes.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Create reimbursement pathways for AI-enabled care
  - Permit separate payment for AI-based services demonstrating clinical value
  - Develop outcomes-oriented pathways for radiology triage and early detection models
  - Establish AI "Meaningful Use" style framework tied to Medicare/Medicaid incentives
  - Create time-limited AI add-on payments in Medicare (3-5 years with step-down) with guardrails against overuse

- Establish federal preemption and national consistency
  - Replace patchwork of 50+ conflicting state AI laws with unified federal framework for disclosure, consent, and privacy
  - Federal guidance should preempt conflicting state activity wherever feasible

- Ban payer-mandated "AI-prohibition" clauses
  - Prohibit payers from restricting provider use of AI for revenue-cycle automation while payers use AI for claims processing

- Reform liability and contracting frameworks
  - Clarify safe-harbor conditions for providers following approved governance practices
  - Encourage shared-risk contracting models aligning vendor accountability with claimed performance
  - Standardize Business Associate Agreement requirements to close transparency gap with vendors
  - Establish federal baselines for algorithmic explainability and ban black-box opacity

- Modernize consent and privacy frameworks
  - Clarify HIPAA obligations under 45 CFR §164.508 for AI-related disclosure and consent
  - Address consent requirements for continuously learning systems

- Create Deemed Status model for AI certification
  - Designate qualified accreditation organizations to certify AI systems meeting safety, effectiveness, and equity standards
  - Similar to existing Deemed Status accreditation at 42 CFR Part 488
  - Would democratize access for smaller hospitals and rural systems

- Modernize Stark Law and Anti-Kickback Statute
  - Include safe harbors and exceptions for data sharing and cybersecurity collaboration

- Make cybersecurity policy incentive-based
  - Reward providers for maintaining reasonable adherence to best practices rather than penalizing for sophisticated attacks

- Support evaluation infrastructure
  - Fund multi-site evaluations, common registries, and shared reference datasets through contracts, grants, and cooperative agreements
  - Use prize competitions to accelerate robustness-testing frameworks

- Develop clinical AI credentialing programs
  - Train healthcare professionals to evaluate, implement, and oversee AI systems
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Fee-for-service reimbursement creates structural disincentive for AI adoption
  - Productivity gains are punitive under fee-for-service models
  - Creates self-defeating cycle where investment gravitates away from high-value applications

- Patchwork of 50+ conflicting state AI laws
  - Many conflict with existing federal privacy and disclosure requirements
  - Creates uncertainty for end-users and compliance burden for vendors

- Power imbalance between AI vendors and clinical end-users
  - Vendors use opaque contracting practices and "black-box" data silos
  - Disproportionate liability shifted to providers
  - Limited ability to evaluate products or foster healthy market competition

- Unclear liability allocation
  - Fear of legal exposure for missed findings or automation errors
  - Unclear risk allocation between vendors and providers
  - Patients need clarity on accountability when AI is involved

- Substantial implementation costs
  - Up-front and ongoing costs for implementing, monitoring, and maintaining AI systems
  - Responsible use requires human oversight, safety reporting, continuous performance auditing

- Patient trust and transparency concerns
  - Privacy and data security worries
  - Algorithmic bias concerns
  - Need for explainability in AI recommendations

- Inequitable payer practices
  - Payers use AI for claims processing and denial management while restricting providers from using similar tools
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Deployed FDA-cleared AI for radiology triage that flags stroke, pulmonary embolism, brain hemorrhage, aneurysm, and spinal fractures within minutes—radiologists still read every image, but AI enables prioritization of critical cases
- Became only health system in central Pennsylvania to deploy AI for breast cancer detection in screening mammograms, enabling earlier detection particularly for women with dense breast tissue, sometimes connecting patients with follow-up care before they leave the appointment
- Even with 100% human over-reads of AI-assisted imaging, achieved measurable FTE efficiency while simultaneously improving detection rates—demonstrating productivity and quality gains can occur together
- Routes AI-related errors through existing patient-safety workflows, including an incident where an automated agent confirmed an appointment that was never scheduled—illustrating need for normalized "learn-from-events" processes
- Uses supervisory AI systems that monitor for drift and trigger alerts, combined with frontline error-intake processes embedded in patient-safety infrastructure
- Estimates generative AI agents could reclaim 15-20 hours per week of clinician time by automating communications, prior authorizations, documentation, and scheduling
- Projects that at scale, AI could automate 1-2 billion hours annually of administrative work, contributing to reductions in the $250-400 billion currently spent on healthcare administration
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Any productivity gains that result from the use of AI would be punitive under the fee-for-service models, creating a self-defeating cycle to the adoption of AI."

- "While payers increasingly rely on AI for claims processing and denial management, they often restrict providers from using similar tools to improve their own administrative efficiency."

- "The result is a democratization of access to high-quality AI, ensuring benefits are not confined to well-resourced academic medical centers."

entities.category: Electronic Health Records and Health IT
entities.label: Meaningful Use

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Regulatory frameworks surrounding AI in clinical care are characterized by significant uncertainty
  - Absence of clear, tailored guidelines for AI technologies generates hesitation among developers and healthcare providers
  - This uncertainty fosters doubt and contributes to slowdown in innovation and deployment of AI solutions
- Current regulatory requirements demand rigorous pre-market approval for AI tools
  - Approval processes are lengthy and costly
  - Poses substantial barrier to entry for startups and smaller companies lacking resources to navigate complex approval processes
  - Promising innovations that could enhance patient care and operational efficiency are delayed or sidelined
- HIPAA compliance complicates data-sharing processes essential for training AI algorithms
  - Patient data privacy requirements make it challenging to collect and utilize extensive datasets needed for effective AI tools
- Liability uncertainty when AI systems provide clinical recommendations deters healthcare providers from adoption
  - Concerns over accountability lead to reluctance in integrating AI into clinical practices
  - Providers fear potential legal repercussions from AI-driven decisions
- Current reimbursement models complicate AI tool adoption
  - Models prioritize traditional, established procedures and technologies
  - Creates financial deterrent for providers to implement AI solutions that may offer better outcomes but lack established reimbursement pathways
- Regulatory frameworks that do not promote interoperability can severely restrict AI functionality and usability
  - Without necessary support for integration, potential benefits of AI in improving clinical efficiency and patient care may be lost
- Current funding constraints on AI research slow down and delay innovation
  - Regulatory policies must adapt to support R&D and encourage stronger collaboration between public and private sectors
- Clinical validation requirements for AI tools pose additional barriers
  - Validation process is resource intensive and time consuming
  - Such challenges can deter developers from pursuing AI innovations

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for healthcare
  - Regulatory uncertainty is primary challenge
    - Absence of clear and consistent guidelines specifically tailored to AI technologies creates atmosphere of hesitation among developers and investors
    - Slows pace of innovation and deployment of AI solutions
  - Complex approval processes for AI tools
    - Lengthy and costly processes deter startups and smaller companies from entering market
    - Reduces diversity and volume of innovative solutions available to healthcare providers
  - Data privacy and security concerns
    - HIPAA compliance imposes strict requirements on patient data handling
    - Makes data sharing challenging, though data is essential for training AI algorithms
  - Integration challenges
    - Integrating AI solutions into existing healthcare systems and workflows is difficult
    - Lack of interoperability between AI tools and EHRs restricts usability and effectiveness
  - Reimbursement model issues
    - Current structures favor established procedures and technologies over innovative AI solutions
    - Slow transition to value-based care models limits financial incentives for providers
  - Liability and accountability issues
    - Uncertainty surrounding liability for AI-driven clinical decisions deters healthcare providers
    - Providers may be reluctant to implement AI tools if they fear being held accountable
  - Cultural resistance to change within healthcare organizations
    - Inherent reluctance to adopt new technologies, particularly AI
    - Concerns about reliability of AI systems, potential job displacement, and need for staff training
  - Lack of standardization in metrics and evaluation frameworks
    - Complicates assessment of AI tool effectiveness
    - Makes it challenging for providers to determine which AI solutions are truly beneficial

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Revise premarket approval process for AI tools
    - Allow more flexible and expedited pathways, particularly for low-risk AI applications
    - Modify requirements in 21 CFR Part 807 (510(k) submissions) to create tiered regulatory framework differentiating AI tools by risk levels
  - Issue clear guidelines specifically tailored to AI technologies
    - Outline necessary steps for compliance including data requirements and validation processes
    - Leverage Quality System Regulation in 21 CFR Part 820, adapting it for AI tool development and deployment
  - Promote interoperability standards for AI integration with EHR systems
    - Prioritize development and enforcement of standards drawing on 45 CFR Part 170 (EHR certification criteria)
    - Expand criteria to include AI interoperability standards for seamless data exchange
  - Accelerate shift toward value-based reimbursement models
    - Incentivize high-quality outcomes rather than volume of services
    - Revisit regulations in 42 CFR Part 400 (Medicare program) to align payment methodologies with performance-based metrics related to AI adoption
  - Create incentives for clinical trials evaluating AI tool effectiveness
    - Adapt IND application process in 21 CFR Part 312 to accommodate AI technologies in clinical trials
  - Encourage public-private partnerships for collaborative R&D
    - Leverage regulations in 42 CFR Parts 400-499 (Medicare and Medicaid programs) to facilitate collaboration between government, academic institutions, and private sector
  - Invest in education and training programs for healthcare professionals
    - Expand conditions for participation in 42 CFR Part 486 (health professional education and training) to include AI competency training

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Liability concerns
    - As AI systems become increasingly autonomous in making clinical recommendations, question of liability for errors or adverse outcomes becomes complex
    - Unclear whether liability lies with healthcare provider, AI developer, or institution utilizing technology
    - Ambiguity creates hesitation among providers to adopt AI solutions
  - Indemnification issues
    - Lack of clear guidelines regarding indemnification agreements between developers and healthcare providers complicates adoption
    - Providers reluctant to utilize AI tools without assurances they won't be held financially responsible for AI-driven decision consequences
    - Establishing frameworks delineating responsibilities and liabilities could alleviate concerns
  - Privacy and security concerns
    - AI systems involve handling sensitive patient data, raising questions about data protection and HIPAA compliance
    - AI algorithms require vast amounts of data for training and improvement
    - Ensuring patient information protection while allowing effective data sharing poses significant challenge
    - Potential for data breaches or misuse can erode trust among providers and patients
  - HHS role recommendations
    - Provide clear definitions of accountability and establish protocols for indemnification
    - Prioritize creation of data privacy and security standards tailored specifically for AI applications in healthcare
    - Facilitate development of best practices for data governance

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Pre-deployment evaluation methods
    - Simulation testing validates AI algorithms against historical clinical data
    - Allows developers to assess AI's predictive accuracy and performance in controlled environment
    - Key metrics: sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value
    - Robustness testing evaluates how AI tools perform under various scenarios including different patient populations and clinical conditions
  - Post-deployment evaluation methods
    - Continuous monitoring of AI performance in real-time clinical settings
    - Feedback loops allow healthcare providers to report outcomes and experiences
    - Metrics to assess: clinical outcomes, user satisfaction, workflow integration
    - Human-centered evaluation methods engage end-users to provide insights into usability, acceptance, and areas for improvement
  - HHS support mechanisms
    - Provide resources and frameworks for AI evaluation to ensure tools meet high standards
    - Establish grants for funding research and development initiatives focused on AI evaluation methodologies
    - Facilitate cooperative agreements with private sector partners to develop standardized evaluation frameworks
    - Create collaborative environment promoting sharing of best practices and lessons learned

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
  - Establish clear accreditation standards specifically designed for AI technologies in healthcare
    - Develop comprehensive framework ensuring technologies meet high standards of safety, efficacy, and reliability
    - Enhance public trust and facilitate adoption by healthcare providers
  - Create streamlined certification process for AI applications demonstrating compliance
    - Certification serves as important signal to providers, payers, and patients about quality and reliability
  - Support development of rigorous testing protocols
    - Foster collaborations between private sector companies and academic institutions
    - Validate effectiveness and safety of AI applications in real-world clinical environments
  - Offer training and certification programs for responsible AI use in clinical care
    - Ensure providers are equipped with necessary skills and knowledge to integrate AI tools
    - Promote effective use and foster culture of innovation within healthcare organizations
  - Provide funding opportunities
    - Encourage development of innovative AI solutions and establishment of testing and certification processes
    - Allocate resources to support research and development initiatives

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or exceeded expectations and where they've fallen short
  - Successes
    - AI-driven diagnostic tools in radiology demonstrate remarkable accuracy in interpreting medical images (X-rays, MRIs, CT scans)
      - Analyze vast amounts of imaging data rapidly
      - Identify patterns that may be missed by human eye
      - Improved early detection of conditions such as cancer
      - Streamlined workflows, reducing time radiologists spend on image interpretation
    - AI tools in predictive analytics effective in identifying high-risk patients for conditions like heart disease or diabetes
      - Analyze EHRs and other patient data to provide actionable insights
      - Enable early interventions preventing costly hospitalizations
      - Contribute to significant cost savings for healthcare systems
  - Shortcomings
    - AI algorithms have demonstrated biases due to training data
    - Results in disparities in care for certain patient populations
    - Highlights importance of developing AI with diverse datasets and thorough validation in real-world settings
  - Promising novel AI tools
    - Natural language processing (NLP) technologies could revolutionize clinical documentation and data entry
      - Automatically extract relevant information from unstructured clinical notes
      - Streamline administrative processes and enhance clinical workflow efficiency
    - AI-powered telemedicine platforms could expand access to healthcare in underserved areas
      - Utilize AI to triage patients and facilitate remote consultations
      - Reduce barriers to care and ensure timely medical attention without in-person visits

- Question 7: Influential roles and administrative hurdles in AI adoption
  - Key decision makers
    - Chief Medical Officers (CMOs) evaluate efficacy and applicability of AI tools
      - Assess clinical benefits and alignment with strategic goals
      - Advocate for integration into practice
    - Chief Information Officers (CIOs) and Chief Technology Officers (CTOs) oversee technology integration
      - Expertise in IT and data management essential for ensuring AI compatibility with EHR systems and clinical workflows
    - Governance bodies (clinical committees, quality assurance boards) evaluate risks and benefits
      - Establish guidelines and protocols ensuring patient safety and clinical standards
      - Their endorsement significantly influences decision-making
  - Primary administrative hurdles
    - Lack of clear regulatory frameworks and guidelines specifically tailored to AI technologies
      - Creates hesitation among decision makers and delays approval and implementation
    - Difficulty understanding legal and ethical implications, particularly concerning patient privacy and data security
    - Established processes and clinical workflows may not readily accommodate new technologies
    - Resistance to change among staff
    - Limitations in accessing high-quality, diverse datasets
    - Reimbursement challenges
      - Current payment models may not adequately recognize or reward value of AI-driven interventions
      - Difficult for organizations to justify investment

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - EHR system integration
    - Many healthcare organizations use different EHR platforms operating in silos
    - Standardized data formats and protocols would ensure patient information is accessible and actionable across platforms
    - Allows AI algorithms to analyze comprehensive datasets encompassing diverse patient populations
  - Standardization of clinical data types
    - Adopting consistent terminologies and coding systems (e.g., FHIR framework) facilitates smoother data sharing and integration
    - Simplifies collecting and analyzing data
    - Enhances AI tools' ability to interpret clinical information accurately
    - Researchers can more easily aggregate data from various studies and clinical trials
  - Benchmarking tools
    - Provide standardized metrics for evaluating AI algorithm performance
    - Enable healthcare organizations to assess effectiveness of different AI solutions in real-world settings
    - Facilitate comparisons across implementations to identify best practices
  - Research collaboration
    - Enhanced interoperability enables data sharing among academic institutions, industry partners, and healthcare providers
    - Collaborative research efforts can lead to groundbreaking discoveries and advancements

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns regarding AI
  - Challenges patients and caregivers wish to see addressed
    - Personalization of care
      - Patients seek AI's ability to tailor treatment plans to unique health needs including genetic factors and lifestyle choices
      - AI can analyze large datasets to provide insights promoting personalized medicine
    - Informed decision-making
      - Caregivers desire AI tools assisting in making informed decisions about treatment options
    - Access to care
      - AI can help bridge gaps in care, especially in underserved areas
      - Remote monitoring and telehealth solutions enhance access to healthcare services
  - Concerns
    - Privacy of personal health data and potential for misuse
      - AI systems require vast amounts of data including sensitive PHI
      - Risk of data breaches is significant concern
      - Healthcare organizations are prime targets for cyberattacks
    - Algorithmic bias
      - Patients worry AI algorithms may perpetuate existing biases leading to inequitable treatment outcomes
      - Biased algorithms can lead to misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment plans, or inadequate access for marginalized populations
      - AI models for predicting patient deterioration may not accurately reflect risk profiles of certain racial groups
      - Lack of trust can lead to lower engagement with healthcare services and poorer outcomes

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - Predictive analytics algorithms
    - Harness vast datasets from EHRs and other sources
    - Create AI systems identifying patients at high risk for developing certain conditions
    - Enable preventive measures and tailored interventions
  - Integration of AI with telehealth technologies
    - COVID-19 pandemic underscored value of telehealth for accessible care
    - Research into AI applications enhancing telehealth interactions (virtual health assistants, AI-driven triage tools)
    - Improve patient engagement and satisfaction
  - AI applications addressing health disparities and promoting health equity
    - Develop algorithms accounting for social determinants of health and contextual factors
    - Create AI tools better serving diverse populations
  - Data privacy and security in context of AI
    - Investigate innovative methods for safeguarding data while allowing effective AI training and operation
    - Help mitigate privacy concerns

- Question 10a: Published findings about impact of adopted AI tools
  - Medical imaging
    - AI tools assist radiologists in interpreting images more accurately and efficiently
    - AI algorithms can match or exceed human performance in detecting conditions (breast cancer in mammograms, lung cancer in chest X-rays, MRI abnormalities)
    - Meta-analysis in Nature found AI systems demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity in identifying cancerous lesions
  - Predictive analytics
    - AI tools analyze EHRs to identify patients at risk for adverse events (hospital readmissions, severe complications)
    - JAMA Internal Medicine study highlighted AI model successfully predicting hospital readmissions for heart failure patients with high accuracy
  - Patient engagement
    - AI-driven chatbots and virtual health assistants provide personalized health information, medication reminders, chronic disease management support
    - Journal of Medical Internet Research study found patients interacting with AI-powered health assistants reported higher engagement levels
  - Operational efficiencies
    - AI improves scheduling, resource allocation, and patient flow management
    - Health Affairs analysis revealed AI algorithms could predict patient demand for services, facilitating better staff planning and reducing wait times

- Question 10b: How literature approaches costs, benefits, and transfers of AI in clinical care
  - Costs
    - Significant upfront investments in software, hardware, and training
    - High costs for acquiring AI tools, integrating into existing systems, and maintaining technologies
    - Ongoing operational expenses including data management, system updates, and personnel training
  - Benefits
    - Improved patient outcomes through more accurate diagnostics, personalized treatment plans, and predictive analytics for early interventions
    - Studies show AI algorithms can outperform human clinicians in certain diagnostic tasks
    - Streamlined clinical workflows, reduced administrative burdens, minimized errors leading to cost savings
    - Automation of routine tasks allows healthcare professionals to focus on complex clinical decisions
    - Evidence-based insights and recommendations improve quality of decision-making
  - Transfers and equity concerns
    - Deployment of AI technologies raises questions about equity and access to care
    - If AI solutions not designed with inclusivity in mind, marginalized populations may face disparities in access to advanced healthcare technologies
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Innovation institute urges HHS to streamline AI regulations, create tiered approval pathways, and address liability uncertainty to unlock AI's potential for improving clinical care while ensuring equitable access.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** New Jersey Innovation Institute
- **Type:** Academic/Research
- **Role/Expertise:** Innovation institute focused on healthcare technology advancement
- **Geographic Scope:** State (New Jersey), with national policy implications
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly involved in developing and promoting AI innovations for healthcare; affected by regulatory barriers that slow innovation and deployment
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe existing federal regulations act as both facilitators and barriers to AI adoption in clinical settings. While regulations are designed to ensure patient safety and protect sensitive health information, they must adapt to accept and support the integration of AI technologies into healthcare. By recognizing, understanding, and addressing these barriers, stakeholders can facilitate AI innovation and adoption, improving overall healthcare outcomes.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Revise premarket approval process for AI tools
  - Create tiered regulatory framework differentiating AI tools by risk levels
  - Modify 21 CFR Part 807 (510(k) submissions) to allow flexible, expedited pathways for low-risk AI applications
- Issue clear guidelines specifically tailored to AI technologies
  - Adapt Quality System Regulation (21 CFR Part 820) to include specific guidelines for AI tool development and deployment
  - Outline necessary steps for compliance including data requirements and validation processes
- Promote interoperability standards for AI integration with EHR systems
  - Expand 45 CFR Part 170 (EHR certification criteria) to include AI interoperability standards
- Accelerate shift toward value-based reimbursement models
  - Revisit 42 CFR Part 400 (Medicare program) to align payment methodologies with performance-based metrics related to AI adoption
- Create incentives for clinical trials evaluating AI tool effectiveness
  - Adapt IND application process (21 CFR Part 312) to accommodate AI technologies
- Encourage public-private partnerships for collaborative R&D
  - Leverage 42 CFR Parts 400-499 to facilitate collaboration between government, academic institutions, and private sector
- Expand AI competency training for healthcare professionals
  - Modify 42 CFR Part 486 (health professional education and training) to include AI training requirements
- Establish clear accreditation standards and streamlined certification processes for AI technologies
- Provide clear definitions of accountability and establish protocols for indemnification
- Prioritize creation of data privacy and security standards tailored specifically for AI applications in healthcare
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Regulatory uncertainty creates hesitation among developers and investors
  - Absence of clear, consistent guidelines specifically tailored to AI technologies
  - Slows pace of innovation and deployment
- Complex, lengthy, and costly approval processes
  - Deters startups and smaller companies from entering market
  - Reduces diversity of innovative solutions available
- HIPAA compliance complicates essential data-sharing for AI training
  - Patient data privacy requirements make it challenging to collect extensive datasets
- Liability uncertainty for AI-driven clinical decisions
  - Unclear whether liability lies with provider, developer, or institution
  - Creates hesitation among providers to adopt AI solutions
- Lack of clear indemnification guidelines between developers and providers
- Current reimbursement models favor established procedures over innovative AI solutions
  - Slow transition to value-based care limits financial incentives
- Lack of interoperability between AI tools and EHR systems
  - Many healthcare organizations use different EHR platforms operating in silos
- Cultural resistance to change within healthcare organizations
  - Concerns about AI reliability, job displacement, and training needs
- Lack of standardization in metrics and evaluation frameworks
- AI algorithms may perpetuate existing biases leading to inequitable treatment outcomes
  - Biased algorithms can lead to misdiagnosis or inadequate access for marginalized populations
- Privacy and security risks with sensitive patient data
  - Healthcare organizations are prime targets for cyberattacks
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies specific CFR citations for regulatory modifications across multiple domains (premarket approval, quality systems, EHR certification, Medicare, clinical trials, professional education)
- Highlights that AI-driven diagnostic tools in radiology can identify patterns missed by the human eye, leading to earlier cancer detection
- Notes that AI predictive analytics have successfully identified high-risk patients for heart disease and diabetes, enabling early interventions that prevent costly hospitalizations
- Points out that AI algorithms have demonstrated biases due to training data, resulting in care disparities for certain patient populations
- Suggests NLP technologies could revolutionize clinical documentation by automatically extracting information from unstructured clinical notes
- Identifies CMOs, CIOs, CTOs, and governance bodies as key decision-makers whose endorsement significantly influences AI adoption
- Emphasizes that FHIR framework adoption could facilitate smoother data sharing and enable researchers to aggregate data from various studies more easily
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "If providers fear being held accountable for decisions influenced by AI they may be reluctant to implement AI tools in their practice."
- "Without the necessary support for integration, the potential benefits of AI in improving clinical efficiency and patient care may be lost."
- "If AI solutions are not designed with inclusivity in mind, marginalized populations may face disparities in access to advanced healthcare technologies."
