structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and background
  - Epic's software has included various forms of AI for decades
  - Currently have over 175 use cases of generative AI released or in active development
  - Through customer work, developed deep understanding of how to speed AI adoption in clinical settings
  - Epic offers Jump Start Cloud Infrastructure program
    - Resulted in customers increasing AI use 2.4 times faster
    - Works by expanding use of existing features and adopting new ones

- Getting to "On" - why healthcare leaders hesitate
  - Generative AI differs from prior healthcare innovations in ways that make clinical leadership reluctant to adopt
  - Because generative AI is probabilistic, performance must be validated not only before deployment but continuously over time
    - Requires skills and resources that may be scarce in healthcare settings
  - Costs driven by compute, storage, and model usage are highly variable
    - Difficult for organizations to predict, budget, or govern using traditional software pricing models
  - AI delivers meaningful benefits
    - Improved clinician productivity
    - Reduced documentation burden
    - Lower burnout
    - More timely clinical decision-making
    - Fewer missed orders
    - Improved throughput
    - Better patient engagement
  - Over time, these gains translate into
    - Reduced staffing pressure and overtime costs
    - Higher-quality documentation
    - Improved access and satisfaction
    - Better clinical outcomes through earlier interventions
  - Realizing benefits requires healthcare leaders to move past understandable initial hesitation and turn AI features on
  - Concrete steps the Department can take now to better align incentives and accelerate adoption

- Documenting ROI
  - Any responsible healthcare administrator will ask whether AI represents a sound investment before deploying
  - Healthcare organizations operate on thinner margins than ever
    - For many, simply not an option to invest in technologies that won't pay off financially
  - Many applications of generative AI in healthcare do present sufficient ROI
  - The Department could help document ROI data in a way providers can rely and act on
  - Example: AI scribe technology
    - AI records and transcribes clinical interactions, then drafts corresponding notes
    - Studies show it can reduce time clinicians spend working after hours
    - Less "pajama time" doesn't by itself offset the cost of the technology
    - True ROI appears downstream: happier clinicians less likely to burn out or leave
      - Reduces recruitment and onboarding costs
  - Recommendation: Department should assemble a repository of AI use cases with demonstrated ROI
    - Conduct independent research to validate vendor claims
    - Highlight metrics relevant to healthcare administrators' bottom-line decisions
    - Should be vendor-agnostic, focusing on use cases rather than specific implementations
      - Avoids advantaging vendors that get their claims published
    - Would serve as trusted reference for organizations evaluating AI investments

- Bridging the Adoption-to-ROI Gap
  - Even with proven ROI, initial adoption may be financially challenging
  - Benefits of AI rarely immediate, often require workflow changes, training, and iteration
  - Early adoption phases marked by
    - Uneven usage
    - Ongoing configuration
    - Infrastructure costs that scale before productivity gains materialize
  - Organizations may incur real costs before seeing offsetting savings
    - Creates financial mismatch that can slow or stall adoption
  - Fee-for-service reimbursement for AI use could quickly become costly
    - For AI with proven ROI, unnecessary
    - Organizations primarily need support through initial scaling period
    - Once broad adoption achieved, AI that delivers value will pay for itself
  - Epic's Jump Start Cloud Infrastructure Package success story
    - Created to simplify and support early adoption of generative AI
    - Covers cloud infrastructure costs for roughly 50 AI features with flat monthly fee based on organization size
    - Encourages responsible, broad adoption rather than narrow pilots driven by short-term cost avoidance
  - Jump Start results
    - Customers demonstrated median month-over-month AI resource utilization growth of 45%
      - Compared to 18% for non-Jump Start customers
    - Adopt more new AI features: 5.5 on average vs. 4.6 over same period
    - Use 17 times more AI resources than non-Jump Start customers of comparable size
    - Differences not explained by organization size or early-adopter effects
      - Jump Start customers are slightly smaller on average
    - Succeeds because it aligns incentives to encourage rapid scaling
    - Once customers reach broad adoption, they see the value and continue using it
  - Recommendation: AI adoption grants covering infrastructure costs for fixed period of time rather than fixed amount of money
    - Traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding
    - Time-based grants encourage organizations to scale quickly, assess value, determine which use cases merit continued investment
    - After grant period ends, organizations can discontinue low-value AI while sustaining beneficial AI
      - Without ongoing government spending
    - Could be structured as true grants or fixed-price arrangements similar to Jump Start
    - Pricing should scale with organization size to avoid disadvantaging smaller or lower-resourced providers
      - Could even be structured disproportionately to advantage smaller organizations
    - Would help organizations escape the "pilot trap" and reach sustainable AI adoption

- Making Local Validation Possible
  - AI adopters rightly have safety concerns
  - Many healthcare AI use cases fall outside FDA oversight
  - Even regulated products poorly served by traditional evaluation methods
  - For probabilistic AI, pre-deployment testing on generalized datasets has limited predictive value for real-world performance
  - Better method is "local validation"
    - Healthcare facility, independent of developer, evaluates how AI performs on its own data in its own workflow
    - Can be done prior to deployment using local but retrospective data
    - Can be done continually post-deployment ("ongoing monitoring") to ensure performance doesn't "drift"
    - AI features can be deemed safe regardless of patient acuity mix at particular organization
  - Local validation matters because AI performance sensitive to workflow integration
    - Example: predictive modeling for imminent medical deterioration
      - Output is probability, not binary
      - Deployer must decide what probability threshold triggers alert for care team
      - This choice significantly impacts model "performance"
      - Low threshold: more false positives
      - Higher threshold: more false negatives
      - Organizations may have good reason to change settings
        - Organizations with dedicated remote monitoring teams may prefer false positives
        - Those relying only on frontline nurses managing large caseloads may not tolerate them
  - Challenge for post-deployment validation
    - Small or rural healthcare organizations often can't afford necessary data science resources and expertise
    - AI performance varies organization to organization, so post-deployment validation can't be outsourced in practice
    - Without appropriate support for local validation, AI could widen rather than narrow the digital divide in healthcare
  - AI developers can help
    - In 2024, Epic released open-source AI Trust and Assurance Suite
      - Automates most resource-intensive aspects of post-deployment monitoring
      - Real-time evaluations use deployer's local data and workflows
      - Can monitor both Epic and third-party AI features
      - Uses deployer's choice of evaluation methodologies
  - Even with access to the Suite, many lower-resourced organizations report staffing and time constraints remain barriers
  - Recommendation: Targeted grant program to support local validation for small and rural providers

- Providing Guidance for AI Validation
  - Even with tools and resources, healthcare organizations may not know how to use them effectively
  - Little consensus today on how to evaluate traditional predictive models, even less for generative AI
  - Gold-standard guidance like NIST AI Risk Management Framework asserts importance of ongoing monitoring but doesn't specify how
  - Vendors providing evaluation services may use proprietary metrics or allow user to select among methodologies
    - Both limit comparability
  - Recommendation: Regulators should define standard metrics and validation processes for AI performance
    - Need not be mandatory; guidance alone would increase confidence and lower barriers to adoption
    - Clear, shared methodologies would allow organizations to focus on performing validation rather than designing it
    - Especially helpful if incorporated into existing tools
  - Validation guidance should be specific to both use case and deployment context, not one-size-fits-all
    - Risks, benefits, and appropriate metrics differ substantially for
      - Documentation assistance
      - Clinical decision support
      - Predictive modeling
      - Patient engagement tools
    - Tailored guidance for common classes of AI applications would allow organizations to focus on meaningful evaluation
      - Rather than reinventing validation frameworks from scratch
  - Clear federal guidance would accelerate adoption by lowering perceived risk of deployment
    - Many healthcare leaders delay or limit AI use not because they believe it's unsafe
    - They lack confidence they're evaluating it correctly
    - Clarifying expectations and establishing shared norms would help organizations move from cautious experimentation to responsible scale

- Building AI Fluency
  - Technology adoption depends on human understanding and trust, not just technical readiness
  - Considerable attention paid to training clinicians on using AI tools effectively, verifying outputs, avoiding automation bias
  - More foundational form of AI fluency also needed to overcome adoption barriers
    - Particularly among non-technical stakeholders: clinical leadership, frontline staff, labor representatives
  - In healthcare, AI unlikely to displace workers
    - Persistent labor shortages across clinical roles
    - Rising demand driven by aging population
    - AI increasingly necessary to sustain access to care
  - Most healthcare AI use cases designed to
    - Reduce administrative burden
    - Support clinical judgment
    - Allow clinicians to spend more time with patients
  - Fears that AI will eliminate jobs remain widespread
    - Increasingly surface in labor negotiations
  - Concerns often stem from lack of shared understanding about what healthcare AI does and doesn't do
  - Without credible, accessible education, AI adoption risks being perceived as cost-cutting rather than capacity-multiplying
    - Can slow implementation, limit usage, or prompt organizations to constrain AI to narrow pilots
  - Recommendation: Federal government should support neutral, trusted education on AI's role in healthcare
    - Develop materials tailored to different stakeholder groups
    - Support peer-to-peer learning networks among healthcare organizations
    - Incorporate AI fluency into existing workforce development and quality improvement programs
    - Would complement vendor-led training by focusing on system-level understanding rather than tool-specific instruction
  - Most effective way to build AI fluency is through experience
    - When clinicians and staff see AI reduce documentation burden, improve workflow efficiency, and support clinical judgment, skepticism gives way to trust
  - Policies enabling earlier, broader, more responsible AI adoption will do more to build AI fluency than education alone
    - By helping organizations move past prolonged pilots into sustained use, Department can ensure AI's benefits are visible, tangible, and widely shared

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Major EHR vendor with 175+ generative AI use cases urges HHS to create ROI repositories, time-based adoption grants, local validation support for small providers, and standardized evaluation guidance to help healthcare organizations escape the "pilot trap" and scale AI responsibly.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Epic Systems
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Electronic health record software developer with decades of AI integration experience; currently has 175+ generative AI use cases released or in development
- **Geographic Scope:** National/International (headquartered in Verona, WI)
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct interest as a major healthcare AI vendor seeking to accelerate customer adoption of AI features; operates Jump Start Cloud Infrastructure program for AI adoption

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We believe generative AI delivers meaningful benefits to healthcare—improved productivity, reduced burnout, better clinical outcomes—but adoption is stalled by uncertainty about ROI, upfront costs before benefits materialize, and lack of validation guidance. The Department can accelerate responsible AI adoption by aligning incentives through time-based grants, creating trusted ROI documentation, supporting local validation for smaller providers, and establishing clear evaluation standards.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Create a vendor-agnostic repository of AI use cases with independently validated ROI data
  - Would validate vendor claims through independent research
  - Highlight metrics relevant to healthcare administrators' bottom-line decisions
  - Serve as trusted reference for organizations evaluating AI investments

- Establish time-based AI adoption grants covering infrastructure costs for a fixed period (not fixed dollar amount)
  - Traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding
  - Time-based grants encourage rapid scaling and value assessment
  - Pricing should scale with organization size, potentially favoring smaller organizations
  - Would help organizations escape the "pilot trap"

- Create targeted grant program to support local validation for small and rural providers
  - These organizations lack data science resources for effective post-deployment monitoring
  - Without support, AI could widen rather than narrow the digital divide

- Develop standard metrics and validation processes for AI performance
  - Guidance need not be mandatory but would increase confidence
  - Should be specific to use case and deployment context, not one-size-fits-all
  - Tailored guidance needed for documentation assistance, clinical decision support, predictive modeling, and patient engagement tools

- Support neutral, trusted education on AI's role in healthcare
  - Develop materials tailored to different stakeholder groups
  - Support peer-to-peer learning networks among healthcare organizations
  - Incorporate AI fluency into existing workforce development programs

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Healthcare leaders hesitate to adopt AI due to unique characteristics of generative AI
  - Probabilistic nature requires continuous validation, not just pre-deployment testing
  - Costs are highly variable and difficult to predict or budget
  - Skills and resources for validation may be scarce in healthcare settings

- Financial mismatch between adoption costs and ROI timing
  - Benefits rarely immediate, require workflow changes and training
  - Organizations incur real costs before seeing offsetting savings
  - Creates barrier that can slow or stall adoption

- Local validation challenges for smaller organizations
  - AI performance varies by organization and workflow context
  - Post-deployment validation cannot be outsourced in practice
  - Small and rural organizations can't afford necessary data science expertise
  - Even with free tools, staffing and time constraints remain barriers

- Lack of consensus on AI evaluation methodologies
  - Little agreement on how to evaluate traditional predictive models, even less for generative AI
  - NIST framework asserts importance of monitoring but doesn't specify how
  - Proprietary metrics and user-selected methodologies limit comparability
  - Many leaders delay AI use because they lack confidence they're evaluating it correctly

- Workforce concerns creating adoption friction
  - Fears that AI will eliminate jobs remain widespread despite labor shortages
  - Concerns increasingly surface in labor negotiations
  - Without credible education, AI perceived as cost-cutting rather than capacity-multiplying

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Jump Start program data reveals dramatic adoption differences based on incentive structure
  - Jump Start customers show 45% month-over-month AI utilization growth vs. 18% for non-participants
  - Use 17 times more AI resources than comparable non-Jump Start customers
  - Adopt 5.5 new AI features on average vs. 4.6 for others
  - Differences not explained by organization size—Jump Start customers are actually slightly smaller on average

- The "pajama time" paradox of AI scribe ROI
  - Reducing after-hours documentation time doesn't directly offset technology costs
  - True ROI appears downstream through reduced clinician burnout and turnover
  - Highlights gap between immediate metrics and actual financial value

- Alert threshold example illustrates why local validation matters
  - Same deterioration prediction model performs differently based on threshold settings
  - Organizations with dedicated remote monitoring teams may prefer false positives
  - Frontline nurses managing large caseloads cannot tolerate false positives
  - "Performance" is inseparable from deployment context

- Epic released open-source AI Trust and Assurance Suite in 2024
  - Automates resource-intensive aspects of post-deployment monitoring
  - Works with both Epic and third-party AI features
  - Even with free access, lower-resourced organizations report staffing and time constraints remain barriers

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Less 'pajama time' for providers, however, does not by itself offset the cost of the technology. The true ROI of AI scribes appears downstream: happier clinicians are less likely to burn out or leave, reducing recruitment and onboarding costs."

- "Traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding; time-based grants would encourage organizations to scale quickly, assess value, and determine which use cases merit continued investment."

- "Without appropriate support for 'local validation' activities, AI could widen, rather than narrow, the digital divide in healthcare."

- "Many healthcare leaders delay or limit AI use not because they believe AI is unsafe, but because they lack confidence that they are evaluating it correctly."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Submitter Information
  - Mosaic Life Tech is a healthcare AI governance advisory firm
  - Principals have over 50 years of combined experience in health information technology
    - Leadership roles in federal health IT programs (ONC, CDC, ASTP)
    - Health information exchange operations serving over four million patients
    - Clinical informatics expertise
  - We advise healthcare executives on establishing governance structures for AI-influenced clinical decisions
  - Comments based on direct participation in federal health IT policy development, industry governance forums, and review of recent survey data on AI adoption and oversight

- Response to Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical device AI
  - The most significant governance challenge is not legal ambiguity at the federal level—it is organizational ambiguity within health systems themselves
  - Over 70% of acute care hospitals now have AI tools influencing clinical decisions
    - EHR-embedded algorithms, operational scheduling tools, documentation assistants
    - Adoption drops sharply among independent, rural, and critical access hospitals
    - Many tools fall outside FDA device regulation
  - The question of "what federal rules apply" often has a clear answer
  - The question of "who inside our organization is accountable for this AI-influenced decision" frequently does not
  - Survey data confirms pervasive governance gap
    - CHIME/Censinet AI Adoption Survey (December 2025): only 59% of healthcare organizations require formal approval before AI implementation
      - Over 40% lack even a basic pre-implementation gate
    - Just 10% use automated monitoring to detect AI capabilities within existing software
    - 51% rely on informal, ad hoc discovery
    - ECRI ranked "Insufficient Governance of AI in Healthcare" as #1 health technology hazard for 2025
      - Warning that failure to develop systemwide governance increases patient safety and liability risk
  - Federal healthcare settings show same pattern
    - January 2026: VA Office of Inspector General reported VHA deployed generative AI tools without coordination with National Center for Patient Safety
      - Despite VHA Directive 1050.01 requiring such coordination
      - OIG found no formal process to track AI-related safety issues
    - Illustrates how even large, sophisticated healthcare organizations can deploy AI without governance structures needed to manage risk
  - The governance gap is widening
    - HTI-5's proposed removal of AI model card transparency requirements at § 170.315(b)(11) and significant reduction in DSI certification criteria
    - Effectively shifts responsibility for evaluating AI safety, bias, and fitness-for-purpose from federal certification to deploying healthcare organizations and the market
    - This shift is navigable for well-resourced health systems with dedicated informatics leadership
    - For small and mid-market organizations, community hospitals, and safety-net providers, the shift creates a governance burden they lack capacity to meet
  - Data confirms a "digital divide" in AI governance capacity
    - ASTP/ONC and AHA: 86% of system-affiliated hospitals use predictive AI versus just 37% of independent facilities
    - HFMA survey: among 18% of health systems with mature AI governance, three-quarters have over $1 billion in net patient revenue
    - Dr. Brian Anderson (CEO, Coalition for Health AI): the digital divide is already being reinforced in the AI space, with rural communities lacking IT staff to deploy and configure AI tools
  - Vendor contracts address liability from vendor's perspective but cannot resolve internal accountability
    - Organizations haven't decided who is responsible for validating AI tools against their patient population, monitoring for drift, or escalating concerns
    - 2025 BMC Health Services Research study identified a "responsibility vacuum" where organizational structures obfuscate who should have accountability for AI monitoring
    - Only 22% of hospital leaders report high confidence they could produce a complete AI audit trail within 30 days if requested by a regulator
  - How HHS can help without adding regulatory burden
    - First: Support and amplify voluntary governance frameworks already emerging
      - Joint Commission and Coalition for Health AI published Guidance on the Responsible Use of AI in Healthcare (September 17, 2025)
      - Provides structure for organizational accountability across seven elements: governance structures, patient privacy, data security, quality monitoring, voluntary safety event reporting, risk and bias assessment, education and training
      - Because Joint Commission holds CMS deeming authority under 42 CFR Part 488, health systems treat this guidance as roadmap for likely future accreditation expectations
      - HHS recognition of such frameworks, without mandating them, would signal that establishing internal governance is expected and supported
    - Second: Consider technical assistance models for under-resourced organizations
      - Regional Extension Center program provides clear precedent
        - Assisted approximately 134,000 primary care providers
        - ONC evaluations found 68% of REC participants achieved Stage 1 Meaningful Use versus just 12% of non-participants
      - Similar model for AI governance could leverage existing infrastructure at HRSA (through FQHCs), SAMHSA, or other agencies
      - Would help smaller organizations develop oversight capabilities they currently lack
    - Third: Fund or disseminate research on effective governance structures
      - Health systems experimenting with AI governance committees, chief AI officer roles, cross-functional oversight bodies
      - KLAS Research noted "stronger organizational governance and ownership are needed to drive broader AI adoption"
      - When governance provides structured way to vet and integrate AI, adoption is more scalable and sustainable
      - Implementation science focused on AI governance through NIH or AHRQ would help field learn faster than trial and error
  - The novel issue is not that AI creates new legal exposure—it is that AI creates decisions crossing traditional departmental boundaries, and most organizations have not adapted governance structures to match
  - Federal clarity that organizational governance is expected, combined with support for building capacity, would do more to accelerate responsible adoption than additional regulation

- Response to Question 5: How HHS can support private sector activities
  - Private sector has already begun developing governance frameworks for AI in clinical care
  - HHS can accelerate adoption by supporting these efforts rather than duplicating them through regulation
  - Joint Commission/CHAI guidance (September 17, 2025)
    - Addresses governance structure, validation, monitoring, training, and board visibility through seven core elements
    - Joint Commission holds CMS deeming authority under 42 CFR Part 488 and accredits more than 80% of U.S. hospitals
    - Health systems paying attention even though it does not create new accreditation standards
    - Legal analysts note this follows Joint Commission's established pattern
      - National Patient Safety Goals began as recommendations before becoming accreditation requirements
      - Health equity guidance from 2023 became mandatory requirements effective January 2024
  - Professional societies building AI-specific infrastructure
    - American College of Radiology launched Assess-AI
      - World's first AI quality registry designed to monitor ongoing performance of imaging AI algorithms in real-world clinical settings
    - ACR's Recognized Center for Healthcare-AI (ARCH-AI) program
      - Recognition framework for facilities meeting governance, model selection, acceptance testing, and monitoring criteria
      - Working toward formal AI accreditation program, ACR Council approval anticipated Spring 2027
    - AMA's STEPS Forward toolkit provides practical governance guidance
      - Committee structure, decision rights, escalation pathways
    - These efforts represent the field building governance from within, informed by clinical realities federal regulation alone cannot capture
  - Concrete ways HHS can support this work
    - Publicly recognize voluntary frameworks as representing reasonable practice
      - Does not require endorsement or mandate
      - Statement that organizations following such guidance are taking appropriate steps would reduce uncertainty and encourage adoption
    - Support accreditation bodies in developing AI governance competencies within existing survey processes
      - Surveyors already assess governance, quality improvement, patient safety
      - Integrating AI oversight into existing frameworks rather than creating separate AI-specific requirements would reduce burden while improving accountability
    - Fund professional organizations in developing training and credentialing for AI governance roles
      - Health systems creating AI governance committees and oversight functions
      - People filling these roles often lack formal training in AI risk assessment or clinical validation methods
      - AMA's STEPS Forward toolkit provides starting point, but broader workforce development needed
    - Support industry-driven testing and validation infrastructure
      - Health systems struggle to validate vendor claims about AI performance on their specific patient populations
      - Shared testing resources, benchmark datasets, or cooperative validation arrangements could help smaller and mid-sized systems lacking internal capacity
  - Risk of heavy federal regulation: freezes current practice into compliance requirements before field has learned what actually works
  - Opportunity in supporting private sector frameworks: governance can evolve with technology while establishing reasonable expectations for oversight

- Response to Question 7: Influential roles and administrative hurdles
  - No single role consistently owns AI adoption decisions across health systems
  - This fragmentation is itself a primary hurdle
  - Industry leadership patterns reflect structural divide between technical authority and clinical oversight
    - CIOs control technology infrastructure, serve on 63% of AI governance committees, but typically not clinicians
    - CMIOs and CNIOs have clinical credibility but serve on only 45% of AI governance committees
      - AMDIS/WittKieffer surveys: only 19% of CMIOs have dedicated leadership budget
      - 40% operate under IT department's budget
    - Quality and patient safety leaders focus on outcomes but rarely consulted during AI acquisition
    - Risk management addresses liability but often learns about AI deployments after the fact
    - Boards ask questions about AI oversight but receive inconsistent answers about accountability
    - CHIME/Censinet survey: 33% of organizations cite "unclear ownership" between IT, clinical, and compliance teams as primary barrier to governance
  - The CFO is often the decisive voice
    - HFMA's 2025 survey of 233 health system leaders: CFOs and CIOs are leading decision-makers when weighing AI vendor offerings
    - Cost reduction cited as most frequent rationale for investment
    - Only 39% of CFOs believed AI investments would actually reduce overall costs, yet financial justification remains primary gate
    - HHS's own RFI identifies reimbursement and payment policy as key levers, recognizing AI adoption decisions frequently come down to financial justification
    - CFOs typically lack clinical or technical context to evaluate AI risk
    - Creates disconnect between person who approves investment and people who understand what is being approved
  - Organizations adopting AI most effectively have established clear governance ownership
    - Cross-functional AI governance committees with explicit decision rights
    - Designated AI oversight roles reporting to executive leadership
    - These structures do not slow adoption—they accelerate it, provided they include explicit decision rights and clear escalation pathways
    - Black Book Research: health systems with AI governance councils twice as likely to achieve ROI within 12 months
    - Programs with dashboards and clear ownership reach early ROI in 7.5 months versus 13.5 months without them
    - KLAS Research: when governance provides structured way to vet and integrate AI, adoption is more scalable and sustainable
  - Primary administrative hurdle is not technology cost or complexity
  - It is the absence of a defined process for answering basic governance questions
    - Who approves the use of this AI tool?
    - Who validates it against our patient population?
    - Who monitors it after deployment?
    - Who decides when to turn it off?
  - HHS could support adoption by funding research on effective governance structures and disseminating findings
    - Health systems solving these problems independently
    - Limited sharing of what works across organizations
    - Implementation science focused on AI governance would help field learn faster

- Closing
  - We appreciate the Department's focus on accelerating AI adoption while maintaining appropriate oversight
  - Across Questions 3, 5, and 7, evidence points consistently in one direction: governance is not a barrier to adoption but an enabler of it
  - Organizations with clear AI governance ownership achieve ROI faster and scale more effectively than those without
  - Most effective path forward supports organizational governance capacity rather than prescribing specific requirements
  - Health systems are ready to act
  - What they need from HHS is not more rules, but clear expectations and capacity support that help them govern AI responsibly

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Healthcare AI governance advisory firm with deep federal health IT experience argues that the real barrier to responsible AI adoption isn't regulatory ambiguity but organizational ambiguity—and urges HHS to support voluntary frameworks and capacity-building rather than adding new rules.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Mosaic Life Tech, LLC (Teresa Younkin, CEO; Jim Younkin, CTO)
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Healthcare AI governance advisory firm; principals have 50+ years combined experience in health IT including leadership at ONC, CDC, ASTP; health information exchange operations serving 4+ million patients; clinical informatics
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly advises healthcare executives on AI governance structures; business depends on health systems needing governance support; has participated in federal health IT policy development and industry governance forums

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We support HHS's goal of accelerating AI adoption in clinical care, but believe the most significant challenge isn't federal legal ambiguity—it's organizational ambiguity within health systems themselves. Governance is not a barrier to adoption but an enabler of it: organizations with clear AI governance ownership achieve ROI faster and scale more effectively. What health systems need from HHS is not more rules, but clear expectations and capacity support that help them govern AI responsibly.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Publicly recognize voluntary governance frameworks (like Joint Commission/CHAI guidance) as representing reasonable practice
  - Does not require endorsement or mandate
  - Would signal that establishing internal governance is expected and supported
  - Reduces uncertainty and encourages adoption

- Create technical assistance programs for under-resourced organizations to build AI governance capacity
  - Model on Regional Extension Center program (which helped 68% of participants achieve Meaningful Use vs. 12% of non-participants)
  - Could leverage existing infrastructure at HRSA (FQHCs), SAMHSA, or other agencies
  - Critical for small/mid-market organizations, community hospitals, and safety-net providers

- Support accreditation bodies in integrating AI governance into existing survey processes
  - Surveyors already assess governance, quality improvement, patient safety
  - Integrating AI oversight into existing frameworks reduces burden while improving accountability
  - Avoids creating separate AI-specific requirements

- Fund professional organizations to develop training and credentialing for AI governance roles
  - People filling governance roles often lack formal training in AI risk assessment or clinical validation
  - Broader workforce development needed beyond existing toolkits

- Support industry-driven testing and validation infrastructure
  - Shared testing resources, benchmark datasets, cooperative validation arrangements
  - Helps smaller systems that can't validate vendor claims on their own patient populations

- Fund and disseminate implementation science research on effective AI governance structures
  - Through NIH or AHRQ
  - Health systems are solving problems independently with limited sharing
  - Would help field learn faster than trial and error

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Pervasive organizational governance gap in healthcare AI
  - Over 40% of healthcare organizations lack even basic pre-implementation approval gates
  - Only 10% use automated monitoring to detect AI capabilities in existing software
  - 51% rely on informal, ad hoc discovery of AI tools
  - Only 22% of hospital leaders confident they could produce complete AI audit trail within 30 days

- HTI-5's proposed changes will widen the governance gap
  - Removal of AI model card transparency requirements shifts burden to deploying organizations
  - Well-resourced systems can handle this; small/mid-market organizations, community hospitals, and safety-net providers cannot

- "Digital divide" in AI governance capacity
  - 86% of system-affiliated hospitals use predictive AI vs. just 37% of independent facilities
  - Among 18% with mature AI governance, three-quarters have over $1 billion in net patient revenue
  - Rural communities lack IT staff to deploy and configure AI tools

- Fragmented decision-making authority creates "responsibility vacuum"
  - No single role consistently owns AI adoption decisions
  - 33% of organizations cite "unclear ownership" between IT, clinical, and compliance teams as primary barrier
  - CFOs often decisive but lack clinical/technical context to evaluate AI risk
  - CMIOs have clinical credibility but only 19% have dedicated leadership budget

- Even sophisticated federal healthcare settings struggle
  - VA deployed generative AI without coordinating with National Center for Patient Safety
  - No formal process to track AI-related safety issues despite directive requiring coordination

- Risk of heavy federal regulation
  - Could freeze current practice into compliance requirements before field learns what works

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Governance accelerates rather than slows AI adoption
  - Health systems with AI governance councils are twice as likely to achieve ROI within 12 months
  - Programs with dashboards and clear ownership reach early ROI in 7.5 months vs. 13.5 months without them

- Joint Commission guidance likely to become mandatory requirements
  - Follows established pattern: National Patient Safety Goals began as recommendations before becoming accreditation requirements
  - Health equity guidance from 2023 became mandatory effective January 2024
  - Health systems already treating current AI guidance as roadmap for future accreditation expectations

- The "responsibility vacuum" phenomenon
  - Vendor contracts address liability from vendor's perspective but cannot resolve internal accountability
  - Organizations haven't decided who validates AI against their patient population, monitors for drift, or escalates concerns
  - Basic questions remain unanswered: Who approves? Who validates? Who monitors? Who decides when to turn it off?

- Regional Extension Center model as proven precedent
  - Assisted 134,000 primary care providers
  - 68% of participants achieved Stage 1 Meaningful Use vs. just 12% of non-participants
  - Demonstrates technical assistance can dramatically improve adoption among under-resourced organizations

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The question of 'what federal rules apply' often has a clear answer. The question of 'who inside our organization is accountable for this AI-influenced decision' frequently does not."

- "The novel issue is not that AI creates new legal exposure. It is that AI creates decisions that cross traditional departmental boundaries, and most organizations have not adapted their governance structures to match."

- "What they need from HHS is not more rules, but clear expectations and capacity support that help them govern AI responsibly."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and organizational background
  - Kooth has pioneered digital mental health with the UK's National Health Service for 25 years
  - Services now available to over 20 million people, supporting hundreds of thousands of young users annually
  - Core partner in California's Children and Youth Behavioral Health Initiative (CYBHI)
  - Designed and launched Soluna: the nation's first statewide, free digital mental health service for 13-25 year olds
    - Co-designed with youth
    - Removes traditional barriers and expands access
    - Integrates with existing supports

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - Regulatory ambiguity around medical device classification creates unintended market consequences
    - Products with clinical evidence face significantly higher regulatory burden including administrative overhead, certification costs, and extended approval timelines
    - General purpose or wellness AI products face less scrutiny
    - Market skews toward tools optimized for user engagement rather than verifiable health outcomes
  - Fragmented expectations across federal agencies and state laws create contradictory requirements
  - Regulators and lawmakers have uneven understanding of "AI" as a category
    - Conflating LLM-based chatbots with classical statistical or machine learning algorithms
    - Leads to technology-blunt rules designed to curb chatbots but unnecessarily constraining lower-risk ML workflows
  - Lack of knowledge or training for front-line practitioners on how to effectively apply AI tools
  - Lack of trust from patients/end users
    - Driven by AI models lacking explainability or transparency
    - Concerns about bias, safety, and privacy

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Prioritize policies incentivizing AI to help clinicians do what only clinicians can do, rather than replacing them
  - Build payment structures that reimburse tools for:
    - Chart reviews
    - Documentation summaries
    - Initiating assessments
    - Clinical decision support functions
  - Goal: clinicians spend more time with patients; health systems supported in using AI to strengthen care rather than reduce it

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Growing inconsistency in how AI systems are classified as medical devices based on context rather than risk
    - Some states require general-purpose generative AI chatbots to implement automated risk/safety assessments
    - When similar risk-detection, triage, or safeguarding algorithms are embedded in purpose-built health care experiences, they may be deemed regulated medical devices
  - This creates regulatory arbitrage and unresolved liability questions
    - Developers incentivized to deploy general-purpose AI with minimal oversight
    - Avoiding more responsible, context-aware designs that trigger medical device regulation
  - Existing frameworks provide limited clarity on liability when AI is used in clinical practice
    - AI tools used beyond intended scope constitute de facto medical device use without regulatory oversight
  - HHS role recommendations:
    - Clarify accountability and shared responsibility across developers, deployers, and clinicians
    - Provide guidance on appropriate use, misuse, and "off-label" deployment of non-medical device AI
    - Promote risk-based, functionally consistent approaches supporting safer adoption and level playing field

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Approaches with greatest external validity combine:
    - Pre-deployment/pilot testing for accuracy, robustness, and failure modes
    - Post-deployment monitoring of real-world performance, bias, and unintended consequences
    - Design research on human-centered experience including patient/user experience, trust, and clinician workflow
    - Outcome-oriented metrics beyond technical performance (access, engagement, equity, clinical outcomes)
  - Current practice problem: developers commonly publish pre-deployment metrics on technical accuracy while glossing over post-deployment monitoring, human-centered research, and outcomes
  - HHS should support the latter evaluation methods through:
    - Grant funding for transparent reporting
    - Publication of shared, more complete evaluation approaches

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
  - Address regulatory asymmetry between AI designed for mental health support and "wellness" products
    - People turn to both types plus general purpose chatbots for mental health reasons
    - Need baseline alignment in safety, transparency, and post-deployment expectations
    - Avoid market incentives favoring less governed products over made-for-purpose technologies
  - Ensure evidence expectations align with real-world AI deployment
    - Accept proportionate real-world evidence
    - Reward AI systems that reach underserved communities, improve outcomes, enhance safety, and drive efficiencies
    - Move beyond products that only deliver outcomes in clinical trial settings
  - Clarify accountability across general purpose AI, wellness AI, clinical AI developers, and clinicians
    - Guidance on liability, appropriate use, and "off-label" usage would increase credentialing efforts
    - Users may not appreciate practical differences between AI product types
  - Invest in shared evaluation infrastructure and outcomes benchmarks
    - Support common evaluation frameworks and shared health outcome measures
    - Interoperability standards across anonymous longitudinal clinical data and population health data
    - Particularly important in behavioral and mental health
    - Could establish AI credentialing or registry program separate from FDA SaMD registration
    - Monitor real-world AI performance with transparent reporting on safety and outcomes over time

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met/exceeded expectations and where they've fallen short
  - AI has extraordinary potential to expand mental health care access
    - Current shortage: as many as 169 million people live in federally designated Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas
  - Human-in-the-loop AI augmented systems allow providers to scale impact while ensuring baseline standard of care through:
    - In-the-moment feedback for best-practice coaching that raises baseline quality
    - Care navigation optimization improving continuity and routing to right level of care
    - Risk and safeguarding systems detecting crisis signals
  - Continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale without AI

- Question 7: Roles and decision makers influencing AI adoption; administrative hurdles
  - Adoption influenced by:
    - Payers and commissioners
    - Provider organizations whose clinical governance bodies and procurement processes determine allowable AI use
    - Users, patients, and constituents who influence provider determinations
    - Individual clinicians exercising professional judgment in how they rely on or interpret AI outputs
  - Administrative hurdles stem from lack of understanding of "AI" technology
    - Conflating general purpose conversational AI with made-for-narrow-purpose, human-in-the-loop AI systems with strict guardrails
  - Opportunity for HHS to push AI educational campaign
    - Dissociate the two classes of tools
    - Instill public confidence in societal benefit of purpose-built, governed AI systems

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Most impactful where it enables linkage across clinical care, population health, and outcomes data
  - Particularly impactful where longitudinal clinical data could be anonymously linked with broader datasets
    - Population health and economic wellbeing trends
  - Machine learning could then support:
    - Modelling of need
    - Measurement of impact
    - Forecasting evolution of care demand and risk over time

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns regarding AI in clinical care
  - Youth expectations in digital mental health:
    - Personalized experience that adapts based on interaction
    - Health outcomes achieved through personalized and adaptive care pathways
  - Practitioner and clinician expectations:
    - AI to reduce or eliminate administrative friction
    - Eliminate repetitive, automatable non-clinical tasks
    - Optimize for providing human-to-human care
  - Central concern from both youth and practitioners: loss of human connection
  - Important that HHS promote AI use subject to preserving human connection
  - Human-led care should be a core tenet of our health care system

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - Research evaluating AI tools in real-world settings
  - Use large scale, longitudinal datasets capturing meaningful health outcomes for anonymized individuals
  - Synthetic data and LLM evaluations (one LLM assessing another) are insufficient as evidence of external validity, safety, and value
    - Useful only at early prototype stages
  - Adoption will accelerate only if research reflects:
    - How AI performance varies across populations, care settings, and contexts
    - Real-world effectiveness (not accuracy from simulated cases) as the evidentiary standard for deployment

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
UK-based digital mental health pioneer now operating in California urges HHS to fix regulatory asymmetries that inadvertently favor unregulated wellness chatbots over purpose-built clinical AI tools, while preserving human connection as the core of care.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Kooth (submitted by Dr. Patrick Johnston, Chief Operating Officer)
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Digital mental health provider with 25 years of experience partnering with UK's NHS; now operating Soluna, the nation's first statewide free digital mental health service for youth (ages 13-25) in California
- **Geographic Scope:** International (UK headquarters, US operations in California)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected by AI regulations as a developer and deployer of AI-augmented digital mental health services; faces regulatory burden that competitors offering unregulated "wellness" products avoid

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We believe HHS should fix the regulatory asymmetry that currently penalizes responsible, purpose-built clinical AI tools while allowing general-purpose chatbots and "wellness" apps to operate with minimal oversight despite being used for the same mental health purposes. AI should be used to help clinicians do what only clinicians can do—not replace them—and payment structures should support this vision. The central concern from both youth users and practitioners is loss of human connection, so human-led care must remain a core tenet of our health care system.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Create payment structures that reimburse AI tools for chart reviews, documentation summaries, assessments, and clinical decision support
  - Enables clinicians to spend more time with patients
  - Supports AI that strengthens care rather than reduces it

- Establish baseline safety, transparency, and post-deployment requirements across all AI used for mental health purposes
  - Apply to purpose-built clinical tools, "wellness" products, and general-purpose chatbots alike
  - Eliminate market incentives favoring less governed products

- Clarify accountability and liability across developers, deployers, and clinicians
  - Provide guidance on appropriate use, misuse, and "off-label" deployment
  - Address regulatory arbitrage where general-purpose AI avoids medical device classification

- Create an AI credentialing or registry program separate from FDA SaMD registration
  - Monitor real-world AI performance
  - Require transparent reporting on safety and outcomes over time

- Launch an AI educational campaign for the public and health care organizations
  - Distinguish general-purpose conversational AI from purpose-built, human-in-the-loop systems with clinical governance
  - Build public confidence in responsible AI tools

- Fund research evaluating AI in real-world settings with longitudinal datasets
  - Move beyond synthetic data and LLM-evaluating-LLM approaches
  - Treat real-world effectiveness as the evidentiary standard for deployment

- Support interoperability enabling linkage across clinical care, population health, and outcomes data
  - Enable anonymous longitudinal data linkage for modeling need and measuring impact
  - Particularly important in behavioral and mental health

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Regulatory asymmetry creates perverse market incentives
  - Products with clinical evidence face higher regulatory burden (administrative overhead, certification costs, extended timelines)
  - General-purpose and wellness AI face less scrutiny despite being used for same purposes
  - Market skews toward tools optimized for engagement rather than health outcomes

- Inconsistent medical device classification based on context rather than risk
  - States requiring safety assessments for general-purpose chatbots
  - Same capabilities in purpose-built health care settings trigger medical device regulation
  - Developers incentivized to deploy general-purpose AI with minimal oversight

- Technology-blunt regulations from uneven understanding of AI
  - Conflating LLM-based chatbots with classical ML algorithms
  - Rules designed to curb chatbots unnecessarily constrain lower-risk, proven technologies

- Lack of clarity on liability when AI is used in clinical practice
  - AI tools used beyond intended scope constitute de facto medical device use without oversight
  - Existing frameworks don't address shared responsibility

- Evaluation practices favor technical metrics over real-world outcomes
  - Developers publish pre-deployment accuracy while glossing over post-deployment monitoring
  - Human-centered research and outcomes measurement are neglected

- Administrative hurdles from lack of AI understanding
  - Organizations conflate general-purpose conversational AI with purpose-built systems
  - Impedes adoption of responsible, governed tools

- Risk of losing human connection in care
  - Central concern from both youth users and practitioners
  - AI should augment, not replace, human-led care

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Operates Soluna, the nation's first statewide free digital mental health service for 13-25 year olds in California, co-designed with youth
  - Part of California's Children and Youth Behavioral Health Initiative
  - Services available to over 20 million people globally

- Identified a specific regulatory arbitrage problem: when risk-detection and safeguarding algorithms are embedded in purpose-built health care experiences, they may trigger medical device regulation—but functionally identical capabilities in general-purpose chatbots face no such requirements

- Quantified the mental health access crisis: as many as 169 million Americans live in federally designated Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas

- Articulated three specific AI capabilities that are "impossible at scale without AI":
  - In-the-moment feedback for best-practice coaching
  - Care navigation optimization
  - Risk and safeguarding systems detecting crisis signals

- Noted that youth expect AI to provide personalized, adaptive experiences—not static tools—suggesting evidence standards should account for personalization

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Products with clinical evidence of health improvement face significantly higher regulatory burden... By contrast, general purpose or wellness AI products face less regulatory scrutiny, thereby skewing the market toward adopting tools optimized for increasing user engagement rather than verifiable health outcomes or population health impact."

- "Continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale, without AI."

- "The central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection; for that reason it is important that HHS promote AI use in clinical care subject to preserving the power of human connection and that human-led care is a core tenet of our health care system."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- This technology has not been studied in a responsible manner, as one would for any "health" intervention
- Already seeing vulnerable people falling prey to irresponsible and essentially useless suggestions
