structuredSections.detailedContent:
- National Council for Mental Wellbeing represents over 3,200 mental health and substance use treatment organizations serving more than 10 million children, adults, and families nationwide
- Appreciates HHS efforts to strengthen effective and responsible technology adoption by providers and increase beneficiary access to digital capabilities
- Thoughtful efforts to drive adoption of safe, high-quality digital health products can increase access to mental health and substance use care, relieve administrative burden, and decrease workforce burden, particularly in shortage areas

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - Adequate reimbursement pathways and resources are needed
    - Digital health products can support care between providers and patients, particularly in rural areas with transportation barriers and provider shortages
    - While Medicare has taken steps to facilitate digital therapeutic reimbursement, adequate methodologies and resources must exist for successful implementation
    - Current obstacles include technological barriers (limited smartphone access), lack of provider knowledge of available tools, lack of psychoeducation for patients, and inadequate reimbursement
  - Behavioral health providers were excluded from HITECH Act of 2009
    - This left many behavioral health providers' IT infrastructure and interoperability lacking
    - AI solution pricing is high partly due to lack of EHR standardization
    - AI software companies must spend resources becoming compatible with dozens of different EHR ecosystems, increasing costs
  - Sustainable financing in the public sector is essential
    - Public sector is one of the largest customer bases
    - Private sector developers need clearer pathways for financial growth to galvanize private funding in early stages
    - Largest customer bases must have means for adoption and capacity for innovation
  - Provider capacity must be considered, and innovation must not add to administrative burden
    - Mental health and substance use staff are already burdened and stretched thin
    - Challenging to take risks on new innovations requiring investment with limited resources and time
    - Essential that new AI solutions are provider driven and do not add to administrative burden
    - AI technology is often built without appropriate clinician input
    - Tools must be adaptable to clinicians' complex needs and integrate smoothly into existing workflows
    - Some tools designed to decrease burden may inadvertently increase it
    - Suggest prioritizing AI that reduces administrative burden before adding AI for clinical care while further research is conducted on risks
  - Innovation must be provider driven and supplement, not supplant, human-provided care
    - Particularly important for older adults, youth, and children
    - More research needed on implications and risks of AI in mental health and substance use clinical care
    - Concerns include AI-induced psychosis, exacerbated depression symptoms, suicidality, and missed opportunities for crisis deescalation
    - Clinicians work in high-stress environments where close attention to wellbeing is critical
    - Implementation of innovative tools needs to be well validated and reliable
    - Risk of false sense of trust and overreliance on tools that still need close monitoring
  - Patient privacy protections must be uncompromised
    - AI implementation must adhere to federal and state privacy laws
    - Providers must understand data privacy protections and legal requirements
    - Technology must comply with data use policies as part of protecting patient privacy
    - Transparency needed on data use, storage policies, risks, and benefits
    - Further provider education and guidance on 42 CFR Part 2 implementation needed
    - Implications of AI use and 42 CFR Part 2 requirements important for development and implementation
  - Education and training for patients and providers is needed
    - Providers need training on digital products that are accurate, safe, efficacious, and reliable
    - Providers must furnish psychoeducation on tool use for patients prior to implementation
    - Patients must have access to devices needed for product use and adequate broadband, particularly in rural areas
  - Current entity restrictions must be considered
    - Urge CMS to engage with states regarding implementation issues where MCOs restrict use of claims provided to value-based contracted providers and AI applications due to uncertainty about privacy, security, and liability risks

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Prioritize tools that reduce administrative burden
    - Digital health products must be accurate, safe, efficacious, and reliable
    - Must comply with all legal requirements, particularly privacy requirements
    - Must provide information and data use policies transparently
    - Must not perpetuate inaccuracy issues in delivering care
    - Products should integrate with providers' existing tools to avoid duplicative efforts
    - Members report that reporting across non-integrated systems creates significant burden
    - Products should recognize variability across state requirements and EHR systems
    - Adequate infrastructure for patient and provider must be in place
  - Efficient funding mechanisms needed
    - Appropriate coding and reimbursement structures must be established
    - Should account for usage of AI tools that support operational efficiencies
    - Reimbursement for provider training and patient education needed for safe and effective implementation
  - AI must be appropriately used in behavioral health care
    - Guardrails needed on use of AI for "therapy" that do not curb innovation and comply with all executive orders
    - Must ensure AI augments rather than replaces in-person/human-provided clinical care
    - Training and oversight needed to prevent overreliance on AI tools in decision making
    - Could lead to escalation of symptoms or missed considerations in complex cases

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Reimbursement remains unclear for AI activities under existing CPT coding and payment structures
    - Unclear what qualifies as billable, what documentation and oversight required
    - Unclear how to ensure organizations and clinicians are not financially penalized for investing in AI
    - HHS needs to provide clear guidance and guardrails to providers and technology vendors
  - Guidance needed on appropriate data storage practices
    - Clear delineation of liability protections when implementing AI solutions
    - HHS may consider evaluating state-level action and provide federal guardrails
  - HHS must protect scope of practice for mental health and substance use clinicians
    - Clear definitions and guidance needed to delineate types of solutions AI provides (e.g., what can be called "therapy")
    - Quality standard guidelines needed so developers understand benchmarks for data integrity, testing, design features, and evaluation
  - Rigorous safeguards needed for AI solutions, particularly those providing emotional support
    - Protections for patients in crisis
    - Suicidal and homicidal risk assessments
    - Screening mechanisms for patients with delusions
    - Assurances that technology complies with patient privacy protections
    - HHS can help by regulating accreditation, certification, and offering pre-vetted solutions meeting federal requirements

- Question 5: How HHS can support private sector activities
  - ADT feeds would be more understandable with AI application
    - ADT feeds do not currently aggregate multiple admissions, discharges, and transfers into packaged episodes of care
    - People transferred between hospital units generate multiple ADT notifications
    - Very difficult and time-consuming for outside providers to understand actual final discharge versus unit transfer
    - Revising ONC and CMS guidance to encourage AI application to simplify ADT feeds into aggregated episodes would greatly help
  - HIE outputs would be more understandable with AI application
    - Finding specific clinical information in HIE query requires opening and reading large numbers of individual clinical documents
    - CMS could encourage HIEs to use AI to provide inquiry responses that are more specific and detailed

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or fallen short of expectations
  - Promising performance outcomes
    - Ambient scribe tools have exceeded performance impact for community behavioral health providers
    - Should focus on ensuring and improving quality while maintaining low barrier-to-entry cost
    - AI tools that streamline burdensome processes for regulatory requirements, intake, and assessment hold promise
    - Screening and assessment tools that screen for multiple conditions efficiently and make evidence-based treatment plan recommendations could be promising
    - VR or real-time training for staff with evidence base may assist in preparing for high-intensity client situations
  - Outcomes that have fallen short
    - ROI has often fallen short given burden and cost of implementation
    - Often unclear that benefits or positive ROI has materialized
    - Can stem from cost and/or implementation burden higher than potential benefits
    - Providers found false expectations around ease of implementation or interoperability
    - Cases where new technology performs duplicative functions already done through EHRs

- Question 7: Decision makers and administrative hurdles to AI adoption
  - Primary administrative hurdles are financing and cost burden
  - Imperative that providers of all types (licensed and non-licensed) are involved from the beginning
    - Providers play essential role in adoption success and clinical outcomes
    - Time and resources needed
  - Critical stakeholders include organization and clinical leaders who oversee care models, workflows, and assume financial risk
  - Private sector certification bodies, state licensing bodies, and national bodies for nurses, doctors, psychologists, psychiatrists, social workers, therapists, and counselors are essential
  - Information and education at colleges and universities while someone is becoming a provider also important

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen opportunities
  - Would allow pooling of data across discrete payers to standardize reimbursement rates and advance value-based care
  - Would allow patient care to extend outside healthcare to community organizations, social service systems, school systems, housing systems, criminal justice systems
    - Where social and environmental factors are addressed
    - Where lifestyle and functional medicine happen
  - Providers need further education from CMS and ONC about opportunities and requirements under new interoperability rules
    - Particularly regarding information blocking
    - Further guidance needed on how interoperability rule interacts with HIPAA and 42 CFR Part 2
    - Not adequate provider education and training on new requirements and financial penalties for information blocking
  - One of AI's most powerful opportunities is aggregating and synthesizing information from multiple sources
    - Requires that sources do not engage in information blocking
    - Further guidance and support from CMS and ONC could help providers revise policies, procedures, forms, and staff training
    - Need to integrate interoperability requirements with updated 42 CFR Part 2 and HIPAA requirements

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns
  - AI may augment provider care and reduce administrative burden
  - Critical to recognize AI-supported care is not the same as professional, human-delivered, formal behavioral health services
    - Cannot replace need for human-delivered services
    - Should prioritize leveraging both human-provided and AI-supported modalities
    - Goal: behavioral health system providing right type and level of care, at right time, constantly reevaluated for continuous improvement
  - Opportunities in AI adoption
    - Tools supporting accurate and tailored diagnosis and treatment plans
    - Reduction in times patients and caregivers need to answer provider questions
    - Improved accessibility across team-based care approaches improving patient experience
  - Patient and provider concerns
    - Privacy protections (what is done with information, who else sees it)
    - Must comply with all applicable federal and state laws
    - Youth-specific privacy and safety protections needed for child and youth-facing technology
    - Concerns about algorithms driving clinical decisions increasing risk of provider overreliance or false sense of security
    - Concerns with patient-facing technologies that could exacerbate depression symptoms, onset of psychosis, homicidality, suicidality, or escalate crisis without sufficient research

- Question 10: Areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - Prioritize research on impacts and implications of clinical decision-supported AI use in mental health and substance use care
  - Currently not a substantial, robust body of literature on complexity of implications
  - Must evaluate risk of clinician overreliance as AI solutions are adopted
  - Research must better understand interaction between AI-supported care and human-delivered services
  - Further research topics to support the field
    - Screening and assessment
    - Effect of AI chatbot use for emotional support and light-touch interventions
    - Existing use and efficacy of AI in community mental health and substance use settings

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
National membership organization representing 3,200+ mental health and substance use treatment providers urges HHS to prioritize AI that reduces administrative burden while ensuring robust safeguards, adequate reimbursement, and research before deploying AI in clinical behavioral health care.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** National Council for Mental Wellbeing
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Membership organization representing over 3,200 mental health and substance use treatment organizations serving more than 10 million children, adults, and families; led by Charles Ingoglia, MSW, President & CEO
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Member organizations would be direct users and implementers of AI in clinical behavioral health care; members face unique challenges including exclusion from HITECH Act funding, workforce shortages, and complex patient populations requiring careful AI safeguards

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We support thoughtful AI adoption that can increase access to mental health and substance use care, relieve administrative burden, and decrease workforce burden—but AI must supplement, not supplant, human-provided care. Given the risks of AI-induced psychosis, exacerbated depression, and missed crisis opportunities, we urge prioritizing AI for administrative burden reduction before clinical applications, while ensuring adequate reimbursement, provider training, and robust patient safety protections.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Prioritize AI tools that reduce administrative burden before deploying AI for clinical mental health and substance use care
  - Ambient scribe tools have shown promise and should be expanded with quality improvements
  - AI for regulatory compliance, intake, and assessment processes should be prioritized
- Establish adequate reimbursement pathways for AI implementation
  - Create appropriate coding and reimbursement structures for AI tool usage
  - Include reimbursement for provider training and patient education
  - Account for operational efficiencies enabled by AI tools
- Ensure AI is provider-driven and supplements human care
  - Require clinician input in AI tool development
  - Establish guardrails defining what AI can be called "therapy"
  - Mandate that AI augments rather than replaces in-person clinical care
- Provide clear guidance on legal and liability issues
  - Clarify what AI activities qualify as billable under existing CPT codes
  - Delineate liability protections for providers implementing AI solutions
  - Provide guidance on data storage practices for providers and vendors
- Establish rigorous safeguards for patient-facing AI
  - Require protections for patients in crisis
  - Mandate suicidal and homicidal risk assessments
  - Implement screening mechanisms for patients with delusions
  - Ensure compliance with patient privacy protections
- Revise ONC and CMS guidance on ADT feeds to encourage AI application
  - Enable aggregation of multiple admissions, discharges, and transfers into packaged episodes of care
- Encourage HIEs to use AI for more specific and detailed query responses
- Provide further education and guidance on 42 CFR Part 2 implementation and its intersection with AI use
- Engage with states regarding MCO restrictions on AI use due to privacy, security, and liability concerns
- Offer pre-vetted AI solutions that meet federal requirements to reduce provider burden
- Prioritize research on AI impacts in mental health and substance use clinical care
  - Study screening and assessment applications
  - Evaluate AI chatbot use for emotional support and light-touch interventions
  - Assess existing AI use and efficacy in community mental health settings

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Behavioral health providers were excluded from HITECH Act funding, leaving IT infrastructure and interoperability lacking
  - AI solution pricing is high partly because software companies must become compatible with dozens of different EHR ecosystems
- Risk of AI adding to administrative burden rather than reducing it
  - Staff already stretched thin in highly stressful environments
  - AI often built without appropriate clinician input
  - Tools designed to decrease burden may inadvertently increase it
- Serious patient safety risks with AI in clinical behavioral health care
  - Cases of AI-induced psychosis documented
  - Risk of exacerbated depression symptoms
  - Concerns about suicidality and missed opportunities for crisis deescalation
  - Risk of false sense of trust and overreliance on tools that need close monitoring
- Privacy and data protection concerns
  - Unclear how AI intersects with 42 CFR Part 2 requirements
  - Providers need to understand data privacy protections and legal requirements
  - Youth-specific privacy and safety protections needed
- Reimbursement uncertainty
  - Unclear what qualifies as billable under existing CPT coding
  - Unclear documentation and oversight requirements
  - Risk of financial penalties for investing in AI technologies
- ROI has often fallen short of expectations
  - Implementation burden and cost frequently exceed benefits
  - False expectations around ease of implementation and interoperability
  - New technology sometimes performs duplicative functions already done through EHRs
- MCOs restricting use of AI applications due to uncertainty about privacy, security, and liability risks
- Inadequate provider education on interoperability rules, information blocking requirements, and associated financial penalties
- Lack of robust research literature on implications of AI in clinical mental health and substance use care

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Ambient scribe tools have exceeded performance expectations for community behavioral health providers—a rare bright spot in AI adoption
- Behavioral health's exclusion from HITECH Act created a lasting infrastructure gap that now compounds AI adoption challenges, as AI companies must build compatibility with dozens of different EHR systems
- Members report that new technology solutions sometimes perform duplicative functions already handled by EHRs, creating burden without benefit
- ADT feeds generate multiple notifications when patients transfer between hospital units, making it "very difficult and time-consuming for outside providers to understand what an actual final discharge is" versus an internal transfer
- Finding specific clinical information in HIE queries requires "opening and reading large numbers of individual clinical documents"—a problem AI could solve
- MCOs are restricting value-based contracted providers from using AI applications due to uncertainty about privacy, security, and liability risks
- The intersection of AI use with 42 CFR Part 2 (substance use confidentiality regulations) creates implementation complexity that hasn't been adequately addressed

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Because mental health and substance use providers were left out of the HITECH Act of 2009, that provided significant funding for the adoption of EHRs, many behavioral health providers' information technology infrastructure and interoperability have been lacking."

- "More research is needed to understand implications and risks in applying AI in mental health and substance use clinical care – particularly in light of considering cases of AI induced psychosis, exacerbated depression symptoms, suicidality, and missed opportunities for crisis deescalation."

- "It is critical to define and recognize that AI supported care is not the same as professional, human-delivered, formal behavioral health services. AI supported care should not attempt to and cannot replace the need for such human-delivered services."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- SITC is the world's leading member-driven organization dedicated to improving cancer patient outcomes by advancing cancer immunotherapy science and application
  - 501(c)(3) not-for-profit medical professional society
  - Members include research scientists, physician scientists, clinicians, patients, patient advocates, government representatives, and industry leaders
  - Focus areas: scientific meetings, education and outreach, field initiatives, collaborations with domestic and international organizations
  - Goal: making "cure" a reality for cancer patients everywhere

- AI utilization is an opportunity to accelerate progress across the cancer care continuum
  - SITC is excited by potential for continued AI implementation while maintaining highest standards of patient safety, privacy, and scientific best practices

- Regulatory Considerations of AI Implementation
  - Transparency regarding LLM AI use within FDA is important to accelerate safe adoption across healthcare
    - AI use is expanding into predictive and risk stratification models, imaging and pathology tools, and generative or clinical decision-support systems
    - Visibility into regulatory evaluation and internal use can help set field-wide expectations
    - Transparency needed around model purpose, performance, data validation, limitations, updating processes, real-world monitoring, and accountability structures
    - This may strengthen trust and support responsible clinical integration
  - Continued clarity needed on how AI model updates over time may affect clinical trial implementation
    - Clear regulatory expectations needed for revalidation, documentation, and post-deployment monitoring
    - This could help maintain trial integrity and consistent performance
    - Single institutions may lack sufficient data to detect rare failures or performance issues in underrepresented populations
    - Collaborative, multi-institutional evaluation frameworks could support earlier safety detection and more equitable performance assessment
  - Clear pathways are critical for sharing AI tools that meet regulatory standards across institutions
    - Could help prevent siloed implementation
    - Shared validation approaches, standardized performance reporting, and coordinated monitoring may improve access to safe and effective AI-enabled care
    - Consortium-based models could support more equitable deployment across healthcare settings while enabling shared learning and continuous performance oversight

- Research and Development Opportunities Through AI Implementation
  - Increased investment in AI-driven biomarker discovery can accelerate identification of clinically meaningful signals
    - Works across multi-omic and clinical datasets
    - Supports emerging clinical AI applications: predictive risk models, imaging and pathology tools, clinical decision-support systems
    - Safe and effective adoption requires strong transparency standards across model purpose, performance, validation, limitations, updating, and accountability
    - Collaborative, multi-institutional evaluation can improve detection of rare failures, ensure performance across unique populations, and promote equitable access to AI-enabled care
  - Investment in infrastructure for bio-banking and sharing of ECQM records in HIPAA-compliant ways
    - Can accelerate and enable high-quality AI development, validation, and real-world monitoring
    - Transparency across data provenance, governance, and intended clinical use can strengthen trust and regulatory confidence
    - Multi-institutional data-sharing and oversight models can improve detection of safety or performance issues
    - Can help prevent fragmented AI implementation across healthcare systems

- SITC offers to further assist or collaborate on this effort
  - Leaders have vast expertise in immunotherapeutic drug development
  - Diverse perspectives on future of cancer care and research
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Leading cancer immunotherapy society urges FDA transparency on AI use, multi-institutional collaboration frameworks, and investment in AI-driven biomarker discovery and data-sharing infrastructure to accelerate safe, equitable AI adoption in oncology.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer (SITC)
- **Type:** Trade Association / Medical Professional Society
- **Role/Expertise:** World's leading organization dedicated to cancer immunotherapy; members include research scientists, physician scientists, clinicians, and industry leaders with expertise in immunotherapeutic drug development
- **Geographic Scope:** National/International
- **Stake in Issue:** AI implementation directly affects cancer research, clinical trials, biomarker discovery, and patient care across their membership and the broader oncology field
structuredSections.corePosition: We see AI as an opportunity to accelerate progress across the cancer care continuum and are excited by its potential while maintaining the highest standards of patient safety, privacy, and scientific best practices. Transparency from FDA about their own AI use, clear regulatory pathways for sharing validated AI tools across institutions, and investment in collaborative data infrastructure are essential for safe, equitable AI adoption in cancer care.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Increase FDA transparency regarding LLM AI use
  - Disclose model purpose, performance, data validation, limitations, updating processes, real-world monitoring, and accountability structures
  - Visibility into regulatory evaluation and internal use can set field-wide expectations
- Establish clear regulatory expectations for AI model updates affecting clinical trials
  - Define requirements for revalidation, documentation, and post-deployment monitoring
  - Develop collaborative, multi-institutional evaluation frameworks for earlier safety detection
- Create clear pathways for sharing AI tools across institutions
  - Develop shared validation approaches and standardized performance reporting
  - Support consortium-based models for equitable deployment and shared learning
- Increase investment in AI-driven biomarker discovery
  - Focus on multi-omic and clinical datasets
  - Require strong transparency standards for all clinical AI applications
- Invest in infrastructure for bio-banking and ECQM record sharing
  - Ensure HIPAA compliance
  - Establish transparency across data provenance, governance, and intended clinical use
  - Support multi-institutional data-sharing and oversight models
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Single institutions may lack sufficient data to detect rare AI failures or performance issues in underrepresented populations
- Risk of siloed AI implementation without clear pathways for cross-institutional sharing
- Fragmented AI implementation across healthcare systems without coordinated data-sharing infrastructure
- Need for equitable AI performance assessment across unique populations
- Maintaining clinical trial integrity when AI models are updated over time
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Identifies a specific gap: individual institutions cannot adequately evaluate AI safety alone because they lack data volume to detect rare failures or assess performance in underrepresented populations
- Proposes consortium-based models as solution for both equitable deployment and continuous performance oversight
- Emphasizes that FDA transparency about their own internal AI use could help set expectations for the entire field
structuredSections.keyQuotations: No standout quotations - the comment is written in formal, technical language without particularly memorable phrasing.

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Community Care Cooperative (C3) background
  - Non-profit, Federally Qualified Health Center (FQHC)-owned Accountable Care and Management Services Organization
  - Founded, governed, and operated by community health centers
  - Supports more than 50 FQHCs across fifteen states and the District of Columbia
  - Operates Medicare, Medicaid, and commercial value-based payment arrangements covering more than 250,000 lives
  - Mission is to leverage collective strengths of FQHCs to improve health outcomes for underserved communities while lowering total cost of care
  - As a Management Services Organization, works closely with health centers on operational, clinical, and technological needs
  - Serves as leader and group purchaser for both clinical and administrative AI solutions
  - Has direct experience supporting real-world deployment of AI tools in under-resourced primary care settings

- Comments focus on two areas
  - How HHS can accelerate applied AI innovation in primary care and community health
  - Regulatory and governance safeguards necessary to maintain patient trust and support responsible adoption

- Accelerating Applied AI Innovation in Primary Care and Community Health
  - Primary care is the only specialty consistently shown to reduce total cost of care through prevention, early detection, and longitudinal chronic disease management
  - Yet it remains chronically under-resourced and under-invested, particularly in community health centers
  - FQHCs serve approximately 14 percent of the U.S. population while receiving a small share of federal health care funding
  - AI adoption strategies designed for well-resourced health systems often do not translate effectively to safety-net settings
  - To realize AI's potential to improve outcomes and advance health equity, primary care providers—particularly FQHCs—must be treated as essential partners in design, validation, and deployment of AI solutions, not simply as downstream adopters

- Where Applied AI Is Delivering Immediate Value
  - Health centers across the network have expressed strong interest in AI tools that deliver near-term operational and clinical value
  - Reducing administrative burden
    - Ambient documentation
    - Coding and billing support
    - Workflow automation that improves accuracy and sustainability while alleviating staff burnout
  - Enhancing patient access and experience
    - AI-enabled voice agents that support appointment navigation, language access, and patient communication
  - Supporting high-quality clinical care
    - Tools that augment clinical workflows such as pre-visit preparation and documentation
    - Must preserve clinician judgment and accountability
  - Trust is foundational to adoption
    - Health centers and patients must be confident AI tools are designed to improve access to care and understanding of health information
    - Not to obscure decision-making or introduce new risks

- Barriers to Adoption in Safety-Net Settings
  - Despite growing interest, FQHCs face meaningful barriers to AI adoption
  - Upfront and ongoing costs
    - Coupled with uncertainty around return on investment in value-based environments
  - Workflow disruption and training demands
    - Particularly in settings with limited staffing capacity
  - Interoperability challenges
    - Integration across EHR instances and versions
    - Avoidance of siloed, proprietary tools
  - Limited internal capacity to vet, govern, and continuously monitor AI solutions
  - These barriers are structural, not philosophical
    - Reflect day-to-day realities of operating in safety-net primary care
    - With the right federal policy levers, they are addressable

- Federal Levers to Enable Practical Adoption (responding to Questions 2 and 8)
  - Align payment models with AI adoption costs
    - Embed technology adoption incentives into CMMI models such as LEAD and ACCESS models
    - Allow advanced or onboarding investments to offset implementation and management costs
  - Leverage trusted intermediaries and dissemination hubs
    - Can convene technology developers and community health centers
    - Reduce duplication and accelerate real-world validation and learning
    - Examples: collaboratives that pair early-stage developers with safety-net providers (ATTUNE-like models) or implementation-focused hubs (Health AI Partnerships)
  - Create mutual incentives for data sharing and interoperability
    - Support bidirectional data flow between providers and developers
    - Avoid data silos that undermine care coordination and population health management

- Data Representativeness
  - FQHCs care for patients who are, on average, sicker, lower-income, and more socially complex than many other populations
  - AI tools developed without meaningful inclusion of safety-net data risk working well for some populations while failing others
  - In worst cases, could exacerbate existing disparities
  - Representative data, ongoing monitoring, and corrective action are essential to ensuring AI advances equity rather than undermining it
  - Community health centers should be viewed as indispensable partners in developing trustworthy, equitable AI

- Regulation, Governance, and Necessary Safeguards
  - Maintaining patient trust is paramount to successful adoption of AI in clinical and administrative settings
  - AI has real potential to improve care delivery, but only if deployed with strong guardrails
  - Human accountability must be preserved
    - AI tools should augment—not replace—clinical judgment
    - Qualified human providers must remain responsible for final clinical decisions
  - Data privacy and transparency are essential
    - Ensure strict adherence to HIPAA and Privacy Act requirements for all AI tools handling protected health information
    - Require clear, explicit disclosure of data use, sharing, and permissions by AI developers
    - Develop a vendor evaluation or certification-like framework analogous to EHR certification
      - Building on emerging work such as the AI Vendor Disclosure Framework developed by Health AI Partnerships
      - Would enable providers to assess privacy protections, data governance practices, and trustworthiness with confidence
  - Such guardrails would reduce uncertainty, support responsible adoption, and protect both patients and providers

- Closing
  - C3 strongly supports HHS' efforts through ONC and CMMI to expand access, reduce administrative burden, and sustain high-quality primary care through responsible use of AI
  - As a primary-care-led, FQHC-owned organization with hands-on deployment experience, C3 and the community health centers we support stand ready to partner with HHS on future pilots, learning collaboratives, and stakeholder engagement
  - Would welcome opportunity to inform implementation efforts with on-the-ground experience from primary care and community health settings
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: FQHC-owned cooperative serving 250,000 patients across 50+ health centers urges HHS to treat safety-net providers as essential AI partners—not downstream adopters—and to align payment models, create vendor certification frameworks, and ensure AI tools are built with representative data to advance rather than undermine health equity.

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- American Dental Association (ADA) response to HHS request for information on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
- ADA background and qualifications
  - Leading authority on oral health in the United States
  - ANSI-Accredited Standards Developer (ASD)
  - Leads national and international dental standards efforts
  - U.S. Technical Advisory Group Secretary to ISO Technical Committee 106 on Dentistry
  - Co-Secretary of systems and devices interoperability working group under ISO/TC 215 (Health Informatics)
  - Over 400 volunteer experts support the Standards Program
  - Published first white paper on clinical and non-clinical AI applications in dentistry in 2022
  - Developed first U.S. standard in AI in dentistry approved by ANSI
  - Additional AI-related standards, white papers, and technical reports forthcoming
- ADA Forsyth Institute research activities
  - Collaborating with industry and academic partners on physics-based digital twins
    - High-fidelity computational surrogates coupling first-principles multiphysics simulation with machine learning
    - Platform continuously ingests experimental data to calibrate model states and material parameters
    - Forecasts performance under novel conditions
    - Solves inverse-design/optimization problems to recommend formulations and processing conditions
  - Supported by five-year award from National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research (NIDCR)
    - Represents NIDCR's first major investment in applying AI to basic oral health research
  - Scientists using AI/ML toolkits for biomedical discovery
    - RNA/CRISPR design guidance
    - Drug discovery
    - Receptor-ligand binding
    - Microbiome profiling
    - Cell culture media formulation
    - Next-generation sequencing analysis
    - Host-pathogen interactions
    - Spatial transcriptomics
    - Integrative multi-omics workflows with metapangenomics
  - These capabilities compress historically resource-intensive cycles by reducing wet-lab iterations
  - Shifts discovery from intuition-driven trial-and-error to predictive, data-driven experimentation

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - Data heterogeneity in training data sets
    - Electronic dental records (EDRs) designed and used differently than electronic medical records (EMRs)
    - Most EDRs focus on documenting procedures and visit-based details rather than standardized diagnostic information
    - Data recorded at granular clinical levels (tooth, surface, probing site) but not maintained in consistent, structured formats
    - ANSI/ADA Standard No. 2000.8, SNODENT (Systematized Nomenclature of Dentistry) exists and is harmonized with SNOMED CT but not widely adopted
    - Procedure-based documentation lacks structured detail on materials, methods, duration, or complexity
    - Heterogeneity leads to unstable modeling, reduced accuracy, and poor application performance
    - Imaging data (radiographs, cone-beam CT, intraoral scans, photos) are most readily available and reliable training data sources
    - Non-standard or unstructured numerical and free-text data introduces significant variability and noise
  - Data interoperability and exchange challenges
    - Dental data often inaccessible, siloed, and stored in systems with limited interoperability
    - Electronic records frequently exhibit low variable completeness
    - Sampling bias when datasets overrepresent specific populations (sicker, healthier, or more affluent)
    - AI models trained on biased data inherit these biases, limiting fairness, reliability, and generalizability
  - Limitations of dental claims data for research and development
    - Claims systems designed for benefit administration and rule-based plan design, not capturing clinical complexity
    - Information in "all-payer" databases is narrow and poorly aligned with AI-driven clinical decision support needs
    - Dental data typically collected and adjudicated based on covered services rather than underlying conditions, disease severity, or treatment complexity
  - Data governance at scale
    - Standardized, secure, automated processes would improve trust in data quality
    - Cannot be managed through fragmented policy and technology landscape
    - State-specific privacy, disclosure, consent, and oversight rules often more restrictive than federal rules
    - Forces AI developers to operate under toughest requirements, increasing operational complexity
    - Inconsistent policies lead to misunderstanding of responsibility for clinician and patient consent, transparency, privacy protections
    - Blurs liability and malpractice accountability
    - Operational barriers to AI adoption include:
      - High costs for building and maintaining implementation infrastructure
      - Extensive training and workforce development challenges amplified by workforce shortage
      - Unclear accountability increases legal and operational risk for dental practices
      - Particularly affects covered entities under HIPAA, False Claims Act, or Section 1157 of the Affordable Care Act

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Strengthen dental data capture and reporting in federally funded programs
    - ADA encouraged by ASTP/ONC, CMS, and other federal agencies recognizing dental-specific data
    - Appreciates inclusion of Code on Dental Procedures and Nomenclatures (CDT) in Corrected Applicable Standard Reference for Procedures within USCDI v5
    - Appreciates ASTP/ONC efforts to ensure CDT maintains parity with other standard terminologies
    - ADA continues pursuing inclusion of dental-specific data elements in administrative and clinical care standards
    - Supports efforts to improve recording of disease and diagnosis data within federally funded programs
    - Encourages HHS to incentivize clinical documentation improvement activities and technology-assisted disease management pilots for dental providers
  - Incentivize modern technology infrastructure
    - Dentistry has not benefited from appropriations supporting electronic health record infrastructure development
    - EHR infrastructure is prerequisite for AI integration
    - Encourages HHS to provide funding to support data exchange
    - Incentives increasingly important for rural and small practices adopting emerging AI technology
  - Establish external, independent validation of dental AI algorithms using synthetic data and informatics standards
    - Small differences between training data and real-world conditions can produce harmful errors
    - External, independent validation using industry-specific and trusted standards is critical
    - ADA committed to developing foundational standards for validating interoperability and AI tools
      - Terminology standards defining vocabulary used in dentistry
      - Data structure and exchange standards defining data models for interoperable communication
      - Content standards defining data required for administrative needs and clinical care
    - Standards critical for creating consistent, structured, high-quality datasets for reliable AI test data
    - Interoperability standards and gold-standard synthetic datasets foundational to improving trust in AI tools
    - ADA supports public-private partnerships ensuring right standards and synthetic gold standard datasets remain in place

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Standardize definition of "Non-Medical Devices"
    - Standardized definition across agencies, organizations, and industry would provide foundation for:
      - Defining need for disclaimers, consent, privacy, and security oversight
      - Implications of telehealth requirements based on type of information exchanged and stored
    - Lack of consistency widens gap in AI adoption driven by liability concerns and malpractice coverage implications
  - Improve AI vendor accountability
    - Dental practices increasingly rely on third-party vendors for critical systems (EDRs, imaging software, appointment management)
    - Rapid expansion of AI tools places significant responsibility on providers to:
      - Judge validation and performance of products
      - Implement and maintain privacy and cybersecurity measures
      - Maintain oversight and assess vendor reliability
    - Gaps in federal regulatory space to assist providers making complex technology decisions
    - ADA recommends HHS establish standardized requirements for labeling AI-based products and tools
      - Designed to improve trust and adoption
      - Strengthen shared decision-making between clinicians and patients
    - ADA recommends HHS strengthen HIPAA requirements and enforcement for electronic dental technology vendors
      - Vendors should certify solutions meet HIPAA and industry security standards
      - Include features like encryption and multi-factor authentication (MFA) as default configurations
    - Clarifying division of responsibilities between providers and vendors ensures practices have tools and support without disproportionate burdens
    - ADA supports simplified, standardized templates for risk analysis and asset management
      - Eases burden on practices
      - Ensures consistent compliance with privacy laws governing AI technology
    - Administrative tasks not supported by practice management or health record systems often performed through workaround applications
      - May not require business associate agreement
      - Shifts compliance accountability to clinicians
    - Strengthening guidance on software system accountability for privacy and data protection will allow clinicians to consider AI adoption for administrative efficiency and clinical decision-making

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Facilitate establishment of gold-standard synthetic data sets
    - AI models only as good as data used in development
    - Establishing gold-standard synthetic data sets based on real-world clinical and administrative data is key to evaluating new AI models before patient care application
    - Evaluation framework should include:
      - Criteria for consistent performance
      - Metrics to monitor bias
      - Reinforcing transparency
    - Pre-deployment evaluation should include:
      - External validation on multi-center datasets
      - Calibration assessment
      - Bias analyses
      - Prospective studies measuring real-world performance without affecting care
    - Post-deployment monitoring should include:
      - Drift detection
      - Version/change-control governance
      - Ongoing performance reporting
      - Structured recording and analysis of AI tool errors
    - Strongest dental AI solutions function as clinical copilots delivering measurable business impact while reinforcing transparency, reliability, and clinician confidence
    - Standards provide roadmaps for evaluating and integrating AI systems into dental practice
      - Establish criteria for safety, efficacy, transparency, and fairness
    - ADA Technical Report No. 1109, Dentistry – Evaluation of Dental Image Analysis Systems Using Augmented/Artificial Intelligence defines consistent performance and bias metrics for pre and post deployment evaluations and reporting

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
  - Support public-private partnerships to solve real-world administrative burdens
    - Credentialing is one of most administratively burdensome aspects of clinical operations
    - AI tools offer meaningful path to improving speed, consistency, and reliability of credentialing decisions
    - Streamlining primary source verification and onboarding improves access to care
    - Removes barrier for clinicians seeking timely employment opportunities
    - Supporting responsible adoption of such tools reduces administrative burden, encourages innovation, strengthens credentialing ecosystem
    - ADA actively collaborating with:
      - CAQH (supporting standardized provider data collection)
      - LightSpun.ai (supporting automated primary source verification across all 50 states)
    - Collaboration offers end-to-end AI-based credentialing solution for dental industry
    - ADA urges HHS to support public-private partnerships for effective AI use

- Question 7: Roles and governing bodies influencing AI adoption; primary administrative hurdles
  - Support needs of small and rural practices when considering barriers and administrative hurdles
    - Despite potential benefits, AI adoption across dental practices remains uneven, particularly among small and mid-sized practices
    - Adoption decisions most influenced by:
      - Practice owners/clinical leadership
      - IT/informatics
      - Compliance/privacy
      - Legal/risk management (especially when regulatory status unclear)
    - Need for such coordination places undue burden on small and rural practices
    - Primary administrative barriers:
      - Lack of and cost of current infrastructure to support implementation
      - High implementation costs
      - Workforce readiness limitations
      - Inadequate interoperability with existing systems
      - Regulatory uncertainty and approval timelines
      - Privacy concerns
      - Unclear liability frameworks
      - Malpractice implications
      - Lack of standardized accountability in industry and performance benchmarks
    - For AI tools within FDA's regulatory framework (diagnostic or decision-support functions):
      - Regulatory clearance/approval process adds substantial time, cost, and uncertainty
      - Affects vendor readiness, procurement timelines, and practices' confidence in performance claims
    - Upfront financial investment (software, hardware upgrades, customization, ongoing maintenance) poses significant obstacle
      - Especially when short-term ROI is uncertain
    - Workforce challenges:
      - Limited training opportunities
      - Need for workflow adaptation
      - May temporarily affect productivity
    - Many AI technologies do not integrate seamlessly with existing EHRs, imaging systems, or practice management platforms
    - Data security, privacy, and HIPAA compliance concerns remain paramount
    - Unresolved questions regarding liability and accountability when AI-generated recommendations used in clinical decision-making contribute to hesitancy
    - Inconsistent data standards and absence of clear regulatory guidance limit dentists' ability to objectively assess AI tools

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Oral health fundamental to improving health and well-being of all Americans
  - HHS can accelerate AI integration in care delivery by establishing a practice network
    - Federated, multi-site R&D and implementation platform
    - Enables rigorous evaluation and deployment of AI tools in real-world clinical workflows
    - ADA's leadership in standards, data analytics, and clinical guidance combined with ADA Forsyth Institute's scientific expertise and translational research capacity
    - Participating clinical sites provide scalable operational environments to test implementation and workflow integration under consistent processes
  - To promote generalizable evidence generation:
    - HHS could encourage alignment with major health IT infrastructure
    - Create opportunities for multi-site analyses and longitudinal outcome assessments of de-identified, aggregated health data
    - ADA can facilitate collaborations to operationalize practice network for efficiently gathering annotated training data and credible evidence
    - Structure reduces redundant, one-off evaluations
    - Improves data confidence
    - Creates scalable pathway from research validation to deployment in cost-efficient manner
  - Enhanced interoperability could unlock AI opportunities by creating large, diverse datasets
    - Drive new AI applications for personalized healthcare, diagnostics, and operational efficiency
  - Outstanding data quality issues due to:
    - Non-conformance to standard data models and terminology
    - Failures to adopt broader healthcare informatics standards (e.g., DICOM)
    - Proliferation of proprietary APIs in dental technology
    - Forces industry to rely on extensive mapping to standards-based APIs by costly third-party data brokers
  - ADA noted several areas for improvement in interoperability in comments to FDA re: Docket No. FDA–2025–N–0287
  - ADA collaborates with HL7 by developing dental content and value sets for FHIR standards
    - Contributors include CMS, DOD, IHS, and private sector companies
    - Ensures implementation guide development aligns with priority information exchange guides (CARIN, DaVinci, other federally mandated and recommended standards) while remaining dental-centric
    - Synergy embeds dental-specific needs in FHIR data model
    - Makes dental data more readily available for AI modeling
  - ADA ready and willing to assist ASTP/ONC in developing USCDI+ dental domain
    - Would further support interoperability and facilitate AI development
    - Establishing dental domain could support agency-wide initiatives
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The nation's leading dental authority urges HHS to address dentistry's unique data fragmentation, fund infrastructure for small practices, and partner with ADA on standards and validation frameworks to unlock AI's potential in oral health care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Dental Association (ADA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** ANSI-Accredited Standards Developer; U.S. Technical Advisory Group Secretary to ISO Technical Committee 106 on Dentistry; Co-Secretary of ISO/TC 215 Health Informatics working group; developer of first U.S. AI standard in dentistry; operates ADA Forsyth Institute conducting AI/ML research with NIDCR funding
- **Geographic Scope:** National and International
- **Stake in Issue:** Represents dental profession's interests in AI adoption; develops standards governing dental AI tools; conducts foundational AI research; members (dental practices) would be directly affected by AI regulations and adoption barriers
structuredSections.corePosition: The ADA believes AI holds tremendous promise for dentistry but faces unique barriers that differ from medicine—particularly fragmented data systems, lack of standardized diagnostic coding, and the absence of EHR infrastructure funding that medical practices received. We urge HHS to recognize dentistry's distinct needs, fund infrastructure for small and rural practices, and partner with ADA on developing validation standards and synthetic datasets that will enable trustworthy AI adoption in oral health care.

structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- ADA Forsyth Institute received NIDCR's first major investment in applying AI to basic oral health research
  - Five-year award supporting physics-based digital twins for next-generation smart dental materials
  - Platform couples first-principles multiphysics simulation with machine learning for parameter estimation and data assimilation

  - Addresses one of most administratively burdensome aspects of clinical operations
  - Automates primary source verification across all 50 states
- Dentistry never received the EHR infrastructure appropriations that medicine did—a prerequisite for AI integration
- Dental imaging data (radiographs, cone-beam CT, intraoral scans, photos) represents the most reliable AI training data source because it's routinely, repeatedly, and longitudinally captured
- The strongest dental AI solutions function as "clinical copilots" delivering measurable business impact while reinforcing transparency and clinician confidence
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Dentistry has not benefited from appropriations to support electronic health record infrastructure development, a prerequisite for AI integration."
- "The current environment of state‑specific privacy, disclosure, consent, and oversight rules which often have more restrictions than federal privacy and security rules, forces AI developers to operate under the toughest requirements, increasing operational complexity."
- "The strongest dental AI solutions ultimately function as clinical copilots, delivering measurable business impact while reinforcing transparency, reliability, and clinician confidence."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Cover Letter
  - Leap Orbit is pleased to respond to ASTP/ONC's RFI on Accelerating AI Adoption in Clinical Care
  - Core belief: the vision for a digitally integrated healthcare future hinges on getting the foundational data layers right
  - Response offers insights on voluntary frameworks for market-driven trust, technical and administrative metadata implications for information blocking, and incentivizing "AI-Ready" data infrastructure
  - Experience developing Convergent provider data management platform informs these insights
  - Extensive real-world implementation of HL7 FHIR standards, particularly Da Vinci PDEX Plan Net Implementation Guide
  - Collaborations with federal entities including ASTP, HRSA (UDS+ Modernization), SAMHSA, and CDC
  - Work presented as case study, not product showcase, to inform sound policies

- Executive Summary: The Paradox of Connectivity
  - Primary bottleneck to clinical AI is no longer algorithm complexity but "trust friction" from fragmented, unverified data pipelines
  - Core challenge is misalignment in how we approach foundational data infrastructure
  - AI acceleration is primarily hindered by lack of "AI-Ready" foundational data
  - AI requires a "bedrock" of accurate, accessible, interoperable data with traceable provenance for safety, equity, and effectiveness
  - Three recommended "Foundational Prerequisites":
    - Market-Driven Trust Frameworks: move from prescriptive "black box" oversight toward voluntary, industry-led standards for data pipeline integrity and provenance
      - Ensures transparency without redundant regulatory mandates
    - Incentivizing Data Integrity: structure payment policy to reward "Consent-Aware" and "AI-Ready" infrastructure
      - Establish reimbursement for digital consent orchestration and data quality
      - Incentivizes high-fidelity provider and patient data necessary for autonomous AI
    - Implementation Sandboxes for R&D: foster public-private technical communities to bridge policy and real-world implementation
      - Allow rapid iteration of emerging standards like Model Context Protocol and FHIR
      - Standards refined by market before broad adoption

- Introduction: About Leap Orbit
  - Founded in 2015, AI-first U.S.-based healthcare IT firm solving health data challenges
  - Products include Convergent Provider Data Management platform and RxGov prescription drug monitoring suite
  - Focus on cloud-native architectures (primarily Microsoft Azure) and API-priority design
  - AI embedded as foundational capability in every product
  - Deep commitment to interoperability through HL7 FHIR standards
  - Leadership in HRSA's UDS+ Modernization and work with ASTP, SAMHSA, CDC
  - Convergent platform natively supports FHIR R4 and conforms to Da Vinci PDEX Plan Net IG
  - Successfully standardized over 98% of provider and facility addresses for clients
  - Believes in "glass box" data automation providing transparency and traceable data provenance
  - Third-party validations include EHNAC Privacy & Security Accreditation and DirectTrust Privacy & Security Re-Accreditation

- Part I: Feedback on Primary Approaches for Federal Action

- Regulation: Impact of Current HHS Frameworks on AI Adoption
  - Current regulations focus on clinical end products but neglect upstream pipeline
    - Process of data ingestion, mapping, cleaning, and transformation before reaching AI model is overlooked
  - Limited visibility into how clinical outputs are generated creates trust vacuum among users
  - Recommended approaches:
    - Voluntary Integrity Frameworks to Advance Market-Driven Trust
      - Align with HTI-5 deregulatory goals
      - Pivot away from prescriptive transparency mandates in Health IT Certification Program
      - Encourage voluntary transparency for upstream pipeline (data cleansing, identity matching, provider roster ingestion)
      - Foster environment where traceable data provenance and confidence scores are market-differentiators
      - Market requires provenance transparency to manage risk in deregulated environment
      - Achieved through foundational data engineering with repeatable FHIR ingestion pipelines
      - Metadata and consent enforcement should be "baked" into data stream at point of origin
      - Experience with Convergent and CareLoaDr AI shows trust is prerequisite for automation
      - Users adopt AI when they can see "behind the curtain" to understand how records were matched or credentials verified
    - Enabling AI-Ready Interoperability through Automated Access
      - AI systems require high-fidelity metadata to autonomously use clinical data without "special effort"
      - Strongly support proposed HTI-5 revisions to 45 CFR § 171.102 to include automated means, autonomous AI, and agentic systems in definitions of "access," "exchange," and "use"
      - Recognizes AI as first-class "actor" in health data ecosystem
      - HHS should clarify through sub-regulatory guidance under 21st Century Cures Act information blocking provisions and 45 CFR Part 171:
        - Failure to exchange clinically necessary provenance, integrity, semantic, and consent metadata may constitute limitation on "use"
        - When metadata is reasonably available and necessary for safe automated or clinical use
      - Metadata should meet three criteria to avoid overburdening implementers:
        - Necessary for clinical safety
        - Necessary for interpretability or automation
        - Already representable in existing standards (FHIR Provenance, Consent, AuditEvent, USCDI elements)
      - Minimal metadata set across clinical AI use cases:
        - Provenance metadata (source, attribution)
        - Integrity metadata (currency, completeness, validation status, encounter context)
        - Semantic metadata (code system, terminology version, transformations, unit normalization, original text)
        - Sensitivity metadata (consent status, use restrictions, sensitivity category, redisclosure constraints, revocation timestamp)
        - AI Generation metadata (AI processing indicator, model identifier, confidence score, human validation status)
      - Current information blocking concerns stem from "black box" approach where data exchanged without context
      - Convergent platform and FHIR Da Vinci PDEX Plan Net IG work demonstrates rich metadata enables AI to trace errors to source files and assign confidence scores
      - Transparency prevents misuse of information blocking exceptions by ensuring third-party AI has technical context for safety and effectiveness
      - Recommend HHS promote voluntary, standardized metadata headers for AI-to-AI communication
        - "Cover sheet" for every piece of data exchanged between AI agents
        - Example: clinical AI predicting conditions receives lab results as raw JSON from disparate sources
        - Standardized headers with provenance, quality scores, privacy/consent info enable instant AI digestion without manual mapping
      - Recommend HHS pilot "Structured Insight Extractor" initiative
        - Use LLMs to ingest legacy, semi-structured data (CCDA clinical summaries, Electronic Lab Reporting data)
        - Extract into high-fidelity FHIR resources
        - Automates upstream pipeline to ensure clinical AI models fed standardized data with clear provenance
    - Standardizing Specialty Data as an Innovation Accelerator
      - Prioritize USCDI+ model (as pioneered in SAMHSA/ASTP Behavioral Health Information Technology Pilot)
      - Establish voluntary, predictable "data floors" for specialty care
      - Standardizing specialty bundles via USCDI+ provides predictable data floor
      - Eliminates need for every AI developer to build custom, site-specific ingestion code
      - Lowers barrier to entry for innovative clinical tools
      - HTI-5 Proposed Rule seeks to reset Certification Program scope to prioritize FHIR-based APIs
      - USCDI+ aligns by providing structured context that LLMs and agentic AI struggle to infer reliably
      - Position USCDI+ as market-driven resource improving AI accuracy and reducing human-in-the-loop validation burden

- Reimbursement: Levers to Promote AI Adoption
  - Greatest administrative burden is "data ingestion" tax - cost of cleaning "dirty" data
  - Payment policy should lower this burden and reward quality of underlying data ecosystem
  - Incentivizing "AI-Ready" Data Infrastructure
    - CMS should include "Data Integrity Scores" in Value-Based Care benchmarks or MIPS reporting
    - Organizations demonstrating high accuracy in provider directories and consent frameworks should be eligible for incentive payments
    - HHS should reward targeted applied machine learning improving "ingredients" of clinical record
    - Incentivize using Data Science capacity to automate normalization, confidence scoring, and identity resolution
    - Once underlying data model is stable and identity resolved with high confidence, risk profile for downstream clinical AI significantly lowered
    - Trusted data foundation impacts payers, providers, and patients:
      - Payers: enables compliance with federal mandates, improves operational efficiency
      - One national payer saved estimated $240,000 per year automating CMS 4I reporting, reduced four-week manual process to under 48 hours
      - Providers: reduces administrative burden of attesting information across systems
      - Patients: reliable directory tools build trust and ensure access to care
    - Organizations using autonomous AI for care coordination and reporting should be eligible for higher shared savings or performance bonuses if leveraging standardized, consent-aware infrastructure
  - Incentivizing AI-Driven "Consent Orchestration"
    - AI models for behavioral health integration or sensitive environmental factors require data protected by strict privacy laws (42 CFR Part 2, state mental health statutes)
    - No standardized, automated way to orchestrate consent across systems
    - High-value data often excluded from AI training sets and real-time clinical feeds to avoid compliance risk
    - HHS should incentivize FHIR-native consent management adoption
    - Establish reimbursement codes for AI tools demonstrating "Consent-Awareness"
      - Ability to programmatically respect granular patient restrictions in real-time
    - Incentivizing automated, standards-based consent solutions accelerates AI adoption
    - Provides scalable, real-time mechanism ensuring TEFCA care delivery is always "consent-aware"
    - Reduces legal risk for providers while allowing patients to maintain control over sensitive clinical narratives
    - Recommend "TEFCA Agentic Orchestrator" initiative
      - Deploy autonomous AI agents across TEFCA for real-time, consent-aware data surfacing
      - Filter massive volumes of network-exchanged data to present only relevant, authorized information to clinicians
      - Demonstrates AI moving from noise source to clinical coordination tool

- Research & Development: Public Private Partnerships and Pilot Programs
  - Technical standards and reimbursement incentives alone don't drive adoption; Communities of Practice do
  - Recommend HHS invest in "Implementation Science" through national or regional technical communities
  - Mission: pilot and fine-tune FHIR standards ensuring they are "AI ready" before wider implementation
  - The "Technical Community" Model for R&D
    - Fund permanent National Technical Communities (modeled after HRSA UDS+ UTC)
    - Act as iterative implementation sandboxes
    - Allow federal agencies to test and revise emerging FHIR Implementation Guides and newer standards like Model Context Protocol with real-world vendors and providers
    - HRSA's UDS+ Unified Test Cooperative example:
      - Providers, administrators, and health IT vendors collectively iterated on new automated reporting standard leveraging FHIR
      - High adoption among community health centers in first year of implementation
    - Rapidly iterating FHIR IGs through technical communities feeds "flywheel" of AI adoption
    - Provides bedrock of consistent "AI-ready" data for autonomous agentic AI systems recognized under modernized information blocking rules
  - Public-Private Partnerships for "Specialty AI Bundles"
    - Use pilot programs (similar to BHIT Pilot Program) to research how specialized datasets can be harmonized for AI consumption
      - Behavioral health, sensitive environmental factors affecting health
    - "Pilot-to-Standard" approach ensures federal R&D investments result in deployable, high-impact clinical tools
    - Move beyond "document transport" to context-aware data resources fueling clinical efficiency and reducing provider burnout

- Part II: Responses to Select Questions

- Q2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes to prioritize
  - Payment Policy
    - Establish specific reimbursement pathways recognizing Digital Consent Management as critical clinical support service
    - Reimburse providers utilizing FHIR-native consent orchestration tools
    - Establishing codes for real-time orchestration of patient privacy preferences provides financial bridge
    - Enables providers to move beyond all-or-nothing data exchange
    - Adopt high-value AI interventions fully compliant with 42 CFR Part 2
    - Incentivizes automated, standards-based tools allowing clinical AI to safely scale across TEFCA
  - Programmatic Design
    - Incorporate "Data Readiness and Provenance Scores" into Value-Based Care benchmarks and reporting
    - Organizations currently lack financial incentive to invest in technical metadata (provenance, confidence scores, semantic tags)
    - This metadata makes clinical records consumable by autonomous systems without "special effort"
    - Prioritizing AI-assisted ingestion (e.g., CareLoaDr AI) automates administrative burden of data cleansing
    - Provides "Glass Box" transparency
    - In era of deregulated certification (HTI-5) where "model cards" are being removed, market requires market-driven integrity scores to manage risk
    - Ensures appropriate clinical care delivery

- Q5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
  - HHS should position as "Facilitator of Industry-Led Testing & Benchmarking"
  - Most impactful support: create "Sandbox Environments" for voluntary testing of AI tool trustworthiness and interoperability against real-world, standardized datasets
  - Specific recommendations:
    - Fund National Testing Collectives (modeled after HRSA UDS+ UTC work)
    - Collectives bridge federal policy and private-sector innovation
    - Support "AI-Ready" designation - voluntary testing badge, not mandatory certification, achieved through technical communities
  - Experience as frontline implementers proves industry-driven testing succeeds with phased, incremental approach to iterate standards before broad adoption
  - As HTI-5 expands "access" definition to include autonomous AI and agentic systems, primary barrier becomes identity and trust
  - HHS can support by endorsing open standards that "credential" AI agents
  - Recommend HHS promote development and voluntary adoption of standardized, FHIR-aligned AI Interaction Profile
    - Enables secure, consent-aware retrieval of clinical context
    - Structured return of AI-generated outputs with appropriate provenance and attribution metadata
  - Using 21st Century Cures Act certification program authority and sub-regulatory guidance:
    - Encourage health IT developers to support standardized AI interaction capabilities
    - Prevent proprietary lock-in
    - Ensure auditable, interoperable, safe AI integration
  - HHS should avoid mandating specific private protocols or requiring proprietary algorithm disclosure
  - Instead promote:
    - Open standards alignment
    - Conformance testing
    - Reference implementations through cooperative agreements and federal funding
    - Allow emerging standards like Model Context Protocol to mature
  - MCP acts as "universal interface" for AI agents
    - Allows AI to securely interact with clinical data while maintaining cryptographically signed audit trail
    - Standardized "handshake" between AI agents and data sources like FHIR
    - Ensures context preserved as information moves between disparate systems
  - Prioritizing outcomes over methods through standards alignment and testing enables private sector to "credential" AI tools
  - Every action permissioned, auditable, traceable without foreclosing industry innovation

- Q8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Scalability trap must be solved for AI to move from "bespoke pilot" to scalable clinical utility
  - Currently every new AI deployment requires custom, site-specific data mapping creating significant training debt
  - Enhanced interoperability transforms fragmented information into "clean" data
    - Normalized, attributed, consent-cleared at point of ingestion
  - Widens market opportunities by eliminating "bespoke training debt"
  - Prioritizing repeatable FHIR ingestion pipelines ensures provenance and consent handled programmatically
  - AI teams can focus on targeted applied machine learning for identity resolution and data stability
  - Rather than spending R&D budget on site-specific data cleansing
  - Moving from custom code to repeatable pipelines is key to making clinical AI deflationary and scalable
  - Tools like CareLoaDr AI using "Glass Box" machine learning to automate mapping of complex provider rosters into standardized FHIR formats reduces cost of entry for all AI developers
  - Propose "National Golden Provider Record" initiative
    - Multi-payer pilot using AI to harmonize disparate administrative rosters and credentialing feeds into single trusted source of truth
    - Resolving provider identity at point of ingestion solves scalability trap for market
    - Provides AI developers predictable foundation of accurate provider data
    - Eliminates redundant, site-specific identity resolution
  - Enhanced interoperability fuels research supporting whole-person care
  - "Plus" categories (behavioral health, sensitive environmental factors) often hampered by data gaps
  - Recommend accelerating domain-specific USCDI+ extensions
  - Experience managing SAMHSA/ASTP BHIT Pilot shows "specialty bundles" are essential ingredients for research-grade AI
  - To fuel large-scale research, prioritize Bulk FHIR for population-level datasets
  - Ensures AI models trained on representative, cross-system data without manual intervention

- Conclusion
  - Accelerating AI adoption in clinical care is about getting fundamentals right
  - Sustainable innovation rests on bedrock of accurate, accessible, interoperable core information
  - Challenges with complex, dynamic provider data are significant but not insurmountable
  - Experience with ASTP, HRSA, and CDC demonstrates path to high-performing, AI-enabled health system begins with relentless focus on foundational data architecture
  - Addressing "prerequisites" of identity, consent, and data integrity ensures AI becomes trusted partner rather than source of administrative complexity
  - Committed to tackling complex data challenges and shepherding approaches supporting AI adoption in clinical care
  - Welcomes continued engagement on achieving shared objectives
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Healthcare IT firm specializing in FHIR implementation and provider data management argues that clinical AI adoption is bottlenecked not by algorithms but by "dirty data," urging HHS to incentivize "AI-Ready" infrastructure, voluntary trust frameworks, and technical sandboxes rather than prescriptive mandates.

  - Model after HRSA UDS+ Unified Test Cooperative
