structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Current regulations neglect the upstream data pipeline
  - Focus on clinical end products but ignore data ingestion, mapping, cleaning, and transformation processes
  - Limited visibility into how clinical outputs are generated creates trust vacuum among users

- "Black box" approach to data exchange creates information blocking concerns
  - Data exchanged without context (provenance, confidence scores, semantic tags)
  - Third-party AI software lacks technical context required to be safe and effective

- "Data ingestion tax" is the greatest administrative burden
  - Cost of cleaning "dirty" data before AI can use it
  - Every new AI deployment requires custom, site-specific data mapping creating significant "training debt"

- High-value sensitive data excluded from AI due to consent complexity
  - No standardized, automated way to orchestrate consent across systems
  - Behavioral health and substance use data (42 CFR Part 2) often excluded from AI training sets to avoid compliance risk

- Organizations lack financial incentive to invest in technical metadata
  - No reimbursement pathway for provenance, confidence scores, and semantic tags
  - Makes clinical records difficult for autonomous systems to consume without "special effort"

- Scalability trap prevents AI from moving beyond bespoke pilots
  - Custom code required for each deployment rather than repeatable pipelines
  - AI teams spend R&D budget on site-specific data cleansing instead of innovation
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Achieved 98% standardization rate for provider and facility addresses through Convergent platform
  - Demonstrates that foundational data quality challenges are solvable at scale

- Automated CMS 4I reporting for a national payer
  - Saved estimated $240,000 per year
  - Reduced four-week manual process to under 48 hours

- HRSA UDS+ Unified Test Cooperative achieved high adoption among community health centers in first year
  - Proves that phased, incremental approach to iterating standards with real-world stakeholders drives adoption
  - Technical communities successfully bridge gap between federal policy and private-sector implementation

- Key insight on trust and automation: users only adopt AI in day-to-day use when they can see "behind the curtain"
  - Understanding specifically how a record was matched or credentials verified is prerequisite for adoption
  - "Glass box" transparency is market differentiator in deregulated environment

- Model Context Protocol (MCP) identified as emerging "universal interface" for AI agents
  - Acts as standardized "handshake" between AI agents and data sources like FHIR
  - Maintains cryptographically signed audit trail while preserving context across disparate systems
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The primary bottleneck to clinical AI is no longer the complexity of the algorithms, but the persistent 'trust friction' created by fragmented, unverified data pipelines."

- "Trust is a prerequisite for automation; users are only willing to adopt AI in day-to-day use when they can see 'behind the curtain' of the system to understand specifically how a record was matched or a provider's credentials were verified."

- "The greatest administrative burden in clinical AI adoption is the 'data ingestion' tax – or the cost of cleaning 'dirty' data."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and company background
  - Penguin Ai appreciates the opportunity to comment on HHS's strategy to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care
  - Healthcare-native automation platform founded by executives from UnitedHealth Group, Optum, and Kaiser Permanente
  - Shares HHS's commitment to deploying AI that is transparent, equitable, and clinically safe
  - Explicitly rejects "Black Box" AI in healthcare
  - Platform built on "Glass Box" principle where every AI prediction is traceable back to a specific source of truth within the patient record
    - Applies to medical coding, clinical appeals, and other functions
  - Traceability and Governance standard is essential for building trust among providers and patients
  - Comments draw on experience deploying autonomous "Digital Workers" for healthcare providers

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for healthcare
  - Three primary friction points stifle rapid, secure, and cost-effective AI deployment
  - Access to Financial and Clinical Data (EMR & RCM Integration)
    - Top technical hurdle is inability to seamlessly integrate with and extract high-quality data from EHRs and RCM systems
    - 76% of revenue cycle leaders rate integration between AI tools and core EHR systems as a major or severe challenge
    - Poor data quality, brittle system interfaces, and lack of standardized APIs prevent AI models from functioning effectively
    - AI models are starved of data needed to generate accurate insights
  - Capital Constraints & Misaligned Incentives
    - Legacy fee-for-service regimes often don't account for efficiency gains of AI
    - "Inertia" from these models means innovations lowering net spending often inadvertently reduce provider revenue
    - Margin pressures (e.g., MLR caps) restrict available capital for payers to invest in long-term AI infrastructure
    - Investments may not yield immediate returns
  - Regulatory Uncertainty & Fragmented Governance
    - Absence of standardized, predictable regulatory framework creates novel legal issues
    - Uncertainty around liability, indemnification, and security - particularly for non-medical devices
    - Ambiguity discourages private sector risk-taking
    - Leads to fragmented adoption standards across the industry
  - The Operational Skills Gap
    - Critical deficit in specialized talent capable of managing data ingestion pipelines and model monitoring
    - Clinicians and administrators often lack necessary "AI Literacy" to evaluate robustness and ethical implications
    - Results in implementation paralysis

- Question 1: Recommendations to mitigate barriers
  - HHS should leverage regulatory and reimbursement pillars to foster predictable environment
  - Mandate Administrative Data Interoperability
    - ASTP/ONC must expand interoperability standards (e.g., USCDI) to explicitly include comprehensive administrative and financial data classes
    - Certification requirements should enforce standardized API access (like FHIR) to both EHR and RCM systems
    - Ensures AI tools can securely access necessary data without vendor friction
  - Modernize Reimbursement
    - Continue shift away from fee-for-service regimes
    - Include payment policies that reward "high-value" AI interventions
    - Align federal incentives so AI is deployed to enhance productivity and reduce burden, not just increase volume
  - Establish a Predictable Regulatory Posture
    - Create regulatory stance that is well-understood, predictable, and proportionate to risks presented
    - HHS must clarify liability and "safe harbor" frameworks for administrative AI tools to enable rapid innovation
  - Support Workforce Readiness
    - Fund development of standardized role-based training and "implementation science"
    - Ensure clinicians and administrators can integrate and oversee high-impact AI systems safely

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - HHS should prioritize shifts that clearly distinguish between Clinical AI (diagnostic/treatment) and Administrative AI (operational/revenue cycle)
  - Critical to align financial rewards with adoption of "high-value" AI that reduces administrative waste
  - Establish regulatory "safe harbor" for transparent, audit-ready administrative tools
  - Reducing administrative burden through automation returns time and energy to clinical care

- Question 2, Part 1: Programmatic Design Changes to Incentivize AI
  - Recommendation 1: Align MIPS Incentives with AI-Driven Burden Reduction
    - Proposed Change (42 CFR Part 414): Revisit Merit-based Incentive Payment System (MIPS), specifically Improvement Activities performance category (42 CFR § 414.1305 and § 414.1380)
    - Actions:
      - Explicitly classify adoption of "AI-assisted Administrative Automation" as High-Weighted Improvement Activity
        - Includes automated coding, chart abstraction, prior authorization submission
      - Create specific sub-category for technologies demonstrating measurable reduction in "time-to-care" or administrative overhead costs
    - Rationale: Current MIPS categories heavily favor clinical data reporting, which often adds to provider burden
      - Financially rewarding AI tools that remove administrative tasks accelerates deployment of technology combating clinician burnout and operational inefficiency
  - Recommendation 2: Transition from Fee-for-Service to Value-Based AI Models
    - Proposed Change (CMMI Authority): CMMI should pilot "Shared Savings for Administrative Efficiency" model building on ACCESS program and WISeR model goals
    - Action: Instead of reimbursing AI as discrete service, allow providers to retain higher percentage of shared savings in APMs if they use certified AI agents to reduce RCM and utilization review costs
    - Rationale: Applauds CMS's ongoing efforts but legacy fee-for-service creates "inertia" diminishing AI's promise
      - If providers reimbursed strictly for volume, little financial incentive to use AI for efficiency
      - Administrative waste estimated at 25% of US healthcare spend
      - Shared savings models targeting administrative waste will supercharge WISeR objectives and drive rapid market adoption

- Question 2, Part 2: Regulatory Revisions to Augment AI Development
  - Recommendation 3: Clarify Scope to Create "Presumption of Safety" for Administrative AI
    - Proposed Change (Guidance on HTI-1 & 45 CFR Part 170): HHS and ASTP/ONC should clear regulatory confusion by establishing distinct boundary between Predictive DSI for clinical care and Administrative AI for RCM, coding, and documentation
    - Action: Issue guidance clarifying AI tools used solely for Payment and Health Care Operations (45 CFR § 164.501) fall outside "Predictive DSI" definition (45 CFR § 170.315(b)(11)) and not subject to "clinical validity" testing requirements
    - Standard to qualify for exclusion: AI must use "Glass Box" / Deterministic Traceability architecture
      - Links every output (e.g., billing code) back to specific, human-verified source in medical record
      - Allows for human override
      - Example: ICD-10 code deterministically hyperlinked to specific sentence in unstructured clinical note
    - Rationale: Administrative errors (wrong billing code) fundamentally different from clinical errors (missed diagnosis)
      - Administrative errors are financial, reversible, and auditable
      - Subjecting operational algorithms to "clinical safety" standards creates regulatory ambiguity that stalls innovation
      - Clarifying transparent administrative tools are not "Predictive DSIs" creates "Safe Harbor" encouraging market to adopt audit-ready automation immediately
  - Recommendation 4: Expand Interoperability Standards for Administrative Data
    - Proposed Change (45 CFR Part 170 - ONC Health IT Certification): Update USCDI standards to support AI-driven administrative workflows
    - Action: Expand USCDI as part of USCDI v7 to include dedicated "Administrative Automation" domain, or expand existing "Health Insurance Information" domain
      - Specifics: Standardize data elements for Prior Authorization Criteria, Payer Denial Reasons, and Claim Status metadata
      - Currently these often exist as unstructured text or proprietary codes
    - Rationale: AI agents require structured data to function deterministically
      - If denial reason is unstructured text, AI must "guess," increasing hallucination risk
      - Standardizing "Health Insurance Information" domain enables AI agents to read claim "state" deterministically
      - Fuels development of robust, interoperable AI solutions for clinical and administrative care
  - Recommendation 5: Modernize HIPAA for "Privacy-by-Design" AI
    - Proposed Change (45 CFR Parts 160 and 164 - HIPAA Privacy Rule): Revisit interpretation of "Minimum Necessary" standards regarding AI training on de-identified data
    - Action: Provide specific safe harbor for "Privacy-by-Design" AI architectures using automated de-identification and edge-processing to scrub PHI before data enters model training environment
    - Rationale: Protecting patient confidentiality is paramount, but ambiguity regarding data "ingestion" for AI improvement leads to implementation paralysis
      - Clear guidance incentivizing architectural privacy (scrubbing data at the edge) rather than contractual privacy (BAAs alone) will augment industry's ability to develop safer, more capable AI models

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - HHS identifies AI software assisting clinical care but not meeting FDA medical device definition as critical innovation area
  - Examples of administrative and general-purpose tools:
    - Ambient Scribes: AI converting spoken clinician-patient dialogue into clinical notes
    - Administrative Automators: Tools for RCM, medical coding, or scheduling
    - Workflow Support: Software managing general office operations or secure messaging platforms
  - HHS should prioritize evaluation methods bridging technical performance and real-world administrative impact
    - Emphasize operational ROI, human-in-the-loop oversight, and long-term model reliability

- Question 4: Evaluation Methods for Digital Workers
  - Evaluation must move beyond simple accuracy to focus on safety and efficiency
  - Pre-deployment: Validation with Real-World Data (RWD)
    - Use secure platforms to validate models against millions of de-identified patient records
    - Include unstructured data to ensure AI can handle clinical complexity before going live
  - Post-deployment: Real-Time Performance Monitoring
    - Implement continuous monitoring to track KPIs such as "cost-to-collect," denial rates, and time-to-authorization
  - Iterative Robustness Testing
    - Regularly test models for "out-of-distribution" robustness
    - Ensure models don't fail when payer rules or clinical documentation patterns change

- Question 4: Key Metrics for Clinical Care
  - Metrics must demonstrate both financial and operational value
  - Operational Throughput: Measuring speed of administrative tasks, such as reducing prior authorization time significantly
  - Accuracy and Correctness: Maintaining high rates of clinical correctness (e.g., 95.5%) in specialized models to ensure patient safety
  - Revenue Integrity: Tracking percentage of appeals overturned and reduction in A/R backlog
  - Algorithmic Fairness: Using subgroup performance metrics to detect and correct bias, ensuring AI doesn't discriminate based on demographic data

- Question 4: Recommendations for HHS Support
  - Foster "healthcare-native" approach to AI evaluation and deployment
  - Human-Centered Evaluation: The "Human-in-the-Loop" Model
    - Establish Feedback Loops: Support development of systems enabling human teams to provide real-time feedback for retraining AI models and refining workflows
    - Prioritize Decision Support: Incentivize AI that "distills and presents" information to human decision-makers, ensuring final clinical and financial decisions remain evidence-based and compassionate
  - Impactful HHS Support Mechanisms
    - Cooperative Research and Development Agreements (CRADAs): Use CRADAs to develop standardized, de-identified datasets startups can use for model validation
    - Grants for Workforce Training: Provide grants to help providers and payers close "skills gap" by training staff to manage and audit agentic AI systems
    - Prize Competitions for Interoperability: Use prize competitions to incentivize creation of "system-agnostic" AI tools that plug into existing EHRs and payer portals without requiring total system "rip and replace"

- Question 7: Roles and decision makers with most influence on AI adoption
  - Successful AI adoption is not merely technical challenge but governance and leadership imperative
  - While executive leadership sets vision, actual integration depends on broader circle of stakeholders managing intersection of clinical necessity, technical infrastructure, and financial viability

- Question 7: The Expanded Circle of Influence
  - C-Suite provides overarching strategy, but successful adoption hinges on specialized roles managing day-to-day operational risks
  - For Providers (Hospitals & Health Systems):
    - Head of Revenue Cycle Management (RCM): Primary driver for adoption due to responsibility for financial health, medical coding efficiency, and billing accuracy
    - VP of Revenue Cycle and CFO: Prioritize AI solutions delivering measurable ROI by reducing "cost-to-collect" and minimizing claim denials
    - Director of Clinician Operations: Crucial for ensuring AI tools provide genuine relief for documentation burdens rather than introducing new administrative tasks
  - For Payers (Insurance Companies):
    - VP of Provider Network Strategy: Influences adoption of tools streamlining data exchange between payers and providers
    - VP of Utilization Management & CMO: Focus on AI-driven prior authorization reviews to ensure medical necessity and accelerate care delivery
    - Director of Regulatory Compliance: Exercises significant oversight to ensure AI data ingestion meets strict HIPAA and civil rights standards

- Question 7: Recommendations for Overcoming Primary Administrative Hurdles
  - HHS must take active role in mitigating "non-technical" barriers to innovation
  - Mitigate the "Skills Gap"
    - Provide support for healthcare organizations to build internal expertise
    - Reduce sole reliance on vendors, fostering culture of trust and effective monitoring
  - Relieve Budgetary Constraints
    - Payers: Address limitations posed by Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) growth to allow necessary technology investments
    - Providers: Create financial pathways to offset high upfront costs and "rip and replace" infrastructure projects in environment of reduced payment rates
  - Address Governance and Liability "Inertia"
    - Clear Liability Guidelines: Establish legal framework clarifying responsibility for AI errors (clinician vs. vendor) to eliminate "wait and see" culture
    - Workflow Transformation Support: Provide guidance for transitioning from manual, legacy workflows to "agentic" systems, recognizing significant change management required
  - Standardize AI Data Provenance and Payloads
    - AI models highly capable of extracting data from unstructured sources (faxes, PDFs, voice)
    - Technical bottleneck is integrating output into core systems
    - Receiving EHR system doesn't speak English; it speaks in discrete codes (SNOMED, ICD-10)
    - If every AI vendor reads a PDF and maps "heart attack" to different code, or outputs data in proprietary JSON format, hospital IT must build custom "plumbing" for every AI tool
    - ASTP should invest in standards (e.g., expanding FHIR Provenance resources) standardizing how AI-extracted data, confidence scores, and source-document traceability are communicated to EHRs
    - Enables plug-and-play interoperability without custom mapping

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Commends ASTP/ONC and CMS for monumental work establishing current Interoperability Framework
  - Interoperability standards (FHIR and USCDI) have succeeded in unlocking "read" access to patient records
  - Market remains constrained by severe asymmetry in "write" access
  - Large EHR and RCM vendors maintain technical and contractual moats restricting third-party AI from pushing insights directly into native workflows
  - "Pull vs. Push" asymmetry relegates new AI vendors to "read-only" data supporting workflows, dashboards, or side-car applications
  - Resulting workflow fragmentation forces clinicians and coders into "swivel-chair" processes
    - Increases cognitive load and hinders adoption of even most accurate AI models
  - Focus must shift from basic data access to:
    - Establishing standards for seamless "write-back" capabilities
    - Standardizing administrative data
    - Creating objective benchmarking tools

- Question 8: Recommendations
  - Recommendation 1: Mandate "Write-Back" Standards for Native Workflow Integration
    - Action: ASTP should prioritize certification criteria (e.g., in upcoming HTI rulemakings) mandating standardized, bi-directional API capabilities for clinical and administrative workflows
      - Must include policies limiting fees legacy vendors can charge third parties for "write" access
    - Rationale: Accelerating AI requires ability to push insights directly into user's native screen
      - Examples: AI-generated Prior Authorization payload, extracted ICD-10 code, pre-visit clinical briefing
      - Without affordable, standardized write-back capabilities, AI cannot achieve primary goal of reducing administrative burden
  - Recommendation 2: Expand USCDI to Include Structured Administrative Data
    - Action: Widen USCDI and USCDI+ frameworks to formally standardize exchange of administrative and financial data
      - Claims Data, Payer Denial Reasons, Prior Authorization Criteria, Claim Status
    - Rationale: AI development bogged down by need to parse unstructured faxes, PDFs, and legacy X12 EDI formats
      - Standardizing administrative elements allows AI to function deterministically across different EHRs and RCMs
      - Expands market opportunities beyond clinical diagnostic AI into high-value back-office automation
  - Recommendation 3: Establish "Glass Box" Provenance Standards
    - Action: ASTP should establish FHIR Provenance standards specifically for AI-generated data
      - Any AI insight pushed into certified health IT system must include metadata linking output directly to human-verified source
      - Example: coordinate mapping highlighting exact sentence in clinical note justifying suggested code
    - Rationale: Trust is major barrier to adoption
      - If AI writes data into EHR, downstream users need to know exactly how conclusion was reached
      - Standardizing AI provenance allows seamless "Human-in-the-Loop" review without disrupting workflow
  - Recommendation 4: Develop Open-Source "Gold Standard" Benchmarking Datasets
    - Action: HHS should support creation of open-source, de-identified datasets combining longitudinal clinical records with claims and payment data
    - Rationale: Research and market competition limited by lack of objective benchmarking tools outside proprietary EHR silos
      - Developers need access to diverse, standardized datasets to evaluate clinical robustness and financial "cost-to-collect" impact
      - Ensures safer and more effective solutions

- Conclusion
  - Applauds ASTP and ONC for leading this conversation
  - Future of healthcare AI lies not in "magic box" solutions but in transparent, auditable, and healthcare-native systems that reduce burden and improve outcomes
  - Welcomes opportunity to discuss "Glass Box" architecture and Governance frameworks further

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Healthcare AI vendor founded by industry veterans advocates for "Glass Box" transparency standards, regulatory safe harbors for administrative AI, and expanded interoperability requirements to unlock write-back capabilities that would let AI actually reduce burden rather than just generate insights.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Penguin Ai (Fawad Butt, CEO)
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Healthcare-native automation platform founded by executives from UnitedHealth Group, Optum, and Kaiser Permanente; deploys autonomous "Digital Workers" for healthcare providers
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected by regulatory frameworks governing administrative AI tools, interoperability standards, and certification requirements for health IT systems

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We explicitly reject "Black Box" AI in healthcare and have built our platform on "Glass Box" principles where every AI prediction is traceable back to a specific source of truth in the patient record. HHS must create clear regulatory distinctions between clinical AI and administrative AI, establishing safe harbors for transparent, audit-ready administrative tools while mandating the interoperability standards—especially write-back capabilities—that would allow AI to actually reduce burden rather than just generate insights that clinicians must manually transfer.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- **Create regulatory safe harbor for administrative AI**
  - Issue guidance clarifying that AI tools used solely for Payment and Health Care Operations fall outside "Predictive DSI" definition
  - Require "Glass Box" architecture linking every output to human-verified source in medical record
  - Recognize that administrative errors are financial, reversible, and auditable—fundamentally different from clinical errors

- **Mandate bi-directional interoperability standards**
  - Prioritize certification criteria requiring standardized write-back API capabilities for clinical and administrative workflows
  - Limit fees legacy vendors can charge third parties for "write" access
  - Establish FHIR Provenance standards specifically for AI-generated data

- **Expand USCDI to include administrative data**
  - Add dedicated "Administrative Automation" domain or expand "Health Insurance Information" domain in USCDI v7
  - Standardize data elements for Prior Authorization Criteria, Payer Denial Reasons, and Claim Status metadata
  - Enable AI to function deterministically rather than "guessing" from unstructured text

- **Align MIPS incentives with AI-driven burden reduction**
  - Classify AI-assisted administrative automation as High-Weighted Improvement Activity (42 CFR § 414.1305 and § 414.1380)
  - Create sub-category for technologies demonstrating measurable reduction in "time-to-care" or administrative overhead

- **Pilot shared savings for administrative efficiency**
  - CMMI should pilot model allowing providers to retain higher percentage of shared savings when using certified AI agents to reduce RCM costs
  - Target administrative waste estimated at 25% of US healthcare spend

- **Modernize HIPAA for "Privacy-by-Design" AI**
  - Provide safe harbor for architectures using automated de-identification and edge-processing to scrub PHI before model training
  - Incentivize architectural privacy rather than relying solely on BAAs

- **Standardize AI data provenance and payloads**
  - Invest in standards (e.g., expanding FHIR Provenance resources) for how AI-extracted data, confidence scores, and source-document traceability are communicated to EHRs
  - Enable plug-and-play interoperability without custom mapping

- **Support workforce readiness and benchmarking**
  - Fund standardized role-based training and "implementation science"
  - Use CRADAs to develop standardized, de-identified datasets for model validation
  - Create prize competitions for "system-agnostic" AI tools

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- **"Pull vs. Push" interoperability asymmetry**
  - Current standards unlock "read" access but large EHR/RCM vendors maintain moats restricting "write" access
  - New AI vendors relegated to dashboards and side-car applications
  - Forces clinicians into "swivel-chair" processes that increase cognitive load

- **Regulatory ambiguity stalling administrative AI adoption**
  - Absence of clear distinction between clinical and administrative AI creates uncertainty
  - Subjecting operational algorithms to "clinical safety" standards inappropriate for reversible, auditable financial errors
  - Novel legal issues around liability and indemnification discourage risk-taking

- **Data integration remains the top technical hurdle**
  - 76% of revenue cycle leaders rate EHR integration as major or severe challenge
  - Poor data quality, brittle interfaces, and lack of standardized APIs starve AI models
  - Administrative data often exists as unstructured text or proprietary codes, forcing AI to "guess"

- **Fee-for-service "inertia" undermines AI value proposition**
  - Innovations lowering net spending often inadvertently reduce provider revenue
  - Little financial incentive to use AI for efficiency when reimbursed strictly for volume
  - MLR caps restrict payer capital for long-term AI infrastructure investment

- **Critical skills gap creates implementation paralysis**
  - Deficit in talent capable of managing data ingestion pipelines and model monitoring
  - Clinicians and administrators lack "AI Literacy" to evaluate robustness and ethical implications

- **Standardization gap for AI outputs**
  - EHR systems speak in discrete codes (SNOMED, ICD-10), not English
  - If every AI vendor maps "heart attack" to different codes or uses proprietary JSON formats, hospitals must build custom "plumbing" for each tool

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- **The "Glass Box" architecture concept**
  - Every AI output (e.g., ICD-10 code) is deterministically hyperlinked to the specific sentence in the unstructured clinical note that justifies it
  - Enables human-in-the-loop review without disrupting workflow
  - Represents alternative to "Black Box" AI that builds trust through traceability

- **Insight on the real interoperability bottleneck**
  - AI models are highly capable of extracting data from unstructured sources (faxes, PDFs, voice)
  - The actual bottleneck is integrating that output into core systems
  - "The receiving EHR system doesn't speak English; it speaks in discrete codes"

- **Mapping the expanded circle of influence for AI adoption**
  - Beyond C-suite, identifies specific roles driving adoption: Head of RCM, VP of Revenue Cycle, Director of Clinician Operations
  - For payers: VP of Provider Network Strategy, VP of Utilization Management, Director of Regulatory Compliance
  - Successful adoption depends on stakeholders managing intersection of clinical necessity, technical infrastructure, and financial viability

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "We explicitly reject the use of 'Black Box' AI in healthcare. Our platform is built on the principle of the 'Glass Box' where every AI prediction, from medical coding to clinical appeals, is traceable back to a specific source of truth within the patient record."

- "Administrative errors (e.g., a wrong billing code) are fundamentally different from clinical errors (e.g., a missed diagnosis). They are financial, reversible, and auditable."

- "The receiving EHR system doesn't speak English; it speaks in discrete codes (SNOMED, ICD-10). If every AI vendor reads a .pdf and maps 'heart attack' to a different code, or outputs the data in a proprietary JSON format, the hospital's IT team has to build custom 'plumbing' for every single AI tool."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- ECRI submitting comments on HHS RFI regarding accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
- ECRI is an independent nonprofit advancing evidence-based practices to improve patient safety, healthcare quality, and cost-effectiveness
- Supports ensuring AI tools in clinical environments are safe, effective, transparent, and worthy of public trust

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector AI innovation and adoption
  - Greatest barriers are not algorithmic limitations but gaps in real-world readiness
  - Specific barriers identified:
    - Procurement processes that don't reward transparency or safety
    - Limited independent validation
    - Fragmented data systems
    - Poor interoperability
    - Inadequate post-implementation monitoring
    - Cybersecurity and reliability vulnerabilities
    - Unclear accountability when AI influences care
  - Highly publicized failures of widely deployed prediction tools demonstrate AI adoption without evidence weakens trust and stalls progress
    - Example: External validation of EPIC's AI-enabled sepsis prediction program showed poor real-world performance
  - America gains most when innovation is tested, proven, and trusted—not when shortcomings are discovered post-deployment
  - Recent analysis highlights clear reimbursement pathways and integrating AI into clinical practice guidelines are essential adoption enablers

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - HHS can strengthen adoption of clinically valuable AI by rewarding transparency, accountability, and sustained performance
  - Recommended actions:
    - Communicate, support, and enforce expectations for clinical AI performance and accountability
    - Expand reimbursement pathways for validated clinical AI interventions
    - Promote transparency standards rewarding vendors who clearly communicate capabilities, limitations, and performance
    - Clarify expectations for managing AI updates and performance drift over time
  - These measures don't restrict innovation—they differentiate serious innovators from opportunistic ones

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Non-medical AI tools (like general-purpose LLM chatbots) create a distinct governance gap
    - Can influence clinical decisions and patient behavior
    - Often sit outside accountability, validation, and lifecycle controls healthcare organizations expect
  - Unique legal and implementation issues:
    - Accountability gaps
      - Diffused liability: Vendor disclaims clinical use, health system assumes tool is "just informational," but clinicians use it for clinically actionable queries—when harm occurs, responsibility is unclear
      - Indemnification mismatches: Contracts often lack meaningful indemnity for clinical harm, hallucinated content, or latent model defects—no one is responsible and improvements aren't stimulated
    - Unvalidated "clinical advice"
      - LLM outputs can be very convincing even when inaccurate, creating automation bias and subtle influence even when clinicians think they're not being influenced
    - Inappropriate generalization
      - Performance varies by context, population, language, and clinical domain
      - Without transparent limitations, LLMs can amplify disparities or mislead in edge cases
    - Update opacity and performance drift
      - Unannounced model changes can alter behavior without warning, undermining clinical governance, training, and auditability
      - Lack of stable versioning makes incident investigation difficult—"What would the model have said on the date of the incident?" may be unknowable
    - Traceability, audit, and record integrity
      - Chat interactions may not be logged in clinically meaningful ways, or logs stored outside organization's control
    - Privacy, security, and secondary use
      - Prompt content may include PHI, sometimes inadvertently
      - Ambiguity around data retention, model training on user data, potential data transfer outside U.S. complicates compliance and risk
      - Tool integrations (browser, plugins, AI agents) expand attack surface and create new routes for data exfiltration or manipulation
      - Cybersecurity risks like prompt injection and tool hijacking

  - Recommended HHS actions:
    - Set enforceable expectations for clinical-impact AI regardless of device status
      - If a system materially influences clinical decisions, it should meet baseline expectations for:
        - Transparency (intended use, verification/validation test methods, limitations, known failure modes)
        - Traceability (versioning, audit logs, data provenance)
        - Lifecycle governance (change management, monitoring, incident reporting)
        - Truthful performance claims (accurate metrics across populations and subpopulations)
    - Use payment and program levers to incentivize governance
      - Through CMS programs, condition participation or reimbursement on healthcare organizations demonstrating they:
        - Maintain an AI inventory (including non-device tools like LLMs)
        - Apply risk-tiered evaluation (piloting) prior to use
        - Implement ongoing monitoring and user training
        - Document when and how AI is used in care processes
        - Have an error reporting program in place
    - Provide standard procurement and contracting language
      - Model contract clauses should cover:
        - Minimum logging/audit requirements
        - Versioning and advance notice of model updates
        - Data use and retention limits (including "no training on our data" unless explicit)
        - Vendor obligations for safety incidents and vulnerability disclosure
        - Realistic indemnification aligned to risk
    - Clarify privacy/security guardrails for LLM workflows
      - Through HHS Office for Civil Rights and ONC, issue practical guidance on:
        - What constitutes acceptable handling of PHI in prompts
        - HIPAA-compliant BAAs and subcontractor chains for LLM vendors who could encounter PHI
        - Minimum security controls for AI-enabled programs with ability to influence patient care (those connected to EHRs)
        - Safe defaults (redaction, local processing options, retention minimization)
    - Create enforceable "truth-in-claims" regime for AI with clinical capabilities
      - Coordinate with FTC and FDA so vendors cannot imply clinical-grade abilities without:
        - Disclosing evaluation methods
        - Specifying populations/settings tested
        - Accurately reporting performance metrics
        - Reporting/disclosing known limitations and uncertainty
  - Non-medical AI devices can shape clinical decisions at scale while escaping normal patient protections
  - HHS doesn't need to micromanage innovation but should communicate expectations, support adoption with practical guardrails, and enforce accountability
  - Strong accountability builds confidence for clinicians, patients, and investors alike

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - ECRI believes HHS should support a "prove-it-first, monitor-it-always" approach for any AI tool capable of influencing patient care
  - Pre-deployment information requirements for vendors:
    - Intended-use, task-based validation
      - Evaluate system on specific clinical tasks it will support
      - Include realistic environment features (representative users, settings, EHR-integrated contexts)
    - Reference-grounded testing
      - Explicitly test convincing but wrong inputs and report whether model agrees or corrects them
      - If device uses LLMs to summarize guidelines or literature, require source traceability and test citation fidelity
    - Human factors and workflow simulation
      - Conduct usability tests in simulated clinical scenarios (time pressure, interruptions)
    - Safety-focused red teaming
      - Structured red-team suites for:
        - Hallucinations (fabricated facts, contraindications)
        - Unsafe omissions (missed warnings, missing follow-up)
        - Over-confident tone
        - Prompt injection/tool hijacking if connected to systems
        - PHI leakage and unintended memorization behaviors
      - Test guardrail behavior—when does it refuse vs. answer with uncertainty vs. answer confidently, and is that acceptable?
      - Assess how effectively uncertainty is communicated to end user
    - Subgroup performance
      - Stratify results by patient factors relevant to use case (age, language, health literacy proxies, comorbidity complexity)
      - Stratify by clinical setting
  - Post-deployment monitoring (HHS should use CMS payment programs to support and incentivize):
    - Continuous monitoring with change control
      - Track versioning and model-change documentation
      - Trigger re-validation when model updates, retrieval sources change, prompts/templates change, or workflow integration changes
    - Real-world performance surveillance
      - Track errors and near-misses tied to AI outputs with CAPA proceedings
      - Establish pathway for clinician feedback ("this is wrong/unsafe") that feeds governance review
      - Ensure reporting structure encourages error-reporting databases like FDA's MAUDE
    - Drift and distribution shift monitoring programs
      - Monitor shifts in case mix, language use, documentation templates, seasonal patterns, new guidelines/drug approvals, subpopulation performance
      - Re-test on rolling benchmark set reflecting current practice
    - Auditable provenance
      - Capture prompt context, model/version, retrieved sources, output, and downstream action (accepted/edited/ignored)
      - Support incident review capability
  - Rigorous evaluation, continuous monitoring, and accountability protects American patients, supports American clinicians, and prevents healthcare system from becoming dependent on opaque technologies we cannot measure, audit, or control

- Question 5: How HHS can support private sector activities
  - Independent testing and certification help strongest products rise
  - ECRI encourages HHS to support independent programs that evaluate:
    - AI robustness
    - Transparency
    - Equity of performance
    - Monitoring readiness
  - Gives buyers confidence and innovators a fair playing field

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or fallen short of expectations
  - Best path: Deploy AI where it demonstrably reduces harm or burden, prove it in real-world practice, hold vendors and deployers accountable when performance drifts
  - U.S. patients shouldn't become test bed for opaque systems without mechanisms to be monitored, audited, or controlled
  - AI performs best when:
    - Task is clearly defined and aligned with user needs
    - Outcomes are measurable
    - Integration is thoughtfully designed
  - Examples of AI working well:
    - Medical imaging applications and time-critical workflows
      - Majority of FDA-cleared AI/ML-enabled devices are in medical imaging
      - Health systems using these for detection/triage and workflow prioritization
      - Strongest value case is speed and consistency for narrow tasks (e.g., flagging suspected findings for urgent review), not replacing clinician judgment
    - Documentation support (AI-enabled ambient scribe use cases)
      - Showing credible benefits in reducing documentation burden and burnout
      - Returning time to clinicians
  - AI struggles when:
    - Models are opaque
    - Alerts overwhelm clinicians
    - Performance erodes over time
  - Examples where AI is struggling:
    - High-stakes clinical prediction without rigorous external validation and monitoring
      - Widely used proprietary sepsis prediction model performed substantially worse than expected in real-world evaluation
      - Missed many true cases and generated false alerts
      - Illustrates risk of deploying models that look good on paper but fail under local workflows and data patterns
    - Generalizability and "silent failure modes"
      - Clinical AI can fail unevenly across populations when training data and evaluation don't reflect real patients and workflows
      - Risks worse outcomes for Americans
    - LLM-enabled tools in clinical settings (non-medical device deployments)
      - Can be confidently wrong, hard to audit, hard to assign accountability
      - Even when positioned as "assistive," can insert subtle bias into decisions, produce plausible but incorrect content, change behavior after updates without traceability
      - Can present legal and consent risks when workflows aren't designed around transparency and patient permission
      - Nothing stifles innovation and growth like litigation
  - America-first approach means insisting on proof, transparency, and accountability before scaling
  - AI should strengthen, not replace, clinical judgment
  - Protect patients from preventable harm
  - Deliver measurable value to healthcare system Americans depend on
  - Prioritize tools that perform reliably in real-world care, enforce clear expectations when they fall short, refuse to outsource trust to opaque technologies

- Question 7: Roles and decision makers influencing AI adoption
  - Adoption decisions should involve:
    - Clinical leaders
    - IT leaders
    - Safety teams
    - Procurement
    - Administration
    - Legal counsel
  - Clear national expectations would help stakeholders align around value rather than novelty

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Interoperability is a strategic asset—AI cannot outperform the data it depends on
  - ECRI encourages continued federal leadership in data standards and exchange
  - Enables U.S. innovators to build tools that work anywhere in the country, and potentially beyond
  - ONC HTI-1 final rule is a meaningful step toward this goal

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns
  - Patients want AI that:
    - Reduces wait times
    - Improves safety
    - Helps clinicians focus on care, not screens
  - Patients worry about:
    - Loss of privacy
    - Unfair or unexplained decisions
    - Errors without recourse
  - Public trust is not a barrier to innovation—it's a prerequisite for success at scale
  - Central patient question: Is AI safe, transparent, and accountable? Does someone remain clearly responsible when AI influences care?
  - Effective HHS approach should ensure AI:
    - Strengthens clinical relationships
    - Protects patient autonomy and privacy
    - Delivers measurable benefits patients can actually see and feel
    - Doesn't introduce new risks hidden behind technical complexity

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - HHS can accelerate adoption through targeted research validating AI's real-world clinical value
  - AI Software for Improving Outpatient Scheduling and Patient Chart Management
    - Uses nonlinear algorithms and machine learning for computer-based tasks
    - Nonclinical applications include optimizing patient scheduling and identifying patient information in medical records
    - Appears to predict patient behaviors (e.g., no-shows) and appointment length, potentially creating scheduling efficiencies
    - Available studies are highly heterogeneous, use different AI models, lack patient outcome reporting
    - Most studies lacked comparison to other methods—unclear how well AI works vs. traditional approaches
    - Large, multicenter studies comparing AI with traditional methods and reporting patient-oriented outcomes are needed
  - AI-based Early Warning Systems for Assessing Sepsis Risk
    - Several AI prediction tools on market intended to identify hospitalized patients at high sepsis risk
    - TREWS:
      - Evidence from two cohort studies suggests can reduce sepsis mortality by enabling earlier diagnosis and treatment
      - Data too limited in quantity and quality to support conclusions
      - Diagnostic accuracy data at risk of bias and need independent validation
      - Additional prospective studies needed to confirm accuracy and compare outcomes with and without TREWS
    - Immunoscore:
      - Evidence from diagnostic cohort study shows can predict imminent sepsis onset with moderate accuracy
      - Whether this translates to time-to-treatment, morbidity, and mortality reduction is unclear—no studies report these outcomes
      - Supports only low-confidence conclusions—no independent validation studies available
      - Additional diagnostic performance studies and longitudinal studies comparing management with and without ImmunoScore needed
  - AI Use in Histopathology and Cytology Practice
    - Digitization of glass slides into high-resolution whole-slide images (WSIs) has facilitated AI application
    - Advances in computational power, data sharing, and AI techniques (machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, NLP) have transformed the field
    - AI aids in large dataset analysis, anomaly detection, tumor grading, workflow optimization
    - Alleviates burden on pathologists while enhancing diagnostic accuracy and efficiency
    - Critical healthcare challenges underscore need for AI adoption:
      - Misdiagnosis remains major issue leading to patient harm, disabilities, deaths
      - AI can provide consistent, accurate, timely diagnostic insights, reducing errors and complementing human expertise
      - AI pivotal in identifying high-risk patients, improving resource allocation, addressing global pathologist shortage
      - Shortage exacerbated by aging workforce and declining interest in specialty
      - AI can automate repetitive tasks, improve accuracy, ensure equitable access
    - Evidence indicates AI-enabled diagnostic reviews of WSI demonstrate high sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy relative to manual review across various malignancies (gastric, breast, prostate cancer)
    - Data for AI-enabled histopathologic diagnosis of other conditions is limited
    - Clinical utility studies show substantial agreement between AI-assisted and manual reviews, suggest reduced review times, may improve detection of missed diagnoses
  - AI-Assisted Quantitative MRI for Monitoring Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Progression
    - MRI use for monitoring MS limited by lengthy protocols, error-prone procedures for identifying disease markers, limited predictive value of existing imaging biomarkers
    - Several AI-based software systems may address these problems and enhance diagnostic accuracy, refine treatment approaches, improve patient outcomes
    - One systematic review examines AI modalities for MS diagnosis based on 23 case-control studies (2016-2023)
    - One SR assesses commercially available AI quantitative volumetric reporting tools
    - Two diagnostic cohort studies compare AI-assisted radiologic reports (Pixly.Neuro.MS, iQ-Solutions MS) with conventional reports
    - Five case series use two different AI software packages (iQ-Solutions MS, icobrain MS) to monitor MS progression
    - Case-control SR reports high sensitivity and specificity for AI programs
    - In two studies comparing AI with standard radiology reports, AI improved new lesion detection
    - AI modalities' effect on patient outcomes is unclear
    - Five case series using AI to monitor disease progression reported mixed results
    - Many studies rely on retrospective data collection—high risk of bias
    - Commercially available AI software packages not adequately tested in clinical situations
    - Additional high-quality studies needed to compare software packages in clinical conditions
  - AI Software for Evaluating Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)
    - Emerging class of AI software applications to facilitate CAD diagnosis and evaluation with coronary CT angiography
    - Many use interactive 3-D rendering of patient's coronary vasculature
    - Clinicians can assess individual lesion risk and overall disease burden
    - Clinical literature consistently highlights AI products for CAD:
      - Improve diagnostic accuracy
      - Streamline clinical workflows
      - Enhance patient risk stratification compared to traditional methods
    - Findings include reduction of invasive procedures, improved mortality rates, higher prognostic value than traditional risk scores or stenosis severity alone
    - More prospective studies reporting actual outcomes needed to validate clinical utility and optimal place in CAD management pathway
    - Evidence base lacks long-term outcomes data, real-world generalizability, and cost-effectiveness studies

- Conclusion
  - AI is actively transforming healthcare
  - Potential to improve clinical outcomes, reduce costs, and minimize healthcare inequities is immense
  - So too is potential for preventable harm
  - ECRI urges HHS to support accountability-first strategy for clinical AI adoption
    - Requiring transparency, performance evidence, and ongoing monitoring
  - AI should enhance, not replace, clinical judgment
  - Protect patient safety
  - Deliver measurable value to healthcare system Americans depend on
  - Human decision-making remains essential
  - By prioritizing tools that are tested, proven, and trusted, HHS can ensure U.S. innovation advances patient care with integrity, equity, and reliability

- Submitted by Scott Lucas, PhD, PE, Vice President, Device Safety, ECRI

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Independent patient safety nonprofit urges HHS to adopt an "accountability-first" approach to clinical AI—requiring transparency, rigorous pre-deployment validation, continuous monitoring, and clear governance expectations before scaling, to ensure innovation serves patients rather than exposing them to opaque, unproven technologies.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** ECRI
- **Type:** Academic/Research (Independent Nonprofit)
- **Role/Expertise:** Independent nonprofit organization advancing evidence-based practices to improve patient safety, healthcare quality, and cost-effectiveness; submitted by Scott Lucas, PhD, PE, Vice President of Device Safety
- **Geographic Scope:** National (headquartered in Willow Grove, PA)
- **Stake in Issue:** Evaluates healthcare technologies and AI tools for safety and effectiveness; directly involved in helping healthcare organizations assess and implement clinical AI responsibly

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We support AI adoption in clinical care but believe the greatest barriers are not algorithmic—they're gaps in real-world readiness, accountability, and governance. HHS should prioritize an "accountability-first" strategy requiring transparency, performance evidence, and ongoing monitoring. AI should strengthen clinical judgment and protect patients, not expose them to opaque technologies that cannot be measured, audited, or controlled.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Set enforceable expectations for clinical-impact AI regardless of device status
  - Require transparency (intended use, validation methods, limitations, known failure modes)
  - Require traceability (versioning, audit logs, data provenance)
  - Require lifecycle governance (change management, monitoring, incident reporting)
  - Require truthful performance claims across populations and subpopulations

- Use CMS payment and program levers to incentivize governance
  - Condition participation/reimbursement on organizations maintaining AI inventories (including LLMs)
  - Require risk-tiered evaluation before deployment
  - Require ongoing monitoring, user training, and error reporting programs
  - Require documentation of when and how AI is used in care processes

- Provide standard procurement and contracting language for health systems
  - Include minimum logging/audit requirements
  - Require versioning and advance notice of model updates
  - Establish data use and retention limits (including "no training on our data" unless explicit)
  - Define vendor obligations for safety incidents and vulnerability disclosure
  - Ensure realistic indemnification aligned to risk

- Clarify privacy/security guardrails for LLM workflows through OCR and ONC
  - Define acceptable handling of PHI in prompts
  - Establish HIPAA-compliant BAA requirements for LLM vendors
  - Set minimum security controls for AI connected to EHRs
  - Establish safe defaults (redaction, local processing, retention minimization)

- Create enforceable "truth-in-claims" regime coordinated with FTC and FDA
  - Require disclosure of evaluation methods
  - Require specification of populations/settings tested
  - Require accurate performance metrics and disclosure of limitations

- Require pre-deployment validation including:
  - Task-based validation in realistic environments
  - Reference-grounded testing for convincing but wrong inputs
  - Human factors and workflow simulation
  - Safety-focused red teaming (hallucinations, unsafe omissions, prompt injection, PHI leakage)
  - Subgroup performance stratification

- Support post-deployment monitoring through CMS incentives:
  - Continuous monitoring with change control and re-validation triggers
  - Real-world performance surveillance with CAPA proceedings
  - Drift and distribution shift monitoring
  - Auditable provenance capturing prompts, versions, outputs, and downstream actions

- Support independent testing and certification programs evaluating AI robustness, transparency, equity, and monitoring readiness

- Continue federal leadership in data standards and interoperability

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Procurement processes don't reward transparency or safety
  - Vendors can imply clinical-grade abilities without disclosing evaluation methods or limitations

- Limited independent validation of AI tools before widespread deployment
  - EPIC's sepsis prediction program performed substantially worse than expected in real-world evaluation

- Non-medical AI tools (like LLM chatbots) create governance gaps
  - Can influence clinical decisions but sit outside normal accountability and validation controls
  - Diffused liability when harm occurs—vendor disclaims clinical use, health system assumes tool is "just informational"
  - Contracts often lack meaningful indemnity for clinical harm or hallucinated content

- LLM outputs can be convincing even when inaccurate
  - Creates automation bias and subtle influence even when clinicians think they're not being influenced
  - Can insert subtle bias into decisions and produce plausible but incorrect content

- Performance varies by context, population, language, and clinical domain
  - Without transparent limitations, AI can amplify disparities or mislead in edge cases
  - Clinical AI can fail unevenly across populations, risking worse outcomes

- Update opacity and performance drift
  - Unannounced model changes can alter behavior without warning
  - Lack of stable versioning makes incident investigation difficult—may be impossible to know what model would have said on date of incident

- Traceability and audit challenges
  - Chat interactions may not be logged in clinically meaningful ways
  - Logs may be stored outside organization's control

- Privacy and security risks
  - Prompts may include PHI inadvertently
  - Ambiguity around data retention, model training on user data, potential data transfer outside U.S.
  - Tool integrations expand attack surface for prompt injection and tool hijacking

- Public trust is prerequisite for success at scale
  - Patients worry about loss of privacy, unfair or unexplained decisions, and errors without recourse

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Detailed evidence reviews of specific AI applications revealing current state of evidence:
  - AI scheduling software: Studies are highly heterogeneous, lack patient outcome reporting, and most lack comparison to traditional methods—unclear if AI actually works better
  - TREWS sepsis prediction: Data too limited in quantity and quality to support conclusions; needs independent validation
  - Immunoscore sepsis prediction: Only supports low-confidence conclusions; no independent validation studies available
  - AI in histopathology: Shows promise for reducing pathologist burden amid global shortage and aging workforce, but data for conditions beyond certain cancers is limited
  - AI for MS monitoring: Many studies rely on retrospective data with high bias risk; commercially available packages not adequately tested clinically
  - AI for coronary artery disease: Consistently shows improved accuracy but lacks long-term outcomes data, real-world generalizability, and cost-effectiveness studies

- Framework distinguishing where AI succeeds vs. struggles:
  - Succeeds: Narrow, clearly defined tasks with measurable outcomes (medical imaging triage, ambient documentation)
  - Struggles: High-stakes prediction without validation, opaque models, alert fatigue, performance drift over time

- Specific governance framework for "prove-it-first, monitor-it-always" approach with detailed pre-deployment and post-deployment requirements

- Insight that nothing stifles innovation and growth like litigation—proper governance protects innovators too

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "America gains the most when innovation is tested, proven, and trusted, not when shortcomings are discovered post-deployment."

- "These measures do not restrict innovation—they differentiate serious innovators from opportunistic ones."

- "Non-medical devices that use AI can shape clinical decisions at scale while escaping the normal checks that protect patients. HHS does not need to micromanage innovation, but it should communicate expectations, support adoption with practical guardrails, and enforce accountability so the systems used in care are measurable, monitorable, and honest about what they are not able to do in a way that end users can understand."

- "'What would the model have said on the date of the incident?' may be unknowable."

- "Public trust is not a barrier to innovation; it is a prerequisite for success at scale."

entities.label: EHR

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- General Points on AI and Data
  - AI relies on data, and good AI relies on good data
  - Without adequate data, downstream HHS AI policies will be ineffective
  - HHS, particularly CMS payment policies, should focus on data fluidity and transparency with robust privacy protections
  - Practical approach means incentivizing FHIR APIs and requiring public key cryptographic protections
  - Only parties with actual right to patient data are the patient, their providers, and their payer

- The Fundamental Data Availability Problem
  - CMS provider payment policies heavily incentivize making data NOT AVAILABLE for AI or any pro-competitive purpose
  - Disproportionate payment for procedures has created dominant business model of oligopoly providers using single EHRs
  - Vast amounts of data used internally and shared with other oligopoly customers
  - External release to potential competition is heavily gated by:
    - Cumbersome procedures
    - Disproportionate costs
    - Ancient protocols
    - Outright throttling of data flows
  - This "information blocking" pattern has persisted for decades while interoperability became the norm in every other economic sector

- Proposed CMS Payment Reform
  - CMS payments should depend on provider documentation being available via modern RESTful APIs
  - CMS standards group could modify X12 so every provider claim includes URL where documentation for billed service is available
  - Documentation should be accessible to patients, their apps (AI and otherwise), and true HIPAA Covered Entities

- HHS Position on Digital Healthcare
  - HHS could be the center of a modern digital healthcare economy serving patients
  - HHS has historically supported FHIR APIs in 2020 Cures Act rule and CMS 0057 payer API rule
  - Now HHS appears to support institutional information blocking through TEFCA

- TEFCA Criticism
  - 2016 Cures Act defines information blocking as interfering with "access, exchange and use" of patient data
  - TEFCA does all three:
    - TEFCA's brokers interfere with data access
    - TEFCA's ancient AOL/Yahoo era protocols interfere with data exchange
    - TEFCA's document-only architecture interferes with data use
  - TEFCA proponents (incumbent data holders and those selling patient data to third parties circumventing HIPAA) claim TEFCA will move to FHIR
  - They have claimed this for 5 years yet TEFCA has not performed a single bona fide API transaction using FHIR
  - TEFCA's antiquated architecture and baroque protocols cannot move large amounts of data needed for robust AI
  - The cellphone Internet economy does billions of JSON transactions DAILY by comparison

- Privacy and Security Concerns with TEFCA
  - Internet uses robust public key cryptography and "zero-trust" security for protected data
  - TEFCA uses "just trust me" faux-HIPAA "permitted uses"
  - This end run around HIPAA is so brittle that QHINs (supposed to vet other participants) are engaged in multiple lawsuits around wholesale misappropriation of patient data
  - The resold data has almost invariably been subsidized by taxpayers

- Recommended Privacy and Security Approach
  - Actual privacy and security should be the ground assumption for HHS AI work
  - RESTful and Bulk FHIR APIs anchored by public key cryptography can provide this level of privacy and security
  - Same approach works in banking and defense
  - HL7 has extensive implementation guides for healthcare versions of these APIs
  - HHS AI use should build on these APIs

- Potential AI Applications with Proper APIs
  - CMS could analyze Medicare claims for fraud (matching actual diagnoses, plausible providers, appropriate services)
  - MA, Medicaid, and commercial plans could use similar tools
  - Working APIs are real-time, allowing fraud detection before claims are paid
  - Bulk FHIR APIs (batch mode population level) would allow CMS, VA, and DOD to correlate patterns of care and best practices with outcomes
  - Current CMS quality measurement uses cumbersome data collection (QRDA for providers, Primary Source Verification for payers)

- Alignment of Ethical AI and Patient Competition
  - Enablement of ethical AI and patient-controlled digitally based competition are largely the same
  - Both rely on same data and same privacy security models
  - Alternate data acquisition tools exist (screen-scraping/RPA, proprietary EHR vendor-gated strategies)
  - Widespread AI use in healthcare will require public APIs
  - Patients want apps that are smart, real-time, low cost, and maximally effective
  - Patients do not want to be bound to incumbent "portals" or TEFCA's convoluted IAS barriers

- Response to Question 1: Biggest Barriers to Private Sector AI Innovation
  - Biggest barrier is that most clinical data is held by few EHR vendors
  - Data only available to whatever AI programs those vendors decide to allow
  - Vast bulk of hidden data has been paid for by US taxpayers
  - Proposed solution: Require EHR vendor patient-portals to run entirely through public APIs rather than hidden connections
  - Any patient data including scheduling not available at same performance level to outside competitive products should be considered information-blocking
  - Cures Act enforcement should target both EHR and provider using that EHR
  - Level playing field would allow patients to get AI-optimized medical advice in forms they choose rather than being bound to provider portals

- Medicare Advantage Star Ratings Reform
  - Massive policy opportunity to open data for AI
  - Star ratings program and HEDIS measures use outdated data feeds
  - Data not exposed to public APIs that AI vendors could use to improve care efficiency and cost-effectiveness
  - Current policy has spawned vendor Payer Platforms where EHR vendors use private APIs to help providers and payers game higher Star ratings
  - CMS should zero out payments for Star rating measures unless data obtained through public API
  - Each data element should be tagged with metadata of public API call
  - Requiring public API data would modernize entire quality measurement landscape through AI
  - Secondary benefits: help reform quality measurement, help payers negotiate better for taxpayers and beneficiaries

- Response to Question 2: Regulatory and Payment Policy Changes
  - Cures Act requires ALL data be made available
  - 2020 ONC Cures Act rule predated widespread LLM use
  - Only requirement was that each data field had vocabulary tag of vendor's choice
  - ASTP should flesh out opportunities to more richly enable AI with this Cures Act requirement

- Response to Question 3: Novel Legal and Implementation Issues
  - Requiring patient permission using modern cryptographic principles would be solid foundation for AI data use

- Response to Question 8: Enhanced Interoperability Opportunities
  - Require large volumes of healthcare data for quality measurement and Star ratings to flow through public APIs
  - Move away from NCQA Primary Source Verification screen reviews
  - Move away from private payer provider platforms (which inevitably become anti-competitive)
  - These would be fundamental interoperability reforms
  - Incentivize modern high-performance RESTful FHIR APIs
  - Move away from TEFCA's brittle, expensive, slow, and insecure document flows
  - This should be central to Federal policy
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Health data infrastructure company argues that TEFCA is information blocking in disguise and that CMS should require public FHIR APIs for all payments and Star ratings to enable AI innovation.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** 1upHealth (Donald W. Rucker, MD, Chief Strategy Officer)
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Scalable serverless cloud data provider leveraging FHIR data standard to combine claims and clinical data for analytics and APIs; Chief Strategy Officer is an MD
- **Geographic Scope:** National (Boston, MA headquarters)
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct business interest in FHIR API adoption and data interoperability; competes against incumbent EHR vendors and TEFCA-based data exchange models
structuredSections.corePosition: Good AI requires good data, and current CMS payment policies actively incentivize making data unavailable. HHS should stop supporting TEFCA (which we consider institutional information blocking) and instead require all provider payments and Star ratings to flow through public FHIR APIs with cryptographic patient permission. The same infrastructure that enables ethical AI also enables patient-controlled competition—both need public APIs with real privacy protections.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Require provider documentation be available via RESTful APIs as condition of CMS payment
  - Modify X12 so every provider claim includes URL where documentation is available
  - Make documentation accessible to patients, their apps, and HIPAA Covered Entities
- Require EHR vendor patient-portals to run entirely through public APIs
  - Any patient data (including scheduling) not available at same performance level to outside apps should be considered information-blocking
  - Enforce Cures Act against both EHR vendors and providers using them
- Zero out Star rating payments unless data obtained through public APIs
  - Each data element must be tagged with metadata of public API call
  - Would eliminate gaming through private vendor Payer Platforms
- Require cryptographic patient permission for AI data use
  - Use public key cryptography and zero-trust security models
  - Follow banking and defense sector approaches
- ASTP should expand definition of "all data" under Cures Act to more richly enable AI
  - Current requirement (vocabulary tags only) predates widespread LLM use
- Replace TEFCA with high-performance RESTful FHIR APIs as central Federal policy
  - Move quality measurement away from NCQA Primary Source Verification screen reviews
  - Eliminate private payer provider platforms that become anti-competitive
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- TEFCA constitutes information blocking under Cures Act definition
  - Brokers interfere with data access
  - Ancient protocols interfere with data exchange
  - Document-only architecture interferes with data use
  - Claims of moving to FHIR have been made for 5 years with no actual FHIR transactions
- EHR vendor oligopoly controls most clinical data
  - Data only available to AI programs vendors allow
  - Taxpayers paid for this data but cannot access it
  - Hidden APIs give vendor portals unfair advantage over competitive apps
- TEFCA's privacy model is fundamentally broken
  - Uses "just trust me" faux-HIPAA "permitted uses" instead of real cryptographic security
  - QHINs (supposed to vet participants) are engaged in multiple lawsuits over patient data misappropriation
  - Resold data was almost invariably subsidized by taxpayers
- Current Star ratings system enables gaming
  - Vendor Payer Platforms use private APIs to help providers and payers game higher ratings
  - Outdated data feeds not exposed to public APIs
- CMS payment policies incentivize data hoarding
  - Disproportionate procedure payments created oligopoly provider model
  - External data release gated by cumbersome procedures, high costs, ancient protocols, and throttling
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Technical comparison revealing TEFCA's inadequacy: The cellphone Internet economy does billions of JSON transactions daily, while TEFCA has not performed a single bona fide FHIR API transaction in 5 years despite promises
- Counterintuitive framing: Ethical AI enablement and patient-controlled competition are "largely one and the same"—both need identical data and privacy infrastructure
- Specific gaming mechanism exposed: Vendor "Payer Platforms" use private APIs to help providers and payers game Star ratings, creating anti-competitive dynamics that public API requirements would eliminate
- Practical enforcement suggestion: Tag each data element with metadata of the public API call used to obtain it, creating audit trail for Star ratings
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "CMS provider payment policies heavily incent making data NOT AVAILABLE whether for AI or any pro-competitive purpose."
- "The 2016 Cures Act defines information blocking as interfering with the 'access, exchange and use' of patient data – TEFCA does all three."
- "This end run around HIPAA is so brittle that TEFCA participants – the same QHINs which are supposed to vet other participants – are currently engaged in multiple lawsuits around their wholesale misappropriation of patient data."
