entities.label: Information Blocking

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- NeuroFlow develops AI-enabled clinical decision support and population health tools deployed nationally across health systems, payers, and federal partners
  - Systems operate exclusively within supervised clinical workflows
  - Do not provide autonomous medical advice or function as direct-to-consumer therapeutic agents
  - Augment licensed clinicians by supporting early detection, risk stratification, care matching, and measurement-informed care at scale

- Public discussion of AI in healthcare often centers on patient-facing chatbots and autonomous systems
  - These tools warrant appropriate oversight but differ materially from clinician-supervised CDS embedded within accountable care teams
  - Federal policy should distinguish between these categories to ensure regulation remains proportional to risk

- Behavioral health is a particularly urgent domain for thoughtful AI policy
  - Comorbid behavioral and physical health conditions drive substantial excess medical spending
  - Behavioral health has long lacked infrastructure parity in federal health IT investment
  - AI-enabled CDS can function as clinical infrastructure, supporting workforce capacity, improving access, and enabling integration under value-based care

- Federal policy should focus on aligning incentives around measurable outcomes and total cost of care rather than treating AI as a discrete reimbursable intervention
  - When payment rewards prevention and integration, adoption of high-value clinical decision support follows naturally

- Key recommendations for HHS to accelerate responsible adoption
  - Differentiate between supervised clinical decision support and autonomous patient-facing AI systems, applying proportionate scrutiny to autonomy and risk
  - Align reimbursement with measurable outcomes and total cost accountability
  - Expand risk-bearing value-based models in Traditional Medicare and Medicare Advantage to create natural incentives for adoption of population health AI infrastructure
  - Strengthen behavioral health accountability within Medicare Advantage and other federal programs to drive systematic measurement-based care
  - Accelerate implementation of USCDI+ Behavioral Health to ensure structured, interoperable behavioral health data
  - Understand AI in behavioral health as infrastructure that operationalizes measurement-informed care and enables scalable integration, not as a replacement for clinicians

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - Primary barriers are not technical limitations in model capability but structural misalignment across regulatory interpretation, data infrastructure, workflow integration, and payment incentives
  - Failure to distinguish clinician-supervised CDS from autonomous AI
    - Public and policy discourse frequently conflates autonomous patient-facing AI systems with clinician-supervised clinical decision support tools
    - Clinician-facing CDS tools operate within established clinical accountability frameworks, surfacing information for licensed clinicians to independently evaluate
    - Section 520(o)(1)(E) of the 21st Century Cures Act excludes certain CDS software from device regulation when clinicians can independently review the basis for recommendations
    - When these categories are treated as equivalent, health systems apply heightened scrutiny designed for autonomous systems to clinician-supervised CDS, creating unnecessary friction
  - Behavioral health infrastructure deficits
    - HITECH Act catalyzed widespread EHR adoption but largely excluded behavioral health providers
    - Behavioral health EHR adoption and data standardization lag significantly behind the rest of the health care system
    - Behavioral health documentation is frequently unstructured
    - Data elements such as symptom scales, suicide risk assessments, and treatment response metrics are inconsistently captured
    - Exchange of behavioral health data across systems remains limited
    - Without improvements to behavioral health data infrastructure, the clinical value of AI tools in this domain will remain constrained
  - Workflow integration and EHR friction
    - Clinical decision support must align with established workflows and integrate into certified EHR platforms without disruption
    - Technical and contractual barriers slow third-party integration and limit timely API access
    - CMS's Health Tech Ecosystem initiative appropriately recognizes these integration challenges
  - Misaligned payment incentives
    - In payment environments that reward volume, organizations investing in prevention and population management may not see immediate financial return
    - Preventing a psychiatric hospitalization improves outcomes and lowers total cost of care, but in fee-for-service models that value accrues to payers rather than providers
    - Risk-bearing value-based arrangements create clear incentives to adopt infrastructure supporting early detection and measurement-informed care
    - Until payment structures reward outcomes rather than activity, organizations best positioned to deploy behavioral health AI face a financial disincentive to do so

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Reaffirm the clinical decision support exemption under the 21st Century Cures Act
    - Section 520(o)(1)(E) excludes certain clinician-supervised CDS tools from device regulation when they enable health care professionals to independently review the basis for recommendations
    - Behavioral health CDS tools that surface evidence-based treatment pathways, risk stratification insights, and treatment response predictions fall within this framework
    - Reaffirming this statutory boundary supports predictable innovation and reduces procurement hesitation
  - Modernize consent infrastructure under 42 CFR Part 2
    - Current rules prevent AI algorithms from accessing substance use disorder data even within integrated care teams where patients have consented to coordinated treatment
    - Patients with co-occurring substance use and mental health conditions represent some of the highest-risk, highest-cost individuals
    - When AI risk stratification models cannot access complete clinical records, their precision is compromised for exactly the patients who stand to benefit most
    - Modernized electronic consent mechanisms would improve integration while preserving privacy protections
  - Accelerate risk-bearing value-based payment models in Traditional Medicare and Medicare Advantage
    - Behavioral health conditions drive substantial excess medical spending when comorbid with chronic disease
    - Organizations accountable for total cost of care develop natural incentives to deploy AI-enabled measurement and population health infrastructure
    - Payment reform, not new technology codes, will be the primary driver of sustainable adoption
    - Current time-based billing in programs like the Collaborative Care Model creates a structural disincentive against efficiency-enhancing technology
      - Organizations using AI tools to manage larger patient panels with better outcomes generate less billable time, not more
    - Payment structures should reward complexity management and measured clinical outcomes including remission rates, crisis prevention, and reduced hospitalizations
  - Strengthen behavioral health accountability in Medicare Advantage and Medicaid
    - Incorporate robust behavioral health outcome measures into Medicare Advantage Star Ratings and managed care contracts
    - Relevant measures include remission benchmarks, proactive screening rates, and avoidable crisis utilization
    - Higher Star Ratings and quality bonus payments would reward plans using validated measurement systems achievable at population scale through AI-enabled infrastructure
  - Establish federal behavioral health AI validation infrastructure
    - HHS should support representative, de-identified validation datasets and shared evaluation methodologies through ARPA-H, NIMH, and related public-private initiatives
    - Limitations in small, non-generalizable datasets undermine trust and slow adoption
    - Federally supported validation infrastructure would reduce duplicative review burdens and create common benchmarks

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Liability for AI-generated high-risk alerts
    - Health systems lack clear expectations for what constitutes an adequate and defensible clinical response to suicide risk detection or crisis prediction alerts
    - A clinician receiving an AI-generated urgent alert faces genuine questions about what the standard of care requires
    - HHS can reduce uncertainty by articulating best practices and tiered response expectations that account for real-world organizational capacity
  - Privacy framework gaps
    - HIPAA's definition of covered entities does not adequately address the range of digital health and AI tools now operating in clinical contexts
    - Patient-facing tools and AI applications connecting to EHRs may handle sensitive behavioral health information without falling clearly within existing privacy and cybersecurity requirements
    - HHS should clarify how HIPAA applies to AI-enabled CDS tools operating within supervised clinical workflows
  - Lack of consistent evaluation criteria
    - Health systems and payers lack consistent criteria for evaluating AI solutions before procurement
    - Each organization conducts its own bespoke security, validation, and governance review
    - Vendors face inconsistent expectations across organizations
    - HHS guidance on explainability, traceability, transparency, and validation would give organizations a coherent framework

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Evaluation should rest on four principles: explainability, traceability, transparency, and validation
  - Explainability
    - Model recommendations must align with established evidence-based workflows and clearly surface the clinical variables that influenced each suggestion
    - Clinicians must understand how outputs relate to guideline-concordant care pathways to exercise independent judgment
    - A clinician who cannot understand why a tool flagged a patient cannot responsibly act on that flag or override it
  - Traceability
    - Every model output must be auditable
    - Systems should maintain logs capturing relevant data inputs, decision thresholds, and model version history
    - Organizations can then review how recommendations were generated and respond to adverse events with a clear record
  - Transparency
    - Requires disclosure of intended use, model limitations, validation evidence, and deployment context
    - Organizations should communicate clearly where and how models operate within clinical workflows
    - Strengthens institutional trust and enables informed procurement decisions
  - Validation
    - Requires ongoing assessment of clinical accuracy, fairness across patient populations, and measurable impact on care delivery
    - For behavioral health applications, validation should examine outcomes such as access to care, symptom improvement, crisis identification, and avoidable utilization
    - Validation is not a one-time event; AI models that continue learning require ongoing monitoring to detect performance drift
  - HHS can accelerate responsible adoption by promoting these core governance principles and encouraging shared outcome benchmarks for behavioral health applications

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities
  - HHS should set clear baseline expectations while allowing accreditation and testing frameworks to evolve through industry and professional collaboration
  - Rather than creating new technology-specific certification programs, HHS should articulate core expectations for clinician-facing CDS
    - Documentation of intended use
    - Disclosure of validation evidence
    - Auditability of outputs
    - Adherence to recognized security standards
  - HHS can strengthen industry-driven testing by supporting shared benchmarking infrastructure
    - De-identified, demographically representative validation datasets in high-impact domains such as behavioral health
    - This infrastructure should function as research support rather than certification
    - Datasets should intentionally include rural populations, Veterans, and high-comorbidity Medicare beneficiaries who are consistently underrepresented
  - Federal convening authority and procurement policies can reinforce these principles by rewarding transparency and measurable outcomes

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or exceeded expectations and where they have fallen short
  - AI has met or exceeded expectations when deployed within supervised clinical workflows to address defined operational and clinical gaps
  - Behavioral health integration
    - AI-enabled referral matching has reduced time to treatment and lowered behavioral health-related inpatient utilization while improving total cost of care
    - When primary care providers are supported by algorithmic matching tools accounting for clinical characteristics, patient preferences, and real-time network capacity, access improves and downstream crisis utilization declines
  - Natural language processing for suicide risk detection
    - NLP systems identify risk signals that structured screening tools alone may not capture
    - In a retrospective database study, more than half of patients flagged by NLP analysis of free-text inputs would not have been identified through structured screening alone
    - These patients were at elevated risk and would have gone undetected without passive, continuous analysis of the language they used outside of formal assessment windows
  - Crisis platform applications
    - AI-enabled triage systems identify high-risk contacts and route them to appropriate responders faster than manual review
    - AI has demonstrated value in training crisis responders using simulated scenarios to build consistent skills at scale
    - These applications do not replace human responders; they ensure highest-risk individuals reach a prepared clinician faster
  - Population-level use cases
    - Suspect diagnosis identification and behavioral health risk stratification models help care teams identify patients with undiagnosed or under-managed conditions
    - When paired with measurement-informed care, these systems support more appropriate resource allocation and reduce avoidable emergency department utilization
  - Measurement-informed care
    - One of the highest-value and most evidence-supported AI applications in behavioral health
    - Involves systematic use of validated assessment instruments during clinical encounters to track symptom trajectories, detect non-response, and guide treatment adjustments
    - Despite demonstrated clinical benefits, MIC is underutilized because manual administration, scoring, and visualization are administratively burdensome
    - AI removes that barrier directly through automated assessment administration, real-time scoring, and longitudinal visualization
    - When AI operationalizes MIC at population scale, it transforms a proven but underutilized practice into routine infrastructure
  - Evidence-based treatment pathways
    - AI systems surfacing guideline-concordant options or predicting treatment response have shown promise in reducing trial-and-error prescribing
    - Particularly valuable in primary care settings where specialty behavioral health expertise is limited
  - Administrative support tools
    - Automated documentation assistance and coding validation have produced efficiency gains reducing clinician burden
    - Clinician burnout and documentation burden directly constrain the capacity of care teams to engage patients meaningfully
  - Where AI tools fall short
    - When deployed outside supervised care environments
    - When not integrated into existing workflows
    - When disconnected from measurable accountability structures
    - Tools functioning as standalone digital interventions without clear linkage to care teams or outcome measurement have produced less consistent impact
    - Evidence base for direct-to-consumer therapeutic AI remains far thinner than for clinician-supervised decision support
  - Greatest potential lies in AI applications that operationalize measurement-informed, guideline-concordant care for patients with complex behavioral and physical comorbidity
    - These populations account for disproportionate healthcare spending
    - Systems supporting early detection, risk stratification, treatment matching, and proactive adherence monitoring within risk-bearing payment models offer the clearest pathway to improved outcomes and cost containment

- Question 7: Roles and decision makers influencing AI adoption
  - Adoption typically shaped by three groups: clinical leadership, information technology leadership, and compliance
  - Chief Medical Officers and clinical governance committees
    - Evaluate whether a tool aligns with evidence-based standards, supports appropriate workflows, and demonstrates meaningful clinical impact
    - Without credible validation evidence and clear articulation of intended use, clinical approval rarely advances
    - These committees often lack shared frameworks for evaluating AI tools and must construct their own review criteria from scratch
  - Chief Information Officers, information security teams, and compliance leaders
    - Control integration, data access, cybersecurity review, and regulatory risk assessment
    - Often determine the pace of adoption more than clinical enthusiasm does
    - In absence of widely accepted evaluation standards, organizations conduct bespoke reviews independently, extending procurement timelines significantly
    - A tool that passes clinical review may sit in IT security review for months
  - Primary hurdle is not resistance to AI but variability in institutional review expectations
    - When no shared framework exists, each organization must define for itself what constitutes sufficient review
    - This variability disadvantages smaller health systems and safety-net providers lacking dedicated AI governance infrastructure
    - Clear federal guidance around baseline transparency and governance expectations would reduce variability while preserving appropriate institutional oversight

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Enhanced interoperability in behavioral health would materially expand safe and scalable AI deployment
  - Most consequential data elements
    - Structured symptom assessments for depression, anxiety, and PTSD
    - Suicide risk indicators
    - Longitudinal treatment response data
    - Medication management history
    - Crisis utilization signals
    - Relevant social determinants of health
  - Today many of these elements exist as scanned forms, free-text notes, or proprietary formats that AI systems cannot reliably access or interpret
  - Emerging evidence suggests passively collected data from smartphones and wearables can serve as objective proxies for mental health status
    - Mobility patterns, sleep duration, and heart rate variability can detect symptom changes and predict deterioration before adverse events
    - As this evidence matures, interoperability infrastructure accommodating these signals will become increasingly important
  - USCDI+ Behavioral Health represents an important step toward standardizing these elements
    - When certified EHR systems support consistent capture and API-based exchange of behavioral health measures, data completeness improves
    - Reliable interoperability enhances both clinical accuracy and comparability across organizations
    - Enables AI developers to build and validate tools against representative datasets rather than proprietary, single-system data
  - Interoperability supports benchmarking and research
    - When outcome measures and utilization data are structured and exchangeable, health systems and payers can more transparently assess performance
    - Particularly important for Medicare and high-comorbidity populations where longitudinal, cross-setting data are essential
    - Single-site studies cannot answer the questions policymakers and payers need answered about AI performance at population scale
  - Enhanced interoperability strengthens care coordination, measurement-informed practice, and accountability across the entire healthcare system
  - The behavioral health field has waited too long for infrastructure parity
  - USCDI+ Behavioral Health combined with meaningful enforcement through EHR certification and payment policy offers a credible path forward

- Question 9: Challenges patients and caregivers wish to see addressed and concerns about AI
  - Patients and caregivers consistently identify access, coordination, and timeliness as primary challenges in behavioral health care
    - Long wait times
    - Difficulty finding appropriate providers
    - Fragmented communication between medical and behavioral health clinicians
    - Delayed identification of worsening symptoms
  - AI-enabled decision support can help address these gaps by supporting earlier detection, more appropriate care matching, and proactive outreach within supervised care teams
  - For individuals managing co-occurring medical and behavioral health conditions, improved coordination is especially important
    - Patients frequently repeat their histories to multiple providers
    - Re-disclose traumatic experiences
    - Navigate disconnected systems without a clear point of accountability
  - Tools helping clinicians recognize untreated depression, substance use disorder, or emerging suicide risk within routine care settings can reduce preventable crises
  - Patient and caregiver concerns
    - Privacy and data security
    - Bias
    - Potential erosion of the clinician-patient relationship
    - Behavioral health information carries particular sensitivity
    - Many patients fear automated systems could displace human judgment or reduce empathy that makes behavioral health treatment effective
  - These concerns reinforce the importance of deploying AI as clinician-supervised decision support rather than autonomous clinical actors
  - Patients want assurance that AI systems have been validated for populations that look like them
    - Rural patients and Veterans should not receive tools trained exclusively on urban academic medical center data
  - When AI is positioned as infrastructure supporting rather than replacing licensed professionals, it can enhance safety, access, and continuity while preserving the clinician-patient relationship

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - HHS should prioritize research evaluating how clinician-supervised AI decision support affects real-world clinical outcomes and total cost of care in integrated settings
  - Particularly important are studies focused on populations with co-occurring behavioral and physical health conditions
    - These patients drive disproportionate utilization and spending across Medicare and Medicaid
  - Research should examine whether AI-enabled early identification, risk stratification, treatment matching, and measurement-informed care reduce avoidable hospitalizations, emergency department visits, and symptom persistence
  - Most existing studies measure algorithmic performance rather than patient-level outcomes
  - The field needs pragmatic effectiveness trials in diverse real-world settings
    - VA Medical Centers
    - FQHCs
    - Rural hospitals
    - Community mental health centers
    - Studies linking AI deployment to remission rates, crisis events, and total cost of care
  - Implementation research is equally important
    - Understanding how AI tools are embedded into primary care and behavioral health workflows
    - How clinicians interpret and act on recommendations
    - What governance structures sustain impact over time
    - What organizational characteristics predict successful implementation
    - How financial incentives affect adoption fidelity
    - What implementation support rural and safety-net providers need
  - HHS should prioritize research examining equity and generalizability
    - Behavioral health prevalence, access patterns, and treatment engagement vary significantly across demographic and geographic groups
    - Current validation datasets often underrepresent rural populations, Veterans, and communities of color
    - Research must evaluate whether AI-enabled tools perform consistently across diverse populations
    - Actively correct for gaps in historical training data that may reflect underdiagnosis rather than lower prevalence
  - HHS should support research on appropriate design and oversight of ongoing performance monitoring for adaptive AI systems
    - Traditional device validation assumes a static intervention
    - AI tools that continue learning in deployment require frameworks for detecting performance drift, managing model updates, and maintaining accountability over time
    - This is an area where the field lacks consensus and federal investment in methodology would benefit all stakeholders

- Question 10a: Published findings about impact of adopted AI tools
  - Technology-enabled referral optimization was associated with improved access to behavioral health care, reduced behavioral health-related inpatient admissions, and reductions in total medical spending in a risk-adjusted insured population
  - Natural language processing for suicide risk detection identified individuals with suicidal ideation who would not have been captured by structured screening alone
    - In a retrospective database study, more than half of flagged users may not have been identified without NLP-based analysis of free-text inputs
    - Direct implications for population health programs relying solely on structured assessment tools
  - Technology-enabled behavioral health integration was associated with reductions in emergency department utilization and improvements in depressive symptom trajectories when measurement-informed care was systematically applied
    - Results held across primary care and OBGYN settings, suggesting the model generalizes across care contexts
  - Collectively, these findings indicate that clinician-facing AI tools, when integrated into care teams and tied to measurable outcomes, can improve access, reduce crisis utilization, and support cost containment

- Question 10b: How literature approaches costs, benefits, and transfers
  - Literature primarily evaluates avoided utilization, symptom improvement, and short-term changes in medical spending
  - Fewer studies assess long-term cost transfers across payment models or fully account for implementation costs
    - Technology infrastructure
    - Staff training
    - Workflow redesign
    - Ongoing maintenance
  - Evidence increasingly suggests AI-enabled behavioral health integration produces the strongest financial signal in risk-bearing or accountable care environments
    - Reductions in hospitalizations and emergency utilization translate directly into total cost savings
  - In fee-for-service models, cost reductions often accrue to payers rather than providers
    - Dampens adoption incentive for clinical organizations bearing implementation cost without capturing financial benefit
  - This misalignment between who invests and who benefits is a core structural problem that payment reform must address
  - Economic analyses ignoring transfer effects will underestimate barriers to adoption and overestimate market incentives for deployment
  - Future research should standardize economic evaluation methodologies
    - Attribution models
    - Total cost of care analyses
    - Better quantify fiscal impact across different reimbursement structures

- Conclusion
  - Behavioral health AI used as clinical decision support does not displace licensed professionals
  - It operationalizes evidence-based pathways, strengthens measurement-informed care, and supports earlier identification of risk within accountable clinical environments
  - The regulatory architecture for these tools is already defined
  - The policy priority is ensuring expectations remain proportionate to their function and risk profile
  - In behavioral health, the need for this infrastructure is particularly urgent
    - Comorbid behavioral and physical health conditions drive disproportionate healthcare utilization and cost, especially within Medicare populations
    - Workforce shortages and fragmented data systems limit ability of care teams to manage these conditions proactively
    - Behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result
    - AI policy must not repeat that exclusion
  - When aligned with risk-bearing payment models, interoperable data standards, and transparent governance principles, AI-enabled decision support can improve access, reduce avoidable utilization, and enhance accountability without compromising clinician judgment or patient trust
  - HHS has the opportunity to distinguish clearly among AI use cases, reinforce predictable regulatory pathways for supervised clinical decision support, and align incentives around measurable outcomes

- References cited
  - Zhu et al. (2025) on linking insured adults to behavioral health care as cost-saving solution
  - Holley et al. (2025) on evaluating GPT models for suicide risk assessment
  - Hartz et al. (2024) on using NLP to detect suicidal ideation and prompt urgent interventions
  - Pardes et al. (2022) on technology-enabled behavioral health integration decreasing ED utilization
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A behavioral health AI company urges HHS to distinguish clinician-supervised decision support from autonomous AI, align payment with outcomes rather than volume, and ensure behavioral health isn't left behind in AI policy as it was during HITECH.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** NeuroFlow
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Developer of AI-enabled clinical decision support and population health tools deployed nationally across health systems, payers, and federal partners; systems operate within supervised clinical workflows for early detection, risk stratification, care matching, and measurement-informed care
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct stake as a developer of clinician-supervised AI tools in behavioral health; regulatory clarity, payment alignment, and behavioral health data infrastructure directly affect their market and ability to deploy tools
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe federal policy must distinguish between clinician-supervised clinical decision support and autonomous patient-facing AI systems, applying proportionate scrutiny based on actual risk. The primary barriers to AI adoption are not technical but structural—regulatory ambiguity, behavioral health data infrastructure deficits, workflow integration challenges, and payment models that reward volume over outcomes. Behavioral health was excluded from HITECH's investment cycle and fell further behind; AI policy must not repeat that exclusion.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Differentiate between supervised clinical decision support and autonomous patient-facing AI systems
  - Apply proportionate scrutiny based on autonomy and risk profile
  - Reaffirm the CDS exemption under Section 520(o)(1)(E) of the 21st Century Cures Act

- Align reimbursement with measurable outcomes and total cost accountability
  - Payment reform, not new technology codes, will be the primary driver of sustainable adoption
  - Reward complexity management and measured clinical outcomes (remission rates, crisis prevention, reduced hospitalizations) rather than time spent on care management activities

- Expand risk-bearing value-based models in Traditional Medicare and Medicare Advantage
  - Creates natural incentives for adoption of population health AI infrastructure
  - Ensure behavioral health outcomes are explicitly included in accountability structures

- Strengthen behavioral health accountability in Medicare Advantage and Medicaid
  - Incorporate robust behavioral health outcome measures into Star Ratings and managed care contracts
  - Include remission benchmarks, proactive screening rates, and avoidable crisis utilization

- Modernize consent infrastructure under 42 CFR Part 2
  - Enable AI algorithms to access substance use disorder data within integrated care teams where patients have consented
  - Implement electronic consent mechanisms for secure data exchange

- Accelerate implementation of USCDI+ Behavioral Health
  - Ensure structured, interoperable behavioral health data
  - Enforce through EHR certification and payment policy

- Establish federal behavioral health AI validation infrastructure
  - Support representative, de-identified validation datasets through ARPA-H, NIMH, and public-private initiatives
  - Include rural populations, Veterans, and high-comorbidity Medicare beneficiaries who are consistently underrepresented

- Articulate best practices and tiered response expectations for AI-generated urgent alerts
  - Account for real-world organizational capacity
  - Reduce liability uncertainty without creating new exposure for responsible organizations

- Clarify how HIPAA applies to AI-enabled CDS tools operating within supervised clinical workflows

- Provide federal guidance on explainability, traceability, transparency, and validation
  - Give organizations a coherent framework for accountable deployment
  - Reduce variability in institutional review expectations
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Regulatory conflation of clinician-supervised CDS with autonomous AI
  - Health systems apply heightened scrutiny designed for autonomous systems to lower-risk supervised tools
  - Creates unnecessary friction and slows adoption of infrastructure tools designed to augment rather than replace professional judgment

- Behavioral health infrastructure deficits from HITECH exclusion
  - Behavioral health EHR adoption and data standardization lag significantly behind physical health
  - Documentation is frequently unstructured; symptom scales, suicide risk assessments, and treatment response metrics are inconsistently captured
  - Exchange of behavioral health data across systems remains limited
  - Without improvements, clinical value of AI tools in this domain will remain constrained

- Payment misalignment creating financial disincentives
  - In fee-for-service models, preventing a psychiatric hospitalization improves outcomes but value accrues to payers rather than providers who made the investment
  - Current time-based billing in Collaborative Care Model creates structural disincentive against efficiency-enhancing technology
  - Organizations using AI to manage larger patient panels with better outcomes generate less billable time, not more

- Workflow integration and EHR friction
  - Technical and contractual barriers slow third-party integration and limit timely API access
  - Delays deployment even when underlying tools are clinically ready

- Liability uncertainty for AI-generated high-risk alerts
  - Health systems lack clear expectations for what constitutes adequate clinical response to suicide risk detection or crisis prediction alerts
  - Clinicians face genuine questions about standard of care requirements

- Variability in institutional review expectations
  - No shared framework for assessing transparency, validation evidence, and accountability structures
  - Each organization conducts bespoke security, validation, and governance reviews
  - Disadvantages smaller health systems and safety-net providers lacking dedicated AI governance infrastructure
  - A tool passing clinical review may sit in IT security review for months

- 42 CFR Part 2 preventing AI access to substance use disorder data
  - Even within integrated care teams where patients have consented to coordinated treatment
  - Patients with co-occurring substance use and mental health conditions are highest-risk, highest-cost individuals
  - AI precision is compromised for exactly the patients who stand to benefit most

- Privacy framework gaps
  - HIPAA's definition of covered entities doesn't adequately address digital health and AI tools in clinical contexts
  - Patient-facing tools may handle sensitive behavioral health information without falling within existing requirements

- Underrepresentation in validation datasets
  - Rural populations, Veterans, and communities of color consistently underrepresented
  - Current validation datasets may reflect underdiagnosis rather than lower prevalence
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Evidence from their deployed AI platforms demonstrates measurable impact
  - Technology-enabled referral optimization associated with improved access, reduced behavioral health-related inpatient admissions, and reductions in total medical spending
  - NLP for suicide risk detection identified more than half of at-risk patients who would not have been captured by structured screening alone
  - Technology-enabled behavioral health integration reduced ED utilization and improved depressive symptom trajectories across primary care and OBGYN settings

- Insight on measurement-informed care as highest-value AI application
  - Despite demonstrated clinical benefits, MIC is underutilized because manual administration, scoring, and visualization are administratively burdensome
  - AI removes that barrier directly, transforming a proven but underutilized practice into routine infrastructure

- Counterintuitive finding on payment structure effects
  - Organizations using AI tools to manage larger patient panels with better outcomes generate less billable time under current models—the better the AI works, the less revenue it generates

- Emerging evidence on passive data collection
  - Passively collected smartphone and wearable data (mobility patterns, sleep duration, heart rate variability) can serve as objective proxies for mental health status, detecting symptom changes before adverse events

- AI applications in crisis platforms
  - AI-enabled triage identifies high-risk contacts and routes them to appropriate responders faster than manual review
  - AI training of crisis responders using simulated scenarios builds consistent skills at frequency and scale traditional supervision cannot achieve
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Preventing a psychiatric hospitalization or reducing emergency department utilization improves outcomes and lowers total cost of care, but in fee-for-service models that value accrues to payers rather than the providers who made the investment."

- "In a retrospective database study, more than half of patients flagged by NLP analysis of free-text inputs would not have been identified through structured screening alone. These patients were at elevated risk and would have gone undetected without passive, continuous analysis of the language they used outside of formal assessment windows."

- "Behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result. AI policy must not repeat that exclusion."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- RUPRI Health Panel background
  - Established in 1993 to provide science-based, objective policy analysis to federal lawmakers
  - Panel members include Keith J. Mueller, PhD (Chair), Alva O. Ferdinand, DrPH, JD, Mark Holmes, PhD, Alana D. Knudson, PhD, Jennifer P. Lundblad, PhD, MBA, A. Clinton MacKinney, MD, MS, Timothy D. McBride, PhD
  - Comments draw on report posted on National Advisory Committee on Rural Health and Human Services (NACRHHS) website
  - RUPRI Center for Rural Health Policy Analysis contributed to that report content

- AI applications could contribute to Panel's vision of High Performing Rural Health System
  - NACRHHS identified policy priorities in rural health system development that would benefit from AI technologies
  - Clinical care priorities highlighted:
    - Improving transportation services in rural places, particularly for high-need patients
    - Participating in new collaborations with other organizations including community-based human service organizations, driven by data analytics
    - Negotiating payment rates with commercial insurance plans, Medicare Advantage plans, and Medicaid Managed Care Organizations
    - Maximizing revenues through improved documentation (coding, patient chronic and episodic condition information)
    - Using information technology to improve care management, particularly for optimizing revenue in new payment models through improved coordination across all service delivery points
    - Adopting new care modalities to enhance access to and use of local services across clinical care and human services continuum
    - Providing in-home health and human services
    - Implementing virtual care including 24-hour nursing care
    - Improving navigation of multiple entry points into health and human services continuum

- Overall themes from NACRHHS report
  - Quality broadband access is major factor and potential barrier
    - Up to 45 million Americans lacked availability of quality broadband services as of March 2024 (FCC Press Release)
  - Implementing innovative technology requires leveraging existing hospital alliances
  - Technology to improve clinician efficiency is means of coping with workforce shortages (though far from sufficient)
    - Ambient listening to populate health records cited as specific example

- General Panel Comments on technology and disparities
  - Technology can be an equalizer but often first exacerbates existing disparities
  - Can reinforce differences in access across geography
  - Technology facilitating ease of access for well-insured populations may divert encounters away from local providers who serve all patients regardless of payment source
    - This diversion could threaten financial viability of local providers
  - While this could occur in any underserved area including urban parts, Panel calls attention to potential unintended consequences in rural places
  - Challenges could intuitively be addressed by:
    - Assuring access to new technology for all residents regardless of payment source, precipitating adjustment to use patterns that enhances access for all
    - Increasing other revenue sources for local providers to maintain operations
  - Panel encourages further research and analysis to anticipate effects
  - Recommends monitoring implementation to identify all consequences
  - Suggests addressing unintended consequences through state and federal policies regarding payment (Medicare and Medicaid) and insurance regulation

- Q5: Supporting private sector activities to promote innovative and effective use of AI
  - Suggest allowing government agencies to work with private entities through cooperative research & development agreements
    - Would facilitate joint access to and use of government databases
  - Suggest developing specific criteria focused on quality of care as AI products move from development to application
  - Specifically recommend regulations be based on evidence of successful application in rural health care delivery
  - Analytical criteria should include:
    - Affordability for healthcare organizations and patients
    - Utility of innovative technology to rural healthcare organizations (may be function of scale and scope of services)
    - Effects on long-term sustainability of access to local services
  - Success should be combination of improved health outcomes and increased availability of services in rural communities

- Q6: AI tools with potential for greatest impact in rural settings
  - AI tools enabling clinicians to spend less time on administrative tasks and more time in patient care would impact access to quality care
  - Tools facilitating communication between local primary care providers and specialty clinicians would have impact
    - Would support local care
    - Would coordinate care across continuum through ease of patient transfer as necessary

- Q7: Administrative hurdles unique to small, independent rural healthcare organizations
  - First hurdle: Access to high-speed broadband capacity remains challenge
    - For some clinics
    - For millions of patients in their homes
  - Second hurdle: Resources necessary to take full advantage of AI are lacking
    - Equipment and software support
    - Personnel (data analysts) to take full advantage of new AI tools
  - Critical need for administrative training and support to:
    - Take full advantage of all new reimbursement codes associated with new technologies
    - Mirror systems used by payers such as claims review and pre-authorization

- Q9: Challenges within health care for rural patients and caregivers to address with AI
  - Generating shorter wait times
    - AI tools facilitating patient check-in and other pre-encounter administrative tasks could help reduce in-clinic wait times
  - More efficient and effective care
    - Tools enhancing access to right information at right time during patient encounters

- Q9: Concerns related to adoption and use of AI in rural settings
  - Rural healthcare patients often face long wait times, inefficient or ineffective care, and high costs
  - AI can be leveraged to enhance effectiveness and efficiency of healthcare delivered to rural patients
    - Would shorten wait times and lower healthcare costs
  - However, AI integration must be conducted through careful and thought-out process
  - AI is new and evolving technology
    - Can generate hallucinations or false results
  - Improperly tested AI applications in healthcare may lead to poorer health outcomes
  - Enhanced AI use in healthcare may lead to:
    - Lack of patient interactions with doctors
    - Accessibility concerns for patients less familiar with technology
  - Without government aid, rural healthcare systems may not benefit as much from AI implementation compared to urban areas
    - Due to lack of infrastructure capacity

- Q10: Research and Development
  - Several NIH systematic reviews concluded AI use in clinical care settings can greatly enhance:
    - Clinical decision-making processes
    - Imaging/diagnosis processes
  - Further NIH studies demonstrate AI use in variety of clinical settings:
    - Achieved comparable clinical outcomes to standard care
    - Created measurable improvements in QALYs and other effectiveness metrics
  - Studies state AI leads to more efficient and effective care by:
    - Enhancing clinical outcomes through improved diagnostic accuracy and personalized treatment
    - Reducing costs by optimizing resource allocation and streamlining care
  - However, there are costs to implementing AI:
    - Lack of published research discussing upfront capital expenditures and integration costs required
    - AI is imperfect technology, may lead to false or potentially missed diagnoses
    - Social costs as some employee positions will be eliminated or greatly altered

- Concluding comments
  - Panel recommends any AI innovation be fully vetted with rural clinicians and patients before being endorsed for widespread use
  - Suggest "rural proofing" that:
    - Accounts for challenges of implementing new technologies in resource-poor environments
    - Tests viability for end users – clinicians and patients
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Rural health policy research institute urges "rural proofing" of AI technologies, warning that without careful implementation and government support, innovations that could help underserved communities may instead widen the urban-rural healthcare divide.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** RUPRI Health Panel (Rural Policy Research Institute)
- **Type:** Academic/Research
- **Role/Expertise:** Science-based policy analysis panel established in 1993; members include PhDs, MDs, JDs, and MBAs from multiple universities specializing in rural health policy
- **Geographic Scope:** National, focused on rural communities
- **Stake in Issue:** Advocates for rural health system development and ensuring AI policies account for unique rural challenges and resource constraints
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe AI applications could contribute to a High Performing Rural Health System, but technology can be an equalizer that often first exacerbates existing disparities. Any AI innovation must be fully vetted with rural clinicians and patients before widespread endorsement, with "rural proofing" that accounts for resource-poor environments and tests viability for end users.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Require "rural proofing" of AI technologies before widespread endorsement
  - Account for challenges of implementing in resource-poor environments
  - Test viability for end users – clinicians and patients
- Allow government agencies to work with private entities through cooperative R&D agreements
  - Facilitate joint access to and use of government databases
- Develop specific quality-of-care criteria as AI products move from development to application
  - Base regulations on evidence of successful application in rural health care delivery
- Establish analytical criteria for AI evaluation including:
  - Affordability for healthcare organizations and patients
  - Utility to rural healthcare organizations (function of scale and scope)
  - Effects on long-term sustainability of access to local services
- Define success as combination of improved health outcomes AND increased availability of services in rural communities
- Address unintended consequences through state and federal policies regarding Medicare/Medicaid payment and insurance regulation
- Provide government aid to ensure rural healthcare systems can benefit from AI implementation
- Ensure administrative training and support for new reimbursement codes and payer systems
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Technology may exacerbate rather than reduce disparities
  - AI facilitating access for well-insured populations may divert encounters away from local providers serving all patients
  - Could threaten financial viability of local providers in rural areas
- Broadband access remains major barrier
  - Up to 45 million Americans lacked quality broadband as of March 2024
  - Affects both clinics and patients in their homes
- Rural organizations lack resources to implement AI
  - Equipment and software support
  - Personnel (data analysts) to take full advantage of tools
  - Training for new reimbursement codes and payer systems
- AI technology risks
  - Can generate hallucinations or false results
  - Improperly tested applications may lead to poorer health outcomes
  - May lead to false or missed diagnoses
- Patient experience concerns
  - Enhanced AI use may reduce patient interactions with doctors
  - Accessibility concerns for patients less familiar with technology
- Without government aid, rural systems may not benefit as much as urban areas due to infrastructure gaps
- Lack of published research on upfront capital expenditures and integration costs
- Social costs as employee positions may be eliminated or greatly altered
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Panel identifies a counterintuitive risk: AI designed to improve access could actually harm rural healthcare by diverting well-insured patients to technology-enabled services, undermining the financial viability of local providers who serve everyone regardless of payment source
- Suggests two-pronged solution: either ensure technology access for all residents regardless of payment source (changing use patterns) OR increase other revenue sources for local providers
- Cites ambient listening to populate health records as specific example of technology helping cope with workforce shortages
- Notes that success criteria should include not just health outcomes but also "increased availability of services in rural communities" – recognizing that a technology could improve individual outcomes while simultaneously reducing overall access
- Proposes "rural proofing" concept as systematic approach to evaluating AI before widespread deployment
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Technology can be an equalizer but often first exacerbates disparities that exist."
- "Technology that facilitates ease of access for well-insured populations may divert encounters away from local providers who serve all patients regardless of source of payment. That diversion could threaten financially viability of local providers."
- "We suggest a 'rural proofing' that accounts for challenges of implementing new technologies in a resource-poor environments and that tests viability for the end users – clinicians and patients."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- ICF is a global consulting and technology firm with more than 50 years of experience supporting federal health agencies
  - Interdisciplinary teams span policy, technology, analytics, and human-centered design
  - Approximately 9,000 full-time and part-time employees worldwide
  - Annual revenues of about $2.0 billion
  - Deep familiarity with CMS program and payment levers that can incentivize safe clinical AI adoption
  - Develops certifiable and auditable frameworks for providers and vendors

- Executive Summary
  - ICF brings long-standing federal footprint with expertise in federal health
  - Supports agencies in modernizing data, technology, and programs
  - End-to-end capabilities spanning strategy and policy, research and evaluation, digital modernization, data platforms and analytics
  - Recommendations focus on translating policy intent into operational requirements, implementable governance, measurable outcomes, and scalable technology approaches
  - Key areas of expertise include payment and program levers through CMS quality programs, certifiable requirements and auditable controls, and evaluation and human-centered methods

- Incentivizing Effective Use of AI in Clinical Care (Q. #2)
  - HHS can and should incentivize effective AI use in clinical care
  - Recommends replicating the CEHRT/Promoting Interoperability playbook
    - Set a clear, testable "floor" for acceptable technology and use
    - Attach meaningful scoring and payment consequences
  - Specific CMS recommendations:
    - Add a QPP definition for certifiable class of clinical AI ("certified clinical AI module") building on existing CEHRT definitional structure in 42 CFR § 414.1305
    - Update PI performance category at 42 CFR § 414.1375 to create scored objective/measure set for "safe and effective clinical AI use"
      - Limited to certified modules
      - Requires auditable basics: version transparency/audit logs, clinician override, performance monitoring
      - Ensures CMS pays for verified capability rather than marketing claims
    - Add AI governance and post-deployment monitoring as high-weight Improvement Activities via IA inventory process at 42 CFR § 414.1355
    - Establish one or more "AI-enabled care" MIPS Value Pathways (MVPs) under 42 CFR § 414.1365
      - Bundle AI expectations across Quality/Cost/IA/PI instead of scattering optional attestations
  - Advanced APM alignment recommendations:
    - Build on existing Advanced APM CEHRT criterion at 42 CFR § 414.1415
    - Require high-impact AI used in clinical decision support or automation to meet certifiable, auditable, monitored standards
    - Direct analogue to how "use CEHRT" is operationalized today
  - CMMI Innovation Models recommendations:
    - Prioritize design changes that lower upfront adoption barrier while only paying for AI when it produces validated value
    - Hardwire an "AI capability track" into high-leverage models
    - LEAD model positioned to launch after ACO REACH concludes end of 2026
      - Allow infrastructure-style payments or enhanced care management payments for certified AI deployment
      - Gate shared savings/benchmarks on monitored performance and equity checks
    - Kidney Care Choices (KCC) extended through 2027
      - Add standardized option for certified AI-enabled risk stratification/care management
      - Require ongoing monitoring and evaluation tied to model endpoints
    - Enhancing Oncology Model (EOM) began July 2023, added second cohort July 2025
      - Ready platform for AI-enabled symptom monitoring and care navigation
    - TEAM as mandatory episode-based model
      - Require transparency and auditability safeguards for AI used in episode identification, discharge planning, or post-acute decision support

- Novel Issues with AI in Non-Medical Devices (Q. #3)
  - AI in non-medical devices in clinical care settings raises novel legal and implementation issues
  - Gaps emerging in existing frameworks governing liability, privacy, safety, and accountability
  - HHS well-positioned through OneHHS approach to lead
  - Two priority actions recommended:
    - Establish voluntary certification framework for non-medical AI tools modeled on ONC's Health IT Certification Program
      - Evaluate and compare transparency and documentation
      - Safety monitoring and performance metrics
      - Bias and fairness assessments
      - Data protection and access safeguards
    - Issue model contracting and procurement guidance building off OMB Memorandum M-25-22 and HHS AI Strategy
      - Model auditability
      - Data rights and ownership
      - Security and privacy protections
      - Prohibitions on unauthorized model training
      - Clear user responsibilities

- Most Promising AI Evaluation Methods (Q. #4)
  - Best methods for ensuring safe, reliable operation with LLM-powered AI solutions are currently subject to research and debate
  - NIST's AI Risk Management Framework provides useful lens with four functions: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage
  - Focus on users is best way to ensure evaluation mechanisms align with desired outcomes
  - Care providers likely to be greatest obstacle to broad adoption
  - HHS should use Human Centered Design to position care providers and patients at center of evaluation method design
  - Encourage incremental, evolutionary approach to create successes and build confidence
    - Creates flywheel effect building momentum
  - Promising evaluation methods:
    - Employing LLMs as judges
    - Adjusting LLM training approaches to penalize hallucinations
    - Using dynamic evaluation methods to adjust benchmarks as LLM capabilities improve
    - Employing observability with tools like Langfuse
    - Red teaming to discover unanticipated LLM vulnerabilities
  - Layered AI evaluations concept:
    - Evaluate LLM responses in isolation
    - Evaluate artifacts created from those responses by AI solutions
    - Evaluate experiences those responses, artifacts, and solutions create
  - Evaluation metrics:
    - Accuracy broken into components: consistency, factual correctness, error count
    - Relevance: how well they answered the question, whether they accessed right data
    - User feedback on relevance essential as people assess relevancy better than machines
    - Tone and clarity requiring human assessment
    - Fact-based and unbiased outputs per OMB Memo M-26-04
  - Supporting development of improved evaluation methods:
    - Develop and provide "gold standard" datasets for training and evaluation
    - Use SBIRs to encourage development of new evaluation methods and tools
    - Focused grants and prize competitions for LLMs-as-judges in clinical care domains
    - Grants for developing additional models and datasets tuned to medical needs
    - Grants for automated mechanisms to dynamically keep models and datasets up to date
    - Grants for effective red-teaming techniques for clinical care scenarios
    - Provide guidance and standards for human-centered evaluation processes
    - Standards for acceptable levels of trust and bias, use of LLMs-as-judges, handling unanticipated failure modes

- Human-Centered Design in Clinical Care AI
  - HCD builds reliability and trust by turning human interactions, recovery from errors, and real-life use situations into clear evaluation goals
  - Model-level checks show if AI can do a task; HCD looks at whether system works reliably in clinical workflows
  - Usability testing with clinicians reveals if uncertainty is communicated clearly, if explanations make sense under time pressure, if interface helps clinicians use AI properly
  - Signals like how often clinicians override AI, make corrections, or hesitate act as early signs of trust and reveal potential system failures
  - HCD complements other LLM evaluation methods by considering real-world context
    - Two models might get similar accuracy scores but HCD testing can reveal one offers explanations more likely to lead to misunderstanding
  - HCD assists effective monitoring after deployment
    - Tracks how trust in system changes over time
    - Catches whether clinicians start ignoring AI outputs, trusting them too much, or changing work in unexpected ways
    - Patterns tracked with feedback tools: pop-up prompts, regular surveys, clinician decisions to override or correct
  - HHS should support HCD approaches including intentional human-to-human engagement
    - Direct observation of clinicians using AI tools
    - One-on-one interviews and small-group listening sessions
    - Uncovers tacit knowledge, context-specific decision cues, early signs of misunderstanding or misuse
    - Builds trust and accelerates adoption by involving clinicians in design decisions

- HHS Support for Private Sector Activities (Q. #5)
  - HHS can support private sector activities using "tried-and-true" federal playbooks:
    - Risk-based premarket review and lifecycle oversight (FDA)
    - Reusable third-party assurance that agencies can rely on (FedRAMP)
    - Scaled compliance pathways mixing self-attestation with independent certification (FCC)
    - Rapid adoption enabled by clear flexibilities, aligned payment, and temporary enforcement discretion (COVID-era telehealth)
  - Common approach: government sets clear, testable expectations and recognizes independent private-sector assurance artifacts
  - FDA approach:
    - Define risk tiers and evidence expectations for clinical AI
    - Formally recognize qualified private sector entities generating standardized "assurance packages"
    - Publish standardized submission templates covering intended use, training/validation data provenance, performance limits, human oversight, post-deployment monitoring
    - Recognize third-party testing/certification programs producing evidence in those formats
    - Creates predictable, scalable path for "trusted AI" eligibility in Medicare and Medicaid
  - FedRAMP approach:
    - Establish "assess once, reuse many times" model for clinical AI assurance
    - Private assessors perform independent evaluations; HHS reuses results across programs
    - Establish reusable authorization construct ensuring standards for security, privacy, auditability, monitoring, and performance
    - Recognize accredited "clinical AI assessment organizations"
    - Allow providers/payers to rely on reusable authorizations to encourage adoption at scale
  - FCC approach:
    - Create two compliance tracks supporting innovation while protecting patients
    - Lower-friction supplier attestation path for low-risk tools
    - Higher-rigor independent certification path for higher-impact clinical uses
    - Permit streamlined attestation for limited-scope administrative AI with audit rights and disclosures
    - Require independent certification for AI materially influencing diagnosis, triage, treatment, or autonomous clinical actions
    - Encourage consistency in labels, logs, and change-management disclosures
  - COVID-era telehealth approach:
    - Remove adoption blockers quickly
    - Align payment and clarify compliance expectations
    - Use time-bound flexibilities and explicit evaluation to decide what becomes permanent
    - Create time-limited "AI adoption pilots" with clear guardrails
    - Aligned reimbursement and reporting
    - Targeted compliance flexibilities where appropriate
    - Use collected evidence to determine what to institutionalize as durable accreditation/certification expectations

- Widening Opportunities through Interoperability (Q. #8)
  - Enhanced interoperability will widen market opportunities, advance clinical research, and accelerate AI development
  - AI effectiveness only as good as consistency, quality, and portability of data
  - Clinical data remains fragmented across EHR platforms, public health systems, research environments, claims systems, and social service data repositories
  - Data often not "AI ready," resulting in unreliable, unsafe, biased, and non-portable outputs
  - Data types with greatest impact:
    - EHR data (diagnosis, medications, lab results)
    - Imaging and diagnostics (DICOM) data
    - Claims and utilization data
    - Public health and surveillance data
    - Genomic data
    - Data from community resources
    - Data from personal devices
    - Unstructured clinical narratives and administrative metadata
  - Data should be longitudinally linked for predictive modeling, clinical decision support, population health assessments, early interventions, and research at scale
  - Recommended standards for consistent, broad adoption:
    - HL7, FHIR, USCDI, DICOM, LOINC, and SNOMED
    - API-driven architectures to embed AI tools directly into clinical workflows
  - Standards allow AI providers to build solutions once and deploy across multiple EHRs, provider networks, research institutions, and public health agencies
  - Reduces need for custom integrations that increase cost, limit competition, and slow innovation
  - Lowers barriers to entry for innovators and expands market for AI-enabled clinical tools
  - Market impact:
    - Interoperable data environments enable clinicians and researchers to confidently use AI tools in diagnostics, pediatric oncology, chronic disease research
    - Allows normalization and semantic alignment across data sources
    - Maintains strong data lineage and provenance
    - Supports transparent documentation
    - Strengthens governance by enabling privacy-preserving de-identification, bias detection, responsible data sharing
    - Streamlines multi-omic and clinical data integration
    - Supports reproducible science
    - Expands opportunities for cross-institution research
  - Benchmarking:
    - Enhanced interoperability enables creation of "gold standard" benchmark datasets
    - Improves model validation across representative cohorts and care settings
    - Recommends government build benchmark datasets or sponsor their development
    - Require standardized structures, robust metadata, and privacy-preserving pipelines
    - Reduces model fragmentation
    - Prevents AI systems from being trained on narrow datasets limiting generalizability or introducing biases

- Specific Areas of AI Research to Prioritize (Q. #10)
  - AI's true value appreciated when it effectively augments human providers within clinical workflows
  - Most healthcare AI research focuses on algorithm accuracy in isolation, ignoring user experience, workflow integration, and human factors
  - HHS should prioritize R&D putting human users at center of AI design and evaluation by embracing HCD
  - Two specific research areas stand out:
    - Human-AI Interaction
      - Addresses greatest obstacle to adoption: clinician buy-in and workflow fit
      - If AI tools are intuitive, reduce human effort, and demonstrably enhance care delivery, providers more likely to embrace them
      - Even highly accurate algorithm will fail if difficult to use or disrupts proven clinical routines
      - Would accelerate transition of AI from impressive technology to core component of trusted approaches
    - Trustworthy AI
      - Encourages adoption by reducing skepticism
      - Provides evidence, methods, and tools to create confidence AI systems are safe, secure, and accountable
      - Clinicians more likely to trust AI if system has been stress-tested for safety and bias, can explain itself, backed by governance
      - Patients more receptive to AI-driven care with transparency and unbiased output
      - Fosters foundational "social license" for AI in healthcare
  - Research should be designed to have direct, positive impact on patients
    - Driving better patient outcomes
    - Improving patient experience
    - Balance science with responsive, personalized care and clear, compassionate communication
  - Research priorities table:
    - Human-AI Interaction: frameworks to measure and improve human-AI collaboration, HCD alignment, prevent automation bias
      - Patient impact: more time with patients, clearer communication, reduced frustration, greater confidence of being heard
    - Trustworthy AI: reliability, explainability, bias mitigation, post-deployment monitoring, align with NIST AI RMF and FUTURE-AI principles
      - Patient impact: confidence in safety and fairness, transparent decisions, reduced risk of harm, equitable treatment
    - Reinforcement Learning and Adaptive AI: optimize sequential decisions, personalize care, safe RL techniques
      - Patient impact: more personalized care, better long-term outcomes, reduced trial-and-error
    - Collaborative and Multi-Agent Systems: human-AI teaming, multi-modal AI, game theory for multi-stakeholder decision-making
      - Patient impact: seamless coordinated care, fewer gaps or redundancies, holistic decisions
    - AI Security and Privacy: defend against adversarial attacks, privacy-preserving AI (federated learning, differential privacy)
      - Patient impact: confidence in data security, protection from malicious AI behavior, willingness to engage with digital tools
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Federal health consulting firm with deep CMS expertise urges HHS to accelerate clinical AI adoption by leveraging existing payment programs, establishing voluntary certification frameworks, and prioritizing human-centered design research to ensure AI tools actually fit clinical workflows.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** ICF Incorporated, L.L.C.
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Global consulting and technology firm with 50+ years supporting federal health agencies; deep expertise in CMS programs, payment levers, health IT certification, and human-centered design
- **Geographic Scope:** National (headquarters in Reston, VA; publicly traded on NASDAQ)
- **Stake in Issue:** Positioned to provide consulting, implementation, and technology services for clinical AI adoption; direct experience translating policy into operational requirements for HHS divisions
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe HHS should accelerate safe and effective clinical AI adoption by using proven CMS mechanisms to create meaningful incentives, establishing voluntary certification for non-medical AI, advancing evaluation methods that combine NIST-aligned risk management with layered testing and robust post-deployment monitoring, and expanding interoperability through consistent adoption of key health data standards. The fastest path forward is to replicate what worked for CEHRT and Promoting Interoperability—set a clear, testable floor for acceptable technology and attach meaningful payment consequences.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Replicate the CEHRT/Promoting Interoperability playbook for clinical AI
  - Add QPP definition for "certified clinical AI module" building on existing CEHRT structure
  - Update PI performance category to create scored objective/measure set for "safe and effective clinical AI use"
  - Add AI governance and post-deployment monitoring as high-weight Improvement Activities
  - Establish "AI-enabled care" MIPS Value Pathways to bundle AI expectations across Quality/Cost/IA/PI

- Extend AI requirements to Advanced APMs
  - Build on existing Advanced APM CEHRT criterion
  - Require high-impact AI in clinical decision support to meet certifiable, auditable, monitored standards

- Hardwire "AI capability track" into CMMI Innovation Models
  - LEAD model: allow infrastructure payments for certified AI deployment, gate shared savings on monitored performance and equity checks
  - Kidney Care Choices: add standardized option for AI-enabled risk stratification with ongoing monitoring
  - Enhancing Oncology Model: platform for AI-enabled symptom monitoring and care navigation
  - TEAM: require transparency and auditability safeguards for AI in episode identification and discharge planning

- Establish voluntary certification framework for non-medical AI tools
  - Model on ONC's Health IT Certification Program
  - Evaluate transparency, safety monitoring, bias assessments, and data protection

- Issue model contracting and procurement guidance
  - Cover model auditability, data rights, security protections, prohibitions on unauthorized model training

- Support development of improved AI evaluation methods
  - Develop "gold standard" datasets for training and evaluation
  - Use SBIRs, grants, and prize competitions for LLMs-as-judges, red-teaming techniques, and dynamic evaluation methods
  - Provide guidance and standards for human-centered evaluation processes

- Adopt federal playbooks for private sector support
  - FDA approach: define risk tiers, recognize third-party testing/certification programs
  - FedRAMP approach: "assess once, reuse many times" with accredited clinical AI assessment organizations
  - FCC approach: two compliance tracks—attestation for low-risk, independent certification for high-impact
  - COVID telehealth approach: time-limited AI adoption pilots with clear guardrails and aligned reimbursement

- Promote consistent adoption of interoperability standards
  - HL7, FHIR, USCDI, DICOM, LOINC, and SNOMED
  - API-driven architectures to embed AI directly into clinical workflows
  - Create or sponsor "gold standard" benchmark datasets with standardized structures and privacy-preserving pipelines

- Prioritize research in Human-AI Interaction and Trustworthy AI
  - Focus on clinician buy-in, workflow fit, and user experience
  - Invest in reliability, explainability, bias mitigation, and post-deployment monitoring
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Care providers are likely to be the greatest obstacle to broad AI adoption in clinical care settings
  - Even highly accurate algorithms will fail if difficult to use or disruptive to proven clinical routines

- Clinical data remains fragmented across EHR platforms, public health systems, research environments, claims systems, and social service repositories
  - Limits ability to create scalable, generalizable AI solutions

- Data is often not "AI ready"
  - Results in unreliable, unsafe, biased, and non-portable outputs
  - Undermines clinical trust and regulatory readiness

- Best methods for ensuring safe, reliable operation with LLM-powered AI solutions are currently subject to research and debate
  - Further work required to devise best approaches for understanding, testing, and evaluating these technologies

- Gaps emerging in existing frameworks governing liability, privacy, safety, and accountability for AI in non-medical devices

- Most healthcare AI research focuses on algorithm accuracy in isolation
  - Ignores user experience, workflow integration, and human factors crucial for adoption and safe use

- Risk of fragmented incentives if AI expectations are scattered as optional attestations rather than bundled coherently
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Layered evaluation concept for clinical AI: evaluate LLM responses in isolation, then artifacts created by AI solutions, then the experiences those create for both patients and providers—this comprehensive approach is especially important because both patients and care providers interact with AI solutions

- Flywheel effect for AI adoption: initial successes build confidence, leading to further adoption and additional successes, creating momentum that expands from there—recommends incremental, evolutionary approach rather than big-bang deployment

- HCD-based monitoring can catch patterns that technical evaluations miss: whether clinicians start ignoring AI outputs, trusting them too much, or changing their work in unexpected ways—tracked through simple feedback tools like pop-up prompts, surveys linked to specific AI uses, or override/correction decisions

- Two models might score identically on factual accuracy, but HCD testing can reveal one offers explanations more likely to lead to clinician misunderstanding of risk

- Signals like how often clinicians override AI, make corrections, or hesitate act as early warning signs of trust issues and potential system failures

- Involving clinicians in HCD processes builds trust and accelerates adoption: they see their concerns reflected in design decisions and experience tools as shaped with them rather than delivered to them
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "This will ensure CMS pays for verified capability rather than marketing claims."

- "If AI tools and solutions are intuitive, reduce human effort, and demonstrably enhance care delivery, providers are far more likely to embrace them. Conversely, even a highly accurate algorithm will fail in practice if it is difficult to use or disrupts proven clinical routines."

- "By investing in trustworthy AI R&D, HHS will foster the foundational 'social license' for AI in healthcare—building public trust and providing the guardrails so innovation can proceed more rapidly without undue risk."
