structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care and its adoption in clinical care
  - Provider buy-in is the practical barrier - their confidence in efficacy and skepticism that tools are "just another expense"
  - Personal experience as patient and parent shows some AI tools positively received
    - Sons' endocrinologists use AI scribes regularly for notetaking and follow-up
    - Both sons have Type 1 diabetes
    - These tools have helped medical community become comfortable with AI
  - Future innovations need to take small steps forward for most providers to embrace them
  - Providers believe in rigorous evidence - trained on randomized clinical trials as gold standard
  - In practice, providers collect data through every observation with their own patients
  - For AI to flourish in clinical care, providers need confidence that algorithms are based on appropriate data
    - Initially will want to vet that answers/support are accurate and align with their own experience before trusting it
  - Gaining provider trust is avenue to buy-in that new tool costs are worth it
  - Policymakers could facilitate AI tool use by:
    - Supporting independent assessments in a clearinghouse
    - Less costly option: identifying preferred characteristics and operational parameters for high-quality clinical tools
    - Information can come from frontline providers working with tools

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices in clinical care and HHS support mechanisms
  - Assessments could be rooted in implementation science framework
  - Workflow is underlying and assumed benefit of AI in clinical care
    - AI use should ease workflow and allow more meaningful patient interaction
  - Data from AI agents in clinical care offers unique opportunity to assess implementation
  - With less burden than previous research, could answer questions like:
    - How do providers use available tools?
    - Is information from tools accurate?
    - Do providers continue using same tool for similar patients or does use drop off?
    - Is practice of care for specific conditions different with tools, and how?
  - Deployment of new tools could allow robust testing in short timeframes
  - Important that testing occur in multitude of different, varied locations
    - Understand how different providers in different settings use new technologies
  - CMS Innovation Center could consider tiered model approach:
    - First: solicit organizations selling AI tools to participate and put tools "to the test"
    - Second: solicit providers and provider groups to participate
    - May need further incentives for providers in some settings (e.g., rural communities)
  - CMS could request large health systems using in-house AI tools to participate with their own technologies
  - Precedent exists: CMS disease management program testing from 2003-2008
  - Ways to evaluate without randomized controlled trials or "usual care" comparison groups
  - Assessment should also consider patient-facing tools supporting providers and patients

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities to promote innovative AI use in clinical care
  - Development of standards for industry-testing could support provider buy-in and confidence in AI tool efficacy
  - Going as far as accreditation and certification may be resource-intensive
    - Only companies with available resources (or able to charge premium) may afford these steps

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities and accelerate AI development
  - Drawing heavily on experience as father of children with T1D and son with father and mother-in-law with same disease
  - For individuals with T1D, lots of data available but not always easily accessible to them or providers
    - Data not integrated with other information
  - Many individuals with T1D use continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) to track blood sugar continuously
    - Not nearly enough people use them
    - Not many providers or patients have access to the data (collected in 5-minute intervals)
  - Freeing up CGM data for patient use with AI algorithms could be major step forward
    - Could support care and optimize insulin dosages
  - Highs and lows from less accurate insulin dosing cause frequent stress and concern
    - Too often result in concomitant mental health conditions
  - Patients using automated insulin delivery devices (insulin pumps) could bring together insulin dosage and blood sugar data for stronger control
  - Non-FDA approved, community-built apps already exist for this purpose (e.g., Trio)
  - T1D could be promising pilot opportunity for federal government given:
    - Prevalence of T1D in United States
    - Annual incidence rate
  - Family members do not use Trio app but have met families that do

- Question 9: Challenges patients and caregivers wish AI to address and concerns about AI adoption
  - As caregiver, significant concern is reliability of potential AI technology
  - T1D is very challenging and frustrating to manage
  - Until cell therapy cures exist, no "silver bullet" solution
  - As father of two teenagers with T1D, each son is completely different in reaction to insulin and carbohydrate-laden foods
    - This is typical of T1D community
  - Faces dichotomy as parent:
    - Desperately wants someone to harness AI/agentic AI to develop tool supporting children
    - Would relieve daily stress from children and wife on making decisions
    - Established that people with T1D make 180-300 additional health-related decisions daily
    - Would love tool to relieve stress of these decisions
  - On other hand, would be cautious about any tool purporting to be solution without knowing it was tested and vetted in standardized, rigorous fashion
  - Core concern: Can we have efficient process to vet tools that is timely, patient-centered, and cost-efficient to provide families confidence that tools in clinical care settings are safe and reliable?

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - Please prioritize Type 1 diabetes space for AI research exploration
  - Just as important: individuals with evidence of T1D antibodies but not yet insulin dependent
  - Technology and ability exists today to screen for all antibodies proven to result in beta cell degradation
  - One son was diagnosed with antibodies and monitored for multiple years before becoming insulin dependent
  - Unfortunately, not everyone who could develop T1D is tested
  - "Crashing into" T1D is very traumatic event for people with disease and loved ones
  - AI research could support:
    - Individuals currently insulin dependent
    - Knowledge of who might most likely have antibodies
    - Individuals who have antibodies
    - Providers and caregivers
  - Information for individuals about to become or currently insulin-dependent has proliferated
    - Making sense of it for each individual case can be overwhelming
  - For both patients and providers (many of whom don't know much about T1D), education modules tied to AI could support better learning and reduce stress of disease

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
A father of two teenagers with Type 1 diabetes urges HHS to prioritize T1D as a pilot for AI innovation, arguing that provider trust, rigorous testing standards, and data interoperability are essential to delivering tools that could relieve the 180-300 daily health decisions his family faces.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Dominick Esposito
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Parent and caregiver of two teenagers with Type 1 diabetes; also has father and mother-in-law with T1D
- **Geographic Scope:** National (United States)
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct caregiver managing daily diabetes care decisions for children; personally invested in AI tools that could reduce decision burden and improve glucose management

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
I desperately want AI tools that could relieve the daily stress my family faces managing my sons' Type 1 diabetes, but I need confidence these tools are rigorously tested and reliable. Providers need to trust AI before they'll adopt it, and that trust comes from evidence-based validation and standards—not just promises. HHS should prioritize T1D as a pilot case for AI innovation given the disease's prevalence and the unique opportunity to leverage continuous glucose monitoring data.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Create a clearinghouse for independent assessments of AI tools, or at minimum, publish preferred characteristics and operational parameters for high-quality clinical AI
  - Draw on frontline provider input
- Root AI evaluations in implementation science frameworks to assess real-world workflow impacts
- Have CMS Innovation Center develop a tiered testing model:
  - Solicit AI tool vendors to put products "to the test"
  - Recruit providers and provider groups to participate
  - Offer incentives for rural and underserved settings
  - Include large health systems with in-house AI tools
- Develop industry-testing standards to build provider confidence without requiring resource-intensive accreditation
- Prioritize enhanced interoperability for continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data
  - Free up 5-minute interval data for patient and provider use
  - Enable integration with insulin pump dosage data
- Prioritize Type 1 diabetes as a pilot area for AI research
  - Include pre-symptomatic individuals with T1D antibodies
  - Develop AI-powered education modules for patients and providers

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Provider skepticism that AI tools are "just another expense" without proven value
- Providers need to vet AI outputs against their own experience before trusting algorithms
- CGM data is collected but not easily accessible to patients or providers
  - Data not integrated with other health information
- Reliability of AI technology is a significant concern as a caregiver
  - T1D management is highly individualized—each of his sons reacts completely differently to insulin and food
  - No "silver bullet" solution exists
- Need for efficient, timely, patient-centered, cost-efficient vetting process
  - Families need confidence that tools are safe and reliable
- Accreditation and certification may be too resource-intensive
  - Only well-resourced companies could afford it, potentially limiting innovation
- Many people who could develop T1D are never tested for antibodies
- "Crashing into" T1D diagnosis is traumatic for patients and families
- Many providers don't know much about T1D, creating education gaps

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Sons' endocrinologists already use AI scribes successfully for notetaking and follow-up, demonstrating that small-step adoption builds provider comfort
- One son was diagnosed with T1D antibodies and monitored for years before becoming insulin dependent—illustrating the opportunity for early intervention
- Has met families using Trio, a non-FDA approved community-built app that integrates CGM and insulin pump data, showing grassroots innovation filling gaps
- The highs and lows from inaccurate insulin dosing frequently cause mental health conditions in T1D patients—an underappreciated consequence
- Each of his two teenage sons with T1D reacts completely differently to the same insulin and foods, illustrating why personalized AI approaches are essential
- References CMS disease management program testing (2003-2008) as precedent for "put your approach to the test" evaluation model

---
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "It is established that people with T1D make 180 to 300 additional health-related decisions a day. We would love a tool to relieve the stress of making these decisions."
- "Can we have an efficient process to vet tools that is timely, is patient-centered, and cost-efficient so that we can provide families confidence that the tools being rolled out into any clinical care setting are safe and reliable?"
- "Unfortunately, not everyone who could develop T1D is tested. And, more unfortunately, 'crashing into' T1D is a very traumatic event for people with this disease and their loved ones."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care and its adoption in clinical care
  - Practically, the biggest barrier is buy-in from providers to use new AI tools
    - Providers lack confidence in AI efficacy
    - Skepticism that tools are "just going to be another expense"
  - As a patient and parent, I have seen some AI tools positively received by providers
    - My sons' endocrinologists use AI scribes regularly for notetaking and follow up
    - Both my boys have Type 1 diabetes
    - These tools have helped the medical professional community become comfortable with AI
  - Future innovations need to take small steps forward for most providers to embrace them
  - Providers believe in rigorous evidence
    - They are trained on randomized clinical trials as the gold standard for care
    - In practice, they collect data through every observation with their own patients
  - For AI to flourish in clinical care, providers need confidence that algorithms are based on appropriate data
    - Initially, they will want to vet that answers/support are accurate and align with their own experience before trusting it
  - Gaining providers' trust is one avenue to getting buy-in that new tools are worth the cost
  - Policymakers could facilitate AI tool use by:
    - Supporting independent assessments of tools in a clearinghouse
    - Or less costly: identifying preferred characteristics and operational parameters for high quality clinical tools
    - Some information can come from providers on the front lines working with tools

- Question 4: Most promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices in clinical care
  - Assessments could be rooted in an implementation science framework
  - Workflow is an underlying and assumed benefit of AI in clinical care
    - AI use by clinicians should ease workflow and allow more meaningful patient interaction
  - Data from AI agents in clinical care offers unique opportunity to assess implementation
    - With much less burden than previous research, we could answer:
      - How do providers use available tools?
      - Is information from tools accurate?
      - Do providers continue using the same tool for similar patients or does use drop off?
      - Is practice of care for specific conditions different with tools, and how?
  - Deployment of new tools could allow robust testing in short timeframes
    - Important that testing occur in varied locations to understand how different providers in different settings use technologies
  - CMS Innovation Center could consider a tiered model:
    - First, solicit organizations selling AI tools to participate and put tools "to the test"
    - Second, solicit providers and provider groups to participate
    - May need further incentives for providers in some settings (e.g., rural communities)
  - CMS could request large health systems using in-house AI tools to participate with their own technologies
  - Precedent exists: CMS disease management program testing from 2003 to 2008
  - Tools can be evaluated without randomized controlled trials or "usual care" comparison groups
  - Assessments should also consider patient-facing tools that support providers and patients

- Question 5: How HHS can best support private sector activities to promote innovative AI use
  - Development of standards for industry-testing could support providers' buy-in and confidence in AI tool efficacy
  - Going as far as accreditation and certification may be resource-intensive
    - Only companies with available resources (or able to charge users a premium) may afford these steps

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities and accelerate AI development
  - Drawing heavily on experience as father of children with Type 1 Diabetes and son with father and mother-in-law with same disease
  - For individuals living with T1D, lots of data available but not always easily accessible to them or providers
    - Data is certainly not integrated with other information
  - Example: Many individuals with T1D use continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) to track blood sugar continuously
    - Not nearly enough people use them
    - Not many providers or patients have access to the data (collected in 5-minute intervals)
  - Freeing up CGM data for patients who might use AI algorithms to optimize insulin dosages could be major step forward
    - Highs and lows from less than accurate insulin dosing cause frequent stress and concern
    - Too often result in concomitant mental health conditions
  - Patients using automated insulin delivery devices (insulin pumps) could bring together insulin dosage information with blood sugar levels for stronger control
  - Non-FDA approved, community-built apps already exist for this purpose (e.g., Trio)
    - My children and family don't use this app but we've met families that do
  - T1D could be promising pilot opportunity for federal government given:
    - Prevalence of T1D in the United States
    - Annual incidence rate

- Question 9: Challenges patients and caregivers wish AI to address, and concerns about AI adoption
  - As a caregiver, significant concern is reliability of potential AI technology
  - T1D is very challenging and frustrating to manage
    - Until we have cell therapy cures, there is no "silver bullet" solution
  - As father of two teenagers with T1D, each boy is completely different in reaction to insulin and carbohydrate-laden foods
    - This is typical of the T1D community
  - I face a dichotomy:
    - On one hand: desperately want someone to harness AI/agentic AI to develop a tool supporting my children
      - Would relieve stress from them and my wife daily on making decisions
      - People with T1D make 180 to 300 additional health-related decisions a day
      - We would love a tool to relieve stress of making these decisions
    - On other hand: would be cautious about any tool purporting to be a solution without knowing it was tested and vetted in standardized, rigorous fashion
  - Core concern: Can we have an efficient process to vet tools that is timely, patient-centered, and cost-efficient to provide families confidence that tools in clinical care settings are safe and reliable?

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - Please prioritize the Type 1 diabetes space for AI research exploration
  - Just as important: individuals with evidence of T1D antibodies who are not yet insulin dependent
    - We have technology today to screen for all antibodies proven to result in beta cell degradation
    - One of my sons was diagnosed as such and we monitored him for multiple years before he became insulin dependent
  - Unfortunately, not everyone who could develop T1D is tested
  - More unfortunately, "crashing into" T1D is very traumatic for people with this disease and their loved ones
  - AI research could support:
    - Individuals currently insulin dependent
    - Knowledge of who might be most likely to have antibodies
    - Individuals who have these antibodies
    - Providers and caregivers
  - Information for individuals about to become or currently insulin-dependent has proliferated
    - Making sense of it for each individual case can be overwhelming
  - For both patients and providers (many of whom don't know a lot about T1D), education modules tied to AI could support better learning and reduce stress of this disease
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A father of two teenagers with Type 1 diabetes offers practical insights on building provider trust in AI tools while pleading for prioritization of T1D as a pilot area where AI could relieve the crushing burden of 180-300 daily health decisions.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Dominick Esposito
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Parent and caregiver for two children with Type 1 diabetes; also has father and mother-in-law with T1D
- **Geographic Scope:** National (United States)
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct caregiver managing daily diabetes care decisions for two teenagers; personally invested in AI tools that could reduce decision burden and improve blood sugar management
structuredSections.corePosition: I desperately want AI tools that could relieve the daily stress my family faces managing Type 1 diabetes, but I need confidence these tools are rigorously vetted before trusting them with my children's health. Providers need to trust AI before they'll adopt it, and that trust comes from evidence-based validation and small, incremental steps forward—not revolutionary leaps.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Create a clearinghouse for independent assessments of AI tools, or at minimum identify preferred characteristics and operational parameters for high-quality clinical tools
- Use an implementation science framework to evaluate AI tools in clinical care
  - Assess workflow improvements, accuracy, and sustained provider usage
  - Evaluate without requiring randomized controlled trials
- Have CMS Innovation Center develop a tiered testing model
  - Solicit AI tool vendors to put their products "to the test"
  - Recruit providers and provider groups to participate
  - Offer incentives for rural and underserved settings
  - Include large health systems with in-house AI tools
- Develop industry-testing standards to build provider confidence
  - Full accreditation/certification may be too resource-intensive and favor well-resourced companies
- Prioritize Type 1 diabetes as a pilot area for AI research
  - Focus on both insulin-dependent individuals and those with antibodies not yet insulin dependent
  - Develop AI-powered education modules for patients and providers
- Improve interoperability for continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data
  - Free up 5-minute interval data for patient and provider use
  - Enable integration with insulin pump data for better control
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Provider skepticism and lack of buy-in
  - Providers view new AI tools as "just another expense"
  - They require rigorous evidence before trusting algorithms
  - Trust must be earned through validation aligned with their clinical experience
- Reliability of AI technology for disease management
  - T1D management is highly individualized—each patient reacts differently to insulin and food
  - No "silver bullet" solution exists until cell therapy cures arrive
- Need for efficient, timely, patient-centered vetting process
  - Families need confidence that tools are safe and reliable
  - Process must be cost-efficient to avoid limiting access to well-resourced companies
- Data accessibility and interoperability gaps
  - CGM data collected every 5 minutes but not accessible to patients or providers
  - Data not integrated with other health information
  - Poor blood sugar control from inadequate data leads to stress and mental health conditions
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Personal observation of AI adoption: Sons' endocrinologists use AI scribes regularly, which has helped the medical community become comfortable with AI—suggesting incremental adoption works
- The 180-300 daily decisions burden: People with T1D make 180-300 additional health-related decisions every day, creating enormous stress for patients and caregivers
- Individual variability challenge: His two teenage sons with the same disease react completely differently to insulin and the same foods—illustrating why AI solutions can't be one-size-fits-all
- Pre-diagnosis monitoring experience: One son was identified as having T1D antibodies and monitored for years before becoming insulin dependent—highlighting opportunity for AI in early detection
- Community-built workarounds: Families are already using non-FDA approved apps like Trio to integrate CGM and insulin pump data, showing demand for solutions the market isn't providing
- "Crashing into" T1D: Many people aren't tested for antibodies and experience traumatic sudden onset—a preventable situation with better screening and AI support
- Mental health cascade: The stress of blood sugar highs and lows from inadequate insulin dosing "too often" results in concomitant mental health conditions
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "It is established that people with T1D make 180 to 300 additional health-related decisions a day. We would love a tool to relieve the stress of making these decisions."
- "I face this dichotomy. On the one hand, I desperately would love for someone to harness the power of AI/agentic AI/etc. to develop a tool to support my children so that some stress is relieved from them and my wife daily on making decisions. On the other hand, I would be cautious about any tool that purported to be a solution without knowing that it was tested and vetted in a standardized and rigorous fashion."
- "Unfortunately, not everyone who could develop T1D is tested. And, more unfortunately, 'crashing into' T1D is a very traumatic event for people with this disease and their loved ones."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- NCPDP is a not-for-profit ANSI Accredited Standards Developer with more than 1,300 members
  - Members include claims processors, data management vendors, federal and state government agencies, insurers, intermediaries, pharmaceutical manufacturers, pharmacies, pharmacy benefit managers, professional services organizations, software vendors, and others interested in electronic standardization within pharmacy services
  - NCPDP provides a forum for diverse membership to develop business solutions, including ANSI-accredited standards and guidance for promoting information exchanges related to medications, supplies, and services

- NCPDP supports HHS' focus on establishing a forward-leaning, industry-supportive, and secure approach to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care

- While NCPDP has no direct comment on HHS' approach to regulation and reimbursement, continued funding of TEFCA is an important part of research and development
  - Improving and enhancing data exchange among all health care participants is critical to improve patient outcomes, reduce administrative burden, and utilize emerging tools such as AI

- Question 1: Barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - One potential barrier is the slow pace at which standards for industry use are named in regulations (MMA, HIPAA, others)
  - Greater allowance for industry to move standards forward more quickly would result in greater innovation in AI
  - Instead of requiring a specific version of a standard, applying a minimum standard version expectation could result in faster updates to processes and expedited innovation for AI tools
  - Industry could adopt new features without waiting for government regulations to adopt new versions
  - Medicare Part D and HIPAA regulations could adopt a process similar to ASTP SVAP to facilitate faster adoption
  - Another barrier is the degree of transparency within AI tools
    - Example: use of AI when renewing a prescription should be disclosed and documented, ensuring transparency to the pharmacist in case of questions or concerns

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - AI tools in clinical care need to be transparent, disclosed, and documented throughout the care continuum for full transparency and accountability
  - New policies designed to enforce transparency are needed for clinicians and patients to trust health care processes using AI tools
  - Regulations needed to determine:
    - Who is responsible when AI generates incorrect clinical guidance
    - How AI documentation should be stored to protect clinicians
    - Safe-harbor provisions for clinicians and payers using CMS-approved, validated AI tools
  - Investing in AI literacy both pre- and post-entering the workforce is key to sustaining and developing AI tools
  - Literacy could be achieved through multiple channels:
    - Funding for creating national AI competency standards for health care providers
    - Grants for operational AI training focusing on medication therapy management, claims resolution, and workflow optimization
    - Working with national education accreditation organizations to develop AI curriculum for students entering health care

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical devices
  - Issues needing resolution at federal level to avoid patchwork of state regulations:
    - Consent for use of AI
    - Transparency
    - Accountability
    - Product liability
    - Regulatory authority
  - NCPDP recommends HHS convene expert panels including representation from patient advocacy groups and professional organizations from across the legal and clinical spectrum of health care

- Question 4: How HHS can support private sector activities
  - NCPDP welcomes and encourages HHS and other governmental organizations to more actively participate in standards development to promote innovative and effective AI use to support clinical care

- Question 5: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - Enhancing interoperability leads to more robust AI training and usage
  - Broader interoperability expands clinical data available for training AI models on health care, with expectation of creating better models and enhancing patient care
  - As participation in TEFCA increases, including pharmacy participation, expansion in interoperability of the care team should be a natural outcome

- NCPDP looks forward to serving as a trusted partner and resource as HHS works to create policies to ease health data exchange and promote innovation of AI in clinical care
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: National pharmacy standards organization urges HHS to speed up standards adoption processes, establish AI transparency requirements, create federal liability frameworks, and expand TEFCA participation to accelerate AI innovation in clinical care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** NCPDP (National Council for Prescription Drug Programs)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** ANSI Accredited Standards Developer for pharmacy services sector; 1,300+ members including pharmacies, PBMs, insurers, government agencies, pharmaceutical manufacturers, and technology vendors
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Develops electronic standards for medication-related information exchange; members directly affected by AI adoption policies in pharmacy and clinical care settings
structuredSections.corePosition: NCPDP supports HHS' approach to accelerating AI adoption in clinical care but believes the slow pace of standards adoption in federal regulations is a major barrier to innovation. We recommend allowing industry to move standards forward more quickly through minimum version requirements rather than specific version mandates, while establishing clear transparency, accountability, and liability frameworks at the federal level to avoid a patchwork of state regulations.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Adopt minimum standard version expectations instead of requiring specific versions in regulations
  - Would allow industry to adopt new features without waiting for government to adopt new versions
  - Medicare Part D and HIPAA regulations could adopt a process similar to ASTP SVAP
- Require transparency, disclosure, and documentation of AI use throughout the care continuum
  - Example: AI use in prescription renewals should be disclosed to pharmacists
- Establish federal regulations addressing:
  - Responsibility when AI generates incorrect clinical guidance
  - How AI documentation should be stored to protect clinicians
  - Safe-harbor provisions for clinicians and payers using CMS-approved, validated AI tools
- Invest in AI literacy for healthcare workforce
  - Fund creation of national AI competency standards for health care providers
  - Provide grants for operational AI training on medication therapy management, claims resolution, and workflow optimization
  - Work with accreditation organizations to develop AI curriculum for healthcare students
- Convene expert panels including patient advocacy groups and professional organizations to address consent, transparency, accountability, product liability, and regulatory authority issues
- Continue funding TEFCA and expand pharmacy participation
- Increase HHS participation in standards development organizations
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Slow pace of standards adoption in federal regulations (MMA, HIPAA) is a barrier to AI innovation
  - Current approach of requiring specific standard versions delays industry ability to innovate
- Lack of transparency in AI tools creates trust issues
  - Clinicians and patients need to know when AI is being used in their care
- Absence of federal framework for AI liability and accountability
  - Risk of patchwork state regulations if federal government doesn't act
  - Unclear who is responsible when AI generates incorrect clinical guidance
- Need for AI literacy across healthcare workforce
  - Both pre-career education and post-entry training needed
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Specific example of transparency need: when AI is used to renew a prescription, this should be disclosed and documented so pharmacists can address questions or concerns
- Suggests adapting existing regulatory framework (ASTP SVAP process) as model for faster standards adoption in Medicare Part D and HIPAA
- Identifies pharmacy participation in TEFCA as key to expanding interoperability and improving AI training data
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Instead of requiring a specific version of a standard, applying a minimum standard version expectation could result in faster updates to processes utilizing the standards and, therefore, expedited innovation available for use with and for AI tools."
- "Consent for the use of AI, transparency, accountability, product liability and regulatory authority are all issues that need to be resolved at a federal level to avoid a patchwork of state regulations."

submitter: American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME)

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- AFSCME is responding to HHS's request for information on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
  - AFSCME represents 1.4 million members nationwide in hundreds of occupations
  - Members include nurses, corrections officers, child care providers, sanitation workers
  - Represents hundreds of thousands of health care workers across clinics, nursing homes, and hospitals

- Fundamental concern with the framing of this RFI
  - The discussion never pauses to consider whether evidence supports widespread AI adoption as a net benefit
  - At this point in generative AI development, we need clearer understanding of:
    - Who is overseeing AI incorporation into new health care use cases
    - What assurances patients and health care workers have that tools meet their claimed competencies
  - Fragmented rollout and unclear regulatory oversight contribute to overall unease about accelerating adoption

- HHS must take a patient-forward and patient-supportive approach
  - Essential to protect patient choice, rights, and privacy
  - Should not impose technology substitutes for human clinical care on patients
    - Examples: AI avatars and ultrasound-administering robots
  - Must maintain clear lines of responsibility and liability for harms from AI use
  - Public investments in AI research should result in affordable care access
    - Should not repeat the flawed approach to drug R&D that allowed monopoly pricing for publicly funded research

- Current state of AI adoption in health care
  - Significant adoption has occurred but use cases remain somewhat limited
  - Uses resulting in high degree of success have been even more limited
  - HHS should pause and engage with outside expert panels to grapple with complexities
  - Burden of proof should be on AI developers to demonstrate products will do no harm
    - Beyond a reasonable level of doubt in clinical applications
  - AI firms operate with constant iteration, addressing issues only after real-world problems emerge
    - AFSCME members do not get to operate with such a carefree attitude
  - Need significant open dialogue about propriety of this approach in health care settings
  - Health care organizations, professionals, and patients need confidence AI tools won't exacerbate health inequities

- Track record of AI health care applications does not live up to hype
  - Recent Nature study found AI chatbots often get it wrong on diagnostic queries
    - Performed no better than simple internet search in guiding patients or diagnosing conditions
  - Recent investigation indicates AI contributing to abnormal rates of botched surgeries
  - AI has potential for productivity increases, enhanced diagnostics, and personalized medicine
    - Question is how to avoid predictable missteps when consequences are dire for patients
  - Tools are only as good as the data they're trained on
  - Patients should not be forced to serve as unwitting guinea pigs for tech corporation profits

- Concerns about adverse labor market impact without appropriate guardrails
  - Paramount importance: all health care workers must be able to override AI recommendations
    - When the individual believes it's not appropriate for the patient in front of them
  - Key principles the health care industry should operate within:
    - AI tools should not solely dictate health care worker workflows
    - AI tools should not solely determine patient acuity for scheduling purposes
    - AI tools should not replace clinician expertise by undermining autonomy or solely establishing care plans
    - Disregarding AI recommendations should not be basis for employer discipline of health care workers
    - Disregarding AI recommendations should not be basis for licensing board disciplinary proceedings

- Conclusion
  - Next generation AI tools have potential to advance human health
  - Must not let hype and boosterism blind us from potential pitfalls
  - Need prudent, responsible actions today so we may all get there together
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Major health care workers union urges HHS to pump the brakes on AI acceleration, demanding patient-first guardrails, worker override rights, and proof of safety before deployment rather than the current "iterate and fix later" approach.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME), submitted by Dalia R. Thornton, Director, Department of Research and Collective Bargaining Services
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Labor union representing 1.4 million public sector workers including hundreds of thousands of health care workers across clinics, nursing homes, and hospitals
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Members are frontline health care workers who will be directly affected by AI tools in their workflows, patient care decisions, and potentially their job security and professional autonomy
structuredSections.corePosition: We are deeply concerned that HHS is asking how to accelerate AI adoption without first asking whether the evidence supports it as a net benefit. The burden of proof should be on AI developers to demonstrate their products will do no harm, and health care workers must retain the ability to override AI recommendations when they believe it's not the right course for their patients. Patients should not serve as unwitting guinea pigs so tech corporations may profit.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- HHS should pause and engage with outside expert panels before accelerating AI adoption
  - Need realistic grappling with complexities of mass-scale AI incorporation across health care use cases
- Place burden of proof on AI product developers to demonstrate safety beyond reasonable doubt
- Maintain clear lines of responsibility and liability for patient harms from AI use
- Ensure public investments in AI research result in affordable care access
  - Avoid repeating drug R&D approach that allowed monopoly pricing on publicly funded research
- Establish key operating principles for health care industry:
  - AI tools should not solely dictate health care worker workflows
  - AI tools should not solely determine patient acuity for scheduling
  - AI tools should not replace clinician expertise or solely establish care plans
  - Disregarding AI recommendations should not be basis for employer discipline
  - Disregarding AI recommendations should not be basis for licensing board proceedings
- Guarantee all health care workers can override AI recommendations when they believe it's not appropriate for the patient
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Flawed framing of the RFI
  - Never considers whether evidence supports widespread AI adoption as net benefit
  - Unclear who oversees AI incorporation into new use cases
  - No assurances that tools meet their claimed competencies
  - Fragmented rollout and unclear regulatory oversight
- Patient safety and rights at risk
  - AI chatbots often get diagnostic queries wrong (Nature study)
  - AI contributing to abnormal rates of botched surgeries (Reuters investigation)
  - Tools only as good as training data, risking exacerbation of health inequities
  - Patients forced to serve as guinea pigs for tech corporation profits
- Inappropriate imposition of technology substitutes for human care
  - AI avatars and ultrasound-administering robots replacing human clinical care
- AI industry culture incompatible with health care standards
  - Constant iteration with issues addressed only after real-world problems emerge
  - AFSCME members don't get to operate with such a carefree attitude
- Adverse labor market impacts without guardrails
  - Risk of AI dictating workflows and scheduling
  - Potential undermining of clinician autonomy and expertise
  - Workers could face discipline for overriding AI recommendations
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Contrast between AI industry and health care worker accountability standards
  - AI firms operate with "constant iteration" fixing problems after deployment
  - Health care workers are held to strict standards and cannot operate with such a "carefree attitude"
  - This cultural mismatch creates fundamental tension when AI enters clinical settings
- Warning about repeating drug pricing mistakes
  - Public investment in AI research could follow same flawed path as drug R&D
  - Resulted in monopoly pricing for drugs developed with taxpayer funding
  - Opportunity to structure AI investments differently to ensure affordable access
- Specific concern about discipline dynamics
  - Workers who override AI recommendations could face employer discipline or licensing board action
  - Creates perverse incentive to defer to AI even when clinical judgment says otherwise
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AFSCME members do not get to operate with such a carefree attitude."
- "Patients today should not be forced to serve as unwitting guinea pigs so that tech corporations may profit."
- "We must not let hype and boosterism blind us from potential pitfalls and the need for prudent, responsible actions today so that we may all get there together."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and organizational background
  - Alliance brings together health information professionals, physicians, hospitals, and front-line healthcare providers
  - Focus is ensuring policy and standards development reflects clinical and operational challenges facing technology users
  - Working collaboratively on priorities for how technology can best support clinical care and operations

- General position on AI in healthcare
  - AI is rapidly transforming healthcare with promising non-clinical and clinical benefits across settings and specialties
  - Concerns remain about how AI models are developed, trained, used, and monitored
  - Significant impact on healthcare operations, patient care delivery, and health outcomes
  - Widespread adoption requires thoughtful oversight and governance frameworks to minimize risks
  - Must ensure appropriate, safe, and ethical use of AI
  - End-users are at the forefront of AI use and well-equipped to collaborate on common principles

- Alliance consensus statement from April 2025
  - Reflects current state of AI in healthcare
  - Provides principles to guide policymakers on appropriate oversight without hampering innovation
  - Highlights that end-users are often brought into development cycle only during implementation
  - Need for end-users to be engaged throughout entire AI development lifecycle
  - Recommends HHS prioritize bringing developers, policymakers, and end-users together from project outset
    - Ensures AI tools fit organizations' unique needs
    - Integrates with existing workflows
    - Promotes trust and confidence in tool use
    - Informs organization-specific governance frameworks

- Overarching policy goals
  - Foster innovation in AI while maintaining health data protection
  - Lower administrative burden
  - Improve health outcomes

- Regulation and oversight of AI
  - AI tools should augment, not replace, end-users' expertise
  - Policy must ensure tools supplement cognitive and administrative tasks while preserving human judgment
  - Requires risk-based approach to oversight
    - Level of scrutiny and validation should be proportional to potential harm
  - HHS should leverage ASTP/ONC's statutory coordinating role with component agencies
    - Office for Civil Rights (OCR)
    - Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS)
    - Other relevant agencies
  - Policies must be aligned across federal government to avoid competing and confusing standards
    - Could lead to non-compliance and increased burden
  - HIPAA regulatory frameworks must incorporate AI data handling practices
    - Protect patient privacy
    - Prohibit data exchange beyond minimum necessary
    - Prohibit secondary use of data without consent
    - Prevent data handling practices enabling unintended or unauthorized reidentification

- Safety and transparency
  - Transparency in AI development is critical
    - What data are used in decision-making
    - Governance plans
    - Ongoing testing and maintenance plans
  - AI developers should provide clear, understandable information on how AI makes predictions
    - Tailored to suit end-user needs
  - Transparency requirements should provide conceptual model on importance of data used for inferences
  - Patients and healthcare organizations must trust companies will maintain data confidentiality
  - AI developers should disclose how patient and end-user data is collected, stored, used, and shared

- Liability
  - AI companies dealing with health data should be held to same standards as HIPAA-covered entities
  - HHS should determine appropriate balance of accountability
    - Consider role of AI developers in creation, maintenance, and use of tools
    - Consider role of end-users in using these tools
  - HHS should partner with Federal Trade Commission on unfair and deceptive trade practices oversight
  - Providers should not be held liable for AI tool performance if they have:
    - Completed good faith evaluations
    - Taken steps to mitigate quality or safety concerns
  - Developers are often best positioned to prevent harm due to their knowledge of development, function, and intended use

- Cybersecurity
  - AI systems deeply integrated into clinical and operational environments face escalating cyber threats
  - Risks to patient safety and organizational integrity
  - End-users depend on AI tools that are secure by design
  - End-users often lack visibility into system vulnerabilities or control over defenses
  - AI developers are best positioned to mitigate and respond to cyberattacks
    - Direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure
  - HHS should strengthen regulatory guardrails for developer responsibility
    - Securing against intrusions
    - Preventing data exfiltration
    - Protecting against model manipulation
  - Holding end-users liable for breaches they cannot control would undermine trust and deter adoption
  - Robust, enforceable cybersecurity requirements for developers needed
    - Must align with federal health data protections
    - Essential to protect patients, sustain trust, and accelerate responsible innovation

- Real-world testing
  - Critical to conduct real-world testing across variety of clinical settings
  - Confirms technologies operate as expected without adverse patient consequences
  - Documentation of identified issues including implicit biases is critically important to end-users
  - End-users often included at end of policymaking and technology development, not beginning
    - Leads to technologies and policies that don't meet intended goals
  - HHS must prioritize policies including end-users throughout policymaking and development processes

- Coordination with NIST
  - Alliance actively working with NIST on AI Standards Zero Drafts Pilot Project
    - Initiative seeks to broaden stakeholder participation in AI standards creation
  - Applauds NIST's commitment to implementing Trump Administration's AI Action Plan directives
  - Alliance has provided feedback on four initial topic areas for draft standards documents
  - Provided feedback on first outline for testing, evaluation, verification, and validation
  - Held listening session with NIST for more detailed feedback
  - Encourages HHS to coordinate with NIST to advance nationwide AI standards
  - AI policy frameworks should be harmonized with such standards

- Closing commitment
  - Committed to being partner in identifying unnecessary barriers to AI innovation, development, and implementation
  - While ensuring patient safety and improving healthcare experience for all stakeholders
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Coalition of health IT end-users urges HHS to include frontline users throughout AI development, place liability and cybersecurity responsibility on developers rather than providers, and coordinate federal policies to avoid conflicting standards.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Health IT End-Users Alliance
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Coalition of health information professionals, physicians, hospitals, and healthcare providers who use health IT; actively engaged with NIST on AI standards development
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Members are frontline users of AI tools in clinical and operational settings; directly affected by liability, cybersecurity, and regulatory compliance requirements
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe AI tools should augment rather than replace end-user expertise, and that developers—not healthcare providers—should bear primary responsibility for AI safety, cybersecurity, and liability. End-users must be engaged throughout the entire AI development lifecycle, not just at implementation, to ensure tools fit organizational needs and integrate with existing workflows.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Bring developers, policymakers, and end-users together from the outset of AI projects
  - Ensures tools fit organizations' unique needs
  - Promotes integration with existing workflows
  - Builds trust and informs governance frameworks

- Implement risk-based approach to AI oversight
  - Level of scrutiny proportional to potential harm
  - Preserves human judgment in clinical decision-making

- Align AI policies across federal agencies through ASTP/ONC's coordinating role
  - Include OCR, CMS, and other relevant agencies
  - Avoid competing standards that create confusion and burden

- Update HIPAA frameworks to address AI data handling
  - Prohibit data exchange beyond minimum necessary
  - Require consent for secondary data use
  - Prevent practices enabling unauthorized reidentification

- Require AI developers to provide clear transparency on:
  - How AI makes predictions
  - What data is used for inferences
  - How patient data is collected, stored, used, and shared
  - Ongoing testing and maintenance plans

- Hold AI companies to same standards as HIPAA-covered entities

- Place primary liability on developers, not providers
  - Providers should not be liable if they completed good faith evaluations
  - Developers are best positioned to prevent harm

- Partner with FTC on unfair and deceptive trade practices oversight

- Require developers to bear primary responsibility for cybersecurity
  - Secure systems against intrusions, data exfiltration, and model manipulation
  - End-users lack visibility and control over system defenses

- Mandate real-world testing across diverse clinical settings
  - Document identified issues including implicit biases
  - Include end-users throughout testing process

- Coordinate with NIST to harmonize AI policy frameworks with national standards
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- End-users are typically brought into AI development only at implementation stage
  - Results in technologies and policies that don't meet intended goals
  - Misses opportunity to ensure tools fit organizational needs

- Risk of competing and confusing federal standards
  - Could lead to non-compliance
  - Increases administrative burden

- AI data handling practices may violate patient privacy
  - Secondary use without consent
  - Data exchange beyond minimum necessary
  - Potential for unauthorized reidentification

- Lack of transparency in AI decision-making
  - End-users don't understand how predictions are made
  - Unclear how patient data is used and shared

- Inappropriate liability allocation
  - Providers may be held responsible for AI failures they cannot control
  - Developers have superior knowledge of tool function and risks

- Cybersecurity vulnerabilities in AI systems
  - Escalating sophisticated cyber threats
  - End-users lack visibility into vulnerabilities
  - End-users cannot control system defenses
  - Holding end-users liable for breaches would undermine trust and deter adoption

- Implicit biases in AI tools may go undetected without proper real-world testing
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Alliance is actively participating in NIST's AI Standards Zero Drafts Pilot Project
  - Provided feedback on four initial topic areas
  - Contributed to outline on testing, evaluation, verification, and validation
  - Held dedicated listening session with NIST
  - Demonstrates practical engagement in standards development

- Published consensus statement in April 2025 on AI in healthcare
  - Developed principles to guide policymakers
  - Represents collective voice of frontline health IT users
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Holding end-users liable for breaches or system failures they cannot control would undermine trust and deter adoption."

- "End-users are often included at the end of the policymaking and technology development process, not the beginning, which leads to technologies and policies that do not meet their intended goals."

- "AI developers, not end-users, are best positioned to mitigate and respond to cyberattacks given their direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Introduction and organizational context
  - AASM appreciates opportunity to comment on AI use in clinical care
  - As clinicians diagnosing and managing sleep disorders daily, we see both promise and practical challenges of AI firsthand
  - In sleep medicine, AI has potential to improve diagnostic accuracy, expand access to care, and support earlier identification and treatment of common and costly conditions
  - Meaningful adoption will only occur if AI tools are reliable, transparent, independently validated, and clearly aligned with patient safety and clinician accountability
  - Comments reflect experience of more than 9,000 AASM physician members and teams working in over 2,400 AASM-accredited sleep programs
  - Members care for patients across diverse practice settings including hospital-based labs, independent sleep centers, and office-based practices
  - Many serve Medicare beneficiaries
  - Clinicians consistently emphasize AI must function as clinical support tool—not a black box that introduces new risks or uncompensated workload

- Barriers to innovation and how HHS can support private sector
  - Persistent uncertainty regarding medical-legal liability
    - Clinicians remain unclear whether responsibility for errors, adverse outcomes, or inappropriate recommendations rests with treating physician or technology developer
    - Creates unacceptable professional and malpractice risk, particularly when clinicians have limited insight into how algorithm was trained, validated, or updated
    - Without clear allocation of accountability, many physicians are understandably reluctant to rely on AI outputs in clinical decision-making
  - Lack of transparency and independent validation
    - Many AI systems function as "black boxes" with little disclosure regarding training datasets, population representativeness, validation methods, known limitations, or real-world performance
    - Lack of explainability prevents clinicians from assessing whether algorithm outputs are reliable for individual patients
    - Performance may vary across demographic or clinical subgroups
    - In sleep medicine, diagnostic determinations frequently drive long-term or lifelong therapy decisions including DME prescriptions and chronic disease management
    - Uncertainty about algorithmic output reliability raises significant patient safety concerns
    - Clinicians must be able to understand and defend rationale for clinical decisions—opaque systems undermine this fundamental professional responsibility
  - Operational and workflow challenges
    - AI tools often introduced without sufficient integration into existing EHR systems or clinical processes
    - Results in fragmented workflows, duplicative documentation, and increased administrative burden
    - Poorly designed tools may add steps requiring clinicians or staff to manually reconcile data, verify outputs, and correct errors
    - Inefficiencies diminish potential benefits and contribute to frustration and burnout
  - Reimbursement barriers
    - Many clinicians concerned payers may assume AI automatically delivers efficiency gains and reduce payment rates or increase productivity expectations
    - In practice, when AI outputs are unreliable, incomplete, or inconsistently formatted, clinicians must spend additional unbilled time reviewing results, reconciling discrepancies, validating data accuracy, and documenting clinical reasoning
    - This time-intensive oversight represents uncompensated labor and shifts costs from technology developers to frontline providers
    - For small, independent, and community-based practices operating on narrow margins, added burdens may make adoption financially infeasible
  - Concerns about consistency and durability
    - Algorithms that update over time without clear version control, locked validation, or re-certification processes may produce different outputs for same clinical scenario
    - Complicates longitudinal care and makes it difficult to compare results over time
    - Variability undermines clinician confidence, complicates quality measurement, and creates challenges for regulatory compliance and clinical documentation
    - Healthcare providers require stable, validated tools whose performance characteristics are predictable and reproducible

- Regulatory recommendations to incentivize AI use
  - AI vendors should bear legal responsibility for performance errors attributable to their algorithms
    - Clinicians should not be held liable for defects in tools they did not design, train, or control
  - Transparency requirements should move AI toward "glass box" model
    - Clear disclosure of training datasets, validation processes, intended use cases, and independently verified performance metrics
    - This information is essential for informed use at point of care
  - Independent validation and standardized benchmarking should occur prior to deployment and at regular intervals thereafter
    - Clinicians need confidence that tools perform consistently across patient populations and practice settings
  - Interoperability and validity across vendors are critical
    - For comparing tools, preventing vendor lock-in, and supporting continuity of care when systems change

- Financial support and reimbursement structures needed
  - Integrating validated AI tools requires significant upfront and ongoing investments
    - Software, licensing fees, IT infrastructure, cybersecurity protections, workflow redesign, staff training, and maintenance
  - For many physician practices, particularly small, independent, and solo practices, costs are substantial and may be prohibitive
  - Need dedicated reimbursement pathways, grants, or implementation assistance
  - HHS should establish clear mechanisms to reimburse clinical time and resources required to appropriately use, monitor, and validate AI outputs
  - Reimbursement should not be reduced based on assumptions that AI automatically creates efficiencies
  - Payment models should recognize AI as clinical decision-support tool that augments rather than replaces physician expertise
  - Clinicians should be compensated for time and professional judgment required to safely integrate these technologies

- Novel legal barriers AI creates in clinical space
  - FDA clearance processes relying predominantly on vendor-supplied validation may create uncertainty when independent verification is not feasible
  - Clinicians require clear regulatory guidance on whether updated or adaptive AI systems require renewed review
  - Need guidance on how material changes in algorithm performance are disclosed
  - Limited explainability complicates informed consent, clinical documentation, and shared decision-making
    - Increases risk of liability
  - If AI use becomes standard of care, practices lacking financial resources to adopt may face disproportionate legal exposure
  - Clear and consistent federal guidance delineating vendor accountability and clinician responsibilities is essential

- Where government has most influence
  - Federal government plays central and indispensable role in shaping trustworthy, accountable, and sustainable AI ecosystem
  - Healthcare delivery operates within highly regulated frameworks for payment, privacy, and safety
  - Federal policy decisions directly influence whether AI tools are adopted responsibly or prematurely without adequate safeguards
  - HHS uniquely positioned to accelerate safe adoption by establishing clear, enforceable standards
  - Regulatory frameworks needed
    - Require independent validation of clinical AI tools
    - Standardized performance metrics
    - Ongoing post-market monitoring
    - Interoperability standards and common technical requirements to promote competition and reduce vendor lock-in
    - Clear disclosure expectations regarding intended use, limitations, training data characteristics, and version updates
  - Privacy and data governance protections
    - Patient data should be used solely for authorized clinical or quality improvement purposes
    - Data should not be sold or repurposed for commercial gain without explicit consent
    - Strong cybersecurity safeguards required
    - Transparent data stewardship requirements, consent standards, and accountability mechanisms necessary to maintain public trust
    - Without these protections, both patients and providers may hesitate to participate in AI-enabled systems

- How interoperability can improve AI market for clinical care
  - Interoperability is fundamental to realizing full clinical utility, safety, and scalability of AI-enabled technologies
  - Without common technical standards, AI tools risk becoming siloed solutions that fragment care, increase administrative burden, and limit practical value
  - Interoperability enables independent verification of performance across environments
  - Promotes transparency and allows providers to evaluate and compare tools based on consistent, objective criteria
  - Essential for building clinician trust and supporting evidence-based procurement decisions
  - Market benefits
    - Promotes competition and innovation by reducing vendor lock-in and lowering switching costs
    - When providers not tied to proprietary systems, vendors must compete on quality, safety, usability, and price rather than exclusivity
    - Can reduce costs for practices and health systems while accelerating improvements in tool performance
  - For smaller practices, interoperable solutions are particularly important
    - Minimize need for costly custom integrations
    - Enable adoption without wholesale replacement of existing infrastructure
  - Clinical benefits
    - Strengthens care coordination and continuity
    - Seamless data exchange allows clinicians to access complete and accurate patient information at point of care
    - Share insights with patients and referring providers
    - Reduce duplicative testing or unnecessary procedures
    - Especially important for longitudinal conditions like sleep disorders and other chronic diseases where consistent tracking over time is essential
  - Need standardized definitions, benchmarking methods, and validation criteria
    - Ensure outputs are meaningfully comparable across tools and vendors
    - Enable regulators, purchasers, and clinicians to assess effectiveness and safety on equal terms
  - Federal leadership in establishing and enforcing these standards is critical

- Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns
  - Significant concerns regarding transparency, bias, privacy, cost, liability, workforce impact, and preservation of patient-clinician relationship
  - Many patients unaware when AI tools are used in their care or how algorithmic outputs influence clinical decision-making
  - Without clear disclosure and meaningful explanation, trust in both technology and healthcare system may be undermined
  - Concerns about equitable performance across diverse populations
  - Protection of sensitive health information especially prominent
  - Patients expect assurance that AI systems are independently validated, free from bias, and subject to strong privacy safeguards
  - Questions about potential cost implications and accountability for errors influence public confidence
  - Federal policy should establish clear disclosure standards
  - Require independent validation and equitable performance evaluation
  - Ensure robust privacy protections
  - Patient-facing education and transparent accountability frameworks essential to building and sustaining trust

- Research areas HHS should prioritize
  - Research that rigorously evaluates whether AI demonstrably improves clinical care and workflow from frontline clinician perspective
  - Studies should assess measurable impacts on clinician burden, time efficiency, patient access, and burnout
  - Ensure AI does not shift additional uncompensated work or legal risk onto providers
  - Examine real-world implementation including workflow integration, oversight requirements, and patient safety outcomes
  - Targeted pilot programs in high-prevalence, high-cost conditions such as obstructive sleep apnea
    - May offer meaningful opportunities to evaluate clinical effectiveness, cost savings, and scalability
  - Explore payment models that support responsible, evidence-based adoption of AI
  - Sustainable reimbursement structures essential for widespread implementation

- Conclusion
  - AI holds significant promise to enhance care delivery
  - Adoption will depend on demonstrated safety, transparency, independent validation, clear accountability, and appropriate financial support
  - By prioritizing these research areas, HHS can promote responsible innovation while ensuring clinicians can confidently integrate AI into patient care
