structuredSections.detailedContent:
- MHK appreciates the opportunity to comment on the RFI regarding strategies to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care
  - Commends HHS, ASTP, and ONC for inviting stakeholder input across regulation, reimbursement, and research and development
  - Goal is to responsibly scale AI tools that improve outcomes, reduce burden, and lower costs for patients and government programs

- Company background and mission
  - MHK delivers solutions supporting more than 90 million lives served by health plans and pharmacy benefit managers nationwide
  - Areas of focus include care management, utilization management (including prior authorization), pharmacy management, appeals and grievances, and population health
  - Mission is to enable health plans to deliver efficient, compliant, and patient-centered care through advanced technology solutions
  - AI can transform care delivery, reduce administrative burden, and improve outcomes when implemented responsibly and transparently

- MHK's AI capabilities and recognition
  - Supports HHS's vision to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care
  - Developed responsible AI enablement within platform
  - AI Agentic event framework has been recognized by Amazon Web Services as a best-in-class approach compared to other solutions in the market
  - Leveraging AI agents to streamline processes and reduce administrative burdens
  - Approach prioritizes compliance, transparency, and interoperability to ensure trust and safety

- Question 1: Biggest barriers to private sector innovation in AI for health care
  - Data fragmentation and quality issues
    - Despite progress through FHIR and related ONC initiatives, access to complete, high-quality, standards-based data across EHRs, payer systems, and ancillary sources remains inconsistent
    - AI systems rely on longitudinal, well-structured datasets
    - Fragmentation increases development cost, limits generalizability, and slows deployment
    - Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration can accelerate adoption by enabling secure access to clinical data across client environments without requiring data migration or format standardization
  - Misaligned reimbursement incentives
    - Fee-for-service reimbursement models do not always incentivize technologies to reduce utilization, shorten administrative cycle times, or lower total cost of care
    - AI tools that improve efficiency or prevent unnecessary services may lack a direct reimbursement pathway
  - Public trust and bias concerns
    - Public concern regarding algorithmic bias, lack of transparency, and risk of automation-driven denials, particularly in prior authorization, creates skepticism and slows adoption even where safeguards exist
    - One approach is to avoid training proprietary AI models independently and instead use industry-standard foundation models (Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini)
    - Provide clinical context through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) via MCP-connected data sources and unstructured document processing
    - This can reduce the introduction of training data bias
    - Remaining bias concerns can be limited to prompt injection (malicious input manipulation), cross-tenant data access, decision confidence fairness, and RAG context completeness (ensuring the right clinical evidence is retrieved for all patient types)
  - Regulatory ambiguity
    - Ambiguity in compliance frameworks, particularly around HIPAA and state-specific privacy regulations, can hinder adoption
    - Implementing comprehensive HIPAA Technical Safeguards (45 CFR § 164.312) could streamline adoption
      - Access Control with 3-tier capability token system (24-hour job tokens, 1-hour agent tokens with automatic expiration)
      - Audit Controls with 365-day CloudWatch retention and 7-year archive capability
      - Integrity Controls with client-specific KMS encryption
      - Person/Entity Authentication via AWS Cognito OAuth2
      - Transmission Security with TLS 1.3
    - Audit framework generates 9 report types (daily/monthly/quarterly/annual/on demand), providing concrete evidence of compliance
  - Legal and governance uncertainty
    - Uncertainty regarding legal frameworks and governance, particularly relating to liability allocation, indemnification, and documentation of human oversight, delays contracting and implementation
    - Especially problematic for AI applications within utilization management or clinical decision support integrated into EHRs
    - Critical to have human-in-the-loop governance at multiple levels
      - Decision separation
      - Confidence-based routing
      - Progressive automation with safeguards
      - Administrative controls

- Question 2: Regulatory, payment policy, or programmatic design changes HHS should prioritize
  - Payment model reforms
    - HHS should prioritize payment models that reward AI-enabled reductions in administrative burden, improvements in timeliness of care, and measurable improvements in outcomes
    - Introduce value-based incentives for AI-enabled solutions and documentation tools that demonstrate reduced administrative burden, costs, and improved outcomes
    - Simplify approval pathways for AI-driven utilization management tools, provided they maintain clinical oversight by licensed professionals
  - Clarify AI role distinctions
    - HHS policy should clearly distinguish between AI that assists clinicians or reviewers and AI that autonomously determines coverage or clinical actions
    - Existing CMS guidance stating that AI may support but not replace individualized medical necessity determinations should be codified

- Question 3: Novel legal and implementation issues for non-medical device AI
  - Unclear liability frameworks when AI informs but does not independently make clinical or utilization management decisions
    - Allocation of liability when AI recommendations are followed or overridden
    - Responsibility for performance drift, bias, or misclassification
    - Documentation standards demonstrating meaningful human review
  - Recommended HHS actions
    - Issue guidance on minimum governance expectations for clinical AI, including human-in-the-loop workflows, audit trails, and documentation requirements
    - Provide optional model contract language addressing indemnification and liability for AI-assisted clinical use cases
    - Clarify permissible uses and disclosures under HIPAA for AI training, monitoring, and safety auditing, including recommended de-identification practices

- Question 4: Promising AI evaluation methods for non-medical devices
  - Promising evaluation methods and metrics
    - Bias and fairness testing using parity metrics
    - Robustness testing with documentation standards
    - Post-deployment continuous performance surveillance of FHIR loads and connections
    - Payer-linked outcomes (appeal rates, overturn rates, adverse events) for AI in prior authorization and care coordination
  - Recommended HHS support mechanisms
    - Fund public-private development of shared datasets (clinical and claims), evaluation protocols, and independent measurement teams
    - Prioritize tools for ambient documentation, prior authorization, and health care navigation
    - Establish grants for model registries and audit tools that use standardized metric schemas and reporting
    - Support competitions that incentivize reproducible processes that reduce bias and improve clinician trust, with performance-based awards

- Question 5: How HHS can support private sector activities
  - Support a voluntary, risk-tiered assurance framework aligned with ONC certification and TEFCA
  - Recognize the distinction between AI platforms that train proprietary models and those that orchestrate industry foundation models
  - Encourage third-party accreditation for prior authorization using AI and clinical documentation using AI (scribes)
    - Audit trails that demonstrate human-in-the-loop governance, explainability, and outcomes (timeliness, overturn rates, adverse events)
  - Codify payer expectations and guardrails for AI use in coverage decisions
  - Ensure the architecture embeds key guardrails
  - Fund AI literacy and governance training for clinicians, medical directors, and utilization management professionals
    - Training curricula could incorporate anonymized examples to teach clinicians how to interpret AI confidence scores, recognize performance drift, and exercise effective oversight

- Question 6: Where AI tools have met or fallen short of expectations
  - Successes
    - Ambient clinical documentation tools have met expectations by reducing clinician burnout, preserving workforce capacity, and improving documentation quality
  - Shortfalls
    - AI deployment often lags expectations due to integration complexity, governance requirements, and validation burden
  - High-potential AI tools
    - Prior authorization orchestration with real-time data retrieval and human override
    - Population-level risk navigation integrating social drivers and benefits data
    - Explainable quality signal extraction beyond traditional HEDIS measures

- Question 7: Key decision makers and administrative hurdles
  - Key influencers and decision makers among health plans
    - Chief Medical Officer/Medical Directors (clinical sponsorship, guardrails)
    - Chief Information Officers and Clinical Informatics (workflow integration, EHR alignment)
    - Utilization Management/Case Management Leaders for payer-side adoption in prior authorization, concurrent review, and care coordination
    - Compliance/Privacy/Legal Officers
  - Primary administrative hurdles
    - Interoperability friction: fragmented EHRs, lack of standardized APIs, mixed protocols
    - Payment uncertainty: fee-for-service rarely rewards deflationary technology, coverage decisions could be slow
    - Validation and monitoring burdens: site-specific performance drift and bias audits
    - Workforce readiness and change management: clinician trust, documentation standards, and governance

- Question 8: Where enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities
  - FHIR to enable plug and play across exchanges for clinical, claims, and prior authorization datasets
  - Claims and clinical linkage for outcomes and total costs supported by AI tools (LOS, readmissions, adverse events, equity impacts)
  - Benchmarking tools such as national model registries with aligned metrics for pre- and post-deployment data

- Question 9: Patient and caregiver challenges and concerns
  - Challenges patients and caregivers wish to see addressed
    - Faster access to care by reducing prior authorization delays and having real-time decisions, as well as transparent reasons for determinations
    - Fewer diagnostic delays because of AI triage and timely routing
    - Increasing clinician presence: AI would reduce time spent on charting and increase time spent listening to ambient documentation
  - Concerns patients and caregivers have
    - Algorithmic bias and unequal denials, particularly in utilization management
    - Significant concern about batch denials caused by automation
    - Sharing data over networks raises privacy and trust concerns

- Question 10: Specific areas of AI research HHS should prioritize
  - Implementation science for AI-enabled prior authorization
  - Real-world performance drift and monitoring methodologies
  - Total cost of ownership and payment models for deflationary technologies
  - Explainable quality measures linked to clinical outcomes

- Conclusion and partnership offer
  - MHK is committed to advancing responsible AI adoption in clinical care
  - Applauds the Administration's efforts to accelerate AI use across the health care system
  - Collaboration between HHS, health plans, and technology partners is essential to achieving better outcomes, reducing costs, and improving patient experience
  - AI agentic event framework has been recognized by AWS as best-in-class
  - Welcomes the opportunity to meet, share learnings, and partner with HHS

- Current MHK AI initiatives
  - Automated Prior Authorization: Leveraging AI to reduce turnaround times while maintaining compliance
  - AI Agentic Enablement: Extracting insights from clinical notes to support decision-making
  - Predictive Analytics for Care Management: Identifying high-risk members for early intervention
  - Member Engagement Tools: AI-driven outreach tools to improve adherence and satisfaction

---
structuredSections.oneLineSummary:
Health plan technology vendor serving 90 million lives urges HHS to align payment incentives with AI efficiency gains, codify human-in-the-loop requirements, and clarify liability frameworks to accelerate responsible AI adoption in prior authorization and care management.

---
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** MHK
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Health plan technology solutions provider; signatories include Chief Compliance Officer, Chief Technology Officer, and Chief Medical Officer; AWS-recognized AI agentic framework developer
- **Geographic Scope:** National (serves health plans and pharmacy benefit managers nationwide)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly develops and deploys AI tools for prior authorization, care management, utilization management, and population health for health plans; regulatory clarity and payment alignment directly affect product viability and market adoption

---
structuredSections.corePosition:
We support HHS's vision to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care and have built what AWS recognizes as a best-in-class AI agentic framework for payer workflows. AI can transform care delivery, reduce administrative burden, and improve outcomes when implemented responsibly and transparently—but current fee-for-service payment models, regulatory ambiguity, and unclear liability frameworks are holding back adoption. HHS should prioritize value-based incentives, codify human-in-the-loop requirements, and provide clear guidance on liability allocation to unlock AI's potential.

---
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Reform payment models to reward AI-enabled efficiency
  - Introduce value-based incentives for AI solutions that demonstrate reduced administrative burden, costs, and improved outcomes
  - Simplify approval pathways for AI-driven utilization management tools that maintain clinical oversight by licensed professionals

- Codify the distinction between AI assistance and AI autonomy
  - HHS policy should clearly distinguish between AI that assists clinicians/reviewers and AI that autonomously determines coverage or clinical actions
  - Existing CMS guidance that AI may support but not replace individualized medical necessity determinations should be codified

- Issue governance and liability guidance
  - Provide guidance on minimum governance expectations for clinical AI, including human-in-the-loop workflows, audit trails, and documentation requirements
  - Offer optional model contract language addressing indemnification and liability for AI-assisted clinical use cases
  - Clarify permissible uses and disclosures under HIPAA for AI training, monitoring, and safety auditing

- Support evaluation infrastructure through public-private partnerships
  - Fund shared datasets (clinical and claims), evaluation protocols, and independent measurement teams
  - Establish grants for model registries and audit tools using standardized metric schemas
  - Support competitions incentivizing reproducible processes that reduce bias and improve clinician trust

- Create voluntary, risk-tiered assurance framework
  - Align with ONC certification and TEFCA
  - Recognize distinction between AI platforms that train proprietary models versus those that orchestrate industry foundation models
  - Encourage third-party accreditation for AI in prior authorization and clinical documentation

- Fund AI literacy and governance training
  - Target clinicians, medical directors, and utilization management professionals
  - Incorporate anonymized examples teaching interpretation of AI confidence scores, recognition of performance drift, and effective oversight

---
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Data fragmentation undermines AI development
  - Access to complete, high-quality, standards-based data across EHRs, payer systems, and ancillary sources remains inconsistent despite FHIR progress
  - Fragmentation increases development cost, limits generalizability, and slows deployment

- Fee-for-service misalignment with AI value proposition
  - Current payment models do not incentivize technologies that reduce utilization, shorten administrative cycle times, or lower total cost of care
  - AI tools that improve efficiency or prevent unnecessary services may lack direct reimbursement pathway

- Public trust barriers from bias and automation concerns
  - Public concern regarding algorithmic bias, lack of transparency, and risk of automation-driven denials creates skepticism
  - Particularly acute in prior authorization context
  - Patients express concern about batch denials caused by automation

- Regulatory and legal ambiguity
  - Ambiguity in compliance frameworks, particularly around HIPAA and state-specific privacy regulations, hinders adoption
  - Existing legal frameworks do not clearly address liability allocation when AI recommendations are followed or overridden
  - Unclear responsibility for performance drift, bias, or misclassification
  - Uncertainty delays contracting and implementation processes

- Integration and validation burden
  - AI deployment often lags expectations due to integration complexity, governance requirements, and validation burden
  - Site-specific performance drift and bias audits create monitoring burdens
  - Interoperability friction from fragmented EHRs, lack of standardized APIs, and mixed protocols

---
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Technical architecture for bias mitigation: Rather than training proprietary AI models, MHK uses industry-standard foundation models (Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation via MCP-connected data sources—an approach that limits bias concerns to prompt injection, cross-tenant data access, decision confidence fairness, and RAG context completeness rather than training data bias

- Specific HIPAA technical safeguards implementation: Detailed 3-tier capability token system (24-hour job tokens, 1-hour agent tokens with automatic expiration), 365-day CloudWatch retention with 7-year archive capability, client-specific KMS encryption, and audit framework generating 9 report types

- Human-in-the-loop governance framework: Multi-level approach including decision separation, confidence-based routing, progressive automation with safeguards, and administrative controls

- AWS recognition: Their AI agentic event framework has been recognized by Amazon Web Services as best-in-class compared to other solutions in the market

- Insight on "deflationary technology" problem: Fee-for-service payment models rarely reward technologies that reduce utilization or costs—a fundamental misalignment that affects AI adoption across the industry

- "AI tools that improve efficiency or prevent unnecessary services may lack a direct reimbursement pathway."

- "Existing CMS guidance stating that AI may support but not replace individualized medical necessity determinations should be codified."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Providence is a non-profit health system operating across seven states: Alaska, California, Montana, New Mexico, Oregon, and Texas
  - Employs 117,000 people
  - Operates 51 hospitals and more than 1,000 clinics
  - Runs a health plan with more than 600,000 beneficiaries
  - Provides senior services, home health, hospice, PACE, and housing
  - Invested $1.9 billion in community benefit in 2024
  - Special focus on serving people who depend on Medicaid coverage

- Gaps in Regulation
  - Patients need federal regulations setting expectations about consent for AI use in clinical care
    - Current patchwork of state-level consent rules creates unreasonable pathway for patients and providers
    - Unclear whether patients can restrict AI tool access to their personal information like they can under HIPAA
    - Unclear how HIPAA privacy rule interacts with AI use on PHI when tool is owned by provider, business associate, or third party
    - Current regulations haven't kept pace with rapid AI development or patient expectations about knowing when and how their PHI interacts with AI tools
  - Providers and AI developers need federal regulations outlining accountability standards
    - When all entities take required steps to vet tools, protect patient rights, and appropriately apply AI, who is responsible when the tool fails and a never-event occurs?
    - Federal accreditation or certification would help parties understand their role in vetting
    - Even the best technology can fail
    - Providers need direction about where accountability should lie when negotiating with national EHR vendors and AI companies who have greater negotiating power than even sophisticated health systems
    - HHS regulations would help keep the patient at the center of negotiations
  - Providence appreciates the president's executive order aimed at preventing 50 discordant state rules on AI use
    - As a multi-state health system, we believe federal government is best positioned to dictate AI use standards
    - HHS should describe which areas of regulation can fall within state purview
    - Many states have taken legislative action sensing federal government intended this to be a state issue
    - Important for HHS to clearly outline states' roles so provider and developer expectations are clear
    - Hospital resources should not be futilely spent on compliance with state schemes that will eventually be preempted by federal rules
  - Regulations should outline areas where providers maintain discretion
    - To reject AI tools
    - To apply more stringent vetting standards
    - To provide patient education and options about their care

- AI in Non-Medical Devices
  - FDA has published guidance for AI in medical devices classified as software as a medical device
  - Gap exists in how federal regulatory landscape will treat AI tools in non-medical devices
    - Includes FDA and HHS reimbursement like Medicare and Medicaid
  - This gap is preventing developers from bringing more tools to market
  - FDA announced January 6 it will ease regulation of non-medical digital health devices that don't make medical claims
  - Providence shares FDA Commissioner's goal to move regulations "at Silicon Valley speed"
  - But lack of regulatory standards is a barrier to clinical adoption
    - Providers are wary of adopting AI-enabled devices without fully vetting technology and risks
    - With federal regulatory parameters including FDA approval, providers could smooth internal vetting to get technology to patients sooner

- Accreditation to Promote Innovation
  - Providence fully supports a nationally recognized accreditation path for AI tools
  - Without third-party accreditation, health care providers must invest their own time and funds to determine whether each AI tool is safe
    - This drains resources that could go to patient care and workforce development
    - It slows AI adoption in clinical care
  - Providence has developed a standardized risk stratification process to examine and improve AI tools before clinical integration
    - Plots tool placement on spectrum from research to direct patient care
    - Patient fear and outcome risk grows as tool moves closer to bedside
    - System adjusts for patient complexity
    - Informs deployment so executives and clinicians understand relative risk of new tools
    - Allows Providence to keep pace with private innovations
  - A national accreditation program would ensure all providers can move at speed of innovation and all patients can be informed about rigors accredited tools have passed
  - HHS should establish a forum for public-private partnerships to take on this work
    - Financial and administrative burden should not fall to federal government
    - Public-private relationship would strengthen accreditation profile and lend trust
  - By requiring accreditation for applicable AI tools deployed for clinical care, HHS would:
    - Accelerate the process
    - Alleviate financial burden on providers vetting on their own
    - Remove uncertainty about risk by knowing which parameters accredited tools have met
  - Given speed of AI technology change, accreditation process should include frequent auditing
    - Ongoing monitoring could take form of public registry of outcomes
    - Similar to procedure for clinical researchers undergoing clinical trials
    - Would build public confidence in AI use
    - Vendors and providers need to ensure patients continue to be confident and safe with clinical AI tools

- Incentives for Electronic Health Record (EHR) Vendors
  - EHR industry currently stands as near monopoly with limited competition among handful of large companies
  - HHS should take steps to promote competition from third-party AI software developers
    - Promote interoperability of third-party software into core EHR products
    - Open market to all interested developers to keep prices down
  - Providence encourages EHR vendors to provide certain AI functionality with baseline product
    - Vendors should not be permitted to keep AI away from providers unable to afford add-on after add-on
  - ASTP should clearly outline when and how certified EHR vendors can market tools as "AI-enabled" or "powered by AI"
    - AI has been part of daily medical practice for years (diagnostic aides, speech-to-text notetaking)
    - Patients today understand "AI-enabled" stamp very differently than decades ago
    - As federal government proceeds with consent and accreditation requirements, these should only apply when truly necessary for patient protection
    - Example: if Providence encounters new software described as "powered by AI," it triggers internal risk stratification review
    - Important for EHR vendors to honestly describe AI use before resources are spent analyzing tools that don't require robust review

- Research and Development
  - HHS should prioritize research on:
    - Use of genetic data in AI tools
    - How to appropriately de-identify PHI as AI tools are developed to protect patient privacy

- Health Care Organization Adoption
  - Three factors influence adoption of clinical AI tools by hospitals:
  - Factor 1: Broad network of third-party providers in hospital's ecosystem
    - Health care is a patchwork of people and organizations working together to follow patients and integrate care needs
    - Difficult for all providers in patient's network to understand technological sophistication and compliance of others
    - Providence clinicians cannot be sure whether patient's medical record contains AI-generated content
    - Cannot be sure if AI-generated content was reviewed by live clinician before sent to our hospital
    - Cannot be sure whether third-party clinician used AI tool to match symptoms to diagnosis
    - Cannot know level of consent patient gave about AI tool use
    - Once patient and record travels more than two or three degrees beyond our health system, we cannot know whether AI was used or level of risk
    - Until all providers operate under same HHS regulations or tools are nationally accredited, organizations will be wary to fully adopt AI across care continuum
  - Factor 2: Workforce concerns about job losses and new risks
    - Organizations face opposition from union voices about future AI integration
    - Even ambient listening tools that cut down on clinical charting can spark resistance
    - Practitioner unions worry about staff downsizing or increased visit frequency expectations
    - Difficult to imagine how HHS can alleviate these fears since they're shared across all industries where AI can encroach on human jobs
    - Important for HHS to understand this is a barrier
  - Factor 3: Lack of funding support for new technology infrastructure
    - Goes beyond funding to purchase actual tools from EHR vendors and third parties
    - AI-powered technology requires more powerful wireless internet and Bluetooth capabilities
    - At basic level: making space in IT closet or adding new rooms for additional wiring and fans
    - At broader level: funding for staff training and compliance monitoring
  - HHS and CMS can work together to ensure funding support by:
    - Offering one-time grants for qualifying hospitals (rural and safety net) to make IT upgrades needed for AI integration
    - Ensuring Medicare reimbursement policies keep pace with clinical acceleration expected as AI adoption grows
    - Clinicians would have freedom to focus on patient during check-up rather than making notes
    - Visits presumably get shorter over time
    - Medicare coding and reimbursement policies need to be mindful of ongoing costs supporting AI technologies
    - AI makes visits more efficient, causing clinicians to bill for shorter time interval codes with typically lower payment rates
    - CMS will need to examine how to make payment rates sustainable for AI-powered clinical practice

- Patient Expectations
  - As President Trump described in his executive order, AI is both a technological revolution and a race
  - Patient focus in our experience has been singular: tell me when you are using AI with my information
  - Barrier to meeting this expectation is widely varied understanding of what "AI" means to patients
    - AI technology is so prevalent in our lives
    - Every email using autocorrect is using AI, but patients surely don't want to consent to spell check tools
    - However, if clinician relies on machine learning diagnostic tool to flag potential cancerous lesion on MRI, patient would want to understand how clinician used that tool and steps taken to confirm result
  - Providers and patients need HHS direction about when to disclose AI and how much disclosure is necessary and reasonable
  - Standardized notice and consent templates from HHS would help
    - Providence would appreciate opportunity to work with HHS to develop simple patient-facing language
    - Example hospital notice: "Your care team might use technology powered by AI that will help them reach a diagnosis"
    - If national accreditation established, consent could state: "clinicians in our facility use AI technology certified by a federal agency, do you consent to the use of that technology?"
    - Standardization similar to patient opt-in for health information exchange
    - HHS-provided templates with small customization options can assuage patient fears and meet expectations to be fully informed

- Conclusion
  - Providence is energized by federal government's interest in accelerating AI adoption in clinical care
  - We look forward to sharing our experience and best practices with HHS and other providers
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A major seven-state non-profit health system with its own AI vetting process urges HHS to establish federal consent standards, national AI accreditation, and clear accountability rules to replace the current patchwork of state regulations and enable faster, safer clinical AI adoption.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Providence / Providence St. Joseph Health
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** Non-profit health system operating 51 hospitals and 1,000+ clinics across seven states; has developed internal AI risk stratification process for vetting clinical AI tools
- **Geographic Scope:** National (Alaska, California, Montana, New Mexico, Oregon, Texas, Washington)
- **Stake in Issue:** Multi-state health system that must navigate inconsistent state AI regulations while vetting and deploying AI tools across diverse care settings; directly affected by EHR vendor market dynamics and Medicare reimbursement policies
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe the federal government is in the best position to dictate AI use standards that benefit patients and providers while supporting rapid innovation. The current patchwork of state-level consent rules has paved an unreasonable pathway for both patients and providers, and hospital resources should not be futilely spent on compliance with state legislative schemes that will eventually be preempted by federal rules. National accreditation would ensure all health care providers can move at the speed of innovation while keeping patients informed and safe.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish federal regulations setting clear expectations for patient consent regarding AI use in clinical care
  - Clarify how HIPAA privacy rules interact with AI use on protected health information
  - Clarify whether patients can restrict AI tool access to their information
- Create federal accountability standards for when AI tools fail
  - Provide direction on where accountability lies when negotiating with EHR vendors and AI companies
- Clearly outline states' roles and responsibilities in AI regulation and enforcement
  - Describe which areas of regulation can fall within state purview
  - Prevent 50 discordant state rules on AI use
- Establish federal regulatory parameters for AI in non-medical devices
  - Include FDA approval pathway to help providers smooth internal vetting processes
- Create a nationally recognized accreditation path for AI tools
  - Establish public-private partnerships to take on accreditation work
  - Require accreditation for applicable AI tools deployed for clinical care
  - Include frequent auditing and public registry of outcomes similar to clinical trials
- Promote competition in EHR market for third-party AI developers
  - Promote interoperability of third-party software into core EHR products
  - Require EHR vendors to provide certain AI functionality with baseline product (not as add-ons)
  - Clearly outline when vendors can market tools as "AI-enabled" or "powered by AI"
- Prioritize research on genetic data use in AI tools and appropriate PHI de-identification
- Provide funding support for AI adoption
  - Offer one-time grants for rural and safety net hospitals to make IT infrastructure upgrades
  - Ensure Medicare reimbursement policies keep pace with clinical acceleration from AI
  - Examine how to make payment rates sustainable when AI makes visits more efficient
- Develop standardized notice and consent templates for patient-facing AI disclosure
  - Work with providers like Providence to develop simple patient-facing language
  - Allow small customization options similar to health information exchange opt-in
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Current patchwork of state-level consent rules creates unreasonable pathway for patients and providers
  - Many states have taken legislative action sensing federal government intended this to be a state issue
  - Multi-state health systems face compliance burden with potentially preempted state schemes
- Unclear accountability when AI tools fail despite proper vetting
  - EHR vendors and AI companies have greater negotiating power than even sophisticated health systems
- Gap in federal regulatory landscape for AI in non-medical devices
  - Lack of regulatory standards is barrier to clinical adoption
  - Developers are prevented from bringing tools to market
- Absence of national accreditation forces providers to invest their own time and funds to vet each AI tool
  - Drains resources from patient care and workforce development
  - Slows AI adoption in clinical care
- EHR industry operates as near monopoly with limited competition
  - Risk that vendors keep AI away from providers unable to afford add-ons
  - Unclear standards for when "AI-enabled" label triggers review requirements
- Uncertainty about AI use across the care continuum
  - Cannot be sure whether patient records contain AI-generated content from third-party providers
  - Cannot know if AI-generated content was reviewed by live clinician
  - Once patient travels two or three degrees beyond our system, cannot know whether AI was used or risk level
- Workforce opposition to AI integration
  - Union voices oppose future AI tool integration
  - Even ambient listening tools that reduce charting burden spark resistance over job security concerns
- Lack of funding support for AI infrastructure
  - AI requires more powerful wireless internet and Bluetooth capabilities
  - Need space for additional wiring and fans
  - Need funding for staff training and compliance monitoring
- Medicare reimbursement may not keep pace with AI-enabled efficiency gains
  - Shorter visits mean billing for lower time interval codes with lower payment rates
  - Payment rates may not be sustainable for AI-powered clinical practice
- Widely varied patient understanding of what "AI" means
  - Difficult to know when disclosure is necessary and reasonable
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Providence has developed a standardized risk stratification process for AI tools before clinical integration
  - Plots each tool on a spectrum from research to direct patient care
  - Patient fear and outcome risk grows as tool moves closer to bedside
  - System adjusts for patient complexity
  - Allows executives and clinicians to understand relative risk of new tools
- Insight on the "degrees of separation" problem: once a patient and their record travels more than two or three degrees beyond Providence's health system, they simply cannot know whether AI was used or the level of risk it carries
- Counterintuitive workforce dynamic: even ambient listening tools designed to reduce clinician burden by automating note-taking spark union resistance over fears of staff downsizing or increased visit frequency expectations
- Hidden infrastructure costs of AI adoption: at a basic level, AI integration means making space in the IT closet or adding new rooms to house additional wiring and fans
- Reimbursement paradox: AI tools that make visits more efficient will cause clinicians to bill for shorter time interval codes with typically lower payment rates, potentially undermining the financial sustainability of AI adoption
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The patchwork of state-level consent rules has paved an unreasonable pathway for both patients and providers to be confident in when consent is needed and how to appropriately secure it."
- "Once the patient and their record travels more than two or three degrees beyond our health system, we simply cannot know whether AI was used or the level of risk it carries."
- "Every email that uses autocorrect is using AI, but surely patients are not interested in giving consent to their physician using spell check tools."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- InterSystems appreciates HHS's Request for Information and the Department's intent to use regulation, reimbursement, and R&D levers to accelerate AI adoption in clinical care
  - We agree progress requires a forward-leaning, industry-supportive approach that also preserves privacy, confidentiality of identifiable health information, and public trust

- AI adoption at scale will depend on interoperability as infrastructure
  - Consistent access to high-quality, standardized data
  - Predictable security and governance
  - Repeatable implementation patterns across settings and vendors

- As an interoperability platform vendor operating across heterogeneous environments, InterSystems recommends HHS prioritize actions that:
  - Expand discrete, standards-based data access for core clinical workflows and public health/quality use cases
  - Reduce variability and integration burden
  - Strengthen trust through security, provenance, and auditability

- HHS indicates it seeks a regulatory posture that enables innovation while protecting patients and maintaining public trust
  - InterSystems recommends HHS prioritize clarity in three areas:

- Accountability and auditability for AI in clinical workflows
  - Encourage requirements for logging and traceability (inputs, outputs, downstream actions) appropriate to clinical risk

- Provenance expectations
  - Promote use of provenance/attribution so clinicians and organizations can understand what data informed AI outputs

- Interoperability guardrails for AI integration
  - Avoid creating "AI-only" proprietary side channels for clinical context exchange
  - AI integration should remain anchored to core interoperability standards and established security controls

- InterSystems encourages HHS to treat interoperability as the enabling infrastructure for AI
  - Pair standards-based data access with strong security, provenance, auditability, and practical implementation guidance
  - This approach allows AI to be deployed safely and consistently across heterogeneous healthcare environments
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Interoperability platform vendor urges HHS to anchor AI adoption in standards-based data access, strong security controls, and auditability requirements rather than allowing proprietary AI side channels.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** InterSystems Corporation
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Interoperability platform vendor operating across heterogeneous healthcare environments
- **Geographic Scope:** National/International (headquarters in Boston, MA)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected as a vendor whose interoperability infrastructure would underpin AI integration in clinical settings
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe AI adoption at scale depends on interoperability as infrastructure—consistent access to high-quality, standardized data with predictable security and governance. HHS should treat interoperability as the enabling foundation for AI, pairing standards-based data access with strong security, provenance, and auditability so AI can be deployed safely across diverse healthcare environments.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Expand discrete, standards-based data access for core clinical workflows and public health/quality use cases
- Reduce variability and integration burden across settings and vendors
- Strengthen trust through security, provenance, and auditability requirements
- Establish accountability and auditability requirements for AI in clinical workflows
  - Require logging and traceability of inputs, outputs, and downstream actions appropriate to clinical risk
- Promote provenance/attribution standards
  - Enable clinicians and organizations to understand what data informed AI outputs
- Create interoperability guardrails for AI integration
  - Prevent "AI-only" proprietary side channels for clinical context exchange
  - Keep AI integration anchored to core interoperability standards and established security controls
- Provide practical implementation guidance for safe, consistent AI deployment
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Risk of proprietary "AI-only" side channels emerging for clinical context exchange
  - Would bypass established interoperability standards and security controls
- Lack of clarity around accountability and auditability requirements for AI in clinical workflows
- Insufficient provenance expectations
  - Clinicians may not understand what data informed AI outputs
- Variability and integration burden across different settings and vendors
- Need to maintain public trust while enabling innovation
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Perspective from vendor operating across heterogeneous healthcare environments highlights the practical challenge of implementing AI consistently across diverse systems
- Frames interoperability not as a feature but as "infrastructure" for AI—a foundational layer rather than an add-on
- Identifies specific risk of AI tools creating parallel data channels that circumvent existing standards
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "AI adoption at scale will depend on interoperability as infrastructure: consistent access to high-quality, standardized data; predictable security and governance; and repeatable implementation patterns across settings and vendors."
- "Avoid creating 'AI-only' proprietary side channels for clinical context exchange. AI integration should remain anchored to core interoperability standards and established security controls."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- American Parkinson Disease Association (APDA) response to HHS Request for Information on AI in clinical care
  - APDA is a nationwide grassroots network dedicated to fighting Parkinson's disease
  - Founded in 1961, has raised and invested more than $313 million for patient services, education, public awareness, and research
  - Works to help approximately one million people with PD in the United States
- APDA Voices of Parkinson's Council (V of P) provided feedback for this response
  - Select group of PD patients, care partners, and caregivers
  - Informs APDA programming, research initiatives, public policy and advocacy efforts, and resource development
  - Provided input on both clinical care challenges AI can address and concerns about AI use

- Clinical Care Challenges that can be Addressed with AI
  - Administrative burden reduction
    - Interest in AI tools that reduce time patients and providers spend on administrative tasks during clinical encounters
    - AI can aggregate patient clinical history over time and across care settings
    - Allows more time for discussing care concerns and treatment options during visits
  - Clinical documentation
    - Interest in AI tools that record clinical visits and generate notes without provider typing during visit
    - Enables increased engagement between provider and patient
    - AI-generated note serves as documentation for provider's medical record and patient visit summary
  - Patient and provider education
    - Interest in AI tools providing individualized, timely, and accurate PD clinical care information to patients and caregivers
    - AI tools validated by PD specialists may improve knowledge, expertise, and quality of care from providers without specialized PD expertise
  - PD screening and diagnosis
    - Early PD symptoms (loss of smell, trouble sleeping) are indicators for many common conditions
    - AI-enabled screening tools may identify meaningful symptom patterns
    - Could efficiently refer patients to PD specialists for more timely screening and diagnosis
  - Symptom monitoring for diagnosed patients
    - Interest in AI to better monitor PD symptoms
    - Identify meaningful changes in symptoms
    - Refer patients to appropriate specialists and services for timely follow-up care
  - Treatment optimization
    - Members frequently mentioned medication management feels like trial-and-error
    - Strong interest in AI tools that assess symptoms and provide individualized recommendations for medication type, dose, and frequency
    - Interest in AI that could provide recommendations or modify treatment plans based on clinical history, patient preferences, and evidence-based treatment
    - Tools that flag potential drug interactions for providers to inform treatment decisions

- Concerns about the Use of AI in Clinical Care
  - Human provider role
    - Support AI tools that supplement but do not supplant provider role in care delivery
    - Concern about providers becoming overly reliant on AI tools
    - Fear that over-reliance would reduce interactions between PD patients and human providers
    - Concern about lack of personalized care
  - Liability and accountability
    - Concern over who is legally liable for patient safety, quality of care, and care outcomes when AI tools are used
    - Concerns about accuracy of AI tools to screen, diagnose, and treat PD
    - Questions about who is accountable for oversight or responsible for inaccuracies
  - Privacy concerns
    - Concerns about data storage, access, and use
    - Questions about whether officials can ensure AI tools comply with HIPAA
  - Cost concerns
    - Concern over insurance coverage for AI tools in clinical care
    - Overall concern about potential increase in patient out-of-pocket costs if AI tools are implemented

- APDA recommendations to HHS
  - Identify oversight and enforcement processes to ensure technologies protect patient privacy, quality of care, and patient safety
  - Identify opportunities to reduce patient costs for AI tools
  - Ensure benefits of new technologies are proportionately distributed based on patient need and value rather than ability to pay out-of-pocket

- Context on PD population
  - Approximately 90,000 new PD cases each year in the US
  - Represents a new PD diagnosis every 6 minutes
  - PD population continues to increase
  - APDA ready to partner with HHS on technological advances including AI
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: National Parkinson's advocacy organization, drawing on patient and caregiver council feedback, supports AI tools that reduce administrative burden and improve diagnosis and treatment while urging HHS to ensure privacy protections, clear liability frameworks, and equitable access regardless of ability to pay.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Parkinson Disease Association (APDA)
- **Type:** Advocacy Group
- **Role/Expertise:** National patient advocacy organization for Parkinson's disease; input gathered from Voices of Parkinson's Council (patients, care partners, caregivers)
- **Geographic Scope:** National (United States)
- **Stake in Issue:** Represents approximately one million people with Parkinson's disease in the US who could benefit from or be harmed by AI tools in clinical care
structuredSections.corePosition: APDA supports appropriate use of AI in clinical care that supplements but does not replace human providers. We see significant potential for AI to reduce administrative burden, improve PD screening and diagnosis, and optimize treatment decisions, but we urge HHS to establish clear oversight, protect patient privacy, ensure accountability for AI accuracy, and prevent cost barriers that would limit access based on ability to pay.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Identify oversight and enforcement processes to ensure AI technologies protect:
  - Patient privacy
  - Quality of care
  - Patient safety
- Identify opportunities to reduce patient costs for AI tools
- Ensure benefits of AI technologies are distributed based on:
  - Patient need
  - Value of the technology
  - Not based on ability to pay out-of-pocket
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Over-reliance on AI reducing human provider interactions
  - Could lead to lack of personalized care for PD patients
- Unclear liability and accountability
  - Who is legally liable for patient safety, quality of care, and outcomes when AI is used?
  - Who is accountable for oversight of AI accuracy?
  - Who is responsible when AI tools produce inaccuracies in screening, diagnosis, or treatment?
- Patient privacy risks
  - Data storage, access, and use concerns
  - Uncertainty about HIPAA compliance for AI tools
- Cost and access barriers
  - Concern about insurance coverage for AI tools
  - Potential increase in patient out-of-pocket costs
  - Risk that benefits flow to those who can afford to pay rather than those with greatest need
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Patient council members describe medication management as feeling like "trial-and-error"
  - Highlights opportunity for AI to provide more systematic, individualized treatment recommendations
- Early PD symptoms (loss of smell, trouble sleeping) overlap with many common conditions
  - AI pattern recognition could help identify PD cases earlier among patients presenting with non-specific symptoms
- Patients want AI-generated clinical notes that serve dual purpose
  - Documentation for provider's medical record
  - Visit summary for patient reference
- Council members specifically want AI tools validated by PD specialists to improve care from non-specialist providers
  - Addresses access gap for patients without access to movement disorder specialists
structuredSections.keyQuotations: "No standout quotations"

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Writing to express serious concerns about proposed acceleration of AI integration into clinical care settings, particularly within behavioral health and psychotherapy
- While healthcare technology innovation can improve access and efficiency, rapid deployment of AI tools in direct patient care raises urgent concerns
  - Patient privacy
  - Safety
  - Scientific validation
  - Preservation of the therapeutic relationship
  - Science and safeguards have not kept pace with implementation speed
- Behavioral health settings involve uniquely sensitive disclosures
  - Trauma histories
  - Suicidal ideation
  - Substance use
  - Deeply personal relational information
- Introduction of AI systems into psychotherapy sessions creates heightened risks
  - Whether for documentation, clinical decision support, or conversational interaction
  - Data security concerns
  - Secondary data use concerns
  - Algorithmic bias
  - Patient consent issues
  - Even de-identified mental health data may be vulnerable to re-identification or misuse
- Many AI-enabled clinical tools currently lack independent, peer-reviewed evidence
  - No demonstrated safety, efficacy, or clinical superiority over existing standards of care
  - In medicine, new interventions typically undergo rigorous testing prior to widespread adoption
  - AI systems influencing diagnosis, treatment recommendations, or therapeutic processes should meet comparable evidentiary standards
  - Without scrutiny, patients may face unintended harm
    - Inaccurate risk assessments
    - Inappropriate treatment suggestions
    - Overreliance on automated outputs
- In psychotherapy specifically, the therapeutic alliance is one of the strongest predictors of clinical outcomes
  - Trust, confidentiality, and relational attunement are foundational
  - Patient uncertainty about data processing, storage, or repurposing may inhibit disclosure and compromise care
  - Innovation must not erode the integrity of this relationship
- Urging the Department to ensure AI integration is guided by:
  - Rigorous, independent research demonstrating safety and effectiveness prior to widespread deployment
  - Clear federal standards governing data storage, secondary use, transparency, and algorithmic accountability
  - Explicit informed consent requirements for AI involvement in behavioral health settings
  - Strong enforcement mechanisms to protect patient privacy and prevent commercial exploitation of sensitive health data
  - Special regulatory consideration for behavioral health and psychotherapy, where ethical stakes are uniquely high
- Technological progress in healthcare should be deliberate, evidence-based, and grounded in enforceable protections
- Innovation must serve patients—not outpace the ethical and regulatory frameworks designed to safeguard them
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A licensed clinical counselor warns that rushing AI into psychotherapy threatens the sacred trust between therapist and patient, urging rigorous evidence standards and special protections for behavioral health's uniquely sensitive disclosures.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Mark O'Brien, LCPC, CADC
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** Licensed Clinical Professional Counselor and Certified Alcohol and Drug Counselor
- **Geographic Scope:** Not specified
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly provides psychotherapy and behavioral health services where AI tools would be deployed; concerned about impacts on therapeutic relationships and patient trust
structuredSections.corePosition: I have serious concerns about accelerating AI integration into behavioral health and psychotherapy. The science and safeguards have not kept pace with implementation speed, and innovation must not erode the therapeutic relationship that is foundational to effective mental health care. Technological progress should be deliberate, evidence-based, and grounded in enforceable protections.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Require rigorous, independent research demonstrating safety and effectiveness prior to widespread AI deployment
- Establish clear federal standards governing data storage, secondary use, transparency, and algorithmic accountability
- Mandate explicit informed consent requirements for AI involvement in behavioral health settings
- Implement strong enforcement mechanisms to protect patient privacy and prevent commercial exploitation of sensitive health data
- Create special regulatory consideration for behavioral health and psychotherapy given uniquely high ethical stakes
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- AI tools in behavioral health lack independent, peer-reviewed evidence of safety, efficacy, or clinical superiority
  - Patients may face inaccurate risk assessments, inappropriate treatment suggestions, or overreliance on automated outputs
- Heightened data security risks with uniquely sensitive mental health disclosures
  - Trauma histories, suicidal ideation, substance use, personal relational information
  - Even de-identified mental health data vulnerable to re-identification or misuse
- Algorithmic bias and secondary data use concerns
- Patient uncertainty about data handling may inhibit disclosure and compromise care
- Therapeutic alliance—the strongest predictor of clinical outcomes—could be undermined
  - Trust, confidentiality, and relational attunement are foundational to effective therapy
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Offers clinical perspective that therapeutic alliance is one of the strongest predictors of outcomes in psychotherapy—a research-backed insight that frames AI as potentially undermining the very mechanism that makes therapy work
- Highlights that mental health data is uniquely vulnerable even when de-identified, given the specific and personal nature of disclosures
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Innovation must serve patients—not outpace the ethical and regulatory frameworks designed to safeguard them."
- "If patients are uncertain about how their data is being processed, stored, or potentially repurposed, this may inhibit disclosure and compromise care."
- "Trust, confidentiality, and relational attunement are foundational. Innovation must not erode the integrity of this relationship."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Heart Failure Society of America (HFSA) response to HHS Request for Information on AI in clinical care
  - HFSA is a multidisciplinary organization working to improve heart failure care through collaboration, education, research, innovation, and advocacy
  - Members include physicians, scientists, nurses, nurse practitioners, pharmacists, and patients

- Heart failure as a compelling use case for responsible AI adoption
  - Common, resource-intensive chronic condition managed across complex, longitudinal care pathways and workflows
  - Approximately 6.7 million Americans over age 20 currently living with heart failure
  - Prevalence expected to rise to 11.4 million by 2050
  - Lifetime risk of developing heart failure is 24% (approximately one in four individuals)
  - In 2022, heart failure contributed to more than 425,000 deaths, accounting for 45% of all cardiovascular deaths nationwide

- Significant gaps in guideline-recommended therapies
  - Fewer than one in four eligible patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction receive comprehensive quadruple guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT)
  - This persists despite clear evidence of benefit
  - Optimal uptake could prevent approximately 1.19 million deaths globally each year

- Clear opportunities for AI in heart failure care
  - Improve operational efficiency
  - Reduce clinician burden
  - Increase reliability of delivering evidence-based care
  - ARPA-H's ADVOCATE program seeks to develop agentic AI systems as "clinician extenders" for heart failure patients and those recovering from myocardial infarction

- Risks of AI in heart failure care
  - Heart failure care is inherently high risk
  - Poorly governed or inadequately evaluated AI tools could cause patient harm through:
    - Preventable hospitalizations
    - Medication-related adverse events
    - Delayed escalation of care
  - Adoption must be accompanied by robust governance, rigorous prospective evaluation, and continuous post-deployment monitoring

- Recommended Principles

- Principle 1: Establish risk-proportionate clinician governance and accountability
  - AI tools informing diagnoses, triage, medication titration, escalation decisions, or discharge planning should have clear clinical ownership and defined human oversight
  - Governance expectations should be proportionate to clinical impact
    - Explicit intended-use statement
    - Documented workflows for clinician override
    - Auditable logs of AI outputs and clinician actions
  - These measures clarify accountability, preserve clinical judgment, and support public trust while enabling innovation

- Principle 2: AI-enabled care should address the full heart failure continuum
  - Approximately one-third of U.S. adults are at risk for heart failure (Stage A)
  - Nearly 24-34% have pre-heart failure (Stage B)
  - Estimated 5-7% of patients with symptomatic heart failure have advanced heart failure
  - Rising rates of obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease are accelerating progression
  - Effective AI must support surveillance, prevention, early detection, treatment optimization, and advanced care across all stages

- Principle 3: Align reimbursement and federal incentives to support implementation and value-based adoption
  - Many clinically meaningful AI tools require substantial upfront investment in:
    - Integration
    - Workflow redesign
    - Training
    - Ongoing surveillance
  - These costs are often difficult for providers to absorb without reliable reimbursement pathway
  - CMS should prioritize payment approaches that reward demonstrated clinical utility and burden reduction
  - Should avoid per-alert or per-use incentives that may drive volume without value
  - Time-limited implementation support and demonstration models would reduce adoption friction
  - CMS should offer incentives rewarding safe deployment of AI

- Principle 4: Shift evaluation from retrospective accuracy to prospective clinical utility, safety, and equity
  - HHS should invest in research evaluating whether AI improves outcomes and reduces burden without introducing new harms
  - Retrospective performance metrics alone are insufficient to justify clinical deployment
  - Evaluation frameworks should incorporate:
    - Human factors testing
    - Alert burden and usability metrics
    - Calibration and subgroup performance
    - Clinically meaningful safety endpoints
  - HHS can accelerate responsible adoption by supporting pragmatic, multi-site randomized trials and post-deployment monitoring

- Principle 5: Establish comprehensive lifecycle evaluation framework for AI tools
  - Appropriate HHS authorities should oversee pre-deployment evaluation and lifecycle regulation of AI tools meeting Software as a Medical Device criteria
    - Requirements for clinical validation, transparency, and post-market performance monitoring
    - Address model drift and safety risks in heart failure populations
  - ASTP/ONC should establish interoperability and auditability standards
    - Ensure AI tools integrate reliably with electronic health records
    - Support clinician interpretability and governance
  - CMS should align reimbursement and quality measurement with broader initiatives
    - Ambulatory Specialty Model
    - Advancing Chronic Care with Effective, Scalable Solutions Model
    - Support coordinated adoption of high-value AI-enabled care

- Encourages HHS to maintain ongoing engagement with clinical experts and patient populations in AI policy development
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Leading heart failure medical society urges HHS to adopt risk-proportionate AI governance, value-based reimbursement, and prospective evaluation frameworks, arguing that heart failure's complexity and treatment gaps make it an ideal proving ground for responsible clinical AI.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Heart Failure Society of America (HFSA), Kenneth B. Margulies, MD (President)
- **Type:** Advocacy Group / Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Multidisciplinary medical society representing physicians, scientists, nurses, nurse practitioners, pharmacists, and patients focused on heart failure care
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Members directly deliver heart failure care and would implement AI tools; organization advocates for policies affecting heart failure treatment quality and patient outcomes

AI can improve efficiency, reduce clinician burden, and increase reliability of evidence-based care—but because heart failure care is inherently high risk, adoption must be accompanied by robust governance, rigorous prospective evaluation, and continuous monitoring to ensure safety, effectiveness, and equity.

  - Require clear clinical ownership and defined human oversight for AI tools informing diagnoses, triage, medication titration, escalation decisions, or discharge planning

- Align reimbursement and federal incentives to support value-based AI adoption
  - Prioritize payment approaches rewarding demonstrated clinical utility and burden reduction
  - Avoid per-alert or per-use incentives that may drive volume without value
  - Provide time-limited implementation support and demonstration models
  - Offer CMS incentives rewarding safe AI deployment

- Shift evaluation expectations from retrospective accuracy to prospective clinical utility

- Establish comprehensive lifecycle evaluation framework for AI tools

  - Delayed escalation of care

- Retrospective performance metrics alone are insufficient to justify clinical deployment

- Significant gaps, variation, and disparities persist in use of guideline-recommended therapies
  - Fewer than one in four eligible patients receive comprehensive quadruple GDMT
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Heart failure as a proving ground for clinical AI
  - The condition's complexity, longitudinal care pathways, and documented treatment gaps make it an ideal test case for responsible AI adoption
  - ARPA-H's ADVOCATE program already targeting agentic AI as "clinician extenders" for heart failure

- Striking treatment gap statistics
  - Despite clear evidence, fewer than 25% of eligible patients receive comprehensive guideline-directed therapy
  - Optimal uptake could prevent 1.19 million deaths globally per year

- Heart failure continuum framework for AI design
  - One-third of U.S. adults at risk (Stage A), 24-34% have pre-heart failure (Stage B)
  - Rising obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease accelerating progression
  - AI must address all stages, not just symptomatic disease

- Governance proportionality concept
  - Oversight expectations should scale with clinical impact rather than applying uniform requirements

- "Retrospective performance metrics alone are insufficient to justify clinical deployment."
