structuredSections.detailedContent:
- Executive Summary
  - Omada has focused on reimbursing health care professionals for digital health use since founding in 2011
  - Collaborated with American Medical Association to adopt first-ever CPT code for fully virtual services (0488T) in January 2018
  - Published peer-reviewed literature on digital health reimbursement and participates actively in industry work groups like AMA Value-Based Care Workgroup
  - Most impactful policy change would be moving more rapidly toward paying for health outcomes instead of paying for time
  - ASTP and CMS should become well versed through wide public input in:
    - The costs of developing and running AI in health care organizations
    - The risks of fraud, waste, abuse, and program integrity challenges with AI use compared to analog or EHR data
  - CMS should use Administrative Procedures Act tools (listening sessions, testimony, RFIs) to collect specific information
    - Policy-making should not be based on casual information collection or unfounded assumptions about low technology costs
  - Responsibly leveraging AI requires significant technical infrastructure, computing power, cybersecurity defenses, and sophisticated diligence from data scientists to clinicians
  - Policy-makers must collect diverse and specific business information from entities bearing these costs today to fairly set payment policies
  - Policy-making should account for the healthcare system 15+ years in the future, not just catch up to today's system

- Vision 2040
  - By 2040, U.S. healthcare system will use AI to reduce time to treat illness and improve health
  - Will leverage digital health foundation built with HITECH Act to document health outcomes achieved
  - AI will help understand outcomes and, more importantly, achieve them
  - Most ambulatory care will track individuals' health using AI to identify intervention opportunities in real time instead of 12-18 month data lag (like prior HEDIS gap remediation work)
  - Acute care hospitals will use AI to predict where interventions can prevent worsening health or bend utilization curves, helping people get acute services at right time while avoiding wasteful care
  - As we move toward 2040, time spent on care becomes less relevant; more relevant factors:
    - Ensuring professionals use AI to accurately document outcomes (good and bad) so we can pay for outcomes instead of time
    - Understanding real costs to professionals of using AI effectively to set appropriate outcome rates
    - Bringing patients along by ensuring they understand how their data contributes to AI solutions and enables collaboration toward better health
    - Freeing up professionals' time for more intense, human-necessary care they're trained for and long to do
  - ASTP and policy-makers should move more rapidly toward value-based and outcomes-based care
  - Policy decisions should be based on widely collected information about costs to develop and run AI-supported health care organizations

- Background on Omada
  - 15-year-old digital health company providing health care services centered on human care teams
  - Focus areas: diabetes prevention, cardiovascular disease prevention, management of diabetes, hypertension, and cholesterol (single or comorbid conditions)
  - Since 2020, offers video-based tele-physical therapy using licensed physical therapists
  - In 2024, launched care tracks within lifestyle behavior change programs to support individuals taking GLP-1s for weight loss
  - Programs connect to third-party FDA-approved devices (Class 3, 2, and 1) and leverage computer vision to process real-time biometric information
  - Data displayed in real time to care professionals in bespoke systems and to members through mobile applications
  - Data forms important part of data science and AI work supporting members
  - Used data science techniques since founding; recently developed own AI-powered features including:
    - Generative AI features for member wellness education
    - Internal tools supporting clinical staff with summarization and clinical recommendations
  - As of February 2026, data stores included 300 million weigh-ins from members
  - Early example of data science use: In 2018, chief data scientist shared at HealthDatapalooza how system discovered member tracking food unexpectedly at 4am
    - Data science team alerted health coach, who started important conversation about work schedule change and managing healthy eating
  - At 2025 Horizon Day event, launched OmadaSpark generative AI chatbot with two key functions:
    - Supporting members with nutrition education
    - Helping members identify their own barriers to healthier lifestyle
  - Since then, launched other member-facing AI educational tools
  - Business model seeks to align interests with members and insurance companies/customers who offer programs and reimburse Omada
  - Latest pricing models for cardiometabolic programs based on each member's activity level to tie payments to desired health outcomes
  - With data science and AI, can improve program efficiency, member engagement and health, and support care professionals' expertise
  - Don't charge customers more for AI use; customers expect these technologies
  - Believe this will be an expectation across healthcare by 2040

- Response to Questions 1 and 2: Barriers to AI Innovation and Policy Changes
  - Public dialogue on barriers focuses on liability for potential harm and privacy issues
  - Omada does not believe potential for mistakes alone must be a barrier to AI uptake
  - All care environments must meet minimum quality and safety standards to reduce mistakes and manage liability
  - AI use is no different; healthcare has navigated new factors for many earlier innovations
    - From accuracy concerns about fax machines using thermal paper to Class 3 FDA-approved devices
  - Safety and allocation of responsibility/liability are important, but Omada sees no federal policy role in influencing liability allocation among business counterparts
  - In privacy area, ASTP could be very helpful
    - Many AI developers may not appreciate AI tools are likely not "conduits" exempt from HIPAA's business associate rules
    - AI developers may not understand HIPAA business associates can use PHI only for healthcare operations of covered entity customers, not their own separate business operations
    - Health care providers may not have access to adequate AI tools meeting HIPAA requirements
  - Educational role for ASTP and OCR to contextualize HIPAA rules for AI advent, similar to what agencies did with HITECH Act for EHRs and cloud computing

- Response to Question 3: Novel Legal and Implementation Issues
  - Many organizations including large multi-site academic health institutions are assessing novel AI issues and developing their own governance structures
  - Omada took cross-functional approach to develop own governance model
    - Leveraged information security "threat model" concept to create model for assessing AI risk in developing healthcare AI features
    - Published as open-source tool
  - Other organizations have looked to establish or leverage outside governance certifications
  - HITRUST and SOC 2 evaluation of security controls, while not federally mandated, have taken root throughout health care system
  - Challenge for customers to obtain reasonable liability limitations or indemnities from largest AI developers
    - This issue exists beyond AI realm and arises with largest players in any industry
  - Many organizations are meeting challenge of thoughtfully developing governance structures
  - ASTP may play role in promoting development of effective, compliant models for stakeholders
  - Important role in helping avoid regulatory capture of AI oversight by large incumbents
  - Any ASTP actions should bear in mind rapid AI evolution and foster innovation even from newcomers

- Response to Question 4: AI Evaluation Methods
  - Omada frequently answers customer questions about AI solution design and controls
    - How safety and quality minimums are ensured
    - How fidelity with clinical standards is maintained
    - How privacy is protected
  - Broadly adopted measures becoming clear almost as fast as AI features are developed
  - ASTP could accelerate development of appropriate measures by cataloging them and fostering common definitions and methods across healthcare
  - Caution against adoption of specific measures via regulation
    - Measures codified in regulation can become inflexible and rapidly outdated, particularly in rapidly evolving spaces

- Response to Question 5: Supporting Private Sector Activities
  - AI use presents important risks: some tools may cause harm, exploit users, misuse data, or fail to work
  - Inadequate AI tools can slow adoption of needed innovations and efficiencies
  - Care must be taken to ensure well-funded and dominant organizations don't control accreditation or certification processes that freeze out smaller, potentially innovative organizations
  - Omada's approach relies on fundamental healthcare compliance concepts, not outside accreditors
  - Healthcare industry understands baseline safety and quality concepts well
  - Specialty society guidelines inform clinical best practices
  - OmadaSpark nutrition education tool fine-tuned with specialty society guidelines and Omada's own clinical practice guides
  - Existing federal policy including latest FDA Guidelines on clinical decision support align with this approach
  - In short term, ASTP can best support AI adoption by ensuring AI embraces unique safety, quality, privacy, and security needs of healthcare system
  - Ensuring developers and health care organizations have aligned understanding of these fundamentals

- Response to Question 7: Decision Makers and Administrative Hurdles
  - Developing or acquiring AI tools is multifaceted and cross-functional project
  - Most effectively approached collaboratively among those with clinical, legal, regulatory, privacy, information security, and data science expertise

- Response to Question 8: Enhanced Interoperability
  - Important step would be ensuring digital health care providers adhering to same legal, privacy, and security standards as traditional providers are treated same under TEFCA governance rules
  - Omada previously shared observations on this topic in comments to HTI-2

- Conclusion
  - Fourth Industrial Revolution in healthcare is here, powered by data science, digital data, and AI
  - ASTP and CMS policy-making can massively impact how technology-enabled care is delivered
  - To leverage AI's transformative potential, policy-making should move more rapidly toward value-based and outcomes-based payment models
  - Appropriate, modern payment policies will incentivize AI use in clinical care
  - Policy-making should be well informed about costs of developing and using AI in clinical practice
  - Leveraging private, secure, and safe AI to produce clinically sound outcomes is a complex endeavor
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: A pioneering digital health company that helped create the first CPT code for virtual services urges HHS to shift from time-based to outcomes-based payment models to truly incentivize AI adoption, while warning against unfounded assumptions about technology costs and regulatory capture by large incumbents.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Omada Health, Inc.
- **Type:** Business (Digital Health Company)
- **Role/Expertise:** 15-year digital health pioneer; collaborated with AMA on first virtual services CPT code; published peer-reviewed research on digital health reimbursement; Chief Privacy & Regulatory Officer Lucia C. Savage signing
- **Geographic Scope:** National (headquartered in Dallas, TX)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected as a digital health provider using AI for diabetes prevention, cardiovascular disease management, and lifestyle behavior change programs; business model tied to outcomes-based payment
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe the most impactful policy change to incentivize high-value clinical interventions would be to move more rapidly toward paying for health outcomes instead of paying for time. Policy-making should be based on widely collected information about the real costs of developing and running AI in healthcare—not unfounded assumptions about low technology costs—and should account for the healthcare system 15+ years in the future, not just catch up to today.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Shift payment models from time-based to outcomes-based reimbursement
  - This is the most impactful policy change to incentivize AI adoption
  - Time spent on care will become less relevant as AI improves efficiency
- ASTP and CMS should gather comprehensive information through formal processes
  - Use Administrative Procedures Act tools: listening sessions, testimony, RFIs
  - Collect specific business information from entities bearing AI development costs today
  - Focus on real costs of developing and running AI in healthcare organizations
  - Assess fraud, waste, abuse, and program integrity risks compared to current analog/EHR systems
- ASTP and OCR should provide educational guidance on HIPAA and AI
  - Clarify that AI tools are likely not "conduits" exempt from business associate rules
  - Explain that business associates can only use PHI for covered entity customers' healthcare operations
  - Contextualize HIPAA rules for AI similar to HITECH Act guidance for EHRs and cloud computing
- Avoid codifying specific AI evaluation measures in regulation
  - Measures become inflexible and rapidly outdated in fast-evolving spaces
  - Instead, catalog measures and foster common definitions and methods across healthcare
- Prevent regulatory capture by large incumbents
  - Ensure accreditation/certification processes don't freeze out smaller innovative organizations
  - Foster innovation from newcomers, not just established players
- Treat digital health providers equally under TEFCA governance rules
  - Digital providers adhering to same legal, privacy, and security standards as traditional providers should receive equal treatment
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Policy-making based on unfounded assumptions about low technology costs
  - Responsibly leveraging AI requires significant technical infrastructure, computing power, cybersecurity defenses, and sophisticated diligence from data scientists to clinicians
  - Policy-makers may not understand the true complexity and cost
- HIPAA compliance gaps in AI development community
  - Many AI developers don't understand business associate requirements
  - Healthcare providers may lack access to adequate AI tools meeting HIPAA requirements
- Risk of regulatory capture by large incumbents
  - Dominant organizations could control accreditation/certification processes
  - Could freeze out smaller, potentially innovative organizations
  - Difficult for customers to obtain reasonable liability limitations from largest AI developers
- Regulations becoming inflexible and outdated
  - Specific measures codified in regulation can't keep pace with rapid AI evolution
- Unequal treatment of digital health providers
  - Digital providers not treated same as traditional providers under TEFCA governance rules despite meeting same standards
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Created first-ever CPT code for fully virtual services (0488T) in collaboration with AMA in 2018, demonstrating pioneering role in digital health reimbursement
- Data science system detected member tracking food at 4am, alerting health coach to start conversation about work schedule changes—illustrating how AI can enable personalized, timely interventions
- As of February 2026, data stores include 300 million weigh-ins from members, showing scale of real-world health data being used for AI development
- Business model already ties payments to member activity levels rather than time, proving outcomes-based payment is viable today
- Don't charge customers more for AI use because customers already expect these technologies—suggesting market is ahead of payment policy
- Developed and published open-source AI risk assessment model based on information security "threat model" concept, offering practical governance approach for others
- OmadaSpark generative AI chatbot fine-tuned with specialty society guidelines and internal clinical practice guides, demonstrating how to operationalize clinical standards in AI
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The most impactful policy change to 'incentivize and... promote high-value clinical interventions' would be to move more rapidly toward a system of paying for health outcomes instead of paying for time."
- "Policy-making about incentivizing the use of AI in clinical practice is not based on casual information collection or potentially unfounded assumptions about the low cost of technology."
- "The Fourth Industrial Revolution in healthcare is here—powered by data science, digital data, and AI in particular."
